visits.text: This morning you leave Montana for Canada via the Chief Mountain International Highway—the shortest and most spectacular route between Glacier and Waterton. Take US-89 north from Babb, then turn onto MT-17. The road crosses the prairies of the Blackfeet Reservation with Chief Mountain as backdrop, a sacred mountain to Native Americans, recognizable by its unique rectangular shape—vertical walls instead of the classic alpine triangle. The border crossing is the highest in elevation along the entire US-Canada border. Have your passport ready—the station is open mid-May through late September.

Arriving in Waterton, drive straight up to the Prince of Wales Hotel for the view. You don't need to stay here—the lobby is open to the public. This 7-story wooden hotel, built in 1926-1927 by the American (not Canadian!) Great Northern Railway, perches on a wind-battered promontory facing Waterton Lake. During construction, 145 km/h winds shifted the building's wings by 7.5 cm—the contractor chose to leave the building slightly askew rather than risk breaking it. The hotel was named for the Prince of Wales (future Edward VIII) hoping he'd sleep there during his 1927 Canadian tour—he preferred his own ranch. The site has been a National Historic Site since 1992.

Take the Red Rock Parkway (15 minutes from the townsite) to Red Rock Canyon. The bright red rocks you'll see are argillite, a sedimentary rock 1.5 billion years old. The red comes from iron oxidation—the green layers you'll spot in places are the same rocks but formed in an oxygen-poor environment. The canyon follows a short loop (20 minutes) and you can even walk in the creek bed in summer. Remnants of hunting camps dating back 9,000 years have been found nearby.

Back at the townsite, detour by Cameron Falls—a stone's throw from the center, it's one of the most accessible waterfalls in the Canadian Rockies. The bridge above places you right over the drop. Watch for slippery ground when there's spray. Then stroll through the little village of Waterton: galleries, shops, and a few restaurants with lake views.

activities.text: For the best panoramic view of Waterton, climb the Bear's Hump—a short but steep hike (2.8 km round-trip, 225 m elevation gain, about 30-40 minutes up). From the summit, you'll look down on Waterton Lake, the Prince of Wales Hotel below, and the prairies stretching to the eastern horizon. No guardrails at the edge—stay cautious. The trail starts directly from the Prince of Wales Hotel parking lot.

Late afternoon, head down to the townsite marina for a cruise on Waterton Lake. The boat travels up the lake to Goat Haunt in the United States—one of the few places in the world where you can cross an international border on a tour boat. The round-trip cruise lasts about 2 hours. Waterton-Glacier is the world's first International Peace Park, created in 1932, and a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1995.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Rockies Loop: Glacier & Waterton

places.name: Abilene

visits.text: Abilene - From Cattle Town to Presidential Legacy: This central Kansas city traces its journey from Wild West cow town to the boyhood home of a five-star general and president.

Dwight D. Eisenhower Presidential Library, Museum and Boyhood Home - Where a Hometown Boy Became a World Leader: Upgraded in 2019 for D-Day's 75th anniversary, this comprehensive complex uses multimedia to let Dwight and Mamie Eisenhower tell their life stories in their own words.
Old Abilene Town - Relive the Cattle Drive Era: This recreated frontier settlement brings Abilene's notorious cattle town days to life, complete with make-believe gunfights erupting in the streets.
Abilene and Smoky Valley Railroad - Vintage Rails Through History: Board historic trains at the original depot for scenic journeys through the Smoky Hill River Valley on restored vintage equipment.

activities.text: Historic Downtown Abilene - Where Presidents Walked: Stroll the streets where young Ike grew up, browsing antique shops and local eateries in well-preserved historic buildings.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

map_keywords: Dwight D. Eisenhower Presidential Library, Museum and Boyhood Home

visits.text: Discover Ah-Shi-Sle-Pah Wilderness Study Area, a remote badlands landscape where extraordinary hoodoo formations called fairy chimneys rise from colorful sedimentary layers deposited millions of years ago. The dramatic erosional features, created by wind and water sculpting soft rock over millennia, create some of the most unusual scenery in New Mexico. Geologists and photographers find endless fascination in these bizarre formations.

Search for dinosaur bones, petrified wood, and other fossils exposed in the eroding hillsides of this paleontological treasure trove. The wilderness study area preserves a landscape virtually unchanged since the age of dinosaurs, where ancient seabeds and river channels left behind the sediments that now reveal prehistoric life. The vast, empty landscape provides profound solitude for those seeking escape from civilization.

activities.text: Photograph the surreal hoodoo formations during golden hour when warm light transforms the multi-colored rock layers into a spectacular display. The remote location requires careful planning but rewards adventurous visitors with landscapes rarely seen by tourists. Camping in this pristine wilderness offers exceptional stargazing opportunities.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

places.name: Aiken

visits.text: Discover Aiken's distinguished equestrian heritage along South Boundary Avenue, a magnificent oak-canopied road reflecting the town's long association with horse country. The Aiken Thoroughbred Racing Hall of Fame and Museum documents this sporting tradition that has shaped community identity for generations. This graceful town combines small-town Southern character with refined sporting culture.

activities.text: Stroll through Aiken's charming downtown, where historic buildings house boutiques and gathering places that reflect the town's genteel atmosphere. The rural setting and equestrian influence create a distinctive character unlike anywhere else in South Carolina. Aiken's peaceful pace and natural beauty make it perfect for travelers seeking authentic Southern relaxation.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

map_keywords: Aiken Thoroughbred Racing Hall of Fame and Museum

places.name: Alamogordo

visits.text: White Sands National Park - The world's largest gypsum dune field: 275 square miles of pure white sand. The gypsum comes from the dried-up Lake Lucero - crystals break into fine grains carried by the wind. Drive the Dunes Drive (8 miles) that plunges into the heart of the dune field. The deeper you go, the more surreal the landscape becomes - an immaculate white desert 360 degrees around. Go late afternoon for golden light, or stay for sunset (check closing time, it varies by season).

New Mexico Museum of Space History - Head up into the hills above Alamogordo for this museum tracing New Mexico's space history. Worth knowing: the world's first nuclear test (Trinity, July 16, 1945) took place 60 miles from here. White Sands Missile Range, adjacent to the park, is still active - if you find the park road temporarily closed, it's due to a missile test.

activities.text: Sledding the dunes - Buy or rent a plastic saucer at the visitor center and slide down the dunes. It's fun, free (except saucer purchase), and kids love it. Gypsum doesn't heat up like normal sand - you can walk barefoot without burning your feet.

Alkali Flat Trail hike - If you crave absolute solitude, this 5-mile loop crosses the heart of the dune field. No shade, no landmarks - follow the marker posts. Bring at least 2 liters of water and don't start midday in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Desert Parks of Texas and New Mexico: Big Bend, Carlsbad, White Sands, and Guadalupe

map_keywords: White Sands National Park New Mexico
Dune Life Nature Trail White Sands
New Mexico Museum of Space History Alamogordo

visits.text: Climb Star Dune, North America's tallest dune at 750 feet. These dunes formed 440,000 years ago when Lake Alamosa dried up and winds carried sand toward the curve of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains—where it still accumulates. Count on 2 to 3 hours round-trip and never go without water: the surface can reach 150°F in summer.

If you're here from May to early July, don't miss Medano Creek: a snowmelt stream that creates a real sand beach with running water at the base of the dunes. Kids splash, photographers set up low-angle shots. By July–August, it's often dried up—check conditions first.

Detour to Zapata Falls, 6 miles south of the park: to reach them, you wade upstream for 100 meters, headlamp recommended for the last section through rock. The reward is welcome coolness after a morning in the dunes.

activities.text: Go sandboarding or sand sledding on the dune slopes: boards rent outside the park for $15–20 per day. The descent is short but the climb on foot is solid cardio. Avoid 11 AM to 3 PM in summer—the sand becomes literally scorching.

Great Sand Dunes is ranked among the quietest national parks in the United States. If you camp at Piñon Flats, walk a mile into the dunefield after midnight—the Milky Way is visible to the naked eye at 7,515 feet elevation, and the silence of a sand desert under the stars has no equivalent.

days_plan.itin_title: Colorado-Wyoming Road Trip: National Parks, Rockies & Wide Open Spaces

visits.text: Santa Fe (60-mile detour) - Take the detour - New Mexico's capital is worth the 60-mile side trip. The Plaza, surrounded by adobe buildings, has been the city center since 1610 - it's the oldest state capital in the United States. The art galleries on Canyon Road (over 80 in one mile) are free to browse.

Meow Wolf (Santa Fe) - A two-story Victorian house where every door, drawer, and passage leads into a parallel universe: bioluminescent forest, spaceship, crystal cave. It's an immersive art experience created by a local collective. Book online - it often sells out.

Albuquerque - Central Avenue - Drive down Central Avenue - Route 66 runs straight through Albuquerque here. Stop in the Nob Hill district, with its shops in 1940s buildings. In the evening, the vintage motel neons light up along the avenue.

Petroglyph National Monument - Over 24,000 petroglyphs carved into volcanic rock by ancestors of the Pueblos and early Spanish settlers. The Boca Negra Canyon trail (3 short loops, ~100 petroglyphs) is the most accessible. Arrive early - there's no shade and it heats up fast.

Indian Pueblo Cultural Center - This museum, managed by New Mexico's 19 pueblos, is the best introduction to Pueblo cultures. You'll see pottery, turquoise jewelry, and weavings. On certain days, dancers in traditional costume perform in the patio - ask at the desk.

Old Town Albuquerque - Founded in 1706, this plaza surrounded by adobe buildings houses San Felipe de Neri Church (1793). The galleries sell Navajo and Zuni turquoise and silver jewelry - beware of imitations.

activities.text: Sunset from the Sandia Peak Tramway - The longest aerial tramway in North America (2.7 miles) takes you to 10,378 feet. The view over Albuquerque and the desert at sunset is extraordinary - the city lights up below while the sky turns red. Bring a jacket, it's 20-25°F cooler at the top.

Taste New Mexico green chile - Here, they ask you 'Red or green?' with every order. Roasted green chile is the local pride - you'll find it on burgers, enchiladas, and even fries. Answer 'Christmas' if you want both.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands
Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica
Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica

places.name: Alliance

visits.text: Three miles north of Alliance on Highway 87, 39 vintage American cars from the 1950s and 60s stand upright in a circle 96 feet in diameter, all spray-painted gray. Carhenge is a full-scale replica of Stonehenge, built in the summer of 1987 by engineer Jim Reinders and roughly 35 family members as a memorial to his father, who once farmed this land. Reinders dedicated it at the summer solstice with songs, champagne and a play his family wrote titled Unhinged. The heel stone is a 1962 Cadillac. Some cars are planted trunk-down in 5-foot pits; the arch cars are welded onto them. Alliance nearly tore it down in the late 1980s - the city council was called after someone reported that a man was burying cars in a field - but public opinion swung in its favor, and in 2013 the city took ownership of the 10-acre site. Enter free; donations welcome.

activities.text: Walk the adjacent Car Art Reserve - a sculpture park that has grown around Carhenge since the late 1980s, where local artists contributed pieces including The Fourd Seasons (a wheat tribute) and a Carnastoga Wagon built from car parts. There is a car on the hill that visitors can sign with a marker - a tradition since the site opened. The Carhenge site became one of the best viewing spots in the country for the 2017 total solar eclipse, drawing thousands of stargazers to this remote corner of the Nebraska panhandle. At night, with no light pollution for miles, the formation takes on an entirely different character - eerie, improbable, and somehow appropriate to the plains.

days_plan.itin_title: Nebraska Sandhills & Pioneer Trails: From Omaha to the Rockies

map_keywords: Knight Museum and Sandhills Center Alliance Nebraska

places.name: Alpine Tx

visits.text: The Museum of the Big Bend, on the Sul Ross State University campus, is the best entry point for understanding the region: geology, Native American history, arrival of Buffalo Soldiers, 1880s railroad expansion, and Chihuahuan Desert fauna are all covered in one well-designed building. Free admission. Allow at minimum 1.5 hours - don't miss the relief model of the region with its dozens of interactive light points.

The Holland Hotel is Alpine's historic heart: built in 1928 in Spanish Colonial Revival style with its arches and ironwork, it already welcomed Southern Pacific Railroad travelers. The terrace at sunset, with the Davis Mountains as backdrop, is one of the day's finest moments. Even if you're not staying, the hotel's worth a stop for a drink.

Downtown Alpine, designated a Texas Cultural District in 2011, can be walked in 45 minutes. Murals on adobe facades tell stories of the frontier, ranches, and railroad history. Spot the "Greetings from Alpine, Texas" mural at the corner of Holland Avenue and 5th Street - the town's most photographed image.

activities.text: Climb to the summit of Hancock Hill, the rise behind Sul Ross State University, for a 360° view over Alpine and the surrounding mountains. The hike is short (under an hour) and undemanding - a tradition among students since 1981, who left an old desk at the summit where every visitor signs a logbook.

Evening local beer at Big Bend Brewing Company, Alpine's craft brewery supplying bars and restaurants throughout the Trans-Pecos. Terlingua Gold, Desert Pale Ale, and their seasonals are served directly in the brew room or on the patio.

days_plan.itin_title: Desert to Alamo: West Texas & Big Bend - 10 Days from El Paso to San Antonio

map_keywords: Museum of the Big Bend Sul Ross State University Alpine
Big Bend Brewing Company Alpine Texas

places.name: Altoona

visits.text: You stand on the observation platform and watch a railroad curve 2,375 feet in diameter carved into the wooded flanks of the Alleghenies - and every 20 to 30 minutes, a freight train sometimes over a mile long traverses it entirely, both ends visible simultaneously on opposite slopes. Horseshoe Curve, inaugurated February 15, 1854 by the Pennsylvania Railroad, solved a fundamental problem: crossing the Alleghenies without slopes too steep for locomotives of the era. Engineer John Edgar Thomson designed a continuous ascending curve that climbs gently over 12 miles from Altoona to the summit at Gallitzin - reducing the Philadelphia-Pittsburgh journey from 23 days by stagecoach to 15 hours by train. Excavated by pick and shovel by Irish immigrants, the curve cost $2.5 million and was classified an engineering wonder alongside the Panama Canal and Empire State Building. In 1942, Nazi agents landed by submarine had the primary mission of blowing it up - arrested by the FBI in New York before reaching their target. More than 111 million tons of freight pass annually, about 45 to 50 trains per day. A funicular from the Visitor Center takes you to track level in 2 minutes. Combo admission with Railroaders Memorial Museum: $12 adult.

You enter the Railroaders Memorial Museum, installed in the Pennsylvania Railroad's former locomotive maintenance building, and discover that Altoona was for a century the beating heart of American railroading. The PRR built its main workshops here in 1850 - at its peak, Altoona employed 15,000 railroaders in a city of 80,000, meaning one job in five directly tied to locomotives. The museum traces this history through full-size locomotives, models, uniforms, and thousands of artifacts. An entire room is devoted to Pennsylvania railroad popular culture: railroaders' lunch boxes, unions, strikes - and the identity of a city built around a single employer for four generations. Open daily May through October 10am-5pm, closed Monday off-season.

You drive 20 minutes west to the Allegheny Portage Railroad National Historic Site at Cresson, and discover the ingenious and slow system that Horseshoe Curve replaced in 1854. Since 1834, Pennsylvania used a combination of canals and steam-powered inclined planes to cross the Alleghenies: wagons were hauled on cables to the ridges, then descended by gravity to the next canal sections. The journey took four days with dozens of transfers - versus 15 hours after the curve opened. Ruins of engineers' houses, steam engine foundations, and a reconstructed rail segment allow you to visualize what was, at the time, one of the technological wonders of the United States. Open daily 9am-5pm; free with America the Beautiful pass, otherwise $7 adult.

activities.text: You settle on the observation platform and wait - not long. Freight convoys pass 45 to 50 times daily; when such a train enters the curve, both ends are visible simultaneously on opposite hillsides - the most photographed image in American railroading. Helper locomotives of 3,200 kilowatts are coupled at the rear to push the heaviest convoys uphill. The only passenger train of the day - the Amtrak Pennsylvanian Pittsburgh-New York - passes mid-morning eastbound. Check the exact schedule on Amtrak's site before your visit and wait for it: it's a special experience at this place.

You visit Baker Mansion, a neoclassical marble residence built in 1849 by Elias Baker, iron master who ran the region's first major foundries before Bethlehem Steel. The house remained in the Baker family until 1914 without major modification, making it one of the best-preserved Victorian interiors in Pennsylvania: original furniture ordered from Philadelphia and Europe, period wallpapers, silverware, and clothing from the 1850-1900 period. The house is 10 minutes from the Railroaders Memorial Museum - the two visits naturally complement each other in a half-day.

days_plan.itin_title: Pennsylvania Loop: From Amish Country to the Appalachians - 11 Days

map_keywords: Horseshoe Curve National Historic Landmark Altoona PA
Allegheny Portage Railroad National Historic Site Cresson

visits.text: You enter the Noé House, built in 1864 as a communal kitchen and now the main museum of the Heritage Society's three-building campus. The exhibits hold 12,000 artifacts and 10,000 archival records tracing the Community of True Inspiration back to its 1714 German roots in the Pietist Awakening. Don't miss the 'Amana Meteorite of 1875,' a genuine chunk of the meteorite whose fiery fall was visible as far as Chicago. Open April through October; admission charged.

You step into Iowa's only surviving operational textile mill, weaving goods continuously since 1859 - through the communal era, the Great Change of 1932, and every decade since. Five full-time artisans still produce blankets, scarves, and table linens on historic looms. The adjacent Warped and Woven shop sells directly from the production floor. The larger mill complex now houses the Hotel Millwright, which opened in 2020. Shop open daily; mill tours by arrangement on Maifest and Oktoberfest Saturdays.

The Amana churches express Pietist humility through architecture: no steeple, no stained glass, plain brick walls, simple wooden benches arranged without ornament. The church in Main Amana is the most accessible and best-preserved example. Services are still held by the Amana Church Society. Visitors are welcome outside service hours. The contrast with mainstream American church design is immediate and striking.

activities.text: You follow the 3.1-mile Kolonieweg (Colony Way) trail from the Amana depot along the Mill Race canal that once powered the colonies' mills. The route connects Main Amana to Middle Amana and circles the 170-acre Lily Lake, where trumpeter swans and river otters make frequent appearances. A second trail cuts through the Amana Forest along an Iowa River bluff. Both trails are flat and easy; free access.

You make your way through Ackerman Winery, operating since 1956 - Iowa's oldest working winery, now run by the Wyant family since 2015. Village Winery and White Cross Cellars both offer tastings within the same tight cluster of villages. For dinner, the Ronneburg Restaurant - named after the 13th-century German castle where the Inspirationists once took refuge from persecution - serves traditional German and American dishes in a converted communal kitchen. The entire circuit covers less than three miles of driving.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs
Iowa Backroads: From the Missouri Bluffs to the Mississippi Cliffs

visits.text: Rock Cafe (Stroud, OK) - Stop for breakfast at this cafe built in 1939 with local stone - it inspired the character Sally in Cars. Burned down in 2008, it was rebuilt stone by stone by the community.

Round Barn (Arcadia, OK) - Take a look at this 1898 round wooden barn, the only one of its kind on Route 66. It nearly got demolished in the 1980s before being saved by a retired carpenter volunteer who restored it alone over four years.

POPS 66 (Arcadia, OK) - You can't miss the 66-foot soda bottle sculpture marking the entrance - this gas station-restaurant offers over 700 varieties of sodas from around the world. Try something weird, you won't find it anywhere else.

Oklahoma Route 66 Museum (Clinton) - The best museum dedicated to Route 66 along the entire route. The exhibit is organized by decades, from the 1920s to the 1980s, with reconstructions of period diners, gas stations, and motels. Allow 1h30.

activities.text: Dinner and atmosphere in Amarillo - The historic downtown on 6th Street has antique shops, bars, and restaurants far better than the tourist zone along the Interstate. If you like challenges, a famous steakhouse offers a free 72-ounce steak if you finish it in an hour.

days_plan.itin_title: Thelma & Louise Movie Route: Utah & Arizona Filming Locations
Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica

visits.text: Amelia Island - Where Southern Charm Meets Atlantic Shores: This barrier island just south of Georgia has captured the hearts of the jet set with its blend of historic downtown Fernandina Beach, pristine Atlantic beaches, and laid-back atmosphere. The island receives more visitors than all other Florida barrier islands combined, yet maintains a peaceful, uncrowded character.

Historic Fernandina Beach - Victorian Elegance Preserved: Wander through 50 blocks of beautifully restored Victorian architecture housing family-owned shops, galleries, and local businesses. This is Old Florida at its most charming, where southern hospitality remains genuine.

activities.text: World-Class Golf - Links Among the Live Oaks: Multiple championship courses have established Amelia Island as a premier golfing destination, with fairways winding through maritime forests draped in Spanish moss.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Stand before Amicalola Falls, Georgia's tallest waterfall tumbling 729 feet over a series of rock ledges into a lush forested gorge. The Cherokee name meaning 'tumbling waters' perfectly describes this spectacular cascade, one of the tallest waterfalls east of the Mississippi River. Trails to both the base and rim provide dramatically different perspectives of the thundering waters.

Hike through the surrounding state park where hardwood forests, mountain streams, and abundant wildlife create ideal conditions for nature photography and peaceful contemplation. The park serves as the southern approach to the Appalachian Trail, attracting hikers from around the world preparing for long-distance adventures. On-site naturalist programs offer archery, animal encounters, and guided wilderness exploration.

activities.text: Follow the 8-mile Approach Trail connecting Amicalola Falls to the official southern terminus of the Appalachian Trail at Springer Mountain. Even short sections of this legendary pathway provide unforgettable hiking through pristine mountain wilderness frequented by deer, wild turkeys, and occasionally black bears.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

visits.text: Disneyland Resort - Where Dreams Come True Since 1955: Walt Disney's original Magic Kingdom continues to enchant visitors with its blend of nostalgia, innovation, and impeccable showmanship. The adjacent Disney California Adventure celebrates the Golden State's landmarks and culture.

Disneyland Park - The Happiest Place on Earth: Eight themed lands transport guests from Main Street USA to the jungles of Adventureland, the futuristic Tomorrowland, and the fairytale castles of Fantasyland. Classic attractions like Pirates of the Caribbean set the standard for immersive themed entertainment.

Disney California Adventure - California, Imagined by Disney: This sister park celebrates the state's culture through attractions like Cars Land, a life-size recreation of the Pixar film's desert town, and the Incredicoaster whipping along the Paradise Bay waterfront.

activities.text: Downtown Disney District - Entertainment Without a Ticket: This outdoor promenade connecting the parks and hotels offers shopping, dining, and entertainment accessible to all visitors without theme park admission.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Jour 1: Sterling Highway Scenic Drive - The return route to Anchorage traverses the western Kenai Peninsula, following Cook Inlet's eastern shore with views of volcanic peaks across the water. The highway passes through the Kenai National Wildlife Refuge and small communities that support commercial fishing and outdoor recreation.

Jour 1: Kenai and Soldotna - These sister cities along the Kenai River serve as the commercial center of the western Kenai Peninsula, with the Kenai Visitors and Cultural Center offering exhibits on the region's Russian heritage and natural history.

Jour 2: Anchorage Museum - The state's largest museum houses comprehensive collections spanning Alaska's art, history, and science. Galleries explore Alaska Native cultures through extensive artifact collections and contemporary art, while the Smithsonian Arctic Studies Center provides access to hundreds of objects representing indigenous peoples across the circumpolar north.

Jour 2: Alaska Native Heritage Center - This cultural facility on the outskirts of Anchorage introduces visitors to Alaska's eleven distinct Native cultural groups through traditional village sites, demonstrations of crafts and customs, and performances of song and dance. The 26-acre campus includes a lake trail connecting authentic reproductions of traditional dwellings representing coastal, interior, and Arctic peoples.

Jour 2: Downtown Anchorage - Alaska's largest city with over 290,000 residents serves as the state's economic and transportation hub. The walkable downtown features public art, historic buildings from the city's 1915 founding, and views of the Chugach Mountains rising immediately to the east.

activities.text: Jour 1: Cook Inlet Beluga Viewing - The waters along the Seward Highway between Girdwood and Anchorage provide opportunities to observe the endangered Cook Inlet beluga whale population from roadside pullouts, particularly during salmon runs.

Jour 1: Turnagain Arm Scenic Corridor - This dramatic fjord southeast of Anchorage features some of the world's highest tides, frequent Dall sheep sightings on adjacent cliffs, and bore tides that can reach several feet in height during extreme tidal exchanges.

Jour 2: Tony Knowles Coastal Trail - This 11-mile paved trail follows the coast from downtown through Earthquake Park and Kincaid Park, offering views of Cook Inlet, opportunities to observe moose and other wildlife, and access to Anchorage's extensive trail system.

Jour 2: Departure Preparation - Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport serves as a major hub for travel between North America and Asia, with comprehensive facilities and services for departing visitors.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords
Alaska Adventure - 14 Days Through the World's Most Remote National Parks
Alaska Adventure - 14 Days Through the World's Most Remote National Parks

places.name: Ann Arbor

visits.text: You enter the University of Michigan's central campus and reach The Diag - the great diagonal walkway around which red brick academic buildings constructed between 1840 and 1940 revolve. Keep your eyes on the ground: an 'M' engraved in granite sets a trap - tradition holds that any student who steps on it before their first exam will inevitably fail. Founded in Detroit in 1817 then moved to Ann Arbor in 1837, the University of Michigan enrolls 48,000 students across 3,207 acres and maintains 30 libraries and 16 museums. UMMA (Museum of Art, free admission) and the Kelsey Museum of Archaeology (also free) are the two most accessible to passing visitors. Join a free student-led walking tour from the Michigan Union at the corner of South State Street.

You stand before North America's largest college football stadium. Michigan Stadium opened in 1927 with 72,000 seats, then expanded in successive stages by digging the ground ever deeper: the playing field now sits 30 feet below street level, and official capacity reaches 107,601 spectators. The Michigan Wolverines hold the all-time record for most wins in NCAA Division I football history. Outside of game days, you can enter the stadium freely on weekdays and descend to the lower stands to measure the scale of this concrete canyon. Game days in fall draw crowds exceeding 110,000 people - book months ahead if you want to attend one.

You push open the door of a former firehouse in the Kerrytown neighborhood and find yourself in one of America's most celebrated delicatessens. Ari Weinzweig and Paul Saginaw opened Zingerman's in 1982 with an obsession: the finest ingredients possible, sourced directly from artisan producers. The Reuben - hand-sliced corned beef, house lacto-fermented sauerkraut, Swiss emmental, and Thousand Island dressing on rye baked in their own bakery - regularly appears on lists of America's ten best sandwiches. Barack Obama orders the Reuben every time he passes through Ann Arbor. Lines sometimes extend out the door at lunch - arrive at 11:15 to be served before the rush.

activities.text: You descend into The Arb on paths that plunge from campus toward the Huron River, and the city vanishes within minutes. Nichols Arboretum is a 123-acre natural space co-managed by the University of Michigan and the city since 1907, with glacial slopes, prairies, and mixed forests traversed year-round by students. The university's official rose garden holds over 800 varieties planted from 1930 onward. The deer you'll encounter at dusk on the lower prairie meadows pay little attention to visitors. Avoid midday on weekdays - students gather on the lawns and quiet disappears.

You cross the threshold of UMMA without paying a cent and access one of America's most extensive university museums - over 20,000 works spanning 5,000 years of art history across 94,000 square feet of space. The Asian collection on the second floor, with its 7th-century Tang dynasty ceramics and Japanese woodblock prints from the Edo period, rivals what many better-known paid museums offer. The galleries of African art and contemporary African American artists complete a panorama rare for an entirely free museum. Come Thursday evenings - open until 8pm with musical programming in the central atrium.

days_plan.itin_title: Detroit to Chicago: Great Lakes Road Trip - 8 Days

map_keywords: University of Michigan Central Campus The Diag Ann Arbor
Michigan Stadium Big House South State Street Ann Arbor
Zingerman's Deli Detroit Street Kerrytown Ann Arbor
University of Michigan Museum of Art UMMA South University
Nichols Arboretum Geddes Avenue Ann Arbor

visits.text: The Maryland State House holds a record few people know—and you'll witness the proof when you walk through the door: it's the oldest state capitol in continuous legislative use in the country, since 1779. From November 26, 1783, to August 13, 1784, this Georgian building served as the seat of the Congress of the Confederation—the city was then the federal capital, between Philadelphia and New York. This is where Washington resigned his commission as commander-in-chief on December 23, 1783, and where the Treaty of Paris was ratified. Free admission, photo ID required.

Spend an hour at the United States Naval Academy, on the banks of the Severn River since 1845. The crypt of John Paul Jones beneath the chapel, the views across the Yard's lawns, and the 18th-century cannons are worth the trip. Guided tours depart at fixed times from the Visitor Center—photo ID required at Gate 1.

The William Paca House on Prince George Street nearly became a parking lot in the 1960s—if you walk past without entering, you'll miss one of the finest colonial gardens on the East Coast. Built between 1763 and 1765 by one of Maryland's four signers of the Declaration of Independence, the five-story Georgian mansion was saved at the last minute and restored. Its two-acre English-style garden is exceptional.

activities.text: Rent a sailboat or join one of the guided boat tours from City Dock—Annapolis is called the sailing capital of the world for good reason. Chesapeake Bay opens up just beyond. Several operators offer morning departures, between $50 and $70 per person.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltimore to the Outer Banks - Historic America from Chesapeake Bay to Charlotte

visits.text: Anza-Borrego Desert State Park - California's Largest State Park: Over 640,000 acres of Colorado Desert wilderness spread across mountains, palm oases, and badlands. In wet years, spring wildflower super blooms carpet the desert floor in impossible colors.

Galleta Meadows - Sky Art in the Desert: Over 130 enormous metal sculptures by artist Ricardo Breceda populate this private estate, free and open to the public. Giant serpents, dinosaurs, and fantastical creatures emerge from the desert landscape.

Borrego Springs - An International Dark Sky Community: With minimal light pollution, this small town earned official designation for its pristine night skies, making it one of the best stargazing destinations in Southern California.

activities.text: Slot Canyon Hikes - Walk Between Narrow Walls of Stone: Several easily accessible slot canyons offer otherworldly experiences navigating between towering walls of sedimentary rock carved by flash floods over millennia.

Palm Canyon Trail - Oasis in the Desert: This 3-mile hike descends into a grove of native California fan palms, the state's only indigenous palm species, creating a green haven amid surrounding desert.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

places.name: Apalachicola

visits.text: Inside a one-room brick building on Gorrie Square, a three-quarter-scale replica shows you the machine that made Florida habitable. Dr. John Gorrie arrived in Apalachicola in 1833 when it was the Gulf's third-largest port. By 1841, watching yellow fever claim up to 70% of his patients, he began hanging ice basins from hospital ceilings - and when northern lake ice arrived too slowly by boat, he invented a mechanical system to make it himself. U.S. Patent No. 8080, granted May 6, 1851: the world's first patent for mechanical refrigeration. One of only two Floridians honored with a statue in the U.S. Capitol. Admission $2; open Thursday through Monday.

The Apalachicola Historic District survived because money ran out. The Civil War blockade killed the cotton trade, the railroads bypassed the town, and nobody had the capital to tear the antebellum buildings down. Walk the result: intact Greek Revival commercial blocks, the Orman House (built 1838, original furnishings still inside), Trinity Episcopal Church from the same year. A two-story height limit has governed the city ever since. Hit Commerce Street along the waterfront at dusk - the Victorian storefronts in that light explain why the town still looks like 1870.

Apalachicola Bay once supplied 90% of Florida's oysters and 10% of the national harvest - the 'Oyster Capital of the World' wasn't marketing. Then came 2013: drought, a harvesting rush triggered by the 2010 Deepwater Horizon scare, saltwater predators flooding a low-flow bay. Landings collapsed from 3 million pounds to near nothing. The state closed wild harvest in 2020. The bay is in slow recovery now, and aquaculture farms like Water Is Life Oysters have emerged in the gap. Farmed Apalachicola oysters are back on local menus - ask your waiter which farm they come from.

activities.text: The oysters that made Apalachicola famous are back, in a new form. Aquaculture farms growing Crassostrea virginica in the same bay produce the same briny-sweet flavor - same mineral-rich water, farmed rather than wild-harvested. Boss Oyster and Tamara's Café Floridita on Water Street both source locally. Order the half-shell, get a cold beer, find a waterfront seat. This is the correct sequence. Get there before 7 p.m. on weekends.

The Apalachicola River drains 19,600 square miles from three states before meeting the Gulf here, and the floodplain it builds is one of the most biodiverse in North America. A kayak puts you inside it: cypress swamps, bottomland hardwood forest, osprey, anhinga, river otter working the banks. Local outfitters launch from the city marina on Water Street. Half-day trips run about three hours. Book 24 hours ahead in spring.

days_plan.itin_title: Florida Panhandle: Tallahassee to Pensacola on the Emerald Coast

map_keywords: John Gorrie Museum State Park Apalachicola

visits.text: Pass through the west entrance and drive 3 km to Apgar Village, the historic heart of Glacier National Park. The village dates to 1892, when Milo Apgar settled here to welcome the park's first visitors. You'll find a visitor center, gift shops, and a kayak and rowboat rental station. The Cedar Tree Gift Shop has the distinction of an old cedar growing literally through its roof.

Walk down to the shore of Lake McDonald—the park's largest lake at 16 km long, 1.6 km wide, and 144 meters deep. The water runs so clear you'll see multicolored pebbles on the bottom, a sight that's become famous on social media. Take time to walk the shoreline hunting for the prettiest specimens of red, green, and blue stones.

Continue by car on the Going-to-the-Sun Road to Lake McDonald Lodge, 15 minutes north. This Swiss-chalet-style wooden lodge dates to 1913. Push through the door and admire the great hall with its massive timbers, fireplaces, and hunting trophies—cowboy painter Charles M. Russell spent summers here telling stories to visitors by the fire. You can grab a meal or simply stroll the dock behind the lodge.

A few minutes from the lodge, stop at the Trail of the Cedars. This 1-km loop on wooden boardwalks winds through a forest of western red cedars, some 500 years old and over 2 meters in diameter. This marks the eastern edge of their range—normally these trees grow in Pacific rainforests. The trail is wheelchair-accessible and takes about twenty minutes.

activities.text: If you want to see Lake McDonald from a different angle, board the DeSMet, a two-deck boat built in 1930 that runs narrated tours from the lodge dock. The cruise lasts about 1 hour and gives you views of the snow-capped peaks along the Continental Divide from the middle of the lake.

Late in the day, settle onto Apgar Beach for sunset. The lake faces west and the reflections on the water with mountains in the background are spectacular. Bring your picnic—there are tables and grills by the lake.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Rockies Loop: Glacier & Waterton

places.name: Arco

visits.text: The black basalt at Craters of the Moon starts without warning - you're driving through sagebrush high desert and then the lava is simply there, covering everything. President Coolidge established the national monument on May 2, 1924, to preserve what he called 'unusual and weird volcanic formations.' The Great Rift - a 52-mile crack in the Earth's crust - produced 60 distinct lava flows from 15,000 to just 2,100 years ago, making this one of the youngest lava fields in North America. In August 1969, astronauts Eugene Cernan and Joe Engle trained here with NASA geologist Ted Foss; Cernan later walked on the Moon during Apollo 17, while Engle was originally assigned to that mission but replaced by geologist Harrison Schmitt. Admission is free. Start at the Robert Limbert Visitor Center.

Climb the Inferno Cone - a steep 0.8-mile round trip up an 800-foot cinder cone - for a panoramic view of the lava field, and on clear days, the Teton Range 100 miles east. Then pick up a free cave permit at the visitor center and head to the Cave Trail, where lava tubes descend into the rock. The tubes stay near freezing year-round regardless of outside temperature; bring two light sources and a jacket. They formed when lava surfaces hardened while molten rock kept flowing inside, then drained, leaving hollow tunnels up to 30 feet high.

The Devils Orchard trail (0.5 miles, paved, free) winds through a kipuka - an island of older terrain completely surrounded by newer lava, hosting some of the oldest juniper trees in Idaho. The spatter cones visible along the loop road are among the best examples anywhere: steep chimneys formed where gas-rich lava blobs welded together above the rift into towers that look, from a distance, like broken teeth.

activities.text: After dark, stay in the park. Craters of the Moon is a certified International Dark Sky Park, and at 5,000 feet with zero nearby light pollution, the Milky Way is not a faint smear but a structure - dense at the core, fading at the edges, with dark lanes of dust visible to the naked eye. Rangers run Star Parties with telescopes through summer. The Lava Flow Campground, set directly on the basalt inside the monument, must be reserved at Recreation.gov for July and August.

Nineteen miles east of the park, Arco is the closest town - and 18 miles beyond that, EBR-1 (Experimental Breeder Reactor No. 1) is a National Historic Landmark where, on July 17, 1955, Arco became the first town in the world powered by atomic energy. The original reactor, control room, and instruments are all intact. Self-guided tours are free from Memorial Day through Labor Day. Allow 45 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Idaho: Sawtooth Mountains, Sun Valley & Craters of the Moon

map_keywords: Craters of the Moon National Monument Idaho
Inferno Cone lava Craters of the Moon
lava tube caves Craters Moon Idaho

places.name: Arrow Rock Mo

visits.text: Pull off I-70 at Exit 89 and drive 13 miles north to Arrow Rock — a National Historic Landmark whose entire town is the attraction. Lewis and Clark passed the limestone bluff here on June 9, 1804; Clark noted 'the Prairie of Arrows' in his journal. The bluff had been a flint-quarry site for 12,000 years of Indigenous cultures, and 'pierre à flèche' appeared on French maps as early as 1732. Today you'll find 45 permanent residents, wooden sidewalks, the 1834 J. Huston Tavern — Missouri's first state-preserved historic site — and the home of painter George Caleb Bingham, separately designated a National Historic Landmark in 1968. Thirty minutes is enough for a quick walk; the tavern serves lunch.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: Arrow Rock State Historic Site Missouri
George Caleb Bingham Home Arrow Rock

visits.text: Explore Asheville's celebrated downtown Art District, where world-class galleries and museums showcase an impressive array of artistic expression. This mountain town in western North Carolina has earned a reputation as a cultural haven, perfectly described as having an urban creative energy ringed by majestic mountain scenery.

Wander through Asheville's diverse art galleries, which feature works ranging from traditional Appalachian crafts to cutting-edge contemporary pieces. The city's thriving arts scene attracts creative talents from across the nation, making it one of the premier cultural destinations in the American Southeast.

activities.text: Experience the unique blend of city sophistication and mountain wilderness that defines Asheville's character. The surrounding Blue Ridge Mountains provide a stunning backdrop for this vibrant community, where natural beauty and cultural richness combine to create an unforgettable destination for both art lovers and outdoor enthusiasts.

days_plan.itin_title: Appalachian Road Trip: Great Smoky Mountains & Blue Ridge
North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains

visits.text: Spend an hour in downtown Aspen to feel the atmosphere of this Victorian town set at 7,995 feet in a valley framed by four ski resorts. The Wheeler Opera House, inaugurated in 1889, still hosts concerts, and art galleries line the main street. The atmosphere is surprisingly peaceful for such a renowned town.

Detour to the Maroon Bells—Maroon Peak (14,163 feet) and North Maroon Peak (14,019 feet)—the two most photographed peaks in Colorado. Maroon Lake in the foreground creates the perfect reflection. In summer, a shuttle bus is mandatory between 8 AM and 5 PM from Aspen Highlands parking—take it early for morning light.

Take time to drive up Independence Pass (12,095 feet) on CO-82: the road follows the Roaring Fork River, crosses through spruce forests, and emerges onto open tundra. At the pass, the Sawatch Mountains stretch as far as the eye can see. Closed November to late May—motorhomes over 35 feet prohibited.

activities.text: Rent a bike and cruise down the Rio Grande Trail, a former railroad bed turned bike path that follows the Roaring Fork River for 25 miles to Glenwood Springs. In the right direction, you descend without hardly pedaling—take the RFTA shuttle back up.

After the road, try Meat & Cheese on Hopkins Ave: a beautiful charcuterie and cheese board from Colorado with a selection of natural wines. Aspen also has several MICHELIN-recommended restaurants for those who want to go further.

days_plan.itin_title: Colorado-Wyoming Road Trip: National Parks, Rockies & Wide Open Spaces

places.name: Athens Ga

visits.text: The name came from a single light bulb. Curtis Crowe threw a Halloween party in his College Street apartment in 1978, the only illumination a 40-watt bulb, and the 40 Watt Club was born. By May 1980 it had actual walls and a stage at 100 College Avenue. Five locations later, it's at 285 West Washington Street - a former thrift store that Barrie Buck, ex-wife of R.E.M.'s Peter Buck, has run since 1987. R.E.M., the B-52's, Nirvana, Widespread Panic - all played here before they were household names. VH1 ranked it second most legendary rock club in America. Check the calendar at the door.

Two square blocks of downtown Athens - bounded by Pulaski, Washington, Lumpkin, and Clayton - hold the Athens Music Walk of Fame. The first ten stars went in September 2020; the 40 Watt Club got its own star in 2024, right outside the front door. Find the B-52's plaque and do the math: these people played their first gig at a Valentine's Day house party in 1977 at 653 N. Milledge Avenue, half a mile from where you're standing. The whole walk takes fifteen minutes. It's a short route for an enormous story.

Monroe Bowers Morton built the Morton Theatre around 1910 - one of the first African American-built, owned, and operated vaudeville theaters in the country, and one of the rare few still standing at 195 West Washington Street. Duke Ellington, Armstrong, Cab Calloway, and Bessie Smith came through in the 1920s, 30s, and 40s. In the 1970s B-52's members were literally working in the restaurant downstairs and rehearsing in the back rooms upstairs. Restored in the early 1990s, it's a community arts center today. Look for the show listings inside.

Three miles from campus, the 323-acre State Botanical Garden of Georgia spreads along the Middle Oconee River - Georgia's official botanical garden, and the headquarters of the Garden Club of Georgia, the first such club in America. Five miles of trails cut through Piedmont woodland. The Alice Hand Callaway Conservatory rises three stories above the International Garden. The newest building - a 2021 Porcelain and Decorative Arts Museum - adds eight gallery spaces that blend botanical art with conservation. Free admission; closed Mondays.

America's oldest state university - chartered in 1785 - sprawls across a hilly campus that connects East to North in about a 20-minute walk. The privet-hedged Sanford Stadium seats 93,000 and is sometimes unlocked on non-game days. More interesting than the stadium: the corner of College Avenue and Clayton Street, where Michael Stipe and Peter Buck first met in the late 1970s. Stipe was an art student; Buck was clerking in the record store below. That encounter produced seven studio albums before 1995.

A steeple standing alone in a small green space on the north end of campus - that's what's left of the church built in 1871 where R.E.M. rehearsed before anyone outside Athens knew their name. Services ended in 1899. The building became a Red Cross headquarters, then a county museum, then student apartments, then nothing. R.E.M. played the space before it was demolished. The steeple is free, quiet, and easy to miss - which is precisely why it's worth finding.

The building at 215 North Lumpkin started as the YMCA in 1889, spent decades as a furniture store, and became the Georgia Theatre in 1977. The B-52's played here in 1978 - their condition for booking was pre-selling $1,500 in tickets themselves. The Police came the following year on their first American tour. R.E.M.'s 'Shiny Happy People' video was shot here. A 2009 fire gutted the structure; full restoration finished in 2011. The rooftop bar opens at 5 p.m. and the view over downtown is the best in Athens.

activities.text: Georgia's official state art museum sits on the UGA East Campus and charges nothing to walk in. Founded in 1945, it holds nearly 20,000 works - American painting, Southern decorative arts, self-taught art, a Kress Collection of Renaissance and Baroque pieces, and a sculpture garden dedicated to rotating work by women artists. The building is a 15-minute walk from the 40 Watt. Open Tuesday through Saturday; Thursday until 9 p.m., which is the best slot on a busy day.

Pick up the free music history map at the Athens Welcome Center (280 East Dougherty Street) and trace the same two-mile geography that R.E.M. and the B-52's covered between 1977 and 1982. End at Weaver D's on Sanford Drive - Michael Stipe borrowed owner Dexter Weaver's slogan 'Automatic for the People' in 1992 for one of the most celebrated albums of the decade. Then find the Murmur railroad trestle on the North Oconee River Greenway: built in 1833, it's on the cover of R.E.M.'s 1983 debut. The detour is worth it.

Athens had a live music crawl before the term existed. The 40 Watt, the Caledonia Lounge, the Georgia Theatre, and the Foundry - an 1850 iron foundry turned venue - all book shows most nights of the week. College radio station WUOG 90.5 FM has broadcast local and national acts since October 16, 1972; tune in during the morning drive and it will tell you who's on tonight. The Flagpole, a free weekly, has complete listings at every bar and record store in town.

Oconee Hill Cemetery on the North Oconee River is a Victorian burial ground that served as a late-night haunt for the Athens creative crowd through the 1970s and 1980s. B-52's guitarist Ricky Wilson is buried here. R.E.M.'s song 'Stumble' references those wanderings directly. Walk through at dusk before the evening shows - 20 quiet minutes that put the music scene in a different kind of context.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia Beyond Atlanta: Music, Gardens & the Deep South
Georgia Beyond Atlanta: Music, Gardens & the Deep South

map_keywords: State Botanical Garden of Georgia Athens

visits.text: You stand above the world's largest effigy mound: the Great Serpent Mound of Adams County measures 411 meters in length, between 1.2 and 1.5 meters in height, and 6 to 7.6 meters in width - an earthen serpent that undulates in seven curves and ends with a tail coiled in a spiral, with a wide-open mouth around an oval mound. The site sits on the edge of a 300-million-year-old meteorite crater. The serpent's head is aligned with the sunset at summer solstice, and the tail points to the sunrise at winter solstice. The identity of the builders remains debated - Adena culture (800 BC) or Fort Ancient culture (1000-1650 AD) - but both cultures inhabited and used the site. The observation tower offers the only accessible aerial view. Arrive at opening at 10am to avoid school buses.

You enter the museum opened by the Ohio Historical Society in 1967 and discover that the debate over the mound's builders is far from settled. The exhibits present the soapstone-carved pipes of the Adena culture - some traveled to quarries in Illinois - and pottery from the Fort Ancient culture. Then comes the plot twist: carbon-14 dating done in the 1990s suggests construction around 1070 AD, which coincides exactly with the appearance of the Crab Nebula supernova in 1054, visible to the naked eye for twenty-three days. You leave with more questions than when you entered - which is the sign of a good museum.

You arrive in Athens descending from the Appalachian foothills of Ohio, a university town of 24,000 whose red brick campus is organized around a College Green planted with century-old elms - Ohio University, founded in 1804, is the oldest university in the Old Northwest Territory. You stroll down Court Street, the main artery lined with cafés, independent galleries, and lively restaurants that student life keeps animated even outside class hours. Push open the door of Little Professor Book Center on Court Street to find a title on Ohio's Native American mound history. If you're here in late October, Athens hosts one of the Midwest's largest street Halloweens: 20,000 people in costume flood the sidewalks.

activities.text: You join archaeologist Bill Kennedy of the Ohio History Connection for the 90-minute guided walk around the mound - the only way to get the complete scientific context, and the rare opportunity to hear a specialist debate the builder theories live. You learn how workers transported tons of earth in baskets from nearby borrow areas, how they shaped the serpent's curves without any metal tools, and why the head is precisely aligned with the summer solstice sunset. Bill Kennedy organizes these tours on the 2nd and 4th Fridays of the month from May to October - if you can time your visit to this schedule, don't miss it.

You leave Court Street on foot toward the Hocking River, and in ten minutes you find yourself on a trail lined with weeping willows and giant sycamores following a peaceful waterway framed by wooded hills. The Hocking River takes its name from the Wyandot word 'hockhocking' - the bottle-shaped river - a name given by the Delaware and Shawnee tribes who fished here for centuries before colonization. You walk to the edges of Strouds Run State Park where the river narrows between fern-covered cliffs. In spring, the banks explode with pink redbud flowers and wild magnolias - the ideal time for this walk.

days_plan.itin_title: Interior Ohio Loop - From Columbus to Mohican Gorges

places.name: Atlanta

visits.text: The Sweet Auburn neighborhood holds the first block of the civil rights story. Walk to the Martin Luther King Jr. National Historical Park and start at the Visitor Center - it's free, and a ranger will orient you before you step outside. From there, Ebenezer Baptist Church is just across the promenade: the building where King was baptized and where he and his father co-pastored is still standing, still holding services on Sundays. Stop at the reflecting pool in front of Freedom Hall and read the crypt of King and Coretta Scott King, set flush into the water. Note that the birth home at 501 Auburn Avenue is currently closed for rehabilitation. Arrive by 9 a.m.; birth home tours, when available, fill on a first-come first-served basis with just 10 spots.

Down the hill from Sweet Auburn, the Georgia State Capitol stands on the site of Atlanta's original City Hall - Federal troops camped here in 1864, three years before the building began rising. Completed in 1889, its gold dome is plated with gold from Dahlonega, Georgia. On the fourth floor, the Capitol Museum holds Confederate-era flags, portraits, and a famously beloved two-headed calf. Outside, look for the bronze statue of the Original 33 - the thirty-three Black legislators expelled from the Georgia General Assembly in 1868, a story the building rarely volunteers. Free admission; open Monday through Friday 8 a.m. to 5 p.m.

Before or after the Capitol, walk Royal Street and the Sweet Auburn Curb Market on Auburn Avenue - operating since 1918 and still doing business with produce vendors and a rotating roster of small food stalls. The neighborhood's double history - as the cradle of Atlanta's Black business class and the birthplace of King - is visible in the blend of churches, old storefronts, and park infrastructure. If you're here on a weekday, swing by Fire Station No. 6 on the park grounds: it was among the first desegregated firehouses in the Deep South, staffed by NPS volunteers.

activities.text: The Civil Rights Walk of Fame runs along Auburn Avenue within the King Historic District. Walk it slowly: bronze footprints are embedded in the sidewalk honoring inductees including Rosa Parks and Coretta Scott King. The whole stretch from the Visitor Center to the King Center and back is about half a mile. Plan for late afternoon when the light is lower and foot traffic thins.

Dinner in Atlanta before tomorrow's early departure east on I-85. The Ponce City Market on North Highland Avenue brings together local restaurants in a restored 1920s Sears building - a comfortable place to decompress after a day heavy with history. Alternatively, the small restaurants on Edgewood Avenue are a ten-minute walk from the park.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast
Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey
Georgia Beyond Atlanta: Music, Gardens & the Deep South
The Black Belt: Atlanta to New Orleans Through the Deep South

map_keywords: Martin Luther King Jr National Historical Park Atlanta

places.name: Augusta

visits.text: Built in 1845 to harness the Savannah River, the Augusta Canal is the only fully intact American industrial canal still running for its original purposes - power, water supply, transportation. Congress made it Georgia's first National Heritage Area in 1996. During the Civil War, Confederate Colonel George Washington Rains chose Augusta for the only gunpowder works ever designed, built, and funded by the Confederate States of America - 28 buildings stretched two miles along this same waterway. The Discovery Center is inside Enterprise Mill at 1450 Greene Street. Guided boat tours aboard replica Petersburg cargo boats depart daily. Book ahead.

Two floors, 12,000 years. The Augusta Museum of History, founded in 1937, opens downstairs with the full regional sweep - 1917 steam locomotive, Civil War cannon, the story of the Georgia Railroad (amended in 1835, the most important company in the state). Upstairs: the largest dedicated James Brown collection in the world. Costumes, family photographs, original vinyl, his 'King of Soul' crown from the 1950s, kiosks playing the hits. Brown grew up in Augusta. He never fully left. Paid entry.

Meadow Garden at 1320 Independence Drive is the oldest documented house in Augusta - built probably in the 1750s, expanded in the 1790s by George Walton, who at 26 became one of the youngest men to sign the Declaration of Independence. He signed his letters 'George Walton of Meadow Garden' from 1791 until his death here in 1804. The Daughters of the American Revolution bought the house in 1900 and opened it to the public on November 21, 1901 - making it one of the oldest house museums in the United States. National Historic Landmark since 1981. Paid entry.

activities.text: Seven miles of flat towpath run from downtown Augusta out to the headgates at Savannah Rapids Park - the original route used by mules pulling canal boats upstream. The most dramatic stretch is the first two miles, past the King Mill and the Sibley Mill, a neo-gothic industrial building from 1882 built on the former Confederate Powderworks site. Herons, otters, and the occasional alligator work the banks. Bike and kayak rentals are at the headgates on weekends in spring and fall.

On Broad Street between 8th and James Brown Boulevard, a life-size bronze of Brown mid-performance has a mounted camera that sends your photo straight to your phone. Then walk the 26-block National Register Historic District: on Broadway, a marker stands at the former pharmacy of Dr. John S. Pemberton, a Confederate Army captain who invented Coca-Cola in 1886. The Riverwalk Augusta along the Savannah River, half a mile from Broad Street, is the best place to catch the evening light.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia Beyond Atlanta: Music, Gardens & the Deep South

map_keywords: Augusta Canal Discovery Center Enterprise Mill

visits.text: Augusta is the easternmost state capital in the United States, and the Maine State House that sits at the top of State Street has been here since 1832. Charles Bulfinch - the same architect who designed the US Capitol before Benjamin Henry Latrobe took over - drew the original plans, built in granite quarried from nearby Hallowell. C. Henri Desmond doubled the building's size in 1910, raising the copper dome to 185 feet. Free guided tours are available weekdays year-round through the Maine State Museum; just call ahead.

Down the hill on the east bank of the Kennebec River stands Old Fort Western, the oldest surviving wooden garrison in North America. It went up in 1754, during the French and Indian War, to serve as a supply depot for Fort Halifax further upriver. Benedict Arnold used it as a staging point in 1775 before leading 1,100 men up the Kennebec toward Quebec. The main structure is the original 1754 building; the two blockhouses were reconstructed in the 1920s. Costumed guides bring the place to life with demonstrations of 18th-century life.

Capitol Park, directly between the State House and the Kennebec, is Maine's earliest documented designed landscape - Augusta residents donated the 34 acres in 1827, and the Olmsted Brothers redesigned it in the 1920s. The park runs right down to the river with wide gravel paths between rows of old maples. It's been on the National Register of Historic Places since 1989. A short walk along the riverfront south of the park reaches the Kennebec River Rail Trail.

activities.text: The Maine State Museum on State Street sits directly behind the State House and covers 12,000 years of Maine history in a single building - from Ice Age mastodons found in a Scarborough marsh to the state's industrial heritage in logging, fishing, and granite quarrying. The 'Made in Maine' gallery reconstructs a complete 19th-century mill town interior. Budget at least two hours; the natural history section alone is substantial.

Augusta sits at the head of tide on the Kennebec River - meaning the tidal influence stops right here. Walking or cycling the Kennebec River Rail Trail from the Capitol Park down toward Hallowell gives you 6.5 miles of flat riverside path through industrial-era warehouses and scenic river bends. The trail runs on a former railroad bed and connects Augusta to the historic town of Hallowell, worth a 20-minute wander for its antique shops.

days_plan.itin_title: Inland Maine: Moosehead Lake, Mount Katahdin & the North Woods

places.name: Austin

visits.text: Texas State Capitol: built from 1882 to 1888 in local pink granite, the capitol measures 92 m tall - 4.5 m taller than the one in Washington. At the time of its inauguration on April 21, 1888 (anniversary of the Battle of San Jacinto), it was the seventh-largest building in the world. Workers from Granite Mountain supplied the granite free of charge; in exchange, the contractors received 3 million acres in the Texas Panhandle - which became one of the country's largest ranches. The free tour takes you up to the dome through 400 rooms and 900 windows.

South Congress Avenue (SoCo): Austin's most photographed street concentrates everything that defines the city's personality within a few kilometers - vintage shops, food trucks, independent galleries, and the now-famous 'I Love You So Much' mural on a café facade. If you're here on a Sunday morning, the flea market and terrace brunches perfectly capture the local vibe.

Barton Springs Pool: fed by natural springs at a stable year-round temperature of 20°C, this 280-m pool in the middle of Zilker Park has been the Austinites' gathering spot for a century. In the heat of a Texas summer, it's a godsend. Admission is modest but charged - arrive early on weekends as crowds can make entry difficult by late morning.

Blanton Museum of Art: on the UT Austin campus, this museum houses Texas's largest university art collection. You'll find European paintings from the Renaissance through the 18th century, a leading Latin American art collection, and remarkable prints. The building itself, designed by Kallmann McKinnell & Wood and opened in 2006, is worth the visit - the central nave is impressive.

Mount Bonnell: Austin's highest point at 238 m elevation offers the best panorama of the city over Lady Bird Lake and the hills stretching westward. There are 106 steps to climb from the parking lot - count on 10 minutes. It's a popular Austinite sunset spot: arrive 30 minutes early to claim a good perch.

activities.text: Live music night on 6th Street: Austin has over 250 concert venues and claims the title 'Live Music Capital of the World.' Thursday night is often the best time - many local bands play with no cover charge. Walk into any bar after 9 PM: country, blues, indie rock, jazz - there's bound to be something for you within a few meters.

Bat-watching at Congress Avenue Bridge: since the bridge's 1980 renovation, cracks between the concrete beams have become North America's largest urban bat colony - between 750,000 and 1.5 million Mexican free-tailed bats settle here each spring. Every evening at dusk, they emerge in a column visible over 1 km away. Position yourself on the bridge's east side or on the Bat Observation Center lawn for the best view. The phenomenon runs from March to November.

Kayak or paddleboard on Lady Bird Lake: 16 km of urban lake framed by the 8 km Trail lining both shores - it's Austin's green heart. Outfitters offer kayak, pedal boat and stand-up paddleboard rentals directly on the banks. Early morning, the lake surface is perfectly smooth and the skyline reflections are spectacular.

BBQ circuit in Lockhart: dubbed the 'BBQ Capital of Texas' by the State Legislature, Lockhart sits 45 minutes from Austin. Four legendary institutions - some over 100 years old - are concentrated within a three-block radius. The brisket is smoked over post oak for a minimum of 14 hours, sliced to order with no plate or silverware - just kraft paper. If you've never eaten real Texas BBQ, this is where it happens.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Texas: Hill Country, Big Bend & Gulf Coast Beaches
Texas Triangle: Dallas, Austin, San Antonio & the Hill Country - 8 Days
Texas Triangle: Dallas, Austin, San Antonio & the Hill Country - 8 Days

map_keywords: Blanton Museum of Art UT Austin

visits.text: Exceptional day ahead: you're driving the Going-to-the-Sun Road, one of America's most spectacular highways. This 80-km road connects the west entrance to the east entrance by crossing the park over the Continental Divide. Built by hand between 1921 and 1933—dynamite, picks, and chisels—it's a National Historic Landmark. The road climbs to Logan Pass at 2,025 m elevation, snaking along vertiginous cliffs. Drive slowly (25 mph in the alpine section) and stop at the overlooks.

At the summit, stop at Logan Pass Visitor Center. This is the road's high point and the trailhead for two of the park's most renowned hikes. Take the Hidden Lake Overlook Trail—4.5 km round-trip with 140 m of elevation gain on wooden boardwalks through alpine meadows. At the end, you overlook Hidden Lake and its turquoise waters. You'll likely spot mountain goats and bighorn sheep—they're accustomed to hikers. Warning: parking at Logan Pass is limited to 3 hours in summer, plan to arrive early morning.

Descending the east side, make a stop at Jackson Glacier Overlook. This is the best spot on the road to view a glacier from your car. The interpretive sign shows comparative photos of the glacier over the decades—the retreat is striking. Continue to St. Mary and take the road toward Many Glacier (40 minutes from US-89).

Arriving in the Many Glacier valley, you'll discover a postcard landscape: Swiftcurrent Lake in the foreground, Many Glacier Hotel by the water, and the snow-capped peaks of Mount Grinnell (2,698 m) and Mount Wilbur (2,841 m) as backdrop. Circle the lake late in the day—the low-angle light turns the mountains golden. This is also the park zone where grizzly sightings are most frequent, especially in summer when wild berries ripen.

activities.text: If you still have energy after the drive, take the easy walk around Swiftcurrent Lake (3.8 km). You'll pass Fishercap Lake where moose sightings are common at twilight. Keep your distance—at least 100 meters for bears, 25 meters for other wildlife.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Rockies Loop: Glacier & Waterton

visits.text: Arriving in Baker, you can't miss the world's tallest thermometer: 134 feet of steel structure, visible from Interstate 15. Its height—exactly 134 feet—is a tribute to the temperature record set at Furnace Creek on July 10, 1913: 134°F. It was Willis Herron, a local businessman, who financed it in 1991 to draw travelers to his town. Desert winds toppled it shortly after its unveiling; it was rebuilt with 4,400 cubic feet of concrete in its structure. The Temp 134 shop at its base tells the whole story, including a frying pan left outside permanently to test whether the heat is enough to cook eggs (it takes 158°F to do so).

Kelso Dunes, in Mojave National Preserve, rank among the world's only thirty 'singing' dunes. These dunes sprawl over 45 square miles and rise nearly 650 feet above the desert floor—the round-trip hike is about four miles with 650 feet of elevation gain. The sand, composed of quartz and feldspar, comes from deposits of the ancient Mojave River and accumulated here about 25,000 years ago. When grains slide down slopes in dry wind, they produce a deep hum audible from a distance—an acoustic phenomenon linked to the specific grain size and extreme aridity of the site. Bring water; the heat climbs fast. No trail markers on this terrain: choose your bearing and head straight for the crest.

Kelso Depot, a historic 1924 station built in Mission Revival style by the Union Pacific Railroad, served to supply steam locomotives with water and coal in the heart of the desert. Restored, it now houses the park visitor center with exhibits on railroad history and the geology of the preserve. Quick stop but useful before or after the dunes.

activities.text: Descend into the lava tube of the Cima volcanic field, a few miles from the dunes. A metal ladder provides access to this basaltic tunnel formed between 15,000 and 20,000 years ago when exterior lava cooled faster than the interior, creating a hollow conduit. Bring a headlamp—the cave is dark at the entrance, but natural skylights illuminate the central sections. Pack a light jacket: the temperature remains cool even in summer, around 60°F.

days_plan.itin_title: California Desert Road Trip: Death Valley and the Mojave

places.name: Baker Nv

visits.text: In 1885, rancher Absalom Lehman started guiding neighbors into this cave for a few cents. Today you pay a bit more, but it's still worth every penny. Lehman Caves is Nevada's longest known cave system—3.2 km through marble and limestone—with 'parachute' formations unique in the world. It's 10°C inside year-round: bring a jacket. Two ranger-led tours: 60 or 90 minutes. Reservation required at recreation.gov.

The Wheeler Peak Scenic Drive climbs 19 km from the visitor center up to 3,013 meters. You cross five ecological zones in one hour of driving—from sagebrush desert to alpine meadows. Stop at Mather Overlook: the view plunges across 150 km of empty valleys. Closed to vehicles over 7.3 meters, closed in winter—check conditions at nps.gov.

Stop by the Great Basin Visitor Center in Baker before entering the park. They have current conditions, available tours, and an exhibit on Great Basin ecology. And they keep the 'Forgotten Winchester': an 1882 rifle found leaning against a juniper in the park in 2014—still loaded, still standing.

Stella Lake and Teresa Lake sit perched above 3,200 meters beneath the north face of Wheeler Peak. The trail from the top of the Scenic Drive is 3.7 km round-trip—short but stunning. The water is ice-cold even in August. At dawn, the reflection of Wheeler Peak on Stella Lake is disturbingly sharp. Arrive early to have the lake to yourself.

The Baker Archaeological Site, accessible from a track east of the village, preserves the remains of a prehistoric Fremont village occupied about 1,000 years ago. 1990s excavations revealed a structured community organization—granaries, communal hearths, shared spaces. Interpretive panel on-site. Free access.

Lexington Arch is a 22-meter natural limestone arch in the park's southern sector—extremely rare in the American West where nearly all arches are sandstone. Access from Route 93A is a 16-km gravel road passable in dry weather, followed by a 5.5-km trail with 270 meters of elevation gain. Avoid after rain—the road gets slippery.

activities.text: The Bristlecone/Glacier Trail starts at 3,012 meters from the Wheeler Peak Campground parking lot: 6.9 km round-trip to the base of the rock glacier, passing through a grove of bristlecone pines. Some of these twisted trees are over 4,000 years old—they were already here when the pyramids were being built. Start early: thunderstorms often roll in around 2pm in summer.

Great Basin is one of the darkest parks in the continental United States—designated an International Dark Sky Park. On clear nights, the Milky Way is visible to the naked eye from Wheeler Peak Campground at 2,999 meters. Rangers lead astronomy evenings on Fridays and Saturdays in summer at the Lehman Caves amphitheater. Schedule at nps.gov/grba.

The Baker Creek area is one of the park's least-visited corners. The Baker Lake loop (12 km, 730 meters of elevation gain) climbs through whitebark pine forests to a lake you'll often have entirely to yourself. Mule deer, marmots, and sometimes—if you're lucky and quiet—a black bear in the rocks.

days_plan.itin_title: Salt Lake City to Great Basin Road Trip: Desert, Salt & Stars
Salt Lake City to Great Basin Road Trip: Desert, Salt & Stars
