visits.text: Start your journey in Fells Point rather than the Inner Harbor—this is the real Baltimore. Founded in 1763, this waterfront neighborhood boasts over 300 buildings on the National Register, making it Maryland's first designated historic district. Frederick Douglass worked here as a caulker in the shipyards until his escape in 1838. Walk the Belgian-block cobblestones of Thames Street in the morning, before the sidewalk cafes fill up.

Fort McHenry deserves half a day, not just a photo from the parking lot. Picture the night of September 13-14, 1814: 25 hours of relentless British bombardment, and the fort held. It was from his own American sloop under flag of truce—detained by the British fleet—that Francis Scott Key scribbled the verses that became the national anthem in 1931. Arrive early in the morning to avoid school groups.

If you only have time for one thing in Baltimore, choose the National Aquarium on Pier 3. Opened in 1981, it draws 1.5 million visitors annually—20,000 animals representing 800 species, including a 500-pound green sea turtle named Calypso. Admission is timed: book online. On Fridays after 5 PM, tickets are half price.

At the Reginald F. Lewis Museum on Pratt Street, spend 60 to 90 minutes in the "Things Hold, Lines Connect" gallery—400 years of African American history in Maryland, with an in-depth section on Frederick Douglass and Harriet Tubman. Free admission on the first Sunday of each month.

The American Visionary Art Museum at 800 Key Highway isn't the art museum you expect. Self-taught artists with no formal training—their works fill seven galleries, plus a 55-foot outdoor windmill covered in mirrors. Plan 90 minutes and leave your usual reference points at the coat check.

activities.text: Walk the Inner Harbor waterfront for a mile to the Historic Ships in Baltimore: the USS Constellation, an 1797 frigate, and the USS Torsk, a World War II submarine, are the two most immersive. Combination ticket around $16.

Broadway Market has been operating since 1786—Baltimore's oldest continuously active public market. Walk down Thames Street to the Horse You Came In On Saloon—this is where Edgar Allan Poe had his last drink before his mysterious death in October 1849. Try the crab cakes and a local beer.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltimore to the Outer Banks - Historic America from Chesapeake Bay to Charlotte

visits.text: Discover Bandelier National Monument, a 33,000-acre preserve protecting the ancestral homes, petroglyphs, and ceremonial sites of the Pueblo people dating back 11,000 years. The dramatic cliff dwellings carved into soft volcanic tuff allow visitors to climb ladders and explore actual ancient living spaces used by communities centuries ago. Hiking trails wind through canyon landscapes where archaeological treasures reveal the sophisticated civilization that flourished here.

Walk among the impressive ruins where the Ancestral Pueblo people built multi-story structures using natural cave formations and hand-carved chambers in the canyon walls. Interpretive programs explain how these communities adapted to the challenging environment, developing agriculture, crafts, and social structures that influenced later Pueblo cultures throughout the region.

activities.text: Watch for the distinctive Albert's Squirrels with their characteristic ear tufts while hiking the monument's scenic trails through ponderosa pine forests and canyon bottoms. The diverse ecosystems support abundant wildlife and provide insights into how ancient peoples utilized natural resources for survival and cultural expression.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

places.name: Bangor

visits.text: The house at 47 West Broadway is unmistakable: blood-red Victorian mansion, wrought-iron fence decorated with bats, spiders, and a three-headed dragon, designed and built by local metalworker Terry Steel in 1982. Stephen King bought the place in 1980 for $135,000 and lived here for over 40 years, writing many of his most famous novels inside. The neighborhood - Bangor's Whitney Park Historic District - inspired the fictional town of Derry in IT. King no longer lives here full-time; the house is now his foundation's archive and a writers' retreat. Visitors are welcome on the sidewalk for photos. Quiet and respectful, please: it's a residential street.

Three blocks from the waterfront on Main Street, the Paul Bunyan statue has been staring down at traffic since February 12, 1959 - Paul's 125th birthday, according to Bangor's official birth certificate on file at City Hall. The 31-foot fiberglass figure weighs 3,700 pounds and is engineered to withstand 110 mph winds. Bangor was the self-proclaimed 'Lumber Capital of the World' in the late 1800s, when sailing ships lined the Penobscot River loaded with Maine timber. Stephen King worked Bunyan into the plot of IT in 1986, animating him with an evil spirit. A time capsule inside the pedestal is scheduled to open on Bangor's 250th birthday: February 12, 2084.

The Maine Discovery Museum on Harlow Street is the largest children's museum north of Boston, with three floors of interactive exhibits covering science, nature, and Maine arts. Even if you're not traveling with kids, the nature floor is legitimately impressive - it traces the food web of a Maine forest ecosystem from soil microbes to moose. It's also one of the few rainy-day options in Bangor.

The Penobscot River Walkway runs along the downtown waterfront, connecting the Paul Bunyan statue to the riverbank park. In the late 1800s this stretch of river was choked with log drives - thousands of trees floating from Moosehead Lake to the paper mills downstream, guided by 'river pigs' with peaveys. The waterfront has been cleaned up into a pleasant park; interpretive panels explain the industrial history. Early mornings bring kayakers and the occasional osprey.

activities.text: Bangor has earned a reputation as a surprisingly serious craft beer town. Geaghan Brothers Brewing on Pickering Square has been pouring since 2013 and collaborates with Maine farmers on ingredient sourcing - their Spinnaker Blonde is a local staple. 2 Feet Brewing nearby leans toward hop-forward IPAs. Both are within easy walking distance of the waterfront. Pair a pint with a stop at Gerald Winters & Son Bookstore on Harlow Street, the best source in Maine for signed Stephen King first editions and King memorabilia.

For a proper immersion in King's Bangor, the SK Tours guided driving and walking tour covers the Jackson Street storm drain (the inspiration for IT's iconic opening), the Thomas Hill Standpipe (which appears in several novels), Mount Hope Cemetery (used as a filming location for Pet Sematary), and a dozen other real-life locations mapped to King's fictional Derry. Tours run seasonally and last about two hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Inland Maine: Moosehead Lake, Mount Katahdin & the North Woods

map_keywords: Stephen King House 47 West Broadway Bangor

places.name: Bar Harbor

visits.text: At low tide, a natural sandbar connects downtown Bar Harbor to Bar Island—you've got about an hour before the sea reclaims it, so don't dawdle. The Abbe Museum, in the village, is devoted to Wabanaki culture and worth a quick visit. Bar Harbor was the late 19th-century summer retreat of the Rockefeller and Vanderbilt families—their wooden cottages survive on the heights.

Climb Cadillac Mountain early in the morning: at 1,530 feet, it's the highest point on the entire East Coast of the United States, and from October 7 to March 6, it's the first place in the country where the sun rises. The 360° view embraces Frenchman Bay, the Porcupine Islands, and on clear days, the Schoodic Peninsula. The summit road winds for 3.5 miles. Hikers will prefer the Cadillac North Ridge Trail (4.4 miles round-trip).

Thunder Hole is a crevice in the pink granite where waves crash with a dull roar—arrive at mid-rising tide for maximum effect. Just north, Sand Beach is one of Acadia's rare sandy beaches, framed by pink cliffs. The water hovers between 50 and 59°F even in August: bring a jacket.

Jordan Pond House is the park's only restaurant—settle on the terrace facing the South and North Bubbles, those glacier-rounded hills framing the pond. The tea house has served popovers since the late 19th century: order them hot with butter and jam, an unchanged tradition from the days of wealthy summer visitors.

Otter Cliff, at the southeastern tip of Mount Desert Island, is a 110-foot granite wall dropping straight into the Atlantic. It's one of the most popular sea cliff climbing sites on the East Coast—if you're not climbing, the view from the trail's edge toward the Cranberry Isles is striking.

If you didn't catch the sunrise from Cadillac Mountain yesterday, this is your last chance before 5 hours of driving toward Vermont. Otherwise, grab a typical Maine breakfast at Café This Way before leaving Bar Harbor—eggs, lobster hash, and strong coffee.

activities.text: Dine on the waterfront facing the Porcupine Islands: the village restaurants serve lobsters and mussels caught that very morning. Take the opportunity to reserve your Cadillac Mountain access for tomorrow—the summit road requires a reservation on recreation.gov from mid-May through late October, and sunrise slots sell out in minutes.

Rent a bike in Bar Harbor and explore Acadia's carriage roads: a network of 45 miles of gravel paths financed by John D. Rockefeller Jr. in the 1910s-1940s, with 16 hand-cut stone bridges. The Eagle Lake Loop (~5.5 miles) is ideal for a half-day in the spruce woods. Count on $30-40 per day for rental.

Go sea kayaking from Bar Harbor: local guides run 3-hour excursions around the Porcupine Islands with passage past seal colonies. The Atlantic is calm on the Frenchman Bay side most summer mornings—several outfitters are clustered on the Village Green. Reservation recommended in July-August.

days_plan.itin_title: Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England
Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England
Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England

map_keywords: Bar Harbor Village Bar Harbor Maine
Acadia National Park Visitor Center Bar Harbor

visits.text: Devil's Lake State Park - Wisconsin's most-visited park, and for good reason: a glacial lake trapped between two 500-foot quartzite bluffs. These rocks, 1.6 billion years old, are among the continent's oldest. The contrast between turquoise water and pink cliffs is striking.

East Bluff Trails - Climb the East Bluff Trail (1.5 miles) for plunging views over the lake and Baraboo Hills. The trail passes through 'talus fields' - piles of quartzite blocks stacked by erosion - a rare habitat for certain moss and lichen species.

Circus World Museum - Baraboo was headquarters of the Ringling Brothers (America's greatest circus) from 1884 to 1918. The museum, housed in the original buildings, preserves decorated circus wagons, posters, and memorabilia. In summer, live circus performances are given under the big top.

activities.text: Swimming and Picnic at Devil's Lake - Both beaches (north and south) are well-maintained. The water is cool but pleasant in summer. Pack a picnic and settle in facing the bluffs - it's the perfect ending to this road trip.
Quartzite Climbing (optional) - Devil's Lake bluffs are a renowned Midwest climbing spot. Over 1,600 routes on quartzite, all levels. Outfitters offer introductory lessons.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

places.name: Barstow

visits.text: Needles, CA - You enter California through the Mojave Desert. Needles, named after the rock spires overlooking the town, is the last stop before 150 miles of desert. Fill up on gas and water here - stations are scarce until Barstow.

Roy's Motel & Café (Amboy) - In the middle of nowhere, literally. This 1930s motel in the heart of the Mojave is being renovated for Route 66's centennial in 2026. The Googie-style neon sign is one of the most photographed on the route. It often exceeds 110°F here in summer - stay in your car if you're not prepared.

Elmer's Bottle Tree Ranch (Oro Grande) - Stop to see these 200 'trees' made of metal pipes topped with colored glass bottles that tinkle in the desert wind. You've never seen anything like it - this folk art installation, created by one man on his property, is mesmerizing.

Calico Ghost Town - If you're traveling with family, stop at this 1880s former silver mining town. Restored in the 1950s, it's a historic park with period buildings, a visitable mine, and a small train. Allow 1 hour for the visit.

activities.text: Crossing the Mojave - The most desolate and spectacular part of the Route. Lunar landscapes, railroad tracks stretching to the horizon, purple mountains. Plan on having music - radio doesn't pick up much between Needles and Barstow.

days_plan.itin_title: Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica

map_keywords: Elmer Bottle Tree Ranch Oro Grande California

places.name: Baton Rouge

visits.text: At 450 feet tall with 34 floors, the Louisiana State Capitol is the tallest state capitol in the United States - and it has no dome. Huey Long insisted on a skyscraper, drawing inspiration from Nebraska's 1920s tower. The Art Deco building was completed in 1932, with floors cut from Italian volcanic stone and 26 types of marble throughout. Long is buried in the garden outside, marked by a 12-foot bronze by Charles Keck. Inside, ask to see the assassination hallway: a display case marks the ground-floor corridor where Long was shot on September 8, 1935, by Dr. Carl Weiss, whose father-in-law Long had just gerrymandered out of a judgeship. Bullet holes in a granite column are still visible. Take the elevator to the 27th-floor observation deck for views of Baton Rouge and the Mississippi.

The Old State Capitol, half a mile toward the river on North Boulevard, is a Gothic Revival castle built in 1847 and rebuilt in 1882 after an accidental fire. The second version added the famous spiral staircase and a stained glass dome. Mark Twain called it a 'monstrosity.' It now houses the Museum of Political History. The contrast with Long's skyscraper a few blocks away - one a Victorian fantasy, the other a Depression-era tower of ambition - tells you something about Louisiana's political imagination.

The USS Kidd, a World War II Fletcher-class destroyer, is moored permanently on the Mississippi riverfront. The ship fought at Leyte Gulf and survived a kamikaze strike in April 1945 that killed 38 men. You can walk nearly the entire vessel - engine room, combat information center, officers' quarters - and the adjacent Veterans Museum holds aircraft and personal accounts from multiple conflicts. The Louisiana State Capitol up the hill, the Old State Capitol behind you, and the destroyer on the water: three different scales of Louisiana history within a fifteen-minute walk.

activities.text: The Capitol Park Museum on River Road covers Louisiana history from pre-Columbian cultures through Hurricane Katrina. The 'Experiencing Louisiana' gallery has a full-scale oil platform section, a French Quarter streetscape, and one of the stronger Mardi Gras artifact collections in the state. Free admission.

Dinner in Baton Rouge before the final leg to New Orleans tomorrow. The stretch of Government Street between downtown and Mid-City has a concentration of local restaurants that aren't aimed at tourists. Book ahead - Baton Rouge is a university city and good tables fill quickly on weekday evenings.

days_plan.itin_title: Louisiana Cultural Journey: New Orleans Jazz to Cajun Bayou Country
Louisiana Road Trip: New Orleans and Cajun Country
Deep South Road Trip: New Orleans and the Natchez Trace
Deep Louisiana Road Trip: Jazz and Bayous
The Black Belt: Atlanta to New Orleans Through the Deep South
Deep Louisiana: Jazz, Bayous & Plantations - 9-Day Loop from New Orleans
The Deep South via Mississippi: Shreveport to New Orleans - 10 Days

map_keywords: USS Kidd Veterans Museum Baton Rouge

places.name: Bayfield

visits.text: Apostle Islands National Lakeshore Visitor Center - Start here in Bayfield to understand the archipelago: 22 islands, 21 within the national park, carved by Lake Superior over billions of years. Rangers give briefings on sea conditions and visiting options. Maps and camping permits available.

Meyers Beach Sea Caves (trail view) - If weather doesn't permit kayaking, take the coastal trail above the caves. Budget 1.5 miles to reach the first cavities, then another mile for the most spectacular ones. From atop the cliff, you'll see the arches, passages, and the famous 'land bridge' spanning the Fissure. The 500-million-year-old Cambrian sandstone takes on almost surreal shapes.

Bayfield - This small Lake Superior harbor town (population under 500) was built on the lumber industry then reinvented itself as a tourist destination. Stroll the sloping streets lined with colorful Victorian houses. The marina is the launching point for all island excursions.

Bayfield Orchards - The peninsula is known for its apples, cherries, and berries. Several farms open their doors to visitors for pick-your-own (in season) or tastings of cider and preserves. It's particularly beautiful in May (apple blossoms) and October (harvest).
Lake Superior Views - Head up to the heights of Bayfield to see the Apostle Islands archipelago spread before you. On clear days, you can make out lighthouses on the nearest islands.

activities.text: Kayaking the Sea Caves - Bayfield's grand adventure. Outfitters offer guided trips of 2.5-3 hours from Meyers Beach. You paddle along the cliff face then enter the sandstone caverns - some large enough to glide through in your kayak. In summer, amber light from the setting sun illuminates the caves' interiors. You must be able to swim and sea conditions must be calm. Book ahead in July-August.

Apostle Islands Cruise - If you're not a kayaker, the 2.5-3 hour cruise tours the islands, passes the lighthouses of Raspberry Island and Devils Island, and skirts the sea caves. Departs from Bayfield harbor.

Big Top Chautauqua - If you're here in summer (June-September), don't miss this performance venue under a blue big-top tent on the Bayfield heights. Concerts, musical shows about Lake Superior history, comedies. Laid-back atmosphere, picnics welcome.

Madeline Island Ferry Crossing - The largest Apostle Island (the only inhabited one) is 20 minutes by ferry. Historic village of La Pointe, Native American museum, Big Bay beach, and 10 miles of roads to explore by bike.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders
Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

map_keywords: Apostle Islands National Lakeshore visitor center

places.name: Beatty

visits.text: Before leaving Las Vegas, detour to Red Rock Canyon, 15 miles west. The 13-mile scenic loop (one-way) runs along cliffs of Aztec Sandstone—a Jurassic-age sandstone 180 million years old, formed by the lithification of massive sand dunes. The blood-red and ochre hues come from iron oxides. The Calico Hills in particular are an explosion of striated color that geologists attribute to the same formation. More than 1,200 climbing routes have been established on these walls, which can rise to 3,000 feet—this is one of the premier climbing destinations in the United States. Look for petroglyphs near Petroglyph Wall (1,000-foot trail from Willow Spring): they were carved at least 800 years ago by ancestral Southern Paiute peoples. Reserve your entry in advance (pass required in season, timed entry).

Two and a half miles west of Beatty on SR-374, take the marked BLM road that leads to the ruins of Rhyolite. This mining town erupted from nowhere in 1905 after gold was discovered in the Bullfrog Hills: 5,000 residents in six months, three banks, a stock exchange, an opera house, a hospital, concrete sidewalks, and electricity. The John S. Cook & Co. bank, completed in 1908, cost more than $90,000 (approximately $3.2 million today) with its Italian marble stairs and stained glass imported from Europe. By 1916, the lights went out for good. What remains is the bank facade, the train depot, and the famous Bottle House—built in 1906 by Tom Kelly with 50,000 bottles salvaged from saloons, for lack of wood in the desert. Just below, the Goldwell Open Air Museum presents a dozen outdoor sculptures installed starting in 1984, including the haunting Last Supper by Belgian artist Albert Szukalski. Free admission, open dawn to dusk.

activities.text: On US-95 between Las Vegas and Beatty, keep an eye on the roadside: wild burros regularly roam the Amargosa Valley, descendants of pack animals abandoned by miners. Don't approach them and don't feed them—they remain wild animals. Allow about two and a half hours of driving from Red Rock Canyon to Beatty.

days_plan.itin_title: California Desert Road Trip: Death Valley and the Mojave

map_keywords: Red Rock Canyon National Conservation Area Nevada
Goldwell Open Air Museum Beatty Nevada

places.name: Beaufort

visits.text: Wander through Beaufort's antebellum historic district on Port Royal Island, where centuries-old oak trees draped in Spanish moss create an enchanting canopy over graceful streets. The John Mark Verdier House stands as a testament to Federal-period architecture, while block after block of lovingly preserved mansions with wraparound porches define this fourth-oldest town in South Carolina.

Experience the vibrant Gullah Geechee culture through guided tours that reveal the unique heritage of the Lowcountry's African American communities. The waterfront park along the Beaufort River provides serene views of boats gliding across calm waters, while local art galleries showcase a surprisingly dynamic creative scene.

activities.text: Drive the Avenue of Oaks on nearby St. Helena Island, a breathtaking canopied road that epitomizes Lowcountry romance. The ancient live oaks form a natural tunnel of greenery and Spanish moss that transports visitors to another era. This scenic route captures the timeless beauty of the Sea Islands.

Venture to Hunting Island State Park, where wide stretches of untouched beach meet maritime forests and salt marshes. Climb the historic lighthouse for panoramic coastal views, walk the Marsh Boardwalk at sunset, and spot dolphins swimming in the waves. The driftwood-strewn shores and wild palmetto groves create landscapes straight from a nature documentary.

days_plan.itin_title: North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains
South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

map_keywords: Avenue of Oaks St Helena Island

visits.text: Mount Hood — Drive the scenic Timberline Trail around Oregon's highest peak at 3,429 meters. This dormant stratovolcano features 12 glaciers and Timberline Lodge, the historic 1937 WPA-built ski lodge appearing in The Shining.

Smith Rock State Park — Hike among 200-meter sheer cliffs of welded volcanic tuff rising above the Crooked River. The park pioneered American sport climbing in the 1980s and offers dramatic high desert scenery with over 1,800 climbing routes.

High Desert Museum — Explore regional natural and cultural history through indoor galleries and outdoor living exhibits. The museum features native wildlife, Old West artifacts, and interpretations of indigenous cultures across 135 acres.

Old Mill District — Browse this shopping and dining district built around preserved 1909 lumber mill smokestacks. The Deschutes River-front development represents Bend's transformation from lumber town to outdoor recreation hub.

activities.text: Deschutes Brewery visit — Tour one of Oregon's oldest craft breweries founded in 1988, instrumental in establishing Central Oregon's beer culture. The original pub serves flagship Black Butte Porter and seasonal specialties.
Deschutes River Trail cycling — Pedal along paved paths following the river through pine forests and lava rock formations. The 12-mile trail system connects parks, breweries, and downtown Bend.

Sunset at Pilot Butte — Climb this 150-meter cinder cone for 360-degree views across the high desert to Cascade peaks. The extinct volcano offers panoramic vistas of Mount Bachelor, Three Sisters, and surrounding volcanic landscape.

days_plan.itin_title: Oregon Natural Wonders: Crater Lake to Pacific Coast Road Trip

places.name: Bentonville

visits.text: Crystal Bridges opened on November 11, 2011, funded by Alice Walton, daughter of Walmart founder Sam Walton. She chose Bentonville - the town where her father opened his first store - to build a world-class museum with free admission. Moshe Safdie designed eight pavilions of wood, concrete, and copper nestled around two ponds fed by a natural spring called Crystal Spring. The collection spans five centuries of American art - from 17th-century colonial portraits through Andy Warhol and Norman Rockwell - plus a Frank Lloyd Wright Usonian house relocated from New Jersey in 2013. Allow at least 2.5 hours, and don't miss the outdoor trails that connect the museum to downtown.

The Momentary is Crystal Bridges' satellite space dedicated to living art and performance. Inaugurated in 2020 in a converted Kraft cheese factory, the space mixes contemporary art exhibitions, concerts, artist residencies, and screenings. If you don't know what to expect, go in anyway - the programming is often more surprising than the industrial address suggests. Admission is free.

The Walmart Museum occupies the very first store opened by Sam Walton in 1950 on Bentonville Square, under the name Walton's Five and Dime. Archives, photos, and objects from the original shop trace the birth of what became the world's largest company by revenue. Allow 45 minutes and you'll leave with a clear sense of the distance between the 1950 shop and today's giant.

Bentonville Square is the central plaza with its early 20th-century brick facades, including Sam Walton's original Five and Dime. Walk the square after the museum: cafes, restaurants, and independent shops have moved in, and the atmosphere is far from what you'd imagine in Walmart's hometown.

activities.text: Crystal Bridges trails: over 3 miles of marked trails through the forest surrounding the museum, dotted with outdoor sculptures including a James Turrell skyspace. The Art Trail follows the ponds and passes the pavilions from the water side. Free, accessible year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: Arkansas Itinerary: Hot Springs, Ozarks and Natural Parks
Arkansas: Hot Springs, Ozarks & Crystal Bridges — 7-Day Loop from Little Rock
Ozarks & Branson: From Missouri Hills to Arkansas Art Galleries

map_keywords: Crystal Bridges Museum of American Art Bentonville

places.name: Berlin Holmes County

visits.text: You drive along the Amish Country Byway, a 264-kilometer scenic route designated Ohio Scenic Byway in 1998 that crosses the rolling hills of Holmes County to the rhythm of black buggies. Holmes County has the highest concentration of Amish population in the world: in 2023, estimates put the Amish population in the community centered on Holmes County at 39,525, half the county's total population. The first Amish settled here in 1809, coming from Somerset County in Pennsylvania, attracted by hills that reminded them of their Swiss ancestors' homeland. Driving these roads, you'll pass buggies (the most conservative, the Swartzentruber Amish, without orange warning triangles), women in pinafore dresses shopping, children walking to school.

You enter a circular room and find yourself enveloped by a 265-square-meter mural painted by hand by artist Heinz Gaugel between 1992 and 1998 - a panoramic chronicle of 500 years of Anabaptist history, from the first persecutions in 16th-century Switzerland to Holmes County farms today. The title Behalt means 'retain' or 'remember' in Pennsylvania German dialect. The guide stops you before each scene: the drowning of Anabaptist martyrs in Swiss rivers in 1527, the embarkation to America, the settlement in Ohio's hills in 1809. You leave with an understanding of why these people still live as they did in the 18th century - and why it's not ignorance, it's a theological choice.

You arrive in Millersburg and stop dead before the Victorian courthouse of 1885 in red sandstone - one of rural Ohio's most beautiful public buildings. The facades of 19th-century shops line the central square in remarkable preservation. Enter one of the general stores: you'll see men in black hats and green vests buying their farm supplies next to a tourist quilt shop, without anyone finding it incongruous. Before leaving, taste a slice of Holmes County Swiss cheese - the county is the leading producer of Swiss cheese in the United States, made according to recipes brought from the Bern region by 19th-century immigrants. Every Saturday, a farmers market sets up on the square.

activities.text: You climb into a black-topped buggy pulled by a Quarter Horse and take the county's gravel roads at a trot, at the speed of local residents since 1809. The Amish driver explains in English the rules of his community - the Ordnung - and why some groups (the Swartzentruber) don't wear the orange road safety triangle, considered too ostentatious. From the buggy, the landscape changes nature: you see farms from the inside, giant vegetable gardens, clotheslines stretched between buildings, and children watching you pass.

You drive along the gravel roads of Holmes County and spot a painted wooden sign in front of a farm - 'Open', and nothing else. You stop. Perhaps a woodworking shop where a man in an apron assembles a solid oak tray without nails or chemical glue, perhaps a bakery where still-warm shoofly pies cool on the counter, perhaps a cheese dairy that just released a wheel. Direct purchases are the rule here: no credit cards, cash only - bring cash. This kind of improvised encounter, at a farm without a sign or Instagram page, is what you'll tell people about when you return.

days_plan.itin_title: Interior Ohio Loop - From Columbus to Mohican Gorges

map_keywords: Amish & Mennonite Heritage Center Walnut Creek Ohio

places.name: Bethlehem Pa

visits.text: You arrive at SteelStacks and the five blast furnaces stop you in your tracks: two columns of rusted steel rising 230 feet, a row of industrial structures stretching nearly a quarter-mile along the Lehigh River - and at their feet, an outdoor amphitheater, lawns, and art galleries. Bethlehem Steel, founded in 1857 on this 1,800-acre site, was America's second-largest steel producer for nearly a century. Steel from these furnaces built the Golden Gate Bridge, the Chrysler Building, the Empire State Building, Madison Square Garden, and World War II warships. Blast Furnace A, the oldest, dates from 1915; each produced 2,600 to 3,000 tons of pig iron daily. They shut down for the last time on November 18, 1995, leaving 30,000 jobs vacant and a city in mourning. Rather than demolishing the structures, the city converted them into a cultural campus that opened in 2011 - a decision inspired by Ruhr Valley industrial conversions in Germany. Campus access is free; paid events are posted on steelstacks.org.

You climb onto the Hoover-Mason Trestle, the elevated half-mile walkway built on the former railroad that supplied the blast furnaces with iron ore and coke - and you walk at the level of the loads that fed the furnaces, just feet from the mouths of the vats. The plunging view of the industrial structures and Lehigh River is unmatched in the region. Panels explain each step of the steelmaking process, from Minnesota ore arrival to pig iron pour. Paid guided tours are organized in season by Historic Bethlehem Museums - the guided version lasts 90 minutes and accesses areas closed to the general public. Come in the morning for the low light on the rusted steel.

You enter the only national museum dedicated to American industrial history, affiliated with the Smithsonian Institution and installed in 2016 in one of Bethlehem Steel's original buildings. Permanent exhibitions trace successive American industrial revolutions with working 19th-century machines. The Bethlehem Steel section presents rare artifacts: original bridge structure plans, 1940s worker photographs. A documentary exhibition recounts Operation Pastorius of 1942: Nazi saboteurs landed by submarine had the primary mission of destroying Altoona's Horseshoe Curve and Bethlehem Steel plants - arrested in New York by the FBI before reaching their target. Open Wednesday-Sunday 10am-5pm, admission $10 adult.

activities.text: You travel back two centuries crossing the Moravian Historic District, Bethlehem's founding core traced in 1741 by German Moravian immigrants fleeing religious persecution in Saxony. These Protestant dissenters built an exemplary self-sufficient community: collective housing, communal farm, tannery, mill - all original brick buildings still stand and are listed on the National Register. The 1741 Gemein Haus now houses the Moravian Museum with its period musical instruments and furniture. The 1758 Sun Inn, a former tavern that hosted George Washington, John Adams, and Benjamin Franklin during their travels between Philadelphia and the northern front, is restored and open for tours.

You settle on the lawn at Levitt Pavilion, a stage designed so the illuminated blast furnaces form the natural backdrop for every concert - an industrial version of Red Rocks, as performing artists describe it. ArtsQuest programs more than 1,000 concerts and eight festivals per year, including the annual MusikFest in August: ten days of concerts with more than 500 bands on eleven simultaneous stages, one of the largest free music festivals in the United States since 1984. The evening atmosphere, when spotlights illuminate the rusted steel of the five blast furnaces while a band plays at the foot of the structures, is one of Pennsylvania's most singular experiences.

days_plan.itin_title: Pennsylvania Loop: From Amish Country to the Appalachians - 11 Days

map_keywords: SteelStacks Campus Bethlehem Pennsylvania blast furnaces
National Museum of Industrial History Bethlehem PA

places.name: Bettles

visits.text: Bettles has about a dozen year-round residents and sits 35 miles north of the Arctic Circle. The NPS ranger station, steps from the airstrip, is your gateway into the 8 million acres of Gates of the Arctic—the least visited and wildest national park in the United States. Rangers provide free bear-resistant containers and topo maps—consultation is mandatory before any backcountry trip. Bettles Lodge, a National Historic Site, has fed and sheltered explorers, geologists, and photographers since the 1940s.

The Koyukuk River runs yards from Bettles Lodge—it was this river that conservationist Robert Marshall ascended in 1929 to discover the two peaks flanking the northern passage: Frigid Crags and Boreal Mountain. He named them the "Gates of the Arctic." The river can be floated from Bettles by canoe or raft for 100 miles to the village of Huslia—local outfitters provide gear.

The North Fork Koyukuk is the park's signature river—it originates at the Gates proper and descends 100 miles to Bettles in Class I-II water. From the air or from banks accessible by bush plane, the river's meanders between the Brooks Range walls give one of the most striking images of Arctic Alaska. If you've booked a floatplane drop-off, this river is where the real Gates of the Arctic begins.

The ridges and open valleys around Bettles, still technically outside park boundaries, host herds of caribou from the spring migration through autumn. The Central Arctic caribou herd—tens of thousands of individuals—regularly crosses the corridor. In August-September, bulls carry full antlers. Observe from high ground with binoculars to avoid disturbing their movements.

activities.text: Flightseeing from Bettles over the Brooks Range—in a Cessna or Super Cub, local pilots know every valley and peak of this range that forms the northern terminus of the Rockies. The Gates of the Arctic themselves (Frigid Crags and Boreal Mountain) are visible from the air. Budget 1.5 to 2 hours of flight for a loop over the main peaks. The evening light in summer is golden and endless.

Off-trail hiking north of Bettles into the tundra and birch-spruce forests bordering the park. There are no marked trails—you walk on moss and lichen, follow valleys. Grizzlies, caribou, moose, and wolves are all present in this transition corridor between boreal forest and Arctic tundra. Go with compass, topo map, bear spray, and satellite communication.

Float trip by raft or canoe on the North Fork Koyukuk—from a few hours to a full day depending on your skill level. The river is calm with a few easy rapids, and the parade of landscapes between the wooded flanks of the Brooks Range is constant. Bettles operators run day floats with bush plane pickup from downriver.

Fishing at Iniakuk Lake or on the Alatna River from Bettles—two spots accessible by bush plane for a day trip, with pilots dropping you off and returning at an agreed time. Rainbow trout, arctic grayling, and northern pike are the main targets. This region has one of the lowest angler densities on Earth—fish have the naive behavior of having never seen an artificial fly.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Adventure - 14 Days Through the World's Most Remote National Parks
Alaska Adventure - 14 Days Through the World's Most Remote National Parks

Gates of the Arctic National Park Brooks Range

visits.text: Experience Big Bend National Park, an expansive wilderness covering over 800,000 acres where the Rio Grande carves dramatic canyons through ancient mountains. The park's vast, open spaces encompass stunning desert landscapes, diverse ecosystems ranging from Chihuahuan Desert to forested highlands, and some of the darkest skies in North America for exceptional stargazing. This remote sanctuary offers solitude and natural beauty on a scale difficult to find elsewhere in the continental United States.

Explore Santa Elena Canyon, where sheer limestone cliffs rise 1,500 feet above the Rio Grande, creating one of the most photographed and awe-inspiring landscapes in Texas. The canyon trail leads visitors through a dramatic corridor where Mexico rises on one side and Texas on the other, separated only by the flowing river below. Morning light illuminating the towering rock faces creates particularly stunning photographic opportunities.

activities.text: Venture into the Chisos Basin, the only mountain range in the United States entirely contained within a national park, offering spectacular views and notably cooler temperatures ideal for summer visits. The Window Trail provides a moderate hike to a natural rock window framing stunning sunset views over the desert below. For more challenging adventures, the South Rim trail rewards experienced hikers with 360-degree panoramas across the vast Big Bend landscape.

Take advantage of Big Bend's designation as a Dark Sky Park, offering some of the best astronomical viewing opportunities in North America thanks to its remote location far from urban light pollution. The park's rich cultural heritage includes evidence of Native American habitation, Spanish explorer routes, and pioneer settlements. Fossils dating back millions of years dot the landscape, making this region a geological wonderland for those interested in Earth's ancient history.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Texas: Hill Country, Big Bend & Gulf Coast Beaches
Far West Texas Circuit: Big Bend and Ghost Towns

visits.text: Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone — Marvel at the 366-meter-deep canyon carved by the Yellowstone River with waterfall views from multiple overlooks. The yellow volcanic rock that named the park creates dramatic walls plunging to the rushing river.

Artist Point — Photograph the Lower Falls plunging 94 meters into the canyon from this iconic viewpoint. Thomas Moran's 1872 painting of this scene helped convince Congress to establish the national park.
Hayden Valley — Drive through this prime grizzly habitat where bison herds graze and wolves hunt. The glacial valley along the Yellowstone River offers wildlife viewing rivaling Lamar Valley.

Norris Geyser Basin — Explore the park's hottest and oldest hydrothermal area with acidic geysers and colorful hot springs. Steamboat Geyser erupts irregularly to 90 meters, the world's tallest active geyser.

activities.text: Big Sky Resort area — Visit Montana's premier ski destination offering summer hiking and mountain coaster rides. The resort at the base of Lone Peak provides year-round mountain recreation.

Gallatin River fly fishing — Try casting for wild trout in this blue-ribbon stream flowing through the Madison Range. The river featured in A River Runs Through It showcasing Montana's fishing culture.
Montana ranch dinner — Feast on grass-fed beef at working ranch restaurants. The agricultural heritage shapes Montana cuisine emphasizing quality meat and local ingredients.

days_plan.itin_title: Montana's Wild Landscapes: Glacier National Park to Yellowstone Geysers

visits.text: Big Sur - Where Mountains Plunge Into the Pacific: This 90-mile stretch of coastline along Highway 1 presents some of the most dramatic scenery on Earth-sheer cliffs dropping 1,000 feet to crashing surf, with redwood canyons carved into the Santa Lucia Mountains.

Bixby Creek Bridge - The Most Photographed Bridge in California: This 714-foot concrete arch, completed in 1932, spans a deep canyon 260 feet above the creek. Its graceful curve against the rugged coastline has become the defining image of Big Sur.

Pfeiffer Beach - Purple Sand and Keyhole Rocks: This hidden beach, down an unmarked road, features purple-tinged sand (from manganese garnet) and a natural arch through which sunset light streams during certain times of year.

McWay Falls - The Waterfall That Pours Onto the Beach: This 80-foot cascade in Julia Pfeiffer Burns State Park drops directly onto a pristine cove, creating one of only two tideland falls on the Pacific coast.

activities.text: Pfeiffer Big Sur State Park - Hike Among Coastal Redwoods: The Big Sur River carves through ancient redwood groves, with swimming holes and hiking trails that climb to panoramic ridgetop views.

Pinnacles National Park - Scramble Through Talus Caves: Just inland from Big Sur, this park protects the remains of an ancient volcano, with soaring rock spires, technical climbing routes, and boulder caves housing the largest colony of Townsend's big-eared bats in California.

days_plan.itin_title: Driving the Pacific Coast Highway: A Golden Gate Bridge to Hollywood Road Trip
The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: You start your road trip at Pictograph Cave State Park, 5 miles southeast of downtown Billings. Walk the three-quarter-mile loop trail to three sandstone alcoves carved from Eagle sandstone 80 million years ago, and look closely at the walls of the largest cave: 106 pictographs painted in red, white, and black between 200 and 2,100 years ago. Excavations from 1937 to 1941 pulled over 30,000 artifacts from the site - one of the most significant archaeological digs on the Northern Plains. Bring binoculars; the paintings are faint and the distance matters. National Historic Landmark. Fee applies for non-Montana residents.

Step inside the Yellowstone Art Museum (YAM) in downtown Billings. Opened in October 1964 inside the former Yellowstone County Jail - a red-brick building dating to 1884 - it's now Montana's largest contemporary art museum, with over 7,400 works in its permanent collection. The Montana Collection covers regional artists from Will James's cowboy illustrations to abstract paintings by Rudy Autio and Deborah Butterfield. In 2009, the YAM received 50 works of minimalist and conceptual art through the National Gallery of Art's 50 Works for 50 States program. The Visible Vault - a publicly accessible art storage room - is rare in any museum. Free for Yellowstone County residents; paid entry for others.

You're standing in front of the Moss Mansion, a red sandstone 28-room house built in 1902-1903 for banker Preston Boyd Moss. The architect was Henry Janeway Hardenbergh - the same man who designed the original Waldorf-Astoria and the Plaza Hotel in New York City. Inside, the mahogany woodwork, onyx fireplace, rose silk and gold leaf wallcoverings, and the electric bell system for servants are all original and untouched. The family owned the home for over 80 years; Melville Moss, the last resident, died in 1984 while arranging for it to become a museum. Paid entry; guided tours at 10am and 1pm in summer.

activities.text: Drive up to the Rimrocks at Zimmerman Park and follow the two-mile paved trail east toward the Yellowstone Kelly interpretive site. The cliffs rise 200 to 500 feet above the valley, and from the top you get the full spread: the Yellowstone River below, the city packed between the rims, and the Beartooth Mountains on the horizon. The Rimrocks formed from sediment deposited 80 million years ago on the shoreline of the Western Interior Seaway - a shallow sea that stretched from the Gulf of Mexico to the Arctic. Free access; sunrise or late afternoon gives the best light.

End the evening along Montana Avenue, the city's historic main street running parallel to the old Northern Pacific Railroad tracks. Many brick buildings date from the 1880s when Billings was founded as a railroad town. Walk to the Western Heritage Center, housed in the 1901 Parmly Billings Library building, which tells the story of the Yellowstone River corridor through photographs and rotating exhibits. Free admission at the Heritage Center; restaurants and bars on Montana Avenue fill up by 7pm on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: Montana East: Big Sky Country from Billings to Bismarck

visits.text: Jour 1: Embark on a culinary world tour without leaving Birmingham at the Pizitz Food Hall, a stunning transformation of the city's once-grand department store into a buzzing international food emporium. Sample Ethiopian injera at Ghion Cultural Hall, slurp Vietnamese pho at PhoPho, or build a rainbow bowl of Mediterranean salads at Eli's Jerusalem Grill beneath soaring ceilings and exposed brick. The central bar pours craft cocktails while the upstairs apartments hint at the building's glamorous retail past.

Jour 1: Stand in solemn remembrance at 16th Street Baptist Church, where a white supremacist bomb shattered Sunday morning peace in 1963, claiming four young girls preparing for services. The guided tour weaves through this still-active congregation's sanctuary, pausing at the stunning stained glass window depicting a Black Christ, donated by the people of Wales in solidarity. This sacred space crystallizes the brutal reality faced by civil rights activists and the resilience that carried the movement forward.

Jour 1: Process the weight of history at Kelly Ingram Park, where powerful bronze sculptures freeze pivotal moments of Birmingham's civil rights struggle in perpetual witness. Four little girls play eternally in innocent joy, forever captured in the instant before tragedy struck across the street. Walk past snarling police dogs straining at their leashes, children huddled against the force of fire hoses, and young protestors behind jail bars, each sculpture a visceral reminder of courage in the face of hatred.

Jour 1: Deepen your understanding at the Birmingham Civil Rights Institute, a world-class museum that traces the African American journey from the humiliations of segregation to the ongoing fight for equality. Step aboard a replica Freedom Riders bus, sit at a recreated segregated lunch counter, and hear firsthand accounts that bring the movement's heroes to vivid life. The institute's position facing Kelly Ingram Park and 16th Street Baptist Church creates a powerful trinity of remembrance and education.

Jour 2: Discover the strategic nerve center of Birmingham's civil rights movement at Historic Bethel Baptist Church, where the fearless Reverend Fred L. Shuttlesworth organized resistance against segregation despite constant threats on his life. Outside, information boards mark the ghost outline of the parsonage destroyed by Klan bombers in an attack that miraculously left Shuttlesworth unharmed. His courage in repeatedly facing violence while advocating nonviolent protest epitomizes the movement's moral power.

Jour 2: Celebrate African American athletic excellence at the Negro Southern League Museum, an intimate treasure trove honoring the baseball heroes who played with passion and skill while barred from the major leagues. Stand at home plate as a hologram of legendary pitcher Satchel Paige winds up and fires fastballs that helped him eventually break baseball's color barrier. The lovingly preserved uniforms, equipment, and photographs reveal how sports provided both escape and pride during the segregation era.

Jour 2: Wander world-class galleries at the Birmingham Museum of Art, where the glass-fronted facade opens onto a serene sculpture court with pools reflecting a sinuous reclining nude. The eclectic collection spans continents and centuries, from American masters to an unexpectedly delightful array of English Wedgwood ceramics that somehow found their way to Alabama. Entry is free, making this an accessible cultural gem.

Jour 2: Ascend to the top of Vulcan, the world's largest cast-iron statue, for panoramic views across the city this mighty Roman god helped build. Created for the 1904 World's Fair, the bare-bottomed blacksmith stands atop Red Mountain as an eternal tribute to Birmingham's iron and steel heritage. The museum below chronicles how deposits of iron ore, coal, and limestone in miraculous proximity fueled the Magic City's explosive growth.

Jour 2: Escape to Railroad Park, a 19-acre urban oasis that has become Birmingham's beloved backyard since opening in 2010. Wander boardwalks through restored wetlands, cross bridges over shallow lakes, and join families gathering on the great lawn as trains rumble past on the adjacent tracks. As twilight descends, the park transforms into a magical landscape of soft lights and long shadows.

activities.text: Jour 1: Join the locals queuing out the door at Saw's Soul Kitchen in the artsy Avondale neighborhood for BBQ that embodies Birmingham's culinary renaissance. The signature Pork & Greens layers impossibly tender pulled pork over creamy stone-ground grits and tangy-sweet collard greens, crowned with crispy onion rings that shatter satisfyingly with each bite. Arrive early because once the meat's gone, it's gone.

Jour 2: Experience a uniquely Southern snack at the Alabama Peanut Company on charming cobblestoned Morris Avenue, where vats of peanuts bubble away in Cajun spice and dill pickle brine. These boiled peanuts, a beloved beach-trip tradition, achieve a soft texture and intense flavor that converts skeptics with the first bite. The airy shop also sells roasted varieties and cheeky Southern and Salty merchandise.

Jour 2: Photograph the iconic Magic City sign, a faithful replica of the neon beacon that once welcomed visitors to Birmingham's train station during the city's industrial heyday. The sign now marks the start of the Rotary Trail, a half-mile green corridor perfect for an evening stroll or morning jog connecting to the city's expanding trail network.

Jour 2: Settle into the warm glow of Bottega Cafe, the casual sibling of James Beard Award-winning chef Frank Stitt's empire, where the spirit of Italian trattorias meets Alabama hospitality. The buzzing dining room with its rich red walls draws foodies seeking wood-fired pizzas, handmade pastas, and an Italian-leaning wine list that rewards exploration. Reservations are wise, but bar seating offers prime people-watching.

Jour 2: Grab a restorative lunch at The Essential, a neighborhood gem on Morris Avenue where globally-inspired small plates come at prices that encourage ordering freely. The Nicoise-ish salad features barely seared tuna over farm greens, while the butter croissant ice cream achieves an indulgent richness that demands to be shared. The eclectic crowd of artists, professionals, and families reflects Birmingham's evolving character.

days_plan.itin_title: Alabama Deep South Itinerary: Civil Rights, Music and Gulf Coast
Alabama Civil Rights Trail: Birmingham to Mobile
Alabama Civil Rights Trail: Birmingham to Mobile

visits.text: The Queen Mine Tour takes you 1,500 feet inside the Mule Mountains aboard a small mining train. For over a century (1877–1975), 8 billion pounds of copper were extracted from these shafts. The guides are former miners—some worked here for decades. Underground temperature is constant between 47 and 59°F, regardless of the heat outside. A yellow slicker, hard hat, and headlamp are provided at the entrance. Book online in advance.

The Lavender Pit is a former open-pit copper mine 1,000 feet deep, opened in 1950 and closed in 1974 after producing 86 million tons of ore. Its wine-red and burnt-orange hues at sunset are spectacular. This is also where "Bisbee Blue" was extracted, a turquoise of intense blue considered one of the finest in the world. A roadside pullout with an interpretive panel allows a five-minute stop.

The Bisbee Mining & Historical Museum is affiliated with the Smithsonian Institution—a rare distinction for a town of 5,000 residents. The permanent exhibit "Bisbee: The Urban Outpost on the Frontier" traces the town's history from the earliest prospectors through the Bisbee Deportation of 1917, when Phelps Dodge expelled 1,300 striking miners in cattle cars. An inglorious episode in American labor history, addressed without flinching.

The Copper Queen Hotel, built in 1902, is the oldest continuously operating hotel in Arizona. Teddy Roosevelt, John Wayne, and General Pershing all slept here. A journal kept at the front desk records guests' encounters with its three supposed ghosts. Even if you're not staying here, step inside to see the Victorian lobby and bar.

activities.text: Old Bisbee is best explored on foot: the town is built into a hillside, linked by staircases carved into the rock—there are said to be more than 1,000 in all. The Brewery Gulch neighborhood was once the haunt of miners' saloons and brothels; today it's a colorful street of art galleries, craft bars, and cafés. Climb up to Tombstone Canyon Road to see the Victorian houses clinging to the slopes.

The galleries of Old Bisbee are worth a detour: the town has attracted artists since the 1970s, when the miners' departure left hundreds of houses vacant and cheap. Today, ceramicists, jewelers, and painters have taken over the alleys. Browse the Main Street shops to find "Bisbee Blue" jewelry—the local turquoise, recognizable by its black veining and color depth.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Arizona: Tucson, Tombstone & Bisbee

places.name: Bismarck

visits.text: Start at the North Dakota Heritage Center and State Museum on the capitol grounds - North Dakota's largest museum and one of the best free museums on the Northern Plains. The 2014 expansion doubled it to 127,000 square feet across four galleries. The paleontology hall holds a life-size T. rex cast, but the showstopper is Dakota - a mummified Edmontosaurus discovered in 1999 near Marmarth, ND, and housed at the Heritage Center since 2008. Dakota is one of only a dozen fully mummified dinosaurs known to science, preserving extensive fossilized skin intact. The Early Peoples gallery displays over 1,000 artifacts from the Mandan, Hidatsa, and Arikara nations. Free admission every day.

Drive 7 miles south to Fort Abraham Lincoln State Park, North Dakota's oldest state park, established in 1907 when Theodore Roosevelt signed over the land. Lt. Col. George Armstrong Custer commanded the 7th Cavalry here from 1873 until he left for the Little Bighorn campaign in May 1876. At its height the fort held 650 men across 78 buildings; all were dismantled by settlers after its 1891 abandonment. The reconstructed Custer House is furnished as it would have been when Custer and his wife Libbie lived there. The On-A-Slant Village - a reconstruction of a Mandan earthlodge settlement occupied from around 1575 until a smallpox epidemic in the 1780s - completes the picture of who lived here before the fort arrived. Paid entry.

Walk the Missouri River waterfront at Keelboat Park, where a full-size replica of the Lewis and Clark keelboat sits at the river's edge. The Missouri at Bismarck is wide, brown, and powerful - the highway of exploration and westward expansion for two centuries. A statue of Sakakawea faces west, the direction she guided the Corps of Discovery. The state capitol dome rises just behind you. Free access.

activities.text: Take the self-guided tour of the North Dakota State Capitol - a 19-story Art Deco tower completed in 1934, the tallest building in either Dakota. The observation deck on the 18th floor gives a 360-degree view over the Missouri River valley and the rolling farmland. The guided tour covers the legislative chambers, murals, and the memorial hall. Free admission; observation deck closes at 4:30pm on weekdays.

Spend the evening in Bismarck's downtown around Main Avenue, where Laughing Sun Brewing and several other local spots sit alongside restaurants serving Northern Plains comfort food. If this is the last night of the road trip, it's a good place to eat well and look back at the week: a thousand-mile arc through battlefields, badlands, dinosaur bones, and the two states where the American West still feels most like itself.

days_plan.itin_title: Montana East: Big Sky Country from Billings to Bismarck
Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: North Dakota Heritage Center State Museum Bismarck
Fort Abraham Lincoln State Park Mandan

visits.text: Columbus - The "Athens of the Prairie." This town of 47,000 inhabitants harbors more than 60 modernist buildings signed by world-renowned architects. In 1954, the Cummins Foundation began paying architect fees for public buildings - provided they chose among the best. Result: schools, churches, and banks designed by I.M. Pei, Eero Saarinen, or Robert Venturi. Stop by the Visitors Center on 5th Street for a walking tour map.

North Christian Church (Columbus) - The last building designed by Eero Saarinen before his death in 1961, completed in 1964. A stone hexagon topped by a 59-meter spire crowned with a golden cross. The interior receives natural light through a central oculus.

Nashville and Brown County - Continuing west, Nashville is an artists' village nestled in the wooded hills of Brown County. Galleries and shops line the main street. The atmosphere is relaxed - it's the kind of place where you stop for coffee and stay two hours.

Brown County State Park - Indiana's largest state park (6,500 hectares), 20 minutes from Nashville and 30 minutes from Bloomington where you'll sleep tonight. The scenic route overlooks offer views of forested ridges. In October, autumn colors attract crowds - arrive early if you're there during that period.

Indiana University Bloomington - IU's campus is one of the Midwest's most beautiful. Enter through the Sample Gates on Kirkwood Avenue, cross the lawns bordered by buildings in local limestone, and descend to the Jordan River (a creek, not a river). The Jacobs School of Music is one of the nation's finest - check if there's a free concert during your visit.

Marengo Cave - 45 minutes south of Bloomington, this cave designated a U.S. National Natural Landmark offers two underground routes: the "Dripstone Trail" (40 min, easy, family-friendly) and "Crystal Palace" (1h, more varied, with stalactites, stalagmites, and calcite columns). The formations were discovered in 1883 by two local children. Underground temperature is constant at 13°C - bring a sweater.

Spring Mill State Park - Halfway between Bloomington and Marengo, this park houses a reconstructed pioneer village with a working water mill. The Gus Grissom memorial (local native, died in the Apollo 1 fire in 1967) displays his Gemini III "Molly Brown" space capsule.

activities.text: Guided tour of the Miller House (Columbus) - Eero Saarinen's 1957 private residence for the Miller family is one of the rare examples of a single-family home signed by the architect. Interiors by Alexander Girard, gardens by Dan Kiley. The "conversation pit" recessed into the living room floor became a model copied everywhere in the '60s. Tour by reservation only.

Bloomington Farmers Market (Saturday morning) - If you're there on the right day, the Showers Common market gathers local producers, cheesemakers, and artisan bakeries. The rest of the week, Kirkwood Avenue between campus and downtown abounds with bookstores, cafes, and restaurants from around the world - the international student population is reflected in the menus.

days_plan.itin_title: Indiana Circuit: Dunes, Caves, and Cities
Indiana Circuit: Dunes, Caves, and Cities
Minnesota in 10 Days: Twin Cities, Lakes, and Lake Superior's North Shore

places.name: Blowing Rock

visits.text: Scale the legendary Blowing Rock, the high-altitude rock formation for which this scenic Blue Ridge Parkway town was named. This unique natural wonder features gusty winds so powerful they seem to return cast objects to the thrower, while the summit delivers unbeatable panoramic views of the surrounding mountain landscapes.

Embrace the challenge of hiking Glen Burney Falls Trail, a strenuous 1.6-mile outback route that rewards adventurers with the discovery of gorgeous waterfalls at trail's end. This wilderness adventure exemplifies the exceptional outdoor experiences available in this beautiful mountain destination.

activities.text: Explore local heritage at the Blowing Rock Art & History Museum, a celebrated center exhibiting diverse artworks that illuminate the overall heritage of Southern Appalachia. The museum's collections provide fascinating insight into the cultural traditions and artistic expressions that have shaped this mountain region for generations.

days_plan.itin_title: North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains

map_keywords: Blowing Rock Art and History Museum

visits.text: Cave of the Mounds - This limestone cave is 488 million years old. Discovered in 1939 during a quarry blasting operation, it remained intact and contains remarkable formations: stalactites, stalagmites, helictites (growing in all directions), and soda straws (hollow, translucent formations). The guided tour lasts about one hour and the underground temperature is constant at 50°F - bring a jacket.
Surface Karst Trail - Above the cave, a trail through restored prairie explains the region's karst geology: sinkholes, fissures, and underground water circulation. Well-designed and educational.

Blue Mound State Park - Southern Wisconsin's highest point (1,710 feet). Two observation towers offer views across the Driftless Area - this Wisconsin region never covered by glaciers that retained unique rolling topography.

activities.text: Driftless Area Hiking - Blue Mound's trails cross wildflower prairies and oak forests. The contrast with the flat lands of the rest of the Midwest is striking.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

visits.text: En route south, stop at Newspaper Rock—a 15-square-foot petroglyph panel covered with over 650 carvings accumulated over 2,000 years by Fremont, Ancestral Puebloan, and Ute peoples. The site is right by the roadside, 12 miles off Highway 191 on the road to Needles. Free and open access.

Don't underestimate Bluff—this tiny village of 250 people on the San Juan River, surrounded by red cliffs. The Bluff Fort Historic Site tells the story of Mormon pioneers' arrival in 1880, after an epic expedition through a gap in the cliff called Hole-in-the-Rock. It's also the gateway to Bears Ears National Monument—sacred land to the Navajo, Hopi, Ute, and Zuni nations.

activities.text: If you have time, do the Valley of the Gods loop (17 miles of dirt road, passable in regular car in dry weather). It's Monument Valley in miniature—isolated buttes and mesas, no tourists and no entrance fee. Allow 1-1.5 hours for the loop.

days_plan.itin_title: Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons

visits.text: Grove Street in downtown Boise is a single block that packs in the largest Basque community in North America - an estimated 16,000 residents whose ancestors left the Pyrenees in the late 1800s to herd sheep across Idaho's mountains. The cornerstone is the Cyrus Jacobs-Uberuaga House, built in 1864 and Boise's oldest surviving brick building; it served as a Basque boarding house from 1910 to 1968 and is now part of the Basque Museum & Cultural Center. The 1914 Anduiza Fronton next door still hosts handball and pala. Bar Gernika, opened in 1991 in a former 1912 Chinese laundromat, is the place for a pintxo and a glass of txakoli. The block is free to walk; the museum charges a small fee and closes on Sundays.

Steel doors clang shut behind you at the Old Idaho Penitentiary, and the sandstone walls - quarried by the inmates themselves from nearby Table Rock starting in 1872 - close in. The prison ran for exactly 101 years, housing over 13,000 people, including Harry Orchard, who assassinated Governor Frank Steunenberg in 1905 with a bomb wired to his front gate. Back-to-back riots in 1971 and 1973 burned the chapel and dining hall; the place closed in December 1973 and was left exactly as it stood that day, calendars still on the walls. Summer guided tours run daily from 10 AM to 5 PM for a fee - go at opening to avoid the wait.

Between 8th and 9th Street downtown lies Freak Alley - the Northwest's largest open-air mural gallery, born in 2002 when local artist Colby Akers painted a single alley doorway and the neighboring businesses asked for more. The project has since swallowed an entire city block and a gravel parking lot, with contributions from more than 200 artists. A $500,000 city renovation in 2018 expanded the space. Every August the murals are painted over during a week-long event and the whole thing starts again. Free and open 24 hours.

The Idaho State Capitol on Jefferson Street was completed in 1920 and is the only state capitol in the United States heated by geothermal water, drawn from a 3,000-foot well directly beneath the building. The interior dome rises 208 feet and is lined with four types of marble, including local Boise Basin stone. Self-guided tours are free on weekdays - check capitol.idaho.gov for guided tour times if you want the full context.

Julia Davis Park on the south bank of the Boise River holds the Idaho State Museum (rebuilt and reopened 2018), the Boise Art Museum, and the Idaho Anne Frank Human Rights Memorial - a free outdoor installation of granite walls engraved with human rights quotations, set among cottonwoods along the river. Thomas Davis donated the land in 1907 in memory of his wife. The park sits between downtown and Boise State University, which means it's lively on weekdays and a genuine retreat on weekend mornings.

Climb the bell tower of the Boise Depot at 2603 W Eastover Terrace and you're at the second-tallest point in the city, with views over the foothills and on clear days, the Sawtooth peaks 100 miles northeast. Built by Union Pacific in 1925 in Spanish Colonial Revival style, this was the terminal where Harriman's express trains unloaded Hollywood celebrities bound for Sun Valley. It's now a free public space; the building itself is worth the visit for the tile work alone.

activities.text: Rent a bike or lace up your shoes and head to the Boise River Greenbelt - 29 miles of paved path tracing both banks of the river through the heart of the city. The trail was born in 1967 from three donated parcels of land; the 1971 Greenbelt Ordinance forced new buildings to set back at least 70 feet from the riverbank, protecting the corridor you're on today. The stretch between Ann Morrison Park and Barber Park is the most scenic, with great blue herons working the shallows in the early morning. Bike rentals are available from several downtown outfitters.

The Basque Block alone gives you two dinner options: Leku Ona, a boutique hotel and restaurant opened in 2005 inside a historic boarding house, and Bar Gernika, where the beef tongue sandwich sells out by noon on Saturdays. For a broader choice, the 8th Street strip packs in everything from wood-fired pizza to craft cocktails within a few blocks. Book ahead for dinner in summer - Boise fills fast in July.

Rent a tube at Barber Park and float 5.5 miles back through the cottonwood corridor of the Boise River Greenbelt to Ann Morrison Park - 2 to 3 hours depending on current, with great blue herons overhead and the city skyline in the background. The water comes from Lucky Peak Reservoir and runs cold; late June through August is the sweet spot. Tube rentals and shuttles back to Barber Park operate on summer weekends from the downstream parks.

The loop closes on 8th Street, where Bittercreek Alehouse has been serving locally sourced Idaho food and regional craft beer since 1997. For a Basque send-off, Bar Gernika is open for lunch Tuesday through Saturday - the lamb sandwich and a Basque cider is the right order. Book a late-afternoon flight from BOI. Nine days and Idaho still has more to give.

days_plan.itin_title: Idaho: Sawtooth Mountains, Sun Valley & Craters of the Moon
Idaho: Sawtooth Mountains, Sun Valley & Craters of the Moon

places.name: Boone

visits.text: Challenge yourself at Rocky Knob Mountain Bike Park, a popular recreation haven spreading over 180 acres with trails designed for all skill levels. This premier destination for hikers and bikers offers exciting outdoor opportunities amid the picturesque Blue Ridge Mountain landscape that defines this charming mountain town.

Browse world-class art pieces inside the Turchin Center for the Visual Arts at Appalachian State University. This celebrated center showcases a stunning blend of creations by national and international artists, establishing Boone as a significant cultural destination in the North Carolina mountains.

activities.text: Experience Boone's thriving craft beverage scene, where local breweries showcase the art of creating refreshing craft beers. Touring the production facilities offers an opportunity to witness the brewing process firsthand and then taste the results, providing a unique conclusion to your exploration of this mountain community.

days_plan.itin_title: Appalachian Road Trip: Great Smoky Mountains & Blue Ridge
North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains

map_keywords: Turchin Center for the Visual Arts

visits.text: Start at Boston Common: founded in 1634, it's the oldest public park in the United States, 44 acres where Puritans grazed their cows before British troops camped their cannons here in 1775. Walk north across the Common to climb toward the Massachusetts State House—its golden dome, designed by Charles Bulfinch in 1798, has dominated the hill ever since.

Don't miss the Freedom Trail under any circumstances: 2.5 miles of red brick connecting 16 Revolutionary War sites through Downtown, the North End, and Charlestown. Laid out in 1951, the route passes the Paul Revere House—built around 1680, it's the oldest surviving structure in downtown Boston—the Old North Church (1723) where two lanterns signaled the British advance on April 18, 1775, and the USS Constitution, the world's oldest commissioned warship still afloat. Costumed guides depart at 11am, noon, and 1pm from Boston Common.

At the end of the Freedom Trail, in Charlestown Navy Yard, the USS Constitution has been waiting since 1797—the world's oldest commissioned warship still afloat. Go aboard: the guided tour (30 min, free) gives access to all three decks. Nicknamed "Old Ironsides" after repelling British cannonballs during the War of 1812, she's still under active command of the U.S. Navy. A hundred yards away, the Bunker Hill Monument has 294 steps leading to harbor views.

Faneuil Hall, gifted to the city in 1742 by merchant Peter Faneuil, was where colonists debated the tea tax before taking action. Step inside: entry is free and the ground floor still houses shops. Just behind it, Quincy Market built between 1824 and 1826 is New England's largest food hall—this is where you grab a proper clam chowder or lobster roll before heading to the North End.

Get lost in the narrow streets of the North End, Boston's Italian neighborhood whose lanes date back to the 17th century. At 19 North Square, the Paul Revere House is the only private residence on the Freedom Trail—Revere lived here when he rode toward Lexington on April 18, 1775. Hanover Street, the main artery, has been lined with cafés and trattorias since Italian immigrants arrived in the 1880s.

Budget at least 2.5 hours for the Museum of Fine Arts: with over 500,000 works across 45 galleries, it's one of the five largest encyclopedic museums in the United States. The American painting collection is remarkable—look for Edward Hopper's 1927 watercolor of Portland Head Light. The Japanese art department, established in the 1870s, remains a world-class reference.

The Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum is worth the trip alone—book ahead as tickets sell out fast. The collector built this Venetian palazzo in 1903 around an interior courtyard garden and assembled 2,500 works: Rembrandt, Vermeer, Titian, Botticelli. In March 1990, thirteen paintings were stolen in the largest art heist in American history—the empty frames still hang in their original positions, according to Gardner's wishes.

Make a detour through the Boston Public Garden, inaugurated in 1837: you'll find America's first public botanical garden and the cast-iron bridge spanning the pond where Swan Boats have been pedaling since 1877. Look for the Make Way for Ducklings statues, installed in 1987 as a tribute to Robert McCloskey's 1941 book—an institution for Boston families.

Back Bay stretches across a former tidal flat filled in between 1857 and 1890—the streets run in alphabetical order from Arlington to Hereford. Stop at Henry Hobson Richardson's Trinity Church (1877), a masterpiece of American architecture whose facade reflects in the glass tower of the John Hancock Tower directly opposite. Newbury Street, steps away, is the city's most prestigious shopping street.

activities.text: In the evening, head up to Beacon Hill: the Federal-era brick rowhouses of Acorn Street and Louisburg Square form one of the best-preserved architectural ensembles on the East Coast, and the gas lamps cast a particular golden glow at nightfall. Acorn Street is often cited as the most photographed street in America.

For dinner, return to the North End and order homemade pasta at one of the trattorias that have held these streets for over a century. Finish at Mike's Pastry or Modern Pastry for a cannolo—the rivalry between these two neighboring addresses has been a local institution for decades.

If the Red Sox are playing tonight, don't miss Fenway Park: opened in 1912, it's the oldest active Major League ballpark, and the atmosphere around the Green Monster—that 37-foot-high left field wall—is worth experiencing even if you're not a baseball fan. Guided tours run daily even without games, around $25.

Cross the Charles River into Cambridge: Harvard's campus, founded in 1636, is the oldest in the United States, and its red-brick courtyards are freely accessible. MIT is walkable from Harvard Square—a striking architectural contrast between 19th-century neoclassical and Calder sculptures planted among the buildings.

days_plan.itin_title: Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England
Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England

visits.text: Stroll Pearl Street Mall, pedestrianized since 1977: four blocks of red brick lined with independent shops, cafes, and award-winning restaurants, with the Flatirons as a backdrop. More than 70% of the businesses are local. Food & Wine ranked it among the ten best food streets in America.

The Flatirons—five slabs of sandstone tilted more than 1,000 feet above the city—are impossible to miss. Chautauqua Park, at their base, gives access to about twenty trails. The First Flatiron Trail (2 miles round-trip) offers a bird's-eye view of the city—start at dawn to beat the crowds.

Wander the University of Colorado Boulder campus, founded in 1876—its red sandstone buildings and red-tiled roofs make it one of the most beautiful campuses in the country. A stroll through the University Mall is enough to grasp the atmosphere of this town at 5,430 feet that has made the outdoors its identity.

activities.text: If you're here on a Saturday morning from May to October, don't miss the Boulder Farmers Market: local producers, food trucks, and artisans at the corner of 13th and Canyon Blvd. It's where the community gathers—ideal for assembling a picnic before the Flatirons.

Boulder has several taprooms steps from Pearl Street. Avery Brewing and Twisted Pine are the local references—order a pint on the patio with the Flatirons in the background. It's the end-of-day ritual for locals, and you'll understand why this town is so appealing.

days_plan.itin_title: Colorado-Wyoming Road Trip: National Parks, Rockies & Wide Open Spaces

visits.text: Whitaker Point (Hawksbill Crag) - Arkansas's Most Photographed Natural Wonder: This dramatic rock outcropping extends over a 100-foot drop into the Ozark wilderness, featured in Disney films and gracing countless magazine covers. The 3-mile round-trip hike rewards with vertigo-inducing views across miles of unbroken forest stretching to the horizon.

Roark Bluff - Where Twin Waterfalls Plunge into Paradise: Two spectacular cascades-Roark Bluff Falls and V-Notch Falls-thunder over 100-foot limestone cliffs into the Buffalo River below. Access by canoe or kayak transforms the journey into adventure.

Buffalo National River - America's First National River: Flowing 150 miles through the Ozarks, this pristine waterway remains wild and free, protected since 1972. Towering 500-foot bluffs, hidden caves, and abundant wildlife make every bend a discovery.

activities.text: Buffalo River Float Trip - Paddle Through Living Geology: Multi-day canoe and kayak journeys pass beneath massive bluffs, through swimming holes, and past riverside campsites where elk now roam the restored bottomlands.

Lost Valley & Eden Falls Cave - Underground Waterfall Wonder: A short trail leads to a natural rock shelter harboring a waterfall that disappears into a cave system. The adventurous can scramble inside to witness Eden Falls plunging into complete darkness.

days_plan.itin_title: Arkansas Itinerary: Hot Springs, Ozarks and Natural Parks

visits.text: Butte Historic District — Explore this former copper mining capital where 100 kilometers of underground tunnels honeycomb the hill beneath Victorian mansions and miners' cottages. The National Historic Landmark preserves boom-era architecture from when Butte was the richest hill on Earth.
Berkeley Pit — View this massive open-pit copper mine now filled with toxic acidic water. The 540-meter-deep pit operated 1955-1982 and demonstrates environmental impacts of hardrock mining.

Museum of the Rockies — Discover Montana's natural history including the world's largest Tyrannosaurus rex skull collection. Jack Horner's dinosaur research center houses fossils from Montana's Hell Creek Formation.
