Montana State University — Visit this land-grant university founded in 1893 featuring brick buildings and mountain-framed campus. The Gallatin Valley institution enrolls 16,000 students.

activities.text: Bozeman Main Street — Browse outdoor recreation shops, galleries, and restaurants in this thriving mountain town. Bozeman balances Old West heritage with modern amenities serving recreationists.
Brewery and distillery tasting — Sample craft beers and spirits using Montana grains and glacial water. Bozeman's beverage scene reflects the state's agricultural bounty.
Gallatin Valley sunset — Watch alpenglow paint the surrounding peaks pink and orange. The valley's mountain rim creates spectacular evening light displays.

days_plan.itin_title: Montana's Wild Landscapes: Glacier National Park to Yellowstone Geysers

places.name: Branson Mo

visits.text: Silver Dollar City isn't your typical theme park. Beneath its 100 wooded acres lies Marvel Cave, a natural cavern known to the Osage for generations and commercially operated as early as 1894. The park opened in 1960 around it, designed as an 1880s mining town. A hundred resident craftspeople work in public—glassblowers, blacksmiths, potters. The roller coasters are serious business: Outlaw Run is a wooden coaster with an 81-degree drop that set a world record when it opened in 2013. Plan a full day.

The Titanic Museum is a half-scale replica of the ocean liner planted right in the middle of Highway 76—you can't miss it. More than 400 authentic artifacts from the 1912 sinking are on display, including recreated first- and third-class cabins. At the entrance, you receive a boarding pass for an actual passenger. You find out at the end whether they survived.

Branson Landing runs along Lake Taneycomo in the heart of downtown—shops, restaurants, and a choreographed water-and-fire show every hour in the evenings. This is where tourists gather after the shows to walk along the waterfront. Not much happening in the morning, much better after 8 PM with the lake lights.

Table Rock State Park stretches 5 miles southwest of Branson, on the north shore of the lake. This is Branson before the shows: wooded trails along limestone bluffs, white-tailed deer at dawn, kingfishers on the rocks. The marina rents boats and marks diving spots on wrecks deliberately sunk to create artificial reefs. Go before 9 AM—you'll have the trails to yourself.

The Shepherd of the Hills Historic Homestead is the original farm described in Harold Bell Wright's 1907 novel—the book that triggered tourism in the Ozarks and the first American bestseller to exceed one million copies. Old Matt's Cabin still stands. The 230-foot observation tower provides the only 360-degree panorama of the surrounding hills.

The Branson Scenic Railway departs from the historic 1905 depot right downtown. Eighty minutes through the White River Valley gorges, tunnels, steel bridges, and sharp turns along the hillside. This is genuine vintage rolling stock. Take a window seat on the right as you depart—the river views are on that side.

activities.text: Table Rock Lake was created in 1958 by a dam on the White River—76 square miles with 857 miles of shoreline. Book a spot on the Showboat Branson Belle, a paddle-wheel steamboat launched in 1994: to lubricate its launch into the water, engineers used two tons of bananas. The 2-hour cruise includes a three-course meal and a variety show. Unapologetically kitschy, but genuinely enjoyable.

Branson counts dozens of dinner shows along Highway 76—country, gospel, comedy, magic. If you only do one, choose the Shepherd of the Hills Outdoor Drama over the generic shows: performed outdoors since 1960 in an amphitheater facing the hills, it tells Harold Bell Wright's 1907 novel—the book that put the Ozarks on the American tourist map, the first American bestseller to exceed one million copies sold.

Rent a boat on Table Rock Lake for the morning—powerboats, jet skis, and kayaks available at the marinas. On weekdays in the off-season, the rocky coves along the east shore are quiet and the water is clear enough for swimming. Largemouth bass fishing is good on this lake—ask the rental operator which areas are open for the day.

If you're traveling with kids, Fritz's Adventure is Branson's second-biggest attraction after Silver Dollar City. Seven stories of zip lines, climbing walls, and tunnels in a 50,000-square-foot building, opened in 2014. Without kids, skip it—but if you have them, it's two hours of peace for you.

days_plan.itin_title: Ozarks & Branson: From Missouri Hills to Arkansas Art Galleries
Ozarks & Branson: From Missouri Hills to Arkansas Art Galleries

map_keywords: Marvel Cave Silver Dollar City Branson
Table Rock State Park Branson Missouri
Shepherd of the Hills Historic Homestead Branson Missouri

visits.text: Ascend Brasstown Bald, Georgia's highest peak at 4,784 feet, where an observation tower provides 360-degree panoramas across four states on clear days. The summit trail climbs through misty forests draped in wildflowers during spring and brilliant foliage in autumn, rewarding hikers with some of the most spectacular mountain views in the American Southeast. Interpretive exhibits at the visitor center explain the region's ecology and Cherokee heritage.

Explore the broader Northeast Georgia Mountains region, a landscape of forested ridges, cascading waterfalls, and crystal-clear streams that has attracted nature lovers for generations. Scenic byways wind through quaint mountain communities where local artisans create traditional crafts and family-owned vineyards produce award-winning wines. The area offers world-class hiking, fishing, and wildlife observation.

activities.text: Visit Château Élan winery for tastings of Georgia wines accompanied by vineyard tours explaining traditional winemaking techniques. The rolling hills, elegant tasting rooms, and relaxing atmosphere make this an ideal introduction to the state's emerging wine region.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

places.name: Breaux Bridge

visits.text: Leave Baton Rouge via I-10 heading west. After 40 minutes, you'll cross the Atchafalaya Basin on an 18-mile elevated highway bridge over America's largest swamp—the view is striking, especially in the morning when mist floats above the cypress trees. Exit at Henderson (exit 115) for a bayou boat tour. Local operators offer 90-minute to 2-hour tours through the basin's channels: you'll see alligators, great blue herons, egrets, and turtles in a landscape of bald cypress draped with Spanish moss.

After the bayou, drive 15 minutes to Breaux Bridge, nicknamed "Crawfish Capital of the World" by Louisiana's legislature since 1959. The small downtown on the banks of Bayou Teche has authentic Cajun charm: painted wooden houses, art galleries, music shops, and restaurants that smell of roux and cayenne. Walk across the bridge spanning the bayou—the first bridge here was built in 1799 by Acadian pioneer Firmin Breaux.

activities.text: Make a detour to Lake Martin, between Breaux Bridge and Lafayette (10 minutes). This nature preserve harbors a colony of herons, egrets, and ibis—during nesting season (March-June), the trees are literally covered with white birds. You can drive along the lake on Rookery Road or rent a kayak to weave between the cypress trees. Alligators are common—keep your distance.

Evening in Breaux Bridge, look for a restaurant with live Cajun music. On Saturday mornings, several venues offer a "zydeco breakfast"—breakfast accompanied by live zydeco music, with people getting up to dance between tables. If you're here on a weekday, inquire about evening "Cajun jams."

days_plan.itin_title: Louisiana Road Trip: New Orleans and Cajun Country

map_keywords: Henderson Levee Road Louisiana swamp tour

places.name: Brooks Camp Katmai

visits.text: Brooks Falls is a 6-foot drop on the Brooks River—nothing spectacular in itself. What is spectacular is that dozens of brown bears, some exceeding 900 pounds, position themselves here to catch sockeye salmon returning to spawn. The NPS viewing platforms, equipped with ramps and waiting areas, are limited to 40 people at a time with hourly rotation in July. In peak season, expect 90-minute waits—come at 5 a.m. or after 7 p.m. to avoid crowds.

The Valley of Ten Thousand Smokes—accessible from Brooks Camp via the park's only vehicle road (16 miles of dirt track)—is the result of the 1912 eruption of Novarupta volcano, the largest of the 20th century worldwide. Millions of tons of ash blanketed the valley across 75 miles. Today the landscape resembles the surface of the Moon: dotted with fumaroles, valleys carved into pumice rock, utterly devoid of vegetation. The park bus makes the round trip from Brooks Camp.

The Katmai Visitor Center at Brooks Camp—modest but well-designed—traces the park's volcanic history and its designation as a national monument in 1918, long before bears made it famous. The daily ranger talks (free) are often among the best you'll hear in the entire U.S. national park system.

The Riffles Platform—the second viewing platform, downstream from Brooks Falls—is often overlooked in favor of the falls. That's a mistake: dominant bears that can't secure a spot at the falls fish here in the shallows, often just yards from the platform. The atmosphere is different, more intimate, and there's almost no wait.

The shore of Naknek Lake from Brooks Camp is a discreet but effective observation post to watch bears stroll the beach, especially late in the day. Naknek Lake is the park's largest (about 242 square miles) and feeds the Brooks River where salmon concentrate. Settle on the rocks in the evening with binoculars—don't attempt to go down to the beach if bears are present.

activities.text: The Brooks River has been a world-class fishing river since the 1950s. Rainbow trout, king salmon, and sockeye salmon can be caught with light flies from the banks, in waders. There's one absolute rule: if a bear approaches your spot, you leave—no discussion. Rangers on-site review the protocols upon arrival. Licenses available at the lodge.

The Dumpling Mountain trail (4.5 miles round-trip, 1,500 feet of elevation gain) starts at Brooks Camp and climbs above treeline to panoramic views over Naknek Lake, the Alaska Peninsula mountains, and on clear days, all the way to the Pacific. Go early in the morning—bears are active on the slopes in the evening.

Kayaking on Naknek Lake from Brooks Camp—kayaks are available for rent through Katmailand. On the water, you're at the same level as bears on shore, at a safe distance. Early morning, the lake is often mirror-flat and the reflection of the volcanoes breathtaking. Avoid outings after 6 p.m.—wind picks up quickly.

Katmai's Fat Bear Week is an internet phenomenon that drew over 800,000 votes in 2021—but before voting online, come observe in real life. In September, bears that spent the summer fishing accumulate fat reserves at an astonishing rate. Some individuals, like the famous Bear 747, can weigh over 1,400 pounds by the start of winter. Rangers at Brooks Camp know the names and histories of every regular bear—ask them.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Adventure - 14 Days Through the World's Most Remote National Parks
Alaska Adventure - 14 Days Through the World's Most Remote National Parks

map_keywords: Valley of Ten Thousand Smokes Katmai

visits.text: Discover Brunswick, the historic mainland gateway to the Golden Isles, where Victorian architecture, tree-lined streets, and scenic tidal rivers create understated coastal charm. The centuries-old Lover's Oak, a massive live oak beneath which couples have traditionally pledged their devotion, spreads its ancient branches over a peaceful park. The Old Town district preserves authentic small-town Georgia character.

Tour Hofwyl-Broadfield Plantation, a remarkably intact antebellum rice plantation documenting the complex history of agriculture, slavery, and Southern society along Georgia's tidal rivers. The quiet marina and working waterfront reflect Brunswick's enduring connection to the sea. Cross the stunning Sidney Lanier Bridge for spectacular views of the coastal landscape.

activities.text: Sample locally crafted spirits at Georgia's only rum distillery, where small-batch production creates distinctive rums with notes of dark cherry and caramel. The mainland location provides convenient access to all the Golden Isles while offering more affordable accommodations than the islands themselves.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

visits.text: Bryce Amphitheater - Bryce Canyon isn't technically a canyon but rather a collection of amphitheaters carved into the Paunsaugunt Plateau. The rocks, deposited at the bottom of an ancient lake 55 million years ago, get their colors from oxidized iron and manganese. Start at Sunrise Point for the overview: thousands of orange, pink, and cream-colored hoodoos rise like a petrified army. This is where the Queen's Garden trail begins.

Sunset Point - Half a mile from Sunrise Point via the Rim Trail, this is the starting point for the Navajo Loop. From the rim, you'll spot Thor's Hammer, a 30-foot hoodoo topped by a precariously balanced caprock that defies gravity. The plunging view into Wall Street, a slot canyon lined with Douglas firs, is vertigo-inducing.

Continue to Inspiration Point and Bryce Point for even broader amphitheater panoramas. Bryce Point, at the end of the road, offers the most spectacular perspective - you take in the entire amphitheater in a single sweeping view.

activities.text: Queen's Garden - Navajo Loop - The must-do hike at Bryce. Descend via Queen's Garden from Sunrise Point (the gentlest descent), walk among the hoodoos past the Queen Victoria formation - a pinnacle that resembles a crowned queen - then climb back up via the Navajo Loop and its tight switchbacks between the towering walls of Wall Street. The loop is 3 miles with 650 feet of elevation change; allow 1.5 to 2 hours. The park sits at 8,000 feet elevation: take your time, the air is thinner than it feels.

If you're here between April and October, stay for sunset from Sunset Point - the hoodoos shift from orange to deep crimson in minutes. The park is also certified Dark Sky: on clear nights, the Milky Way is visible to the naked eye. Bring a warm layer; it can get chilly at this elevation even in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Desert Road Trip: Zion, Bryce & Grand Canyon
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Southwest Desert Road Trip: Zion, Bryce & Grand Canyon
Utah's Mighty Five: Zion, Bryce Canyon, Capitol Reef, Arches, and Canyonlands

places.name: Buffalo

visits.text: Frank Lloyd Wright's Darwin Martin House — Tour this Prairie School masterpiece completed in 1905, considered Wright's most important work before Fallingwater. The complex features 394 art glass windows and doors creating geometric light patterns throughout interconnected spaces.

Albright-Knox Art Gallery — Explore this museum renowned for its contemporary and modern art collection spanning Impressionism to Pop Art. The 1905 Greek Revival building houses works by Picasso, Matisse, Pollock, and Warhol.

Buffalo City Hall — Ascend to the 28th-floor observation deck of this 1931 Art Deco skyscraper for panoramic city and lake views. The 115-meter tower features intricate Native American motifs and Art Deco ornamentation throughout its limestone facade.

Canalside — Stroll along Buffalo's revitalized waterfront where the Erie Canal met Lake Erie beginning in 1825. The harbor development features historic naval vessels, kayak launches, and winter ice skating on refrigerated outdoor rinks.

activities.text: Buffalo wings at Anchor Bar — Taste the original Buffalo wing invented here accidentally in 1964 by Teressa Bellissimo. The deep-fried chicken wings tossed in cayenne pepper hot sauce and served with celery and blue cheese became a global phenomenon.

Elmwood Village neighborhood walk — Browse boutiques, galleries, and cafes in this vibrant historic district listed on the National Register. Tree-lined streets showcase Victorian and Colonial Revival architecture from Buffalo's Gilded Age prosperity.

Sunset at Times Beach Nature Preserve — Observe migrating birds and watch sailboats on the Niagara River at this 37-hectare urban wilderness. The preserve's wetlands and meadows attract over 200 bird species along the Atlantic Flyway.

days_plan.itin_title: Empire State Road Trip: From Manhattan Skyscrapers to Niagara Falls

map_keywords: Darwin Martin House Frank Lloyd Wright

places.name: Burlington Vt

visits.text: Church Street Marketplace makes itself known the moment you set foot in Burlington: a four-block pedestrian street voted best public space in the United States by USA Today in 2022, with over 100 shops and restaurants and street musicians on every corner. The ECHO Leahy Center for Lake Champlain, on the waterfront, is an interactive museum featuring the lake's 81 fish species—worth seeing if you're traveling with children.

Waterfront Park opens onto Lake Champlain with New York's Adirondacks as backdrop—settle on the boardwalk at sunset. Named by Samuel de Champlain in 1609, the lake stretches for 120 miles and contains 6.8 trillion gallons of water. The Burlington Bike Path follows 7.5 miles of shoreline and joins the Island Line Trail, a converted railway running for miles above the lake's waters.

If you're here on a Saturday morning, the Burlington Farmers Market takes over City Hall Park: local producers, artisan bakeries, cheesemakers, and maple syrup cabins side by side. Taste a creemee—the local maple soft serve—before hitting the road toward Providence.

activities.text: Burlington is regularly ranked among the cities with the highest density of breweries per capita in the United States—try Foam Brewers on the waterfront, Zero Gravity, or Switchback Brewing Company. In the evening, the Spirit of Ethan Allen offers dinner cruises on the lake with views of the Adirondacks at sunset.

Six miles south, the Shelburne Museum is one of the most comprehensive collections of Americana in the country: 150,000 objects distributed across 39 historic buildings including a steamboat that once sailed Lake Champlain. Budget 3 hours minimum—open May through November, admission ~$30.

days_plan.itin_title: Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England
Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England

map_keywords: South End Arts District Burlington Vermont

visits.text: Leave Los Angeles via the Pacific Coast Highway heading north. The first Malibu beaches begin 19 miles from downtown—this is where movie stars' homes have clustered since the 1920s. Stop at Zuma Beach for a break before continuing to Santa Barbara.

Santa Barbara calls itself the 'American Riviera.' Mission Santa Barbara, founded in 1786, is one of California's 21 Franciscan missions and one of the few never abandoned since its founding. The neoclassical facade you see today dates from 1833. Stearns Wharf, California's oldest working pier built in 1872, extends 2,100 feet into the sea—ideal for spotting dolphins and seals.

activities.text: Arrive in Cambria by late afternoon. This small village of 6,000 residents is wedged between wooded hills and Pacific cliffs. Moonstone Beach is scattered with translucent pebbles—the moonstones that gave the beach its name. Dine facing the ocean.

days_plan.itin_title: California Road Trip: San Francisco, Yosemite & Death Valley

places.name: Cannon Beach

visits.text: Three Capes Scenic Route — Navigate this 64-kilometer coastal loop featuring Cape Meares, Cape Lookout, and Cape Kiwanda. Dramatic headlands, sea stacks, and old-growth Sitka spruce forests create one of Oregon's most scenic drives.

Oregon Coast Aquarium — Discover Pacific Northwest marine life in exhibits featuring sea otters, seals, sharks, and jellyfish. The Newport aquarium played a crucial role in rehabilitating Keiko, the orca from Free Willy.

Yaquina Head Outstanding Natural Area — Climb Oregon's tallest lighthouse standing 28 meters and visit tide pools at Cobble Beach. The basalt headland jutting into the Pacific offers seal and whale watching opportunities.

Historic Bayfront — Explore Newport's working waterfront featuring seafood markets, restaurants, and sea lion docks. The fishing fleet and marine science facilities maintain the harbor's maritime character since the 1860s.

activities.text: Whale watching — Spot gray whales migrating along the Oregon coast from December through May, with resident whales visible year-round. The headlands offer prime viewing as 20,000 whales pass during spring migration.
Sea lion observation — Watch Steller sea lions hauling out on docks beneath the Yaquina Bay Bridge. Hundreds of these massive marine mammals gather year-round, barking and jostling for space.
Clam chowder tasting — Sample Oregon's famous seafood at Bayfront restaurants serving award-winning chowder made with fresh local clams. Many establishments claim the best chowder on the coast.

days_plan.itin_title: Oregon Natural Wonders: Crater Lake to Pacific Coast Road Trip

map_keywords: Cape Meares Cape Lookout Cape Kiwanda
Yaquina Head lighthouse and tide pools
Steller sea lion viewing Yaquina Bay

visits.text: Hike the Skinny Dip Falls Trailhead, where scenic forest trails lead through beautiful Appalachian woodland to a stunning waterfall and refreshing swimming spot. This natural treasure offers the perfect escape into the wilderness that surrounds this charming Haywood County town near Asheville.

Explore Graveyard Fields, a unique high-altitude area offering mysterious and captivating views of the Blue Ridge Mountains. This popular hiking destination features unusual landscapes and abundant wildflowers, providing outdoor enthusiasts with unforgettable mountain experiences.

activities.text: Discover local artistry at Cold Mountain Art Collective, where regional artists showcase their creative works inspired by the surrounding mountain landscape. Browse handcrafted items at Willow Hill Soap Company, known for their artistic and decorative candles that make perfect souvenirs of your mountain adventure.

days_plan.itin_title: North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains

visits.text: Kennedy Space Center - Where Humans Reach for the Stars: This historic launch complex has sent every American astronaut into orbit since the 1960s. The visitor complex offers encounters with real astronauts, full-scale rocket displays, and the actual Space Shuttle Atlantis.

Rocket Garden - Walk Among Giants: Stand beneath authentic rockets from every era of American spaceflight, from Mercury through Apollo, experiencing the scale of humanity's greatest engineering achievements.

Canaveral National Seashore - 24 Miles of Undeveloped Atlantic Coast: NASA's need for privacy created this unintended benefit-a pristine refuge protecting sea turtle nesting beaches and 1,000+ plant species.

activities.text: Rocket Launch Viewing - Watch History Being Made: Multiple companies now launch from Cape Canaveral, with public viewing areas offering front-row seats to thundering liftoffs that shake the ground miles away.

IMAX Theater and 4D Experience - Space Exploration Immersion: State-of-the-art theaters present the wonders of space exploration through footage shot by astronauts aboard the International Space Station.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Provincetown is worth the two-hour drive from the Cape's entrance—don't skip it. The Pilgrim Monument (1910) recalls that the Pilgrims anchored the Mayflower here in November 1620, before Plymouth, and drafted the Mayflower Compact. The 252-foot granite tower is the tallest all-granite structure in the United States. A mile away, Race Point Beach spreads its 100-foot dunes facing the open Atlantic.

Chatham delivers the postcard Cape Cod image: Main Street lines up shops, art galleries, and seafood restaurants in white clapboard buildings. Chatham Fish Pier comes alive early morning when the boats arrive—you can buy fresh tuna directly from the fishermen. Chatham Lighthouse (1878) overlooks the beach from atop a bluff.

Cape Cod National Seashore has protected 50 square miles of coastline since 1961: Atlantic beaches, dunes, pine forests, and salt marshes. Enter through the Salt Pond Visitor Center in Eastham for a trail map. Coast Guard Beach and Nauset Light Beach are the Cape's wildest beaches—cool water, few people on weekdays.

The moment the ferry docks in Oak Bluffs, head to Trinity Park: over 300 painted wooden cottages in vivid colors form a unique ensemble, the "Gingerbread Houses" built from the 1860s onward on the site of a Methodist camp meeting. The central cast-iron Tabernacle (1879) still hosts summer concerts. Circuit Avenue, the main street, is lined with shops and restaurants.

Edgartown, founded in 1642, is the island's historic center. The 19th-century whaling captains' houses line their white facades along Water Street—stop at the Martha's Vineyard Historical Society, at the Cooke House (1765), which preserves documents and objects related to the whaling era. A 3-minute ferry crosses the channel to Chappaquiddick Island.

At the island's western tip, the Gay Head Cliffs of Aquinnah rise 200 feet above the Atlantic. Step to the edge to watch the clay change color with the light—ochre, red, white, gray. These lands belong to the Wampanoag Tribe of Gay Head (Aquinnah), which regained them in 1987. The Gay Head Lighthouse (1844) is reachable on foot from the parking lot. Caution: erosion is rapid.

activities.text: Embark on a whale watching trip from MacMillan Pier in Provincetown: 3-4 hour excursions to Stellwagen Bank National Marine Sanctuary, 5 miles north of the Cape, allow you to observe humpback whales, finbacks, and dolphins. The Dolphin Fleet offers daily departures May through October—book online, ~$50 per person. Morning trips have the best sea conditions.

The Cape Cod Rail Trail winds for 25 miles along a 19th-century railway bed—rent a bike and go: the path crosses dunes, glacial ponds, and scrub oak forests. Rental points dot the route. It's the most pleasant way to cross the Cape without a car.

Rent a bike at the ferry exit in Oak Bluffs and ride the island via State Road to Aquinnah (14 miles): the route crosses oak forests, meadows, and skirts Tisbury Pond. Martha's Vineyard is flat and the bike network well developed—count on ~$30 per day.

In the evening, go eat in Menemsha, the small fishing port on the west side of the island. The lobster is caught that very morning and cooked to order—eat on the docks watching the boats come in and the sun set over Vineyard Sound.

days_plan.itin_title: Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England
Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England

visits.text: Explore Capulin Volcano National Monument, where an extinct cinder cone volcano rises dramatically from the surrounding plains, providing a remarkable example of geologically recent volcanic activity in the American Southwest. The paved road spirals to the crater rim, where hiking trails circle the edge and descend into the volcanic crater itself. From the summit, views extend across four states on clear days, revealing the vast grassland plains that characterize this corner of New Mexico.

activities.text: Watch for diverse wildlife including elk, coyotes, and black bears that inhabit the volcanic slopes and surrounding grasslands. The monument preserves not only geological features but also prairie ecosystems that once supported vast bison herds and continue to shelter numerous species adapted to this high plains environment.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

visits.text: Carlsbad Caverns - Descend via the natural entrance: a gaping maw in the rock where a 1.25-mile trail spirals 750 feet underground. The route passes monumental limestone formations before reaching the Big Room, the largest single cave chamber in North America - 357,000 square feet, roughly six football fields. The temperature stays at 56°F year-round, so bring a jacket. If your knees can't handle the descent, an elevator drops you straight into the Big Room.

Carlsbad Museum and Art Center - If you still have energy after the caverns, swing by this small free museum in town. You'll see Permian fossils, Southwestern art, and a collection of Native American pottery.

activities.text: Bat Flight Program - From May through October, between 200,000 and 500,000 Brazilian free-tailed bats emerge from the caverns at dusk to hunt insects. Settle into the stone amphitheater facing the cave entrance. The show begins when a black spiral starts twisting skyward - it lasts 20 to 40 minutes. Rangers give a presentation before the flight. Free, no reservations needed.

days_plan.itin_title: Desert Parks of Texas and New Mexico: Big Bend, Carlsbad, White Sands, and Guadalupe

visits.text: Descend into Carlsbad Caverns National Park, where more than 100 limestone caves beneath the Chihuahuan Desert reveal spectacular underground formations created over millions of years. The park's namesake Carlsbad Cavern features the famous Big Room, one of the largest cave chambers in North America, where massive stalactites and stalagmites create an otherworldly landscape 750 feet below the surface. Guided tours reveal increasingly remote sections of this subterranean wonderland.

Marvel at Lechuguilla Cave, the nation's deepest limestone cave at 1,567 feet and fourth longest, containing rare mineral formations found nowhere else on Earth. While this cave is closed to general visitors due to its scientific importance, exhibits explain the extraordinary discoveries made within its pristine passages. The park protects over 300 caves beneath the desert surface.

activities.text: Witness the spectacular evening bat flight from May through October, when hundreds of thousands of Brazilian free-tailed bats spiral from the cave entrance at dusk to hunt insects across the desert. Ranger programs explain the ecology of these remarkable creatures and their importance to the cave ecosystem. Drive Walnut Canyon Desert Loop to experience the Chihuahuan Desert landscape above ground.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

places.name: Carmel By The

visits.text: Carmel-by-the-Sea - A Storybook Village on the Pacific: This enchanting town has no street addresses, no streetlights, no chain stores, and no high heels allowed on sidewalks without a permit. Fairy-tale cottages with names instead of numbers line streets designed for wandering.

Mission San Carlos Borromeo de Carmelo - California's Most Beautiful Mission: Founded in 1770 by Father Junípero Serra, this graceful stone church with its distinctive bell tower served as headquarters for the California mission chain. Serra is buried at the altar.

Carmel Beach - White Sand Without the Crowds: This pristine crescent of powdery white sand, backed by Monterey cypress, consistently ranks among California's most beautiful beaches. Dogs run free and bonfires glow at sunset.

activities.text: Point Lobos State Natural Reserve - The Crown Jewel of the State Park System: Often called 'the greatest meeting of land and water in the world,' this reserve protects 550 acres of headlands, coves, and meadows where sea lions bark and sea otters float in kelp beds.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

map_keywords: Mission San Carlos Borromeo de Carmelo

visits.text: In Carthage, Chris works for Wayne Westerberg in a grain silo, befriends the locals and finds temporary balance.
Chris returns to Carthage, works again for Westerberg, saves money and finalizes his plan for a solo expedition to Alaska.

activities.text: Vol prochaine étape

days_plan.itin_title: Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey
Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey

places.name: Casper

visits.text: Pull off I-25 for the National Historic Trails Interpretive Center - free admission, and one of Wyoming's better history museums. Casper sits at the crossing of four western migration corridors: Oregon, California, Mormon Pioneer, and Pony Express trails. The museum covers the 400,000 emigrants who crossed this ground between 1841 and 1868, with hands-on exhibits including a virtual covered wagon ride and a treadmill that simulates pulling a handcart. A 27-minute film addresses US treaty violations with Plains tribes. Allow 75-90 minutes.

Fort Caspar Museum sits on the banks of the North Platte River in a reconstructed 1865 military post - named for 2nd Lieutenant Caspar Collins, killed at the Battle of Platte Bridge Station that year. A typo in the 1889 town registration swapped the 'a' for an 'e' in Casper, which is why the town and the fort are spelled differently. The museum covers prehistoric Plains Indians, the frontier army, and the energy industry. Open daily May through September.

days_plan.itin_title: Wyoming Beyond Yellowstone: Cowboys, Hot Springs & Bighorn Medicine Wheel

map_keywords: National Historic Trails Interpretive Center Casper Wyoming

places.name: Cave City Ky

visits.text: Welcome underground. Mammoth Cave is the world's longest known cave system - 426 miles of mapped passages to date, 1.5 times the length of the world's second-longest system. The limestone rock began forming 330 million years ago when Kentucky was still at the bottom of a tropical sea. Native Americans explored these passages 4,000 years ago: their woven moccasins and reed torches have been found several miles from the entrance. Stop at the Visitor Center first to pick up your tickets - the cave stays 54°F year-round, bring a layer even in summer.

Take the Historic Tour first (2 hours, 2 miles, about 540 steps): it's the signature experience. You descend into the Rotunda where enslaved people mined saltpeter for gunpowder during the War of 1812, then pass the tuberculosis huts built in 1842 by Dr. Croghan - who believed underground air would cure his patients. They all died. Gothic Avenue is lined with soot inscriptions left by early visitors as far back as the 19th century. Stephen Bishop, enslaved and then freed guide, mapped much of the system in the 1830s; his grave is visible near the entrance.

In the afternoon, follow up with the Frozen Niagara Tour (1h15, quarter-mile, easy): a different section of the system, visually very different. Here formations dominate - stalactites, stalagmites, and the great cascading rock draperies that look like a petrified waterfall. Two tours in one day are enough to get a complete picture of the system's diversity.

If you want to go deeper, the Violet City Lantern Tour (3 hours, 3 miles) completely changes the atmosphere: rangers turn off all electric lighting and you advance by lantern like in the 19th century. The immensity of the passages becomes palpable when artificial light disappears. It's the favorite tour of several park rangers - and one of the most powerful underground experiences of this road trip.

9 miles north of the park, Lost River Cave in Bowling Green deserves a stop: it's one of the largest cave openings in the eastern United States, with an underground river navigable by boat. The cave served as a nightclub in the 1930s - a sign recalls this improbable period at the entrance. The boat tour lasts 45 minutes.

activities.text: Between tours, enjoy the surface: the Green River Ferry (free, NPS-operated) crosses the river every 10-15 minutes. On the other side, the shaded banks are nearly deserted on weekdays. The First Creek Lake Loop trail (1.5 miles, flat) follows a wet prairie frequented by deer and wild turkeys - a good way to decompress between underground plunges.

In the evening, Cave City a few miles south has decent restaurants and affordable motels. If you prefer sleeping in the park, The Lodge at Mammoth Cave is right on-site - convenient for being there first thing when tours open the next morning.

For thrill-seekers, the Wild Cave Tour (6 hours, 5.5 miles) is the park's most demanding tour: six hours crawling through tight passages, climbing in the dark, and squeezing through sections the standard tours never visit. Bring old clothes - you'll emerge covered in mud. Minimum age 16, reservation required well in advance.

If you prefer staying above ground, the park's 70 miles of trails traverse the karst hills of Kentucky - a landscape of sinkholes, springs, and depressions typical of this limestone terrain. The Sal Hollow Trail (4.5 miles) follows a wooded valley along a creek, nearly empty of people.

days_plan.itin_title: Louisville, Mammoth Cave & Congaree - Caves and Swamps of the Deep South
Louisville, Mammoth Cave & Congaree - Caves and Swamps of the Deep South

map_keywords: Mammoth Cave Wild Cave Tour Kentucky
Violet City Lantern Tour Mammoth Cave
Mammoth Cave surface trails Green River
Lost River Cave Bowling Green Kentucky

places.name: Cedar Rapids

visits.text: You enter a 50,000-square-foot Smithsonian Affiliate on the Cedar River's south bank, which began in 1978 as the Czech Fine Arts Foundation. Its crown jewel is the largest collection of traditional Czech and Slovak kroje (folk costumes) outside the Czech Republic and Slovakia, including pieces dating back to the 16th century. The Immigrant Home exhibit - a surviving 19th-century Bohemian house relocated to higher ground after the devastating 2008 Cedar River flood - walks you through five generations of one family's American story. Admission charged.

Cedar Rapids native Grant Wood painted American Gothic in 1930 in a studio right here in downtown Cedar Rapids. The museum holds the world's largest public collection of Wood's work, from early Impressionist experiments to the regionalist paintings that made him a national figure. The Grant Wood Studio itself is a separate site a few blocks away - advance booking required for studio access. Admission charged.

You drive 30 miles south to Iowa's original state capitol, which housed the legislative assembly from 1842 until 1857, when the seat of government moved west to Des Moines. The building then became the anchor of the University of Iowa campus - a National Historic Landmark standing at the heart of the Pentacrest. The cornerstone was laid in 1840, and the restored period rooms inside represent the building's 1840s-1850s appearance. Free admission; closed Sundays and Mondays.

activities.text: You push open the door to Prairie Lights, founded in 1978 by Jim Harris and moved in 1982 to its current 11,000-square-foot quarters - the same building where Iowa City's literary society met in the 1930s, hosting readings by Robert Frost, Carl Sandburg, and Langston Hughes. Seven Nobel Prize laureates in literature have read here, including Toni Morrison and J.M. Coetzee. The Live from Prairie Lights reading series runs four or five nights a week. Check the calendar before your visit.

You wander along 16th Avenue SW through Cedar Rapids' Czech Village, passing the NCSML, where North America's only Orloj - a working replica of Prague's medieval astronomical clock - stands in front of the museum. The adjacent NewBo (New Bohemia) district has converted old industrial buildings into a food market, breweries, and galleries. You can walk both neighborhoods in under an hour.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa and Midwest Road Trip: Great Plains Loop
Iowa and Midwest Road Trip: Great Plains Loop
Iowa Backroads: From the Missouri Bluffs to the Mississippi Cliffs

map_keywords: National Czech Slovak Museum Cedar Rapids

visits.text: Cedarburg Historic Downtown - Over 200 nineteenth-century buildings line the streets of this small town north of Milwaukee. Old woolen mills converted into shops and galleries at Cedar Creek Settlement, a covered wooden bridge (Wisconsin's last), and cobblestone streets full of charm. This is one of the state's best-preserved downtowns.
Cedarburg Covered Bridge - Built in 1876, this is Wisconsin's last remaining covered bridge. It spans Cedar Creek in a wooded setting north of town.
Cedar Creek Settlement - The former woolen mill (1864) now houses a winery, art galleries, and craft shops. The limestone building is impressive.

activities.text: Craft Breweries and Distilleries - Cedarburg has embraced the craft trend. Several breweries and a distillery offer tastings. This is Wisconsin - beer is serious business here.
Cedar Creek Walk - Easy, shaded trail following the creek through town. Pleasant in late afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

visits.text: Explore Chaco Culture National Historical Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site preserving the most remarkable concentration of ancient Pueblo architecture in North America. Between 850 and 1250 CE, Chaco Canyon served as the ceremonial, administrative, and economic center of a vast regional network spanning thousands of square miles. Massive great houses containing hundreds of rooms and multiple stories demonstrate sophisticated engineering and astronomical knowledge.

Visit Pueblo Bonito, the largest and most famous of Chaco's great houses, featuring over 600 rooms arranged in a perfect D-shape aligned to celestial events. The precision construction using millions of carefully shaped sandstone blocks continues to amaze archaeologists and visitors alike. Petroglyphs throughout the canyon document the spiritual and practical knowledge of these ancient peoples.

activities.text: Discover Fajada Butte and the famous Sun Dagger, where spirals carved into rock create a solar calendar marking solstices and equinoxes with remarkable precision. The park's remote location far from light pollution makes it exceptional for stargazing, designated an International Dark Sky Park. Hiking trails connect major ruins while revealing the canyon's stark desert beauty.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

places.name: Chamberlain Sd

visits.text: Pull off I-90 at exit 263 and walk the keelboat-shaped balcony of the Lewis and Clark Welcome Center — the Missouri River spreads below you in both directions, and the replica of the Corps' 55-foot keelboat gives you the scale. Just outside, *Dignity: Of Earth and Sky* by sculptor Dale Lamphere rises 50 feet in stainless steel — a Lakota and Dakota woman with outstretched quilt-patterned wings, facing the river the expedition came up in 1804. The Akta Lakota Museum at St. Joseph's Indian School, two miles east, holds one of the most comprehensive collections of Lakota art and artifacts in the Great Plains. Free admission.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: Chamberlain South Dakota keelboat Lewis Clark
Dignity of Earth Sky statue Chamberlain

visits.text: Paisley Park—Book ahead: Prince's studio and residence, opened to the public in 2016, is by appointment only. Inside this 65,000-square-foot white building, you'll see his recording studios, private concert hall, costumes, and instruments. The guided tour lasts 90 minutes.

Minnesota Landscape Arboretum—Fifteen minutes from Chanhassen, take time to explore this 1,200-acre botanical garden, the largest in the Midwest. You can tour it on foot or by car along a 3-mile loop. If you come in fall, the colors are extraordinary.

activities.text: Make a detour through Excelsior, a village on the shores of Lake Minnetonka. The lake covers 14,000 acres with 78 miles of shoreline—it's been the site of summer estates since the 19th century. Stroll the pier and watch the boats pass.

days_plan.itin_title: Minnesota in 10 Days: Twin Cities, Lakes, and Lake Superior's North Shore

visits.text: Stroll through Charleston's Historic District, where cobblestone streets wind past pastel-colored antebellum homes adorned with flower-filled window boxes. Rainbow Row's iconic facades and the Battery's waterfront promenade showcase architectural treasures spanning centuries. Every corner reveals ornate iron gates, hidden courtyards, and perfectly preserved Southern elegance.

Take a harbor boat tour to Fort Sumter National Monument, where the first shots of the Civil War were fired in 1861. The journey across Charleston Harbor offers stunning views of the city skyline and the Ravenel Bridge, with frequent dolphin sightings along the way. Explore the massive brick fortifications, examine historic cannons, and climb the ramparts for sweeping coastal panoramas.

Explore Magnolia Plantation and Gardens, home to some of the oldest public gardens in America where ancient live oaks frame romantic bridges, winding pathways, and reflective ponds. The Audubon Swamp Garden features haunting cypress trees, boardwalks over still green waters, and abundant wildlife including alligators and countless bird species. The historic Garden Train offers a relaxed way to tour these enchanting grounds.

Discover Middleton Place, featuring the oldest landscaped gardens in America with terraced lawns sweeping toward the Ashley River beneath massive oaks trailing Spanish moss. The perfectly sculpted hedges, secret pathways, and seasonal blooms create living works of art unchanged for centuries. The Stableyards present heritage animal breeds and demonstrations of 18th-century plantation life.

activities.text: Visit Boone Hall Plantation, entering through the legendary Avenue of Oaks where dozens of massive live oaks draped in Spanish moss create an unforgettable natural cathedral. The preserved brick slave cabins along Slave Street contain powerful exhibits presenting the history of enslaved people. The plantation home, gardens of climbing roses, and working orchards complete this profound historical experience.

Stand in awe beneath the Angel Oak Tree, a natural monument estimated to be centuries old with limbs stretching so far they touch the ground before curving skyward again. This massive specimen creates its own cathedral of leaves and branches on Johns Island. The surrounding park provides a peaceful setting for contemplating one of nature's most impressive creations.

Tour the Middleton Place House Museum, filled with original furniture, portraits, and artifacts chronicling the prominent Middleton family and the people who lived and worked on the plantation. The serene riverside setting and meticulously maintained gardens offer hours of peaceful exploration through one of the most meaningful historic sites in the South.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains
South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains
West Virginia Loop - From the Appalachians to New River Gorge

places.name: Charlotte Nc

visits.text: The NASCAR Hall of Fame opened on May 11, 2010, for $160 million in the heart of Uptown Charlotte—and even if you're not a racing fan, you'll spend 3 hours without realizing it. The story begins with 1930s bootleggers who modified their cars to outrun the police; NASCAR was founded in 1948. The "Glory Road," a ramp banked at 33 degrees like Talladega, displays 18 historic cars.

The Harvey B. Gantt Center for African-American Arts + Culture on South Tryon Street bears the name of the first Black student admitted to Clemson University (South Carolina) in 1963, and Charlotte's first Black mayor from 1983 to 1987—enter to understand why this institution is unique in the South. Its rotating exhibitions explore African American art and culture with real curatorial rigor. The building by Gantt Huberman Architects is itself a fine piece of architecture.

If you only have time for one museum in Charlotte, choose the Mint Museum Uptown in the Levine Center for the Arts. Its 145,000 square feet house the largest collection of ceramics and decorative arts in North America, including the largest collection of English porcelains in the United States.

The Bechtler Museum of Modern Art, right across from the Mint Uptown, is devoted to mid-20th-century European avant-garde art—and you'll find Miró, Giacometti, and Warhol assembled by a Swiss family with remarkable taste. Mario Botta's sandstone building is itself an architectural sculpture.

activities.text: Uptown Charlotte is compact and pleasant on foot—start from Romare Bearden Park, named after the great collage artist born in Charlotte in 1911, and walk up Tryon Street to 7th Street. In the evening, South End and NoDa (North Davidson) await with the city's best tables and a live music scene almost every night.

End this journey with a real North Carolina barbecue meal: slow-smoked pork shoulder, vinegar-pepper sauce, mayo-free coleslaw. It's a distinct style from Texas or Tennessee. Several recognized pit masters operate within 30 minutes of Uptown. Don't leave without trying it.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltimore to the Outer Banks - Historic America from Chesapeake Bay to Charlotte

map_keywords: Bechtler Museum of Modern Art Charlotte

visits.text: You're stopping in Staunton and finding one of Virginia's best-preserved Victorian downtowns - the town was spared by the Civil War and its red brick facades from the 1880s-1900s are virtually intact. Staunton is the birthplace of Woodrow Wilson, the 28th President of the United States, born here in 1856; his birthplace has become a presidential museum. The Beverley Street district lines up independent restaurants, galleries, and shops in period commercial buildings. It's an ideal lunch stop - allow 1.5 hours to stroll at leisure before getting back on the road to Charlottesville, 40 km to the east.

You're climbing Monticello Hill and standing before the house that Thomas Jefferson himself designed, modified, and perfected over forty years starting in 1768. The facade is familiar to all Americans: it has appeared on the nickel, the five-cent coin, since 1938. Jefferson - architect, inventor, philosopher, 3rd President of the United States - lived here until his death on July 4, 1826, exactly fifty years after the signing of the Declaration of Independence that he drafted. The estate has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987. Book your guided tour online in advance - morning slots fill quickly during high season.

You're walking along the Lawn - the central lawn of the University of Virginia - framed by the red brick pavilions that Jefferson himself designed between 1817 and 1826 to embody his ideal of 'a university based on freedom and reason.' The Rotunda that closes the axis to the north is inspired by Rome's Pantheon, at half scale. The campus is a UNESCO World Heritage Site along with Monticello - it's the only university campus in the world to share this distinction with its founder's home. Self-guided tours are free at any time; free guided tours depart from the Welcome Center several times daily.

activities.text: You're visiting the white wooden Presbyterian manse where Woodrow Wilson was born in 1856 - the only American president to earn a doctorate, the only one to receive the Nobel Peace Prize (1919) for his role in creating the League of Nations after World War I. The adjacent museum traces his presidency (1913-1921): the progressive New Freedom laws, entry into war in 1917, the Fourteen Points, and the Senate's failure to ratify the Treaty of Versailles that broke his health. His 1919 presidential Pierce-Arrow is exhibited in the original garage.

You're walking on the Downtown Mall, an 8-block pedestrian brick street paved in the 1970s, which has become one of the liveliest pedestrian shopping streets in the American South. Hundreds of independent restaurants, galleries, bars, and art-house cinemas animate this corridor year-round. The atmosphere blends UVA students, long-time residents, and tourists from Monticello. In late afternoon, settle on a terrace to observe local life before getting back on the road the next morning to Washington.

days_plan.itin_title: West Virginia Loop - From the Appalachians to New River Gorge

visits.text: Rock City Gardens — Explore this 1930s attraction atop Lookout Mountain featuring massive rock formations, gardens, and views across seven states from Lovers Leap. The Swing-A-Long Bridge spans a 55-meter gorge while Ruby Falls cascades 44 meters underground.

Tennessee Aquarium — Discover freshwater and saltwater ecosystems in North America's largest aquarium housing 12,000 animals. The $45 million complex features two buildings exploring river to ocean environments.

Walnut Street Bridge — Walk across this 732-meter pedestrian bridge built in 1891 as one of the world's longest spanning structures. The bridge connects downtown to North Shore arts district offering river and mountain views.

Hunter Museum of American Art — View American art from Colonial to contemporary in this clifftop museum. The 1904 classical mansion and 1975 brutalist addition house works by Mary Cassatt, Ansel Adams, and regional artists.

activities.text: Incline Railway ride — Board the world's steepest passenger railway climbing 72.7% grade up Lookout Mountain. The 1895 cable car offers panoramic views across Chattanooga and the Tennessee River.
Riverfront dining — Enjoy farm-to-table Southern cuisine at restaurants overlooking the Tennessee River. The revitalized waterfront features local ingredients and mountain views.

days_plan.itin_title: Tennessee Music & Mountains Road Trip: Nashville to Great Smoky Mountains

visits.text: Chauvin Sculpture Garden - At the end of a narrow road hugging Bayou Petit Caillou, 100 km south of New Orleans, this sculpture garden is one of the most striking outsider art sites in the United States. Between 1990 and 2000, Kenny Hill, a solitary bricklayer, created about a hundred life-size concrete statues: angels with light bulb halos, cowboys, self-portraits with black and white painted faces, and a 14-meter brick lighthouse with figures clinging to its sides. Kenny Hill abandoned everything one day in 2000 and disappeared. The site, restored and managed by Nicholls State University, is free and open from 8 AM to 5 PM.

Bayou Petit Caillou - Driving to Chauvin, you'll cross one of the most authentic landscapes in deep Louisiana. Houses on stilts, trawlers moored, herons on fence posts. This is the Louisiana tourists never see—the one of fishermen and Cajun communities living at water level.

activities.text: Watch the shrimp boats on the bayou - Chauvin is a shrimping community. The colorful boats moored along the bayou are a spectacle in themselves. If you're there in spring, the Blessing of the Fleet is an annual event that brings the Cajun community together at the sculpture garden.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Louisiana Road Trip: Jazz and Bayous

places.name: Cheyenne

visits.text: The Wyoming State Capitol's gold-leafed dome has been gilded five times since 1900 - from the north it floats above the city like something European. Inside, the first-floor rotunda has checkerboard marble floors and a cherry-wood staircase shipped from Ohio. The building was finished in 1890, the same year Wyoming became the 44th state, and the second-floor chamber is where delegates voted to enshrine women's suffrage in the state constitution - Wyoming women had held that right since 1869, five decades before the 19th Amendment. Self-guided tours are free, Monday through Friday during business hours.

Cheyenne was nothing but open plains until 1867, when Union Pacific drove its tracks west and the city appeared in a matter of months. That story is inside the Cheyenne Depot Museum, housed in the original 1886 railroad station - a National Historic Landmark. The second floor holds a wood-carved Big Boy locomotive model and a full-scale model railroad display; labels cite their sources like footnotes, which is unusual and worth slowing down for. Plan 90 minutes if you read everything.

The Cheyenne Frontier Days Old West Museum holds one of the largest collections of horse-drawn carriages in the country and thousands of rodeo artifacts tracing the event to its first day: September 23, 1897, when Union Pacific agent Frederick W. Angier organized a one-day cowboy roundup that drew 4,000 people. By year two, 20,000 showed up. Today it draws 300,000 visitors over 10 days and pays out more than $1 million in prize money. Worth 60-90 minutes even outside rodeo season.

Thirty minutes north of downtown, the Quebec 01 Missile Alert Facility State Historic Site is the only publicly accessible Peacekeeper Missile Alert Facility remaining in the world. Topside is a self-guided museum of Cold War daily life; underground guided tours take you through the actual launch control room - codes, lockboxes, escape hatches designed to fill with sand after a nuclear blast. The 90th Missile Wing at F.E. Warren Air Force Base still operates Minuteman III missiles today. Free topside; paid entry for the underground tour.

The Wyoming Historic Governors' Mansion at 300 East 21st Street is a Colonial Revival building from 1904 that housed 19 Wyoming First Families - among them Nellie Tayloe Ross, the first female governor in United States history, elected in 1924. Docent-led tours move through rooms decorated to represent different eras of occupancy. Open Tuesday through Saturday; free admission.

The Nelson Museum of the West downtown holds one of the larger collections of saddles, carriages, and Old West memorabilia in the region - cowboys, lawmen, Native Americans, and soldiers across the frontier period. Free admission for most of the year. Allow 60 minutes and combine with a walk through the historic district nearby.

activities.text: Scattered across downtown Cheyenne are dozens of 8-foot cowboy boots painted by local artists, each depicting a different slice of Wyoming history - the Big Boots art trail. Pick up a location map at the Depot Museum visitor kiosk, or call (307) 316-0067 for a cell phone audio tour where artists explain their own work. The main cluster near the historic district takes 45-60 minutes to cover, and you'll see more of the old town than any guided bus tour would show you.

Curt Gowdy State Park sits 24 miles west in the foothills of the Laramie Mountains - granite outcrops, rainbow trout in Granite Springs Reservoir, pronghorn antelope grazing in sight of the trail, and over 35 miles of mountain biking. It's one of the few spots near Cheyenne where the city genuinely disappears. Day use fee applies.

The Cheyenne Street Railway Trolley runs a 90-minute narrated loop of the historic district - railroad quarter, Capitol Hill, historic mansions, Fort D.A. Russell. Quickest way to orient yourself before deciding which buildings to revisit on foot. Runs in summer only; paid entry; departs from Depot Plaza.

Close out the loop at Westby Edge Brewing downtown - local craft beer, the same city where the itinerary started nine days ago. Denver is 90 minutes south on I-25; factor that into your checkout time and don't book a departure before noon.

days_plan.itin_title: Nebraska Sandhills & Pioneer Trails: From Omaha to the Rockies
Wyoming Beyond Yellowstone: Cowboys, Hot Springs & Bighorn Medicine Wheel
Wyoming Beyond Yellowstone: Cowboys, Hot Springs & Bighorn Medicine Wheel

map_keywords: Cheyenne Frontier Days Old West Museum
Quebec 01 Missile Alert Facility Cheyenne Wyoming
Nelson Museum of the West Cheyenne

places.name: Chicago

visits.text: You emerge into Millennium Park from Michigan Avenue and Anish Kapoor's Cloud Gate stops you - 110 tons of polished steel in a giant bean shape reflecting Chicago's skyline and passersby in its curved surfaces, transforming each visitor into an actor in a permanent installation. The park, inaugurated in 2004 after a $325 million budget overrun for a final cost of $475 million, receives over 25 million visitors annually - America's most visited public park. Frank Gehry's Jay Pritzker Pavilion hosts free Grant Park Orchestra concerts every summer evening June through August. Free admission; take the L train to Madison/Wabash rather than searching for parking.

You climb steps guarded by two 1894 bronze lions and enter America's densest museum for major works per square foot. Founded in 1879, the Art Institute holds the world's second-largest collection of Impressionist and Neo-Impressionist paintings after the Musée d'Orsay: Seurat's 'Grande Jatte,' Picasso's 'Old Guitarist,' Edward Hopper's 'Nighthawks,' and Grant Wood's 'American Gothic' are reachable on foot within 10 minutes of each other. Renzo Piano designed the Modern Wing in 2009, adding 264,000 square feet of naturally lit galleries. Open daily 11am-5pm, until 8pm Thursday; admission: $35 adult - book online, weekend lines are long.

You descend stairs from Wacker Drive to Chicago River level and find yourself in another city - a 1.5-mile promenade at the base of Loop skyscrapers, at water level. From this perspective, Willis Tower, the Gothic 1925 Tribune Tower, and Mies van der Rohe's steel-and-glass towers from the 1950s form a complete panorama of modern architecture history in a single glance. Orange water taxis cross your path, and transparent kayaks are rentable at the main dock to explore the river arms. Come in the evening for tower lights reflected in water - the Riverwalk stays lively until 10pm in summer.

You push open the door of what looks like a marble palace at the corner of Randolph and Michigan - and enter for free one of America's most beautiful civic buildings. Built in 1897 as Chicago's first public library, the building holds two halls with 38-foot diameter Tiffany glass domes - American Art Nouveau masterpieces in colored glass that diffuse changing light depending on time of day. Floors and columns are Guatemalan green onyx and Carrara marble. Since its conversion to a cultural center in 1991, admission has been entirely free. The vast majority of tourists walk past without entering - don't make this mistake: it's one of Chicago's most beautiful interiors, open Monday-Friday 8am-7pm.

You cross Hyde Park to the neoclassical building housing the Western Hemisphere's largest science and technology museum, lodged since 1933 in the Palace of Fine Arts from the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition. Its treasure is the German submarine U-505, captured on the high seas in June 1944 by the U.S. Navy - America's first capture of an enemy warship since the War of 1812. The 252-foot submarine has been displayed inside the museum since 1954 in a gallery built around it. Open daily 9:30am-5:30pm; admission: $21 adult - book online to avoid weekend lines.

You get off the L train at Damen and walk on Milwaukee Avenue - lined with BYOB restaurants, alternative bookstores, art galleries, and vintage boutiques in Victorian houses built by Polish and Ukrainian immigrants in the late 19th century. The neighborhood was the center of Chicago's alternative rock scene in the 1990s - the Smashing Pumpkins played their first concerts here. The adjacent Ukrainian Village preserves several 1910s-1920s Ukrainian Orthodox churches with gilded domes, including Holy Trinity Cathedral designed by Louis Sullivan in 1903. Come on weekends for cafe terraces spilling onto sidewalks and farmers' markets in Wicker Park square.

activities.text: You board from the Michigan Avenue dock and the boat ascends the Chicago River under drawbridges succeeding each other every two minutes. In 90 minutes, a Chicago Architecture Center guide describes 50 buildings spanning 130 years of modern architecture - from the world's first skyscraper (Home Insurance Building, 1885, demolished 1931) to the 1964 Marina City tower, whose circular apartments have the shape of a corn cob. Chicago invented vertical architecture because the city was devastated by fire in 1871 and rebuilt in twenty years by architects who had no precedent to respect. Book 48 hours ahead in July-August - cruises often sell out same-morning.

You push open a blues club door and the smell of warm beer and electric amps hits your nostrils - you're in the city that transformed acoustic Delta blues into urban electric blues in the 1940s. Muddy Waters and Howlin' Wolf settled in Chicago after the war, plugged their guitars into amps in Southside clubs, and created a sound that would influence the Rolling Stones and Led Zeppelin. Kingston Mines, open since 1968, is Chicago's oldest continuously operating blues club - two stages alternate bands without ever stopping until 4am. Buddy Guy's Legends (700 S. Wabash) is the most reliable address for guaranteed musical quality.

You grab a Divvy e-bike at Millennium Park and launch onto the 29-mile paved path running along Lake Michigan's shore, with Loop skyscrapers as a permanent backdrop for the first three miles. The Lakefront Trail was honored as one of North America's best bike routes - flat, with public beaches succeeding each other for summer swimming. On clear days from the north shore, you glimpse Indiana dunes 30 miles to the southeast - visually closing the entire road trip loop. Avoid Saturday between 11am and 4pm in summer: the path gets so crowded it becomes dangerous for cyclists.

You start at Gene & Jude's for the Chicago-style hot dog - Vienna Beef sausage in steamed bun, yellow mustard, neon green relish, raw onions, tomatoes, pickles, and sport pepper, but NEVER ketchup, an unyielding rule since opening in 1941. Then you go to Al's Beef (1079 W Taylor Street, open since 1938) for Italian Beef: roasted rump steak sliced as thin as paper, served in a bun dipped directly in garlic-and-herb cooking jus, born in the 1930s in West Side Italian neighborhoods to feed construction workers cheaply. Order each dish in its canonical version: hot dog without ketchup, Italian Beef 'dipped and hot' with spicy giardiniera. These two dishes are Chicago's true popular culinary markers - deep-dish is for tourists, these are for locals.

days_plan.itin_title: Chicago to New Orleans Road Trip: Route 66 & the Mississippi
Chicago to New Orleans Road Trip: Route 66 & the Mississippi
Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica
The Great Lakes Grand Loop: Chicago, Milwaukee, Door County, Sleeping Bear, Mackinac, Pictured Rocks - 12 Days
