places.name: Cincinnati

visits.text: The road trip begins in Cincinnati, where Charlie and Raymond start their journey in Rain Man. Walk across the Roebling Suspension Bridge—it's the prototype for the Brooklyn Bridge, built in 1866 by the same engineer. At 1,057 feet, it was the world's longest suspension bridge at the time. When cars drive over the metal grates, the bridge "sings"—a sound that locals love. The view of Cincinnati's skyline from the middle of the bridge is perfect in the morning light.

Right at the bridge's Ohio-side foot, head to the National Underground Railroad Freedom Center. The museum tells the story of slavery and the clandestine network that helped enslaved people escape to the North. Cincinnati was a critical stop—the Ohio River marked the border between slave states and free states. Allow 90 minutes.

Head up to Over-the-Rhine, the old German neighborhood. The 19th-century brick facades now house craft breweries, galleries, and cafés. Vine Street and Main Street are the liveliest. The neighborhood gets its name from German immigrants who, when crossing the Miami and Erie Canal to work, said they were "crossing the Rhine."
Stop at Fountain Square, Downtown's beating heart. The Tyler Davidson Fountain (1871) is the city's symbol. This is where the opening scenes of Rain Man establish Cincinnati's atmosphere.

activities.text: Swing by Findlay Market, Ohio's oldest covered market (1852). Try Cincinnati chili—a chili served over spaghetti, topped with shredded cheese and onions. Locals call it a "three-way." It's strange and delicious.

days_plan.itin_title: The Rain Man Route: An American Road Trip

map_keywords: National Underground Railroad Freedom Center Cincinnati

visits.text: You turn off Route 66 at Foyil and drive four miles east to find a 90-foot concrete totem pole rising from the back of a sandstone turtle - built entirely by one retired woodworking teacher, Nathan Edward Galloway, who moved to this farm in 1937 and spent eleven years on the project from 1937 to 1948. He collected most of the materials himself from a nearby stream: 28 tons of cement, 6 tons of steel, and 100 tons of sand and rock. The 200 bas-relief images spiraling up the pole include four 9-foot Native American figures near the top and tropical reptiles near the base, drawn from old National Geographic photographs. The adjacent Fiddle House holds hundreds of handmade fiddles - none playable - that Galloway carved at night after long days on the totem. Free admission, open daily.

You pull up to an 80-foot grinning blue whale beached beside a pond - and the story behind it is stranger than the object itself. Zoologist Hugh Davis spent two years welding steel rod and applying hand-mixed cement, one five-gallon bucket at a time, to build this as a surprise 34th-anniversary gift for his wife Zelta, revealed to her on September 7, 1972. Zelta collected whale figurines and had never seen a live one. What started as a family swimming hole became one of Route 66's most photographed landmarks. The City of Catoosa purchased the property in 2021 and a new visitor center opened in early 2026, ahead of Route 66's centennial. Entry is free; the interior of the whale is open for exploration.

You stand on the 20-acre hillside Will Rogers bought in 1911 with plans to retire here. He never made it - he died in an Alaska plane crash alongside aviator Wiley Post on August 15, 1935. His widow Betty donated the land and his papers to Oklahoma, and the memorial opened November 4, 1938, his 59th birthday, built from locally quarried limestone. Route 66 was officially renamed the Will Rogers Highway the same year he died. Inside: 12 galleries of ropes, saddles, radio transcripts, and a theater showing his films. Outside, his tomb reads: 'I never met a man I didn't like.' Twelve galleries take about 90 minutes.

activities.text: You leave Tulsa on the original Route 66 alignment - not the interstate - passing through Catoosa and the string of small towns that kept the Mother Road alive after the bypass. Oklahoma holds more miles of original drivable Route 66 alignment than any other state on the route. Between Tulsa and Claremore you watch for vintage neon signs, original narrow-lane concrete sections, and the turnoff to Catoosa where the Blue Whale waits. Pull over whenever something catches your eye - that's how this road has always worked.

You wind down the day on Claremore's compact Main Street, within walking distance of the Will Rogers Memorial. Hammett House Restaurant has been a Claremore institution since 1969 - classic American fare and homemade pies in a room that hasn't tried too hard to reinvent itself. It's the kind of place where regulars know their server's name and the menu hasn't needed a major overhaul in decades. Book a table if you're arriving on a weekend evening.

days_plan.itin_title: Oklahoma Route 66 & Native Heritage: Tulsa to OKC

visits.text: Three oversized electric guitars rise from a concrete island at the corner of North State Street and DeSoto Avenue - the old intersection of Highways 61 and 49. The sign reads 'The Crossroads.' Whether Robert Johnson really met the Devil here around 1930 is unprovable, but what's documented is the transformation: Son House heard Johnson play in 1930 and described him as near-unlistenable; two years later Johnson returned with a technique that left House speechless. His fewer than 30 recordings became foundational to the blues and influenced everyone from Eric Clapton to the Rolling Stones. Worth a stop, a photo, and a minute of silence.

Inside a 1918 freight depot built for the Yazoo and Mississippi Valley Railroad, the Delta Blues Museum has been running since 1979 - Mississippi's oldest music museum. Its collection centers on the culture that produced the music: the sharecropper cabin where Muddy Waters lived on Stovall Farms sits in the main gallery. Exhibits trace the migration of bluesmen north along Highway 61 to Memphis, St. Louis, and Chicago. Don't miss the listening stations and the artifacts annotated in Son House's own hand. The museum is at #1 Blues Alley Lane. Allow 90 minutes; pick up the printed Clarksdale walking map at the desk.

Ground Zero Blues Club opened in May 2001 in a former cotton-grading warehouse, deliberately unrefurbished. Mismatched chairs, Christmas lights, graffiti walls: it looks like it's never been cleaned up, which is exactly the point. Academy Award-winning actor Morgan Freeman co-founded it with then-Clarksdale mayor Bill Luckett and Howard Stovall to give local musicians a regular venue. Live music runs Wednesday through Saturday, usually from around 9 p.m.; cover is typically $10. Freeman still shows up occasionally. Come for dinner too - the Southern food is genuine.

activities.text: Ten minutes south of downtown, Hopson Plantation is where the first successful mechanical cotton picker was field-tested - the machine that made hundreds of thousands of Delta sharecroppers redundant and set off the Great Migration north. One of the original pickers stands on the property. The old commissary is now a bar; the Shack Up Inn next door rents genuine sharecropper shacks as accommodation. An hour here gives the blues its economic and social backstory more concisely than any museum exhibit.

Red's Lounge on Sunflower Avenue is the real thing: a cinderblock juke joint with a wood-burning stove and no stage, musicians set up in the corner. Smaller, rawer, and more local than Ground Zero, it's where Delta blues residents actually go on Friday and Saturday nights. Red Paden has been running it for decades. No cover most nights, no cocktail menu - cold beer and loud music in a room the size of a living room.

days_plan.itin_title: Mississippi Delta Blues Trail: Memphis to Jackson

visits.text: Clearwater Beach - Sugar Sand and Turquoise Water: Consistently rated among America's best beaches, this Gulf Coast gem offers powdery white sand and the calm, crystal-clear water the region is famous for.

Clearwater Marine Aquarium - Home of Winter the Dolphin: Made famous by the 'Dolphin Tale' films, this rescue facility rehabilitates marine animals while educating visitors about ocean conservation. Meet the animals that inspired the movies.
Pier 60 - Sunset Celebration Capital of Florida: This famous pier extends into the Gulf of Mexico, hosting nightly sunset celebrations with street performers, artisans, and spectacular views.

Caladesi Island State Park - Florida's Most Pristine Beach: Accessible only by boat, this Gulf island remains one of the last undeveloped barrier islands in Florida, offering unspoiled beaches and nature trails.

activities.text: Sunset at Pier 60 - A Nightly Festival: Join locals and visitors for the daily celebration of Florida's legendary Gulf Coast sunsets, complete with entertainment and crafts.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Experience Clemson's charming college-town atmosphere, where the walkable downtown centers on College Avenue with its shops, cafes, and historic character. Outside major university events, the area maintains a relaxed pace perfect for leisurely exploration. The Clemson Area African American Museum presents important regional history through thoughtful exhibits.

Visit Clemson University's beautiful campus, where historic buildings blend with modern facilities amid landscaped grounds. Memorial Stadium and other landmarks reflect the university's prominent role in South Carolina culture. The campus provides pleasant walking paths and architectural interest for visitors exploring the Upstate.

activities.text: Browse local boutiques and galleries reflecting the creative energy of this academic community. The downtown district offers an authentic college-town experience with regional character. Clemson's position near both mountain wilderness and larger cities makes it an excellent base for exploring the Upstate region.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

places.name: Cleveland

visits.text: You enter the building designed by architect I.M. Pei - a 46-meter-high glass and steel pyramid perched on the Lake Erie shore, inaugurated on September 1, 1995, in the presence of Yoko Ono and Little Richard. Cleveland beat New York, Memphis, and Chicago to host this museum in 1986, notably because this is where disc jockey Alan Freed popularized the term "rock and roll" in the 1950s from WJW station. The 14,000 square meters of exhibition space house thousands of artifacts - John Lennon's handwritten lyrics, Michael Jackson's stage costumes, Janis Joplin's Porsche - as well as The Garage, an interactive space where you can play drums, guitar, or keyboards on real stages. Book your time slot online to avoid waiting; plan at least 2.5 hours.

You enter one of the oldest and most vibrant public markets in the United States. The yellow brick building with its 42-meter clock tower was inaugurated in 1912, designed by Hubbell & Benes at a cost of $680,000, but the market's history dates back to 1840 when two Ohio City mayors gave this land to the municipality with the obligation to keep it as a public market forever. Today, 70 merchant families - of Irish, German, Slovenian, Italian, Hungarian, Greek, and Middle Eastern origin - sell meats, cheeses, breads, fish, and flowers in its 100 indoor stalls and 82 outdoor arcade stands. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1973, it's open Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Saturday from 8am.

The Cleveland Museum of Art, founded in 1913 and expanded several times, possesses a collection of 61,000 artworks spanning 5,000 years of human history - and permanent admission is free. Its white marble Beaux-Arts building on the edge of Wade Lagoon in University Circle is flanked by a contemporary glass wing designed by Rafael Viñoly in 2012. The museum's strength lies in its medieval armor, East Asian art, and a beautiful Impressionist section. Gallery One, at the entrance, is an interactive space where you can create your own compositions from works in the collection.

activities.text: The Ohio City neighborhood, just west of downtown Cleveland, is one of the best examples of urban rehabilitation in the American Rust Belt. Around the West Side Market, a concentration of craft breweries, restaurants, and cafes has moved into Victorian houses and converted warehouses. Great Lakes Brewing Company, founded in 1988, is one of Ohio's first craft breweries and its beers - notably the seasonal Christmas Ale - are a local institution. Continuing north along Detroit Avenue, you reach the Detroit Shoreway neighborhood and its independent art galleries.

Cleveland's North Coast Harbor stretches along Lake Erie's southern shore - the fourth largest of the Great Lakes by surface area but the smallest by volume and the shallowest, making it the most storm-sensitive. The promenade takes you between the Rock Hall, the Great Lakes Science Center, and Voinovich Bicentennial Park, with views of the harbor and the Erie Islands in the distance. In summer, sailboats and kayaks crisscross the bay; in winter, the lake can freeze along its shores and the view is spectacular.

days_plan.itin_title: Pittsburgh to Detroit: Steel, Rock & Motown - 9 Days Across the Great Lakes

map_keywords: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame Cleveland
West Side Market Cleveland Ohio City

visits.text: Cocoa Beach - The Authentic Florida Surf Town: This laid-back community has never strayed from its beach-town roots. From Ron Jon Surf Shop to classic Florida motels, the area welcomes visitors to experience genuine coastal living.

Ron Jon Surf Shop - The World's Most Famous Surf Shop: This 52,000-square-foot temple to beach culture operates 24 hours a day, 365 days a year, selling everything from surfboards to souvenirs across two floors.

activities.text: Surfing the Space Coast - Catch Your First Wave: Consistent Atlantic swells make Cocoa Beach one of the best places in Florida to learn surfing, with numerous schools offering lessons for all ages and skill levels.

Rocket Launch Viewing from the Beach - Front Row to Space: Many beachfront locations offer unobstructed views of launches from Kennedy Space Center, combining beach time with the spectacle of spaceflight.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

places.name: Cody

visits.text: Five museums share one roof at the Buffalo Bill Center of the West, 720 Sheridan Avenue - a Smithsonian Affiliate since 2008 and the oldest museum complex dedicated to the American West, founded in 1917. The five: Buffalo Bill Museum (Wild West show artifacts and personal effects), Cody Firearms Museum (7,000 individual firearms, the most comprehensive collection in the US), Whitney Western Art Museum (Remington, Russell), Draper Natural History Museum (Yellowstone geology and wildlife), and Plains Indian Museum (Arapaho, Lakota, Crow and Cheyenne objects; first curator was George Horse Capture of the A'aninin tribe). Your ticket is valid for two consecutive days.

Old Trail Town on the western edge of Cody is 26 historic frontier buildings gathered from original sites across Wyoming by archaeologist Bob Edgar, starting in 1967. The collection includes a fur traders' cabin from 1883, a dance hall, a saloon, and the grave of mountain man John 'Liver-Eating' Johnston. One of the few places in the West where the structures are original, not reconstructed. Paid entry; open May through September.

The Irma Hotel on Sheridan Avenue opened in November 1902 - Buffalo Bill Cody's own hotel, built for $80,000 and named after his youngest daughter. At the center of the dining room sits a cherrywood back bar said to have been a gift from Queen Victoria in recognition of a Wild West show command performance at her court; the hotel's current owner acknowledges there is no documentary proof, but the bar has been here since opening day. Walk in for a drink or a meal without booking; the Cody Gunfighters do a free shootout on the front sidewalk at 6 p.m. nightly in summer.

activities.text: Attend the Cody Nite Rodeo at Stampede Park - every single night from June 1 through August 31 since 1938, making Cody the only town in the United States with a nightly rodeo. Former Wild West show performer Carly Downing founded it, originally calling it the 'Pup Rodeo.' The 6,000-seat arena sits on a terrace above the Shoshone River west of downtown; gates open at 7:00 p.m., rodeo starts at 8:00 p.m. A bus departs from most hotels for ~$7 round-trip. Paid entry; book ahead in peak summer.

West of Cody, US-14/16/20 follows the North Fork of the Shoshone River through 52 miles of Shoshone National Forest that Theodore Roosevelt called 'the most scenic 50 miles in the United States.' Dramatic volcanic formations line both sides. No Yellowstone entrance fee applies if you turn around at the East Gate - back in Cody in 2-3 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Wyoming Beyond Yellowstone: Cowboys, Hot Springs & Bighorn Medicine Wheel

map_keywords: Buffalo Bill Center of the West Cody Wyoming

places.name: Colorado Springs Co

visits.text: Park at the Garden of the Gods Visitor Center and set out on foot—the park has been free since 1909, when Charles Perkins bequeathed it to Colorado Springs with one condition: "free forever." Its 300-million-year-old red sandstone formations were tilted vertical by the same tectonic uplift that pushed up Pikes Peak. Cathedral Spires, Kissing Camels, Balanced Rock—everything is within a 10-minute walk.

Drive up Pikes Peak via the Pikes Peak Highway—19 miles of paved road from Cascade to 14,115 feet. The song America the Beautiful was composed after a climb to the summit in 1893. At the top, the cafeteria serves donuts fried at 14,115 feet. Toll $15/person, allow 2.5 hours round-trip.

activities.text: Before heading back to Denver (1h15 on I-25) or Colorado Springs airport, take one last bike ride through Garden of the Gods. The park offers marked bike trails among the red formations—Pikes Peak as a backdrop, free entry, and 6 million visitors a year who'll prove you right for coming back.

places.name: Columbia Sc

visits.text: Congaree National Park is 18 miles southeast of Columbia. Don't be fooled: it's not a swamp, it's a floodplain. Floods from the Congaree River cover about 80% of the park ten times a year and that's precisely what feeds these giant trees - the park harbors champion specimens of 15 species, including a Loblolly pine over 167 feet tall. It's the largest old-growth bottomland forest in the United States, designated a UNESCO biosphere reserve. Start at the Harry Hampton Visitor Center to check boardwalk conditions.

Take the Boardwalk Loop (2.4 miles, accessible): the elevated walkway first crosses a mixed forest of pines and hardwoods, then descends into the low section where bald cypresses and their conical knees emerge from the flooded ground. Weston Lake, a former river meander at least 20 feet deep, is accessible from the boardwalk - watch the banks: alligators have been spotted. Bring mosquito repellent: not optional.

If you have the energy, the Weston Lake Loop (4.6 miles) pushes deeper into the forest and follows oxbow lakes where vegetation is especially dense. This is where wildlife photographers wait for herons, kingfishers, and woodpeckers - eight different woodpecker species live in the park.

At the corner of Main and Gervais Streets, the South Carolina State House bears visible scars: look for the bronze stars on the exterior façade. They mark the exact spots where artillery shells from General Sherman's troops struck the building during the burning of Columbia in February 1865. The African American Monument in the grounds traces the history of Black South Carolinians from the arrival of the first enslaved people.

The South Carolina State Museum occupies a former cotton mill from 1893, listed on the National Register of Historic Places. Four floors cover natural history, state history, science, and art. A digital planetarium, observatory, and 4D theater are integrated into the building. Allow 2 hours to tour it properly.

The Hampton-Preston Mansion (Historic Columbia) is one of Columbia's oldest surviving houses. The guided tour doesn't just recount the planter family - it also explores the lives of the enslaved people who ran the property. It's one of the most honest approaches to Southern history in the city.

activities.text: The Cedar Creek Canoe Trail (20 marked miles) is one of the best ways to enter Congaree: you paddle under a canopy of cypresses and tupelos, in black water colored by tannins. Kayaks and canoes rent in Columbia for around $50 per day. Allow 3 to 4 hours for a round trip on the main section. Check water levels before you go.

Evening in Columbia, the Vista District is the liveliest neighborhood for dinner. The local barbecue sauce is mustard-based - very different from Louisville's bourbon barbecue. Try Doc's BBQ for the classic version.

Riverbanks Zoo & Garden is regularly ranked among the ten best zoos in the United States: nearly 2,000 animals on a site crossed by the Saluda River, with a suspension bridge over the rapids. The giraffe feeding station and Mingle with Marsupials are the most popular. Plan a minimum of 3 hours, more with children.

If you're here on a Saturday, the Soda City Market on Main Street closes the road from 9am to 1pm. Up to 5,000 people gather: arepas, Korean bulgogi, bratwursts, Caribbean jerk chicken - Columbia counts residents from over 200 countries. It's the best quick introduction to South Carolina's capital city diversity.

days_plan.itin_title: Louisville, Mammoth Cave & Congaree - Caves and Swamps of the Deep South
Louisville, Mammoth Cave & Congaree - Caves and Swamps of the Deep South

map_keywords: Congaree National Park Harry Hampton Visitor Center

places.name: Columbus Ga

visits.text: The CSS Jackson is 225 feet of ironclad hull sitting in a museum at 1002 Victory Drive - the largest surviving Confederate warship in the world, salvaged from the Chattahoochee River mud in the 1960s. It was built at the Confederate Naval Shipyard less than a mile from here, nearly finished when General Wilson's cavalry captured Columbus in April 1865 and set it ablaze. The National Civil War Naval Museum opened around it in 2001 - the only institution in America dedicated entirely to the naval war. Also here: a full-scale replica of Admiral Farragut's flagship USS Hartford, and an actual cutter from that same ship. Paid entry.

Francis Joseph Springer, an immigrant from Alsace, opened his opera house at 103 10th Street on February 21, 1871. It was immediately the finest theater between New York and New Orleans. Edwin Booth played Hamlet here in 1876. Oscar Wilde lectured. Tom Thumb performed. Ma Rainey sang. Buffalo Bill came through. By 1959 the building was a movie house and nearly demolished five years later before a preservation campaign saved it. President Carter made it Georgia's State Theater in 1971, its centennial year. One of seven theaters in America with National Historic Landmark status. Tours Monday and Wednesday at 3:30 p.m., $5 per person.

Thirty miles north on US-27, Callaway Gardens covers 2,500 acres of the Pine Mountain foothills - opened May 21, 1952 by Cason J. Callaway on exhausted former cotton land. The Cecil B. Day Butterfly Center opened inside it on September 25, 1988: 7,300 square feet of glass dome, North America's largest tropical butterfly conservatory, maintained at 80°F year-round with over 1,000 butterflies of 50-plus species in free flight. Named for the founder of Days Inn, funded by his wife Deen Day Sanders. Blue Morpho butterflies arrive in spectacular numbers every September. Paid entry.

activities.text: The Columbus Riverwalk connects the Naval Museum, the Columbus Iron Works Convention Center - second-largest iron producer in the Confederacy, completed in 1853 - and the 26-block National Register Historic District. On Broadway, a marker stands at the former pharmacy of Dr. John S. Pemberton, a Confederate Army captain who invented Coca-Cola in 1886. Cobblestone streets, old brick mill buildings, the Chattahoochee rolling past: this stretch of Columbus does more work than most history museums.

The Callaway Brothers Azalea Bowl and Chapel Trail is a 2.4-kilometer loop past the 1962 Ida Cason Callaway Memorial Chapel - fieldstone walls, wooden beams, 16th-century Gothic lines - and down to Mountain Creek Lake. A Pioneer Log Cabin from the 1830s, moved here from Troup County in 1959, sits on the path. For longer miles, the 10-mile paved Discovery Bike Trail links every major attraction. Robin Lake's one-mile white sand beach is open in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia Beyond Atlanta: Music, Gardens & the Deep South

map_keywords: National Civil War Naval Museum Columbus Georgia
Callaway Gardens Butterfly Center Pine Mountain Georgia

places.name: Columbus Oh

visits.text: You stroll beneath the 17 illuminated arches of High Street, the visual signature of the Short North - a district that didn't exist before 1980. In the 1970s, this stretch of High Street north of downtown was nothing but a succession of abandoned buildings with boarded-up windows. It was local police who gave the neighborhood its name: 'Short North', because it lay north of downtown and short of the Ohio State University campus. The renaissance began with a 'cooperative opening' of galleries in 1984, now known as the Gallery Hop and held every first Saturday of the month. Today, over 300 businesses animate this mile of High Street, most of them independent. Come on the first Saturday of the month for the Gallery Hop, or midweek to avoid the weekend crowds.

You enter the largest privately funded historic residential district in the United States to be entirely listed on the National Register of Historic Places: 233 acres of red brick houses built between 1840 and 1880 by German immigrants who then constituted 30% of Columbus's population. World War II marked the beginning of decline - the neighborhood became shameful to bear a German name - and demolitions began in the 1950s. In 1960, a group of residents founded the German Village Society, paid out of pocket for restoration, and secured historic protection in 1963. The result is remarkable: original brick-paved streets, walled back gardens, and the Book Loft, a bookstore housed in a labyrinth of 32 rooms in connected houses. Arrive early to wander before the patio crowds arrive.

Founded in 1876 and housed since 1995 in a former red brick warehouse, the North Market is Columbus's oldest continuously operating public market. About 35 permanent merchants sell Ohio-local products: artisan cheeses, grass-fed sausages, honey from urban apiaries, vegetables from Franklin County farms and Ohio's Appalachian region. On Saturday mornings, the outdoor market brings together over 60 seasonal producers. It's the best time to buy provisions for the road - grab some local cheeses and sausages for upcoming picnics in Hocking Hills.

You push open the doors of the Columbus Museum of Art - founded in 1878, one of the oldest museums in the United States still operating in its original city - and immediately encounter the Wonder gallery, inaugurated in 2016: 700 square meters dedicated to creativity and imagination, the first permanent space of its kind in the world in a museum. The permanent collections gather 11,000 works including Picasso, Hopper, and Matisse, as well as paintings by George Bellows - a Columbus native, major figure of the Ashcan School. Admission is free on Sunday: if you can time your visit to this slot, it's the best time to go without spending the usual $18.

You enter beneath the glass canopies of the Franklin Park Conservatory - a grand greenhouse built in 1895 in the Crystal Palace style of the 1893 Chicago World's Fair - and find yourself wandering among seven different biomes under 13 acres of glass. Then you look up at the chandelier dominating the main hall: 1,400 pieces of glass blown by Dale Chihuly, the colors changing with daylight. Chihuly installed a permanent collection of his sculptures here, from orange glass trumpets emerging from water basins to cobalt blue glass towers lining the walkways. In the outdoor Japanese garden - 1.2 hectares created for Columbus's bicentennial in 1812 - allow at least thirty additional minutes.

You descend toward the Scioto River right in the heart of Columbus and discover the Scioto Mile - 3.2 kilometers of linear park inaugurated in 2011 after a $38 million investment. From Broad Street bridge, you have before you the photo of Columbus: the Statehouse crowned with its stone drum as backdrop, the Scioto reflecting evening lights, pedestrian walkways connecting both banks. In summer, cafés and food trucks set up on the riverbanks and free concerts happen on weekends. Rent a kayak or pedal boat at the Paddleboat Quarry to see the skyline from the water - it's the angle postcards don't use, and it's the best.

You walk on The Oval - Ohio State University's central esplanade - surrounded by red sandstone collegiate Gothic buildings, and realize you're on the largest university campus in the United States by student enrollment: 61,000 enrolled in 2023. The Thompson Library, completed in 1913 and restored in 2009, raises its neo-Romanesque facade at the north corner of the Oval - enter to see the 12-meter-high atrium lined with marble. The university was founded in 1870 on land donated by William Neil, whose farm covered the entire area of what is today Victorian Village and the campus. If contemporary architecture interests you, don't miss the Wexner Center for the Arts designed by Peter Eisenman in 1989 - one of the first American deconstructivist buildings, all grids and colliding forms.

You drive 20 minutes north of Columbus and find yourself facing two burial mounds of the Hopewell culture - earthen mounds carefully constructed between 100 BC and 500 AD by a people who built networks of geometric enclosures throughout the Ohio Valley without ever knowing the wheel or metal tools. One of the mounds is 65 meters in diameter. You enter the reconstructed burial chamber and try to imagine the ceremonial: bodies wrapped in hides, offerings of copper from Lake Superior and mica from North Carolina. Archaeologists Squier and Davis excavated these mounds in 1845-1847 - their work constituted the first serious scientific study of Ohio's pre-Columbian cultures. The attached museum displays their discoveries with original artifacts.

You park your car in a quiet residential neighborhood in northwest Columbus, descend a 1.5 km trail through a wooded ravine - and suddenly you hear the water before you see it. Hayden Falls plunges 12 meters over a sandstone escarpment surrounded by a forest of beeches and tulip trees that the suburban zone hasn't managed to swallow. It's one of the last preserved riparian forests in Franklin County, ten minutes from downtown Columbus, and almost no outside traveler knows it. Neighborhood residents jealously guard the secret - you'll understand why as soon as you're there. On weekdays, you have a good chance of being there alone.

activities.text: You enter one of the rare American capitols without a dome - Columbus's Greek Revival building, whose construction lasted twenty-two years (1839-1861), is topped with a stone drum pierced with windows instead of the usual dome. The central rotunda features a stained glass ceiling and a mosaic of Ohio's Great Seal. The Senate chamber and House chamber retain their original 19th-century furnishings. The Statehouse has been listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1972. Self-guided tours allow free access to all public rooms.

You push open the doors of COSI - Center of Science and Industry - a 320,000-square-foot glass building perched on the edge of the Scioto River, ranked among the ten best science museums in the United States. You move from one gallery to another: space, energy, health, Ohio's natural history, with dozens of hands-on experiments to manipulate rather than observe. The 300-seat digital planetarium projects shows every hour - book upon entry or you'll miss your time slot. The glass walkways overlooking the Scioto from the second floor offer the best free view of Columbus.

You enter the Brewery District via Sycamore Street and find yourself in a former neighborhood of ice warehouses and 19th-century industrial breweries, converted into an alignment of taprooms in red brick. Push open the door of Elevator Brewery & Draught Haus: you're in an 1897 building with cast-iron columns that was successively a brewery, a warehouse, and an elevator factory - the original pulleys and rails are still on the ceiling. Barley's Brewing Company, opened in 1992, was Columbus's first microbrewery; their rack of wooden casks behind the bar has a saloon vibe that fits the neighborhood perfectly. Over a dozen tasting venues within a 500-meter radius - take your time.

You stroll along the mile of High Street on the first Saturday of the month starting at 6pm and understand why the Short North Gallery Hop has become one of the most attended cultural events in the United States. In 1984, a few visionary gallerists organized a first 'cooperative opening' in half-empty premises - the neighborhood was then blighted. Forty years later, over 300 establishments simultaneously open their doors: openings with artists present, musical entertainment on sidewalks, cocktails in shops. Even if you're not there on a first Saturday, the Short North's permanent galleries remain open during the week - check their individual hours on the Short North Alliance website.

You pass through the entrance of Columbus Zoo and have before you 220 acres divided into seven geographic regions - Africa, Asia, Americas, Australasia - with 9,000 animals and a freshwater aquarium inaugurated in 1999 among the largest in the Midwest. This zoo, founded in 1927, was the first in the world to breed gorillas in captivity: the gorilla Colo was born there in 1956, a world event at the time. Jack Hanna, director from 1978 to 1992, transformed the establishment from a decrepit zoo into a world reference institution for species preservation. Plan a full day: lines lengthen from mid-morning on summer weekends. Buy your ticket online to skip the ticket office.

You grab a bike from CoGo Bike Share - over 50 stations in Columbus, a few dollars for a half-day - and head north on the Scioto Greenways, a network of over 100 miles of trails entirely separated from traffic. The section along the Scioto from downtown to Griggs Dam, 16 km north, is the most spectacular: you follow the river between natural prairies, riparian forests where herons fish at low tide, and parks whose lawns descend to the water. Griggs Dam, built in 1906, forms an artificial lake of 8 km surrounded by wooded cliffs - total serenity less than 30 minutes from downtown Columbus. It's the best way to end this Ohio loop.

days_plan.itin_title: Interior Ohio Loop - From Columbus to Mohican Gorges
Interior Ohio Loop - From Columbus to Mohican Gorges
Interior Ohio Loop - From Columbus to Mohican Gorges

map_keywords: Short North Arts District Columbus Ohio
Columbus Zoo and Aquarium Powell Ohio

visits.text: Jour 1: Kenai River Corridor - The world-famous Kenai River flows from Kenai Lake through Cooper Landing and on to the Cook Inlet, supporting legendary salmon runs that attract anglers from around the globe. The river's turquoise waters, colored by glacial flour, wind through a forested valley with mountain views.
Jour 1: Kenai Lake - This 24-mile-long glacial lake fills a stunning mountain-framed basin, offering scenic beauty and recreational opportunities including boating, paddling, and shoreline hiking.

Jour 2: Kenai Peninsula Fisheries - The rivers and lakes of this region support some of the world's most productive salmon fisheries, with five Pacific salmon species returning in runs that can number in the millions. The Kenai River holds the world record for king salmon at 97 pounds, drawing anglers seeking trophy catches.

Jour 2: Russian River Confluence - The junction of the Russian and Kenai rivers creates one of Alaska's most popular fishing destinations, with sockeye salmon runs so abundant that combat fishing, with anglers standing shoulder to shoulder, has become a cultural phenomenon.

activities.text: Jour 1: Scenic Kenai River Float - Gentle rafting trips drift through the upper Kenai River canyon between Kenai Lake and Skilak Lake, passing through pristine wilderness with opportunities to observe eagles, moose, bears, and spawning salmon depending on the season. Guides share information about the region's natural and human history.

Jour 2: Guided Salmon Fishing - Professional guides provide boats, equipment, and expertise for pursuing king salmon (May-July), sockeye salmon (June-August), and silver salmon (August-September) on the Kenai River and its tributaries.
Jour 2: Trout and Char Fishing - Beyond the famous salmon runs, the region supports excellent populations of rainbow trout and Dolly Varden char, with fishing opportunities throughout the season.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords

places.name: Copper

visits.text: Jour 1: Wrangell-St. Elias National Park Visitor Center - The primary information facility for America's largest national park, this complex introduces the 13.2-million-acre wilderness through exhibits on the region's volcanic geology, massive glaciers, and historic copper mining heritage. The center serves as the gateway to understanding this UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Jour 1: Ahtna Cultural Center - Adjacent to the visitor center, this museum preserves and shares the heritage of the Ahtna Athabascan people who have inhabited the Copper River basin for thousands of years. Exhibits include a traditional fish wheel demonstrating the ingenious technology used to harvest salmon runs.

Jour 2: Copper River Basin Wilderness - This remote region offers access to some of Alaska's most pristine river systems, flowing through valleys flanked by glaciated peaks exceeding 16,000 feet. The watershed supports all five species of Pacific salmon, making it world-renowned among fisheries biologists and anglers alike.

activities.text: Jour 1: Copper River Valley Orientation - The tiny community of Copper Center sits at the junction of the Copper and Klutina rivers, surrounded by the Wrangell, Chugach, and Alaska mountain ranges. This strategic location has made it a crossroads for indigenous traders, gold seekers, and modern travelers alike.

Jour 2: Copper River Rafting - Float trips through this wilderness basin range from calm scenic journeys suitable for families to challenging whitewater adventures. The glacially-fed rivers wind through landscapes of extraordinary beauty, with wildlife viewing opportunities throughout.

Jour 2: Salmon Fishing Expeditions - The Copper River produces some of the world's most prized salmon, with king, sockeye, and silver runs occurring throughout summer. Guided excursions provide equipment and expertise, with catch processing and shipping services available.

Jour 2: Klutina River Fishing - Tributary streams offer additional angling opportunities in more intimate settings, with runs of salmon and resident populations of rainbow trout and Dolly Varden char.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords

map_keywords: Wrangell-St. Elias National Park Visitor Center

visits.text: USS Lexington (CV-16) is an Essex-class aircraft carrier commissioned in February 1943. At 872 feet long, she participated in virtually every major Pacific naval operation between 1944 and 1945, racking up so many missions that the Japanese declared her sunk four separate times - hence her nickname 'The Blue Ghost'. She remained in service until 1991, making her the longest-serving Essex-class carrier in all of American naval history. Since 1992, she's been permanently moored on Corpus Christi's waterfront. Five self-guided circuits cover 250,000 square feet spread across 11 decks. Allow a good two hours.

The Texas State Aquarium, facing the USS Lexington, focuses exclusively on Gulf of Mexico and Caribbean fauna. You'll observe bottlenose dolphins, sharks, rays, sea turtles and coastal birds in tanks recreating their natural habitats. Operators run dolphin-watching trips from the neighboring dock - the animals are often visible from the jetty's edge without even boarding.

The bronze statue of Selena Quintanilla-Pérez stands on the Mirador de la Flor, on Corpus Christi's waterfront. Selena, nicknamed the 'Queen of Tejano Music', grew up here and was murdered in the city on March 31, 1995, at age 23. Her death triggered national mourning in Hispanic communities. The small Selena Museum, run by her family, traces her career a few streets away.

activities.text: Padre Island National Seashore stretches 70 miles of coastline between Corpus Christi and Port Mansfield - it's the longest undeveloped barrier island in the world. Beyond the first Malaquite Beach parking lot, the track becomes sandy and requires a 4x4 with tires deflated to around 15 psi. Don't leave without checking fuel level and bring water: there are no services for the next 60 kilometers. In return, you'll have the beach to yourself. From June through August, park rangers organize releases of young Kemp's ridley hatchlings - the world's most endangered sea turtle - at Malaquite Beach around 6:45 AM. Call the Hatchling Hotline at (361) 949-7163 for times.

days_plan.itin_title: Gulf Coast: Lafayette to South Padre Island - 9 days

places.name: Cortez Co

visits.text: Book your Cliff Palace tour in advance on recreation.gov ($5/person): 150 rooms and 23 kivas in a sandstone alcove, built between 1190 and 1270 by the Ancestral Puebloans. In 1888, two cowboys lost in a snowstorm were the first Europeans to see it. The hour-long guided tour descends into the site via stone ladders—exactly as its occupants did.

For Balcony House, prepare yourself: a 32-foot ladder planted in the cliff, then a 2-foot-wide tunnel to crawl through—exactly how the Puebloans entered their homes to defend themselves. The guided tour lasts 1 hour. Not recommended for the claustrophobic, highly recommended for everyone else.

Stop by the Chapin Mesa Archeological Museum before your guided tours: the 30-minute intro to 1,400 years of Puebloan history—ceramics, tools, reconstructions—radically changes how you read the sites. Just behind, Spruce Tree House (3rd largest site in the park) is accessible without a guide.

Take the Mesa Top Loop Road (6 miles) for the remains of 10th-century villages—well before the cliff dwellings. Park Point, the park's highest point at 8,571 feet, offers a 360° view of the Four Corners states.

activities.text: Finish the day on the Petroglyph Point Trail (3 miles, loop) that follows the canyon walls to a panel of petroglyphs carved in the rock. It's one of the few trails accessible without a guide in the Chapin Mesa area—pick up the map at the museum before leaving.

In the evening in Cortez, sit down at Absolute Bakery & Café—the reference table in town for a simple, local dinner. If curiosity strikes, the Four Corners Monument is 40 miles west: the only place in the United States where four states meet at a point.

days_plan.itin_title: Colorado-Wyoming Road Trip: National Parks, Rockies & Wide Open Spaces

map_keywords: Cliff Palace Mesa Verde National Park
Mesa Top Loop Road Mesa Verde

visits.text: You walk out of the 1881 Spring Hill Ranch visitor center and the wind hits you immediately - this is what 170 million acres of North America once felt and sounded like before the plow came. Established on November 12, 1996 as the 370th unit of the National Park System, this 10,894-acre preserve protects the only National Park unit devoted entirely to the tallgrass prairie. Less than 4 percent of the original grassland survives today, and nearly all of it is here. The three-story German bank barn on site has 19,000 square feet of storage space; the 1882 Lower Fox Creek schoolhouse is a short walk uphill. A bison herd roams the pastures; over 40 miles of trails cross the rolling ground.

Stand at the base of Broadway Street and look up: the 113-foot red mansard roof of this 1873 courthouse lords over the entire town. Architect John G. Haskell - also the first architect of the Kansas State Capitol - designed it for 600 residents who approved a bond issue in 1871. Workers quarried local limestone by hand and hauled blocks weighing up to seven tons by horse-drawn wagon from a nearby quarry. Inside, a three-story walnut spiral staircase rises from logs cut along the Cottonwood River. Self-guided tours run Monday-Friday, free of charge.

Walk the three-block main street between the stone bridge over the Cottonwood River and the courthouse steps and you are in a town whose limestone went into the U.S. Capitol building in Washington, D.C. By 1885, six quarries near here were shipping magnesian limestone by rail to every state in the country. Art galleries, antique stores, and a few restaurants line the street. On select summer Friday evenings, the Emma Chase Café hosts Friday Night Music, with local fiddlers performing on the porch.

activities.text: Tune your radio to 1680 AM before you leave town - three transmitters along Highway 177 broadcast the history, ecology, and fire management of the Flint Hills as you drive. The 47-mile route from Council Grove south to Cassoday passes through uninterrupted tallgrass prairie with no billboards and almost no buildings. Spring burns (typically April) turn entire hillsides orange at night; the hills are vivid green through June and rust-red by October. Five miles south of Cottonwood Falls, a designated scenic overlook gives the best panoramic view of the rolling rangeland.

Take the Southwind Nature Trail (1.75 miles) and you will pass the bison pasture without needing a guide - just check the NPS website for current herd locations before you go. The preserve has over 40 miles of trails total, from short loops near the historic buildings to the 3.5-mile Two Section Trail that climbs to a scenic overlook. Spring and early summer bring prairie wildflowers in waves: purple coneflower, prairie turnip, and Indian paintbrush. Mornings are best for birding; greater prairie-chickens are visible near the preserve in spring.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Flint Hills & Frontier: Wichita to Dodge City

places.name: Council Bluffs

visits.text: You climb to a 1936 monument perched on a Loess Hills bluff, marking the exact spot where Lewis and Clark held their August 2, 1804 council with Otoe and Missouri tribal leaders. From this height, the Missouri River unfurls below, Council Bluffs sprawls to the east, and the Omaha skyline stretches west across the water. The carved stone relief depicts the historic meeting; interpretive panels flesh out the story of that first diplomatic encounter on the Corps of Discovery's journey. Visit in early morning or late afternoon when the light softens and the heat relents.

You step into Council Bluffs' 1905 Carnegie Library - a Beaux Arts gem worth the visit on its own - which houses a museum that opened May 10, 2003, precisely 134 years after the transcontinental railroad's completion. The exhibits trace more than 150 years of American railroading, from the Pacific Railroad Act signed by Lincoln in 1862 through today's continental freight operations. Interactive displays and a detailed HO-scale model railroad share floor space with historical photographs, original documents, and artifacts that bring the iron-horse era to life. Open Thursday through Saturday; confirm hours before you go.

About 15 miles north of Council Bluffs, this 1,268-acre preserve delivers a true walk through the Loess Hills - a nearly unique landform found only here and in northwest China, sculpted from windblown glacial silt that ridges up to 300 feet above the Missouri River floodplain. Ten miles of trails run from easy boardwalks to steep ridge scrambles. The 45-foot HawkWatch observation tower ranks among the top 25 raptor-monitoring sites in North America, tallying an average of 13,000 migrating raptors each year from September through December. Weekday mornings see the fewest visitors; vehicle fee applies.

activities.text: The Loess Hills Scenic Byway stretches more than 200 miles along the Missouri's eastern bank. You don't need the full route - even the northern stretch between Crescent and Council Bluffs reveals why these wind-sculpted ridges matter: razor-edged crests, remnant tallgrass prairie, and sweeping views over the river valley. Follow it south from Hitchcock Nature Center back into town for a fitting natural close to the day.

The blocks around Pearl Street reward a short walk on foot. You pass the Golden Spike monument marking the city's role as the eastern anchor of the transcontinental railroad. The RailsWest Museum and the preserved home of General Grenville Dodge - Union Army engineer and Union Pacific's chief surveyor - both sit within a few minutes' drive. Solid dinner options line the downtown streets, and the whole area is compact enough to cover on foot in an hour.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa Backroads: From the Missouri Bluffs to the Mississippi Cliffs

map_keywords: Union Pacific Railroad Museum Council Bluffs

visits.text: Crater Lake National Park — Marvel at America's deepest lake filling the caldera of Mount Mazama volcano that collapsed 7,700 years ago. The 594-meter-deep lake's impossibly blue water results from exceptional purity and depth absorbing all colors except blue.

Rim Drive — Circle the lake on this 53-kilometer scenic road climbing to over 2,100 meters with 30 viewpoints. The summer-only drive showcases constantly changing perspectives of Wizard Island, Phantom Ship, and surrounding peaks.

Wizard Island — Photograph this 230-meter cinder cone rising from the lake's west side, created by volcanic eruptions after the caldera formed. Boat tours allow hiking to the summit crater during summer months.

Watchman Peak Trail — Hike 1.6 kilometers to this fire lookout offering the best sunset views over Crater Lake. The relatively easy trail climbs 128 meters to panoramic vistas encompassing the entire caldera.

activities.text: Crater Lake Lodge visit — Stop at this 1915 rustic lodge perched on the caldera rim with floor-to-ceiling windows framing lake views. The National Historic Landmark preserves original stone fireplaces and craftsman-style architecture.

Stargazing at dark sky — Observe the Milky Way arcing overhead in one of America's darkest national parks. The high elevation and remote location create exceptional conditions for astronomy and meteor shower viewing.

Ranger talk — Attend evening programs explaining the volcanic geology, formation, and unique ecology of Crater Lake. Rangers share the Klamath tribe's legend of Llao and Skell battling to create the sacred lake.

days_plan.itin_title: Oregon Natural Wonders: Crater Lake to Pacific Coast Road Trip

visits.text: Turkey Run State Park - Indiana's second state park (1916), Turkey Run conceals sandstone gorges carved by glacial waters 20,000 years ago. Trail 3 is the most spectacular: 2.7 km through Bear Hollow, a narrow canyon where you'll climb wooden ladders bolted into the rock. The "Punch Bowl" on this same trail is a cylindrical hole carved by swirling ice blocks. Bring shoes with good traction - the rocks are slippery even in dry weather.

Shades State Park - 8 km northeast of Turkey Run, along Sugar Creek. The ravines are narrower and more shaded. Less crowded than its neighbor, the park offers a wilder atmosphere. The trail to Silver Cascade Falls is worth the detour after rain.

Parke County Covered Bridges - Parke County counts 31 of them, a national record. They date from the 19th century, when the roof protected the wooden planks from rain and snow. The Narrows Covered Bridge, at the end of Turkey Run's trail 5, is one of the most photogenic.

activities.text: Combined hike on trails 3, 5, and 9 at Turkey Run - The grand loop of 7.5 km chains together the park's three most renowned trails: ladders, canyons, Falls Canyon waterfall, and the "Ice Box," a rock overhang where the air remains cool even in midsummer. Allow 3 to 4 hours with photo stops.

Sugar Creek descent by canoe or kayak - Outfitters offer trips from 5 to 21 km on the river that traverses both parks. From the water, you'll see the sandstone cliffs from another angle. Weekend reservations recommended.

days_plan.itin_title: Indiana Circuit: Dunes, Caves, and Cities

visits.text: Crystal River National Wildlife Refuge - The Manatee Capital of the World: Every winter, hundreds of manatees gather in the warm spring waters here, creating the most accessible manatee-watching experience on Earth.

Three Sisters Springs - Snorkel with Manatees: This unique opportunity allows swimmers to enter the water with these gentle giants, observing them feeding, resting, and socializing in their natural winter habitat.

activities.text: Manatee Snorkeling Tours - Face to Face with Florida's State Marine Mammal: Licensed operators guide small groups into the springs for close encounters with manatees, an experience unavailable anywhere else on the planet.
Kings Bay Kayaking - Paddle Through Crystal Waters: Explore the spring-fed bay and its network of tributaries where manatees, dolphins, and sea turtles share the remarkably clear waters.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Discover Cumberland Island, Georgia's largest and wildest barrier island where 17 miles of pristine beaches stretch beneath towering dunes and maritime forests of moss-draped oaks. Wild horses descended from colonial-era stock roam freely across 36,000 acres of protected wilderness, creating unforgettable encounters along empty shorelines. The island remains deliberately undeveloped, accessible only by ferry from St. Marys.

Explore the haunting Dungeness Ruins, crumbling remains of Thomas Carnegie's Gilded Age mansion slowly being reclaimed by resurrection ferns and climbing vines. The grand Plum Orchard Mansion showcases meticulously preserved Carnegie-era architecture and furnishings. The historic First African Baptist Church and Stafford Plantation cemetery document the island's complex history of enslaved people and freed African Americans.

activities.text: Hike or bike over 50 miles of trails winding through moss-draped maritime forest, past salt marshes teeming with wildlife, and along windswept beaches where loggerhead sea turtles nest each summer. Limited ferry capacity and minimal facilities preserve the island's remote, mysterious character for those willing to plan ahead.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

places.name: Custer Sd

visits.text: Before you go down, read this figure: 95% of all known boxwork formations in the world are found in this cave. These calcite honeycomb structures lining the walls—like mineral honeycombs 60 to 100 million years old—exist virtually nowhere else on Earth. Theodore Roosevelt made it the world's first underground park in 1903. Since then, 170 miles of passages have been mapped, and speleologists estimate probably as many more remain to be explored.

Choose the Garden of Eden Tour if you want to see the formations without exhausting yourself: 1 hour, quarter-mile, 150 steps, intermediate level. If you're adventurous, the Historic Candlelight Tour descends into unimproved sections by candlelight—$17, 16+ only, reservation required. Either way, bring a jacket: the cave stays at 53°F year-round.

On the surface, don't leave without walking 30 minutes on the Elk Mountain Trail. Wind Cave protects one of only four genetically pure bison herds in North America—along with Yellowstone, Utah's Henry Mountains, and Alberta's Elk Island. You'll often see them from the trail, at a safe distance. Stay on the path.

Custer State Park begins just to the north—71,000 acres, 1,500 free-ranging bison. Sylvan Lake, framed by pink granite, is where Doane Robinson—South Dakota state historian—got the idea in 1923 to carve faces into the Black Hills. He was looking at the Cathedral Spires above the water. This is where it all began. Swim in the lake if you're here in summer; the water is crystal clear.

activities.text: The Wildlife Loop Road runs 18 miles through the park's open prairies—drive slowly, windows down, preferably morning between 7-9am or evening before sunset. Bison, deer, pronghorn, and wild burros (descendants of animals abandoned by 1874 miners) often cross the road. No guarantees, but the success rate is very high.

Needles Highway (SD-87): 14 miles of road winding between pink granite needles, passing through tunnels dynamited through solid rock and opening onto views you won't see anywhere else. Stop at Eye of the Needle. Warning: the road is narrow—max tunnel height 11'3", width 8'7". RVs and trailers, skip this one.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Hills & Badlands: Road Trip Through the Heart of the American West

map_keywords: Wind Cave National Park Visitor Center Hot Springs South Dakota
Wind Cave Garden of Eden Tour
Wildlife Loop Road Custer State Park

places.name: Cuyahoga Valley Peninsula

visits.text: You discover the most popular waterfall in Cuyahoga Valley National Park - a 20-meter cascade over sandstone slabs flowing into Brandywine Creek, forming a turquoise pool at the bottom of a forested hollow. The 2.5-kilometer loop trail leading from the parking lot passes the Brandywine Inn house, dating from 1848, which served as a stop on the old canal route. The national park itself, designated a national recreation area in 1974 then promoted to national park status in 2000, extends over 33,000 acres between Cleveland and Akron along the Cuyahoga River - a name of Mohawk origin meaning "crooked river." Arrive early morning for mist on the falls; parking often fills by mid-morning on weekends after 10am.

You follow one of the park's most remarkable ecological resilience stories: this marsh was recreated in the 1980s by a beaver colony that spontaneously moved into the ruins of an abandoned auto park after industrial waste had been cleaned up. The beavers dammed a small stream, transforming wasteland into a wetland teeming with life - great blue herons, wood ducks, kingfishers, and raccoons now live here side by side. A wooden boardwalk lets you observe the entire ecosystem without disturbing it.

You walk or bike on one of the best-preserved towpaths in the United States - the former towpath of the Ohio & Erie Canal, built between 1825 and 1832 over 496 kilometers to connect Lake Erie to the Ohio River. Before the canal, Ohio was a transportation wilderness; after its construction, the state's agricultural products could reach eastern markets in days. Interpretive signs dot the 34 kilometers of trail in the park, explaining how mules towed boats at 4 km/h from this same path less than two centuries ago.

activities.text: The Cuyahoga Valley Scenic Railroad offers historic train excursions that follow the Cuyahoga River through the entire length of the park, from Akron to Rockside Road in the north. It's the most contemplative way to see the park's oak, maple, and beech forests without crossing roads - especially in autumn when colors invade the hillsides. The train is particularly popular with cyclists who board from a northern stop and pedal back down the Towpath Trail. In autumn, book several weeks in advance.

The Ritchie Ledges are sandstone formations carved by retreating glaciers more than 10,000 years ago - moss and fern-covered cliffs and crevices that contrast with the surrounding forest. In the 19th century, these rocks were already a playground and picnic spot for Cleveland and Akron residents. The trail passes Ice Box Cave, a narrow gorge where the temperature remains near freezing even in midsummer thanks to cold air trapped between the sandstone walls.

days_plan.itin_title: Pittsburgh to Detroit: Steel, Rock & Motown - 9 Days Across the Great Lakes

map_keywords: Brandywine Falls Cuyahoga Valley National Park
Cuyahoga Valley Scenic Railroad Peninsula Ohio

places.name: Dallas

visits.text: Dealey Plaza and the Sixth Floor Museum: it was from the sixth floor of this red-brick building - the former Texas School Book Depository - that Lee Harvey Oswald fired at Kennedy on November 22, 1963. The museum, opened in 1989 and operated as a nonprofit, preserves over 90,000 items related to the assassination. You'll see the reconstructed sniper's nest, the life-size FBI model of Dealey Plaza used by the Warren Commission, and the teletype machine that announced the president's death to the world. Plan to book your tickets online - the museum is only open Wednesday through Sunday.

Dallas Museum of Art: founded in 1903, the DMA ranks among the ten largest art museums in the United States, with over 24,000 works spanning 5,000 years of human creativity - from ancient Egypt to contemporary American art. Permanent collection admission has been free since 2013: you have no excuse to skip it. Don't miss the Level 1 pre-Columbian collection or the Level 2 Impressionists - Monet, Degas, Van Gogh. The building itself, designed by Edward Larrabee Barnes and opened in 1984, is worth the visit.

Reunion Tower GeO-Deck: the geodesic sphere perched 561 feet (171 m) high has been Dallas's signature landmark since 1978. The GeO-Deck observation platform sits at 143 m - count on 65 seconds in the elevator. On clear days, you can see Fort Worth 50 km away. Inside, a 16-meter interactive screen called the Halo displays a 3D map of Dallas with information about the city's landmarks.

Pioneer Plaza: right in the heart of downtown, this plaza features 49 life-size bronze sculptures depicting a herd of Texas Longhorns crossing a stream. If you want a quintessentially Texan photo without driving 50 km out of town, this is your spot - the sculptures are full-scale and kids can climb on them. It's also one of the few places in Dallas where you grasp why the region earned the nickname 'Cowtown.'

Bishop Arts District: in the North Oak Cliff neighborhood, about a hundred independent shops, restaurants, galleries and bars occupy perfectly preserved 1920s buildings. It's the ideal place to finish this circuit - street art murals line the facades, and if you want one last BBQ or Texas craft beer, you'll find what you're looking for without searching.

Perot Museum of Nature and Science: designed by architect Thom Mayne (Morphosis) and inaugurated in 2012, this concrete cube perched above ground on columns is one of Dallas's most daring buildings. The 11 permanent galleries cover paleontology (a fine hall on Texas dinosaurs), geology, space and human sciences. Easily count on 2 hours if you're curious - the interactive experiences absorb far more time than you'd anticipate.

activities.text: Live music night in Deep Ellum: Dallas's blues, jazz and rock club district has been around since the 1920s - Blind Lemon Jefferson played here before becoming a legend. The massive murals on colorful building facades are unmissable even if you never step inside a bar. Arrive after 9 PM to catch local bands in full swing, and go Thursday or Friday for the best lineups.

Bike or walk around White Rock Lake: the 15 km cycling loop follows the lake shores in a setting of oaks and cypresses - it's Dallas's Central Park. Outfitters offer bike rentals at the boathouse on the north shore. Early morning, herons and ducks are omnipresent and the lake is perfectly calm. Count on 1.5 hours at a leisurely bike pace or 3 hours on foot.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Texas: Hill Country, Big Bend & Gulf Coast Beaches
Texas Triangle: Dallas, Austin, San Antonio & the Hill Country - 8 Days
Texas Triangle: Dallas, Austin, San Antonio & the Hill Country - 8 Days

map_keywords: Perot Museum of Nature and Science Dallas
White Rock Lake Dallas bike trail

places.name: Davis Wv

visits.text: You're making a detour to Spruce Knob en route to Davis - it's on the way from Seneca Rocks via Route 33, and it would be a shame to pass within 15 km without climbing to West Virginia's highest point. At 1,482 meters elevation, the summit vegetation - spruces twisted by prevailing westerly winds, stunted into flags all pointing in the same direction - resembles Canadian taiga more than the surrounding Appalachian forests. On clear days, the observation tower offers a 360° panorama of the Allegheny Mountains. Arrive early in the morning to see the valley mists before they dissipate.

You're descending the 214 wooden stairs to the Blackwater Falls observation platform: the river plunges 17 meters over a Pennsylvanian sandstone escarpment, tinted a characteristic amber-brown by tannic acid released from red spruce and hemlock needles fallen into the water. It's this singular color that gave the river - and the park - its name. Illustrator David Hunter Strother was the first to describe and sketch the falls for a wide audience, in 1852. Come early or late in the day for raking light on the gorge - the canyon runs eight miles long.

You're emerging at Lindy Point after 0.3 miles of easy walking from the parking area, and you're discovering what many consider the most beautiful view in West Virginia: from a ledge at 915 meters elevation, you overlook Blackwater Canyon in its full width, with the park's white lodge below and the Alleghenies stretching to the horizon. In October, the red leaves of maples, orange hickories, and yellow birches cover the canyon flanks in a spectacular spectrum. This overlook is particularly photogenic in late afternoon when the setting sun's light enters the canyon from the west.

activities.text: You're following the Elakala trail from the lodge on a five-minute walk, and you're discovering a series of four tiered waterfalls totaling 40 meters in height, carved into the walls of Blackwater Canyon. Shay Run water tumbles from ledge to ledge through vegetation of ferns, hemlocks, and abundant mosses that creates a high-altitude rainforest atmosphere. Elakala Falls is much less crowded than the main falls - you have good chances of finding yourself alone, even in high season, if you come in the morning.

You're walking along the canyon's south rim for several miles with views plunging to the amber river snaking 200 meters below. The canyon is geologically young - the river has carved this 8-mile chasm in less than a million years through an Appalachian sandstone plateau. You're crossing forests of balsam firs whose oldest specimens are 200 years old, species usually confined to Canada and the Northeast's high peaks. The best time is morning, when the mists from the canyon floor slowly rise toward the ridges.

days_plan.itin_title: West Virginia Loop - From the Appalachians to New River Gorge

map_keywords: Blackwater Falls State Park Davis WV

visits.text: Daytona Beach - The World's Most Famous Beach: Twenty-three miles of hard-packed white sand have earned this Atlantic destination its legendary status. This is one of the few beaches where you can drive directly onto the sand and park steps from the surf.

Daytona International Speedway - The World Center of Racing: Home to the Daytona 500 and numerous NASCAR events throughout the year, this 2.5-mile tri-oval track also hosts concerts and special events. Tours take visitors behind the scenes of racing history.

Daytona Beach Boardwalk and Pier - Classic Americana: This oceanfront landmark features arcades, rides, shops, and restaurants stretching along the shore, anchored by the historic pier extending into the Atlantic.

activities.text: Beach Driving - A Florida Tradition Since 1903: Early automobile pioneers raced on this hard-packed sand, and today visitors can experience the unique thrill of parking their vehicle right on the beach with waves crashing nearby.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

places.name: Deadwood Sd

visits.text: The first thing that strikes you about Mount Rushmore isn't the size—it's that it was done without a single fatality. Between 1927 and 1941, Gutzon Borglum and 400 workers dynamited 450,000 tons of granite while dangling in mid-air from 3/8-inch steel cables. The four faces each measure 60 feet. Arrive before 9am—after that, the tour buses roll in. In the evening, the lighting ceremony led by rangers is free and often surprisingly moving.

Seventeen miles away, the Crazy Horse Memorial has been under construction since 1948—and that's precisely what makes it fascinating. Lakota chief Henry Standing Bear wrote to sculptor Ziolkowski in 1939: "My people want the white man to know that the red man has great heroes too." The finished sculpture will stand 563 feet tall and 641 feet long. The face (287 feet) was completed in 1998, the left hand in 2023. Not a penny of federal funding—everything comes from ticket sales and private donations.

Deadwood emerges at the bottom of a wooded gulch, and Main Street still looks like 1876. The entire town is designated a National Historic Landmark. On August 2, 1876, Wild Bill Hickok sat here, at Saloon No. 10, back to the door for the first time in his life. Jack McCall shot him in the back of the head. Hickok was holding black aces and black eights—the Dead Man's Hand, known worldwide ever since. McCall was acquitted the next day by a miners' court, then retried and hanged.

The Adams Museum (100 Sherman Street) is free and worth an hour. Photo archives from when the town had 25,000 people, recreation of Hickok's card game, history of the 1875 gold rush—everything is well told, without bad taste. It's the best antidote to the slightly too-kitsch reenactments on Main Street.

Mount Moriah Cemetery sits on the hill above town, perched among the pines. Wild Bill Hickok is there, metal bust on his grave. Calamity Jane rests 20 inches away—she'd asked to be buried near him before dying in 1903. Seth Bullock, Deadwood's first sheriff and future close friend of Theodore Roosevelt, is higher up the slope. The view over the entire valley from the top is worth the climb.

Take US-14A north from Deadwood and let Spearfish Canyon pull you in. Limestone cliffs rise 1,500 feet above Spearfish Creek, covered in ponderosa pines and aspens. In fall, the colors are strikingly vivid. Stop at Bridal Veil Falls and Roughlock Falls—two waterfalls accessible from roadside in less than 5 minutes' walk. Total: 20 miles of scrolling scenery.

In Lead (pronounced "Leed"), the Homestake Mine operated from 1876 to 2001—the most productive in North America, with more than 1,200 tons of gold extracted over 125 years. Today, 8,000 feet beneath your feet, the Sanford Underground Research Facility uses the old galleries for dark matter detection experiments. The surface guided tour explains how a 19th-century open-pit mine became a particle physics laboratory.

Sturgis has 7,000 residents the rest of the year—and in August, between 400,000 and 700,000 bikers descend for the Sturgis Motorcycle Rally, held continuously since 1938. Outside rally time, Main Street keeps its leather shops and legendary bars. The Sturgis Motorcycle Museum & Hall of Fame is worth an hour: the event's history is well documented, and some machines on display are remarkable.

activities.text: At Saloon No. 10 (657 Main Street), actors reenact Wild Bill's death daily—1pm, 3pm, 5pm, and 7pm, May through September. Settle in, order a beer, watch McCall shoot the greatest gunslinger of the Wild West in the back. It's touristy, unapologetic, and honestly well done. Free entry.

Since 1989, gambling has been legal in Deadwood and slot machines and blackjack tables have invaded the historic hotels on Main Street. The Bullock Hotel (633 Main Street), built in 1895 by Seth Bullock himself, is the most distinctive place to end the evening: the building is intact, and the sheriff's ghost—they say—still roams the hallways.

The Spearfish Canyon Trail follows the river for several miles before climbing to the plateau for a plunging view of the gorge. Hiking shoes and water—the trail can be slippery near the waterfalls. Spearfish Creek water is cold and clear: in July-August, some people swim in the pools. Free parking at Roughlock Falls.

On Upper Main Street in Deadwood, the Broken Boot Gold Mine is a real 1876 mine converted into an attraction. Tunnels, ore carts, and panning technique—a few flakes guaranteed in your pan. Genuinely fun with kids, and honest about what real gold miners' lives were like in 1876.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Hills & Badlands: Road Trip Through the Heart of the American West
Black Hills & Badlands: Road Trip Through the Heart of the American West

map_keywords: Mount Rushmore National Memorial Keystone South Dakota
Crazy Horse Memorial Custer South Dakota
Spearfish Canyon Scenic Byway South Dakota

visits.text: Death Valley National Park - The Hottest, Driest, Lowest Place in North America: This vast desert wilderness holds the world record for highest reliably recorded air temperature (134°F). Yet within its 3.4 million acres, life persists in remarkable adaptations, and landscapes of surreal beauty unfold.

Badwater Basin - Stand at 282 Feet Below Sea Level: Hexagonal salt flats stretch toward distant mountains at the lowest point in North America. The basin supports a unique ecosystem including the endemic Badwater snail.

Zabriskie Point - Sunrise Over Martian Badlands: These eroded mudstone formations glow gold and amber as first light strikes their contours, creating one of the most photographed desert scenes on Earth.

Mesquite Flat Sand Dunes - Saharan Splendor in California: These undulating dunes near Stovepipe Wells rise up to 100 feet, their pristine surfaces erased and redrawn by desert winds. Dawn and dusk light transforms them into sculptural masterpieces.

activities.text: Artist's Palette - A Mountain Painted in Minerals: This hillside showcases nature's full palette through oxidized metals creating stripes of pink, green, purple, and gold across barren slopes.

Racetrack Playa - The Mystery of the Sailing Stones: Rocks weighing hundreds of pounds leave long tracks across this remote dry lakebed, moved by a rare combination of ice, wind, and water only recently explained by science.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Vesterheim Norwegian-American Museum—The largest museum of Norwegian heritage outside Norway. Across 12 buildings, it traces the history of Norwegian immigrants who settled this valley starting in the 1850s. Collections of rosemaling (decorative painting), textiles, farm tools, and a recreated farmstead. Plan 90 minutes.

Dunnings Spring—Right in downtown Decorah, this 200-foot waterfall drops from a limestone cliff into a shaded ravine. A staircase and walkway bring you to the base of the falls. It's a 5-minute walk from downtown—perfect for a cool-off break.

Decorah Fish Hatchery—This state trout hatchery, nestled in a valley, raises rainbow and brown trout. The spring water feeding the pools is crystal-clear. Admission is free and kids love watching the fish. Right next door, a bald eagle nest is streamed live by webcam—Decorah became famous thanks to these eagles.

Ice Cave—Just outside Decorah, this natural rock cavity holds ice year-round thanks to a natural ventilation system. Cold air descends into the cave in winter and stays trapped. In mid-summer, you'll see ice formations while it's 85°F outside. A short trail leads from the parking area.

Phelps Park—On the heights above Decorah, this park offers views across the Upper Iowa River valley and its limestone bluffs. A trail descends to a rustic stone arch bridge, photogenic and surrounded by ferns.

activities.text: Stroll downtown Decorah—Water Street is lined with independent shops, cafés, and craft breweries. The town has youthful energy despite its small size (8,000 people), thanks to Luther College, which has been here since 1861.

Biking or walking the Trout Run Trail—This 11-mile loop circles Decorah following the Upper Iowa River, passing bluffs, prairies, and woods. It's one of Iowa's most beautiful bike trails. Bike rentals available in town.

Canoeing or kayaking the Upper Iowa River—The river winds between 100-foot forested limestone bluffs. Outfitters offer rentals with shuttle service. The Decorah-to-Bluffton section (9 miles) is the most popular—plan 3 to 4 hours at a relaxed pace.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa and Midwest Road Trip: Great Plains Loop
Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs
Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs

places.name: Delaware Water Gap

visits.text: The Delaware Water Gap is where the Delaware River carved a spectacular breach through the Appalachian ridge, separating Mount Minsi in Pennsylvania (1,460 feet) from Mount Tammany in New Jersey (1,527 feet) across less than a mile at river level. This natural passage has been used for millennia by Lenape Native American populations, then by European settlers who traced the first road in 1793 - today followed by I-80. The National Recreation Area, created in 1965 on 70,000 acres, attracts more than 4 million visitors per year, making it one of the most visited national parks in the United States. Admission is free and the park is open 24/7, allowing you to enjoy dawn light on the river.

Raymondskill Falls is Pennsylvania's tallest waterfall, with a total drop over three tiers measuring approximately 150 feet - practically as much as Niagara Falls seen from the side, as park rangers like to point out. It's located at the northern end of the recreation area, near Milford. The access trail from the parking lot is short but steep for 400 meters; it provides views of all three successive tiers from the top. In spring, after April rains, flow is at its maximum. In summer, volume is lower but the forest environment of beech and hemlock pines makes the falls equally beautiful.

Millbrook Village is one of the few 19th-century villages preserved within an American national park. Founded in the 1830s around a grist mill, the village has a dozen restored buildings - forge, general store, church, barn - that give a concrete sense of rural life in last century's New Jersey. The National Park Service organizes old-fashioned days on summer weekends with artisans in period costume. The village is on the New Jersey side of the park, accessible from Old Mine Road, the oldest commercial road in the United States according to some historians, dating from the early 17th century.

activities.text: Mount Tammany is the Delaware Water Gap's signature hike, and deservedly so. The Red Trail starts yards from the I-80 bridge and climbs directly into the forest over exposed rocks. The 1,527-foot summit offers the most photographed view in the park: the Delaware Water Gap breach from above, with the river winding 1,300 feet below and Pennsylvania forests as far as the eye can see. More than 260 bird species have been recorded in the park, and peregrine falcons have nested on Milford cliffs since 2018, for the first time in 70 years. Arrive in the morning to avoid the crowds and have the views to yourself.

The Delaware is the East Coast's free-flowing river - one of the few major American rivers without a dam from end to end, earning its status as a 'National Scenic and Recreational River.'
