Canoeing through the park offers a unique perspective on the Water Gap from water level, with wooded cliffs rising on both sides. Several outfitters in Delaware Water Gap Borough and Dingmans Ferry offer half-day trips with return shuttle. Water is generally at its lowest in summer (ideal for beginners) and highest in spring after snowmelt.

days_plan.itin_title: Pennsylvania Loop: From Amish Country to the Appalachians - 11 Days

map_keywords: Delaware Water Gap National Recreation Area

places.name: Demopolis

visits.text: Gaineswood at 805 South Cedar Avenue is one of the more unusual buildings you will see on this trip. General Nathan Bryan Whitfield started with a two-room dogtrot cabin in 1843 and spent the next 18 years redesigning and expanding it into a Greek Revival mansion - acting as his own architect, drawing from architectural treatises and his travels north. He was also a musician and inventor: the barrel organ he designed, which plays from large cylinders with pins, is still functional. The two front rooms have matching domed ceilings with cupolas; the mirrors in the ballroom are angled to reflect each other to infinity. By 1860, Whitfield owned 235 enslaved people and the plantation produced 600 bales of cotton a year. The house cannot be separated from that labor. Tours run with local docents who know every detail. Admission charged.

Bluff Hall, a ten-minute walk from Gaineswood, sits on a white limestone cliff above the Tombigbee River - that is where the name comes from. The Federal-style house was built in 1832 by merchant Allan Glover as a wedding gift for his daughter and her husband, cotton plantation owner Francis Strother Lyon. The Marengo County Historical Society has restored roughly 75 percent of the authentic furnishings. The textile and clothing collection is one of the stronger holdings. On the grounds, a log cabin saved from a demolition site was found to date to the original French expatriate Vine and Olive Colony settlement of 1817.

Lyon Hall on Main Street was built between 1850 and 1853 by enslaved workers for George Strother Gaines - the same Indian trader who originally owned the land Gaineswood was built on. The connection between these three properties maps a dense network of land ownership, political power, and forced labor in a small corner of the Alabama Black Belt. Check local hours before visiting.

activities.text: Demopolis City Landing on the Tombigbee River gives you a different angle on this town. The river was the economic artery that carried Demopolis cotton to Mobile and from there to global markets. A fifteen-minute walk along the riverfront connects the antebellum architectural legacy of the town to the water that made it possible. Fishing boats still use the landing; it's a quiet place in late afternoon.

Foscue Creek Park, just west of town on U.S. 80, offers a short walk down to the water - a good way to clear your head after a day of house museums. The park is operated by the Army Corps of Engineers and has a boat ramp and picnic area along the river. It's a place where the landscape reasserts itself.

days_plan.itin_title: The Black Belt: Atlanta to New Orleans Through the Deep South

map_keywords: Gaineswood National Historic Landmark Demopolis Alabama

visits.text: Jour 1: Denali National Park Visitor Center - The primary orientation point for this six-million-acre wilderness preserve, featuring comprehensive exhibits on the park's ecosystems, geology, and the cultural history of the Athabascan people who have inhabited this region for millennia. Rangers provide daily programs and essential information for exploring the park's single 92-mile road.

Jour 1: Denali Park Road (First 15 Miles) - The only section accessible to private vehicles, this scenic drive passes through taiga forest and offers the first glimpses of Denali when skies are clear. Pullouts at Savage River provide hiking access and wildlife viewing opportunities.

Jour 2: Tundra Wilderness Tour (62 miles) - This seven to eight-hour narrated bus journey penetrates deep into Denali's wilderness, crossing Polychrome Pass with its multicolored volcanic formations and descending to the Toklat River basin. The route traverses prime habitat for grizzly bears, wolves, caribou, Dall sheep, and moose, with knowledgeable drivers stopping for wildlife sightings.

Jour 2: Kantishna Experience (92 miles) - The full-day twelve-hour expedition travels the entire length of the park road to the historic mining district of Kantishna. This immersive journey maximizes wildlife viewing opportunities and reaches the most remote accessible areas of the park, with Wonder Lake offering iconic Denali reflections on calm days.

Jour 2: Eielson Visitor Center - Located at mile 66, this sustainable facility perched at 3,500 feet elevation provides panoramic views of Denali and the Muldrow Glacier. Exhibits explain glaciology and the mountain's unique weather patterns that make summit visibility a treasured occurrence.

activities.text: Jour 1: Denali Flightseeing - Aerial tours departing from the park entrance reveal the immense scale of the Alaska Range, with glaciers flowing from Denali's flanks and vast expanses of untouched wilderness stretching to every horizon.

Jour 1: Nenana River Rafting - The Nenana River paralleling the park entrance offers both scenic float trips through pristine canyon scenery and exhilarating Class III-IV whitewater sections through Nenana Canyon.

Jour 1: Guided Wilderness Hiking - Naturalist-led excursions venture into roadless areas of the park, teaching backcountry navigation and wildlife awareness while exploring tundra ecosystems rarely seen by casual visitors.

Jour 2: Transit Bus System - The National Park Service operates a hop-on, hop-off shuttle allowing independent exploration. Visitors can disembark at any point to hike unmarked wilderness routes, then catch a later bus to continue deeper into the park or return to the entrance.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords

places.name: Denver

visits.text: Begin at Denver Union Station in the heart of LoDo. This Beaux-Arts building in carved granite opened in 1914 - exactly one hundred years before its reopening as a cultural hub in 2014. At its peak in the 1940s, the station welcomed over 50,000 people daily, including thousands of soldiers heading to World War II. Theodore Roosevelt and Franklin Roosevelt passed through here, as did Queen Marie of Romania. Today it houses a mix of restaurants, shops, and the Crawford Hotel on the upper floors. The Great Hall on the ground floor remains open to all - visit in the evening when the lighting is superb.

From there, walk up to Larimer Square, Denver's first historic district, whose 1870s brick facades were saved from demolition in the 1960s by a visionary developer, Dana Crawford. The street concentrates independent restaurants and bars in a preserved setting - it's one of the few places in Denver where you'll truly feel the city's history.

End the evening on the 16th Street Mall, the mile-long pedestrian artery running through downtown. The Daniels & Fisher Tower, built in 1910, rises 249 feet here - it was then the tallest building west of the Mississippi. Perfect for dinner and surveying the surrounding neighborhoods before heading to altitude tomorrow.

Take the Peak-to-Peak Scenic Byway (CO-7 then CO-72) to descend toward Denver - it's Colorado's oldest scenic route, created in 1918, covering 55 miles of ridgelines between Estes Park and Black Hawk. The road passes aspen forests, abandoned mining villages, and offers direct views of Longs Peak (14,259 feet), the park's highest point. In autumn, it's a spectacle of golden leaves. Stop in Nederland at 5,600 feet elevation for a coffee break before descending to Boulder.

Boulder, Pearl Street Mall - this several-block pedestrian street in old Boulder concentrates restaurants, independent shops, and street musicians. The University of Colorado is nearby, giving the area a young, international atmosphere. Stop here for lunch before continuing toward Denver.

En route to Denver, make a detour to Red Rocks Amphitheatre in Morrison (9 miles southwest of Denver), even if you don't have a concert tonight. The site is freely accessible outside events. Two red sandstone monoliths 300 feet tall - Ship Rock and Creation Rock - frame the stage and naturally absorb sound, making it one of the world's finest natural acoustics. The 9,525-seat amphitheater was built by the CCC in the 1930s; the Beatles played here in 1964, U2 in 1983 (the 'Under a Blood Red Sky' concert). Climb to the top for views of Denver and the eastern plains.

Use this final day in Denver to explore what you missed on arrival. The Denver Art Museum holds one of the country's most important Native American art collections - the building itself, a prism-shaped extension designed by architect Daniel Libeskind and opened in 2006, is worth seeing from outside. Allow at least 2 hours to tour it.

The RiNo (River North Art District) neighborhood is Denver's liveliest area for murals and craft breweries. Denver counts over 400 breweries in Colorado, and several of the best known have their taprooms in this district. Stop by late afternoon before the airport.

activities.text: Prepare for your ascent to altitude: Denver sits at 5,280 feet (the famous 'Mile High City'). If you're coming from the coast, plan to drink plenty of water starting tonight - altitude sickness can appear above 8,000 feet, and Trail Ridge Road exceeds 12,000 feet. A night in Denver to acclimate before heading higher is genuinely useful.

Morning run or yoga at City Park - Denver's largest park (330 acres) offers direct views of the Rockies on clear days. It's also home to the Denver Museum of Nature and Science and the Denver Zoo if you're traveling with family.

days_plan.itin_title: Rocky Mountain National Park Loop from Denver
Rocky Mountain National Park Loop from Denver
Rocky Mountain National Park Loop from Denver
Nebraska Sandhills & Pioneer Trails: From Omaha to the Rockies

map_keywords: Peak to Peak Scenic Byway Colorado

visits.text: Drop your bags at Denver Union Station—built in 1914 in Beaux-Arts style and restored in 2014, this is where the train from the airport arrives. Beneath the grand glass atrium, local bars and restaurants have replaced the old ticket counters. All of LoDo is walkable from the front steps.

Dedicate at least two hours to the Denver Art Museum—one of the most important Native American art collections in the United States, with over 18,000 works across two buildings. The Frederick C. Hamilton Pavilion designed by Daniel Libeskind, inaugurated in 2006, is worth the stop for its architecture alone. The Western American and pre-Columbian collections are outstanding.

Climb the west steps of the Colorado State Capitol to stand exactly one mile high—5,280 feet above sea level in the famous Mile High City. The gilded dome, re-leafed in 1980 with 200 ounces of Colorado gold, gleams for miles on clear days. Free guided tours on weekdays.

End your day at Larimer Square, Denver's oldest block: red brick facades from the 1870s, award-winning restaurants, and lively patios on a single city block. Arrive after 6 PM for the atmosphere at its peak—this is the spot for your first-night dinner.

activities.text: Take a walk through RiNo (River North Art District): massive murals cover the facades of old industrial warehouses, and taprooms line a few blocks. Denver has more than 100 breweries in the city—it's one of America's craft beer capitals. An hour on foot is enough to hit three or four.

Walk the 16th Street Mall, a mile-long pedestrian artery with a free shuttle every 5 minutes. Hop on and off at will to explore neighborhoods, food trucks, and street musicians—everyday Denver, not the guidebook version.

days_plan.itin_title: Colorado-Wyoming Road Trip: National Parks, Rockies & Wide Open Spaces

places.name: Des Moines

visits.text: You approach the only five-domed capitol in the United States - construction ran from 1871 to 1886, topped by a central dome sheathed in 23-karat gold leaf that rises 275 feet above the city. Inside, 29 types of imported marble line the walls and floors, while 12 native Iowa hardwoods appear throughout the legislative chambers and corridors. Free docent-led tours run Monday through Saturday, covering the Governor's Suite, the law library, both legislative chambers, and the historic Supreme Court chamber. Arrive early on a weekday for the quietest experience.

The museum's centerpiece is a full-skeleton replica of a Hebior mammoth - a species whose bones have been unearthed from Iowa's glacial soil. Permanent galleries trace Iowa's story from pre-statehood territorial days through the Civil War - in which 76,000 Iowans fought for the Union - to the present, with a recent addition covering Iowa's surprising film history. Free admission, free parking in the garage across Grand Avenue. Of the museum's 80,000-plus artifact collection, roughly 1,500 are on display at any given time.

You cross into Western Gateway Park, where this 4.4-acre sculpture park opened in 2009 thanks to a gift from Des Moines art collectors John and Mary Pappajohn - the largest single donation in Des Moines Art Center history. More than 30 works by artists including Louise Bourgeois, Jaume Plensa, and Ai Weiwei populate the green space. Plensa's towering 'Nomade' figure greets you at the entrance. Free admission; open sunrise to midnight year-round.

Built between 1923 and 1928 for businessman Carl Weeks, this 42-room, 22,000-square-foot manor was modeled directly on the 15th-century King's House in Salisbury, England. English oak woodwork and roof timbers dating to the Elizabethan era were imported whole for the construction. Inside you encounter fine art, rare manuscripts, historic musical instruments, and tapestries assembled over decades of passionate collecting. The surrounding gardens are equally impressive. Admission charged; tours run on scheduled days.

What began as a 1939 greenhouse has grown into a seven-acre botanical center in downtown Des Moines. The geodesic dome conservatory shelters thousands of plant species from around the globe. Outside, specialty gardens - water garden, hillside garden, Lauridsen savannah - reward unhurried walking. Admission charged; outdoor gardens peak in late spring and early fall.

You drive about 30 miles northeast to Madrid, where this half-mile bridge spans the Des Moines River Valley 13 stories above the water. Steel frames along the span are angled to evoke the underground coal-mine tunnels that once honeycombed Iowa's landscape. At dusk, blue LED lighting transforms the structure into something closer to art installation than trail infrastructure. The trail surface is paved and flat. Free access, open year-round.

activities.text: You walk from the Capitol steps down to Court Avenue through the East Village - independent bookstores, craft breweries, Raygun's sharp Iowa-centric merchandise, and some of Des Moines' best dinner spots packed into a few walkable blocks. Nearly all the neighborhood's historic buildings appear on the National Register of Historic Places. Return in the evening when the bar scene comes alive and runs late.

Three separate buildings designed by Eliel Saarinen (1948), I.M. Pei (1968), and Richard Meier (1985) form a unified museum campus on Grand Avenue. The permanent collection spans American and European modern and contemporary art. The adjacent Clare and Miles Mills Rose Garden wraps around the building's south side, in full bloom June through October, and welcomes visitors free of charge at any hour.

Running Saturdays from May through October, the Downtown Farmers Market takes over Court Avenue with more than 300 local vendors - Iowa-raised produce, artisan goods, prepared foods, and live music filling the street. Arrive before 9am for the best selection and smallest crowds. On non-Saturday mornings, East Village coffee shops offer a slower version of the same local-food experience.

You drive just northwest of downtown into Urbandale, where Living History Farms stretches across 500 acres and recreates Iowa farm life in three distinct eras: a 1700 Ioway Indian farm, an 1850 pioneer homestead, and a working 1900 horse-powered farm. Costumed interpreters work the fields year-round - you might watch them plow, harvest, or butcher livestock depending on the season. Admission charged; allow a full half-day to cover the grounds properly.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa and Midwest Road Trip: Great Plains Loop
Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs
Iowa Backroads: From the Missouri Bluffs to the Mississippi Cliffs
Iowa Backroads: From the Missouri Bluffs to the Mississippi Cliffs

map_keywords: State Historical Museum Iowa Des Moines
High Trestle Trail Bridge Madrid Iowa

places.name: Destin

visits.text: Destin's East Pass was an accident. A 1929 hurricane blocked the original waterway; locals dug a trench and the storm surge widened it overnight into the only direct Gulf outlet from the 13,856 km² Choctawhatchee Bay. That pass now drives tidal water twice a day through a narrow channel - producing the impossible emerald color that fills every Destin photo. Walk the HarborWalk boardwalk along the working fishing harbor: charter boats, open-air restaurants, live music most evenings. Free to enter. Charter fishing season runs March through October.

Henderson Beach State Park exists because someone stopped the bulldozers. The last undeveloped coastal scrub in Destin was acquired by the state specifically to prevent what happened to the rest of the shoreline. The park's surface sand runs up to 350 feet thick - the same quartz that makes every beach on this coast white. A nature trail threads the scrub behind the dunes; 60 campsites connect by boardwalk to the beach. Get to the boardwalk at sunrise before anyone else does. Fee applies.

Crab Island is not an island. It's a submerged sandbar - one to four feet deep at high tide - sitting just north of the Destin Bridge where East Pass meets the bay. The Army Corps created it accidentally in the 1930s when dredging deposited spoil sand in the shallows; by the 1980s boaters had found it and turned it into a floating social scene. Today hundreds of pontoon boats anchor here on summer afternoons. At high tide, Gulf water flooding in through the pass turns the sandbar a Caribbean blue-green that makes every photo look like it was taken somewhere far more exotic.

activities.text: Renting a pontoon from HarborWalk and anchoring at Crab Island is the defining Destin afternoon. The water is 1 to 4 feet deep - enough to wade, float, and let the current work. The color peaks at high tide when Gulf water pushes in through East Pass; check the tide table before you book. Alcohol can't be sold at the sandbar but you can bring your own cooler. Book before 9 a.m. in summer; boats are gone by 10.

The Choctawhatchee Bay dolphin population is large and resident - sunset cruises from Destin Harbor find them reliably from late spring through fall. Most operators run two-hour evening trips departing around 6 p.m. In summer the bay is also nursery habitat for juvenile bull sharks and cobia; a good captain will point them out near the surface. Book at least 24 hours ahead in July and August.

days_plan.itin_title: Florida Panhandle: Tallahassee to Pensacola on the Emerald Coast

map_keywords: Henderson Beach State Park Destin Florida

places.name: Detroit

visits.text: You stand before the little blue and white house on West Grand Boulevard - Hitsville U.S.A., the birthplace of Motown. This is where Berry Gordy Jr., son of an African American family from Detroit who had worked on Ford assembly lines, founded his label in 1959 with an $800 family loan and a quality control method inspired by the auto industry. From 1959 to 1972, Diana Ross & The Supremes, Stevie Wonder, Marvin Gaye, The Temptations, and the Jackson 5 recorded their hits in the tiny Studio A - whose equipment, instruments, and creaky floorboards are entirely preserved. Founded in 1985 by Berry Gordy's sister, the museum is currently undergoing expansion to 50,000 square meters. Advance booking online is mandatory as hour-long guided tours regularly sell out several weeks in advance.

The Detroit Industry murals - painted by Diego Rivera from 1932 to 1933 on the four walls of the Rivera Court - are the DIA's centerpiece and one of the most important mural works of the 20th century. Rivera depicted Detroit's auto industry in a socialist-realist style that glorifies workers on Ford assembly lines - at the very moment when the Great Depression was ravaging the city. The work is listed as a National Historic Landmark. The museum itself, founded in 1885, holds over 65,000 works ranked among America's six finest collections - including the first Van Gogh to enter an American collection, a Self-Portrait from 1887. The German Expressionism collection is one of the most important outside Europe.

Michigan Central Station is Detroit's great ruined cathedral - an 18-story Beaux-Arts station inaugurated in 1913 by Reed & Stem, the same architects as New York's Grand Central Terminal, abandoned in 1988 and left to decay for 30 years until its purchase by Ford Motor Company in 2018. The restoration, completed in 2024, made it a technology and arts campus while preserving the grandeur of the main hall with its marble columns, vaults, and restored stained glass. The station is now partially open to the public - check the program on the Michigan Central website.

You enter the largest historic public market in the United States - 43 acres of bustling merchant district, whose heart is formed by five large brick halls built between 1892 and 1966. Eastern Market began in 1841 on Cadillac Square before being renamed and moved to this site in 1891 when area farmers needed larger space to supply the rapidly expanding industrial city. Saturday market, open from 6am to 4pm year-round, attracts between 30,000 and 40,000 people in summer - Detroit families buying directly from Michigan and Ohio farmers. The district includes over 150 permanent businesses and surrounding streets are covered with more than 100 murals commissioned during the annual Murals in the Market festival.

Belle Isle is a 4 square kilometer island in the middle of the Detroit River, minutes from downtown by bridge, now a Michigan state park since 2014. Frederick Law Olmsted - the same landscape architect who designed New York's Central Park - laid out its gardens in 1883. The island houses an Art Deco aquarium from 1904 (the oldest public aquarium in the United States still operating), a tropical conservatory, and remarkable views of both riverbanks. On Sunday mornings, Detroiters run and bike there in a peaceful atmosphere that contrasts with the city's noise.

Founded in 1965 by Dr. Charles H. Wright, a pioneering African American from Detroit, this museum is one of the world's largest museums devoted to African American history and culture. Its permanent exhibition And Still We Rise traces 600 years of history from West African kingdoms, the slave trade, slavery, Reconstruction, the civil rights movement, to contemporary culture. The museum is located in Midtown, steps from the DIA - combining both museums in a single day gives a deep and moving understanding of America.

You visit the three-story red brick building where Henry Ford designed and assembled the first Fords between 1904 and 1910 - notably the legendary Model T, the first automobile mass-produced for the general public, whose commercialization began in October 1908. The factory is smaller than you'd imagine: Ford had set up a secret room on the third floor, accessible through a hidden door, to design the Model T out of sight of skeptical investors. The building, listed as a National Historic Landmark, is managed by an association of passionate volunteers who explain with remarkable precision how the Model T changed the world.

You descend below street level onto one of the most original urban promenades in the United States - a disused railway trench from the former Grand Trunk Railway converted in 2009 into a 3-kilometer bike and pedestrian path connecting Eastern Market to the riverfront. The concrete walls of the trench are entirely covered with high-quality street art - one of Detroit's largest legal graffiti spots. The path runs past community gardens installed on vacant lots and passes under several bridges that create an unusual urban tunnel sensation.

Greektown is one of the few ethnic downtown Detroit neighborhoods to survive decades of depopulation - a handful of blocks on Monroe Street where Greek restaurants sit alongside groceries and pastry shops since the 1920s. Greek immigration to Detroit began in the early 20th century, attracted by the auto industry. Today, the opening of Greektown Casino in 2000 revitalized the neighborhood, which remains a popular dinner destination for Detroiters. The Pegasus Taverna restaurant, open since 1980, serves roasted lamb and spanakopita in traditional Mediterranean taverna decor.

activities.text: The RiverWalk promenade runs along the Detroit River for 8 kilometers from the marina to Belle Isle, the island park in the middle of the river. Detroit's geographic curiosity: it's the only major American city where you look south to see Canada - Windsor, Ontario, sits directly across on the opposite shore. The riverfront rehabilitation project transformed former industrial wastelands into a corridor of walkways, gardens, and street art spots frequented by Detroit families. In summer, free concerts take place along the shore.

Detroit's Midtown is one of the American neighborhoods where post-industrial transformation is most visible and radical. The 2013 municipal bankruptcy crisis freed up immense urban spaces that artists, entrepreneurs, and associations have reinvested. Over 100 international street art murals cover buildings around Woodward Avenue and adjacent streets - some commissioned by Cass Corridor galleries, others by the annual Murals in the Market festival at Eastern Market. The vacant lots between renovated houses still speak to the Motor City's industrial heyday in the 1950s.

The Henry Ford Museum, opened in 1929 by Henry Ford himself on 29 hectares in Dearborn, is one of the most unusual and impressive history museums in the United States. It houses the Lincoln Continental limousine in which John F. Kennedy was assassinated on November 22, 1963, the bus where Rosa Parks refused to give up her seat in 1955 in Montgomery, the chair in which Abraham Lincoln was sitting when assassinated, and hundreds of locomotives, automobiles, airplanes, and farm machinery. A museum that tells America through its objects, not just its great men.

Corktown is Detroit's oldest residential neighborhood, named after Irish immigrants from County Cork who settled there in the 1840s fleeing the Great Famine. Dominated by the restored silhouette of Michigan Central Station - the Ford campus inaugurated in 2024 - the neighborhood is experiencing a remarkable renaissance with restaurants, breweries, and artist studios investing in Victorian houses and former shops. Batch Brewing Company and City Winery have opened in former factories, and Michigan Avenue has become one of the city's liveliest streets.

Comerica Park, inaugurated in 2000 to replace the legendary Tiger Stadium, is one of the American League's most appreciated baseball stadiums for its family atmosphere and view of Detroit's skyline. The Detroit Tigers, founded in 1894, are one of the oldest professional American baseball franchises and have produced legends like Ty Cobb and Al Kaline. The stadium is nicknamed "The Corner" by fans in homage to the original Michigan and Trumbull intersection where Tiger Stadium stood since 1912. Even if baseball leaves you indifferent, the experience of a game night at the stadium with a hot dog and local beer is an authentic way to end your Detroit stay.

The Guardian Building from 1929 is Detroit's Art Deco masterpiece - a 40-story tower in Pewabic Pottery glazed brick whose interior hall stuns at every visit: gold and orange mosaic vaults, polychrome marble from floor to ceiling, brass fixtures. Architect Wirt Rowland himself called it the "cathedral of finance." When Detroit was the world capital of automobiles in the 1920s, its businessmen built as if prosperity could never end. This hall is the most brilliant proof - and admission is free.

places.name: Dickinson Enchanted Highway

visits.text: Pull off at exit 72 and turn north to see Geese in Flight first - certified by the Guinness Book of World Records in 2002 as the world's largest scrap metal sculpture: 110 feet tall, 154 feet wide, weighing over 78 tons. Gary Greff built all seven sculptures alone over 30 years, welding flattened oil well tanks and salvaged steel into flying geese, a tin farm family, deer, grasshoppers, fish, pheasants, and Theodore Roosevelt on horseback. Drive the full 32 miles south to Regent to see them all. At the end of the road, Greff converted his former high school into the Enchanted Castle hotel. Free access to all sculptures; donations welcome at the gift shop.

days_plan.itin_title: Montana East: Big Sky Country from Billings to Bismarck

map_keywords: Geese in Flight sculpture Gladstone ND
Gary Greff metal sculptures North Dakota

visits.text: You are standing on the original Boot Hill Cemetery site - the burying ground for men who 'died with their boots on.' Front Street around you recreates downtown Dodge City as it looked in 1876, and the 20,000-plus artifacts in the complex include more than 200 original firearms. The Long Branch Saloon here is modeled on period photographs of the original, which opened around 1874, burned in 1885, and hosted the June 1883 Dodge City Peace Commission photograph - one of the most reproduced images of the Old West. In summer, gunfight reenactments run at noon and 6 p.m. The Kansas Cowboy Hall of Fame and a Gunsmoke exhibit are also on site.

Walk through the only limestone house in Dodge City and you are in the oldest building in the city still standing on its original foundation. Bavarian immigrant John Mueller had 24-inch-thick walls quarried from Sawlog Creek, 12 miles northeast of town - the same quarry that supplied Fort Dodge - and hired fellow German craftsman William Strubel to hand-build the walnut spiral staircase, its newel post inset with bird's-eye maple. Built between 1879 and 1881, the house passed to only one other family: the Schmidts, who kept it until 1968. The desk where Dodge City founder Robert M. Wright wrote his 1913 book Dodge City: The Cowboy Capital is still inside.

Drive nine miles west of Dodge City on US-50 and you reach a National Historic Landmark where 2 miles of wagon ruts survive across 140 acres of unplowed prairie - among the longest and best-preserved sections of the entire trail. For 60 years, wagon wheels cut this path through the sod, sometimes four abreast. The ruts are most visible when the sun is low; visit in late afternoon for the best light on the depressions. A short interpretive boardwalk explains the site; it also preserves remnants of the 1880s Soule Canal irrigation project.

activities.text: Walk Wyatt Earp Boulevard and you are on the route of the original Front Street, where 16 saloons operated simultaneously during the cattle trade's peak years. The Dodge City Trail of Fame embeds bronze boot prints and narrative plaques for Wyatt Earp, Bat Masterson, Doc Holliday, and other figures from the era. The El Capitan bronze sculpture nearby depicts a full-scale Texas Longhorn steer, commemorating the five million head that ended their drives here. The Wyatt Earp statue outside Boot Hill marks the spot where Earp enforced the 'Deadline' at the railroad tracks - south of which anything went.

Book ahead and spend the evening in the Long Branch, where a variety show has run every summer since 1957 - one of the longest-running seasonal variety shows in the country. Miss Kitty, singing bartenders, can-can dancers, and gunfighters re-enact the entertainment that Chalk Beeson's five-piece orchestra once provided nightly at the original saloon. The original Long Branch, named by owner William Harris after Long Branch, New Jersey, served Anheuser-Busch beer kept cold in summer by ice shipped by train from Colorado, and hosted gambling from five-cent chuck-a-luck to thousand-dollar poker pots.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends
Kansas Flint Hills & Frontier: Wichita to Dodge City

places.name: Door County Fish Creek

visits.text: The limestone cliffs of Cave Point drop straight into Lake Michigan: you stand at the edge of a ledge and watch waves slowly carving caves beneath your feet for millennia. This is Door County's most photographed spot. By canoe or kayak, you can slip into the low caves when weather permits; from the ridge trail, you can see 15 meters deep into the turquoise water. Arrive early in the morning before the fog or late afternoon for golden light on the limestone.

To reach the Cana Island lighthouse (1870), you cross on foot across a stone causeway sometimes covered by water. The 22-meter white limestone tower is one of the most photographed of Door County's 11 lighthouses. The lighthouse is managed by the Door County Maritime Museum and open from May to October - the tower is climbable for an additional fee.

Peninsula State Park is the peninsula's largest park with 1,500 hectares of forest and 13 kilometers of Green Bay shoreline. Climb the Eagle Tower - a wooden observation platform - to embrace the Chambers and Horseshoe islands offshore. The Eagle Bluff Lighthouse from 1868 offers guided tours from May to October: the keeper and his family lived in these six rooms for decades of near-total isolation. Arrive early for the cliff-edge trails, packed after 10am in July-August.

From early July to mid-August, the Montmorency cherry orchards open for free picking. Door County produces millions of pounds of cherries annually thanks to Lake Michigan's moderating effect on temperatures - cherry blossoms in May are as spectacular as the fruit in July. Outside picking season, farm markets offer jams, pies and juice year-round. Plan the morning for this stroll among the orchards before heading to Ephraim for lunch.

Ephraim is the only Door County village founded by Moravian settlers from Prussia in 1853, who banned alcohol sales until 1969. You find yourself in a row of white clapboard houses, a church from 1857 still standing, and Anderson Dock whose graffiti-covered warehouse has become a local art gallery. The sunset from the dock, with the colors of Green Bay before you, is unanimously cited as the peninsula's most beautiful - plan to be there around 7pm.

activities.text: The fish boil is Door County's culinary tradition: chunks of Lake Michigan whitefish, potatoes and white onions boiling in a giant cauldron. The finale is spectacular - kerosene thrown on the flames makes them leap two meters high to eliminate fish oils. Then comes cherry pie. The recipe comes from Scandinavian and Finnish immigrants who fished here in the 19th century.

Seen from a kayak, Cave Point changes its nature: you enter the caves at water level, beneath limestone arches, and the lake bottom appears through water so clear it seems there's nothing beneath the hull. Door County counts over 200 shipwrecks in its waters - testimony to the sinister reputation of the Death's Door passage.

The Death's Door passage crossing takes 30 minutes and drops you on the only year-round inhabited island of the peninsula. Washington Island is known for its concentration of Icelandic-origin residents, drawn in the 19th century by a landscape that reminded them of their homeland. Circle the island by rental bike in 1h30, then catch the ferry back in early afternoon to return to Fish Creek in time for sunset at Ephraim.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders
The Great Lakes Grand Loop: Chicago, Milwaukee, Door County, Sleeping Bear, Mackinac, Pictured Rocks - 12 Days
The Great Lakes Grand Loop: Chicago, Milwaukee, Door County, Sleeping Bear, Mackinac, Pictured Rocks - 12 Days

map_keywords: Cherry orchards Door County Sturgeon Bay
Whitefish Dunes State Park Door County
Newport State Park wilderness Door County

visits.text: Dry Tortugas National Park - America's Most Remote National Park: Located 70 miles west of Key West, this small chain of islands requires a ferry or seaplane to reach, rewarding visitors with pristine beaches, exceptional snorkeling, and a massive Civil War fortress.

Fort Jefferson - The Largest Masonry Structure in the Americas: This hexagonal fortress rises from Garden Key, its walls constructed from 16 million bricks yet never completed despite 30 years of construction. Its most famous prisoner was Dr. Samuel Mudd, convicted of conspiracy in Lincoln's assassination.

activities.text: Snorkeling the Pristine Reefs - Caribbean Waters in American Territory: The clear, protected waters surrounding the islands offer some of the best snorkeling in North America, with coral formations and tropical fish rivaling any Caribbean destination.

Camping Under the Stars - Sleep in the Fortress Shadow: Limited primitive camping allows overnight visitors to experience the islands after day-trippers depart, with star-filled skies unspoiled by light pollution.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: You drive 45 miles north to this 2,526-acre monument, established in 1949, which preserves 206 earthen mounds perched along the Mississippi River bluffs in northeastern Iowa. Of the 206, thirty-one are effigy mounds constructed between 500 BCE and 1300 CE in the shapes of bears and birds - a ceremonial practice found nowhere else but the Upper Mississippi Valley. Great Bear Mound measures 137 feet long and 70 feet wide. The Marching Bear Group - ten bear effigies and three bird forms - stretches nearly a quarter mile along a bluff top. Fourteen miles of trails wind through the site; free admission.

Iowa's most-visited attraction is a Smithsonian Affiliate covering the history and ecology of the Mississippi River across multiple buildings at the Port of Dubuque. Six aquarium tanks hold river creatures from giant catfish to playful river otters. Indoor galleries trace the steamboat era, locks-and-dams construction, and riverman culture through artifacts and interactive exhibits. The preserved Fred W. Woodward riverboat sits docked outside. Admission charged.

You walk along the riverfront to the only surviving shot tower west of the Mississippi River - a 120-foot masonry tower built in 1856, listed on the National Register of Historic Places. The tower produced lead shot for muskets for just two years before being undercut by a St. Louis competitor. After standing abandoned since 1911, it was restored in 2006. A historical marker tells the full story of its brief industrial life.

You board what bills itself as the world's shortest, steepest scenic railway - built in July 1882 by former Dubuque mayor J.K. Graves, who grew tired of the long buggy route up the bluff to his home. A fire destroyed the original structure in 1884; Graves rebuilt and opened it to the public. The 296-foot bluff ride takes about a minute each way, and the view from the top sweeps across Dubuque, the Mississippi, and into Wisconsin and Illinois simultaneously. Fee applies.

activities.text: You follow the Great River Road National Scenic Byway along the Iowa bank of the Mississippi south through Bellevue and Sabula. The 40-mile stretch between Dubuque and Guttenberg is especially dramatic: sandstone bluffs drop straight to the river, lock-and-dam structures appear in roadside pullouts, and small river towns preserve intact 19th-century commercial streets. Free access; drive at your own pace and stop where the view demands it.

The Port of Dubuque's revitalized riverwalk and entertainment district wraps around the Mississippi River Museum at the foot of the bluffs. Stone Cliff Winery, housed in the historic Dubuque Star Brewing Company building, ranks among the most atmospheric dinner options in town. The view of the river and old warehouse brick after dark makes a fitting close to a day spent on the Mississippi.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs
Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs
Iowa Backroads: From the Missouri Bluffs to the Mississippi Cliffs

visits.text: Gooseberry Falls State Park—Stop here first, 40 miles north of Duluth. Five waterfalls tumble down the Gooseberry River before it empties into Lake Superior. Take the Lower Falls trail—it's short, easy, and dramatic. If you come in fall, the contrast of brown water against black rock and red foliage is striking.

Split Rock Lighthouse—Climb up to this lighthouse perched on a 130-foot cliff above Lake Superior. Built in 1910 after a storm damaged 29 ships, it operated until 1969. If you're here on November 10, you'll see it lit in memory of the crew of the Edmund Fitzgerald, lost in 1975. Allow an hour for the guided tour.

activities.text: Arriving in Duluth late in the day, head down to Canal Park to watch the Aerial Lift Bridge in action—this 1905 vertical lift bridge rises in under a minute to let cargo ships pass. Locals gather to watch the ships enter the harbor like it's theater. The foghorn and the rising bridge are hypnotic.
Walk along the Lakewalk, the boardwalk that follows the canal. Lake Superior is the world's largest freshwater lake by surface area (31,700 square miles)—from Duluth, you can't see the far shore.

days_plan.itin_title: Minnesota in 10 Days: Twin Cities, Lakes, and Lake Superior's North Shore

places.name: Duluth Mn

visits.text: Check the freighter schedule before arriving at the bridge: when a 1,000-foot laker passes under the Aerial Lift Bridge, the deck rises to 138 feet in under three minutes. It's one of the few lift bridges still operating in the United States. The Maritime Center posts the schedule—or search online for "Duluth ship schedule" so you don't miss a passage.

Step aboard the William A. Irvin, a 1938 ore freighter converted into a floating museum. This ship holds a record: 13,856 tons of iron ore unloaded in 2 hours and 55 minutes. You'll explore the engine room, officers' quarters, and the hull from the inside—an immersion into the industrial era of the Great Lakes.

Glensheen is 39 rooms built on the shores of Lake Superior between 1905 and 1908 for mining magnate Chester Congdon—original furnishings intact, carved woodwork, family china. The story takes a dark turn with the 1977 murder case; the guides don't shy away from it. Book online; the full tour lasts 90 minutes.

If trains speak to you, the Lake Superior Railroad Museum is worth the stop. It's housed in the 19th-century historic train depot with steam locomotives and period railcars. In summer, you can board the North Shore Scenic Railroad for a ride to Two Harbors—16 miles of coastline seen from the windows.

activities.text: Walk the Lakewalk from Canal Park: 8 miles of paved trail along Lake Superior, from Bayfront Park to Brighton Beach. The lake covers 31,700 square kilometers and you need to walk alongside it to truly grasp its scale. Along the way, keep an eye out for stones—Lake Superior agates, red and orange semi-precious stones deposited by glaciers, turn up here.

The Great Lakes Aquarium is the only freshwater aquarium in the United States dedicated entirely to the Great Lakes basin. Sturgeon, river otters, lake trout—native species alongside exhibits on invasive species threatening the ecosystem. Perfect if the weather turns during the day.

days_plan.itin_title: Minneapolis, Voyageurs, Isle Royale & the Upper Peninsula

map_keywords: William A. Irvin Ship Museum Duluth

places.name: Eagle River

visits.text: Eagle River Chain of Lakes - Welcome to the Northwoods. Eagle River boasts the world's longest chain of navigable lakes: 28 lakes connected by natural channels spanning 28,000 acres. You can boat from one lake to another without ever touching land. The landscape is a mix of pine forests, lakefront homes, and wooden docks.

Downtown Eagle River - Classic Northwoods small-town center with fishing tackle shops, burger joints, and Friday fish fries (Wisconsin's breaded fish tradition). Summer vacation Americana at its finest.

activities.text: Guided Fishing - The lakes teem with largemouth bass, muskellunge, and walleye. Local guides offer half-day or full-day boat trips. The musky is the ultimate trophy - some specimens exceed 4 feet.

Kayaking or Paddleboarding the Lakes - Quieter alternative to fishing. Rent a kayak or SUP and explore the channels between lakes. The water is clear and relatively warm in summer (72-77°F in July-August).

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

map_keywords: Eagle River chain of lakes Wisconsin

visits.text: Step back in time at the historic Chowan County Courthouse, a remarkable Georgian architectural landmark dating back to 1767 that dominates Edenton's charming downtown skyline. This beautifully preserved building represents one of North Carolina's finest examples of colonial-era civic architecture and offers insight into the region's early American heritage.

Tour the magnificent Penelope Barker House, an exceptional example of Greek Revival-Federal architectural fusion built in 1782. This elegant historic home showcases period artifacts representing life in colonial and early American Edenton, providing visitors with a fascinating window into the town's distinguished past.

activities.text: Enjoy a leisurely stroll through Colonial Waterfront Park while soaking up breathtaking views of Albemarle Sound. The park offers a peaceful setting to appreciate Edenton's scenic waterfront and historic charm. Browse local artworks at the Chowan Arts Council, which highlights the creative talents of regional artists in this picturesque historic community.

days_plan.itin_title: North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains

places.name: El Paso

visits.text: Mission Trail - Three Spanish missions from the 17th and 18th centuries line up along the Rio Grande south of El Paso. The oldest, Ysleta (1682), is the oldest continuously active mission in Texas. Socorro (1691) preserves ceiling beams decorated by Native American artisans. San Elizario, a bit farther, is a former presidio (fort) with a charming arcaded plaza. Allow half a day for all three.

Franklin Mountains State Park - The largest urban park in the United States: 25,000 acres of desert mountain right in the city. Take the Wyler Aerial Tramway to Ranger Peak (5,600 feet) for views of El Paso, Ciudad Juárez in Mexico, and New Mexico—three states at once. If you prefer to hike, the Ron Coleman Trail (3-mile round trip) climbs to a saddle with similar panoramas.

Downtown El Paso - Step inside the Plaza Theatre (1930), a Spanish Colonial Revival and Art Deco masterpiece, even without a show. The neighborhood around San Jacinto Plaza mixes 19th-century architecture with street art. If your passport allows, walk across the bridge to Ciudad Juárez—the border is five minutes on foot.

Hueco Tanks State Park (en route) - Thirty miles east of El Paso, detour to these syenite rock formations containing over 3,000 Native American pictographs (the Tigua, Apache, and Kiowa peoples succeeded one another here for 10,000 years). The "tanks" are rock basins that hold rainwater—a vital desert resource. Reserve ahead: access is limited to 70 visitors daily.

Scenic Drive - Back in El Paso, drive up this panoramic road winding along the flanks of the Franklin Mountains. At sunset, the view of illuminated El Paso and Ciudad Juárez is spectacular—two million lights spread across the Rio Grande valley.

El Paso Museum of Art - If you have time before your flight, this small free museum presents a fine collection of Southwestern art: desert landscape paintings, Spanish colonial sacred art, and contemporary border art.

activities.text: Border Tex-Mex cuisine - El Paso is the American capital of authentic Tex-Mex. Try red or green enchiladas at one of the restaurants in Segundo Barrio, the oldest Mexican-American neighborhood in the United States.

Final Tex-Mex meal - Before leaving El Paso, treat yourself to one last border feast. Green chile enchiladas (New Mexico green chile sauce) are the local specialty. The red-versus-green sauce debate is taken very seriously here.

days_plan.itin_title: Far West Texas Circuit: Big Bend and Ghost Towns
Far West Texas Circuit: Big Bend and Ghost Towns
Desert Parks of Texas and New Mexico: Big Bend, Carlsbad, White Sands, and Guadalupe
Desert Parks of Texas and New Mexico: Big Bend, Carlsbad, White Sands, and Guadalupe
Desert to Alamo: West Texas & Big Bend - 10 Days from El Paso to San Antonio

map_keywords: Hueco Tanks State Park El Paso

places.name: Elkhart

visits.text: You enter the world's only museum entirely dedicated to recreational vehicle history and understand why Elkhart County produces over 80% of RVs manufactured worldwide. In 1931, a local resident named Milo Miller built a trailer for his family - he sold it before even reaching his destination, built a second, a third, and never stopped. Today, over 1,000 manufacturers and suppliers operate in the county. The museum displays the world's oldest known surviving trailer (1913), Mae West's 1931 house car, and the trailer built for Charles Lindbergh in 1939. Open Monday-Saturday 9am-5pm, Sunday 10am-3pm; admission: $12 adult.

You ring the bell at a Beaux-Arts mansion in beige limestone built in 1910 for Albert Beardsley - heir to a pharmaceutical fortune - and a guide introduces you to 38 rooms that seem frozen in the 1900 Paris Exposition. The decorative arts collection is exceptional for a city Elkhart's size: Louis XV furniture, silverware, Sèvres porcelain, and paintings from American schools of the Progressive Era, in a carefully preserved period arrangement. The French garden freely accessible in summer contrasts with the working-class homes of the surrounding neighborhood - an immediate visual reminder of the economic gulf between industrialists and employees at the turn of the 20th century. Guided tours Tuesday-Sunday 10am-4pm; admission: $12.

You turn onto the first secondary road south of Goshen and enter a different rhythm: black horse-drawn buggies trot along the shoulder, houses without power lines succeed each other behind straight-trimmed hedges, and roadside stands offer vegetables and jams on the honor system. Elkhart County is home to America's third-largest Amish community with approximately 35,000 members. The Heritage Trail Driving Tour is a 90-mile loop with audio guide downloadable free from the local tourism office website. Download the audio before leaving Elkhart - mobile coverage is spotty on the county's back roads.

activities.text: You enter Wellfield Botanic Gardens from a former industrial site rehabilitated in 2010 and are immediately surprised by the ambition of the achievement: twelve themed gardens range from Indiana native prairies to formal water gardens, with steel sculptures commissioned from regional artists that change twice yearly. A wooden walkway spans the central wetland - wait for dawn or dusk to observe great blue herons feeding there in silence. Tulips and magnolias explode in April, irises and peonies in June. On July weekends, hundreds of families flood the paths - come on weekdays for quiet.

You put on a safety vest and step onto an assembly line where technicians build recreational vehicles almost entirely by hand - from small camper vans to 50-foot luxury motorhomes. Thor Motor Coach, the world's largest RV producer with over 40 brands, opens its lines to visitors on weekdays. Some high-end models incorporate over 50,000 different parts. You leave with a radically different idea of what 'made in America' still concretely means today. Book several weeks ahead - spaces are limited and tours often sell out.

days_plan.itin_title: Detroit to Chicago: Great Lakes Road Trip - 8 Days

map_keywords: RV MH Hall of Fame Museum 21565 Executive Parkway Elkhart
Ruthmere Mansion Museum Beardsley Avenue Elkhart Indiana
Wellfield Botanic Gardens Prairie Street Elkhart Indiana
Heritage Trail Amish backroads Goshen Middlebury Nappanee Indiana
Das Dutchman Essenhaus Middlebury Indiana Amish restaurant

visits.text: Newport State Park - Wisconsin's only wilderness park and the state's only certified Dark Sky Park. Over 2,300 acres of forest, 7 miles of Lake Michigan shoreline, and 30 miles of trails. Here, no paved roads or developed campgrounds - just walk-in campsites. The atmosphere is radically different from the more touristy rest of Door County.
Lake Michigan Shoreline - The coast mixes sandy beaches and rocky shores. The water is cold (even in summer, expect 60-65°F) but remarkably clear. Sunrises over the lake are spectacular.

activities.text: Newport State Park Hiking - The Europe Bay Loop Trail (5 miles) follows the coast through white cedar and birch forest before reaching Europe Bay beach - one of the peninsula's most secluded.

Night Sky Observation - If skies are clear, return to the park after dark. Light pollution is virtually nonexistent here. The Milky Way is visible to the naked eye, and August meteor showers (Perseids) are a highlight.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

visits.text: The International Wolf Center in Ely is one of the few places in the world where you can observe wolves in semi-wild conditions in their natural environment. The exhibit traces the history of wolf reintroduction in northern forests. The resident wolves are visible through large viewing windows throughout the day—take your time in front of them.

Dorothy Molter lived alone for 56 years on Knife Lake, 15 miles from the nearest road—the last non-Native resident of the Boundary Waters. She made homemade root beer for the thousands of paddlers who stopped by her place. After her death in 1986, her two cabins were dismantled and rebuilt in town to form this museum.

activities.text: Take time to paddle on the lakes around Ely before heading out. Local outfitters—Piragis Northwoods, Ely Outfitting—offer day trips by canoe or kayak without committing to the Boundary Waters. The lakes are closed to motors: absolute silence, loons calling, pine reflections. The best preview of the million acres stretching beyond.

days_plan.itin_title: Minneapolis, Voyageurs, Isle Royale & the Upper Peninsula

places.name: Ely Nv

visits.text: The East Ely depot closed in 1983 when Kennecott shut down the copper operation. It's remained virtually untouched since—supply rooms, offices, steam locomotives still in the yard. The Nevada Northern Railway is now listed as a National Historic Landmark and the 'Ghost Train of Old Ely' still runs certain weekends on the original tracks. Check dates at nnry.com—it's Ely's most distinctive experience.

The White Pine Public Museum displays over 300 minerals from the region—gold, silver, raw copper—and the replica of a cave bear discovered in the county in 1982, extinct 10,000 years ago. On your way out, the Nevada Northern caboose parked by the entrance and the reconstructed pioneer cabin in the yard are worth a look.

The Ward Charcoal Ovens look like six giant igloos lost in the desert, 29 km south of Ely. Eastern European immigrants built them between 1873 and 1876 to fuel nearby silver smelters. Each kiln stands 9 meters tall and produced 35 tons of charcoal per load. Free entry, on-site parking.

activities.text: Walk the Ely Renaissance Society murals circuit: over 35 frescoes and sculptures on the brick walls of Aultman Street, painted by artists from around the world since 1999. Each piece tells a story—a Finnish miner, a Basque family, a Pony Express horse. Map available at the visitor center across from Hotel Nevada.

days_plan.itin_title: Salt Lake City to Great Basin Road Trip: Desert, Salt & Stars

map_keywords: Nevada Northern Railway Museum East Ely
White Pine Public Museum Ely Nevada
Ward Charcoal Ovens State Historic Park Ely

visits.text: Ephraim Village - This postcard village is a lineup of whitewashed buildings facing Eagle Harbor. Founded in 1853 by Norwegian Moravian immigrants, it has kept its character - no bars or liquor stores (the town stayed 'dry' until 2016). Browse art galleries, shops, and cafes along the main road.
Eagle Harbor Sunset - In the evening, head to the village pier or beach. The sun sets behind Green Bay, illuminating the harbor's calm waters. This is Door County's most celebrated sunset spot.
Moravian Church - The small white church at the village center dates from 1857. It's still active. The adjacent cemetery tells the story of the Norwegian community's early decades.

activities.text: Bonfire and Fish Boil - Door County tradition: fish boil involves boiling Lake Michigan whitefish with potatoes and onions in a huge outdoor cauldron. The show's climax: the 'boil over,' when the cook throws kerosene on the fire to make the cauldron overflow and eliminate the grease. Several Ephraim establishments offer it on summer evenings.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

visits.text: Essex Steam Train & Riverboat - Journey Through Time on Rails and Water: Board a vintage steam locomotive at the historic 1892 Essex Station for a 2.5-hour round-trip adventure through the Connecticut River Valley. Two operating steam locomotives pull various passenger coaches, including a seasonal open-air car, on a 12-mile journey to Deep River Landing.

Becky Thatcher Riverboat Cruise - Glide Past Castles and Covered Bridges: Transfer to a classic riverboat for a picturesque cruise along the Connecticut River, taking in views of Gillette Castle perched above, the Haddam Swing Bridge, and the ornate Goodspeed Opera House along the tree-lined shores.

Connecticut River Museum - Celebrating 410 Miles of Living History: Housed in the historic Steamboat Dock building in downtown Essex, this museum explores the cultural and natural history of New England's longest river through artwork, memorabilia, interactive exhibits, and guided river cruises.

activities.text: Essex Village - Connecticut's Most Perfect Town: This carefully preserved village consistently ranks among America's most charming small towns, with Federal-era architecture, yacht-filled harbor, and quiet streets that embody New England's maritime heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: Connecticut Coastal Drive: From Maritime Mystic to Ivy League New Haven

places.name: Estes Park

visits.text: Arriving in Estes Park, make your first stop at the Beaver Meadows Visitor Center at the park's east entrance - the building was designed by Frank Lloyd Wright's architecture firm. Inside, a 20-minute film provides park context before you enter. Purchase your pass here if you haven't already, and verify Trail Ridge Road status (info line is 970-586-1222).

Then head down to Moraine Park, a vast meadow shaped by glaciers over 15,000 years ago. It's one of the park's best locations for observing elk, particularly early morning and late afternoon. In September-October, you'll hear the bugling of males during the rut - Estes Park counts approximately 3,000 elk in its surroundings and has even renamed October 'Elktober'. Don't try to approach too closely: elk are massive and unpredictable.

In town, pass by the Stanley Hotel. Built in 1909 by Freelan Oscar Stanley - co-inventor of the Stanley Steamer automobile - this white Georgian hotel with 140 rooms dominates Estes Park from a hillside. Stephen King spent a night here in 1974, snowed in, and drew inspiration from the atmosphere to write 'The Shining'. Kubrick's film wasn't shot here, but King produced his own TV movie at the Stanley in 1997. Day tours and nighttime 'ghost' tours are offered if the subject intrigues you.

The three-lakes hike from Bear Lake Trailhead is Rocky Mountain National Park's most iconic trail. In 3.6 miles round-trip with 700 feet of elevation gain, you'll visit Nymph Lake (covered with yellow pond lilies in summer), Dream Lake (turquoise waters reflecting the cliff face of Hallett Peak, 12,713 feet), and Emerald Lake (nestled at 10,110 feet at the base of the massif). The emerald color of the latter comes from glacial silt suspended in the water. Allow 2 to 3 hours depending on your pace. Arrive before 8am to avoid parking saturation - a free shuttle from the Glacier Basin overflow parking operates every 10-15 minutes in season.

If you have energy after the lakes, Sprague Lake is a few minutes' drive on Bear Lake Road - it's a flat 0.8-mile loop around a lake with superb reflections of surrounding peaks, accessible to strollers and wheelchairs. It's also one of the park's best spots for sunrise photography.

activities.text: Stroll the Estes Park Riverwalk along Fall River - a flat path that crosses downtown and runs past local shops and restaurants. It's ideal for a first evening at altitude (7,522 feet) before climbing even higher tomorrow.

Optional hike to Lake Haiyaha from the same area: a junction between Nymph and Dream Lake leads there by adding 2 miles and 350 feet of elevation gain. This fourth lake is less crowded and offers a wilder character, with scattered granite blocks on the shore. Ideal if you want solitude after the crowds at the main three lakes.

places.name: Eureka Springs

visits.text: Eureka Springs was founded on July 4, 1879, around springs reputed to have healing properties - within two years, the town already counted 10,000 inhabitants, making it Arkansas's fourth-largest city. Since 1970, the entire downtown has been listed on the National Register of Historic Places. Walk through Spring Street and Main Street: the 500 local stone buildings lining the winding streets nearly all date from 1890 to 1910. Eureka Springs' peculiarity: its streets never cross at 90 degrees - the chaotic topography of the Ozarks made a grid layout simply impossible. Some buildings have entrances on different floors depending on which side of the street you approach from.

Thorncrown Chapel rose from the ground in 1980, commissioned by a retired schoolteacher, Jim Reed, who wanted to offer a meditation space to passersby who stopped to admire the view from his property. Architect E. Fay Jones - a Frank Lloyd Wright apprentice at Taliesin - imposed an absolute constraint: no structural element could be larger than what two men could carry through the woods, to avoid damaging the site. Result: a 48-foot-high chapel, 425 windows, over 6,000 square feet of glass, built entirely of local pine. The AIA ranked it the fourth-best building of the 20th century. Come early morning or late afternoon - the light playing through the wooden lattice changes every hour.

Christ of the Ozarks is a 65-foot white concrete statue inaugurated on June 25, 1966, on Magnetic Mountain, commissioned by evangelist Gerald L.K. Smith. You spot it from the road long before you arrive - it dominates the woods from a ridge and remains visible for miles. Access to the site is free; if you're there in the evening, the lights illuminating it after sunset give it a striking presence. Nearby, The Great Passion Play has been staged every summer since 1968 - the most-attended outdoor drama in the United States according to the Institute of Outdoor Theatre.

activities.text: Eureka Springs trolley system: covers four routes and serves the entire historic downtown. It's by far the best way to explore the town without exhausting yourself on the hills - the winding, steep streets punish careless walkers. A day pass allows unlimited on-off boarding at all stops. Trolleys run from April to October.

Turpentine Creek Wildlife Refuge is a 450-acre sanctuary that shelters big cats rescued from captivity - tigers, lions, leopards, cougars, primarily former circus animals or private ownership. Guided tours of the enclosures are offered several times daily. Allow at least 2 hours to tour it properly.

days_plan.itin_title: Arkansas Itinerary: Hot Springs, Ozarks and Natural Parks
Arkansas: Hot Springs, Ozarks & Crystal Bridges — 7-Day Loop from Little Rock
Ozarks & Branson: From Missouri Hills to Arkansas Art Galleries

map_keywords: Christ of the Ozarks statue Eureka Springs

visits.text: Everglades National Park - The River of Grass: This UNESCO World Heritage Site and International Biosphere Reserve protects 1.5 million acres of subtropical wilderness, home to the endangered Florida panther and millions of alligators.

Shark Valley - Fourteen Miles into the Wild: Cycle or tram through sawgrass prairie to an observation tower offering 360-degree views across seemingly endless wetlands teeming with alligators and wading birds.

Anhinga Trail at Royal Palm - Guaranteed Wildlife Encounters: This half-mile boardwalk trail at Royal Palm provides some of the most reliable wildlife viewing in Florida, with alligators, anhingas, herons, and turtles visible year-round.
Flamingo Visitor Center - Where Fresh Water Meets Salt: This southern outpost provides access to Florida Bay's mangrove estuaries, with kayaking, camping, and dolphin-watching opportunities.

activities.text: Airboat Tours - Skimming the River of Grass: These flat-bottomed boats powered by aircraft propellers are the traditional way to explore the Everglades, accessing areas no other vessel can reach.
Backcountry Camping - Sleep Among the Chickees: Platform campsites called 'chickees' allow overnight stays in the heart of the wetlands, an unforgettable wilderness experience.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

places.name: Fairbanks

visits.text: Start at the Morris Thompson Cultural and Visitors Center, 101 Dunkel Street downtown - it's your essential first stop in Fairbanks. Opened in 2008 and completely free, the building houses 9,000 square feet of permanent exhibits on Interior Alaska's Indigenous peoples: life-size dioramas, a reconstructed fishing camp, Koyukon Athabascan artifacts. The center honors Morris Thompson, an Indigenous leader born in Tanana in 1939 who died in the Alaska Airlines 261 crash in 2000 while returning from vacation in Mexico. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours to see everything, and grab your maps and local tips before heading out.

Steps from the center, Golden Heart Plaza marks Fairbanks' historic heart. The central statue - 'The Unknown First Family' - was designed by sculptor Malcolm Alexander to honor the city's founding families. Fairbanks was born in 1901 when a merchant named E.T. Barnette accidentally ran aground here with 130 tons of goods meant for other goldfields. The Chena River runs alongside the plaza - this is where early 20th-century gold seekers moored their steamboats.

Drive the Parks Highway back north: the road parallels the Alaska Range for long stretches, with open viewpoints toward the mountain chain to the east. Several pullouts allow stops - if skies are clear, the view south may give you a final glimpse of Denali at 20,310 feet from the highway. In Fairbanks, stop at the Museum of the North on the University of Alaska campus: the building itself is worth the visit - designed by architect Joan Soranno in 2005, its angular lines evoke alpine ridges and auroral curtains. Inside, allow at least 2 hours: the highlight is Blue Babe, a 36,000-year-old steppe bison mummy, emerged intact from permafrost in 1979. The park's gold gallery is the largest public collection of Alaska gold.

Late afternoon, Creamer's Field Migratory Waterfowl Refuge 3 miles from downtown is a former 2,200-acre farmstead transformed into a refuge for migratory birds. It's also one of the best in-town spots to watch northern lights in season without leaving Fairbanks - vast open fields, no intrusive artificial light.

Final morning in Fairbanks depending on your flight time. If you still have time, return to the Morris Thompson Center to collect your aurora certificate if you saw any - rangers issue it free. The Antler Arch, right next door, is an arch built from over 100 caribou and moose antlers collected throughout Interior Alaska - obligatory farewell photo. Fairbanks International Airport is 7 miles southwest of downtown.

activities.text: In the evening, walk along the banks of the Chena River. What you'll see depends entirely on the month: between May 17 and July 27, the sun never sets - Fairbanks accumulates 70 consecutive days of continuous daylight. From August 21 through April 21, nights turn dark again and northern lights can appear - Fairbanks sits directly beneath the auroral oval at 65° north. If you're here during aurora season, head away from city lights toward Cleary Summit (20 miles) or along Chena Hot Springs Road for darker skies.

Free evening in Fairbanks: Pioneer Park (formerly Alaskaland) is an open-air historical park with period buildings from the 19th-century gold rush, restaurants, and Indigenous craft shops - open in summer only. If you're here during aurora season (August 21 - April 21) and skies are clear, head toward Cleary Summit 20 miles out for a standard viewing site, or continue along Chena Hot Springs Road for darker skies.

If your flight is in the afternoon, Chena Hot Springs (56 miles east on Chena Hot Springs Road, fully paved) deserves a final detour: natural geothermal springs in a forest setting, and in all seasons an Aurora Ice Museum carved from permanent ice. In winter, many come to soak in the hot pools while watching the northern lights - an experience few places in the world can offer.
