University of Alaska Museum of the North Fairbanks

visits.text: Hill-Stead Museum - A Private Collection Rivaling Major Museums: Step inside this stately Colonial Revival mansion to view European masterpieces including works by Monet and Degas that the Pope family collected during the Gilded Age. The 33,000-square-foot estate retains its original furnishings and decor from the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

Sunken Garden and Grounds - 152 Acres of New England Beauty: Explore rolling hills, serene walking paths through lush woodlands, and the famous Beatrix Farrand-designed Sunken Garden. The grounds achieve peak splendor in autumn when foliage transforms the landscape into a masterpiece.

activities.text: Poetry Festival and Cultural Events - Where Art Meets Nature: The museum hosts the renowned Sunken Garden Poetry Festival each summer, along with year-round lectures, concerts, and educational programs in this sublime setting.

days_plan.itin_title: Connecticut Coastal Drive: From Maritime Mystic to Ivy League New Haven

places.name: Fayetteville Wv

visits.text: You're overlooking one of the world's largest steel arch bridges: 924 meters total length, with a 518-meter arch span, 267 meters above the New River - higher than the Eiffel Tower from its base. Built between 1974 and 1977 in COR-TEN steel that weathers to a protective rust without ever being repainted, the bridge reduced gorge crossing time by a factor of 40: it once took 45 minutes on winding roads, now it takes less than a minute. The bridge was the world's longest steel arch for 26 years; it has appeared on West Virginia's state quarter since 2005. The Canyon Rim Visitor Center offers the best view from its observation deck north of the bridge.

You're walking the Long Point Trail (4.5 km round trip) to an exposed overlook on the south rim where you see the bridge from a different angle: the rusted arch silhouetted against the deciduous-covered hills, and 267 meters below the green river glittering among the boulders. Designated a national park in December 2020 - the newest in the United States at that date - New River Gorge protects 28,500 hectares of gorges and Appalachian forests around a river paradoxically among the continent's oldest, estimated at 320 million years. Wear closed-toe shoes: the trail crosses rocky sections and exposed roots.

You're strolling through this Victorian town of 3,000 residents whose historic center dates from the 1880s, when the region's coal mines ran at full capacity. Today Main Street lines up outdoor shops, cafes, and several craft breweries that thrive on adventure tourism. The granite cathedral of the 1903 Methodist church and the 1895 county courthouse dominate the central square. Stroll in the evening after your day of activities to dine in one of the restaurants animated by the community of local climbers and rafters.

activities.text: You're navigating at the bottom of a 267-meter-deep canyon on class III to V rapids depending on flow - the most powerful in the eastern Appalachians. The New River flows from south to north, counter to the Appalachian relief, which attests to its antiquity predating the tectonic movements that formed the mountains. The passage beneath the New River Gorge Bridge seen from the bottom of the water, 267 meters above your helmet, is an experience in itself. Book several weeks ahead for July-August.

You're walking on a 60-centimeter-wide catwalk suspended beneath the bridge deck - 924 meters out, 924 meters back, safety harness required, the green river 267 meters beneath your feet. This catwalk was originally built for structural inspection; guides tell you the story of the 1970s construction, the technical challenges of the COR-TEN arch, and anecdotes from Bridge Day - the third Saturday in October, when the bridge is closed to traffic and hundreds of BASE jumpers leap from the deck.

days_plan.itin_title: West Virginia Loop - From the Appalachians to New River Gorge

map_keywords: New River Gorge National Park Canyon Rim Visitor Center
Long Point Trail New River Gorge

visits.text: Push open the unassuming doors of Muscle Shoals Sound Studio and enter hallowed ground where the magic of American music was forged by four visionary session musicians. This modest building on a busy highway witnessed Cher reinventing herself, Rod Stewart finding his raspy soul, and the Rolling Stones laying down three tracks during a legendary recording session. Stand on the same porch where Mick and Keith posed for photos, or recreate Cher's iconic album cover shot at the entrance to 3614 Jackson Highway.

Experience Frank Lloyd Wright's democratic vision at the Rosenbaum House, the only Wright-designed structure in Alabama and a pristine example of his affordable Usonian concept. Built in 1939 for newlyweds Stanley and Mildred Rosenbaum, every element from built-in furniture to clerestory windows bears the architect's revolutionary touch. The intimate guided tour reveals how Wright believed families should spend less time indoors and more time embracing nature's beauty.

Pay homage to one of history's most remarkable women at Ivy Green, the Tuscumbia birthplace where Helen Keller learned to communicate despite being deaf and blind from infancy. Walk the grounds where teacher Anne Sullivan's patient devotion unlocked a brilliant mind, and visit the pump where Helen first connected the sign for water to the cool liquid flowing over her hands. The preserved family home and gardens offer profound insight into the determination that made Keller a global advocate for the disabled.

activities.text: Dig into golden-fried Southern comfort at Champy's, a roadside institution where chicken is cooked to order and arrives crackling hot in red plastic baskets. The no-frills atmosphere of melamine tables and checkered tablecloths sets the stage for seriously good eating, from succulent bone-in pieces to tangy coleslaw and buttery baked beans. Check out the vintage jukebox and neon signs while you lick your fingers clean.

Cap your Shoals exploration with refined seasonal cuisine at Odette, Florence's destination restaurant where locally sourced ingredients meet Southern flair in an elegant yet relaxed setting. The charcuterie board is legendary, with over a dozen artisanal cheeses, house-pickled vegetables, and cured meats that the server recites from memory like poetry. Reserve a table on the terrace for golden-hour dining.

Rest your head at the GunRunner Boutique Hotel, a gloriously quirky retreat carved from a former car showroom where an original Harley guards the stairway. Each of the ten suites pays tribute to local legends, from the Sam Phillips room honoring the Sun Records founder to spaces celebrating Muscle Shoals musicians and Billy Reid fashion. Guitars wait to be strummed, the rooftop terrace beckons for nightcaps, and the central bar feels like a rockstar's private living room.

days_plan.itin_title: Alabama Deep South Itinerary: Civil Rights, Music and Gulf Coast

places.name: Flagstaff

visits.text: Grand Canyon - Mather Point - Even though the Grand Canyon isn't on Route 66, it's 80 miles north of Flagstaff and skipping it would be absurd. Mather Point, right next to the visitor center, offers the first view into the canyon. 277 miles long, 6,000 feet deep, 2 billion years of geological strata. Nothing prepares you for what you see.

Bright Angel Trail - You don't need to descend to the bottom (2-day round trip). The hike down to the first rest house (1.5 miles round trip, 1h30) already gives you a dizzying perspective. Warning: it's always hotter at the bottom than the top, and the climb back up takes twice as long as going down. Bring water.
Yavapai Point - The best spot for sunset. The strata shift from red to purple as shadows descend into the canyon. Arrive 30 minutes before sunset - spots fill up quickly.

activities.text: Evening in Flagstaff - This college town at 7,000 feet elevation is cool and pleasant even in summer. Route 66 runs right through the historic downtown. Craft breweries are plentiful and downtown is pedestrian-friendly and lively in the evening.

days_plan.itin_title: Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica

map_keywords: Grand Canyon South Rim Mather Point Arizona

visits.text: Embrace the relaxed, bohemian atmosphere of Folly Beach, where wide sandy shores, gentle waves, and colorful surf shops create an irresistible coastal vibe. The fishing pier provides perfect sunrise viewing as the sky transforms into a pastel masterpiece reflected on the water. This barrier island maintains an authentic beach town character distinct from more developed coastal destinations.

Explore the Lighthouse Inlet Heritage Preserve at the island's northern tip for striking views of the historic Morris Island Lighthouse standing sentinel offshore. Quieter beaches stretch along this protected area, perfect for long contemplative walks. The preserve's maritime forest and salt marsh ecosystems showcase the natural beauty that once defined the entire Carolina coast.

activities.text: Kayak or paddleboard through the salt marshes surrounding Folly Beach, gliding past egrets, dolphins, and hidden waterways that reveal the Lowcountry's ecological richness. The island's surf breaks attract wave riders, while the funky downtown area invites evening exploration. Folly's laid-back energy blends natural beauty with creative spirit in equal measure.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

places.name: Fort Benton

visits.text: The Grand Union Hotel opened November 2, 1882, at a total cost of $200,000 — the finest hotel between St. Louis and Seattle, built at the absolute peak of the steamboat era, seven years before Montana became a state. US Army officers, Canadian Mounties, and road agents all signed the same register. The Northern Pacific Railroad arrived in Helena in June 1883 and killed the steamboat trade almost overnight; the hotel went bankrupt by 1884. Restored and reopened in 1999, it's now Montana's oldest continuously operating hotel — 26 rooms, many overlooking the river. Dinner at the Union Grille is the best meal on the entire trail.

The Steamboat Levee stretches a mile along the Missouri through the center of Fort Benton, and this is where the trail becomes visceral. A full-size keelboat replica is moored at the levee; interpretive signs tell the story of the July 13, 1805 Lewis and Clark passage — the farthest navigable point of the Missouri and the last flat water before the Great Falls portage. The remains of the original adobe fort from 1847, renamed Fort Benton on Christmas 1850, stand at the levee's north end. The bridge across the Missouri, standing since 1888, closes the downstream view.

The Museum of the Upper Missouri and the Museum of the Northern Great Plains share a campus two blocks from the levee and together cover Fort Benton's full arc — fur trade, steamboat era, the Whoop-Up Trail to Alberta, homestead boom, and ranching culture. The Old Fort Benton site next door preserves two original adobe blockhouses from the 1840s trading post — the oldest standing structures in Montana. Allow 75 minutes for both; combined paid admission.

activities.text: Fort Benton is the put-in point for the Upper Missouri River Breaks National Monument — 149 miles of Wild and Scenic River corridor that the National Park Service describes as the most intact section of the Missouri River Lewis and Clark traveled. Float trips ranging from half-day to week-long expeditions launch here. Upper Missouri River Guides and Missouri River Outfitters both operate from Main Street. Even a half-day float downstream gives you white cliffs, eroded sandstone formations, and the same view Clark described on May 31, 1805.

Walk Fort Benton end to end in the evening when the light hits the levee cottonwoods from the west. The story of Old Shep — the border collie who met every train for five and a half years waiting for his deceased owner, becoming national news in 1939 — is told in a bronze statue near the levee. The entire walkable town grid: three museums, two river outfitters, a saloon, and the Grand Union Hotel, all within six blocks.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: Museum of the Upper Missouri Fort Benton

places.name: Fort Davis

visits.text: Guadalupe Mountains National Park deserves a stop even without summiting. From the Visitor Center parking lot, you face the El Capitan cliff rising 2,000 feet above the plain like a ship's prow, with Guadalupe Peak (8,751 ft, highest point in Texas) behind it. The park protects the world's most extensive Permian fossil reef, formed 265 million years ago in a shallow inland sea. The Pinery Trail (1.9 miles RT from the Visitor Center) passes the ruins of a Butterfield Overland Mail station (1858) without difficult elevation gain - ideal for capturing the park's atmosphere in an hour.

Fort Davis National Historic Site is one of the best-preserved frontier forts in the American West. Founded in 1854 on the Chihuahua Trail to protect wagon trains, it became the base for Buffalo Soldiers between 1867 and 1885 - infantry and cavalry regiments composed of formerly enslaved African Americans, who served under white officers. The adobe and local stone buildings are carefully restored; weekend historical reenactments are worth catching.

Balmorhea State Park is home to the largest natural spring-fed swimming pool in the United States, fed by the artesian San Solomon Springs. Flow exceeds 15 million gallons per day, and the temperature holds steady at 72-76°F year-round. Water clarity is exceptional - you'll swim alongside endemic freshwater fish. Reserve ahead: the pool regularly fills to capacity in season.

activities.text: McDonald Observatory sits atop Mount Locke and Mount Fowlkes at 6,791 feet elevation in the Davis Mountains, 16 miles northwest of Fort Davis. Jeff Davis County laws strictly limit light pollution - the result: some of the darkest skies in the continental United States. The observatory was financed by banker William Johnson McDonald's bequest (died 1926) and opened in 1939; the 82-inch Struve telescope was then the world's second-largest. Star Parties (Tuesday, Friday, and Saturday evenings, about 2 hours) offer views of planets, nebulae, and star clusters through telescopes. Book several weeks ahead in high season - dress warmly, temperature drops 18°F once the sun sets.

Desert Parks of Texas and New Mexico: Big Bend, Carlsbad, White Sands, and Guadalupe
Desert to Alamo: West Texas & Big Bend - 10 Days from El Paso to San Antonio

visits.text: Fort Lauderdale - The Venice of America: Over 300 miles of navigable waterways have earned Fort Lauderdale its nickname, with yacht-lined canals and waterfront mansions creating a unique urban landscape.
Las Olas Boulevard - Fort Lauderdale's Downtown Heartbeat: This tree-lined thoroughfare extends from downtown to the beach, lined with boutiques, galleries, restaurants, and sidewalk cafes.

Fort Lauderdale Beach - Where Families Replaced Spring Breakers: The wide, clean beach along A1A has evolved from its wild spring break reputation into a family-friendly destination with excellent hotels and restaurants.

Biscayne National Park - 95% Water, 100% Wonder: Florida's second-largest national park protects pristine coral reefs, mangrove shorelines, and Elliott Key, the largest island in the park's 173,000 acres.

activities.text: Water Taxi - Cruise the Canals: Hop aboard water taxis that navigate Fort Lauderdale's intricate canal system, connecting waterfront restaurants, shops, and attractions throughout the city.
Biscayne Snorkeling and Diving Tours - Explore Underwater Paradise: Boat tours depart daily from the Biscayne Institute for snorkeling adventures over coral reefs and dives to underwater wrecks.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

places.name: Fort Myers

visits.text: Fort Myers and Sanibel Island - Gateway to Island Paradise: This Southwest Florida region combines historic Edison and Ford estates with some of the most beautiful barrier island beaches in the state.

Edison and Ford Winter Estates - Where Genius Took Vacations: Thomas Edison and Henry Ford were neighbors in Fort Myers, and their adjacent estates now form a 20-acre museum showcasing inventions, gardens, and the friendship between two of history's greatest innovators.

Sanibel Island - World-Famous Shelling Destination: The island's unique east-west orientation acts as a net for Gulf shells, creating what collectors call the 'Sanibel Stoop' as visitors bend to gather treasures along the shore.

J.N. 'Ding' Darling National Wildlife Refuge - 6,400 Acres of Protected Paradise: This wildlife sanctuary covers nearly half of Sanibel Island, protecting crucial habitat for roseate spoonbills, alligators, and hundreds of other species.

activities.text: Fort Myers Beach - Tiki Bars and Gulf Sunsets: This laid-back beach town offers classic Florida experiences with seafood shacks, tourist shops, and miles of sandy shoreline.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

map_keywords: J.N. Ding Darling National Wildlife Refuge

places.name: Fort Peck Mt

visits.text: Fort Peck Dam — the world's largest hydraulically filled earth dam, built by the Army Corps of Engineers between 1933 and 1940 — created a reservoir with 1,520 miles of shoreline through the Charles M. Russell National Wildlife Refuge. Lewis and Clark passed this stretch May 8-11, 1805, noting the Milk River confluence. The dam's visitor center covers both the engineering story and the boomtown of 10,000 workers that appeared and vanished in eight years during the Depression. Stop 45 minutes, then continue west toward Fort Benton.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: Charles M. Russell National Wildlife Refuge Montana

visits.text: Fort Smith National Historic Site - Where the Wild West Met Justice: Walk the grounds where 'Hanging Judge' Isaac Parker presided over the lawless frontier, sentencing 160 outlaws to the gallows including notorious figures like Cherokee Bill. The reconstructed courtroom, jail cells, and gallows bring the brutal justice of the 1870s-1890s to vivid life.

Trail of Tears Overlook at Belle Point - Sacred Ground of Sorrow: Stand at the confluence of the Arkansas and Poteau Rivers where thousands of Cherokee, Choctaw, Creek, Chickasaw, and Seminole people passed during their forced removal from ancestral homelands. Interpretive exhibits honor this dark chapter with dignity and remembrance.

Historic Garrison Avenue - The Longest Main Street in America: Stroll this 5-mile boulevard where Victorian-era architecture houses independent boutiques, galleries, and eateries in buildings that once served soldiers, settlers, and frontier justice.

activities.text: U.S. Marshals Museum - Legends of Law Enforcement: This striking museum overlooking the Arkansas River chronicles 225 years of the nation's oldest federal law enforcement agency through interactive exhibits and artifacts from famous cases.

days_plan.itin_title: Arkansas Itinerary: Hot Springs, Ozarks and Natural Parks

visits.text: Shipshewana and Amish Country - Heading southeast from South Bend, stop in this village at the heart of America's third-largest Amish community. Black buggies share the road with cars. If you're there on Tuesday or Wednesday, the outdoor flea market (May to September) is one of the Midwest's largest. The rest of the week, craft shops and cheese dairies are worth the stop.

Fort Wayne - Riverfront and Museum of Art - Fort Wayne sits at the confluence of three rivers (St. Marys, St. Joseph, and Maumee). The riverside Promenade Park is landscaped with green spaces, water features, and a canal. Two blocks from the park, the Fort Wayne Museum of Art occupies a 1920s Art Deco building and displays a collection of 19th to 21st century American art - a quick visit but a good complement to the walk.

activities.text: Lunch at an Amish farm - Several farms around Shipshewana offer "family style" meals served at communal tables: fried chicken, mashed potatoes, apple pie. No online reservations - show up in person.

days_plan.itin_title: Indiana Circuit: Dunes, Caves, and Cities

places.name: Fort Worth

visits.text: Fort Worth Stockyards: between 1866 and 1890, over four million head of cattle passed through Fort Worth - the city earned its 'Cowtown' nickname during this era. Today, the historic stockyards district hosts the world's only daily cattle drive: twice daily at 11:30 AM and 4 PM, period-costumed cowboys drive a herd of Texas Longhorns down Exchange Avenue. It's free and surprisingly compelling right in the heart of the city.

Kimbell Art Museum: this building designed by Louis Kahn, opened in October 1972 (two years before the architect's death), is unanimously considered one of modern architecture's masterpieces. Kahn conceived sixteen cyclonic concrete vaults pierced by plexiglass skylights that diffuse silvery natural light - what critics called 'a lighting system without equivalent in the history of architecture.' The collection is remarkable: you'll find a Caravaggio, a Velázquez and a La Tour, among others. Take time to sit for a moment - the quality of light changes with the hour.

Sundance Square: 35 blocks of Fort Worth's historic downtown with perfectly restored 19th-century red-brick facades. If you still have energy after the Stockyards, this is the best place to end the day on a terrace - more urbane and elegant atmosphere, with art galleries and independent restaurants within easy reach.

activities.text: Honky-tonk night at Billy Bob's Texas in the Stockyards: founded in 1981 in a former cattle exhibition hall, it's the world's largest honky-tonk - 6,000 m² of dance floor, 40 bars and an indoor rodeo arena. Professional rodeo takes place every Friday and Saturday night. Arrive before 8 PM for a good spot facing the ring.

days_plan.itin_title: Texas Triangle: Dallas, Austin, San Antonio & the Hill Country - 8 Days

map_keywords: Billy Bob's Texas Fort Worth honky-tonk

places.name: Four Corners

visits.text: Stand at the Four Corners Monument, the only point in the United States where four state boundaries converge, allowing visitors to simultaneously occupy New Mexico, Arizona, Utah, and Colorado. The quadripoint marks a unique geographical curiosity that draws travelers seeking to literally be in four places at once. Navajo Nation vendors offer authentic crafts and traditional foods at the monument.

Photograph the bronze disk embedded in granite marking the exact intersection of state boundaries surveyed in the 19th century. While the monument itself occupies a remote desert location, the opportunity to claim presence in four states simultaneously creates memorable experiences and unique photo opportunities for visitors from around the world.

activities.text: Explore the surrounding Navajo Nation landscape where traditional hogans, grazing sheep, and vast horizons preserve ways of life stretching back centuries. The monument provides a starting point for adventures into multiple states, each offering distinct landscapes and attractions within short driving distances.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

map_keywords: Arizona Utah New Mexico Colorado border

places.name: Fredericksburg Tx

visits.text: Enchanted Rock is a pink granite dome rising 425 feet above the Hill Country, formed over a billion years ago. It's the largest pink granite monadnock in the United States - 640 acres of exposed rock. The Comanche, Tonkawa, and Apache held it sacred: the rock emits creaking sounds at night as it contracts after a hot day, a genuine phenomenon still impressive today. In 1841, Texas Ranger Jack Hays holed up alone atop the rock against a Comanche war party and held them off for three hours from the summit. Reserve your entry ahead - the park regularly reaches capacity on spring weekends.

The National Museum of the Pacific War is the only American museum entirely dedicated to the Pacific theater in World War II, located in Fredericksburg because Admiral Chester Nimitz, Commander-in-Chief of Allied naval forces in the Pacific, was born here in 1885. The exhibition covers Pearl Harbor (1941) through the Japanese surrender (1945) through weapons, vehicles, uniforms, and testimonies. Allow at minimum 2 hours.

Fredericksburg was founded in 1846 by John O. Meusebach leading a group of German immigrants - the first convoy included about one hundred pioneers. Main Street preserves the heritage: Hill Country limestone houses, Sunday Houses (small weekend cottages built by farmers to attend church in town), and architecture straddling the line between Rhineland village and Texas town. The nine blocks of the historic center can be walked in 30 minutes.

Pedernales Falls State Park offers one of the Hill Country's most pleasant natural swimming spots: pools carved in limestone by the Pedernales River, with cascades and a setting of Texas oaks. Water level and swimming access vary with rainfall - check conditions on the park website before heading out. The Twin Falls hike (1.9 miles RT) follows the river to two secondary falls rarely mentioned in guides.

Luckenbach is a hamlet of three buildings - an 1849 post office-store-bar, a blacksmith shop, and a barn - serving as an informal country music venue. Willie Nelson and Waylon Jennings made the place famous in the 1970s. On weekends, local musicians play under the oaks starting in the afternoon, no reservation or tickets - bring your chair and your beer.

activities.text: The Highway 290 Wine Road concentrates some forty wineries over 30 miles between Fredericksburg and Johnson City. The Hill Country is the United States' fifth wine region by number of wineries. The varieties that succeed best on limestone and clay-limestone soils are Tempranillo, Viognier, Mourvèdre, and Sangiovese. Tastings happen directly at the producers, often outdoors under oak trees - avoid stringing too many estates together in one day, the road is winding.

The LBJ Ranch in Stonewall, 16 miles east of Fredericksburg, is the family ranch of 36th President Lyndon Baines Johnson, designated a National Historical Park. You can see LBJ's childhood home, his grave under a grove of trees, and the private airstrip he built for Air Force One. Guided bus tours provide access to the ranch proper.

The Willow City Loop is a 13-mile loop on a private rural road (open to public) north of Fredericksburg, traversing the hills and granite ravines of the Llano Uplift. In April, you'll find here one of Texas's finest bluebonnet displays - the Texas bluebonnet, state flower - and masses of Indian paintbrush along the roadway. Outside bloom season, the landscape of rolling hills and walnut trees remains handsome, but the road is mainly worth the trip in spring.

Hamilton Pool Preserve is a natural grotto with a 50-foot waterfall dropping into a jade-green pool carved in limestone, 28 miles west of Austin. Swimming is subject to regular water quality testing and may be prohibited after heavy rains. Reservation required to enter the park - slots book out several weeks ahead.

visits.text: The Tower District is the neighborhood that makes you want to linger in Fresno longer than you'd planned. Built in the 1930s as a streetcar suburb, it centers around the Tower Theatre - a 1939 Art Deco gem designed by S. Charles Lee, the architect behind several legendary Hollywood movie palaces. The building cost $100,000 during the Depression, a staggering sum at the time, and seated 900 in an auditorium oriented 45 degrees from the facade. Now converted into a live performance venue, it hosts jazz, comedy, and rock shows. Stroll down Olive Avenue in the evening - the street closes on Thursdays for the night market strung with fairy lights - and take the opportunity to prep your gear before heading up into the mountains tomorrow.

Return to Fresno via CA-41 along the Sierra foothills - past peach and orange groves you may have flown over on arrival. Stop in Coarsegold, a historic Gold Rush village whose antique shops and rustic architecture deserve a few photos before you hit the valley floor.

If you didn't have time to see them on Day 1, revisit the Forestiere Underground Gardens - 65 rooms, grottos, and subterranean gardens hand-dug by Baldassare Forestiere from 1906 to 1946 across 10 acres, descending up to 25 feet below the surface. The guided tour lasts about an hour. The site is closed from December to mid-March and on Tuesdays, so check ahead.

Final day in Fresno - take the opportunity to explore downtown, often overlooked but dotted with numerous murals and outdoor sculptures. Pedestrian Fulton Street cuts through the historic core and concentrates a handful of restored Art Deco buildings. It's also a chance to visit Armenian Town, the historic neighborhood of the Armenian diaspora that helped develop Central Valley agriculture in the early 20th century.

activities.text: Detour to the Forestiere Underground Gardens before heading to your hotel. A Sicilian immigrant, Baldassare Forestiere, spent 40 years (from 1906 until his death in 1946) digging with a pickaxe - no blueprints, no architect - a network of 65 rooms, grottos, and subterranean gardens across 10 acres, descending up to 25 feet below ground. He planted citrus trees and grapevines that still thrive today. The site has been listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1977. Plan for about an hour of guided tour - book online, as group sizes are limited.

If you have kids or are simply curious, Fresno Chaffee Zoo is worth a late-day stop. It's home to more than 190 species across 30 acres and notably features an area dedicated to Sierra Nevada wildlife. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours depending on your pace.

Before you leave or return your car to the airport, take one last walk through the Tower District. On Thursday evenings, the Olive Avenue night market, strung with fairy lights, gathers local producers and artisans - if you're leaving another day, the street's cafes and art galleries are worth a stop for one last Sierra coffee.

days_plan.itin_title: Sierra California Road Trip: Sequoia and Kings Canyon
Sierra California Road Trip: Sequoia and Kings Canyon
Sierra California Road Trip: Sequoia and Kings Canyon

places.name: Friday Harbor

visits.text: San Juan Islands Ferry — Cruise through the scenic San Juan archipelago aboard Washington State Ferries, weaving between 172 named islands and countless rocks. The 3-hour journey from Anacortes offers chances to spot orcas, harbor seals, and bald eagles.

Lime Kiln Point State Park — Watch for orca whales from this rocky shoreline on San Juan Island's west side, one of the world's best land-based whale watching locations. Three resident pods totaling 75 southern resident orcas frequent these waters May through September.

American Camp National Historical Park — Explore this southern San Juan Island site commemorating the 1859-1872 Pig War boundary dispute between the US and Britain. Rolling prairies, historic buildings, and seaside trails reveal the peaceful resolution of this territorial conflict.

English Camp — Visit the British Royal Marines encampment on Garrison Bay featuring restored barracks, blockhouse, and formal garden. The peaceful setting with white picket fences belies the tense standoff that nearly sparked war over a pig.

activities.text: Kayaking among the islands — Paddle calm waters between rocky islets watching for harbor seals, river otters, and porpoises. The protected channels offer beginner-friendly sea kayaking with dramatic scenery and abundant marine life.

Friday Harbor exploration — Browse art galleries, boutiques, and seafood restaurants in this charming port town. The walkable downtown maintains its maritime character with fishing boats moored alongside whale watching vessels.

Fresh seafood dinner — Dine on Dungeness crab, Penn Cove mussels, and Pacific salmon caught in surrounding waters. Island restaurants emphasize local ingredients with water views and casual Northwest atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Washington State's I-90 Corridor: Seattle, Snoqualmie Pass & Alpine Lakes

map_keywords: Washington State Ferries San Juan Islands

places.name: Furnace Creek

visits.text: En route to Death Valley, Kings Canyon National Park offers one or two impressive scenic stops if the road is open - the canyon itself is one of North America's deepest, with walls descending up to 2,400 meters below the ridges. Check road conditions before departing: certain sections close in winter. The descent into Death Valley then takes several hours and crosses landscapes that change dramatically - from alpine forest to arid badlands.

Zabriskie Point is Death Valley's most photographed overlook - and for good reasons. From its 229-meter elevation, you survey a labyrinth of yellow and brown hills sculpted by water over thousands of years, with Death Valley's salt flats in the background and the Panamint Mountains behind. The central landmark is called Manly Beacon, named after one of the first Gold Rush prospectors to cross the valley. The site takes its name from Christian Zabriskie, director of the Pacific Coast Borax Company. Come at golden hour, preferably sunset - the colors shift from straw yellow to burnt orange in minutes. It also served as the setting for Michelangelo Antonioni's 1970 eponymous film.

Badwater Basin is the lowest point in North America - 86 meters below sea level. Look toward the cliff face opposite: a "Sea Level" sign hangs 86 meters up, barely visible from the road. The white, cracked surface you see is pure salt, residue of a prehistoric 100-kilometer-long lake that has since evaporated. The salt polygons form through successive cycles of freezing, thawing, and evaporation. In summer, ground temperatures can exceed 70°C - visit before 10am or after 4pm. From here, the summit of Telescope Peak in the Panamint Mountains rises 3,368 meters above you, less than 30 kilometers away as the crow flies.

Mesquite Flat's dunes are the park's most accessible and best-known - a dune field formed about 10,000 years ago by winds converging in a Y-shaped valley, trapping sand between the mountains. The tallest dune reaches 30 meters. There are no marked trails: you walk freely on the sand. Early morning, the dunes bear the nocturnal tracks of kangaroo rats and rattlesnakes - watch where you step. It's also one of the filming locations for Star Wars Episode IV (Tatooine). Visit at sunrise for shadows cast across the ridges.

activities.text: Take Artist's Drive in late afternoon - a one-way 14-kilometer loop along the multicolored cliffs of Artist's Palette. The pink, purple, green, and orange hues come from iron and manganese oxides in the volcanic rock. The lower the light drops, the more intense the colors become. The road is accessible to standard cars and takes about 45 minutes.

Death Valley is one of the world's finest places for stargazing - no artificial lighting for dozens of kilometers in any direction. After dinner, settle in the desert at a distance from the Furnace Creek parking lot and wait for your eyes to adjust. The Milky Way is visible to the naked eye on moonless nights. Check moon phases before your stay.

Stop at Devil's Golf Course halfway between Furnace Creek and Badwater: a field of raw, sharp salt stalactites covering the valley floor for kilometers. The name comes from the idea that only the devil could play golf on such terrain. Walk carefully - the formations cut. It's one of the park's strangest landscapes.

days_plan.itin_title: California National Parks Road Trip: Yosemite, Sequoia, and Death Valley
California National Parks Road Trip: Yosemite, Sequoia, and Death Valley
California Desert Road Trip: Death Valley and the Mojave

Furnace Creek Visitor Center Death Valley
Mesquite Flat Sand Dunes Death Valley

visits.text: Gainesville - A Funky College Town with Serious Natural Wonders: Home to the University of Florida, this eclectic city combines vintage shops, dive bars, and craft coffee with some of Florida's most remarkable geological sites.

Paynes Prairie Preserve State Park - Wild Horses and Bison in Florida: This 21,000-acre savanna south of Gainesville is one of the few places in Florida where wild horses and bison roam free across grasslands that once fed cattle drives to the port of St. Augustine.

Devil's Millhopper Geological State Park - A Sinkhole Garden of Eden: This 120-foot-deep sinkhole creates a miniature rainforest ecosystem where 232 steps descend past waterfalls and ferns to a primeval world.
University of Florida Campus - Architectural Beauty and Gator Pride: Wander through expansive greens surrounded by red brick buildings at one of America's largest public universities.

activities.text: Devil's Den Spring - Prehistoric Underground Pool: This privately owned spring inside a dry cave offers year-round snorkeling and scuba diving in 72-degree water where ancient fossils have been discovered.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: You climb Bouthillier Street to an Italianate brick house built in 1859-60 and presented to Grant by grateful local Republicans upon his return from the Civil War on August 18, 1865. Grant used it as his official voting address during the 1868 presidential campaign and visited throughout his presidency (1869-1877), returning one last time in 1880. His children donated the house to Galena in 1904 as a public memorial. Original furnishings remain throughout the rooms. Interpreter-led tours run Wednesday through Sunday.

You wander along Main Street past the DeSoto House Hotel, opened in 1855, where both Abraham Lincoln and Stephen Douglas lodged and spoke during the 1858 campaign. More than 125 shops and restaurants occupy intact 19th-century commercial buildings along the steep half-mile corridor. The Galena & U.S. Grant Museum holds Grant memorabilia including Thomas Nast's monumental 1895 painting Peace in Union - 9 feet tall and 12 feet wide. Museum admission charged; walking the street itself is free.

activities.text: Galena Cellars Vineyard sits on rolling farmland in the Galena driftless country a few miles south of downtown. The Driftless Region - bypassed by the last glacial advance - gives this corner of Illinois and Iowa its distinctive topography: limestone ridges and creek-cut hollows unlike the flat plains stretching south and west. The vineyard produces cold-hardy grape varietals; an outdoor patio delivers long views over the hills. A downtown tasting room on Main Street is also open daily.

You drive 20 miles east of Galena to Apple River Canyon, where the river has sliced 200 feet deep through Silurian dolomite limestone - geology roughly 430 million years old, with fossils exposed along the creek bed. Five trails cover the canyon rim and valley floor, offering some of the most dramatically vertical landscapes in the region. Fee applies for park access.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa and Midwest Road Trip: Great Plains Loop
Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends
Iowa Backroads: From the Missouri Bluffs to the Mississippi Cliffs

visits.text: The Strand, Galveston's main historic district street, was nicknamed the 'Wall Street of the South' in the late 19th century - Galveston was then the largest city in Texas and the world's leading cotton port. On September 8, 1900, a Category 4 hurricane swept across the island with 130-mph winds and a 15-foot storm surge. The catastrophe claimed roughly 8,000 lives - estimates range from 6,000 to 12,000 - and remains the deadliest natural disaster in United States history. The Victorian buildings that survived along The Strand are now restored. Stroll the sidewalk and study the cast-iron facades: some details date from the 1880s.

Bishop's Palace, on Broadway Avenue, is one of the most spectacular Victorian mansions in the United States. Built between 1887 and 1892 by architect Nicholas Clayton for the Gresham family, then purchased by the Catholic Diocese of Galveston in 1923, it survived the 1900 hurricane thanks to its stone and steel structure. The interior - paneling in different precious woods, carved fireplaces, stained glass - has remained virtually intact since construction. Allow 45 minutes for the guided tour.

The Galveston Seawall, built starting in 1902 following the hurricane, now measures over 10 miles long and 17 feet high - one of the largest civil engineering projects in American history. Simultaneously, the entire city was raised several feet by sand pumping. The seawall runs along Seawall Boulevard, lined with seafood restaurants and bars. If you're there in the evening, the raking light on the Gulf from the seawall is magnificent.

activities.text: Galveston Island's beaches stretch 32 miles. East Beach and Stewart Beach, on the island's east end, are the liveliest with facilities and restaurants. Gulf water reaches 80-86°F from June through September. Watch out for jellyfish in summer - an anti-jellyfish spray sold locally can make swimming more comfortable.

Operators based at Galveston Harbor run boat tours to observe bottlenose dolphins in Galveston Bay. These dolphins are year-round residents in the Gulf's shallow waters. Morning tours offer the best viewing chances, before maritime traffic disturbs the pods.

days_plan.itin_title: Gulf Coast: Lafayette to South Padre Island - 9 days

visits.text: Yellowstone National Park North Entrance — Enter America's first national park through the Roosevelt Arch built in 1903. The Montana gateway provides access to Mammoth Hot Springs and Lamar Valley wildlife viewing.

Mammoth Hot Springs — Explore terraces of travertine formed by hot springs depositing calcium carbonate. The ever-changing formations create rainbow-colored steps where thermophile bacteria produce brilliant hues.

Lamar Valley — Scan the Serengeti of North America for wolves, grizzlies, bison, and elk. This glacial valley offers the park's premier wildlife viewing with herds visible across open sagebrush landscapes.
Tower Fall — Photograph this 40-meter waterfall plunging from volcanic pinnacles. The cascade named for the tower-like formations dropping into the Yellowstone River canyon.

activities.text: Wildlife photography in golden hour — Position for dawn or dusk when animals are most active and lighting is optimal. Lamar Valley and Hayden Valley offer best opportunities.
Gardiner riverside dining — Enjoy elk steaks and bison burgers at restaurants overlooking the Yellowstone River. This park gateway town maintains Western character.

days_plan.itin_title: Montana's Wild Landscapes: Glacier National Park to Yellowstone Geysers

places.name: Gary Indiana

visits.text: You park in front of a 1930s Art Deco building facing Lake Michigan and discover one of America's least-known historic landmarks. This National Historic Landmark honors Octave Chanute - a French-American engineer who conducted the world's first glider flight tests here from Miller Beach dunes in 1896, laying theoretical foundations that directly inspired the Wright brothers. The second floor hosts an exhibition on the Tuskegee Airmen, America's first African American military pilots. The open terrace on the lake offers clear views of Chicago 28 miles away on clear days. Marquette Park beach is free and continuously accessible - one of Indiana's finest, five minutes from the highway.

You park in front of a 400-square-foot brick house on an ordinary residential street and read the commemorative plaque on the facade: this is where the nine Jackson family children were born and raised, including Michael in 1958. The family left Gary in 1969 for Los Angeles, the same year the Jackson 5 signed their first contract with Motown Records in Detroit. The address - 2300 Jackson Street - likely inspired the siblings' stage name, though no source confirms this officially. The house is private and not open for tours; the exterior can be freely photographed from the public sidewalk. The surrounding neighborhood concretely illustrates Gary's economic decline since the 1970s.

You cross Miller Beach - a neighborhood of artists' bungalows sandwiched between the national park dunes and steel mills - and emerge on white sand beach integrated into the national park perimeter. The visual paradox is one of the Midwest's most striking: to your left, Gary Works' industrial towers smoke day and night; to your right, wooded dunes; before you, Lake Michigan. It's exactly on these dunes that Octave Chanute experimented with his gliders in 1896. Come at sunset - orange light reflects on water between steel towers, and the contrast produces one of Indiana's most astonishing photos.

activities.text: You drive up Washington Street toward downtown Gary and look up at City Methodist Church - a 1926 neo-Gothic church built for 1,500 worshippers, abandoned in the 1970s, and since colonized by trees piercing its collapsed ceiling. Its Gothic arches invaded by vegetation have been photographed by artists worldwide, making Gary one of America's most documented post-industrial sites. The former Union Station, Palace Theater, and rows of condemned Victorian houses testify to a city that counted 100,000 residents in 1930 and has lost half. All this is visible from public streets in daytime - don't stray from main routes.

You drive along Lake Shore Drive westward and glimpse, from the public road, the titanic structures of Gary Works - a forest of blast furnaces and cooling towers set directly on Lake Michigan's shore. Founded in March 1906 by U.S. Steel on leveled marshes and dunes, this mill was built in less than two years with an entire city constructed around it to house its workers. At its 1953 peak, Gary Works produced over 35 million tons of steel annually and employed 32,000 people. Today North America's largest steel complex, its workforce is reduced to a few thousand. Stop at dusk - the staging between lake, dunes, and heavy industry resembles nothing else in America.

days_plan.itin_title: Detroit to Chicago: Great Lakes Road Trip - 8 Days

visits.text: Great Smoky Mountains National Park — Explore America's most visited national park protecting 2,114 square kilometers of ancient mountains. The UNESCO World Heritage Site preserves old-growth forests, diverse wildlife including black bears, and over 2,100 kilometers of streams.

Cades Cove — Drive the 18-kilometer loop through this historic valley preserving 19th-century homesteads, churches, and grist mills. White-tailed deer, wild turkeys, and black bears frequent the meadows surrounded by mountains.
Clingmans Dome — Hike to the park's highest point at 2,025 meters for 360-degree views from the observation tower. The half-mile paved trail gains 100 meters elevation through spruce-fir forest.
Laurel Falls — Walk 4.2 kilometers roundtrip to this 24-meter waterfall, one of the park's most popular destinations. The paved trail follows a mountain stream through forest to the cascading falls.

activities.text: Wildlife watching — Scan for black bears, elk, and synchronous fireflies depending on season. Early morning and dusk offer best viewing in Cades Cove and Cataloochee Valley.
Gatlinburg strip exploration — Browse Appalachian crafts, moonshine tastings, and family attractions. The mountain resort town offers tourist amenities and Smoky Mountain access.

days_plan.itin_title: Appalachian Road Trip: Great Smoky Mountains & Blue Ridge
Tennessee Music & Mountains Road Trip: Nashville to Great Smoky Mountains

visits.text: Stroll along Georgetown's Front Street, the main corridor of this remarkably preserved historic district running parallel to the Sampit River. Eighteenth and nineteenth-century buildings frame the waterfront, creating one of the most relaxed and picturesque settings in the Lowcountry. The town's unhurried pace and architectural character transport visitors to an earlier era of Southern coastal life.

Visit the Rice Museum housed in the historic Old Market Building, examining the role of rice cultivation in shaping South Carolina's economy and culture. Georgetown once served as the center of the colonial rice industry, and the museum's exhibits illuminate this pivotal chapter of regional history. The Belltower overlooking the waterfront has become an iconic symbol of the town.

activities.text: Walk the Harborwalk Marina at sunset, where open waterfront paths provide views of the Sampit River and surrounding marshlands. The measured, unhurried atmosphere invites lingering strolls past historic buildings and working boats. Georgetown's authentic character offers an alternative to busier coastal destinations while maintaining easy access to surrounding beaches and natural areas.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

places.name: Gettysburg

visits.text: You begin your Gettysburg visit at the Museum and Visitor Center, the essential starting point for understanding the deadliest battle of the Civil War. From July 1 to 3, 1863, Union and Confederate armies clashed here on the hills and fields of Pennsylvania, leaving more than 51,000 dead, wounded, or missing in three days. The museum preserves the Cyclorama - a 360-degree panoramic painting 377 feet in circumference depicting Pickett's Charge on July 3. The introductory film, produced by History Channel, effectively prepares you for the battlefield visit. Book tickets online; the museum is open 8am to 5pm March through November, 9am to 4pm in winter.

The 9.4-square-mile Gettysburg battlefield is traversed by more than 40 miles of roads dotted with 1,300 monuments, 400 cannons, and 148 historic buildings, making it one of the largest outdoor sculpture collections in the world. You climb Little Round Top, the hill whose defense by Colonel Joshua Chamberlain on July 2 saved the Union line, then descend into the jagged rocks of Devil's Den, scene of fierce fighting by Confederate sharpshooters. The Virginia Memorial marks the starting position of Pickett's Charge, that desperate frontal assault across a mile of open field on July 3, which cost the Confederacy more than 6,000 men in less than an hour. Hire an official Licensed Battlefield Guide for a 2-hour car tour (about $80), or download the NPS app for a self-guided tour.

The Soldiers' National Cemetery, consecrated on November 19, 1863, is where Abraham Lincoln delivered the Gettysburg Address - one of the shortest and most powerful speeches in world political history, 272 words that redefined the meaning of the war and the nation. The cemetery holds more than 3,500 Union soldiers, including hundreds of unknowns identified by their state of origin. The headstones arranged in concentric semicircles create a particular solemnity. Come in late afternoon, when the tour buses have left and the place regains its calm - the low light on the rows of white stones is striking.

activities.text: The Gettysburg bike tour is a different experience from the car tour: you cross the same fields as the soldiers, you feel the slope of the hills, and you physically understand why Little Round Top was so strategic to hold, and why Pickett's Charge across a mile of open ground was a near-suicidal mission. GettysBike offers guided or self-guided tours with electric bikes for those wanting to conserve energy. The battlefield is dotted with interpretive signs and QR codes linked to the NPS app. Arrive early in the morning: battlefield roads are quiet before 9am and crossing the misty fields in the dawn light is memorable.

Gettysburg is reputed to be one of the most haunted towns in the United States, and it would be hard to doubt after three days of carnage on its soil. Candlelit Ghost Tours at night walk through downtown streets and around former field hospitals, recounting stories of soldiers who died on site and testimonies from current residents. Whether you believe in ghosts or not, the nighttime setting of historic downtown Gettysburg, its 19th-century houses, and the quality of historical narratives accompanying the paranormal anecdotes make the outing worthwhile.

days_plan.itin_title: Pennsylvania Loop: From Amish Country to the Appalachians - 11 Days

map_keywords: Gettysburg National Military Park Visitor Center

visits.text: Alaska Marine Highway Ferry - The six-hour crossing from Valdez to Whittier traverses Prince William Sound aboard state-operated ferries that accommodate vehicles and passengers. This scenic voyage passes glaciers, forested islands, and abundant marine wildlife, with onboard naturalists during summer months providing interpretation.

Whittier - This unusual community exists almost entirely within two large buildings, a legacy of its WWII military origins. The town serves as a major port for Prince William Sound cruises and provides access to the Anton Anderson Memorial Tunnel, North America's longest combined highway-rail tunnel at 2.5 miles.

Portage Valley - The route from Whittier passes through this glacier-carved valley, with views of Portage Glacier from visitor facilities operated by the U.S. Forest Service. The receding glacier, which has retreated significantly in recent decades, offers a visible example of climate change impacts.

activities.text: Girdwood Resort Area - Nestled in the Chugach Mountains 40 miles south of Anchorage, this four-season resort community offers aerial tram rides to 2,300-foot elevation for panoramic views, hiking trails through temperate rainforest, and access to both alpine and coastal ecosystems.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords

visits.text: You pull into Makoshika State Park - the name comes from Lakota, meaning 'bad land' - and the landscape justifies it: hoodoos, caprocks, fluted hillsides, and natural bridges carved from Hell Creek Formation sandstone. Over ten dinosaur species have been found here, including T. rex and Triceratops. A 600-pound juvenile female Triceratops skull, excavated during a 1990-91 dig led by paleontologist Diane Gabriel, is on display at the visitor center. In 1997, Jack Horner and Bob Harmon found a nearly complete Thescelosaurus skeleton here. The K-T boundary line - the geological marker of the mass extinction 66 million years ago - is visible in the rock walls. Fee applies for non-Montana residents.

Stop at the Frontier Gateway Museum in downtown Glendive. It houses a full-size skeleton cast of 'Margie', a Struthiomimus found near Glendive, plus exhibits on the Yellowstone River, the town's railroad history, and the cattle era. William Clark described these badlands in his journal on July 30, 1806, calling them the 'birnt hills' - the pale sandstone cliffs visible today from the river. Free or low-cost admission.

Walk down to the Yellowstone River at Jaycee Park. This stretch is one of the best places in Montana to find Yellowstone agates - semi-precious stones formed in gas pockets in volcanic rock and polished over millennia by the current. The river is broad and shallow with sandbars good for wading; late summer exposes the most gravel. No permit required for personal quantities.

activities.text: Drive the Makoshika scenic road through the heart of the badlands and stop at Sand Creek Overlook and Artist Vista Overlook. The light changes the color of the formations dramatically throughout the day; late afternoon turns the rock faces from grey-brown to deep orange. The disc golf course runs right through the badlands, which gives a sense of scale even if you don't play. Passable by regular vehicle in dry conditions.

Go agate hunting on Yellowstone gravel bars east of Glendive. Look for stones with translucent banding or a waxy sheen at public river access sites maintained by Montana Fish, Wildlife & Parks. Bring wading shoes and a bucket. The best bars are exposed at low water in late summer. No permit required for personal quantities.

days_plan.itin_title: Montana East: Big Sky Country from Billings to Bismarck

visits.text: Leave New Orleans via River Road (LA-18) heading west—this route follows the Mississippi for 75 miles between NOLA and Baton Rouge. Before the Civil War, this was one of America's wealthiest regions: sugarcane plantations stretched as far as the eye could see. First stop 30 miles out: Whitney Plantation in Wallace. This is Louisiana's only museum entirely dedicated to the history of slavery. Three memorials, period slave cabins, and an 1870 church built by freedmen tell the story of those who lived and worked here by force. The guided tour lasts 90 minutes—it's intense and necessary.

Continue 15 miles to Vacherie for Oak Alley Plantation, Louisiana's most famous. Its avenue of 28 live oaks planted in the early 18th century forms a quarter-mile vegetative tunnel between the road and the house. The Greek Revival mansion dates from 1839, built by Jacques Roman. Six replica slave cabins have been reconstructed based on archaeological excavations, and the walls bear the names of more than 220 people enslaved on this property between 1836 and 1865.

Four miles from Oak Alley, Laura Plantation offers a striking contrast. This is a Creole plantation—no white columns here, but a colorful cypress wood house raised on brick pillars in Louisiana vernacular style. The estate dates from 1805 and was managed by Creole women for four generations. The tour is narrated from the manuscript of Laura Locoul, born here in 1861. This is also where the Br'er Rabbit stories were collected, told by the plantation's Senegalese slaves.

activities.text: If you still have time, drive 3 miles south to San Francisco Plantation, the most colorful on River Road—its painted facade recalls New Orleans houses. Otherwise, head to Gonzales (30 minutes) for the night. Gonzales proclaims itself the "Jambalaya Capital of the World"—if you find a local restaurant open, order some and judge for yourself.

days_plan.itin_title: Louisiana Road Trip: New Orleans and Cajun Country

visits.text: Grand Canyon South Rim — The stratified Paleozoic sequence above crystalline basement tells 500 million years of Earth history.
Rim Trail walk — One-way strolls with shuttle support; carry water and watch your footing near unfenced edges.

activities.text: Sunset viewpoint — Choose Hopi, Yavapai, or Hermits Rest for long light on temples and buttes.

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest Desert Road Trip: Zion, Bryce & Grand Canyon

visits.text: Dead Horse Point (filming location): away from the roads, vertiginous panorama over the canyon where the two women take the measure of the scope of their flight.
Edge of the Grand Canyon (in the story): surrounded by police and helicopter, Thelma and Louise refuse to surrender and choose to accelerate into the void.

days_plan.itin_title: Thelma & Louise Movie Route: Utah & Arizona Filming Locations
Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey

places.name: Grand Island

visits.text: The Stuhr Museum of the Prairie Pioneer sprawls across 200 acres southwest of Grand Island and is one of the most ambitious living history complexes in the Midwest. The centerpiece is Railroad Town - over 60 original structures relocated from across the Great Plains to recreate an 1890s prairie village complete with a blacksmith, a church, a general store, a Pawnee earth lodge, and the birthplace of Henry Fonda (born here in 1905; he personally helped relocate and restore the house in 1966). The circular Stuhr Building was designed by Edward Durell Stone - the architect of the Kennedy Center - opened in 1967 on a surrounding moat. Costumed interpreters bring the town to life from May through September. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2015. Paid entry.

The Crane Trust Nature and Visitor Center, west of Grand Island along the Platte River, sits at the center of one of the world's great wildlife events. Each spring - mid-February to mid-April, peaking in late March - over one million sandhill cranes converge on a 70-mile stretch of the Platte, representing more than 80 percent of the world population. Fossil records place cranes in Nebraska for nine million years, predating the Platte River itself. Each individual bird stays about 29 days, consuming nearly 1,600 tons of waste corn before continuing to Canadian and Alaskan breeding grounds. Guided sunrise and sunset tours during migration sell out weeks ahead - book early at the Crane Trust website.

activities.text: At dawn, drive 20 miles west to Kearney and watch cranes from the free public viewpoints: the Fort Kearny hike-bike trail bridge or the Plautz Crane Viewing Platform south of Gibbon. During peak migration in March, the pre-dawn silence gives way to the rolling rattle of hundreds of thousands of birds; the mass lift-off at sunrise can blot out the sky. At dusk, they pour back to the river in a stream that lasts an hour, silhouetted against the setting sun. Outside migration season, the Platte corridor is still productive for pelicans, herons and migrating waterfowl.

After the Stuhr Museum, take the route along the Platte's south bank back toward Grand Island - the river is shallow, braided and wide here, and herons, white pelicans and deer are routine sightings from roadside pulloffs. Stolley Park on the west edge of Grand Island is a quiet spot for a riverside picnic before dinner. The downtown holds several well-preserved late 19th-century commercial blocks and reliable local restaurants.

days_plan.itin_title: Nebraska Sandhills & Pioneer Trails: From Omaha to the Rockies

map_keywords: Stuhr Museum Prairie Pioneer Grand Island
Crane Trust Nature Visitor Center Grand Island
Plautz Crane Viewing Platform Gibbon Nebraska

places.name: Grand Junction

visits.text: Start at Colorado National Monument, just outside Fruita. Rim Rock Drive winds 23 miles along red sandstone cliffs carved by millions of years of erosion. Stop at Independence Monument View—this 450-foot monolith is the park's tallest, and every Fourth of July a flag is raised at its summit. If you only have time for one short hike, take Otto's Trail (1-mile round trip): the plunging canyon views are worth every step.

Late in the day, cruise through downtown Grand Junction. This is mainly your chance to fuel up and stock water—once you pass Green River, services become scarce. Also locate a grocery store to pack picnic supplies for the coming days.

Before heading back to Grand Junction, allow yourself one last stop at Chimney Rock, visible from Highway 24 at the park's western entrance. This ochre sandstone chimney capped with a darker rock hat is one of Capitol Reef's icons. A 3.6-mile loop trail circles the formation—the view from the top embraces the entire Waterpocket Fold.

On Highway 24 heading east, spot the Behunin Cabin right by the roadside. Built in 1882 by Elijah Behunin from red sandstone to blend into the landscape, this tiny one-room cabin sheltered a family of 13. The stop takes only a few minutes but the story is worth the detour.

The return via I-70 crosses the San Rafael Swell—and this stretch of highway is spectacular in itself. For about 30 miles, the road cuts through geologic cross-sections of red and gray sandstone, with cliffs on either side. Several rest areas offer viewpoints. Take time to stop before returning to Grand Junction.

activities.text: If you're a cyclist, Rim Rock Drive is considered one of Colorado's finest road-biking routes—locals call it the "Tour of the Moon." Otherwise, take a short walk from the Saddlehorn Visitor Center to watch desert bighorn sheep that populate the cliffs.

If your flight or plans allow, save 1.5 hours at day's end for another pass through Colorado National Monument on the Fruita side—this time in late-afternoon light. The sandstone colors change completely compared to the morning of Day 1. The Devil's Kitchen Trail (1.5 miles) is a good option for a final short hike.

days_plan.itin_title: Utah Canyon Loop: Capitol Reef & Goblin Valley
Utah Canyon Loop: Capitol Reef & Goblin Valley

map_keywords: I-70 Utah Scenic Drive San Rafael Swell

places.name: Grand Lake

visits.text: The day is dominated by crossing Trail Ridge Road, the highest continuous paved road in the United States at 12,183 feet elevation. Over 48 miles total (including 11 miles above 11,000 feet), it connects Estes Park to Grand Lake by crossing the Continental Divide. Built from 1929 to 1938 by the Civilian Conservation Corps, it receives up to 25 feet of snow in winter and requires an average of 42 days of snow removal each spring at a cost exceeding $30,000. Stop at Rainbow Curve (10,829 feet) for the view over the glacial valley, then at the Alpine Visitor Center (11,796 feet) - the highest National Park Service visitor center in the United States. Bring a jacket even in summer: temperatures at the summit are often 20°F colder than in Denver.

On the west side, the Kawuneeche Valley follows the headwaters of the Colorado River - the river that irrigates seven states and provides water to 40 million people originates in this park. Stop at the Kawuneeche Visitor Center, then watch for elk and moose in the meadows bordering the river. The Holzwarth Historic Site, accessible from a parking lot just north of the center, preserves cabins from a 1917 ranch - one of the country's first dude ranches.

Grand Lake, founded in 1881, is Colorado's largest natural lake with a maximum depth exceeding 380 feet. The small town counts fewer than 500 permanent residents but its boardwalk concentrates shops and restaurants with no chains - it's one of the few small Colorado mountain towns to have resisted commercial standardization. Late afternoon, take the short walk to Point Park for panoramic views of the lake and surrounding peaks.

activities.text: Rent a kayak or canoe on Grand Lake - the Grand Lake Yacht Club, founded in 1902, is one of the world's highest yacht clubs at 8,369 feet elevation. You can also take a motorboat to explore the two connected lakes, Grand Lake and Shadow Mountain Lake. Rental outfitters offer kayaks and boats by the hour or day from lakeside marinas.

days_plan.itin_title: Rocky Mountain National Park Loop from Denver

map_keywords: Trail Ridge Road Rocky Mountain National Park
Alpine Visitor Center Rocky Mountain National Park
Rainbow Curve Overlook Rocky Mountain National Park
Kawuneeche Valley Visitor Center Grand Lake

places.name: Grand Marais Mn

visits.text: Grand Marais sits on a natural double harbor 40 miles from the Canadian border, between the Sawtooth Mountains and Lake Superior. The Ojibwe called it Gitchi-Ba-Too-Biig, the double bay. Budget Travel Magazine named it America's Coolest Small Town. Downtown spans three blocks around Harbor Park—art galleries, artisan shops, seafood restaurants—with the lake as a constant horizon.

Walk out to Artist's Point, the rocky promontory jutting into the lake east of town. From the point, you overlook both harbor basins on one side and the vastness of Lake Superior on the other. In bad weather, waves crash just feet away—the atmosphere completely transforms. The walk from the harbor parking lot takes 15 minutes.

activities.text: The North House Folk School, established on the lakeshore since 1997, offers workshops in blacksmithing, timber framing, basketry, bread baking, or birch-bark canoe building. If your timing lines up with a one-day workshop, sign up: it's one of the most authentic North Shore experiences. Otherwise, browse the shop and tour the campus.

days_plan.itin_title: Minneapolis, Voyageurs, Isle Royale & the Upper Peninsula

map_keywords: Cascade River State Park Grand Marais
North House Folk School Grand Marais

visits.text: Great Bend and Cheyenne Bottoms - North America's Most Important Bird Migration Route: Arrive at sunrise to witness one of nature's greatest spectacles as hundreds of thousands of birds utilize this critical stopover on their continental journeys.

Cheyenne Bottoms Wildlife Area - The Largest Inland Wetland in the United States: This 41,000-acre complex of marshy basins provides the richest wetland feeding grounds in the central flyway, critical to the survival of some 320 bird species that frequent these waters.

Kansas Wetlands Education Center - Understanding an Ecological Treasure: Exhibits explain the international importance of this freshwater marsh and its role in supporting migrating waterfowl and shorebirds each spring and autumn.

Quivira National Wildlife Refuge - A Rare Inland Saltwater Marsh: Just south of Great Bend, this unusual Midwest ecosystem features trails leading to closer views of ducks, geese, and several endangered species in a landscape unlike anywhere else in Kansas.

activities.text: Sunrise Birding - Watch Pelicans Take Flight: Time your visit for dawn to observe pods of American white pelicans lifting off the water, beginning another day of their remarkable migration.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

places.name: Great Falls

visits.text: The Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail Interpretive Center is built into a bluff overlooking the Missouri, and the Corps spent more time here than anywhere outside of winter camp. The portage of five successive waterfalls took 33 days in June and July 1805, covering 18 miles on improvised log-wheeled wagons through prickly pear cactus, grizzly bear territory, and flash-flooding coulees. The 25,000-square-foot center's centerpiece is a two-story diorama of the portage; you can test your strength on a simulated boat-hauling station. An actor-portrayed 45-minute audio walk reconstructs the scene in William Clark's voice. Paid entry ($8 adults); closed Mondays and Tuesdays outside peak season.

Giant Springs Heritage State Park is a quarter-mile walk from the Interpretive Center along the River's Edge Trail, and it holds one of the largest freshwater springs in the world. Lewis described it on June 18, 1805 as 'a most beautiful fountain' — the spring discharges at 156 million gallons per day through limestone. The Roe River, flowing just 200 feet from the spring to the Missouri, long claimed the title of the world's shortest river — a distinction that has since been contested and removed from the Guinness records. The park has picnic areas, trout ponds, and a wildlife center. Free admission.

The C.M. Russell Museum covers an entire city block and holds close to 1,000 original works by Charles Marion Russell — painter, sculptor, and self-described cowboy who arrived in Montana at age 16 in 1880 and spent the rest of his life documenting the West before it vanished. His log cabin studio, built in 1903 from western red cedar telephone poles, stands on the museum grounds alongside his family home — both National Historic Landmarks since 1965. More than 3,000 pieces of Western art fill 16 galleries. Paid entry; open daily in summer.

activities.text: The River's Edge Trail runs 60 miles of paved non-motorized path through Great Falls along the Missouri, connecting the Interpretive Center to Giant Springs, the five waterfall overlooks, and the CMR Museum district. Rent a bike in town and ride the section from Giant Springs past Rainbow Falls and Black Eagle Dam — about 8 miles of riverside trail with the actual waterfalls that stopped the Corps for 33 days. The falls look modest now, controlled by power dams, but the side canyons and cottonwood banks still tell the story.

First Peoples Buffalo Jump State Park, 10 miles south of Great Falls off I-15, is a mile-long sandstone cliff where Indigenous hunters drove bison for at least 2,000 years — the remains of millions of processed animals lie in the talus below. An interpretive trail and shelter explain the archaeology. It predates Lewis and Clark by millennia, but it gives the Great Falls stop a deeper Indigenous context that the expedition barely acknowledged during its grueling month-long portage. Free admission; 30-minute drive from downtown.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: Lewis Clark National Historic Trail Interpretive Center Great Falls Montana
Giant Springs State Park Great Falls
CM Russell Museum Great Falls Montana

places.name: Green Bay

visits.text: Green Bay is the smallest city in the United States to own an NFL franchise - the Packers, founded in 1919, are also the only professional football team collectively owned by its fans (approximately 360,000 shareholders). Lambeau Field, inaugurated in 1957 and expanded to 81,441 seats, remains one of the league's most legendary stadiums. On non-game days, the guided stadium and Packers Hall of Fame tour lasts 1h30 - you enter the locker rooms, walk on the field, and touch a replica Lombardi Trophy. Book online if visiting during season (September to January).

The National Railroad Museum in Green Bay assembles one of the largest collections of locomotives and railcars in the United States, including the Union Pacific's Big Boy - the largest steam locomotive ever built, 132 tons, put into service in 1941. You can board several restored railcars, including Eisenhower's presidential car used during World War II. Count on 1h30 to complete the outdoor yard and indoor exhibitions.

The CityDeck runs along the Fox River right in downtown Green Bay - a wooden boardwalk over the water with restaurant patios and benches facing the evening reflections. This is where locals gather after work, far from the Lambeau Field excitement. Take advantage to dine on the riverfront before tomorrow's long leg to the Upper Peninsula.

activities.text: The Bay Beach Wildlife Sanctuary spans 280 hectares bordering Green Bay. This municipal refuge shelters deer, foxes, otters and injured raptors under rehabilitation - all observable from free marked trails. It's one of the rare urban green spaces where you can find yourself alone with nature less than 5 minutes from downtown. Go in late afternoon for the best observation chances.

Green Bay is the perfect meeting point between Wisconsin's two prides: beer and cheese. Downtown bars serve local craft beers accompanied by regional creamery cheeses - aged cheddar, colby, and the curious cheese curd, fresh cheese balls that squeak under the tooth. A simple and authentic way to close the day before the big transit to the Upper Peninsula tomorrow morning.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Lakes Grand Loop: Chicago, Milwaukee, Door County, Sleeping Bear, Mackinac, Pictured Rocks - 12 Days

map_keywords: Green Bay Packers Hall of Fame
Bay Beach Wildlife Sanctuary Green Bay

places.name: Green River

visits.text: Drive to Wedge Overlook, nicknamed Utah's "Little Grand Canyon." At the end of a well-maintained dirt road (passable in a regular car in dry weather), you'll reach the edge of a 1,200-foot drop above the San Rafael River. The panorama is staggering—and you'll likely be alone staring into the void. No services on-site, no water, no safety barriers.

On your way down through Buckhorn Draw, stop at the Buckhorn Wash Pictograph Panel. This Barrier Canyon–style rock art dates back more than 2,000 years—the life-size figures are among the most impressive in the Colorado Plateau. The site is accessible right from the road, about 3 miles north of the San Rafael River bridge.

In Green River, step inside the John Wesley Powell River History Museum, perched on the riverbank. Don't miss the 25-minute film "The Great Unknown," which traces Powell's 1869 expedition—three months and 1,000 miles on rivers no one had ever mapped. Downstairs you'll find replicas of period boats and a small dinosaur exhibit.

activities.text: Green River is Utah's melon capital. If you're passing through in September, the Melon Days Festival is in full swing. Year-round, it's also the launch point for kayak or raft trips on the Green River—the Desolation Canyon stretch offers Class I–II rapids accessible to beginners.

days_plan.itin_title: Utah Canyon Loop: Capitol Reef & Goblin Valley

map_keywords: John Wesley Powell River History Museum Green River

visits.text: Marvel at Falls Park on the Reedy, an urban oasis where the Reedy River tumbles over a dramatic waterfall surrounded by gardens, stone pathways, and shady trees in the heart of downtown Greenville. The curved Liberty Bridge suspension walkway floats above the falls, offering sweeping views that never fail to impress. This transformed space represents one of the finest urban parks in the American South.

Stroll along the Swamp Rabbit Trail, miles of scenic pathway following the Reedy River through beautiful natural areas and charming neighborhoods. The trail connects downtown attractions to outlying communities, providing opportunities for walking, running, and cycling. Greenville's investment in green spaces and pedestrian infrastructure has created a remarkably livable city.

activities.text: Explore downtown Greenville's wide, walkable streets lined with boutique shops, art galleries, and diverse dining options. The city's vibrant arts scene includes world-class performances and exhibitions throughout the year. Flowering gardens, hidden nooks, and benches overlooking the river invite visitors to slow down and appreciate this mountain-ringed cultural destination.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

visits.text: Moosehead Lake is the largest lake in New England - 40 miles long, up to 20 miles wide, and sitting at 1,023 feet of elevation in the boreal forest. Greenville perches at its southern tip, and from the public boat launch you can see Mount Kineo, an 800-foot rhyolite cliff that erupts from the water near the middle of the lake. The waterfront downtown is small but purposeful: outfitters, a diner, and Northwoods Outfitters - the main gear and guide hub for the region. Arrive before dusk and drive Lily Bay Road north of town - moose are frequently spotted in the ditches and beaver ponds along this stretch.

Berthed at the town dock, the SS Katahdin - locally called 'the Kate' - is the oldest vessel afloat built at Bath Iron Works, launched in 1914. She carried tourists and supplies to the Mount Kineo Resort, then spent decades as a lumber towboat hauling log booms across the lake until 1975. The Moosehead Marine Museum rescued her in 1976 and restored her completely in the 1990s, including replating the hull at Bath Iron Works. She runs cruises from June through October - the three-hour version goes north past Beaver Cove and Sugar Island, with narration on the lake's history and wildlife.

Lily Bay State Park sits 9 miles north of Greenville on the eastern shore of Moosehead Lake. The 952-acre park has lakefront campsites that rank among the most scenic in New England, plus a small swimming beach, hiking trails, and boat launch. Even on a day visit, the views across the lake toward Squaw Mountain are worth the trip. Open year-round; summer brings paddlers and anglers, winter brings snowshoers and cross-country skiers.

activities.text: Northwoods Outfitters on Lily Bay Road runs the moose safaris that Yankee Magazine voted best in New England. Registered Maine Guides take small private groups out at dawn and dusk - the times when moose are most active - either by vehicle on logging roads or by canoe on remote beaver ponds. Success rates are high: guides claim over 1,000 sightings a year. Book the early-morning tour for the best light and the highest probability of seeing bulls.
