visits.text: Humboldt - Small Town, Big-Thinking Revival: A devoted team of entrepreneurs has launched an effort to update this small eastern Kansas town in surprisingly innovative ways.
Base Camp Humboldt - Scandinavian-Inspired Glamping: Rental cabins perch around a large fishing pond, with bike rentals providing access to adjacent rail trails for multi-day adventures.
Southwind Rail Trail and Prairie Spirit Trail - 51+ Miles of Trail Adventures: These connected trails offer cycling and hiking through Kansas countryside, accessible right from Base Camp.
Humboldt Mercantile - Kansas-Made Goods and Craft Coffee: Browse a curated selection of Kansas-made products while enjoying locally roasted coffee drinks.
Water Tower Mural - Humboldt's Instagram Moment: Snap a selfie beside the colorful mural depicting the town's notable water tower proclaiming 'Life is fine in Humboldt.'

activities.text: Rail Trail Cycling - Pedal Through Eastern Kansas: Rent a bike and explore dozens of miles of converted rail corridors passing through prairie landscapes and small towns.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

visits.text: Jour 1: Step into humanity's greatest adventure at the US Space & Rocket Center, where towering Saturn V rockets pierce the Alabama sky and the dreams of space exploration come alive. Wander through the awe-inspiring Saturn V Hall, home to one of only three remaining moon rockets, and stand beneath the very engines that propelled astronauts to the lunar surface. Don't miss the meticulously recreated International Space Station module, where you'll discover the ingenious solutions astronauts use for everyday tasks in zero gravity.

Jour 1: Channel your inner astronaut at Space Camp, the legendary program that has inspired generations of future scientists and explorers since 1982. Whether you're traveling with curious kids or harboring your own cosmic dreams, the center offers immersive experiences from simulated shuttle missions to underwater astronaut training. The gift shop is a treasure trove of NASA memorabilia and space-themed souvenirs you won't find anywhere else.

Jour 2: Wander through the tree-lined streets of the Twickenham Historic District, where antebellum mansions draped in Southern grandeur transport you to a pre-Civil War era of cotton wealth and gracious living. This remarkable neighborhood boasts Alabama's finest concentration of historic homes, their columned porticos and wraparound verandas whispering tales of a bygone age. Pick up a self-guided walking tour from the Visitor Bureau and let the free audio guides bring each architectural treasure to life.

Jour 2: Discover artistic treasures overlooking the serene waters of Big Spring Park at the Huntsville Museum of Art, a jewel box of carefully curated collections. The haunting black-and-white photographs of the Vietnam: The Real War exhibition offer profound reflections on conflict, while the luminous landscapes of the Ponchin legacy celebrate the artistic bonds between a French father and son. Marvel at the Buccellati silver menagerie, where semi-precious stones and gleaming silver transform into lifelike creatures of extraordinary craftsmanship.

Jour 2: Find your peaceful center at Big Spring Park, Huntsville's beloved green heart where the city's founding spring still bubbles up from ancient limestone caves beneath your feet. Stroll across the graceful Japanese bridge reflected in lily-pad-dotted waters, or spread a picnic blanket on the manicured lawns where festivals and community gatherings have brought neighbors together for generations. The smaller park section features a dancing fountain fed by the original spring that drew settler John Hunt to establish his cabin here in 1805.

Jour 2: Journey back to the summer of 1819 at Alabama Constitutional Hall Park, where delegates gathered in a humble cabinetmaker's workshop to forge the state's founding document. The meticulously reconstructed wooden buildings surrounding the central courtyard paint a vivid portrait of frontier life, from the postmaster's quarters to the blacksmith's forge. Join a guided tour to peek inside furnished interiors and imagine the heated debates that shaped Alabama's destiny.

Jour 2: Browse the nostalgic aisles of Harrison Bros. Hardware, where original wooden counters, brass cash registers, and floor-to-ceiling shelves evoke the general stores of yesteryear. Though the nails and tacks have given way to locally crafted treasures and Rocket City memorabilia, the store's authentic 19th-century character remains gloriously intact. This is the place to find that perfect American-made souvenir that captures Huntsville's unique blend of history and innovation.

Jour 3: Lose yourself in 112 acres of botanical wonder at the Huntsville Botanical Garden, a volunteer-driven labor of love that has blossomed into one of the South's premier horticultural destinations. Glide through the grounds on a golf buggy tour, pausing at the Damson Aquatic Garden where a Japanese pavilion floats serenely above pools carpeted with water lilies. Time your visit for summer when the Daylily Garden erupts in a sunset palette of gold, coral, and rose that will take your breath away.

Jour 3: Watch children's imaginations take flight in the enchanting Children's Garden, where wading pools sparkle in the sun, dinosaur bones await discovery, and fairytale creatures peek from hidden corners. The butterfly house offers pure magic as kaleidoscopic wings flutter around you, landing on tropical blooms and sipping from shallow pools in a living rainbow of color.

Jour 3: Ascend Monte Sano to discover Burritt on the Mountain, the eclectic retirement retreat of homeopathic physician Dr. William Henry Burritt, who sought the cooler mountain air and woodland tranquility above the city. Tour the 1930s mansion furnished with period pieces, then wander the historic park where relocated log cabins and wooden farmhouses reveal how Alabama's pioneers lived and worked. Chat with costumed interpreters, meet the friendly farm goats, and try your hand at traditional handicrafts at the folk school.

Jour 3: Step into a creative wonderland at Lowe Mill, the South's largest independent arts facility housed in a magnificently repurposed textile mill. Over 200 working artists have transformed this industrial cathedral into a vibrant village of studios, galleries, and cafes where you can watch potters throw clay, painters layer color, and jewelers craft wearable art. Check the schedule for concerts on the dock, where musicians perform on a raised walkway while audiences picnic around the iconic water tower.

activities.text: Jour 1: Rise early and join the locals at the beloved Blue Plate Cafe for an authentic Southern breakfast that will fuel your adventures. Sink your fork into fluffy scrambled eggs alongside crispy bacon, impossibly creamy cheese grits, and the quintessential Southern duo of buttermilk biscuits smothered in rich sausage gravy. The bottomless coffee flows freely as regulars swap stories at the counter, offering a genuine taste of Huntsville hospitality.

Jour 1: As evening falls, treat yourself to refined Southern cuisine at Connors Steak & Seafood, where Gulf Coast flavors meet prime cuts in an elegant yet welcoming atmosphere. The Cajun Red Grouper, mesquite-grilled to perfection and served with fluffy couscous and tender collard greens, showcases the region's culinary heritage. Start with the legendary Voodoo Shrimp bathed in a spicy remoulade sauce that'll have you reaching for another.

Jour 2: Savor a leisurely lunch at Pane e Vino, Chef James Boyce's Italian-inspired haven with a terrace overlooking the park's shimmering waters. The handmade butternut squash ravioli, draped in sage brown butter, offers a delicate counterpoint to the hearty Southern fare you'll encounter throughout your journey. Pair it with a crisp glass of wine as you watch ducks glide across the lake.

Jour 3: Fuel your creativity at Piper and Leaf, a Lowe Mill gem specializing in artisan tea blends crafted from locally foraged ingredients and organic herbs. Sample their signature Southern sweet tea reimagined with wild honeysuckle or choose from inventive blends that capture Alabama's seasons in every sip. The whimsically decorated shop invites lingering over a steaming cup.

Jour 3: Hunt for Huntsville's vibrant street art, starting with the massive Koi mural where giant fish seem ready to gulp you whole at the intersection of Spring and Spragins Streets. Stand on the marked spot to experience the stunning 3D effect, then seek out the playful Welcome to Huntsville rocket mural facing Big Spring Park. Art enthusiasts can extend their treasure hunt along the Clinton Row Color Walk and the mysterious Downtown Secret Art Trail.

days_plan.itin_title: Alabama Deep South Itinerary: Civil Rights, Music and Gulf Coast

visits.text: Hutchinson - Where Space Race History Meets Subterranean Wonder: This central Kansas city delivers world-class attractions both above and below ground, from space exploration to salt mine adventures.

Cosmosphere - The World's Largest Combined Collection of U.S. and Russian Space Artifacts: Kansas's only Smithsonian-affiliated museum boasts flown spacecraft from the first three human-carrying space programs-Mercury, Gemini, and Apollo-relaunching the Space Race through extraordinary exhibits.

Strataca - 650 Feet Below Kansas: A 6-ton hoist drops visitors through a frozen aquifer and layers of rock to a museum carved from a former section of an active salt mine, with detailed underground exhibits explaining what secrets lie stored beneath the prairie.

Kansas State Fair - September Celebration of Sunflower State Pride: Each September, Hutchinson hosts the Kansas State Fair, celebrating all things Kansas with livestock exhibitions, carnival rides, concerts, and agricultural displays.

activities.text: Underground Salt Mine Tour - Experience a World Without Sunlight: Explore the vast chambers and corridors carved from ancient salt deposits, learning about this unusual underground world and its modern uses.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

visits.text: The Metropolitan Museum of Art — Explore 5,000 years of art across 2 million square feet in this encyclopedic museum founded in 1870. Highlights include the Egyptian Temple of Dendur, European Old Masters, and the American Wing's period rooms.

Vanderbilt Mansion — Tour this Gilded Age palazzo built in 1899 for Frederick Vanderbilt, grandson of Commodore Cornelius Vanderbilt. The 54-room Beaux-Arts mansion showcases Renaissance Revival architecture and Hudson River School landscape design across 211 acres.

FDR Presidential Library and Museum — Visit America's first presidential library opened in 1941, containing Franklin D. Roosevelt's papers and artifacts from his unprecedented four terms. The museum chronicles the New Deal, World War II, and Eleanor Roosevelt's advocacy.

Walkway Over the Hudson — Cross this 2-kilometer pedestrian bridge suspended 65 meters above the Hudson River, the world's longest elevated pedestrian bridge. Built on a former 1889 railroad bridge, it offers sweeping valley and mountain views.

activities.text: Wine tasting in Hudson Valley — Sample award-winning wines at family-owned vineyards planted in the rolling hills. Rieslings and Cabernet Francs thrive in the microclimates while tasting rooms offer farm-to-table pairings with Hudson River views.

Culinary Institute of America campus visit — Explore the world's premier culinary college founded in 1946, housed in a former Jesuit seminary. Student-run restaurants serve exceptional cuisine prepared by tomorrow's master chefs in training.

Antique shopping in Cold Spring — Browse Victorian-era storefronts along Main Street filled with vintage treasures, Hudson River School paintings, and artisan crafts. This charming village nestled below Storm King Mountain retains its 1800s character.

days_plan.itin_title: Empire State Road Trip: From Manhattan Skyscrapers to Niagara Falls

visits.text: Idaho City peaked in September 1863 with 6,267 residents - larger than Portland at the time and the biggest city in the entire Pacific Northwest. Gold had been discovered in the Boise Basin on August 2, 1862; within eight months, the area became the largest settled region on the Pacific coast. The town was first called Bannock City, renamed in late 1863 by the Idaho Territorial Legislature to avoid confusion with Bannack, Montana. Walk the old main street past original frontier-era buildings; the Boise Basin Museum, housed in a historic post office building, tells the whole story with artifacts and photographs. Free admission.

Boot Hill Cemetery is a short walk above town, and the names on the markers are a ledger of the gold rush: gunfights, mining accidents, diphtheria, freezing. An 1863 Kansas newspaper described Idaho City simultaneously as the next El Dorado and one of the most desolate places on earth. Beyond the cemetery, the surrounding foothills are still scarred by dredge tailing piles from operations that ran here until 1952 - ridges and furrows that look from the right angle like frozen waves.

Highway 21 northeast of Idaho City is the Ponderosa Pine Scenic Byway - and the name earns its keep. The road climbs through dense pine forest, follows the South Fork of the Payette River through a tightening canyon, and passes the marker for the ghost town of Sawtooth City (founded 1879, abandoned 1888, now a few collapsed foundations in the trees). Stop at the roadside viewpoints: this was once the busiest supply route in Idaho Territory, carrying freight by pack mule before the first wagon road opened in 1864.

activities.text: Head 22 miles northeast on Highway 21 to Kirkham Hot Springs, where thermal waterfalls pour directly off rocky cliffs into pools on the bank of the South Fork Payette River. Source water reaches 120-135°F; the pools cool to a comfortable 95-110°F depending on proximity to the river. The site sits at 4,000 feet in Boise National Forest and is day-use only (7 AM to 9 PM); bring $5 cash or an America the Beautiful interagency pass. Arrive before 9 AM on summer weekends - by mid-morning it's packed.

The stretch of Highway 21 between Lowman and Stanley is 58 miles of mountain road that takes about 90 minutes with stops - and the stops are worth it. Galena Summit at 8,701 feet is the high point, with a pullout where the full Sawtooth Valley suddenly opens below. In winter and spring this road can close without notice due to avalanche risk; check Idaho 511 before leaving Idaho City.

days_plan.itin_title: Idaho: Sawtooth Mountains, Sun Valley & Craters of the Moon

places.name: Indiana Dunes

visits.text: You park the car and immediately grasp the paradox of this place: before you, a white sand beach stretches along Lake Michigan; behind you, Gary's steel mill smokestacks rise into the sky. Indiana Dunes became America's 61st national park in February 2019, after half a century of conservationists battling industrialists who wanted to transform the dunes into an oil port. Botanist Henry Chandler Cowles formulated the concept of ecological succession here in 1899, after observing how plants progressively colonize bare dunes to create entire forests. The park counts over 1,100 plant species - a botanical density higher than most national parks. Start at the Visitor Center for a map and to check rip current alerts before any swimming.

You stand at the foot of a 126-foot dune slowly but inexorably moving inland at a documented rate of approximately 4 feet per year, swallowing trees in its path. Mount Baldy is one of the Great Lakes' most active moving dunes - in 2013, a child was swallowed in a sudden hole created by a decomposing tree under the sand; since then, free climbing has been prohibited and stabilization programs are underway. Comparative plaques on the beach show photographs from 1955 and 2020: the dune has advanced over 230 feet in 65 years. The beach at its base is freely accessible and offers, on clear days, a direct view of Chicago's Loop towers 30 miles on the horizon.

You drive along Lakeshore Drive and spot, incongruously amid dunes and pines, five Art Deco and modernist pavilions from the 1930s that seem to have landed here by mistake. These houses were pavilions from Chicago's 1933 'Century of Progress World's Fair' - an exposition on technological progress. The following year, developer Robert Bartlett had them transported by barge across the frozen Lake Michigan in winter 1935. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places, they're now rented by private individuals who maintain their restoration. Their sharp geometric forms contrast surreally with the dunes and boreal forest - photograph them from Lakeshore Drive in the morning for direct light.

activities.text: You walk 200 meters from the parking lot before white sand beach appears, with Lake Michigan's blue-green waters before you and 165-foot wooded dunes at your back. The Dune Succession Trail - a 1.5-mile loop from West Beach - is the park's most educational: you pass from bare beach to pioneer grasses, shrubs, pines, and oaks, observing the ecological succession Cowles described in 1899. Lake Michigan reaches 72 to 77°F in July-August - clear water surprisingly close to an industrial metropolis. Avoid swimming in strong north winds; rip currents form quickly in this sector.

You start from Mineral Springs Road parking and plunge into a forest of northern white cedars alongside Cowles Bog - one of Indiana's most protected wetland habitats. The trail then climbs wooded dune ridges before emerging on Lake Michigan beach, far from West Beach crowds. Over 350 bird species have been recorded in the park. Bring light gaiters in spring: the trail crosses unavoidable wet areas after April rains.

visits.text: Monument Circle - At the heart of Indianapolis' grid, this roundabout shelters the Soldiers & Sailors Monument, 86 meters of limestone inaugurated in 1902. Designed by German architect Bruno Schmitz, it honors Indiana soldiers from the Revolutionary War onward. Climb the 330 steps (or take the elevator to step 290, then 49 more steps) for a panorama 70 meters above the city. The statue at the summit, "Miss Indiana," brandishes a sword and torch.

Indianapolis Motor Speedway Museum - Indiana's most famous site. The museum, renovated in 2025, displays more than 75 race cars in a 3,500 m² building nestled between turns 1 and 2 of the oval. You'll see every car that won the Indy 500 since 1911, a tire-change simulator, and a recreation of Gasoline Alley through the decades. Take the bus tour of the track - you'll ride over the famous "Yard of Bricks," the original brick start-finish line. Plan 2 to 3 hours on site.

Indiana War Memorial - Indianapolis' memorial district covers 10 hectares in the heart of downtown, one of the nation's most extensive memorial complexes outside Washington D.C. The main building, inspired by the Mausoleum of Halicarnassus, houses a sanctuary on the top floor bathed in bluish light. Outside, the USS Indianapolis memorial recalls the 1945 sinking - of 1,200 sailors, only 317 survived.

Newfields - This 60-hectare campus combines the Indianapolis Museum of Art and a landscaped park. Collections range from classical European painting to contemporary American art. The outdoor garden changes with the seasons - in summer, light installations animate it in the evening. Allow at least 2 hours.

Eiteljorg Museum - Dedicated to Native American arts and the American West, this museum along the canal presents both Western landscape paintings and traditional objects of indigenous peoples. If the subject speaks to you, it's one of the finest museums of its kind outside the Southwest.

Mass Avenue - This diagonal street, rare in Indianapolis' grid, concentrates galleries, independent restaurants, and cocktail bars. The brick facades and vintage signs give it a character missing from the rest of downtown.

New Harmony - Allow 4 hours' drive from Bloomington to reach this village of 690 inhabitants on the Wabash River, site of two successive utopias. In 1814, German pastor Georg Rapp established his community of separatist Lutherans there - in ten years, they built 180 buildings. In 1825, Welsh industrialist Robert Owen purchased the entire town and attempted to create a "community of equals." The experiment failed within two years, but the intellectuals he had brought in (the "Boatload of Knowledge") founded America's first free public school there. Begin at the Atheneum, the visitor center signed by Richard Meier (1979) - an angular white block that stands out in the rural landscape. Next, walk to Philip Johnson's Roofless Church (1960): a brick enclosure without a roof where the sky serves as ceiling, with gilded bronze portals at the entrance and a Jacques Lipchitz statue under the interior dome. Finish with the Harmonist Labyrinth, a hedge maze conducive to contemplation.

Lincoln Boyhood National Memorial (Lincoln City) - Heading back toward Indianapolis (allow another 3 hours' drive), stop at this site where Abraham Lincoln lived from age 7 to 21. His mother Nancy is buried here - she died poisoned by "milk sickness" in 1818 after drinking milk from a cow that had grazed on white snakeroot. A reconstructed farm shows the living conditions of pioneers of that era.

activities.text: Canal Walk in late afternoon - Stroll along the White River Canal on foot or in pedal boats. The 2.5 km route traverses White River State Park, lined with gardens and sculptures. In summer, the evening light on the water is particularly pleasant.

Evening at Fountain Square - Ten minutes southeast of downtown, this retro neighborhood houses a vintage bowling alley on the top floor of an Art Deco building, concert halls, and rooftop bars. The atmosphere is laid-back and local - far from the tourist circuits.

Walk through the Harmonist Labyrinth and New Harmony gardens - The hedge labyrinth, inspired by the one created by the Harmonists in the 19th century, invites contemplative walking. The surrounding gardens, maintained by the local club, conceal a "Tree of 40 Fruits" (a grafted tree bearing 40 varieties of stone fruits).

places.name: International Falls Mn

visits.text: Stop at the Rainy Lake Visitor Center first, before setting foot on the water. The park covers 218,000 acres on the Canadian border and most of it is only accessible by boat—skip this step and you'll miss the best parts. Rangers will orient you on tours, boat access, and current conditions. The rocks visible from the dock are between 1 and 3 billion years old.

Rainy Lake is the largest of the park's four lakes: 60 miles long, 930 miles of shoreline. From the Visitor Center dock, you get your first reading of the landscape—dark water, dense pine forests, vast sky. These are the same waters that French-Canadian voyageurs paddled in canoes to transport furs northward as early as the 18th century.

The Ellsworth Rock Gardens exist nowhere else: Jack Ellsworth hand-carved more than 200 sculptures into the granite shoreline of Lake Kabetogama between 1944 and 1965, surrounded by terraced wildflowers. You can only reach them by boat from the Kabetogama Visitor Center—30 minutes of navigation. A garden built alone in the wilderness, and left intact.

Kettle Falls Hotel has stood since 1910 at the crossroads of four lakes in the heart of the park—you can only get there by boat or seaplane. Its bar is slightly tilted, a legacy of a century of ground settling. A Finnish postmistress is said to have stayed on after her husband disappeared. A stop between shores worth visiting for the atmosphere alone.

activities.text: Book the Grand Tour boat excursion from the Rainy Lake Visitor Center: 2.5 hours on the lake with an NPS ranger. The tour passes by Little American Island—where gold was discovered in 1893, triggering a rush—the Harry Overson Fish Camp, and historic cabins. It's the best way to grasp the park's history and geography in half a day.

In the evening, park on a forest road north of Rainy Lake and look up. Voyageurs is certified Dark Sky—no light pollution for dozens of miles around. The Milky Way is visible to the naked eye on most clear nights from June through September. During strong solar activity, the northern lights appear even in summer.

Rent a motorboat or kayak at Kabetogama to explore the park's 26 interior lakes. The Kabetogama Peninsula—the heart of the park, inaccessible by car—can only be discovered from the water. Look for moose at sunrise along the shallow shorelines, and common loons nesting on the islands. More than 55 miles of trails are accessible by boat.

Second night in the area—use it to try for the northern lights. Park far from any lighting and wait. Voyageurs ranks among the best aurora viewing areas in the United States: International Falls has a population of barely 5,000. September nights are the most promising.

days_plan.itin_title: Minneapolis, Voyageurs, Isle Royale & the Upper Peninsula
Minneapolis, Voyageurs, Isle Royale & the Upper Peninsula

map_keywords: Rainy Lake Visitor Center Voyageurs National Park

visits.text: En route to Iowa City, stop at the Amana Colonies (30 minutes north). Seven villages founded in 1855 by German Pietists who lived in a self-sufficient community until 1932. The brick and sandstone buildings have remained intact. You'll find woodworking shops, a communal brewery, a bakery producing wood-fired bread, and a distillery. The Woolen Mill, operating since 1857, still produces wool blankets. It's a National Historic Landmark.

Arrive in Iowa City, Iowa's first capital and a university town. The Old Capitol Building (1840) sits atop a hill at the center of the University of Iowa campus—this is where Iowa was admitted to the Union in 1846. The Greek Revival-style building is restored and open to the public (free). In 2008, UNESCO designated Iowa City a "City of Literature"—the first in the United States—thanks to the renowned Iowa Writers' Workshop, which has trained dozens of Pulitzer Prize winners.

activities.text: The downtown Pedestrian Mall is where students gather: independent bookstores (Prairie Lights is an institution), cafes, restaurants, and bars. The atmosphere is young, relaxed, and intellectual—a refreshing contrast after the small rural towns elsewhere on the circuit.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa and Midwest Road Trip: Great Plains Loop
Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs

places.name: Islamorada

visits.text: Windley Key Fossil Reef Geological State Park - A former coral quarry transformed into a geological park. The cut rock walls reveal cross-sections of fossilized reef dating back 125,000 years - you're literally looking at corals, shells, and sponges frozen in limestone. It's an open-air geology lesson wrapped up in a 30-minute walk.

Hurricane Monument - This Art Deco coral rock monument beside US-1 honors the 423 World War I veterans who died in the Labor Day Hurricane of 1935. This Category 5 cyclone, packing 185 mph winds, remains the most powerful storm ever to strike the United States. The evacuation train sent from Miami arrived too late - it was blown off the tracks by the storm surge.

Indian Key Historic State Park - This tiny island off Islamorada, accessible only by kayak or boat, served as a wrecker's village in the 1830s. In 1840, Seminoles attacked and burned it to the ground. Today, foundations overgrown with tropical vegetation and an observation tower remain. The atmosphere feels like a forgotten island lost to time.

activities.text: Feeding the tarpon at Robbie's Marina - Buy a bucket of baitfish and toss them from the dock. Silver tarpon over three feet long leap out of the water to snatch the fish - the spectacle is guaranteed. Pelicans will try to steal your bait. Go in the morning to avoid the crowds.

Kayaking to Indian Key - Outfitters at Robbie's Marina rent kayaks for the paddle to Indian Key (15-20 minutes). The crossing is easy in light wind - check conditions before launching. On the island, a marked trail loops around in 30 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West
Florida Keys Road Trip: Key Largo to Key West

map_keywords: Windley Key Fossil Reef Geological State Park

visits.text: Taughannock Falls — Hike to this 65-meter waterfall plunging into a glacial gorge carved over 10,000 years ago. The falls exceed Niagara in single-drop height, cascading over layered Devonian shale and sandstone deposited 350 million years ago.

Cornell University — Tour this Ivy League institution founded in 1865 on East Hill overlooking Cayuga Lake. The 930-hectare campus features Gothic Revival buildings, the Herbert F. Johnson Museum of Art designed by I.M. Pei, and spectacular gorge views.

Ithaca Falls — Discover this 46-meter waterfall cascading through the heart of downtown Ithaca. The easily accessible cataract flows over Devonian bedrock, creating a powerful spectacle especially during spring snowmelt or after heavy rains.

Buttermilk Falls State Park — Explore ten cascading waterfalls descending 152 meters through a narrow gorge. The Gorge Trail winds past frothing pools and mossy cliffs where Buttermilk Creek has carved intricate patterns into ancient sedimentary rock.

activities.text: Cayuga Lake wine trail — Visit family wineries along the western shore of the Finger Lakes' longest lake stretching 61 kilometers. Cool-climate Rieslings and sparkling wines benefit from the lake's moderating influence and well-drained glacial soils.

Ithaca Commons shopping — Stroll this pedestrian mall filled with independent bookstores, artisan boutiques, and farm-to-table restaurants. The outdoor plaza hosts farmers markets and live music in a vibrant college-town atmosphere.

Sunset cruise on Cayuga Lake — Glide across the tranquil waters as the sun sets behind the western hills. Evening cruises offer wine tastings while waterfowl and the occasional bald eagle provide natural entertainment.

days_plan.itin_title: Empire State Road Trip: From Manhattan Skyscrapers to Niagara Falls

visits.text: Jackson is an hour east of Vicksburg via I-20. Start at the Mississippi Civil Rights Museum, opened in 2017. The interactive galleries trace the civil rights movement in the state that was the scene of some of its most violent episodes - the Freedom Rides of 1961, the forced integration of Ole Miss in 1962, the assassination of Medgar Evers in 1963. It's a dense and moving museum; plan at least 1.5 hours.

Right next door, the Old Capitol Museum occupies the former neo-Greek capitol from 1839. This is where Mississippi voted for secession in 1861. Admission is free and the exhibits cover the state's history from Native American nations onward. The building itself, with its columned portico, is one of the most beautiful downtown.

activities.text: Explore the Fondren neighborhood, Jackson's bohemian corner. This small district is packed with independent bookstores, galleries, and Southern restaurants. This is where you'll find the best fried catfish and fried chicken in the city - Mississippi cuisine is simple, generous, and often served on paper plates.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep South Road Trip: New Orleans and the Natchez Trace
Chicago to New Orleans Road Trip: Route 66 & the Mississippi

visits.text: The Two Mississippi Museums opened December 9, 2017 - the state's bicentennial - and the Mississippi Civil Rights Museum is the only state-operated civil rights museum in the country. Its eight galleries run from the Middle Passage through Reconstruction, Jim Crow, and the movement years 1945-1975. At the building's heart, a 40-foot sculpture lights up every 30 minutes with gospel songs that powered the marches; when it activates, it stops people mid-conversation. The Emmett Till section alone justifies the trip: the Bryant's Grocery doors are here, alongside the original Jet magazine pages from 1955 that broke the story open nationwide. Free every Sunday. Allow three hours.

By 1908 one-third of Farish Street was Black-owned - doctors, banks, jazz clubs, a hospital, record studios that would shape American music. Medgar Evers ran the NAACP's state field office from above the Big Apple Inn, the restaurant that's been serving pig ear sandwiches since Mexican immigrant Juan Mora opened it in 1939. The district was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1980; by 1995 the National Trust listed it among America's Most Endangered Places. Walk the nine-block stretch for the Blues Trail markers and the restored Alamo Theatre. Lunch at the Big Apple Inn - order the pig ears, which Mora invented from a box of butcher scraps he refused to throw away.

Pulitzer Prize-winning author Eudora Welty lived at 1119 Pinehurst Street from 1925 until her death in 2001 - 76 years in the same house, writing nearly all her fiction in the same bedroom. She bequeathed the Tudor Revival home and its entire contents to the State of Mississippi; every wall is still lined with books in the positions she left them. Her mother Chestina designed the garden the same year the family moved in, planting over 40 varieties of camellias that bloom through winter. Designated a National Historic Landmark in 2004. Tours are guided only - reserve ahead. The 3 p.m. tour ends in the garden, which is the right way to close the trip.

activities.text: The Mississippi Blues Trail runs through Jackson with markers most visitors walk right past - the Subway Lounge plaque marks a legendary basement club; the Ace Records marker on Capitol Street celebrates one of the South's most successful independent labels of the 1950s. Download the Mississippi Blues Trail app before arriving, then spend two hours following markers on foot through downtown and out toward the Medgar Evers home site. Each marker is a dense paragraph of verified history; the cumulative walk gives Jackson its full musical and civil rights context before catching a flight.

F. Jones Corner and Johnny T's Bistro & Blues bookend the Farish Street nightlife revival - Johnny T's sits in the same building as the old Crystal Palace, where Duke Ellington and Louis Armstrong performed in the 1940s and 1950s. Both venues run live music on weekend nights with a nominal cover. The pineapple vodka at F. Jones Corner is a local institution. This is where Jackson residents actually go on a Friday night, which makes it the more interesting option.

days_plan.itin_title: Mississippi Delta Blues Trail: Memphis to Jackson
The Deep South via Mississippi: Shreveport to New Orleans - 10 Days

places.name: Jackson Wy

visits.text: Settle into Jackson's Town Square, framed by its four elk antler arches. Historic saloons like the Million Dollar Cowboy Bar have kept their saddles bolted to the bar stools since the 1930s. Art galleries and outdoor shops line the square—the capital of high-end Wild West.

Stop at Snake River Overlook in Grand Teton NP: this is where Ansel Adams photographed the Tetons and the Snake River in 1942. The river meanders in the foreground, the spires of the Teton Range rise 7,000 feet behind—nothing has changed. Arrive at golden hour.

Reserve an hour and a half for the National Museum of Wildlife Art, carved into the cliff overlooking the National Elk Refuge: 5,000 works dedicated to wildlife—paintings, sculptures, and prints by artists like Bierstadt and Russell. In winter, 6,000 to 8,000 elk winter in the refuge visible from the museum windows.

Prepare yourself: the reflection of the Tetons in Jenny Lake exceeds every photo you've seen. The Jenny Lake Visitor Center is housed in a 1920s photo studio. Take the ferry (10 minutes) to reach the foot of Cascade Canyon without the extra 2-mile walk.

Wake up early for Schwabacher Landing: this beaver pond 6 miles from Moose Junction offers the park's most perfect reflection of the Tetons. Before sunrise, morning mist hangs over the water and moose graze on the banks. Gravel access road, passable in regular cars.

Set up at Oxbow Bend, east of Signal Mountain: the Snake River's lazy meander attracts moose, trumpeter swans, herons, and white pelicans. When there's no wind, Mount Moran (12,605 feet) reflects in the water—bring binoculars and a telephoto lens.

Walk into Jackson Lake Lodge, built in 1955 and designated a National Historic Landmark—its 60-foot-high windows frame a frontal view of Mount Moran and the Teton Range. Even if you're not staying here, it's one of the most spectacular rooms in the American West.

Choose String Lake for your last morning in the park: between Jenny Lake and Leigh Lake, it's the quietest spot to walk along the base of the Tetons just a few hundred meters from the walls. The flat 2-mile trail offers direct views of Teewinot Mountain. In summer, the shallow waters are warm enough for swimming—a rare luxury in a national park.

Don't miss Mormon Row on Antelope Flats Road: the Moulton Barn, a wooden barn from the 1890s, is one of the most photographed homesteads in the United States with the snow-capped Tetons behind. Arrive before 9 AM to have the scene to yourself.

For this last day in the park, opt for Taggart Lake Trail (3.5 miles round-trip)—less crowded than Cascade Canyon, gentle climb to a glacial lake framed by moraines. The rock slabs testify to the glaciers that shaped the valley 15,000 years ago.

activities.text: Float the Snake River with a guide. Inside Grand Teton NP, the current is gentle (Class I–II)—perfect for wildlife watching: ospreys, beavers, moose, and occasionally black bears on the banks. The float lasts 2–3 hours. Barker-Ewing and Triangle X are the top-rated operators.

Grab breakfast at The Bunnery on Cache Street—Jackson's most popular table since 1975, ideal before leaving early for the park. Local cuisine revolves around bison, elk, and trout. For dinner, Gather offers a menu focused on Jackson Hole producers.

Take the ferry for Cascade Canyon Trail (5 miles from the landing, 12 miles on foot)—Grand Teton NP's signature hike. After the granite canyon, you emerge onto an alpine meadow framed by the peaks of the Tetons. Leave early with water and food for silence before the groups arrive.

In the evening, drive up Signal Mountain: the 5-mile road leads to a 360° panorama of Jackson Hole valley, the Tetons to the west, and the Gros Ventre Mountains to the east. It's the park's best car-accessible viewpoint for sunset.

Take the Jackson Hole Aerial Tram from Teton Village: in 12 minutes, you ascend 4,139 vertical feet to reach the summit of Rendezvous Mountain at 10,450 feet. The panorama sweeps the entire Teton Range, Jackson Hole valley, and on clear days, all the way to Yellowstone. Hiking trails depart from the summit.

In the evening, drive slowly on Antelope Flats Road and Gros Ventre Road at sunset—elk, bison, coyotes, and black bears are regularly spotted at dusk. Stay in your vehicle or keep 100 meters distance: park rules and common sense.

places.name: Jacksonport

visits.text: Cave Point County Park - Dolomite cliffs plunge straight into Lake Michigan. When wind blows offshore, waves rush into underwater caverns and explode back out through vents with thunderous noise. In calm weather, the water is so clear you can see the bottom at 20-25 feet. This is Door County's most photographed spot - and probably its most spectacular.

Whitefish Dunes State Park - Right next to Cave Point. Lake Michigan's west shore's highest sand dunes (93 feet at the tallest point). The mile-long sandy beach is gorgeous for swimming. The Red Trail (2.5 miles) crosses the dunes and cedar forest.
Jacksonport - Small village on the Lake Michigan side, facing sunrises. Quieter atmosphere than Fish Creek or Ephraim on the Green Bay side. A few shops and cafes along the road.

activities.text: Kayaking at Cave Point - Paddle along the cliffs and glide into small caves carved by erosion. Outfitters from Sturgeon Bay or Jacksonport offer guided trips. The lake must be calm - check conditions in the morning.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

visits.text: Jacksonville - Florida's Largest City by Land Area: Located 30 minutes from Georgia, this sprawling metropolis spreads across both banks of the St. Johns River, offering urban attractions alongside easy beach access.

Five Points Neighborhood - Where Hip Meets Historic: Browse antique shops, sample craft brews at local gastropubs, and experience Jacksonville's most eclectic district, where Victorian homes neighbor trendy boutiques.

Jacksonville Zoo and Gardens - Face-to-Face with Big Cats: This acclaimed facility features one of the most impressive tiger habitats in the Southeast, allowing visitors remarkable proximity to these magnificent cats.

activities.text: St. Johns River Water Taxi - See the City from the Water: Cruise between attractions while enjoying panoramic views of Jacksonville's skyline reflecting off the river that defines the city.

Jacksonville Beaches - 30 Minutes to Paradise: Neptune Beach, Atlantic Beach, and Jacksonville Beach offer distinct personalities along miles of Atlantic shoreline, from family-friendly to surf-focused.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

places.name: Jasper Ar

visits.text: Buffalo National River was designated America's first national river on March 1, 1972, ending several decades of dam proposals. The date isn't coincidental: it's exactly 100 years after the creation of Yellowstone, the first national park, that Nixon signed this law. The river flows freely for 135 miles through the park, between white limestone bluffs that sometimes exceed 500 feet in height. It's one of the few undammed rivers in the continental United States - a rarity best appreciated from a canoe. Jasper is the ideal starting point for accessing the central and upper sections.

Hawksbill Crag, also known as Whitaker Point, is Arkansas's most photographed rock promontory: a sandstone outcrop overhanging a forested canyon hundreds of feet below. The trail from the parking lot runs about 3.4 miles round trip, with some exposed sections approaching the edge. The view embraces miles of forest without a single building visible - plan to be there early morning to avoid weekend crowds.

activities.text: Canoeing the Buffalo River: the Ponca-Kyle's Landing section (about 11 miles) is the river's most spectacular stretch - 500-foot bluffs, Class I-II rapids, emerald waters. Outfitters offer canoe rentals and shuttle service from Ponca and Jasper. River flow is most favorable April through June; in summer levels sometimes drop, so check before you go.

Elk viewing in Boxley Valley: a herd of elk was reintroduced to the Boxley Valley grasslands starting in 1981. The park now hosts Arkansas's only wild elk herd. The valley grasslands, visible from the road, are the best place to observe them - arrive at dusk or dawn, especially September through November during the rut.

Lost Valley Trail hike: 2.2 miles round trip through a verdant canyon ending in a limestone cave, Eden Falls Cave, at the back of which hides a concealed waterfall. Bring a headlamp - without it, you'll miss the best part. The trail is easy to the cave entrance, then requires ducking for a few yards.

days_plan.itin_title: Arkansas: Hot Springs, Ozarks & Crystal Bridges — 7-Day Loop from Little Rock

Lost Valley Trail Boxley Valley elk

visits.text: Walk along Jekyll Island's ethereal Driftwood Beach, a surreal landscape where sun-bleached trees twisted into sculptural forms rise from golden sands. The entire island operates as a state park, ensuring conservation and wildlife protection across 5,700 acres of beaches, marshes, and maritime forests. This otherworldly scenery has attracted filmmakers, featuring in major productions including X-Men and The Walking Dead.

Explore the historic Millionaires' Village where Vanderbilts, Rockefellers, and Pulitzers once gathered in elegant 'cottages' that defined Gilded Age opulence. The Georgia Sea Turtle Center rescues and rehabilitates injured sea turtles, offering close encounters with these ancient mariners and their inspiring recovery stories. Passionate guides explain each turtle's journey from injury through rehabilitation.

activities.text: Cycle the scenic island loop past serene marshes, under canopies of moss-draped oaks, and along beaches where dolphins frequently surface offshore. Picnic on windswept dunes while spotting wildlife including deer, alligators, and countless shorebirds in this carefully preserved coastal sanctuary.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

places.name: Jim Thorpe

visits.text: You step into one of the best-preserved Victorian mansions in Pennsylvania. Asa Packer, founder of the Lehigh Valley Railroad and Lehigh University, had this Italianate mansion built in 1861 on the hillside overlooking Jim Thorpe. The interior has remained virtually unchanged since his death in 1879: original woodwork, Victorian wallpapers, period furniture, and personal collections. Jim Thorpe - formerly Mauch Chunk until 1954 - was founded in 1818 as the lower terminus of a gravity railroad transporting anthracite coal from hillside mines to the Lehigh canal. The town became so prosperous that by the late 19th century, it was the second most visited tourist destination in the United States after Niagara Falls. Guided tours last 45 minutes and depart on the half hour; book in advance during the Fall Foliage Festival in October.

The Old Jail Museum is one of the most fascinating visits in the Pocono Mountains. This 1871 penitentiary, still standing with its original cells, is famous for the execution of the Molly Maguires - a secret society of Irish miners who fought against inhumane working conditions in the coal mines during the 1870s. Seven of them were hanged in the prison yard. In cell 17, a handprint on the stone wall, left according to legend by one of the condemned who swore his innocence before being executed, has remained visible despite several layers of paint and plaster. The guides recount the story of the condemned with a level of detail that makes the visit memorable - even skeptics leave troubled.

The Jim Thorpe mausoleum, honoring the Native American athlete considered the greatest American sportsman of the 20th century, stands at the town entrance on Route 903. Thorpe won the decathlon and pentathlon at the 1912 Stockholm Olympics, played Major League Baseball for the New York Giants, and was one of the first NFL players. His wife, Patricia, chose to lay his remains to rest in this Pennsylvania town he had never visited while alive - in exchange for an annuity to fund a memorial. The town, then in economic decline after the mine closures, accepted the offer in 1954 and adopted his name. It's a story of mutual benefit that became legend.

activities.text: The Lehigh Gorge Scenic Railway carries passengers in century-old cars along the Lehigh River, beneath towering cliffs and through tunnels carved into the gorge rock. The rail line is the direct descendant of the Central Railroad of New Jersey, which transported anthracite coal from Carbon County mines to coastal steel mills. In autumn, the gorge slopes transform into a blazing tableau of reds and oranges reflected in the river. The 75-minute journey offers the most spectacular views, particularly at Glen Onoko, a former Victorian resort destination.

The D&L Trail (Delaware & Lehigh National Heritage Corridor) runs along the Lehigh gorge on former 19th-century canal and railroad routes, with the river on one side and remnants of the canal on the other. The trail is virtually flat, as it follows the original railroad route, making it accessible to all levels. Several bike rental shops in Jim Thorpe offer standard and electric bikes. In autumn, this section ranks among the most beautiful cycling routes on the East Coast for fall foliage.

days_plan.itin_title: Pennsylvania Loop: From Amish Country to the Appalachians - 11 Days

map_keywords: Lehigh Gorge Scenic Railway Jim Thorpe

places.name: Joshua Tree

visits.text: The drive from Death Valley to Joshua Tree passes through Barstow in the Mojave. Once in the park, stop at Hidden Valley, a basin surrounded by quartz monzonite rock formations—the characteristic rock type of the park, formed 100 million years ago and sculpted by erosion ever since. The Hidden Valley loop trail is 1 mile, easy. Continuing east, Skull Rock is a formation whose two cavities carved by erosion suggest eye sockets—the associated trail is 1.7 miles.

The Barker Dam trail is a 1.7-mile loop deep in the rock formations. It leads to a small man-made reservoir built by ranchers in the early 20th century to water their cattle—today it's one of the few permanent water sources in the park and a prime spot for observing local wildlife. Along the way, you'll pass pictographs painted directly on the rock by Serrano and Cahuilla peoples.

The Cholla Cactus Garden is one of the park's most peculiar spots: 10 acres covered with teddy bear cholla, also called 'jumping cactus' because its spiny segments detach and attach at the slightest contact—a fearsome dispersal mechanism. The loop is a quarter mile and crosses a transition zone between the Mojave Desert and the Colorado Desert. Don't touch anything, wear closed-toe shoes, and go at sunrise to see the chollas glow orange in the raking light.

activities.text: Sunset over Joshua Tree's rock formations is an experience unto itself. The quartz monzonite boulders turn pink, orange, and red depending on the angle of light, and the Joshua Trees—those tree yuccas that grow only at this elevation and in this precise geographic zone—take on the appearance of silhouettes cut from paper. Set up at Keys View, at 5,185 feet elevation, for a panorama embracing the Coachella Valley and, on clear days, Mexico 90 miles away.

In the evening, head to a local bar in the town of Joshua Tree for live music. The town has long attracted artists and musicians—U2 recorded the album 'The Joshua Tree' here in 1987, and the area continues to nurture an active music scene blending desert blues, country, and indie rock.

visits.text: Julian - A Gold Rush Town Famous for Apples: This entire mountain town is a California Historical Landmark, its 1870s gold mining heritage preserved in wooden storefronts and false-front buildings. Unexpectedly, the high altitude creates perfect apple-growing conditions.

Julian Pioneer Museum - Gold Rush to Apple Orchards: This small museum traces the town's evolution from mining camp to apple capital, displaying gold mining equipment alongside Victorian-era artifacts.

Apple Orchards and Cider Mills - Taste Julian's Famous Fruit: Numerous u-pick orchards and farm stands offer the chance to select your own apples from dozens of heirloom varieties, while cider mills press the fresh juice.

activities.text: Julian Apple Pie - The Town's Claim to Fame: Multiple bakeries compete for the title of best apple pie, each guarding their recipes jealously. Warm slices with cinnamon ice cream fuel heated debates about which reigns supreme.

Eagle and High Peak Mine Tour - Descend into Julian's Golden Past: This 1,000-foot tunnel tour reveals the hard-rock mining techniques that drew prospectors to these mountains, with gold veins still visible in the walls.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Mendenhall Glacier—12 miles from downtown Juneau, accessible by bus or taxi—is the only major glacier in Southeast Alaska accessible by road. The glacier descends from the Juneau Icefield (over 1,500 square miles) and has retreated more than 2 miles since the 1930s. From the Visitor Center platform, the blue face is in direct view above Mendenhall Lake. The trail to Nugget Falls (0.9 miles one-way) follows the shore to the base of the waterfall—one of the most beautiful accessible hikes in Alaska.

The Alaska State Capitol in downtown Juneau is one of the few American capitols without a dome. The building was constructed in 1931 as the Federal and Territorial Building—Alaska wasn't yet a state. It became the seat of state government only after Alaska's admission to the Union in 1959. Free guided tours (45 minutes) show the Senate and House chambers with their Alaska wood inlays, and explain how to govern a 663,000-square-mile state from a capital accessible only by air or sea.

The Last Chance Mining Museum, perched at the end of a narrow road northeast of downtown, occupies the remnants of the Alaska-Juneau mine—one of the world's largest low-grade gold mines at its peak in the 1930s. The steam-powered machinery and partially restored galleries tell how they extracted half an ounce of gold per ton of rock. The admission is modest and the experience worthwhile.

activities.text: Tracy Arm Fjord, 55 miles southeast of Juneau, is one of Alaska's most spectacular fjords—25 miles of rock walls covered in waterfalls leading to the twin Sawyer glaciers. Fast boats depart Juneau harbor in the morning and return late afternoon. The fjord is often choked with blue icebergs in summer—the boat weaves between them, yards from glacier faces that calve regularly.

Humpback whale watching in the Inside Passage channels from Juneau is among Alaska's most reliable in summer. Over 100 individuals frequent these waters from June to September to feed. Charters offer 3- to 4-hour trips from the harbor—bubble-net feeding behavior is regularly observed, something few other spots in the world can offer.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Adventure - 14 Days Through the World's Most Remote National Parks

visits.text: Hawaii Volcanoes National Park — Witness ongoing volcanic activity at Kilauea, one of the world's most active volcanoes. The 1,348-square-kilometer park encompasses the summit caldera, lava tubes, steam vents, and barren lava fields from recent eruptions.

Thurston Lava Tube — Walk through this 150-meter tunnel formed 500 years ago when lava drained leaving a hollow tube. Ferns and native plants surround the entrance while the interior reveals smooth walls created by flowing molten rock.

Chain of Craters Road — Drive 30 kilometers descending from summit to sea across vast lava fields. The scenic route passes extinct craters, petroglyphs, and the Holei Sea Arch where lava enters the ocean creating new land.

Jaggar Museum Overlook — View the Kilauea caldera from the Hawaiian Volcano Observatory perched on the crater rim. When active, the lava lake glows orange against night skies while sulfur dioxide plumes rise from the summit.

Mauna Kea Summit — Ascend to 4,207 meters atop Hawaii's tallest mountain for sunset and stargazing from the world's premier astronomical observation site. Thirteen international telescopes crown the dormant volcano rising 10,200 meters from seafloor to summit.

Akaka Falls State Park — Hike the 0.6-kilometer loop trail through bamboo groves and rainforest to viewpoints of 135-meter Akaka Falls. The free-falling plunge pool empties into a lush gorge while 30-meter Kahuna Falls cascades nearby.

Rainbow Falls — Photograph this 24-meter waterfall best viewed in morning light when rainbows form in the mist. Located in Hilo, the cascade plunges over a lava cave where legend says Hawaiian goddess Hina lives.

Puuhonua o Honaunau National Historical Park — Explore this reconstructed Hawaiian place of refuge where kapu breakers and defeated warriors found sanctuary. The 73-hectare park preserves royal grounds, fishponds, and the Great Wall built around 1550.

activities.text: Black sand beach at Punaluu — Walk across volcanic sand formed by lava shattering on contact with cold ocean water. Endangered green sea turtles bask on the black beach beneath coconut palms in this remote South Point location.

Kona coffee farm tour — Learn how 100% Kona coffee grows on the volcanic slopes of Hualalai and Mauna Loa. Family farms offer tours explaining cultivation, harvesting, and roasting of this premium Hawaiian coffee variety.

Historic Kailua-Kona waterfront — Stroll past Hulihe'e Palace and Mokuaikaua Church, Hawaii's oldest Christian church built in 1820. The oceanfront village served as King Kamehameha I's royal compound from 1812 until his 1819 death.

Manta ray night snorkel — Swim with graceful manta rays attracted to plankton illuminated by underwater lights off Kona coast. The 4-meter wingspan giants glide inches from snorkelers in an unforgettable marine encounter.

Green sand beach hike — Trek 4.8 kilometers to Papakolea Beach, one of only four green sand beaches worldwide. Olivine crystals eroding from the cinder cone create the distinctive olive-colored sand accessible only by foot.

Fresh poke bowl dinner — Sample cubed raw fish marinated in soy sauce, sesame oil, and limu seaweed at local markets or restaurants. This Hawaiian staple showcases the islands' Pacific Rim culinary influences.

days_plan.itin_title: Hawaii Island Paradise: Volcanoes, Waterfalls & Turquoise Seas
Hawaii Island Paradise: Volcanoes, Waterfalls & Turquoise Seas

places.name: Kalamazoo

visits.text: You drive through Battle Creek and understand why this city of 52,000 calls itself 'Cereal City USA': this is where Dr. John Harvey Kellogg, a Seventh-day Adventist physician, invented corn flakes in 1894 while seeking healthy food for his sanitarium patients. His brother Will Keith Kellogg founded the Battle Creek Toasted Corn Flake Company here in 1906 - today Kellogg's, one of the world's largest food corporations. Charles William Post, a former sanitarium patient, copied the idea and founded Post Cereals in the same city in 1895. For decades, the smell of toasted grains floated over the entire town. The Kellogg's Discovery Center traces this history with interactive installations on nutrition, industrial heritage, and American breakfast culture. Allow 2 hours on-site. Open Tuesday-Saturday 10am-5pm, admission $8 adult.

You arrive at the Gilmore Car Museum in Hickory Corners, between Battle Creek and Kalamazoo, and find 90 acres of prairie dotted with historic barns filled with 400 vehicles spanning 130 years of American automotive history - the Midwest's largest open-air collection. Founded in 1966 by Donald Gilmore, heir to a Michigan pharmaceutical fortune, the museum gathers rarities like the first series-production Ford Mustang (1964), 1920s Packards, a 1942 Lincoln Continental, and dozens of racing cars. The architecture of restored 19th-century barns housing the collections gives the place a particularly successful atmosphere: you pass from barn to barn as through a village. Open daily May through October, 9am-5pm; admission $15 adult. Reserve at least 2 hours.

You enter Bell's Brewery's Eccentric Café in Kalamazoo and you're standing in one of the most influential breweries in American craft beer history. Larry Bell opened his first brewery in 1985 in a converted soup kitchen, with a 15-gallon kettle and a detergent pump for hops - making it one of the very first microbreweries in the Midwest. Their Two Hearted Ale, an IPA with pine and grapefruit aromas named after a Michigan river dear to Hemingway, was voted best beer in America by the American Homebrewers Association in 2017. The Eccentric Café, their historic pub, still offers the full range on tap with pub fare in a brick-and-timber space. Open Monday-Thursday 11am-10pm, Friday-Saturday until 11pm.

activities.text: You explore downtown Kalamazoo on foot between multiple craft breweries - the city was voted Beer City USA two consecutive years in 2012 and 2013, a nationally voted title among beer enthusiasts. Bell's Brewery is the historic reference, but Arcadia Brewing (English-style ales since 1996) and Kalamazoo Beer Exchange (dynamic pricing system based on demand, like a stock exchange) offer complementary experiences in the same walkable downtown district. Kalamazoo is also home to Western Michigan University (23,000 students), giving downtown a particularly lively student atmosphere in the evening.

You enter the Kalamazoo Institute of Arts, a municipal art museum with entirely free admission, and find a permanent collection of 6,000 works spanning 19th-century American art to the present. The museum also organizes public art classes and temporary exhibitions that regularly attract nationally recognized contemporary artists. The building itself, renovated in 2012, integrates naturally lit spaces that highlight collection pieces. A 45-minute break here contrasts nicely with the industrial day between Kellogg's and the Gilmore Car Museum.

days_plan.itin_title: Detroit to Chicago: Great Lakes Road Trip - 8 Days

Battle Creek Kellogg's Discovery Center Michigan

places.name: Kanab

visits.text: Kanab bills itself as "Little Hollywood"—over 100 films and TV shows were shot in its surroundings, from John Wayne westerns to Planet of the Apes. The town of 5,000 is a strategic crossroads between Zion, Bryce, Grand Canyon, and Lake Powell. Take a stroll through downtown—the Western storefronts are authentic, not sets.

Twenty minutes south, Coral Pink Sand Dunes State Park is a field of pink sand dunes—Utah's only one. The dunes rise up to 100 feet high. You can walk on them, slide, or simply contemplate the contrast between pink sand and surrounding red cliffs.

activities.text: Kanab is also the gateway to The Wave (Coyote Buttes North), the Southwest's most famous sandstone formation—but permits are awarded by lottery and very limited (64 per day). If you don't have a permit, White Pocket in the Vermilion Cliffs offers equally spectacular rock formations—you need a 4x4 and GPS, but no permit.

days_plan.itin_title: Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons

map_keywords: Coral Pink Sand Dunes State Park

places.name: Kansas

visits.text: Walk across the glass bridge at the National WWI Museum and look down — beneath your feet are 9,000 red poppies, each representing 1,000 combatant deaths. This is America's only federally designated WWI museum, built beneath the 217-foot Liberty Memorial tower that Kansas City erected after 83,000 citizens raised $2.5 million in just ten days in 1919. The 1921 site dedication was the only time the supreme commanders of all five Allied nations stood in the same place. From the tower's observation deck you get the best panorama in Kansas City. Budget two hours for the museum plus the tower. Paid entry; the Over There Café is open without a ticket.

The Arabia Steamboat Museum in the City Market is running its final year — the Hawley family announced closing in November 2026 after 35 years, so this visit has a deadline. On September 5, 1856, the 171-foot steamboat Arabia hit a tree snag and sank 6 miles west of Kansas City, carrying 200 tons of cargo bound for 16 frontier towns. The Missouri River shifted course, and the Arabia lay buried 45 feet underground beneath a Kansas cornfield for 132 years until treasure hunters dug it up in 1988. The collection is the largest single assemblage of pre-Civil War artifacts in the world. Allow 90 minutes.

Four badminton shuttlecocks, each 18 feet tall and weighing 5,500 pounds, sit on the lawn of the Nelson-Atkins Museum of Art as if giants had just abandoned a game. Artists Claes Oldenburg and Coosje van Bruggen installed them in July 1994, using the neoclassical building as a net and the 22-acre lawn as the court. The museum itself holds one of the country's great encyclopedic collections — standout rooms include the ancient Chinese galleries, a Caravaggio, and the photography wing. Free admission; free outdoor access at any hour.

activities.text: Kaw Point Park sits at the confluence of the Kansas and Missouri Rivers on the east side of Kansas City, and a short interpretive loop tells the story of Lewis and Clark's camp here on June 26, 1804. Ten minutes from downtown, often overlooked, and worth the detour — standing at the exact meeting point of the two rivers connects Day 4 directly back to the Corps. The park is small, the trail is under a mile, and the industrial backdrop makes the historical fact feel even starker.

The City Market district surrounding the Arabia Steamboat Museum has operated as a public market since 1857. On weekends, the outdoor farmers' market draws crowds to stalls selling Missouri produce, local charcuterie, and every kind of food. Kansas City smoked meat culture is distinct enough that a meal here qualifies as cultural research — the tradition of low-and-slow hickory-smoked brisket, burnt ends, and ribs runs through dozens of family-owned joints within a few miles of downtown.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends
Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: National WWI Museum Memorial Kansas City
Nelson-Atkins Museum of Art Kansas City

visits.text: From Bluff, take US-163 south. About 10 miles from Monument Valley, the road opens onto the Southwest's most famous view—the straight line of asphalt shooting toward the Mittens. This is the exact spot where Forrest Gump stops running in the film. Pull over on the shoulder for the photo.

Monument Valley isn't a national park—it's a Tribal Park managed by the Navajo Nation. The sandstone buttes rise up to 1,000 feet above the desert floor. The Valley Drive is a 17-mile dirt road with 11 numbered stops. You can drive it yourself (allow 2 hours with stops), but the track is rough in places—a high-clearance vehicle is preferable. The Mittens (East and West) and Merrick Butte are the three most photographed formations. According to Navajo legend, these are a giant's hands scattered after a great battle.

The Wildcat Trail is the park's only unguided hike—a 3.2-mile loop around West Mitten Butte. Allow 2 hours. You see the butte from every angle, up close, with desert silence all around. Everything else in the park (Mystery Valley, Hunts Mesa, petroglyphs) requires a Navajo guide—outfitters offer Jeep tours from the visitor center.

activities.text: Don't miss sunrise and sunset at Monument Valley. The buttes change color in minutes—from deep red to purple. From the visitor center parking lot, the panoramic view of the Mittens at sunset is free and accessible without entering the park. At night, zero light pollution—the Milky Way is spectacular.

days_plan.itin_title: Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons

visits.text: The Sun Valley Lodge opened on December 21, 1936, built in seven months on the former 3,888-acre Brass Ranch for $1.5 million by Union Pacific Railroad chairman Averell Harriman - who also installed the world's first chairlift here, designed by engineer Jim Curran using banana-loading conveyor technology from tropical cargo ships. The four-story, X-shaped building looks today exactly as it did then: concrete poured inside rough-sawn forms and acid-stained to resemble wood. Walk the hallways - they're lined with black-and-white photographs of Clark Gable, Ingrid Bergman, and Marilyn Monroe. In suite 206, Hemingway finished writing For Whom the Bell Tolls in the fall of 1939.

About 1.5 miles northeast of the Lodge on Trail Creek Road, a bronze bust by sculptor Robert Berks marks the Hemingway Memorial. It was dedicated on July 21, 1966 - his birthday, five years after his death in nearby Ketchum. The inscription is a eulogy Hemingway himself wrote in 1939 for a friend killed in a hunting accident, ending: 'Now he will be part of them forever.' The road closes in deep winter. Trail Creek itself runs below the memorial, clear enough to see the bottom.

On North Main Street, Hemingway's grave in the Ketchum Cemetery sits between two large spruce trees under a flat granite slab - usually decorated with bottles of whiskey left by visitors. He moved to Ketchum permanently in 1959 and died here on July 2, 1961. Nearby, the free Wood River Museum of History and Culture (4th and Walnut, Tuesday-Saturday 11 AM-6 PM) has an exhibit with original artifacts and a vintage typewriter you can use. The museum also covers Sun Valley's ski history and the local mining era.

activities.text: The Pioneer Cabin Trail climbs 4.5 miles west of Ketchum to a stone cabin at 9,400 feet, built by Union Pacific in 1937 as a ski hut. Three thousand feet of elevation gain, mostly in the first two miles through shaded pine - the hard part is done early. From the ridge, the Pioneer Mountains open up in every direction. Allow 5-6 hours round trip; late June through September for best conditions. Dogs welcome off-leash.

The Pioneer Saloon on Main Street has been here since Hemingway's era and still displays one of his personal shotguns - a 1953 Winchester Model 21 he used bird hunting on nearby Silver Creek. The Ram restaurant opened in 1938 and is the oldest at the resort. For the full literary cocktail experience, the Sawtooth Club serves the original Hemingway daiquiri recipe brought back from the El Floridita bar in Havana.

days_plan.itin_title: Idaho: Sawtooth Mountains, Sun Valley & Craters of the Moon

places.name: Key Largo

visits.text: John Pennekamp Coral Reef State Park - America's first underwater park, established in 1963. It encompasses 92 square miles of Atlantic Ocean along Key Largo. The visitor center houses a 30,000-gallon saltwater aquarium - a solid preview of the reef if you're not diving. Cannon Beach and Far Beach are the park's two beaches, small but peaceful. Arrive early in the morning - weekend parking fills up fast.

Dagny Johnson Key Largo Hammock Botanical State Park - This park protects one of the last remaining West Indian tropical hardwood hammock forests in the Keys. The main trail (1.5 km round trip) winds through a dense canopy of gumbo-limbo and mahogany trees. You might spot the Key Largo woodrat, an endangered species found only on this island.

activities.text: Snorkeling at Christ of the Abyss - Key Largo's most iconic excursion. This 8.5-foot bronze statue, submerged 25 feet deep near Dry Rocks, is a replica of the 1954 original placed off Genoa, Italy. Operators within Pennekamp park offer 2.5-hour boat trips - the statue's crown sits 6-10 feet below the surface, easily visible while snorkeling. Book the day before or morning of your visit.

Kayaking through Key Largo's mangroves - Outfitters offer guided trips through sheltered mangrove channels, protected from wind. The water runs shallow and crystal-clear - you'll see rays, juvenile lemon sharks, and herons. Best in late afternoon when the heat eases off.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West
Florida Keys Road Trip: Key Largo to Key West

map_keywords: John Pennekamp Coral Reef State Park
Christ of the Abyss Key Largo
Dagny Johnson Key Largo Hammock Botanical State Park

places.name: Key West

visits.text: Seven Mile Bridge - The most memorable stretch of the Overseas Highway. This 6.8-mile bridge (not seven miles, but the name stuck) connects Marathon to the Lower Keys above strikingly clear turquoise water. Alongside it, the 1912 railroad bridge, partially demolished, remains visible - its broken arches give the landscape a post-apocalyptic edge. No stopping on the bridge itself, but you can pull over at either end for photos.

Bahia Honda State Park - Often ranked among America's most beautiful beaches. The sand is fine, the water clear and shallow for dozens of yards. Three beaches to choose from: Sandspur Beach (the largest), Calusa Beach (facing the old bridge), and Loggerhead Beach (more secluded). The park has limited capacity - arrive before 10am in high season or you'll find the gates closed.

National Key Deer Refuge on Big Pine Key - These miniature Key deer (24-30 inches at the shoulder) live only in the Lower Keys. The population numbers around 700-800. The best viewing spot is the Blue Hole, a former quarry pit filled with fresh water where alligators coexist with the deer. Drive slowly on Big Pine Key - speed limits are reduced to protect the deer.

Ernest Hemingway Home & Museum - The 1851 colonial mansion where Hemingway lived from 1931 to 1939. This is where he wrote 'For Whom the Bell Tolls' and 'The Snows of Kilimanjaro.' The real stars are the 50-60 cats ruling the property - about half are polydactyl (six-toed), descendants of Snow White, a cat given to Hemingway by a ship's captain. The 30-minute guided tour (included with admission) also covers the $20,000 pool from 1937, the penny embedded in the ground, and the bar urinal converted into a fountain by his ex-wife.

Southernmost Point - The famous concrete buoy marking the southernmost point in the continental United States (24°33'N), 90 miles from Cuba. Lines form almost constantly for the photo - early morning or late afternoon is calmer.

Fort Zachary Taylor State Park - The best swimming and snorkeling spot in Key West proper. The brick fort (1845-1866) served as a naval base during the Civil War. The beach is rocky (bring water shoes) but tropical fish swarm a few yards from shore. The trees provide shade, rare in Key West.

Key West Cemetery - This 1847 cemetery, right in the middle of town, is one of Florida's strangest. Graves are above-ground (the water table sits 12 inches down). Some epitaphs are deliberately comic - the most famous reads 'I told you I was sick.' A 30-minute wander is plenty.

Dry Tortugas National Park - Fort Jefferson - Full day devoted to this excursion 70 miles west of Key West. The ferry departs at 8am and returns around 5:30pm (2 hours 15 minutes each way). Fort Jefferson is a massive red brick hexagon planted on Garden Key - one of the largest coastal forts ever built in the United States, with walls 8 feet thick. Construction began in 1846 and was never completed. During the Civil War it served as a prison - the most famous inmate was Dr. Samuel Mudd, convicted for setting the broken leg of John Wilkes Booth after Lincoln's assassination. The guided fort tour runs 45 minutes.

Duval Street and Old Town - Key West's main drag, 1.25 miles between the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic. Morning, before the cruise ship crowds arrive, is the best time to wander among Victorian wooden houses, art galleries, and cafes. At the street's end, Mile Marker 0 marks the beginning (or end) of US Route 1, which stretches from Key West to the Canadian border in Maine - 2,369 miles.

Custom House Museum - This red brick 1891 building, formerly the port customs house, now houses a local art and history museum. Exhibits cover wrecking (salvaging shipwrecks, Key West's main industry in the 19th century), cigar manufacturing, and military history. Handsome Richardson Romanesque architecture.

Key West Butterfly and Nature Conservatory - A 5,000-square-foot tropical greenhouse housing 50-60 butterfly species in free flight, plus flamingos and tropical birds. Restful after days in full sun - the interior is shaded and humid.

activities.text: Swimming and lounging at Bahia Honda - Take your time here. The water is the most beautiful in the Keys - turquoise, warm, calm. If you have mask and snorkel, the snorkeling from the beach is decent, with scattered coral and tropical fish. In late afternoon, the low sun casts golden tones across the sand.

Sunset at Mallory Square - Every evening, two hours before sunset, the square transforms into an impromptu festival: jugglers, fire-breathers, musicians, street vendors. Everyone gathers facing west to watch the sun drop into the Gulf of Mexico. Arrive early for a good spot.

Snorkeling at Dry Tortugas - The water around Fort Jefferson is exceptionally clear (50-100 foot visibility) and depths range from 5 to 15 feet. Snorkeling gear is included with the ferry ticket. You'll see barracuda, sea turtles, grouper, and intact coral formations - this is some of Florida's finest snorkeling. Pack waterproof sunscreen - there's almost no shade on the island.

Key lime pie tasting - The Keys' signature lime pie is THE local dessert. The original recipe uses Key West limes (smaller and more tart than standard limes), sweetened condensed milk, and a Graham cracker crust. Sample several versions for comparison - every establishment has their take. The frozen-on-a-stick version dipped in chocolate is a street classic.

Final sunset or departure - If driving back to Miami (160 miles, 3.5-4 hours), leave in early afternoon to avoid night driving on the Overseas Highway. Otherwise, Key West airport offers direct flights to Miami, Fort Lauderdale, and other Florida cities.

visits.text: Experience the pristine natural beauty of Kiawah Island, where endless stretches of soft sand meet towering dunes and lush maritime forests untouched by commercial development. Sunlight filters through ancient trees in the most captivating ways, while deer, herons, and dolphins appear regularly throughout the landscape. This barrier island represents coastal South Carolina at its most refined.

Cycle along miles of shaded pathways winding through forests, past lagoons filled with wildlife, and directly onto beaches where firm sand allows extended rides along the shore. The island's world-famous golf courses, including the Ocean Course with its dramatic oceanfront holes, attract players from around the globe seeking championship-caliber challenges amid stunning scenery.

activities.text: Paddle through Kiawah's salt marshes at sunset when the landscape transforms into golden hues reflected across calm waters. Beach days reveal why this island consistently ranks among America's finest, with uncrowded shores and crystal-clear waters. The quiet luxury and naturally wild character create an atmosphere of peaceful exclusivity.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

places.name: Kingman

visits.text: Seligman - Stop here - this is where Route 66 was saved. In 1987, Angel Delgadillo, a local barber, founded the Route 66 Association of Arizona to prevent the Mother Road from fading into obscurity after its decommissioning. The few-block village inspired Radiator Springs in the movie Cars. The Snow Cap Drive-In, founded by Angel's brother in 1953, is a temple of kitsch with gags on the menu ('dead chicken' for chicken) and squirting mustard.

Seligman - Kingman: the most beautiful stretch - The 90 miles between these two towns is the best-preserved section of all of Route 66. Two lanes, no traffic, the same road as in 1955 through sagebrush and ranches. Take your time.

Hackberry General Store - Make the stop 30 miles east of Kingman - this 1930s former gas station is frozen in time: period pumps, rusted signs, red Corvette parked out front. It's as authentic as Route 66 gets.

activities.text: Kingman neon lights - If you arrive after dark, Andy Devine Avenue (the local Route 66) lights up with vintage neon. Kingman is also the gateway to the Historic Route 66 toward Oatman - a winding mountain road with wild burros on the pavement.

days_plan.itin_title: Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica
