Southwest Desert Road Trip: Zion, Bryce & Grand Canyon
The Rain Man Route: An American Road Trip

visits.text: Lassen Volcanic National Park - The Only Place on Earth with All Four Volcano Types: Walk through a geothermal wonderland where the earth bubbles, hisses, and steams beneath your feet. This living laboratory showcases plug dome, shield, cinder cone, and composite volcanoes all within park boundaries.

Bumpass Hell - Three Miles into the Largest Hydrothermal Area in the Cascades: Follow the boardwalk trail past roaring fumaroles, bubbling mudpots, and scalding pools painted in otherworldly hues of orange, yellow, and turquoise. The trail honors Kendall Bumpass, who lost a leg when he broke through thin crust into boiling water.

Sulphur Works - Drive-Up Geothermal Theater: Steam vents and bubbling mud pools mere feet from the road offer the easiest access to the park's volcanic activity. The sulfurous smell announces your arrival before you see the first wisp of steam.

activities.text: Lassen Peak Summit Trail - Climb an Active Volcano: This 5-mile round-trip hike ascends 2,000 feet to the 10,457-foot summit, offering 360-degree views of the volcanic landscape. Lassen Peak last erupted in 1915, creating the devastated area still visible today.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Walk six blocks from 6th Street to South Park and you are inside a National Register Historic District with 108 contributing properties, most built between 1856 and 1953. At 729-731, the House Building is the only commercial structure to survive Quantrill's 1863 raid intact. The Eldridge Hotel at 701-703 was Quantrill's main tactical target; the current five-story brick building dates from 1928, and each August 21 a white sheet is hung from a window to mark the anniversary. Free State Brewing Company at 636 Massachusetts opened in 1989 as the first legal brewery in Kansas in over a century.

You enter through the marble foyer of the 1888 Watkins National Bank Building at 1047 Massachusetts Street - marble staircases, stained-glass windows, and mosaic floors intact - and get the most thorough account of Quantrill's Raid in any Lawrence museum. The free permanent collection covers Douglas County history from Bleeding Kansas through the 20th century. James Patti's sculpture of Langston Hughes as a boy is here; Hughes grew up in Lawrence and based his 1930 novel Not Without Laughter on his childhood in this town.

Climb Mount Oread to the KU campus and you are on a National Historic District that opened in 1866 - one year after Quantrill's raid nearly prevented Lawrence from funding the new university at all. The Spencer Museum of Art (over 45,000 objects) and the KU Natural History Museum are both free and both worth at least 30 minutes each. James Naismith, the inventor of basketball and the only KU coach with a losing record, coached here and is buried in Lawrence.

activities.text: Download the free 1.5-mile self-guided walking tour from explorelawrence.com and follow Quantrill's path through 10 surviving sites - from South Park, where guerrillas staged their approach, to the Methodist Church that became a makeshift morgue by evening. The raid killed 20 percent of the town's male population and left 85 widows. Quantrill himself had lived in Lawrence under an alias and knew the town intimately before leading 450 men into it at dawn. The Watkins Museum also runs a paid Bleeding Kansas Bus Tour on select summer Saturdays for a wider sweep of the region.

End your day the way Lawrence does: with a pint and a plate on Mass Street. Free State Brewing Company has poured its flagship Ad Astra amber ale - named for the Kansas state motto, 'To the stars through difficulty' - since opening in 1989 as the first legal brewery in Kansas in over a century. For dinner, the Mad Greek at 9th and Massachusetts does flaming saganaki tableside. The Granada at 1020 Massachusetts, a Beaux-Arts venue that opened in 1912 with a 35-foot vaulted ceiling, hosts live music most nights under a chandelier the size of a small car.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends
Kansas Flint Hills & Frontier: Wichita to Dodge City

visits.text: You enter a landscape where the granite is 550 million years old and the animals have been here - with a thirty-year gap - for over a century. The refuge was established July 4, 1901, making it the oldest facility in the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service system. On the roads you share space with free-roaming bison, Texas longhorn cattle (established on the refuge in 1927), Rocky Mountain elk (introduced from Jackson Hole, Wyoming, in 1908), white-tailed deer, and prairie dog towns. The Longhorn Trail - a 1.5-mile loop around French Lake - consistently produces bison and longhorn sightings. Drive at 15mph on all refuge roads; bison cross without warning and have the right of way in every sense.

You drive three miles of paved switchbacks to the summit of Mount Scott at 2,464 feet - the highest accessible point in the refuge - and step out into a 360-degree view of the granite-studded prairie below, Fort Sill to the south, and the wind farm on the Slick Hills to the north. The rock beneath your feet is part of the Mount Scott Granite, intruded roughly 530 million years ago. On a clear day you can see the curvature of the land. The drive takes about 20 minutes each way; stay for the full rim walk before heading back down.

You walk through a 66-acre granite complex inside the refuge built by WPA workers in 1934-35 to replicate biblical Jerusalem - Herod's Court, Pilate's judgment hall, the Garden of Gethsemane, watchtowers, and perimeter walls, all quarried from local rock with a $94,000 federal grant. It was built for the 'Prince of Peace' Easter passion play, which Rev. Anthony Mark Wallock first staged near Medicine Park in 1926 with five cast members and 200 attendees. Today it's the longest-running Easter pageant in the United States. A mile east, you find Medicine Park - a cobblestone resort village from 1908 where the buildings are faced with rounded granite pulled from local creek beds, and where legend holds that Al Capone kept a cabin. Both are free to visit.

activities.text: You push into 5,000 acres of designated wilderness where jumbled granite boulders and short cliff faces have drawn climbers since the 1960s and 70s. The Access Fund and Wichita Mountains Climbers Coalition maintain established routes on the rock faces. If you'd rather hike than climb, the 2.4-mile Sunset Trail crosses the wilderness to Treasure Lake - rocky underfoot, worth every step, but bring significantly more water than you think you need. There is no potable water anywhere on refuge trails, and summer heat in southwest Oklahoma will empty your bottles faster than any mountain trail.

You drive to a town of about a dozen people to eat a burger made from the animals you watched grazing this morning. The Meers Store has been here since 1901, and its 'Meersburger' uses longhorn beef culled annually from the refuge's own herd - the same Texas longhorns established on the refuge in 1927. The building looks like it was assembled over decades because it was. Meers is open Thursdays through Sundays only; arrive before 7pm or plan on a wait at the door.

days_plan.itin_title: Oklahoma Route 66 & Native Heritage: Tulsa to OKC

visits.text: Deception Pass Bridge — Cross this dramatic 555-meter steel cantilever bridge connecting Whidbey Island to Fidalgo Island 55 meters above churning tidal waters. The 1935 structure frames views of rocky islands and forest-clad cliffs plunging to the sea.

Leavenworth Bavarian Village — Stroll this Alpine-themed town transformed in the 1960s with Bavarian architecture, beer gardens, and German restaurants. The former logging town reinvented itself as a tourist destination in the Cascade Mountains.

Waterfront Park — Walk along the Wenatchee River through this landscaped park featuring traditional German gazebos and gardens. The park hosts festivals year-round from Oktoberfest to Christmas lighting ceremonies.

Nutcracker Museum — View over 7,000 nutcrackers from around the world in this unique collection spanning six rooms. Examples range from medieval functional tools to ornate Bavarian figures and modern artistic interpretations.

activities.text: German cuisine sampling — Feast on schnitzel, bratwurst, spätzle, and apple strudel at authentic Bavarian restaurants complete with lederhosen-clad servers. Beer gardens serve German imports and local craft brews in festive atmosphere.

Icicle Gorge Trail hiking — Trek along the cascading Icicle Creek through old-growth forest and granite canyons. The relatively easy 4-mile trail offers swimming holes and dramatic mountain scenery in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness.

Wine tasting at local wineries — Sample Riesling, Chardonnay, and Pinot Noir at tasting rooms surrounding Leavenworth. The Cascade foothills' elevation and temperature variations create favorable growing conditions.

days_plan.itin_title: Washington State's I-90 Corridor: Seattle, Snoqualmie Pass & Alpine Lakes

visits.text: You're wandering through one of Appalachia's best-preserved towns: 236 acres of Lewisburg's heart have been listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1978, with Federal and Neoclassical houses dating from the late 18th century. The town was founded in 1782 by the Virginia Assembly, on a site occupied since 1751 by settlers near Lewis Spring. During the Civil War, the Battle of Lewisburg on May 23, 1862 saw Union forces under Colonel George Crook repel the Confederates - you can still see graffiti that soldiers from both sides carved into the walls of a building used as a hospital. In 2011, Frommer's Budget Travel named Lewisburg 'America's Coolest Small Town.'

You're walking through the door of one of only four Carnegie Halls still operating worldwide - there's of course one in New York, but also here, in an Appalachian town of 4,000 residents. Andrew Carnegie funded this neo-Georgian building in 1902 with a donation of $26,750 for the Lewisburg Female Institute, supplemented by $10,000 from the local community. When the college closed in 1972 and the building risked demolition, residents formed an association in 1983 and raised $3 million to save it. Today Carnegie Hall hosts concerts, exhibitions in three free galleries, and art classes year-round.

You're stopping before this gray stone church built in 1796, considered the oldest church building in continuous use west of the Alleghenies. It survived the Civil War because both armies used it in turn as a field hospital. The cemetery surrounding it dates partly from the Revolutionary War and the War of 1812; several officers from George Washington's army rest here. The interior, simple and white, retains its original oak pews. A quick visit suffices to grasp its atmosphere of historical quietude.

activities.text: You're setting out on one of the longest and most beautiful bike trails in Appalachia: 77 kilometers on the former Chesapeake & Ohio Railroad route, with an almost zero grade along the Greenbrier River. You're cycling between white limestone cliffs, green river meanders, and horse farms that have produced famous Greenbrier County beef for generations. The river has been renowned for trout fishing since the 19th century. Red brick railroad tunnels pierce the limestone ridges on certain sections.

You're descending into Lost World Caverns just north of Lewisburg and walking through a 500-meter underground gallery whose ceiling, 400 million years old, is covered with colossal stalactites and stalagmites. The showpiece is a 5-meter-tall stalagmite named 'Goliath,' one of the largest known specimens in Appalachia. The region sits on a Devonian limestone plateau riddled with underground caverns linked by active aquifer networks - Lost World Caverns is just one window into this system.

days_plan.itin_title: West Virginia Loop - From the Appalachians to New River Gorge

visits.text: Discover the Davidson County Historical Museum, housed in an elegant 19th-century Greek Revival building that provides an excellent opportunity to learn about Lexington's rich history and culture. The museum's fascinating exhibits chronicle the development of this charming Piedmont community through artifacts and historical displays.

Admire the stunning collection of works at the Bob Timberlake Gallery, celebrating one of North Carolina's most beloved artists. The gallery showcases Timberlake's masterful paintings capturing the essence of Southern rural life and the natural beauty of the Carolina landscape.

activities.text: Experience Lexington's lively winery scene, which offers plenty of opportunities to sample excellent local wines. The serene atmosphere at Childress Vineyards provides the perfect setting for tasting award-winning dry white wines while enjoying the beautiful rolling Piedmont countryside that surrounds this celebrated vineyard.

days_plan.itin_title: North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains

places.name: Liberal

visits.text: Liberal - Dorothy's Other Kansas Home: This southwestern Kansas town offers another immersive Wizard of Oz experience, competing with Wamego for fans of the beloved story.

Dorothy's House and the Land of Oz - Follow the Yellow Brick Road: Dorothy herself leads your whirlwind tour through this attraction, meeting the Scarecrow, Tin Man, and Cowardly Lion on the magical path to the Wizard.
Dorothy's Farmhouse - Where the Tornado Touched Down: Tour the recreated Kansas farmhouse, imagining the moment young Dorothy was swept away to the magical Land of Oz.
Oz Memorabilia Museum - Decades of Oz Collectibles: Browse extensive displays of Wizard of Oz merchandise, props, and artifacts spanning the beloved story's long history.

activities.text: Pancake Race - Liberal's Quirky Tradition: Each Shrove Tuesday, Liberal hosts an international pancake race, competing against Olney, England, in a tradition dating to 1950.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

map_keywords: Dorothy's House and Land of Oz Liberal Kansas

visits.text: The Nebraska State Capitol is unlike any other statehouse in America - deliberately so. Architect Bertram Grosvenor Goodhue won a nationwide competition in 1920 with a design that broke entirely from the dome-on-a-drum template of Washington, D.C. Built in four phases from 1922 to 1932 in Indiana limestone at a cost just under $10 million (paid in full at completion), the 400-foot tower was the first state capitol to incorporate a functional skyscraper into its structure. Goodhue said Nebraska's flat landscape demanded altitude and beacon effect; the tower is visible 20 miles away. It was the tallest building in Nebraska until Omaha's Woodmen Tower rose in 1969. Inside, sculptor Lee Lawrie's mosaics and Hildreth Meiere's tile floors trace the Procession of Life from fossils to humans. The unicameral legislature that meets here is the only single-chamber state legislature in the United States. Free guided tours run on the hour.

The Historic Haymarket District - eight blocks of 1880-to-1920 wholesale warehouses just west of downtown - was Lincoln's commercial engine when the jobbing trade here reached into South Dakota, Wyoming and Montana. The Lincoln City Council designated it a landmark in 1982; in 1985 it became the first urban warehouse district in the country to join the National Trust for Historic Preservation's Main Street program. The 1927 Lincoln Station, a neoclassical Burlington depot still operating as an Amtrak stop, anchors the west end. The Saturday Farmers Market (May to October) draws 150 vendors and up to 10,000 shoppers. Walk the loading-dock alleys for original ironwork and hand-painted signs from a century of commerce.

The Sunken Gardens in Antelope Park, first planted in 1930, hold up to 30,000 plants across tiered beds that change three times a year - free to enter. A short drive west brings you to Pioneers Park Nature Center, a 668-acre grassland preserve where bison and elk roam open pastures: a genuine patch of pre-settlement Great Plains on the edge of the state capital. The nature center has hands-on wildlife exhibits; the trail network is free and open year-round. The two together make for a restorative late afternoon before dinner in the Haymarket.

activities.text: The Sheldon Museum of Art on the University of Nebraska campus holds a quietly impressive collection of American art, with particular strength in 20th-century abstraction and Plains-connected artists. Philip Johnson designed the 1963 travertine-and-glass building, worth seeing in its own right. Admission is free. From the Sheldon, a 15-minute walk through campus along the historic Centennial Mall leads back to the Haymarket.

Spend the evening in the Haymarket for dinner - Nebraska's first brewpub, Lazlo's Brewery & Grill (opened 1991), anchors the district, and the streets stay lively past dark. If you're here on a Saturday morning before departing, the Farmers Market is the right moment to stock up on local honey, aged cheese and smoked meats for the Sandhills drive ahead.

days_plan.itin_title: Nebraska Sandhills & Pioneer Trails: From Omaha to the Rockies

visits.text: Lindsborg - More Swedish Than Sweden: Visitors from Scandinavia themselves say this Kansas town feels more authentically Swedish than modern Sweden, thanks to its traditional festivities and architecture.

Coronado Heights - A Castle Overlooking the Prairie: Climb this 300-foot-tall bluff crowned with a castle-like stone shelter for panoramic views across the Kansas landscape that Spanish conquistadors once traversed.

Wild Dala Horses - A Herd of Painted Wooden Creatures: The Swedish Dala horse tradition inspired these colorful sculptures scattered throughout Lindsborg, each painted with unique designs waiting to be discovered.
Hemslöjd Swedish Gift Shop - Take Home a Piece of Sweden: Browse traditional Swedish crafts, including personalized Dala-shaped house signs, at this authentic Scandinavian emporium.

activities.text: Swedish Heritage Walking Tour - Discover Little Sweden: Explore the architecture, shops, and traditions that have made this Kansas town a living tribute to Scandinavian culture.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

visits.text: Litchfield Hills - Connecticut's Most Pristine Countryside: Quiet country roads wind through this rural region where small towns seem lifted straight from a painting. The famous Litchfield town green exemplifies New England charm, surrounded by 18th-century homes and historic churches.

Lake Waramaug - A Glacial Jewel Shared by Three Towns: This idyllic lake stretches across Kent, Warren, and New Preston, its shores lined with forests, farms, and a state park offering swimming, hiking, and spectacular fall foliage viewing.

West Cornwall Covered Bridge - The Most Photographed Span in Connecticut: This beloved 1864 covered bridge carries traffic across the Housatonic River in a scene that defines New England's romantic appeal, especially when autumn colors reflect in the waters below.

activities.text: Housatonic River Paddling - Whitewater to Flatwater Adventures: The Housatonic offers paddling for all skill levels, from challenging rapids at Rattlesnake in Falls Village and Bull's Bridge in Kent to gentle flows through Cornwall's meadows and farms.

Eagle Landing State Park - Winter Wildlife Spectacle: Visit Haddam's riverside park where bald eagles congregate from December through March to nest and fish along the riverbank, one of New England's premier eagle-watching destinations.

Connecticut Wine Trail Northwest - Vineyard Hopping in the Hills: Several wineries dot the Litchfield Hills region, including Connecticut Valley Winery with award-winning varietals and Sharpe Hill Vineyard featuring a Zagat-rated restaurant amid rolling countryside.

days_plan.itin_title: Connecticut Coastal Drive: From Maritime Mystic to Ivy League New Haven

places.name: Little Rock

visits.text: Little Rock Central High School National Historic Site commemorates one of the most documented civil rights crises in American history. In September 1957, nine Black high school students - the Little Rock Nine - presented themselves at the entrance of this then all-white high school. Arkansas Governor Orval Faubus had mobilized the National Guard to block them. After 18 days of standoff, Eisenhower federalized the guard and sent the 101st Airborne Division to escort the nine students to their classrooms on September 25, 1957. Ernest Green, the group's only senior, graduated there on May 27, 1958 - Central High's first Black graduate. The NPS Visitor Center adjoins the building, which still operates as a high school. The museum traces the crisis day by day with archives and testimonies.

The Clinton Presidential Library occupies a cantilevered building over the Arkansas River, designed to suggest a bridge to the future according to its architect. The library covers the Clinton presidency (1993-2001) with archives, the Oval Office reconstructed identically, and exhibitions on the decade's major issues - NAFTA, Kosovo, DOMA, the Lewinsky affair. Allow at least 75 minutes: the Kosovo war archives and original impeachment trial documents are worth pausing over. It's one of the most-visited presidential libraries in the United States.

The River Market District is the pedestrian quarter along the Arkansas River: the covered market, restaurants, and bars concentrate in a few blocks between the bridge and the Clinton Library. It's the ideal place to end the trip - beer on a terrace facing the river, dinner in the district.

activities.text: Big Dam Bridge: at 4,226 feet, it's the longest pedestrian and bicycle bridge built on a dam in the United States. It connects both banks of the Arkansas River and forms part of an 18-mile network of bike paths and pedestrian trails. The view from the bridge's center embraces the entire valley - sunset recommended.

Pinnacle Mountain hike: the granite summit of Pinnacle Mountain State Park rises about 1,000 feet above the Arkansas River valley, about twenty minutes from downtown Little Rock. The West Summit Trail runs 1.6 miles round trip with about 700 feet of elevation gain. The climb is steep on the final section - kids enjoy scrambling on the bare rock at the summit. Panoramic views across Lake Maumelle and the city.

days_plan.itin_title: Arkansas Itinerary: Hot Springs, Ozarks and Natural Parks
Thelma & Louise Movie Route: Utah & Arizona Filming Locations
Arkansas: Hot Springs, Ozarks & Crystal Bridges — 7-Day Loop from Little Rock

map_keywords: Little Rock Central High School National Historic Site

places.name: Lone Pine

visits.text: The route from Oakhurst to Death Valley passes through Mammoth Lakes, a summer ski resort at 7,900 feet on the Sierra Nevada slopes, then Bishop, a small ranching town in the Long Valley. Take a break in Bishop—it's the last real town before the desert expanses. After Bishop, the road hugs the eastern face of the Sierra Nevada before dropping into Death Valley. The landscape transition is striking: pine forests then bare granite, high-altitude desert and finally scorching plains.

At Lone Pine, stop at the Alabama Hills, a field of orange boulders sculpted into bizarre shapes at the foot of Mount Whitney. These landscapes have served as backdrops for hundreds of Hollywood westerns since the 1920s—from John Ford films to recent television series. Movie Road lets you drive among the most spectacular formations. Look for natural arches that frame the 14,505-foot Mount Whitney in the background.

Artist's Drive is a 9-mile loop through the multicolored badlands on the valley's eastern flank. The highlight is Artist's Palette, a geological formation where exposed rocks display manganese oxides (purple), iron silicates (green), and iron oxides (red and orange). The colors are most vivid in mid-morning. The road is one-way—plan your route accordingly.

Dante's View is a viewpoint at 5,475 feet elevation that looks directly down on Badwater Basin—the elevation difference between the two points is 5,755 feet, one of the greatest in the United States over such a short horizontal distance. On clear days, you can simultaneously see the lowest point in the United States (Badwater) and Mount Whitney, the highest point in the continental U.S., 85 miles away as the crow flies. Arrive at sunrise to watch the shadows of the Panamint Mountains settle over the salt flats.

The Mesquite Flat Sand Dunes, near Stovepipe Wells, are the most accessible and most photographed dunes in the park. They reach about 100 feet high. Go early in the morning or late afternoon—at these times the long shadows reveal the undulations of the sand and the tracks of nocturnal animals.

activities.text: Badwater Basin is the lowest point in North America: 282 feet below sea level. The salt flats covering the basin floor form hexagonal crystals—a phenomenon caused by repeated evaporation cycles in an area that receives only 1.9 inches of rain per year. Look up at the cliff face across from you and find the 'Sea Level' sign mounted 282 feet above your head. Avoid walking on the salt between 10 AM and 4 PM in summer—temperatures regularly exceed 113°F. Zabriskie Point, 19 miles north, offers a viewpoint over the ochre and gold badlands at sunset—these eroded hills provided the setting for a 1970 Michelangelo Antonioni film.

Set out at sunrise from Lone Pine for a photography session on Route 190. At this hour, the raking light transforms every sand ripple, every salt polygon, and every rock face. Death Valley is one of the places on earth where light changes most dramatically throughout the day.

days_plan.itin_title: California Road Trip: San Francisco, Yosemite & Death Valley
California Road Trip: San Francisco, Yosemite & Death Valley

map_keywords: Badwater Basin Death Valley National Park
Artist's Drive Death Valley National Park
Mesquite Flat Sand Dunes Death Valley

visits.text: Stand atop Lookout Mountain at Rock City Gardens, a remarkable natural park where ancient rock formations, lush gardens, and whimsical stone pathways lead to Lover's Leap overlook with views extending across seven states. The 180-foot suspension bridge provides exhilarating passage above forested ravines while cave grottos reveal fairytale scenes tucked into the mountain's mysterious recesses. Towering sandstone cliffs and carefully cultivated gardens create unforgettable vistas.

Descend into the mountain at Ruby Falls, where an underground pathway leads to a spectacular 145-foot waterfall cascading within an ancient limestone cavern. The illuminated cave formations and thundering waters create a subterranean spectacle unlike anything above ground. Guided tours reveal the geological history of this remarkable natural wonder.

activities.text: Visit the dramatically positioned Covenant College, whose Gothic Revival stone buildings rise castle-like from the clifftops with commanding views across Georgia, Tennessee, and Alabama. Sunset Rock offers photographers ideal conditions for capturing the Blue Ridge Mountains bathed in golden evening light.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

places.name: Los Angeles

visits.text: Enter Los Angeles via Santa Monica. The pier, built in 1909, extends 1,600 feet into the Pacific Ocean and hosts an amusement park with a Ferris wheel. This is where Route 66 ends—a sign at the end of the pier marks it. A few steps away, Venice Beach and its boardwalk are unmissable: skaters, musicians, street artists, and bodybuilders at Muscle Beach on the sand create a perpetual spectacle.

The Getty Center perches on a Brentwood hilltop, 110 acres above the city. Designed by architect Richard Meier and opened in 1997 after 13 years of construction at a cost of one billion dollars, the campus is clad with 16,000 tons of travertine quarried near Tivoli, Italy. Admission is free—only parking has a fee. The permanent collection spans European paintings from the Middle Ages to the 19th century, including Van Gogh's Irises painted in 1889. The view of Los Angeles from the terraces alone is worth the visit.

Drive up to Griffith Observatory for the best view of the Hollywood Sign. Built in 1935 with funds bequeathed by Griffith Jenkins Griffith, the building sits at 1,134 feet elevation on the southern slope of Mount Hollywood. Admission is free—only the planetarium charges a fee. The Hollywood Sign facing you dates from 1923—originally built as 'Hollywoodland' to promote a housing development, shortened to 'Hollywood' in 1949. The observatory has served as a backdrop for Rebel Without a Cause with James Dean (1955) and La La Land (2016).

Beverly Hills means Rodeo Drive and its luxury boutiques spanning 1,000 feet, but also residential streets lined with mansions reflecting a century of Hollywood wealth. An hour's walk from Rodeo Drive to Greystone Mansion Park—an 18-acre estate with a 1928 stone mansion you can visit for free—gives a good sense of the city's social geography.

activities.text: Stroll along the Hollywood Walk of Fame, the 1.6 miles of star-studded sidewalks lining Hollywood Boulevard and Vine Street. More than 2,700 stars have been embedded since 1960, each bearing the name of a film, television, music, or circus artist. Grauman's Chinese Theatre, opened in 1927, is where you'll find star hand and footprints set in concrete.

Visit one of the major Hollywood studios offering guided tours. The visit takes you onto active sound stages, outdoor sets, and behind the scenes of film production. Book online in advance—spots are limited and large groups may be turned away at the last minute.

days_plan.itin_title: California Road Trip: San Francisco, Yosemite & Death Valley
California Road Trip: San Francisco, Yosemite & Death Valley
Driving the Pacific Coast Highway: A Golden Gate Bridge to Hollywood Road Trip
The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths
Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica
Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey
The Rain Man Route: An American Road Trip

map_keywords: Hollywood Walk of Fame Los Angeles

places.name: Loudonville Oh

visits.text: You place your hands on the stone parapet of the overlook and look down: the Clear Fork Gorge plunges 300 feet below your feet over a width of 1,000 feet, carved into sandstone by the meltwater of the Wisconsin glacier 14,000 to 20,000 years ago. The National Park Service designated this gorge a National Natural Landmark in 1967: the giant hemlocks and native white pines carpeting the walls have survived here since the glaciation, protected by a cool microclimate that Ohio's summer heat never reaches. Legend has it that Johnny Appleseed - John Chapman, the itinerant planter who roamed Ohio from 1800 to 1845 - knew this gorge and planted apple trees along the Clear Fork River. Come at dawn to see the bottom-of-gorge mists rise toward the ridges.

You cross Mohican State Park's covered bridge on foot - a wood and steel structure built in 1969 that spans the Clear Fork River at the bottom of the gorge, replacing a suspension footbridge that the Civilian Conservation Corps had built in the 1930s. You stop in the middle of the deck and look downstream: the river flows between sandstone cliffs on which century-old hemlocks cling, the water is so clear you see brown trout swimming among the blocks. Late afternoon light enters through the bridge's two openings and traces two rectangles of golden sun on the wooden planks - the Mohican photo everyone's looking for. Arrive between 4pm and 5pm for the best light.

You arrive in Loudonville and immediately understand why the town boasts of being the 'Canoe Capital of Ohio': Main Street is lined with canoe outfitters competing for customers with hand-painted signs, and the smell of river permeates the entire downtown. Yet the town of 2,700 has a discreet and endearing historic center - 1890s commercial facades line the brick sidewalks, and the Mohican River flows silently two blocks away. In spring, when white trilliums and bloodroots bloom on the banks, you understand why locals don't want the rest of the world to discover this corner of Ohio. Settle in for the night at one of the lodges or campgrounds in Mohican State Park 5 miles away.

activities.text: You descend into the gorge on a trail rebuilt in 2020 by Mohican Trails Club volunteers along the original Civilian Conservation Corps route from the 1930s. At the bottom, you follow the Clear Fork River on its south bank between dripping sandstone cliffs and century-old hemlocks whose roots grip the walls - the river is so clear you see the pink sandstone bottom through the water. Then you climb back up the north wall on a switchback trail, and emerge at the stone observation terrace: the green river 300 feet below, the opposite cliffs, the hemlock canopy at eye level. Every meter of elevation is worth ten on the descent. Hiking boots required - wet rocks at the gorge bottom don't forgive smooth soles.

You paddle on the Mohican River and enter the Ohio few travelers see: transparent water over a pale pink sandstone bottom, sycamores whose branches meet above you in a vegetal tunnel, great blue herons motionless on gravel banks taking off unhurriedly as you approach. In places you glimpse rainbow trout swimming upstream in the shaded sections. The 8-mile section between Clear Fork and Brinkhaven is the wildest - you'll only encounter local fishermen. Ask your outfitter for the return shuttle rather than backtracking: Mohican Canoe Livery, Loudonville Canoe Livery, and Mohican Adventures all offer this service, shuttle included in the price.

days_plan.itin_title: Interior Ohio Loop - From Columbus to Mohican Gorges

map_keywords: Mohican State Park Clear Fork Gorge

places.name: Louisville

visits.text: Start at Churchill Downs - and don't just peer through the gates. The track has been operating since 1875, and those twin spires that became Kentucky's symbol were built in 1895. The first Kentucky Derby ran here on May 17, 1875: African American jockey Oliver Lewis won aboard Aristides. The Kentucky Derby Museum on-site shows a 360° screen film that recreates the two-minute race. Off-season access to the grandstands costs $3.

Three stories on the Ohio River tell one life: the Muhammad Ali Center is dedicated to a local kid, born here in Louisville in 1942. It's not just about boxing - the museum dwells on his refusal to join the army in 1967, his civil rights activism, his improvised poetry. You can practice shadow boxing against a projection of the champion. Allow 90 minutes to do it justice.

Outside, a 120-foot, 68,000-pound baseball bat leans against the brick façade of the Louisville Slugger Museum & Factory - you can see it from the end of the street. Inside, the production line runs live and you can touch Babe Ruth's bat. More than a third of professional baseball clubs in America still use wood made here.

Old Louisville is the largest Victorian residential district in the United States - 48 blocks of red brick mansions built between 1880 and 1910, larger than San Francisco's. Plan an hour walking around Saint James Court and Central Park: the private gardens are often immaculate and the atmosphere feels worlds away from the busy downtown.

Spend your morning at the Frazier History Museum on Museum Row: it's also the official Welcome Center for the Kentucky Bourbon Trail. Four floors of Kentucky history, thoroughly documented military collections, and a solid introduction to bourbon culture before heading out to distilleries. If you're only visiting one history museum in Louisville, make it this one.

The 21c Museum Hotel, a few blocks away, is a special case: it's a luxury hotel whose lobby and hallways function as a contemporary art museum, open free 24/7. The golden David statue at the entrance, flanked by giant red penguins, sets the tone. Even if you're not staying here, wandering the public spaces is worth half an hour.

Look up as you walk Main Street: the second-largest concentration of cast-iron façades in America after New York's SoHo. These 19th-century arcaded buildings were built for river port warehouses. Each façade has different cast-iron details - flowers, lion heads, columns with capitals. An hour is enough to take it all in.

activities.text: If you're only doing one bourbon tasting in Louisville, do it at Angel's Envy on Whiskey Row. Their whiskey is finished in port wine barrels - slightly fruity, very different from standard bourbon - and the tasting room is one of the most polished downtown. Figure $25 and 90 minutes. Book online.

For dinner, head to NuLu (East Market Street), Louisville's foodie district. If you want to try the Hot Brown - an open-faced roast turkey sandwich with bacon and Mornay sauce invented in 1926 - the Brown Hotel that created it is a 10-minute walk toward downtown. NuLu's craft breweries often have food trucks out in the evening.

Cross the Big Four Bridge early in the morning - a former railroad bridge converted to a pedestrian walkway over the Ohio River. The low-angle light hits the river and Louisville's skyline behind you makes for great photos from mid-span. On the other side, Jeffersonville, Indiana has a few cafés for breakfast.

The Belle of Louisville is the last Mississippi-style steamboat still in service, built in 1914 and operated by the city. Public two-hour cruises travel up the Ohio River. Dinner and jazz cruises are offered in the evenings in season. Check schedules at belleoflouisville.org.

days_plan.itin_title: Louisville, Mammoth Cave & Congaree - Caves and Swamps of the Deep South
Louisville, Mammoth Cave & Congaree - Caves and Swamps of the Deep South

map_keywords: Main Street Cast Iron Facades Louisville

visits.text: The descent from the Porcupine Mountains first runs along Upper Peninsula forests, then crosses the invisible border into the lower peninsula to emerge at Manistee - a 19th-century town on the edge of the Manistee River, a few hundred meters from Lake Michigan. Its Victorian red-brick commercial blocks have been listed on the National Register of Historic Places; the Riverwalk promenade runs along the old pine timber loading dock that made Manistee one of Michigan's wealthiest cities in the 1880s. Stop for coffee and cherry pie before continuing to Ludington.

Ludington State Park borders the mouth of Hamlin Lake with 4 kilometers of sandy beach along Lake Michigan - one of Michigan's best state parks for swimming and sunsets. The Big Sable Point lighthouse (1867) is accessible via 5 kilometers of trail from the park's north parking; its 34-meter black-and-white striped tower is one of Michigan's most photographed. This is your last evening facing Lake Michigan before crossing it tomorrow morning.

The SS Badger dock is a few blocks from downtown Ludington. On your arrival evening, watch the ferry return to port - 125 meters of riveted steel maneuvering in reverse into its slip with surprising precision for a 1952 vessel. The pier and loading sheds still have railroad tracks embedded in the lower deck, vestiges of the era when the Badger transported C&O Railway cars from shore to shore.

activities.text: Book your passage at ssbadger.com if you haven't already - it's the only passenger-car ferry still operating on Lake Michigan, designated a National Historic Landmark since 2016. The Badger carries up to 620 passengers and 180 cars in a single voyage. Arrive at the dock 45 minutes before departure for vehicle boarding: the ferry loads from the stern, and watching your car disappear into the ship's belly is an experience in itself.

Stearns Park beach, ten minutes walk from downtown, faces due west over Lake Michigan. In July and August, the sun sets directly on the water - a rare image on the Great Lakes where the lake's eastern shore offers sunrises over water, not sunsets. Tomorrow morning at 9am, you'll take the ferry in this same direction, heading to Manitowoc.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Lakes Grand Loop: Chicago, Milwaukee, Door County, Sleeping Bear, Mackinac, Pictured Rocks - 12 Days

places.name: Luray Va

visits.text: You're entering Shenandoah National Park through the Front Royal entrance at milepost 0 and beginning one of America's most famous scenic drives: 169 kilometers of ridgeline at 35 mph with 75 overlooks opening onto the Shenandoah Valley to the west and the Piedmont to the east. The road was built in the 1930s by the Civilian Conservation Corps, a New Deal program that employed thousands of unemployed workers; President Roosevelt inaugurated it in 1936. On this northern section, stop at Hogback Overlook (milepost 21) for a 270° panorama across five counties, and pass through Mary's Rock Tunnel at milepost 32 - carved through granite one billion years old. Start before 9am to see deer grazing in the meadows before the midday heat.

You're descending beneath the Shenandoah Valley floor into the largest caverns in the eastern United States, discovered in 1878 by Andrew Campbell and Benton Stebbins. Beneath your feet: a network of galleries carved through limestone over millions of years, adorned with giant stalactites and stalagmites - some reaching 4 meters high. The showpiece is the Stalacpipe Organ, a one-of-a-kind instrument built in 1956 whose 37 notes are produced by gentle percussion on stalactites chosen for their resonance across 14 acres of caverns. The caverns maintain a constant temperature of 12°C year-round - bring a jacket even in midsummer.

You're stopping at the exact midpoint of Skyline Drive: Big Meadows is the park's largest open meadow - about twenty hectares of pastureland at 1,070 meters elevation, surrounded by oak and hickory forests. This meadow existed long before the park: the mountain families displaced in the 1930s to create Shenandoah had grazed their herds here for generations. Today it's one of the park's best spots for observing white-tailed deer at twilight. The adjacent Byrd Visitor Center traces the park's human and geological history with permanent exhibits.

activities.text: You're climbing toward the park's second-highest summit - 1,235 meters - on a trail that crosses columnar basalt cliffs formed by lava flows 570 million years ago. From the summit you overlook the entire Shenandoah Valley at once, and on clear days the Blue Ridge stretches 100 kilometers to the south. The trail begins at Skyland Resort, the park's oldest site: Stony Man Camp, as it was called then, was already welcoming vacationers from Washington and Baltimore as early as 1888. Arrive early - Skyland parking is among the first to fill in summer.

You're descending on a 1.4-kilometer round-trip trail to Dark Hollow Falls, which plunges 21 meters over basalt rocks formed 570 million years ago. It's the waterfall closest to a road in the entire park, and therefore the most crowded - come early in the morning to enjoy the calm and the light filtering through giant tulip poplars. The climb back up is steep but short: about 20 minutes of effort. Wear shoes with traction - the wet rocks near the falls don't forgive smooth soles.

days_plan.itin_title: West Virginia Loop - From the Appalachians to New River Gorge

map_keywords: Skyline Drive Front Royal entrance Shenandoah

visits.text: On July 6, 1898, the Mackinac Island city council voted to ban automobiles after a first 'horseless carriage' had terrorized local horses. Michigan made it state law in 1960. You step off the ferry onto Main Street in an intact 1898 Victorian town: horse-drawn carriages, cobblestones, Grand Hotel on its hill with its 201-meter veranda - the world's longest. Plan to take the ferry from Mackinaw City midday after the drive from the Porcupine Mountains - the crossing lasts 20 minutes.

Arch Rock is a natural limestone arch with a 15-meter opening suspended 46 meters above Lake Huron. For the Ojibwa, it was a sacred place where the spirits of the lake came to rest. The trail from Main Street leads there in 20 minutes on foot; if you're on bike, accessible from the ridge path on the east side. Don't expect solitude in July - but the formation remains spectacular.

activities.text: M-185 circles the entire island in 13 kilometers - it's technically a state highway, and the only one in the American network where automobiles have been illegal since inception. You share it with horse-drawn carriages, riders and other cyclists. The view of the Mackinac Bridge to the south - 8 kilometers of suspension bridge inaugurated in 1957 - gradually opens from the west shore. Plan on a leisurely 1h30 stopping at viewpoints.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Lakes Grand Loop: Chicago, Milwaukee, Door County, Sleeping Bear, Mackinac, Pictured Rocks - 12 Days

places.name: Macon

visits.text: Seventeen thousand years of human habitation. That's what Ocmulgee Mounds National Historical Park represents - one of the most continuously inhabited places in North America, right on the edge of Macon. The Great Temple Mound, 55 feet tall, was built around 900 CE by Mississippian people. Over 2,000 artifacts spanning from 10,000 BCE are in the visitor center museum. Eight miles of trails run through 1,860 acres. The Earth Lodge - a partially reconstructed council chamber with an original clay floor from around 1015 CE - is easy to overlook; don't. Free admission, open daily.

In 1969, the Allman Brothers Band signed with Capricorn Records in Macon and rented a Tudor-style house at 2321 Vineville Avenue - communally, several members at once, from 1970 to 1973. That's the Big House. It's a museum now, every room filled with instruments, costumes, setlists, and recordings from the years that produced 'At Fillmore East' and 'Eat a Peach.' The unsettling part is that the rooms still look like rooms - kitchen, bedrooms, living room - not like cases in a gallery. Paid entry.

Perched on Coleman Hill at 934 Georgia Avenue, the Hay House is the most ambitious building in antebellum Georgia - 18,000 square feet, 24 rooms, Italian Renaissance Revival style, built between 1855 and 1859 by William Butler Johnston after a three-year European honeymoon. Italian craftsmen brought to Macon did the work. It had hot and cold running water, central heat, gas lighting, a speaker-tube system, and a French lift - extraordinary for the 1850s. National Historic Landmark since 1974, managed by the Georgia Trust since 1977. Paid entry.

activities.text: The bronze statue of Otis Redding moved in 2025 from Gateway Park to the corner of Cotton Avenue and Cherry Street, in front of the Zelma Redding Amphitheater at the new Otis Redding Center for the Arts. The seven-foot figure shows him seated on a dock, guitar out - the image of '(Sittin' On) The Dock of the Bay,' recorded three days before his plane went down on December 10, 1967. He was 26. Redding had grown up in Macon after his family moved from Dawson, Georgia when he was five. The Otis Redding Museum at 339 Cotton Avenue, steps away, is free to enter.

Rose Hill Cemetery opened in 1840 on the Ocmulgee River. When the Allman Brothers arrived in Macon in 1969, they adopted it immediately - walked the paths at night, wrote music here, found names for songs in the stones. 'In Memory of Elizabeth Reed' takes its title from a grave they found. Duane Allman died on October 29, 1971, at the corner of Hillcrest and Bartlett. Berry Oakley died one year later, three blocks from the same spot. Both were 24. Gregg Allman joined them in 2017. The three graves are together on Carnation Ridge. Free, open daily.

map_keywords: Ocmulgee Mounds National Historical Park Macon
Allman Brothers Big House Museum Macon
Otis Redding Center for the Arts Macon

visits.text: Stroll through Madison's remarkably preserved antebellum district, where grand 19th-century mansions line oak-shaded streets virtually unchanged since the Civil War. Sherman's army famously spared this elegant town during the March to the Sea, preserving architectural treasures including Heritage Hall and the historic Rogers House. Manicured gardens, decorative fountains, and refined Southern storefronts create timeless elegance.

Visit Rose Cottage, built by Adeline Rose after she gained her freedom, offering moving insights into African American history in the antebellum South. The Steffen Thomas Museum showcases bold folk art in striking contrast to the town's refined classical architecture. Antique shops throughout the walkable historic district contain fascinating treasures from centuries past.

activities.text: Experience Madison's genteel Southern hospitality while exploring the town's carefully preserved heritage spanning two centuries of American history. The intimate scale and exceptional preservation make this one of Georgia's most photographically rewarding destinations for lovers of historic architecture.

days_plan.itin_title: Connecticut Coastal Drive: From Maritime Mystic to Ivy League New Haven
Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast
Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

visits.text: Chris settles into bus 142, hunts small game, reads, writes in his journal and initially savors the complete solitude of wilderness life.
Weakened by hunger and probable poisoning, Chris becomes too weak to leave or hunt and dies alone in Magic Bus 142.

days_plan.itin_title: Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey
Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey

visits.text: Getty Villa — Explore this spectacular recreation of a 1st century Roman villa housing J. Paul Getty's collection of Greek and Roman antiquities. The 44,000 works displayed in the peristyle gardens and galleries evoke Herculaneum.

El Matador State Beach — Descend the steep stairs to this wild cove hidden between sculpted cliffs and rock arches. The geological formations create sea caves and natural pools at low tide, a photographer's paradise.

Malibu Pier — Stroll along this historic 230-meter pier built in 1905, anchor point of the Malibu surfer lifestyle. Malibu Farm restaurant serves farm-to-table cuisine while dolphins and sea lions patrol the crystal-clear waters.

Adamson House — Visit this 1929 Spanish-Moorish residence adorned with 3,000 colorful Malibu Potteries ceramic tiles. The artisanal azulejos decorate fountains, stairs and patios of this property listed as a historic monument overlooking Surfrider Beach.

activities.text: Hiking at Solstice Canyon — Explore the shaded trails of this coastal canyon leading to the romantic ruins of the Roberts Ranch House destroyed by fire in 1982. The Tropical Terrace Trail crosses an unexpected tropical oasis with waterfalls and ferns.

Seafood lunch at Nobu Malibu — Savor chef Nobuyuki Matsuhisa's Japanese-Peruvian fusion cuisine on the glass terrace directly overlooking the Pacific. The black cod miso is legendary and the people-watching opportunities unmatched.

Surf session at Surfrider Beach — Learn to surf at this legendary spot with long, regular waves, birthplace of modern California surfing in the 1960s. Local schools provide equipment and coach beginners on this beach classified as a World Surfing Reserve.

days_plan.itin_title: Driving the Pacific Coast Highway: A Golden Gate Bridge to Hollywood Road Trip

visits.text: Mammoth Mountain - California's Legendary Ski Destination: This volcanic peak rises to 11,053 feet, receiving an average 400 inches of annual snowfall that fuels one of America's longest ski seasons, often extending into June or July.

Devils Postpile National Monument - Nature's Perfect Geometry: These 60-foot columns of basalt, formed by cooling lava 100,000 years ago, display hexagonal symmetry so precise it appears engineered. The nearby 101-foot Rainbow Falls completes the geological wonderland.

Mammoth Lakes Basin - Alpine Paradise at 9,000 Feet: Five crystal lakes fill glacier-carved bowls beneath towering Sierra peaks, offering swimming, fishing, and hiking amid some of California's most pristine mountain scenery.

activities.text: Mammoth Mountain Gondola - Rise Above the Sierra: Even in summer, the gondola whisks visitors to 11,053 feet for 360-degree views spanning from the White Mountains to Yosemite's high country.

Hot Creek Geological Site - Geothermal Springs Meet Trout Stream: Hot springs bubble up through a creek renowned for fly fishing, creating swirling patterns where cold and hot water mix against a backdrop of jagged peaks.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Mammoth Spring State Park - Where 9 Million Gallons Surge Hourly from the Earth: One of America's largest springs creates an instant river from a 10-acre basin of impossibly blue water. The Mammoth Spring discharges enough crystal-clear flow every day to supply a city of 300,000 people, maintaining a constant 58°F year-round.

Historic Frisco Depot - Gateway to the Ozarks: This restored 1886 railway station recalls the era when Mammoth Spring served as a resort destination for Victorian travelers seeking the spring's legendary waters. The depot now houses exhibits on regional history and railroad heritage.

activities.text: Spring Lake Dam & Spillway - Watch a River Being Born: The spring's outflow creates Spring River, famous for trout fishing. Walk the dam and watch millions of gallons cascade over the spillway every hour, feeding one of the Ozarks' finest cold-water fisheries.

Spring River Water Trail - Float the Ozarks' Clearest Stream: Beginning at the spring's outlet, this designated water trail offers gentle paddling through pristine Ozark scenery, past towering bluffs and through swimming holes.

days_plan.itin_title: Arkansas Itinerary: Hot Springs, Ozarks and Natural Parks

visits.text: Manhattan and the Flint Hills - Gateway to America's Last Tallgrass Prairie: This university town serves as the perfect launching point for exploring the vast grasslands that once covered 170 million acres of North America.

Flint Hills Discovery Center - Interactive Gateway to the Prairie: This state-of-the-art facility immerses visitors in the ecology, history, and culture of the Flint Hills through hands-on exhibits, films, and programming.

Konza Prairie Biological Station - Hike Through Living History: A network of nature trails crisscrosses this 8,600-acre research prairie, offering intimate encounters with the tallgrass ecosystem that once dominated the continent.

Tallgrass Prairie National Preserve - 11,000 Acres of Protected Prairie: Ranger-guided tours and the Southwind Nature Trail lead visitors through this remarkable preserve to bison pastures and scenic overlooks, one of the last places to experience what pioneers encountered crossing the Great Plains.

Flint Hills National Scenic Byway - 47 Miles of Prairie Splendor: This designated scenic route winds through pastoral landscapes where cattle and cowboys still roam, much as they have for over a century.

activities.text: Council Grove Santa Fe Trail Celebration - 200 Years of Western Heritage: This historic town along the Santa Fe Trail hosts anniversary celebrations commemorating the trail's bicentennial, including old west gunslinger reenactments.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

visits.text: Marathon - This village of 400 souls in the middle of nowhere is an improbable but charming stop. The Gage Hotel (1927), built by a rancher as a way station, has been tastefully restored: leather furnishings, terracotta floors, a courtyard planted with cacti. Even if you're not staying, grab a drink at the bar or have dinner at the restaurant (Texan and Mexican cuisine).

Gage Gardens - Behind the hotel, stroll through these arid gardens cultivating desert-adapted plants. Roses, herbs, and cacti in a peaceful setting. If you're an early riser, have breakfast here—it's the most pleasant spot in Marathon.

Marathon galleries - Step into the two or three small art galleries exhibiting works inspired by the Big Bend desert. If you're looking for a souvenir, the local crafts (pottery, silver jewelry) are quality and reasonably priced.

activities.text: Stargazing - Marathon sits in one of the darkest spots in the United States. Step outside the hotel after dinner and look up: the Milky Way is visible to the naked eye, shooting stars are frequent. If you have binoculars, bring them.

days_plan.itin_title: Florida Keys Road Trip: Key Largo to Key West
Far West Texas Circuit: Big Bend and Ghost Towns

places.name: Marfa

visits.text: The Chinati Foundation, established in 1986 by Donald Judd on 340 acres of a former military base (Fort D.A. Russell), is one of the world's most distinctive contemporary art sites. The centerpiece: 100 untitled works in mill aluminum (1982-1986), all with identical exterior dimensions (41 × 51 × 72 inches) but each with a unique interior configuration. Judd installed these 100 boxes in two former artillery sheds, which he transformed by replacing garage doors with long gridded glass walls and doubling the buildings' height with arched roofs. Chihuahuan Desert light streams through these windows and constantly shifts the reflections on the aluminum. Allow at minimum 2.5 hours for the full collection - guided tours are recommended.

Prada Marfa is a 2005 installation by artists Elmgreen & Dragset, planted on Route 90 about 37 miles east of Marfa near the village of Valentine. A fake Prada boutique in the middle of the desert: an actual storefront with merchandise from the fall-winter 2005 collection (authentic bags and shoes), but a door that doesn't open. You can pull over on the shoulder anytime - access is free, and the photo at sunrise with the empty desert behind is the best shot.

Downtown Marfa, population 1,700, centers around the Presidio County Courthouse (1886) in red brick, visible from a distance. The Judd Foundation, distinct from Chinati, manages Donald Judd's studios and libraries in former airplane hangars downtown. A guided tour (2 hours) provides access to the artist's working and living spaces - a dive into the methods of a man who refused to separate art from the space where it exists.

activities.text: The Marfa Lights are mysterious lights observed in the plain at the foot of the Chinati Mountains since at least 1883. A purpose-built viewing platform on Route 67, 9 miles east of Marfa, lets you watch for them at night. Their origin remains debated - atmospheric refraction, mineral phosphorescence, or other optical phenomena. Arrive after 9pm on a moonless, cloudless night to maximize your chances. Bring a sweater: it gets cold even in summer once the sun goes down.

days_plan.itin_title: Far West Texas Circuit: Big Bend and Ghost Towns
Desert Parks of Texas and New Mexico: Big Bend, Carlsbad, White Sands, and Guadalupe
Desert to Alamo: West Texas & Big Bend - 10 Days from El Paso to San Antonio

map_keywords: Chinati Foundation artillery sheds Marfa Donald Judd
Prada Marfa installation Route 90 Valentine Texas

visits.text: Your first vision of Yosemite unfolds at Tunnel View - a lookout built in 1932 at the exit of the Wawona Tunnel, blasted in 1933 through solid granite with 230 tons of dynamite. This is where Ansel Adams immortalized the valley: El Capitan on the left (900 meters of vertical wall), Bridalveil Fall on the right, Half Dome in the distance. Arrive early in the morning - before 8am if possible - to avoid the crowds and catch the raking light on the granite. Don't leave without walking to the El Capitan meadows to look up at the climbers: from below, they look like tiny dots inching up a wall smooth as glass. A typical ascent takes seven to ten days.

The Lower Yosemite Fall loop is the park's most-traveled trail, and for good reason: in twenty minutes round-trip from the parking lot, you find yourself at the base of a waterfall that drops 739 meters in total (cumulative upper and lower height). The lower fall alone measures 98 meters - equivalent to a 30-story building. It peaks in April-May with snowmelt; in summer, it can nearly disappear. Allow 30 minutes on the trail.

Glacier Point is the park's most spectacular overlook - at 2,199 meters elevation, 975 meters above the valley floor. From here, you look Half Dome straight in the eye. Half Dome rises to 2,693 meters and its northwest face displays a 93% verticality - making it the steepest cliff face in North America. Native Americans called it Tis-sa-ack, "the split rock." Glacier Point Road is closed from approximately November to May - check conditions before coming.

activities.text: Late in the day, return to the Valley Loop Trail for sunset. When golden light strikes El Capitan's granite, the face shifts from gray to orange-pink in minutes. Find a quiet spot in the meadows - it's one of the park's simplest yet most striking spectacles. Bring a flashlight for the return.

days_plan.itin_title: California National Parks Road Trip: Yosemite, Sequoia, and Death Valley

visits.text: Head out to the Black Rocks at the tip of Presque Isle Park: 1.7-billion-year-old basalt cliffs rising 15 feet above Lake Superior. It's Marquette's unofficial cliff-jumping spot—the water reaches 60-65°F in August. The park is a 323-acre peninsula with forest trails, a beach, and views of the iron ore docks. Arrive early.

Sugarloaf Mountain is a 45-minute climb from the parking lot. At the summit, 470 feet above Lake Superior, three observation platforms give you the city, the lake, the ore docks, and the forest in 360 degrees. The steep trail up the front or the gentle route around the back—both lead to the same panorama. Free, open year-round.

activities.text: Dead River Falls is 10 minutes from downtown. A half-mile trail follows the river to a series of waterfalls cascading into pools of polished granite. The currents are treacherous—stay on the rocks at the edge. The setting—spruce, moss, dark water—captures the stark beauty of the Upper Peninsula.

days_plan.itin_title: Minneapolis, Voyageurs, Isle Royale & the Upper Peninsula

visits.text: Mashantucket Pequot Museum & Research Center - The Largest Native American Museum in the World: This tribally owned 308,000-square-foot complex brings the story of the Mashantucket Pequot Tribal Nation to life through immersive multisensory experiences. The incredibly realistic Pequot Village recreation transports visitors to pre-contact New England life.

185-Foot Glass and Stone Observation Tower - Views Across Southern New England: Rise above the surrounding landscape for panoramic views of the Connecticut wilderness, wetlands, and coastline stretching to Long Island Sound (reopening early spring 2025 after renovations).

Permanent Galleries and Dioramas - 10,000 Years of History: Two levels situated below ground house extensive exhibits on regional Native American history, from the Ice Age to the present, through films, interactive programs, archival materials, and meticulously crafted dioramas.

activities.text: Connecticut Wine Trail - Sample Award-Winning New England Wines: Thirty-three member wineries spread across the state offer tastings of varietals uniquely influenced by Connecticut's terroir. Saltwater Farm Vineyard near Stonington adds briny coastal notes to its wines, while farms like Bishop's Orchards combine vineyards with markets and bakeries.

days_plan.itin_title: Connecticut Coastal Drive: From Maritime Mystic to Ivy League New Haven

visits.text: Jour 1: McCarthy Road - The 60-mile gravel route from Chitina to McCarthy follows the abandoned Copper River & Northwestern Railway bed, crossing the Kuskulana and Gilahina trestle bridges. This adventurous drive takes approximately 3.5 hours and requires vehicles permitted for unpaved roads, passing through pristine wilderness with views of surrounding glaciers and peaks.

Jour 1: McCarthy Historic District - This remote outpost at the end of the road was once a bustling supply town for the Kennicott copper mines. Today the small community maintains its frontier character, accessible only by footbridge across the Kennicott River, with no running water, limited electricity, and a population that swells slightly in summer.

Jour 2: Kennicott Mines National Historic Landmark - This remarkably preserved ghost town represents one of the most significant early 20th-century copper mining operations in North America. The fourteen-story concentration mill and surrounding structures cling to the mountainside above the Kennicott Glacier, abandoned in 1938 when ore deposits were exhausted. Ranger-led and private tours explore the industrial complex's haunting red buildings.

Jour 2: Root Glacier - A short hike from Kennicott leads to one of Alaska's most accessible glaciers, where visitors can walk onto the ice without technical equipment. The glacier's surface features crevasses, meltwater streams, and ice caves that shift and change throughout the summer season.

activities.text: Jour 1: Air Taxi from Chitina - Bush planes offer a scenic 30-minute alternative to the rough road, flying over glaciers, peaks, and abandoned railway infrastructure before landing on the gravel strip near McCarthy. This aerial approach provides stunning perspectives on Wrangell-St. Elias's vast wilderness.

Jour 2: Guided Glacier Hiking - Naturalist guides lead excursions onto Root Glacier, providing crampons and instruction for safe travel across the ice. Extended trips venture deeper into the glacier's blue ice formations and seracs.
Jour 2: Ice Climbing - The glacier's features provide natural climbing terrain, with professional guides offering instruction for beginners and challenging routes for experienced climbers.

Jour 2: Wrangell-St. Elias Flightseeing - Aerial tours reveal the park's staggering scale, with nine of North America's sixteen highest peaks, countless glaciers, and active volcanoes visible from above. Flights may include glacier landings for an immersive wilderness experience.
Jour 2: Nizina River Rafting - Whitewater and scenic float trips explore the wild rivers draining the Wrangell Mountains, passing through canyons and along glacial moraines rarely seen by visitors.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords

visits.text: Effigy Mounds National Monument—Iowa's only national monument. Across 2,500 acres along the Mississippi, 206 Native American mounds are preserved, including 31 shaped like animals: bears and birds, all oriented head-downriver. The oldest date to 500 BC. Start at the visitor center, then take the Fire Point Trail (3-mile round-trip)—it passes the Little Bear Mound and leads to a viewpoint 300 feet above the Mississippi. The view is breathtaking.

Pikes Peak State Park—Nothing to do with Colorado's Pikes Peak. This state park offers Iowa's highest overlook above the Mississippi: 500 feet of vertical bluff. A boardwalk trail leads to Bridal Veil Falls, a cascade tumbling into a wooded ravine. From the main overlook, you see the confluence of the Wisconsin and Mississippi Rivers—a panorama stretching for miles.

River Bluffs Scenic Byway—This scenic route follows the bluffs above the Mississippi between McGregor and Guttenberg. Views of the river and deciduous forests rank among the Midwest's finest, especially in fall when the maples turn red.

Guttenberg—This riverside village was founded by German immigrants who named it after Gutenberg (with one T, later corrected). The town overlook offers clear views of the river and lock system. Walk along the levee—it's peaceful.

Motor Mill Historic Site—Ten miles west of Guttenberg, this 1869 limestone mill stands beside the Turkey River. Six stories, 3-foot-thick walls—one of the largest stone mills in the Midwest. It was never profitable and closed after just two years. The ruins are photogenic.

activities.text: Late in the day, head down to McGregor—this tiny riverside village lines up antique shops and cafés in 19th-century buildings. The atmosphere feels timeless.
If you're curious, stop at Spook Cave—a cave you tour by rowboat on an underground river. The entrance is a hole in the bluff just wide enough for the boat. Kitschy but fun.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs
Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs

places.name: Medicine Lodge

visits.text: Gypsum Hills Scenic Byway - Red Rock Country in Kansas: This 42-mile route reveals a landscape of flat mesas, deep canyons, and rusty buttes complementing green cedar trees and prairie grasses-scenery that defies Kansas stereotypes.

Carry Nation Home - The Hatchet-Wielding Temperance Leader's Story: In Medicine Lodge, learn about the remarkable woman who smashed law-breaking saloons across Kansas and beyond in her crusade against alcohol.
Lake Coldwater - Oasis in the Red Hills: Cool off with swimming, boating, and fishing at this scenic lake, keeping watch for wintering bald eagles, migrating white pelicans, and ospreys.
Gyp Hills Guest Ranch - Authentic Ranch Experience: Ride horses across hilly pastures, assist with livestock chores, catch fish, or simply absorb the dramatic Red Hills scenery.

activities.text: Red Hills Photography - Capture Kansas's Hidden Landscape: The rusty tones of the Gypsum Hills create stunning photographic opportunities, especially during golden hour when the rocks seem to glow.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

map_keywords: Carry Nation Historic Home Medicine Lodge

places.name: Medora

visits.text: Drive the 36-mile Scenic Loop Road through Theodore Roosevelt National Park's South Unit. The herd of roughly 300 bison often walks straight across the road; wild horses roam the southwestern section; prairie dogs pop up at Skyline Vista. Roosevelt came here in September 1883 to hunt bison and left co-owning the Maltese Cross Ranch. He came back after devastating personal loss in 1884, and it was riding alone through these formations that his grief turned into purpose. The park is the most direct physical evidence of how a place can change a person. Paid entry.

Stop at the Maltese Cross Cabin, just outside the South Unit visitor center. Ranch managers Sylvane Ferris and Bill Merrifield built it during the winter of 1883-84 at Roosevelt's request - a story-and-a-half ponderosa pine log structure with three rooms and wooden floors, considered a 'mansion' by badlands standards. After Roosevelt's presidency the cabin was exhibited at the 1904 St. Louis World's Fair and the 1905 Lewis and Clark Centennial in Portland before ending up on the Bismarck capitol grounds - it didn't arrive at its current location until 1959. Free with park admission; ranger tours in summer.

Cross the river to visit the Chateau de Mores, a 26-room wood-frame house built in 1883 by French aristocrat Antoine de Vallombrosa, the Marquis de Mores. His plan: slaughter cattle in Medora, ship dressed meat east in refrigerated railcars, bypass Chicago, get rich. He bought over 44,000 acres and named the town after his wife Medora von Hoffman. The whole enterprise collapsed in 1886 - overgrazing, falling beef prices, and the brutal winter of 1886-87 killed it. The original furnishings survive intact. Paid entry; open May-September.

activities.text: Hike the Wind Canyon Trail - a short, steep 0.4-mile out-and-back from the Scenic Loop Road that puts you on a sandstone promontory above a bend in the Little Missouri River. The view is one of the best in the park: badland formations in every direction, the river cutting a green corridor through pale rock, and usually complete silence. Do it early morning before the heat builds; the wind at the top is usually strong enough to explain the name.

Drive 7 miles east on I-94 to the Painted Canyon Visitor Center overlook at exit 32. It's the first real view most people get of the North Dakota badlands - the canyon drops away in layers of red, grey, and cream banded rock with no warning from the flat prairie above. The overlook is accessible day and night; the visitor center is seasonal. Free access. Use this stop to grasp the scale before heading into the park itself.

days_plan.itin_title: Montana East: Big Sky Country from Billings to Bismarck

map_keywords: Theodore Roosevelt National Park South Unit Medora
Chateau de Mores Medora North Dakota

places.name: Medora Nd

visits.text: Exit I-94 at Medora and the North Dakota Badlands open up immediately — eroded buttes, prairie dog towns, and bison grazing the coulees of Theodore Roosevelt National Park's South Unit. Roosevelt ranched here in the 1880s after the deaths of his wife and mother on the same day; he later said he would never have become president without his time in the Badlands. Drive the 36-mile scenic loop from the visitor center — bison are nearly guaranteed year-round. Roosevelt's original Maltese Cross Cabin stands reconstructed behind the visitor center. Paid park entry; the Painted Canyon overlook 7 miles east on I-94 is free.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: Theodore Roosevelt National Park South Unit Medora
Painted Canyon Theodore Roosevelt National Park

visits.text: Copper Falls - The tea-colored water of the Bad River (stained by tannins, not copper) plunges 29 feet down 1.2-billion-year-old black lava. The falls twist through several tiers in a canyon so narrow the walls rise 65-100 feet on either side. Wooden walkways and stone stairs bring you up close.

Brownstone Falls - Just downstream, the Tyler Forks River joins the Bad River by plunging 40 feet over a red lava shelf. This is often the most photogenic of the two falls, especially after rain when the flow explodes.

Devil's Gate - Further down the gorge, this massive rock formation creates a natural gateway over the river. Stone stairs (built by the CCC in the 1930s) descend to the water's edge. The place is impressive in its scale.
Observation Tower - At the end of the CCC trail, this tower offers views across the forested hills of the Penokee Range to the north and, on clear days, Lake Superior on the horizon.

activities.text: Doughboy's Nature Trail - This 1.7-mile loop around the gorges is the heart of the park. Budget 2 hours if you take your time at the six viewpoints. The trail is named for the WWI veterans who built the first suspension bridges here in 1922. No pets allowed on this trail.
Swimming at Loon Lake - After the hike, head down to the lake for a swim in clear water surrounded by forest. The sandy beach is well-maintained.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

places.name: Memphis

visits.text: At 450 Mulberry Street, the pale turquoise facade of the Lorraine Motel looks nearly unchanged from April 4, 1968. A wreath hangs on the second-floor balcony outside Room 306 - the spot where Martin Luther King Jr. was shot while talking to Jesse Jackson below. Walter Bailey had bought and renamed the hotel in 1945 after his wife Loree and the jazz song 'Sweet Lorraine'; during segregation it hosted Aretha Franklin, Otis Redding, and Ray Charles, all passing through to record at nearby Stax. The National Civil Rights Museum opened here in 1991 and was overhauled in a $27.5 million renovation in 2013-2014. Budget two and a half hours minimum - the 260-artifact span covers five centuries of civil rights history. Arrive before 10 a.m. on weekdays to beat school groups.

The building at 706 Union Avenue is shockingly small - a converted auto-glass repair shop that Sam Phillips rented in 1950 for $150 a month. He opened Memphis Recording Service on January 3 that year, ripped out the tin roof, replaced it with acoustic tiles, then spent weeks clapping across every inch of the room to map its echo. That obsession produced the 'slapback' reverb on Elvis Presley's July 5, 1954 recording of 'That's All Right (Mama).' In March 1951, Jackie Brenston and his Delta Cats recorded 'Rocket 88' here - widely considered the first rock 'n' roll single. Designated a National Historic Landmark in 2003, the studio still records at night. Tours run daily; book online - they sell out on weekends. You get to touch the same microphone Elvis used.

Jim Stewart and Estelle Axton - a brother and sister - bought an old movie theater on McLemore Avenue in 1959 and installed their recording equipment. From 1960 to 1974, a string of hits followed that still stop you cold: Otis Redding's 'Try a Little Tenderness', Booker T. & the M.G.'s 'Green Onions', Isaac Hayes' Academy Award-winning 'Theme From Shaft.' Of the 900 singles recorded here, 167 charted in the pop top 100. The original building was razed after Stax went bankrupt in the 1970s; the current museum opened May 2003, rebuilt on the same site, with over 2,000 exhibits including Hayes' gold-trimmed 1972 Cadillac Eldorado. The recreated Studio A alone is worth the trip. Give yourself two hours.

Beale Street was founded in 1841, and by the 1920s it had become the main artery of Black Memphis - barber shops, blues clubs, churches, and a 2,000-seat park auditorium where President Theodore Roosevelt spoke in 1902. W.C. Handy moved here from Clarksdale in 1903 and wrote 'Beale Street Blues' in 1916; a teenage Elvis Presley absorbed the sound by hanging around the clubs in the 1950s. Congress recognized it as the 'Home of the Blues' in 1977. Today the three-block entertainment district runs live music every night. Arrive early evening to walk the street before the crowds arrive, then stay for a set at B.B. King's Blues Club or the Rum Boogie Cafe.

activities.text: Every day at 11 a.m., a red carpet unrolls in the Peabody Hotel lobby and five North American Mallards ride the elevator down from their rooftop Duck Palace - a $200,000 marble enclosure with a bronze fountain. The tradition started as a tipsy prank in 1933 when general manager Frank Schutt put live hunting decoys in the lobby fountain after a hunting trip; by 1940, former circus trainer Edward Pembroke had formalized the march and served as Duckmaster for 50 years. The Peabody at 149 Union Avenue has been the social hub of Memphis since 1925. Arrive 20-30 minutes before 11 a.m. to claim a front-row spot along the red carpet.

An evening blues walk starting at Beale and Second, working east through the neon-lit blocks, is the right way to end a Memphis day. Stop at the W.C. Handy statue in Handy Park, then duck into a bar for a live set - cover charges are typically under $10 and bands rotate all night. The street stays open until 5 a.m. on weekends. For the most authentic sound, look for smaller venues away from the main tourist drag; Thursday through Saturday brings the best bands.

Chicago to New Orleans Road Trip: Route 66 & the Mississippi
Tennessee Music & Mountains Road Trip: Nashville to Great Smoky Mountains
Mississippi Delta Blues Trail: Memphis to Jackson
