The Black Belt: Atlanta to New Orleans Through the Deep South

map_keywords: Melrose Estate Natchez National Historical Park
Forks of the Road Slave Market Natchez

visits.text: Longwood is America's largest octagonal house - 32 rooms planned across 6 floors, crowned by a Byzantine-inspired onion dome. Philadelphia architect Samuel Sloan designed it in 1859 for planter Haller Nutt. When the Civil War broke out in 1861, the Northern workers laid down their tools and went home. Those tools are still there, where they left them, in the unfinished upper floors. Only the basement was completed; the family lived there. Nutt died bankrupt in 1864. His widow Julia occupied the basement until her death, 35 years later. Allow 1 hour for the guided tour.

Rosalie Mansion, built in 1823 on the bluff above the Mississippi, served as Union forces headquarters during the Civil War. Its Rococo Revival furniture is period and considered among the South's best-preserved ensembles. Climb to the terrace to see the river, over a kilometer wide at this point - the barges continuously descending give an idea of the commercial traffic that made Natchez's 19th-century fortune.

Descend to Natchez Under-the-Hill, the old port district at the bluff's base, which for a century was one of America's most violent places - taverns, gambling houses, and brothels welcoming riverboat boatmen. Only a block or two of that past remain, but the setting on the Mississippi merits a late afternoon stop, especially if you want to embark for a sunset boat tour.

activities.text: The first miles of the Natchez Trace Parkway: this 715 km historic road follows the route of an ancient path successively used by bison, Natchez and Chickasaw Native Americans, then American pioneers. The section north of Natchez traverses a remarkable forest of oaks and cypress, with no trucks or billboards permitted. The first 50 kilometers suffice to feel the atmosphere.

Sunset from Natchez bluffs over the Mississippi: at 30 meters above the river, the view embraces the Mississippi's meanders for several kilometers. Operators offer sunset boat outings from Natchez Under-the-Hill.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Louisiana: Jazz, Bayous & Plantations - 9-Day Loop from New Orleans
The Deep South via Mississippi: Shreveport to New Orleans - 10 Days

places.name: Natchitoches

visits.text: Natchitoches - pronounced 'Nack-a-tish' - is the oldest permanent settlement in the Louisiana Purchase territory, founded in 1714 by French explorer Louis Juchereau de Saint-Denis, four years before New Orleans. Its 33-block historic downtown, bordered by Cane River Lake, is one of only two National Historic Landmark Districts in Louisiana, along with NOLA's French Quarter. The red-brick facades and iron-lace balconies of Front Street served as the main filming location for Steel Magnolias (1989). Wander the cobblestones in late afternoon when the golden light reflects off the lake - it's the most photogenic moment of the day.

Fort St. Jean Baptiste is a complete reconstruction of the 1732 French fort, built on the site of the original post founded by Saint-Denis. Open Wednesday through Sunday, it gives a concrete sense of what this colony lost halfway between Louisiana and Spanish Texas represented. Don't miss the black powder firing demonstrations on weekends.

Cane River Creole National Historical Park encompasses two plantations designated as National Historic Landmarks. Oakland Plantation is one of the best-preserved French Creole properties in Louisiana. Magnolia Plantation retains rare original slave cabins and its intact cotton-pressing equipment - direct testimony to the economy that made this region's fortune. Plan at least two hours for both sites, which are about fifteen kilometers apart south of Natchitoches.

activities.text: Taste Natchitoches meat pies: these fried pockets of spiced beef and pork are a local specialty dating back to the French colonists of the 18th century. Bakeries and grocers on Front Street serve them hot all day - try one before hitting the road, it's the town's essential snack.

days_plan.itin_title: The Deep South via Mississippi: Shreveport to New Orleans - 10 Days

map_keywords: Cane River Creole National Historical Park

visits.text: New Glarus - Wisconsin's 'Little Switzerland.' Founded in 1845 by 108 immigrants from the Canton of Glarus, this town has taken its Swiss heritage all the way: flower-decked chalets, German signage, cheese factories, and bakeries. The historical museum recreates a nineteenth-century Swiss village.

New Glarus Brewing Company - Wisconsin's most cult brewery. Their Spotted Cow (a creamy farmhouse ale) is sold only in Wisconsin - making it a prized contraband item for Illinois neighbors. Free tour of the exterior and beer garden. Tastings at the bar.
Historical Museum - Several reconstructed buildings: chalet, cheese factory, forge, school. The story of Swiss immigration to Wisconsin is well told.

activities.text: Sugar River State Trail - This former rail line converted to bike trail crosses 24 miles of rolling countryside between New Glarus and Brodhead. It passes through a railroad tunnel and several bridges. Bike rentals available in the village.

Local Cheese Tasting - Wisconsin is America's top cheese producer. Around New Glarus, several artisan cheese factories offer tastings of gruyère, aged cheddar, and fresh cheese curds (the famous 'squeak-squeak' that squeaks under your teeth).

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

places.name: New Haven

visits.text: Yale University - One of America's Oldest and Most Prestigious Institutions: Founded with roots dating to the 1640s, this Ivy League campus spans 200 acres featuring buildings from three different centuries. Free student-led walking tours reveal Gothic treasures like Sterling Memorial Library and the iconic Harkness Tower rising 216 feet above the historic campus.

Yale University Art Gallery - The Nation's Oldest University Art Museum: Open since 1836, this world-class institution houses over 300,000 works spanning ancient civilizations to contemporary masters, all with free admission in a stunning Louis Kahn-designed building.

Yale Peabody Museum of Natural History - Newly Renovated Wonder: After four years of extensive renovations reopening in 2024, this museum showcases extraordinary collections including the famous Age of Reptiles mural and dinosaur specimens that have educated visitors for generations.

Marsh Botanical Garden - Eight Acres of Living Laboratory: This university-managed garden features greenhouses, outdoor collections, and research facilities supporting both scientific study and public enjoyment of horticultural diversity.

activities.text: New Haven Pizza Trail - Birthplace of American Pizza Perfection: This college town claims America's finest pizza tradition, with legendary establishments perfecting thin-crust pies since the early 1900s. The famous 'white clam pie' has achieved national recognition, while multiple pizzerias have earned spots on 'best in America' lists.

Louis' Lunch - Where the Hamburger Was Born: This tiny establishment claims to have served America's first hamburger sandwich, still prepared on original vertical cast-iron grills using a century-old recipe that draws pilgrims from around the world.

Long Wharf Food Truck Paradise - Latin American Street Food Hub: A cluster of vendors along Long Wharf Drive serves authentic tacos, empanadas, Cuban plantains, and other Latin American specialties from mobile kitchens.

days_plan.itin_title: Connecticut Coastal Drive: From Maritime Mystic to Ivy League New Haven

map_keywords: Yale Peabody Museum of Natural History

visits.text: Avery Island Tabasco Factory — Tour the source of Tabasco pepper sauce produced on this salt dome island since 1868. The McIlhenny Company factory processes peppers grown on-site into the iconic sauce shipped worldwide in 195 countries.

Jungle Gardens — Wander through 67 hectares of botanical gardens featuring 900-year-old Buddha statue, bamboo groves, and lagoons. The sanctuary on Avery Island protects snowy egrets nearly hunted to extinction for their plumes in the 1890s.

Shadows-on-the-Teche — Explore this 1834 plantation home preserved by the National Trust for Historic Preservation on Bayou Teche's banks. The Classical Revival mansion retains original furnishings and documents four generations of the Weeks family.

Konriko Rice Mill — Discover how rice is processed at America's oldest operating rice mill and company museum. The 1912 facility explains Louisiana's rice industry history while demonstrating milling equipment still producing Konriko brand rice.

activities.text: Bayou Teche paddle — Kayak or canoe down Louisiana's most historic waterway winding through moss-draped cypress swamps. The scenic 201-kilometer bayou offers wildlife viewing and access to Cajun culture along its banks.

Boudin trail food tour — Sample different boudin recipes at specialist shops and gas stations along this unofficial culinary trail. Each establishment claims the best recipe for this essential Cajun sausage served hot from steamers.

New Iberia Main Street — Browse antique shops and cafes in this historic downtown preserving 19th-century commercial buildings. The compact Main Street reflects South Louisiana's French-Spanish heritage in its architecture and culture.

days_plan.itin_title: Louisiana Cultural Journey: New Orleans Jazz to Cajun Bayou Country
Deep Louisiana Road Trip: Jazz and Bayous

visits.text: Start at Jackson Square, the heart of the French Quarter since 1721. This 2.5-acre plaza, modeled after Paris's Place des Vosges, witnessed Louisiana's transfer from France to the United States in 1803 during the Louisiana Purchase. At the center stands an 1856 equestrian statue of General Andrew Jackson. Around you: artists display their work along the wrought-iron fence (200 permits issued annually), jazz musicians perform on the sidewalks, and tarot readers offer their services—all in a delightfully chaotic New Orleans fashion.

Facing the square, St. Louis Cathedral is the oldest continuously active Roman Catholic cathedral in the United States. The current building dates from 1789, rebuilt after the great fire that devastated the French Quarter in 1788. On either side stand the Cabildo (former seat of Spanish colonial government, now Louisiana State Museum) and the Presbytère (Mardi Gras museum). Cathedral admission is free—take a look at the rococo-style altar.

Cross Decatur Street to the French Market, the oldest public market in the United States (operating since 1791). You'll find stalls selling local produce, Cajun spices, hot sauce, and souvenirs. At the market's end, the old U.S. Mint building houses the New Orleans Jazz Museum—it's the only building in the U.S. to have served as a mint for both the United States and the Confederacy.

Walk up Royal Street—the French Quarter's gallery and antique dealer street, much quieter than Bourbon Street. The wrought-iron balconies adorned with ferns are a sight in themselves. Look for the initials forged into the railings: they identify the Creole families who built these houses in the early 19th century.

Take the St. Charles Avenue streetcar from Canal Street—the oldest continuously operating streetcar line in the world, designated a National Historic Landmark in 2014. The wooden cars date from 1923-1924, with no air conditioning—open windows are part of the charm. The ride costs $1.25 (exact change required). Get off at Washington Avenue to enter the Garden District.

The Garden District is the neighborhood of grand mansions built by wealthy Americans after the 1803 Louisiana Purchase. Unwilling to live with the Creoles in the French Quarter, they built their own palaces on the other side of Canal Street. You'll see Greek Revival, Italianate, and Queen Anne-style homes, shaded by centuries-old oaks draped with Spanish moss. Stroll Prytania, Coliseum, and First Streets for the finest facades.

Lafayette Cemetery No. 1, at the corner of Washington Avenue and Prytania Street, is one of New Orleans' famous "cities of the dead" cemeteries. Because the water table is so high, graves are built above ground in marble and brick vaults. This cemetery dates from 1833 and has been used as a filming location for several movies and series. Check opening hours before visiting—access is sometimes restricted.

Head down Magazine Street, parallel to St. Charles Avenue, for six miles of independent shops, galleries, and restaurants. This is the best spot for non-touristy shopping: antiques, vintage clothing, bookstores, contemporary art galleries. If you're hungry, po'boys (giant sandwiches stuffed with fried seafood or meat) are the specialty to order here.

Start the morning at Mardi Gras World, a massive warehouse on the Mississippi riverfront where Mardi Gras floats are built. The guided tour lasts about an hour and you'll see artisans at work on giant papier-mâché and fiberglass sculptures—some over 20 feet tall. New Orleans' Mardi Gras attracts more than a million visitors annually, and the floats you see here parade down St. Charles Avenue during carnival season (January-February).

Head to the National WWII Museum, ranked among America's finest museums. Founded in 2000 in New Orleans because this is where Andrew Higgins designed the landing craft used on D-Day. The museum spans several buildings and you could easily spend a full day—allow at least 2 to 3 hours for the main exhibitions. The planes, tanks, and submarines on display are authentic.

Late afternoon, ascend to Vue Orleans, the observation deck on the 34th floor of the former World Trade Center. The 360-degree view covers the Mississippi, French Quarter, Superdome, and Lake Pontchartrain. The interactive exhibit on the same floor traces the city's history through its various colonial periods.

Return route to New Orleans, but not via the interstate. Take US-90 eastbound then branch onto LA-1 along Bayou Lafourche, nicknamed "the longest cul-de-sac in the world"—a straight road following the bayou for dozens of miles, lined with houses on stilts and shrimp boats moored along the banks. Stop in Thibodaux, a small Cajun town on the bayou, for coffee and a photo of the historic downtown.

Arriving in the New Orleans area, make a detour through Barataria Preserve at Jean Lafitte National Historical Park (free admission, 30 minutes south of NOLA). Elevated wooden trails traverse a cypress and palmetto swamp where you can observe alligators, nutria, and water birds without needing a boat. The main trail is 2.2 miles and accessible to all.

Last day in New Orleans—spend it on what you've missed. If you haven't been to City Park yet, now's the time. This 1,300-acre park (larger than New York's Central Park) features 800-year-old oaks, the New Orleans Museum of Art (NOMA), and the Sydney & Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden—a contemporary sculpture garden outdoors among cypress trees and lagoons, free and open to all.

Take the Canal Street streetcar (City Park/Museum line) to get there—it's another historic line that crosses Mid-City, a residential neighborhood experiencing renewal with good restaurants and local bakeries.

activities.text: Late afternoon, settle into a café terrace overlooking Jackson Square for café au lait and beignets—the local specialty served here since the Civil War. Beignets are squares of fried dough covered in powdered sugar, always served in threes.

Evening, head to Frenchmen Street (not Bourbon Street—locals will tell you). This is where the city's real jazz clubs are concentrated. You can listen to live music in several clubs without paying cover—just walk from bar to bar and follow what moves you. Jazz was born here in New Orleans in the early 20th century, and Frenchmen Street is its beating heart today.

Continue by streetcar to Audubon Park (Calhoun Street stop). This 350-acre park lined with centuries-old oaks borders the Mississippi River. You can walk, jog, or simply sit beneath the trees. Audubon Zoo is at the back of the park if you're traveling with children.

Return by streetcar downtown and enjoy a free evening. If you want to explore the Creole culinary scene, restaurants in the Warehouse District (between the French Quarter and Garden District) offer a good balance of local cuisine and less touristy atmosphere than Bourbon Street.

Make a detour through the Tremé neighborhood, the oldest African American neighborhood in the United States. This is where enslaved people gathered on Sundays at Congo Square (now in Louis Armstrong Park) to play music and dance—a site considered one of jazz's birthplaces. The neighborhood gave its name to the HBO series.

Last evening in NOLA: if you haven't done it yet, take a stroll down Bourbon Street for the atmosphere (loud, touristy, but it's a must at least once). Bars are open around the clock and you can walk with your drink in the street—it's legal here, unlike most American cities.

Return to New Orleans late afternoon. Take advantage of this last evening to explore the Bywater neighborhood, east of the French Quarter. This is the city's creative district: giant murals, alternative galleries, cocktail bars in former warehouses. The atmosphere is less touristy than the French Quarter—this is where the city's artists and musicians live.

Final hours in NOLA: return to the French Quarter for one last café au lait, one last beignet, one last piece of jazz in the street. Louis Armstrong Airport (MSY) is 25 minutes by car from downtown. If you're returning your rental car, allow some margin—traffic on I-10 can be heavy during rush hour.

places.name: New York

visits.text: Start at the Frick Collection, 1 East 70th Street. This 1914 mansion once belonged to Henry Clay Frick, a steel magnate who spent 40 years assembling European masterpieces. You'll see three of the 36 known Vermeers in the world - including Officer and Laughing Girl, where window light illuminates the young woman's face while the officer remains in shadow. Don't miss Bellini's St. Francis in the Desert, considered by many art historians to be the finest painting in America, or Fragonard's The Progress of Love series - rejected by Madame du Barry, Louis XV's mistress, then purchased by Frick from J.P. Morgan. The rooms are never crowded: you see the paintings up close, without barriers. Reserve your timed tickets online.

Walk up Fifth Avenue to the Metropolitan Museum of Art at 82nd Street. With over 2 million works spanning 5,000 years, it's the largest art museum in the Americas. If you could only see one thing, head straight to the Temple of Dendur in the Sackler Wing - an Egyptian temple from 15 BC reassembled stone by stone in a glass-walled gallery overlooking Central Park. The Egyptian collections number 26,000 pieces, including the gilded sarcophagus of a priest of Amun. The Vermeers, Rembrandts and Caravaggios are on the second floor in the European galleries. In season, go up to the rooftop - the skyline view at sunset is one of the city's finest. Budget at least 2 hours, even rushing.

Continue to the Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum, 1071 Fifth Avenue. The building itself is the work of art - Frank Lloyd Wright spent 16 years designing it (1943-1959) and never saw the opening, dying six months before completion. The 400-meter spiral ramp takes you down through the collections in a continuous helix. You'll find Kandinskys (the world's largest collection of his works), Picassos, Mondrians, Chagalls and Modiglianis. The building has been a UNESCO World Heritage site since 2019. Take the elevator to the top and walk down: it's the best way to visit.

End the day at the Museum of the City of New York, 1220 Fifth Avenue at 103rd Street. Housed in a neo-Georgian building from 1932, it chronicles 400 years of the city's history. The scale model of future New York and Berenice Abbott's photo collection (Changing New York, 1935-1938) are worth the trip. The surrounding neighborhood - East Harlem - is less touristy and gives you a glimpse of a more authentic New York.

Begin at the American Museum of Natural History, Central Park West at 79th Street. Founded in 1869, it houses 34 million specimens. The star is the 94-foot blue whale suspended in the Milstein Hall of Ocean Life - a life-size model built in 1969. On the fourth floor, the dinosaur fossils include a nearly complete Tyrannosaurus rex skeleton and a 50-foot Barosaurus rearing up on its hind legs in the Theodore Roosevelt entrance hall. The Rose Center for Earth and Space, a 90-foot glass sphere, houses the Hayden Planetarium. Budget at least 2 hours, even moving quickly - the museum covers 2 million square feet.

Right across the street at 170 Central Park West, the New-York Historical Society is the city's oldest museum, founded in 1804. It holds one of the two remaining original copies of the Declaration of Independence printed on July 4, 1776, along with all 435 original watercolors from John James Audubon's Birds of America. The basement gallery, DiMenna Children's History Museum, is perfect if you're traveling with family. Less famous than its neighbors, the place is rarely crowded.

If you're traveling with children, make a stop at the Children's Museum of Manhattan, 212 West 83rd Street. Across five floors, interactive exhibits cover science, health and the arts - everything is hands-on, manipulable, testable. The EatSleepPlay space for under-4s is particularly well designed. Even without kids, you can tour it in 30 minutes to understand the American approach to learning through play.

Finish the day in Harlem with the National Jazz Museum in Harlem, 58 West 129th Street. It's a small museum, but the story is enormous: Harlem was the birthplace of jazz in the 1920s-1940s, with the Cotton Club, Savoy Ballroom and Apollo Theater just blocks away. The museum offers guided listening sessions, rare archival recordings of Duke Ellington, Billie Holiday and Charlie Parker, and live sessions some evenings. The surrounding neighborhood deserves a stroll - the brownstones on Lenox Avenue haven't changed in a century.

Start at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), 11 West 53rd Street. Founded in 1929, it's the world's first museum entirely dedicated to modern art. Van Gogh's The Starry Night (1889) is in gallery 5-5 on the fifth floor - go at opening time, the room fills up within an hour. Right nearby, you'll find Picasso's Les Demoiselles d'Avignon (1907), Monet's Water Lilies (immersive room on the second floor) and Dalí's The Persistence of Memory with its melting clocks. The sculpture garden (Abby Aldrich Rockefeller Sculpture Garden) is a peaceful haven in the middle of Midtown. Budget at least 2 hours.

Walk to the Museum of Arts and Design (MAD), 2 Columbus Circle. This 5-story museum is devoted to contemporary craft - ceramics, jewelry, textiles, glass, wood. Exhibitions rotate frequently, but what never changes is the open studio on the 6th floor where you can watch artists work live. The top-floor restaurant offers panoramic views of Columbus Circle and the southwest entrance to Central Park.

Head down to Pier 86 on the Hudson River for the Intrepid Sea, Air & Space Museum. You board the USS Intrepid, a 900-foot World War II aircraft carrier that also served during the Cold War and Vietnam. On the flight deck, about thirty aircraft are on display, including an Air France Concorde (the only one you can tour in New York) and an A-12 Blackbird, the fastest spy plane ever built (Mach 3.35). Next to the carrier, the submarine USS Growler - one of the few Cold War missile submarines open to the public - can also be toured. Budget 1.5 hours.

If you're interested, stop by Madame Tussauds New York on 42nd Street, right in Times Square. It's pure entertainment: wax figures of American and international celebrities, from sports to cinema. The main draw is the building itself and the frenetic atmosphere of Times Square around it. You can do it in 45 minutes.

Start at the Whitney Museum of American Art, 99 Gansevoort Street, at the foot of the High Line in the Meatpacking District. Renzo Piano's 2015 building is a machine for looking at the city - the stepped terraces offer views of the Hudson and the New Jersey skyline. The museum holds over 26,000 works of 20th and 21st century American art: Edward Hopper (the world's largest collection of his work, with 3,600 pieces bequeathed by his widow), Georgia O'Keeffe, Jean-Michel Basquiat, Andy Warhol. The Whitney Biennial, organized since 1932, is the essential gathering for contemporary American art.

Take the subway to the Lower East Side for the Tenement Museum, 103 Orchard Street. Here, no display cases - you tour real apartments in an 1863 building where 7,000 immigrants lived between 1863 and 2011. The guided tours (mandatory, in English) recreate the living conditions of families from Ireland, Germany, Italy or China. One apartment has remained as it was in 1869 - original wallpaper, coal stove, toilets in the courtyard. Book in advance: slots fill up quickly.

Head to the far south of Manhattan, at Bowling Green, for the National Museum of the American Indian. It occupies the Alexander Hamilton U.S. Custom House (1907), a stunning Beaux-Arts building with ceilings painted by Reginald Marsh depicting the great explorers. Admission is free - it's a Smithsonian museum. The collections cover 12,000 years of indigenous peoples' history across the Americas, from Kwakwaka'wakw ceremonial masks of the Northwest Coast to Navajo textiles.

End with the National September 11 Memorial & Museum, at the foot of the former World Trade Center towers. At ground level, the two square pools occupy exactly the towers' footprints - water falls 30 feet into a central void. Underground, the museum preserves the original tower foundations, an 11-ton twisted steel column and the Survivors' Staircase, the stairway by which hundreds escaped. Plan 1.5 hours and prepare yourself emotionally - it's an intense visit.

Begin the day in Brooklyn with the Brooklyn Museum, 200 Eastern Parkway. New York's second-largest museum at 560,000 square feet, it's far less crowded than the Met - and that's a good thing. The Egyptian collections are among the best in the United States, with pieces dating back 5,000 years. Judy Chicago's The Dinner Party (1979), a monumental feminist installation with 39 place settings dedicated to famous women from history, has a permanent room on the 4th floor. The American art galleries and temporary exhibitions regularly receive critical acclaim.

Take the subway to Court Street station for the New York Transit Museum, housed in a decommissioned 1936 subway station in Downtown Brooklyn. On the original platforms, about twenty vintage subway cars are lined up - from 1907 to today. You can board, sit on the wicker benches of early 20th century cars and compare them with modern trains. The museum also traces the construction of the New York subway, begun in 1900: 236 miles of track, 472 stations, 1.6 billion rides per year. Kids love it - and so do adults.

Finish the marathon with the Ellis Island National Museum of Immigration. Take the ferry from Battery Park - the combined ticket includes the crossing, access to Ellis Island and the Statue of Liberty pedestal. Between 1892 and 1954, 12 million immigrants passed through this building - over 40% of current Americans can trace an ancestor through Ellis Island. The Great Hall on the second floor, with its vaulted Guastavino tile ceiling, is where each arrival was inspected - those who didn't pass the medical exam were sent back. Search for your family name in the free database - you might be surprised. The ferry passes by the Statue of Liberty on the return: enjoy the view from the deck.

activities.text: Between museums, take advantage of Central Park. The entrance at 72nd Street (Bethesda Terrace) is a stone's throw from the Frick. Late in the day, stroll down Madison Avenue for its galleries and grab a drink at one of the Upper East Side bars - Gilded Age atmosphere guaranteed.

After the jazz museum, if you're there on a Wednesday or Saturday evening, check the Apollo Theater schedule (253 West 125th Street) - the Wednesday Amateur Nights have been running since 1934. Ella Fitzgerald was discovered there at 17.

Take advantage of your Times Square visit to watch the city's spectacle at nightfall - the giant screens and crowds are part of the New York experience. Grab a slice of pizza standing at the counter, like a real New Yorker.

After the Tenement Museum, explore Orchard Street and the Lower East Side on foot. It's the historic immigrant neighborhood: Jewish delis, vintage shops, street art. Try a pastrami sandwich at one of the century-old delis in the area - it's a New York institution.

Before taking the ferry, stroll through Battery Park and walk along the harbor esplanade. The view of the Statue of Liberty, Governors Island and New Jersey is superb in the morning. On the way back, if you still have energy, walk across the Brooklyn Bridge - 1.1 miles over the East River, with Manhattan as backdrop.

visits.text: Oregon Caves National Monument — Explore this marble cave system formed by acidic water dissolving limestone over millions of years. Guided tours wind through passages featuring stalactites, flowstone, and the Ghost Room chamber.

Cannon Beach — Walk the wide sandy beach dominated by 72-meter Haystack Rock, the third-tallest sea stack in the world. The iconic monolith hosts nesting tufted puffins from April to July and tide pools teeming with marine life.

Ecola State Park — Hike coastal trails offering dramatic Pacific Ocean vistas from forested headlands. The park preserves the site where Lewis and Clark expedition members obtained whale blubber from local tribes in 1806.

Seaside Promenade — Stroll this 2.4-kilometer boardwalk along Seaside's beach where Lewis and Clark boiled seawater to make salt during winter 1806. The turnaround monument marks the expedition's westernmost point.

activities.text: Tide pool exploration — Examine sea stars, anemones, hermit crabs, and sea urchins in rocky pools at low tide. Haystack Rock's base reveals diverse intertidal ecosystems during minus tides.

Gallery browsing in Cannon Beach — Explore art galleries showcasing Pacific Northwest landscape paintings, glasswork, and sculptures. The artistic community attracts visitors to boutique shops and studios.
Fresh seafood dinner — Dine on Dungeness crab, Pacific oysters, and salmon at oceanfront restaurants. Cannon Beach restaurants emphasize locally caught seafood with sunset ocean views.

days_plan.itin_title: Oregon Natural Wonders: Crater Lake to Pacific Coast Road Trip

visits.text: Niagara Falls State Park — Witness 750,000 gallons of water per second plunging 51 meters over the American Falls and Bridal Veil Falls. America's oldest state park, established in 1885, offers multiple viewing platforms along the Niagara Gorge rim.

Cave of the Winds — Descend 53 meters into the Niagara Gorge to stand within 6 meters of Bridal Veil Falls on the Hurricane Deck. Waterproof ponchos barely protect against the thundering spray and wind gusts reaching 110 km/h.

Maid of the Mist — Board this iconic boat tour operating since 1846 for a close encounter with Horseshoe Falls. The 20-minute voyage navigates the turbulent basin where 3,160 tons of water crash down every second.

Niagara Power Vista — Learn how the falls generate 4.4 million kilowatts of hydroelectric power at this interactive museum overlooking the Robert Moses Power Plant. Exhibits explain how diversion tunnels harness the river's energy while maintaining the falls' flow.

activities.text: Niagara Gorge hiking — Trek the challenging trails descending into the 91-meter-deep gorge carved by 12,000 years of erosion. The Gorge Trail follows the Niagara River's whitewater rapids past giant boulders and exposed rock layers.

Evening illumination viewing — Watch as 3 billion candlepower of LED lights transform the falls into a rainbow spectacle after sunset. The illumination tradition began in 1860 with primitive searchlights and continues nightly year-round.

Wine tasting on Niagara Wine Trail — Sample ice wines and cold-climate varietals at boutique wineries in the Niagara Escarpment appellation. The moderating lake effect and limestone soils produce exceptional Rieslings and Cabernet Francs.

days_plan.itin_title: Empire State Road Trip: From Manhattan Skyscrapers to Niagara Falls

places.name: North Platte

visits.text: You pull off the highway in North Platte and suddenly understand why the city exists: Bailey Yard spreads in every direction across 2,850 acres - eight miles long and two miles wide at its widest point - making it the largest railroad classification yard on earth, recognized in the Guinness Book of Records in 1995. Union Pacific handles 14,000 rail cars through this yard every 24 hours, 365 days a year. The Golden Spike Tower and Visitor Center, shaped like a railroad spike and opened in 2008, lets you ride to the 8th floor for a panoramic view of the endless rows of cars, the two 'hump' sorting mounds, and 315 miles of interlocking track. Former railroaders staff the observation decks and can walk you through exactly how a car gets from an incoming train to its correct outbound route. Paid entry; plan 60 to 90 minutes.

activities.text: While in North Platte, stop briefly at the site of the North Platte Canteen marker downtown - from 1941 to 1946, local women organized to meet every troop train that passed through, sometimes more than 20 a day, providing free coffee, sandwiches and a moment of warmth to over six million soldiers during World War II. The story is told inside the Golden Spike Tower visitor center. If you have an extra 30 minutes, Buffalo Bill's Scouts Rest Ranch - his actual working ranch north of town, where he launched his Wild West Show touring operation - is a state historical park with the original 1886 mansion open for tours.

days_plan.itin_title: Nebraska Sandhills & Pioneer Trails: From Omaha to the Rockies

map_keywords: Golden Spike Tower North Platte Nebraska
Buffalo Bill Ranch State Historical Park North Platte

visits.text: The drive from San Francisco to Yosemite passes through Mariposa, a small town that was one of the most active during the 1850s Gold Rush. Its wooden courthouse, built in 1854, is the oldest still in operation in California. Stop for 30 minutes to walk the main street—the period architecture is remarkably well preserved.
The Mariposa Museum and History Center traces the region's history from the Miwok people to the Gold Rush. Small but well documented—allow 45 minutes to an hour to see everything.

As you enter the valley, Bridalveil Fall greets you: a 620-foot waterfall that flows year-round—one of the few in the park that never completely dries up. In strong wind, the water is blown sideways, which earned it its English name. The Ahwahneechee people, the valley's Native Americans, called it 'Pohono,' the spirit of the puffing wind. The access trail is half a mile round-trip from the parking lot, reachable in 15 minutes.

El Capitan is one of the world's largest granite walls: 3,000 feet of vertical rock rising from the valley floor. The first free climb was completed in 1993 by Lynn Hill—a performance that stunned the global climbing community. From Tunnel View as you enter the valley, you get the classic frame with El Capitan on the left, Half Dome in the distance, and Bridalveil Fall on the right.

Yosemite Falls is the tallest waterfall in North America: 2,425 feet total in three sections. The Lower Yosemite Falls trail is an easy 1-mile loop leading to the base. In spring during snowmelt, the flow is spectacular—by summer, the falls can be nearly dry.

Tioga Road (Highway 120) is the only road that crosses Yosemite from east to west at high elevation. It climbs to 9,945 feet at Tioga Pass—the highest highway pass in California—before reaching Tuolumne Meadows at 8,600 feet. The road is typically closed from late October to late May depending on snow. Along the way, stop at Olmsted Point: a granite overlook offering a sweeping view down into Tenaya Canyon with Half Dome in the background. Tuolumne Meadows is the largest subalpine meadow in the Sierra Nevada—nearly 2 miles of open grassland crossed by the Tuolumne River, framed by granite domes. A quiet place with a completely different atmosphere from the main valley.

activities.text: Near Oakhurst, the Yosemite Mountain Sugar Pine Railroad offers steam train rides on narrow-gauge tracks that were used from 1899 to 1931 to haul timber from the sequoia forests. The 4-mile journey winds through pine forests and offers a glimpse of Sierra Nevada country before entering the park. Check seasonal schedules.

Drive up to Glacier Point for sunset—it's the park's most famous viewpoint. The overlook sits at 7,214 feet elevation, 3,200 feet above the valley floor. From here, you have Half Dome directly in front of you, Vernal Fall and Nevada Fall below, and the High Sierra peaks stretching to the horizon. Ansel Adams captured some of his most iconic photographs here. Plan for an hour's drive from the valley and arrive 45 minutes before sunset to secure a spot.

Return to Yosemite Valley for stargazing. Yosemite Valley is recognized as one of California's premier stargazing sites thanks to minimal light pollution. In summer months, the Milky Way is visible to the naked eye from the valley meadows. Set up at Cook's Meadow or the Swinging Bridge Picnic Area for clear skies.

days_plan.itin_title: California Road Trip: San Francisco, Yosemite & Death Valley
California Road Trip: San Francisco, Yosemite & Death Valley
California Road Trip: San Francisco, Yosemite & Death Valley
Sierra California Road Trip: Sequoia and Kings Canyon
Sierra California Road Trip: Sequoia and Kings Canyon

visits.text: Oakland - San Francisco's Cooler, Edgier Sibling: This diverse city across the bay has emerged as a cultural powerhouse, with thriving arts scenes, innovative cuisine, and proud communities that celebrate authenticity over pretension.

Oakland Museum of California (OMCA) - The State's Story Under One Roof: This unique institution weaves together California's art, history, and natural sciences through immersive galleries and rotating exhibitions exploring the Golden State's identity.

Lake Merritt - America's First Official Wildlife Refuge: This tidal lagoon in the heart of downtown Oakland, designated in 1870, provides sanctuary for migrating birds while offering residents jogging paths, gardens, and waterfront relaxation.

activities.text: Children's Fairyland - Where Storybooks Come to Life: This beloved theme park, opened in 1950, inspired Walt Disney to create Disneyland. Whimsical sets bring fairy tales and nursery rhymes to three-dimensional life for young visitors.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Northwest Kansas Rock Formations - Kansas's Best-Kept Natural Secrets: For decades, travelers sped past these otherworldly geological treasures, but today these Niobrara chalk formations rank among Kansas's most remarkable attractions.

Little Jerusalem Badlands State Park - A Mile-Long City of Stone: Tucked in a valley between Scott City and Oakley, this state's largest Niobrara chalk formation features 100-foot-tall rock faces and pillars. A scenic rim trail circles the towering formations that earned the park its biblical name.

Monument Rocks - 70-Foot Chalk Pillars Rising from the Prairie: The surrounding sprawl of shortgrass prairie makes these Niobrara chalk pillars and natural arches appear even more dramatic, geological sentinels standing watch over the Kansas plains.

Arikaree Breaks - 30 Miles of Yucca-Studded Canyonlands: In the far northwestern reaches of Kansas, these spectacular gullies and bluffs sprawl across the landscape, with Lookout Point providing the best vantage over this unexpected terrain.

activities.text: Chalk Pyramids Trail - Walk Among Ancient Seabed Remnants: These 80-million-year-old formations were deposited when an inland sea covered Kansas, leaving behind the shells and skeletons of marine creatures compressed into chalk.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

visits.text: Rainbow Springs State Park - Florida's Fourth Largest Spring: Crystal-clear water in impossible shades of blue and green flows from one of Florida's most beautiful spring heads. The spring-fed Rainbow River maintains a constant cool temperature year-round.

Rainbow River Tubing - Float Through Paradise: Drift downstream on the crystal-clear, spring-fed river, passing beneath canopies of live oaks while fish swim visibly in the sandy bottom below your tube.

activities.text: Paddling the Rainbow River - Kayak Through Living Color: The remarkable clarity of this spring-fed waterway reveals an underwater world of fish, turtles, and aquatic plants that make every paddle stroke a discovery.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

places.name: Ohiopyle

visits.text: Fallingwater is the most admired architectural work in the United States - American Institute of Architects members voted it 'best all-time work of American architecture' in 1991, and UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 2019. Frank Lloyd Wright, then 67 and late in his career, designed this house in 1935 for Edgar J. Kaufmann, owner of Pittsburgh's Kaufmann department stores. The commission was simple: a weekend forest retreat. The result stunned the architectural world: a 5,300-square-foot house built in cantilever directly over a waterfall on Bear Run creek, with reinforced concrete balconies that appear to float without visible support. The interior incorporates a natural rock as the fireplace hearth, polished local stone floors reflect the nature outside, and corner windows without posts open onto a 360° forest. Book tickets online several weeks in advance - spaces sell out quickly, admission: $30 adult for the standard guided tour.

Ohiopyle Falls - a twenty-foot cascade spanning the full width of the Youghiogheny River - is the park's visible heart and the starting point for whitewater navigation. The Visitor Center observation platform offers a direct view of the falls from inside the building, allowing you to admire the spectacle even in rain. It was here that George Washington passed in 1754 during the Seven Years' War and considered - then abandoned - the idea of using the river as a military supply axis, the rapids being too violent. In summer, dozens of rafts and kayaks run the Class III-IV rapids just downstream from the falls - a sight in itself from shore.

A few minutes' drive from Ohiopyle, Cucumber Falls is one of Pennsylvania's most photographed waterfalls: a 30-foot bridal veil plunging into a natural pool framed by moss-covered rock walls. The access trail from the Chalk Hill Road parking lot is short, flat, and accessible to all. The unique feature: you can walk behind the falls between the rock wall and the water curtain, offering a rare and slightly damp perspective. Arrive early in the morning - it's one of the park's most visited spots in summer - or late afternoon when tour buses have left.

activities.text: The Youghiogheny River at Ohiopyle is one of the best rafting rivers on the East Coast. The Lower Yough, Class III-IV over seven miles, attracts thousands of amateur and experienced paddlers each summer. Local outfitters - Wilderness Voyageurs, White Water Adventurers - provide wetsuit, helmet, and life jacket, and prepare groups with a safety briefing before departure. The name Ohiopyle comes from a Lenape word meaning 'it turns white' - a direct reference to the rapids' foam. Even if you don't paddle, watching groups run the rapids from shore is a free and lively spectacle all summer.

The Great Allegheny Passage is a 150-mile rail-trail connecting Pittsburgh to Cumberland, Maryland, entirely built on converted former railroads. The section from Ohiopyle is one of the most spectacular, running along the Youghiogheny gorge through a dense forest of hemlock and maples. The trail is nearly flat, making it accessible to all fitness levels. A few miles downstream is the junction with the Casselman River, a wild picnic spot popular with locals. In autumn, red maples and golden beeches transform the GAP Trail into a tunnel of colors.

days_plan.itin_title: Pennsylvania Loop: From Amish Country to the Appalachians - 11 Days

map_keywords: Great Allegheny Passage GAP Trail Ohiopyle

visits.text: Enter the Okefenokee Swamp, a 438,000-acre wilderness where glassy blackwater channels wind through ancient cypress and tupelo forests draped in Spanish moss. This vast wetland, one of the largest intact freshwater ecosystems in North America, supports over 600 species including American alligators, river otters, and countless wading birds. The mysterious, primordial atmosphere creates a hauntingly beautiful landscape unlike anywhere else in the country.

Walk the elevated Chesser Island Boardwalk to an observation tower providing panoramic views across endless prairies of floating peat islands and wildlife-rich marshlands. The preserved homestead reveals how hardy pioneers once scratched out existence in this watery wilderness. The visitor center explains the swamp's unique ecology and cultural history.

activities.text: Glide through mirror-calm waters on guided boat tours where experienced naturalists point out alligators basking on logs, herons stalking fish, and turtles sunning on cypress knees. The earthy, mossy scent of the wetlands and calls of countless birds create a sensory immersion in one of America's last great wild places.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

visits.text: You walk between the two bronze Gates of Time: 9:01 on one side for the last moment of ordinary life, 9:03 on the other for the first moment of aftermath. Between them, 168 bronze chairs stand in nine rows - one per floor of the Murrah Building - nineteen of them smaller to mark the children from the day care center. The Reflecting Pool occupies what was Fifth Street, where McVeigh parked the Ryder truck. The Survivor Tree, an American elm that stood next to the building, absorbed the full force of the blast and still grows at the east end of the memorial. Inside the museum, you hear the actual audio from a water board meeting taking place across the street when the bomb went off - then the explosion itself, mid-sentence. Allow at least two hours.

You enter through a foyer dominated by James Earle Fraser's 18-foot-tall plaster 'End of the Trail' - a lone Native American slumped on a weary horse, cast in 1915 and reproduced more widely than almost any other American sculpture. Chester Reynolds of Kansas City campaigned in the early 1950s for a national Western institution; Oklahoma City won the bid by donating property on Persimmon Hill, and the museum opened in 1955. The 220,000-square-foot building holds 28,000 artworks and artifacts, the world's largest collection of American rodeo photography, and the Prosperity Junction gallery - a full-scale replica turn-of-the-century Western town you walk through. Plan two and a half hours.

You stand in front of the only state capitol in America that ever had working oil wells on its grounds. When oil was struck south of downtown in December 1928, drilling crept north until derricks surrounded the building itself; one well was slant-drilled from across the street to tap reserves directly beneath the Capitol. Production ended in 1986. The dome you see today was added in 2002 - the original building opened without one in 1917 because funds ran out. The Capitol is also one of only two state capitols on the historic Route 66 alignment. Free tours run on weekdays.

You walk down Exchange Avenue and hear an auctioneer's rapid-fire chant drifting through the morning air - the Oklahoma National Stockyards, founded October 3, 1910, still runs cattle auctions on Mondays and Tuesdays, and by the early 1980s had become the world's number-one stocker-feeder cattle market handling over a million animals per year. The half-mile of Exchange Avenue is a living Western district: Langston's Western Wear has been here since 1913, Little Joe's Boots has sold handmade cowboy boots since 1950, and Cattlemen's Steakhouse has been open since 1910. Stockyards City is on the National Register of Historic Places and still smells exactly like what it is.

You step into a building whose design references the Mississippian mound culture - the central 109-foot 'Sky Journey' tower rises above a complex opened in September 2021 after two decades of planning, built to represent Oklahoma's 39 federally recognized tribal nations on land that was once Indian Territory. The permanent galleries cover 12,000 years of continuous Native presence in what became Oklahoma, from pre-contact civilizations through the forced removals of the 1830s, the allotment era, and the present. The Harvest Moon Café inside serves food influenced by tribal food traditions - stop for lunch if the timing works.

You finish the trip at the 17-acre Myriad Botanical Gardens in the heart of downtown - an easy walk from the memorial and Bricktown. The Crystal Bridge Tropical Conservatory is a 224-foot glass cylinder housing a rainforest and desert ecosystem side by side; it opened in 1988 and is still one of OKC's stranger buildings in the best sense. The outdoor gardens are free and open daily; the Crystal Bridge carries a small admission fee. If the weather holds, it's a calm, unhurried end to seven days on the road.

activities.text: Tonight you walk the mile-long Bricktown Canal, opened in July 1999 as part of the MAPS voter-approved program that transformed what had been an abandoned 1890s warehouse district into Oklahoma City's main entertainment draw. The red-brick buildings date from 1898 to 1930; their facades now house restaurants, bars, and live music. The Chickasaw Bricktown Ballpark - home of the Triple-A OKC Dodgers - opened in 1998 right on the canal. For dinner, Vast on the 49th floor of the Devon Energy Center gives you the full Oklahoma plains panorama on a clear night, or stay canal-level at any of the restaurants along the water.

You find sculptor Paul Moore's bronze panorama at the south end of the canal - dozens of life-size figures in full gallop, capturing the April 22, 1889 Land Run when 50,000 settlers charged simultaneously across the border into unassigned Indian Territory. Some claimed 160-acre plots within an hour. The full monument was completed in 2008 and spans a city block. It's free, it's lit at night, and it takes about fifteen minutes to walk the full length of it.

You board the water taxi at the canal dock and spend forty minutes moving through the mile-long Bricktown Canal - past the Land Run Monument, the Chickasaw Bricktown Ballpark, and the converted 1890s-1930s warehouse facades that now house restaurants and bars. The canal opened in July 1999 as part of the MAPS program; guides on board give the district's full history from abandoned warehouses to the city's main entertainment draw. The taxi runs seasonally from spring through fall.

You close out the trip at a table in Bricktown, where the red-brick walls and the canal outside do most of the atmosphere work for you. Mickey Mantle's Steakhouse at 7 Mickey Mantle Drive sits right on the canal and has been an OKC staple since 1995, the year of the bombing - the city rebuilt around it. If you want something lighter, the Bricktown Brewery has been on E. Sheridan since 1992 and serves its own beer in a 1903 building. Either way, you're eating in a place that was boarded up twenty years before you arrived and has been alive ever since.

days_plan.itin_title: Thelma & Louise Movie Route: Utah & Arizona Filming Locations
Oklahoma Route 66 & Native Heritage: Tulsa to OKC
Oklahoma Route 66 & Native Heritage: Tulsa to OKC

visits.text: Arnolds Park Amusement Park—This 20-acre park on the shore of West Lake Okoboji has been operating since 1889. Its wooden roller coaster, The Legend, dates to 1927 and ranks as the 13th-oldest operating coaster in the world. Park admission is free—you pay only for the rides. Climb aboard the Ferris wheel for sweeping views across the lake.

Iowa Great Lakes Maritime Museum—Right next to the amusement park, this small museum showcases antique wooden boats and the region's lake history. If you have time, walk one block west to the Abbie Gardner Cabin, an 1856 log structure tied to the Spirit Lake Massacre, a violent chapter in pioneer history.

Gull Point State Park—On the north shore of West Lake, this park offers a sandy beach, shaded trails, and picnic areas under the oaks. It's quieter than Arnolds Park—perfect for a calm morning by the water.

Take a drive to Spirit Lake, Iowa's largest natural lake at 5,700 acres. The small town of the same name has the charm of a tree-lined Midwest village. Nothing flashy, but the atmosphere feels genuinely authentic.

activities.text: Boat cruise on West Lake Okoboji—The Queen II excursion boat runs 75-minute narrated tours around the lake's bays and shoreline estates. West Lake Okoboji is one of only three lakes in the world with such intensely blue water (the others being Lake Geneva and Bavaria's Königssee, according to locals). True or not, the water is genuinely deep blue thanks to the lake's 135-foot depth and spring-fed clarity.

Biking the Spine Trail—This 14-mile bike path links the communities around the lakes. The stretch between Arnolds Park and Milford hugs the shoreline with open water views. Bike rentals available on-site.
In the evening, catch sunset from the west shore of the lake—locals gather with coolers and folding chairs. The vibe recalls old-time resort towns from another era.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs
Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs

visits.text: You step into the Old Market and it takes a moment to register that these cobblestone streets and cast-iron canopies have been here since the 1880s, when the district was the engine of Omaha's wholesale produce trade. Bounded by 10th and 13th Streets and listed on the National Register of Historic Places, the neighborhood was nearly demolished in the 1960s until Sam Mercer converted his family's red-brick warehouses rather than tear them down - his first move was opening a French restaurant in the former Gilinsky Fruit Company building in 1968. Today, over 45 restaurants, galleries and boutiques fill the ground floors of what the Register recognizes as one of the most intact 19th-century warehouse districts in the Midwest. The Saturday Farmers Market (May to October) draws 150 vendors and up to 10,000 shoppers. Don't leave without ducking into the Old Market Passageway - a covered arcade of small shops that most visitors walk straight past.

The Durham Museum occupies Omaha's former Union Station, an Art Deco building designed by Gilbert Stanley Underwood - the same architect behind the lodges at Bryce Canyon and Zion - and opened in 1931. At its peak the station served eight railroad lines, ranking Omaha as the fourth-largest rail center in the United States; it closed in 1971 and was donated by Union Pacific to the city two years later. Congress designated it a National Historic Landmark in December 2016. Inside you'll walk through restored 1940s storefronts, board full-size train cars on Track 1, and examine the Byron Reed coin collection, assembled in the 1880s and donated to the city at his death. The working soda fountain in the lobby is original - order a phosphate at the counter.

The Joslyn Art Museum was a gift to Omaha from Sarah Joslyn, who donated more than $3 million in 1928 as a memorial to her husband George - at the time the richest man in Nebraska. The 1931 Art Deco building, clad in Georgia marble and designed by John and Alan McDonald, was ranked among the 100 finest buildings in the United States seven years after opening. The collection of over 12,000 objects spans 5,000 years; the museum stewards one of the country's most important Karl Bodmer painting holdings in its Western Studies center. Ed Ruscha donated over 40 works in 2018. A 42,000-square-foot addition opened in September 2024. General admission is free.

activities.text: Henry Doorly Zoo covers 160 acres and has been rated the world's best zoo by TripAdvisor and No. 1 in the U.S. by USA Today multiple years running. The Desert Dome is the world's largest indoor desert - 84,000 square feet across two levels under the largest glazed geodesic dome on earth, housing three desert ecosystems side by side. Below it, Kingdoms of the Night is the world's largest nocturnal exhibit. The Lied Jungle is North America's largest indoor rainforest. Arrive at 9 a.m. when the gates open, start at the Desert Dome, and work back toward the entrance.

In the evening, walk across the Bob Kerrey Pedestrian Bridge - a 3,000-foot cable-stayed span over the Missouri River linking Nebraska and Iowa. The view back toward Omaha from the Iowa side is the best free panorama of the city, and the bridge is lit after dark. Return to the Old Market for dinner: Plank Seafood Provisions for oysters, Le Bouillon for modern French, or Ted & Wally's scratch-made ice cream to finish. The First Friday gallery walk (first Friday each month) keeps Old Market galleries open late with free entry.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa and Midwest Road Trip: Great Plains Loop
Nebraska Sandhills & Pioneer Trails: From Omaha to the Rockies
Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

visits.text: Blue Spring State Park - Summer Swimming, Winter Manatee Watching: This remarkable spring offers completely different experiences by season. Summer visitors swim in the constant 72-degree water, while winter brings hundreds of manatees seeking warmth as ocean temperatures drop.

Manatee Viewing Boardwalks - Gentle Giants by the Hundreds: Elevated walkways provide extraordinary views of Florida's beloved marine mammals, with peak season (November-March) bringing the largest congregations anywhere in the state.

activities.text: Spring Head Diving Competition - Local Kids at Play: Watch (or join) as children compete to see who can free dive deepest into the spring's blue depths, a time-honored tradition at this beloved swimming hole.

days_plan.itin_title: Connecticut Coastal Drive: From Maritime Mystic to Ivy League New Haven
The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Kennedy Space Center — Explore NASA's launch headquarters featuring the Space Shuttle Atlantis, Saturn V rocket, and Mission Control. Interactive exhibits chronicle space exploration history while viewing platforms overlook active launch pads on Cape Canaveral.

Rocket Garden — Walk among actual Mercury, Gemini, and Apollo rockets standing vertically as they did before launch. These historic spacecraft carried America's first astronauts into orbit during the 1960s space race.

Astronaut Encounter — Meet veteran astronauts who share firsthand accounts of living and working in space. Q&A sessions offer unique insights into spacewalks, zero gravity, and life aboard the International Space Station.

activities.text: Lunch with an Astronaut — Dine alongside space veterans who recount their missions over a meal. This exclusive experience provides unprecedented access to NASA's heroes in an intimate setting.

Cocoa Beach surfing — Try surfing on this 10-kilometer beach where eleven-time world champion Kelly Slater learned to ride waves. Consistent Atlantic swells and warm waters create ideal conditions for beginners and experts alike.

International Drive evening — Browse outlet malls, entertainment complexes, and restaurants along Orlando's tourism corridor. The 18-kilometer strip offers family entertainment from observation wheels to wax museums.

days_plan.itin_title: Florida's Diverse Coast: Everglades Adventure to Miami Art Deco
The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Discover Padre Island National Seashore, the longest undeveloped barrier island in the world, stretching over 70 miles along the Gulf of Mexico with pristine beaches untouched by commercial development. The protected coastline provides critical nesting habitat for endangered Kemp's ridley sea turtles, with public releases of hatchlings creating unforgettable wildlife encounters during nesting season. The island's wild, windswept beauty offers a serene escape into coastal wilderness.

Explore the diverse ecosystems where Gulf waters meet coastal dunes, grasslands, and tidal flats supporting remarkable biodiversity. The Malaquite Visitor Center provides orientation to the park's natural and cultural history, including the shipwrecks that dot the treacherous coastline. Designated sections allow four-wheel-drive vehicle access to remote beach areas far from crowds.

activities.text: Experience world-class bird watching opportunities, as Padre Island lies along the Central Flyway migration route, attracting hundreds of species throughout the year including rare shorebirds and raptors. Fishing enthusiasts can cast into the surf for redfish, speckled trout, and other Gulf species along miles of accessible shoreline. Swimming, beachcombing, and camping under starlit skies provide classic coastal recreation in an unspoiled natural setting.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Texas: Hill Country, Big Bend & Gulf Coast Beaches

visits.text: Horseshoe Bend is 10 minutes from Page. The trail is 1.5 miles round-trip from the parking lot and ends at a viewpoint on the edge of a 1,000-foot drop. Below, the Colorado traces an almost perfect horseshoe meander through red sandstone. No guardrails along most of the rim—stay cautious, especially with children. Come early morning or late afternoon for light and to avoid crowds.

Push on to Upper Antelope Canyon, the world's most photographed slot canyon. Sunlight pierces openings above and creates beams that illuminate sandstone streaked with pink, orange, and purple. The visit is only with a Navajo guide (the canyon is on Navajo land) and lasts about 1 hour. Book weeks in advance in high season. The noon slot offers the best light beams in summer. Lower Antelope Canyon is a cheaper and more adventurous alternative (stairs, ladders).

Late in the day, check out Glen Canyon Dam—this 710-foot-high dam holds back Lake Powell, a reservoir 186 miles long with 1,960 miles of shoreline (more than California's entire Pacific coast). The visitor center offers a plunging view of the dam and canyon below.

For your second day in Page, explore what most tourists miss. Wahweap Overlook offers panoramic views of Lake Powell and Navajo sandstone cliffs. It's free, little-known, and the morning light is superb.

If you're seeking solitude, Stud Horse Point is an off-the-beaten-path viewpoint 20 minutes from Page. A short dirt track leads to a promontory with 360° views of desert striped in red and white. Very few visitors—it's a local secret. Passable in regular car in dry weather.

activities.text: If you have time, a boat excursion on Lake Powell accesses flooded side canyons and isolated red sand beaches. Outfitters offer half-day trips from Wahweap Marina.

If you didn't do Antelope Canyon yesterday, now's the time. Otherwise, spend the morning kayaking on the Colorado between Glen Canyon Dam and Lees Ferry—red sandstone cliffs plunge directly into turquoise water. Outfitters offer half-day trips.

days_plan.itin_title: Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons

visits.text: Palm Springs - Midcentury Modern Paradise in the Desert: This Sonoran Desert oasis has attracted Hollywood celebrities since the 1930s, its clean-lined architecture and year-round sunshine creating an aesthetic preserved in over 800 documented modernist buildings.

Palm Springs Aerial Tramway - 10 Minutes from Desert Floor to Alpine Forest: The world's largest rotating tramcars climb nearly 6,000 vertical feet to Mount San Jacinto State Park, where temperatures drop 40 degrees and pine forests replace palms.

Palm Springs Art Museum - World-Class in the Desert: This respected institution houses significant collections of modern and contemporary art, Native American artifacts, and glass sculpture, with a satellite architecture museum downtown.

activities.text: Midcentury Modern Architecture Tours - The World's Finest Concentration: Preservation groups offer tours of homes designed by legends like Richard Neutra, Albert Frey, and Donald Wexler, many still privately owned but viewable from the street.

Joshua Tree National Park - Where Two Deserts Collide: Just an hour away, this otherworldly landscape of twisted Joshua trees and jumbled boulder piles spans both the Mojave and Colorado deserts, offering world-class rock climbing and surreal photography opportunities.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Marvel at Palo Duro Canyon State Park, known as the Grand Canyon of Texas, featuring dramatic landscapes of colorful rock formations, steep mesa walls, and extensive hiking trails stretching over 120 miles long and up to 20 miles wide. The canyon's geological formations display a vivid palette of red, orange, and white hues that become especially stunning at sunrise and sunset. As the nation's second-largest canyon, this natural wonder offers breathtaking vistas at every turn.

Hike the Lighthouse Trail to reach the park's iconic Lighthouse Peak, a towering hoodoo formation that has become the symbol of Palo Duro Canyon. The moderate trail winds through varied terrain showcasing millions of years of geological history carved by the Prairie Dog Town Fork of the Red River. The panoramic views from this landmark reward hikers with some of the most spectacular scenery in all of Texas.

activities.text: Explore the CCC Trail, which provides insights into the remarkable work of the Civilian Conservation Corps during the 1930s, who built much of the park's infrastructure during the Great Depression. Wildlife enthusiasts can observe the Texas state bison herd that has been reintroduced to the park, along with diverse bird species and other native animals. The Pioneer Amphitheater within the park hosts concerts, theatrical performances, and special events throughout the season.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Texas: Hill Country, Big Bend & Gulf Coast Beaches

places.name: Panama City Beach

visits.text: The resort strip that defines Panama City Beach ends at St. Andrews State Park's gate. Behind it: 1,200 acres of former military reservation purchased from the U.S. government in 1947 for $2.50 an acre, protecting one of the last stretches of intact coastal scrub in Northwest Florida. The War Department emplaced two 155mm guns in the dunes here in 1942 for harbor defense - one original gun mount still stands near the jetty, shaded by a pavilion. A mile and a half of Gulf beach, a calm lagoon, two nature trails. Fee applies.

Shell Island exists because of the Army Corps. In the 1930s they dredged a pass between St. Andrews Bay and the Gulf; the spoil sand accumulated into a 7-mile undeveloped barrier island with no buildings, no roads, no cars. Spanish explorers, pirates, and the U.S. Navy all passed through here at different points; the island kept washing their traces away. Today you cross by shuttle from St. Andrews - a 15-minute ride to a beach that feels completely removed from the resort town behind you. Sea turtles nest here May through October. Take the first crossing at 9 a.m.

Gator Lake Trail and Heron Pond Trail at St. Andrews thread through coastal scrub communities that used to cover this entire coastline before development. Alligators sun on the Gator Lake banks, herons stalk the shallows, white-tailed deer browse the dune edges at dusk. These trails pull a fraction of the crowd that hits the beach parking lot. Weekday mornings give you the boardwalks to yourself.

activities.text: The St. Andrews jetty has been creating an artificial reef since the 1930s - flounder, sheepshead, rays, and the occasional sea turtle moving through the pass. Snorkel the Gulf side of Shell Island at high tide: visibility regularly hits 30 feet in calm conditions, and the color of the water over white sand at that depth is something you won't forget. Gear rents at the park concession or from the shuttle operator.

Thomas Drive, three miles east of the resort zone, is where the local fish houses are - places buying direct from the Panama City Beach fleet for decades. Gulf red snapper filleted the same day it was caught tastes nothing like what arrives frozen on Front Beach Road. Ask your server if the catch is local; in the right season, it always is. Go at sunset, find a deck with a water view.

days_plan.itin_title: Florida Panhandle: Tallahassee to Pensacola on the Emerald Coast

map_keywords: St Andrews State Park Panama City Beach
Shell Island Panama City Beach Florida

places.name: Park

visits.text: Park City nearly died twice: when the silver ran out, then when the mountain burned in 1898. Everything you see on Main Street was rebuilt after that fire. George Hearst—the press baron's father—made his fortune here with the Ontario Mine, bought for $30,000 and yielding over $50 million. The street is now lined with 200 shops and restaurants in those century-old buildings. Walk to the top: the view of the Wasatch is worth the climb.

Head down to the basement of the Park City Museum to see the 19th-century territorial jail cell—intact, bars and all. The museum occupies the old courthouse and traces the town's history from the silver boom to the 2002 Olympics. It's compact, well-done, and in one hour you'll understand why Park City survived when so many mining towns around it vanished.

Ten minutes north of Main Street, the Utah Olympic Park still hosts athletes training on the ski jumps and bobsled runs from the 2002 Olympics. You can watch from the stands for free. The Joe Quinney Museum traces Utah's Olympic history since the 1930s. Summer bobsled rides and zip-lining cost extra but are worth it if you're game.

activities.text: Take the Town Lift from Main Street—it drops you right onto Park City Mountain's trails. As soon as the snow melts (usually May-June), over 640 km of marked trails open for mountain biking and hiking. Rent a bike at the bottom of the street or come on foot: even an hour up high completely shifts your perspective on the town.

High West Distillery occupies an 1906 stable on Main Street—Utah's first legal distillery since 1870. Order a Rendezvous Rye at the bar or join a guided tasting. On the terrace in the evening, with the mountains behind, it's one of the best ways to end a day in Park City.

days_plan.itin_title: Salt Lake City to Great Basin Road Trip: Desert, Salt & Stars

map_keywords: Park City Main Street Historic District

visits.text: Explore Brookgreen Gardens, where lush pathways wind past one of the finest outdoor sculpture collections in America displayed beneath towering oaks draped in Spanish moss. The gardens blend nature and artistry seamlessly, with hundreds of works ranging from classical to contemporary pieces positioned among reflective pools and manicured lawns. This former rice plantation now serves as a sanctuary of beauty and culture.

Wander through the extensive botanical gardens featuring seasonal blooms, themed garden rooms, and hidden corners perfect for quiet contemplation. The Lowcountry Zoo showcases native wildlife in naturalistic settings, providing insight into regional ecosystems. Brookgreen's combination of art, nature, and history creates hours of peaceful, inspiring exploration.

activities.text: Discover the sculpture garden's shaded paths where art and nature interweave in constantly surprising ways. Each season brings different blooms and shifting light that transform the artistic experience. The gardens' commitment to preserving Lowcountry culture and environment makes this destination both beautiful and meaningful.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

visits.text: Step back nearly 1,000 years at Pecos National Historical Park, where substantial ruins of Pecos Pueblo and a Spanish colonial mission church reveal centuries of cultural interaction and conflict in the American Southwest. The park preserves one of the largest and most important archaeological sites in the region, where the Pecos people built a thriving trade center connecting Plains and Pueblo cultures.

Walk among the massive adobe walls and reconstructed kiva structures that once housed a population of over 2,000 people at the height of Pueblo civilization. The adjacent mission ruins demonstrate Spanish colonial efforts to convert indigenous peoples, creating a layered historical landscape of profound significance for understanding the region's complex heritage.

activities.text: Explore the park's trails connecting archaeological sites with natural landscapes that supported human habitation for thousands of years. Interpretive exhibits explain the daily lives, spiritual practices, and eventual abandonment of this once-great pueblo, whose descendants now live among the Jemez Pueblo people.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

visits.text: A day of road-tripping through central Iowa countryside. First stop: Winterset (40 minutes southwest of Des Moines), John Wayne's birthplace and Iowa's covered bridge capital. Six bridges survive in Madison County, built between 1868 and 1884. Roseman Bridge is the most famous—it's the one from the 1995 film with Meryl Streep and Clint Eastwood. Robert James Waller's novel sold 50 million copies. You can walk across the bridge and read messages left by visitors on the interior walls.

Holliwell Bridge, the longest of the six (33 meters), spans the Middle River amid rolling prairies. Count on 30 minutes of gravel road to reach it—that's part of the charm. If you have time, also visit the Cutler-Donahoe Bridge, accessible from Winterset's City Park without dirt roads.

In downtown Winterset, visit the John Wayne Birthplace Museum—the modest 4-room house where Marion Robert Morrison (his real name) was born in 1907. The adjacent museum contains personal items, film costumes, and a photo collection.

Drive 1 hour 15 minutes east to Pella, a Dutch town transplanted to Iowa. The Vermeer Mill, built in 2002 by craftsmen from the Netherlands, is North America's tallest working windmill—37 meters. It still grinds grain. The Molengracht Canal downtown, with its gabled houses and tulips (in May), will make you feel like you've landed in the Netherlands. The Tulip Time Festival in May attracts 150,000 visitors.

activities.text: End the day with a Dutch pastry at one of Pella's bakeries—the letterbanketen (almond paste puff pastry sticks) and stroopwafels are homemade.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa and Midwest Road Trip: Great Plains Loop
Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs

places.name: Pensacola

visits.text: 350,000 square feet. 150 aircraft. 4,000 artifacts. Free. The National Naval Aviation Museum on NAS Pensacola is the world's largest of its kind, and it earns the title. NAS Pensacola opened in 1914 as the first naval air station in the United States - every Navy, Marine Corps, and Coast Guard aviator trained here. The NC-4 that crossed the Atlantic in 1919 is here. The sole surviving aircraft from the Battle of Midway is here. In the Blue Angel Atrium, four A-4 Skyhawks hang in diamond formation above you. The Blue Angels practice Tuesday and Wednesday mornings March through November; the public can watch from the flight line. As of early 2026, public access is weekends only - confirm current policy before visiting.

Fort Pickens was completed in 1834, survived the Civil War without Confederate capture, and played a darker role starting October 25, 1886, when Geronimo and 15 Apache warriors stepped off a train in Pensacola under guard. Local businessmen had lobbied the government to route the prisoners here rather than to St. Augustine - for tourism. By February 1887, 459 visitors on a single Sunday were paying 50 cents to stare through the casemate bars. Geronimo was moved to Alabama in 1888 and died at Fort Sill, Oklahoma, in 1909, still a prisoner. Part of Gulf Islands National Seashore; fee applies, National Park pass accepted.

Palafox Street is where Pensacola's layered history shows up most clearly. A Spanish expedition landed near here in 1559 - 46 years before Jamestown - making this one of the oldest European-settled sites in North America. The five-block corridor mixes Spanish Colonial buildings with 19th-century commercial architecture; the Seville Quarter at the south end turns historic warehouses into live music venues after dark. Walk north to Christ Episcopal Church, then south toward the bay at sunset. The light on the brick in late afternoon is worth timing your day around.

activities.text: Santa Rosa Island's western section - Fort Pickens Road inside Gulf Islands National Seashore - gives you 7 miles of protected Gulf beach with no resort buildings in sight. The quartz sand here was ground from Appalachian mountains and carried south by rivers over millions of years; mid-afternoon, it turns white in a way that makes every other Florida beach look beige. Swim, walk the full length, or rent a bike at the park entrance and ride the dune line road along the Gulf.

The Fish House on Pensacola Bay has been sourcing local Gulf catch for 30 years and has a deck directly over the water. Last meal on the Panhandle: Gulf red snapper, local shrimp, maybe oysters from one of the farms rebuilding the region's shellfish culture. Get there before 6 p.m. to watch the sun drop behind the Pensacola Bay Bridge - a fitting end to a coast that started at a spring pumping 400 million gallons a day and finishes at the bay where naval aviators have been taking off since 1914.

days_plan.itin_title: Florida Panhandle: Tallahassee to Pensacola on the Emerald Coast

map_keywords: Fort Pickens Gulf Islands National Seashore

visits.text: Petit Jean State Park - Arkansas's First and Most Beloved State Park: Perched atop a flat-topped mountain bearing the name of a legendary French explorer's disguised lover, this park offers 95-foot Cedar Falls plunging into a misty canyon, ancient Native American rock shelters, and Seven Hollows Trail winding through otherworldly geological formations.

Cedar Falls - The Crown Jewel Cascade: Descend into Cedar Creek Canyon to witness water plummeting nearly 100 feet into an emerald pool surrounded by towering bluffs. The 2-mile round-trip hike rewards adventurers with one of Arkansas's most photographed natural wonders.

Rock House Cave & Native American Bluff Shelters - Windows into Ancient Arkansas: Explore shallow caves where indigenous peoples sought shelter thousands of years ago, their presence still visible in mysterious petroglyphs etched into stone walls.

activities.text: Seven Hollows Trail - Journey Through a Wonderland of Stone: This 4.4-mile loop traverses seven distinct geological formations, from slot canyons to natural bridges, through a primeval landscape that feels untouched by time.

days_plan.itin_title: Arkansas Itinerary: Hot Springs, Ozarks and Natural Parks
