places.name: Philadelphia

visits.text: You step inside the most important building in American history. It was in this room - the Pennsylvania State House, completed in 1753 - that 56 delegates signed the Declaration of Independence on August 4, 1776, and where the United States Constitution was adopted in 1787. Architect Andrew Hamilton, assisted by master builder Edmund Woolley, designed this red brick building in the Georgian style: symmetry, classical proportions, wooden steeple. Today a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1979, the building is managed by the National Park Service. Ranger-led tours last 30 minutes and depart every half hour - book online in advance, as morning slots fill quickly in high season.

The Liberty Bell is one of the most historically charged symbols of the United States, and its famous crack tells more than any text ever could. Commissioned in 1751 by the Pennsylvania Assembly to commemorate the 50th anniversary of William Penn's Charter of Privileges, the bell weighs 2,000 pounds and bears an inscription from Leviticus: 'Proclaim LIBERTY Throughout all the Land unto all the Inhabitants Thereof.' It cracked permanently on George Washington's birthday in 1846 and has never rung since. It was the abolitionists of the 1830s who gave it the name 'Liberty Bell,' adopting its inscription as a banner against slavery. The Liberty Bell Center is open daily from 9am to 5pm, admission is free - arrive before 10am to avoid the security line.

Installed since 1893 in the former hall of the Reading Railroad's train station, this covered market is one of the oldest continuously operating food markets in America. You walk past more than 80 stalls run by local producers, Pennsylvania Dutch butchers, Amish bakers, and restaurateurs from dozens of different backgrounds. Amish vendors - present only on Wednesdays and Fridays - sell pies, homemade pretzels, fresh cheese, and jams. The market is open daily from 8am to 6pm, free admission. Arrive at opening to grab a hot Philly cheesesteak sandwich before the lines grow.

The Philadelphia Museum of Art is one of the largest museums in America, with more than 240,000 works covering 5,000 years of art history. Its monumental staircase, made world-famous by the 1976 film Rocky, has become a pilgrimage site in its own right - a bronze statue of Sylvester Stallone stands at the foot of the steps. Founded in 1876 for the Centennial Exposition, the Greek marble building dominates the city from atop Fairmount Hill. Its Impressionist collection - featuring Cézanne, Renoir, Duchamp - is among the best outside Europe. The museum is open Wednesday to Monday from 10am to 5pm, closed Tuesday; admission: $25 adult, online booking recommended on weekends.

The Mütter Museum is one of the most fascinating and most disturbing medical museums in the United States - a collection founded in 1858 by Dr. Thomas Dent Mütter, pioneering reconstructive surgeon, to train medical students in anatomy and pathologies. You discover anatomical specimens preserved in jars, wax casts of medical anomalies, the megacolon of a man who died from chronic constipation weighing 62 pounds, thousands of skulls measured by physician-anthropologist Samuel Morton in the 19th century, and a histological slice of Einstein's brain. The museum is open daily 10am to 5pm, admission: $22 adult - booking strongly advised on weekends as admission is limited.

The 9th Street market, known as the Italian Market, is the oldest continuously operating outdoor market in the United States, established in the late 19th century by southern Pennsylvania Italian immigrants. Across six blocks of South Philly, fruit and vegetable stands, butchers, cheese shops, delicatessens, and bakeries succeed one another under metal awnings. The market was made world-famous by a scene in the 1976 film Rocky, where the boxer runs among the stalls at dawn. Today, Italian vendors neighbor Mexican, Vietnamese, and African groceries, faithfully reflecting the demographic evolution of South Philadelphia. Come in the morning for fresh produce.

The National Constitution Center, opened in 2003, is the only museum in the world devoted to the United States Constitution, signed three blocks away in 1787. Its interactive exhibitions trace the Founding Fathers' debates, the history of each amendment, and major American constitutional crises since 1791. Signers' Hall features life-size bronze statues of the 42 delegates who signed the Constitution, in their positions and with their supposed expressions at the moment of signing - you can photograph yourself among them. The permanent '1787' exhibition uses immersive technologies to recreate Constitutional Convention debates. Admission: $16 adult, open daily 9:30am to 5pm.

activities.text: The walk through Old City and Society Hill is the best way to understand what Philadelphia was before it became a metropolis. You stroll along Elfreth's Alley - 32 brick houses built between 1702 and 1836, designated a National Historic Landmark - then the cobbled streets of Society Hill, the residential quarter of 18th-century merchants and notables. Christ Church (1744) was the parish of George Washington and Benjamin Franklin. Go in late afternoon to catch the warm light on the brick facades and nearly deserted alleys.

Eastern State Penitentiary, opened in 1829, was the most famous - and most controversial - prison of the 19th-century America. Its architect, John Haviland, designed a seven-spoke radial plan allowing a single guard to monitor all corridors from a central point, a revolutionary concept at the time that influenced hundreds of prisons worldwide. Inmates were held in total isolation - a reformist method meant to encourage redemption. Al Capone was briefly incarcerated here in 1929; his cell has been reconstructed to show the relative comfort enjoyed by the mobster. Today partially in ruins, the prison offers audio-guided tours through cells overgrown with vegetation.

Rittenhouse Square, one of five original squares traced by William Penn on his 1682 plan, is the residential and cultural heart of bourgeois Philadelphia. The square is surrounded by some of the city's finest architectural facades - Second Empire buildings, brownstone mansions, and 1900s Beaux-Arts buildings. On weekdays, the square is the lunch spot for professionals from surrounding law firms and offices; on weekends, farmers markets and outdoor concerts animate it. The 20th Street art galleries concentrate some of Pennsylvania's best private galleries.

South Street has been Philadelphia's alternative artery since the 1960s, when rock and funk artists and musicians made it their reference neighborhood against urban renewal policies threatening the south side. Today, the street mixes vintage shops, outsider art galleries, world street food restaurants, bars, and clubs. The most singular attraction is Philadelphia's Magic Gardens (1020 South Street), a monumental mosaic created since 1994 by artist Isaiah Zagar on the facades, courtyards, and basements of a half-block, using tiles, mirrors, bottles, and found objects. Open Thursday-Monday 11am to 5pm, admission: $10.

days_plan.itin_title: Pennsylvania Loop: From Amish Country to the Appalachians - 11 Days
Pennsylvania Loop: From Amish Country to the Appalachians - 11 Days

map_keywords: Philadelphia Museum of Art Rocky Steps

visits.text: Start at the Desert Botanical Garden, set within Papago Park across 55 acres. More than 50,000 plants fill the grounds, including multi-century-old saguaros and rare species you won't find anywhere else. Plan at least two hours to explore the trails—go early in the morning, before the heat sets in.

The Heard Museum (2301 North Central Avenue) deserves a half-day on its own. Its collection of over 40,000 objects—Navajo and Zuni jewelry, Hopi kachina dolls, textiles—traces the history of Southwestern Native peoples with rare precision. Don't miss the dance performances held on select evenings.

Roosevelt Row, the downtown arts district, is the best place to grasp Phoenix's creative energy. Giant murals, independent galleries, and local designer boutiques line the neighborhood. If you're there on the first Friday of the month, the First Friday Art Walk transforms the district into a street festival running until 10 p.m.

If you have time before returning the car, Taliesin West in Scottsdale is worth the detour: it's the winter camp Frank Lloyd Wright built for himself starting in 1937 in the Sonoran Desert, and it remained his primary studio until his death in 1959. The 90-minute guided tour shows how Wright integrated architecture into the desert landscape—angles, local materials, and natural light serving the topography. It remains an active architecture school today.

Old Town Scottsdale, about twenty minutes from Phoenix, concentrates Western art galleries, turquoise shops, and a few blocks of early 20th-century adobe architecture. The district is pedestrian in the afternoon and pleasant to wander before heading back toward Phoenix.

activities.text: Late afternoon, climb Camelback Mountain via the Echo Canyon Trail—2 miles with 900 feet of elevation gain—for a panoramic view over the Phoenix basin before sunset. Bring water: the climb is short but steep.

In the evening, stay in Roosevelt Row for dinner. The neighborhood concentrates many of downtown's best restaurants, with cuisines ranging from Middle Eastern to Mexican influences—true to Arizona's melting pot.

The Japanese Friendship Garden (Ro Ho En) in downtown Phoenix is one of the city's most tranquil addresses: 3.5 acres of Japanese garden with koi ponds, waterfalls, and a tea house, designed in tribute to Phoenix's sister city, Himeji. A peaceful interlude before concluding the circuit.

days_plan.itin_title: Arizona Grand Loop: Grand Canyon and Desert Highways
Arizona Grand Loop: Grand Canyon and Desert Highways
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons

places.name: Pierre

visits.text: The South Dakota Cultural Heritage Center on Governors Drive is built into a hillside in the shape of a Lakota winter camp structure, and the architecture signals that this museum takes Indigenous history seriously. Exhibits run from 12,000-year-old Clovis points through the Lakota Nation to the Lewis and Clark passage in September 1804 — when the Corps had a tense armed standoff with Lakota warriors over upriver passage near present-day Fort Pierre. Out-of-state paid entry; South Dakota residents free. Plan 90 minutes.

Five miles north of Pierre on Route 1804 — literally named for the expedition — the Oahe Dam Visitor Center tells the story of the fourth-largest man-made reservoir in the United States. Construction ran from 1948 to 1962, when President Kennedy dedicated it; the dam displaced entire Lakota communities and submerged traditional homelands under 231 miles of reservoir stretching north to Bismarck. A small exhibit connects the dam back to Lewis and Clark's river passage. The center is free, open weekdays 9 to 4.

Farm Island Recreation Area, 4 miles east of Pierre on the Missouri, has the Lewis and Clark Family Center — hands-on interpretive site open Memorial Day to Labor Day where you can examine dug-out canoe replicas, animal hides, and walk terrain the expedition camped on. Across the river, a monument on the Fort Pierre levee marks the September 1804 confrontation with Chief Black Buffalo — an encounter that almost ended the expedition before it reached the Mandan villages.

activities.text: The South Dakota State Capitol grounds have the 1910 building at the center — 115 acres with an artesian-fed lake, the Fighting Stallions Memorial, and the Flaming Fountain veterans' memorial. The capitol interior has murals, tile work, and a rotunda that rewards a 30-minute self-guided walk. Everything on the grounds is free and accessible daily. Pierre is one of the least-visited state capitals in the country — the lack of crowds makes the building more accessible, not less interesting.

Lake Oahe stretches 231 miles from Pierre north to Bismarck, and the Missouri River access points near Pierre offer some of the best walleye and northern pike fishing in the Great Plains. Shore fishing from Oahe Downstream Recreation Area is free. The 26-mile Lewis and Clark Bicentennial Trail runs from Pierre along the river through rolling prairie and cottonwood groves — a gravel and dirt multi-use path where you're more likely to see white-tailed deer than another person.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: South Dakota Cultural Heritage Center Pierre

visits.text: Dollywood — Experience Dolly Parton's theme park celebrating Appalachian culture with rides, shows, and craft demonstrations. The 60-hectare park features world-class roller coasters, traditional music, and Southern food opened in 1986.

Roaring Fork Motor Nature Trail — Drive this 9-kilometer one-way loop through old-growth forest past log cabins and mill. The narrow gravel road follows mountain streams with opportunities for short hikes to waterfalls.
Grotto Falls — Hike 4.3 kilometers roundtrip to the only waterfall in the park allowing walking behind the 8-meter cascade. The trail passes through rhododendron thickets and hemlock forests.

activities.text: Moonshine tasting — Sample legal Tennessee moonshine at Ole Smoky Distillery or others lining Gatlinburg's main strip. The clear corn liquor once made illegally in mountain stills now drives tourism.
Smoky Mountain pancake breakfast — Fuel up with buttermilk pancakes, country ham, and red-eye gravy at Pancake Pantry or similar institutions. The hearty Southern breakfast tradition sustains hikers.
Pigeon Forge evening show — Attend dinner theater featuring country music, comedy, or magic. The resort town offers family entertainment rivaling Branson, Missouri.

days_plan.itin_title: Tennessee Music & Mountains Road Trip: Nashville to Great Smoky Mountains

places.name: Pinedale Wy

visits.text: Detour to Fremont Lake, 4 miles from Pinedale: the 7th deepest lake in the United States (696 feet) and 2nd largest natural lake in Wyoming. Its translucent waters reflect the granite ridges of the Wind River Range. The marina overlook offers a panorama of peaks exceeding 13,000 feet—a quick stop but a memorable one.

Take Skyline Drive from Pinedale: 14 miles to the Elkhart Park Trailhead at 9,020 feet. The overlook just before the terminus offers the best car-accessible panorama of the Wind River Range—Fremont Peak (13,745 feet) and seven other summits over 13,000 feet identified on an interpretive sign. In autumn, the aspens turn gold in September.

days_plan.itin_title: Colorado-Wyoming Road Trip: National Parks, Rockies & Wide Open Spaces

map_keywords: Skyline Drive Elkhart Park Pinedale Wyoming

visits.text: Pinnacle Mountain State Park - The Pyramid Rising from the Valley: This striking 1,011-foot cone-shaped summit dominates the horizon west of Little Rock, offering two distinct trails to its rocky apex. The West Summit Trail challenges hikers with boulder-scrambling adventure, while the East Summit provides a gentler ascent through 2,000 acres of pristine Arkansas wilderness.

activities.text: Sunset Summit Experience - Watch the River Valley Ignite in Golden Light: Time your ascent to reach the exposed rock summit as daylight fades, witnessing the Arkansas River Valley transform into a canvas of amber and crimson stretching to distant mountain ridges.

Arboretum & Nature Trails - 70 Acres of Botanical Discovery: Wander through diverse ecosystems from wetlands to hardwood forests, where interpretive trails reveal the flora that earned Arkansas its 'Natural State' designation.

days_plan.itin_title: Arkansas Itinerary: Hot Springs, Ozarks and Natural Parks

places.name: Pittsburgh

visits.text: The two Carnegie Museums of Pittsburgh occupy the same monumental building on Forbes Avenue, built starting in 1895 thanks to philanthropy from Andrew Carnegie, the Scottish immigrant who became Pittsburgh's steel magnate. The Museum of Art holds more than 34,000 works emphasizing contemporary art and photography - it's considered America's first contemporary art museum. The Museum of Natural History is famous for its dinosaur collection, including several specimens discovered during expeditions financed by Carnegie himself in the late 19th century. The minerals and gems hall, the Ancient Egypt section, and the public paleontology lab ('Paleolab') are among the most popular attractions. Buy the combo ticket online in advance; allow 3 to 4 hours for both museums.

The Strip District is Pittsburgh's liveliest and most authentic neighborhood, wedged between the Allegheny River and a railroad track. In the 19th century, it was the industrial quarter par excellence: Andrew Carnegie launched his first steel mills here, ALCOA began its commercial aluminum production here, and George Westinghouse built his first air brake workshops here. Since the 1990s, the Strip has converted into an international food and cultural market: Primanti Brothers (legendary sandwiches with fries included in the bread), Wholey's Fish Market, Pennsylvania Macaroni Company, and dozens of ethnic groceries line Smallman Street. The Heinz History Center, Pennsylvania's largest history museum, is also located here. Come on a Saturday morning for maximum atmosphere.

The Andy Warhol Museum is the largest American museum dedicated to a single artist - seven floors presenting the work of Andrew Warhola, born in Pittsburgh in 1928 to Slovak immigrant parents. The collection of 900 paintings, 77 sculptures, and 4,000 photographs covers Warhol's entire career: Campbell's soup screen prints, portraits of Marilyn Monroe and Mao Zedong, his experimental films, and multimedia installations. The museum also displays Warhol's personal archives - his notebooks, polaroids, notes - revealing the lesser-known side of this Pittsburgh native who remained attached to the city until the end. Open Wednesday-Sunday 10am to 5pm, admission: $20 adult.

The Heinz History Center is Pennsylvania's largest history museum and a Smithsonian Institution affiliate - a rare affiliation that guarantees collection quality and rigor. Installed since 1996 in a former refrigeration plant in the Strip District, on the very street where Andrew Carnegie started his first steel mills in the 1870s, the museum covers 250 years of regional history on six floors. The permanent exhibition traces the rise of the steel empire, the period of mass immigration (Pittsburgh welcomed successive waves of Irish, Poles, Italians, and African-Americans between 1870 and 1940), and inventions that came out of the city - radio, aluminum, polio vaccine. The section dedicated to Pittsburgh sports culture (Steelers, Pirates, Penguins) is equally remarkable. Admission: $18 adult.

The Phipps Conservatory, built in 1893 in the Oakland neighborhood, is one of the finest Victorian greenhouses in the United States, gifted to Pittsburgh by businessman Henry Phipps, Andrew Carnegie's associate. Its metal and glass architecture - 25,000 square feet of glazed surfaces - houses themed greenhouses of tropical plants, desert cacti, orchids, giant ferns, and carnivorous plants. Seasonal floral displays change several times per year and attract photographers from around the world. The conservatory is also one of the most sustainable museums in the United States, LEED Platinum certified for its environmental initiatives. Admission: $20 adult, open daily 9:30am to 5pm.

The Mattress Factory is a contemporary art museum installed in a former mattress factory in Pittsburgh's Mexican neighborhood - and it's one of the most original art museums in the United States. Since 1977, it has invited artists to create permanent or temporary installations in its industrial spaces, some of which have become worldwide references. Yayoi Kusama's 1996 permanent installation, 'Repetitive Vision,' is one of the first 'Infinity Rooms' the Japanese artist has created worldwide - and one of the few accessible to the public in a permanent museum context. The institution presents about ten immersive installations simultaneously in its three interconnected buildings. Admission: $25, open Wednesday-Sunday 10am-5pm.

activities.text: The Duquesne Incline is one of two funiculars still operating in Pittsburgh - cables pulling up the hillside that transported residents from the lower industrial quarter to Mount Washington's residential neighborhoods since 1877. From the summit, the panoramic view of the 'Confluence of the Three Rivers' - Allegheny, Monongahela, and Ohio - is Pittsburgh's most famous, and for good reason: it's precisely this strategic triangle of land that the French and English fought over in the 18th century, giving birth to Fort Duquesne then Fort Pitt. Go at sunset for the city lights coming on over the bridges.

Point State Park occupies the exact point where the Allegheny and Monongahela join to form the Ohio - one of the most strategic geographic points in all of pre-colonial North America. It was here that the French built Fort Duquesne in 1754, the English renamed it Fort Pitt in 1758, and this position became the pivot of control over the entire continental interior. The monumental fountain at the point's end, active May through October, propels water 130 feet high. The Fort Pitt Museum in the park displays models of the original fortifications and Seven Years' War artifacts.

Pittsburgh is home to one of Pennsylvania's most active craft brewing scenes, with more than 20 independent breweries throughout the city. Church Brew Works, installed since 1996 in Lawrenceville's Saint John the Baptist Church (1902), is probably the most visually impressive: shiny copper vats sit beneath Gothic vaults, barrels occupy the side naves. East End Brewing, in industrial warehouse mode in Pittsburgh's East End strip, is the local reference with its seasonal ales and lively outdoor courtyard. City Brew Tours organizes 3-hour guided minibus tours with tastings at several breweries - online booking required.

Pittsburgh has 446 bridges - more than any other city in the world according to urban planners - and the best way to appreciate them is from the water. Gateway Clipper Fleet operates sightseeing cruises from Point State Park that run along all three rivers' shores, pass under the city's symbolic yellow bridges, and offer Mount Washington panoramas from water level. The daytime cruise lasts two hours; Friday and Saturday night cruises include a musical performance.

days_plan.itin_title: Pennsylvania Loop: From Amish Country to the Appalachians - 11 Days
Pennsylvania Loop: From Amish Country to the Appalachians - 11 Days
Pittsburgh to Detroit: Steel, Rock & Motown - 9 Days Across the Great Lakes
Pittsburgh to Detroit: Steel, Rock & Motown - 9 Days Across the Great Lakes

map_keywords: Carnegie Museum of Natural History Oakland Pittsburgh
Andy Warhol Museum Pittsburgh North Shore
Point State Park Pittsburgh Three Rivers
Heinz History Center Strip District Pittsburgh

places.name: Porcupine Mountains

visits.text: Park the car at the end of the forest road and walk ten minutes to the overlook. Before you opens Lake of the Clouds: a mountain lake perched in the forest gap of the Porcupine Mountains, ringed by maples, beeches and birches. The state park preserves 15,000 hectares of old-growth forest unexploited since the pre-industrial era - one of the last stands of primary forest east of the Mississippi. In autumn, when the maples turn red, this panorama ranks among the Midwest's finest. Arrive early morning to catch the mist on the lake.

The Presque Isle River tumbles in a succession of waterfalls and rapids before pouring into Lake Superior - five named cascades in a few kilometers, connected by a 3 km loop trail with suspension bridges over the gorges. You cross Manabezho Falls, Manido Falls, Nawadaha Falls - Ojibwe names, like all the toponymy of this region. The water is tinted tannic brown by decomposing leaves, making the rapids resemble a Scottish river under stormy skies. Don't miss the suspension bridge at the mouth where the river meets the lake - the contrast is striking.

The trail descending from Lake of the Clouds overlook to the Big Carp River crosses 5 kilometers of old-growth forest without touching a road - trees over 300 years old, spongy ground of moss and ferns, total absence of noise except wind and birds. This is where you understand why the Porkies, as locals call them, are among the only true wilderness spaces in the eastern United States. Black bears frequent the park - rangers recommend making noise while hiking.

activities.text: Summit Peak is the highest point in the Porcupine Mountains and the highest summit in Michigan east of the Keweenaw Peninsula. The observation platform at the summit gives a 360° panorama: old-growth forest in every direction, Lake Superior to the north like a sea without horizon, and if the sky is clear, you can make out Wisconsin in the distance. Northern lights are observable from this point about ten nights a year in autumn and winter - the Upper Peninsula is at the edge of the magnetic zone where they become frequent.

The Porcupine Mountains park preserves 15,000 hectares of old-growth forest unexploited since the pre-industrial era - one of the last stands of primary forest east of the Mississippi. The maples, beeches and basswoods reach diameters impossible to encircle with your arms. The Union Spring Trail leads 3 km to a natural mineral spring that's been gushing from a rock fissure for millennia. Leave early morning to hear woodpeckers and owls before the sun rises.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Lakes Grand Loop: Chicago, Milwaukee, Door County, Sleeping Bear, Mackinac, Pictured Rocks - 12 Days

map_keywords: Porcupine Mountains Wilderness State Park Michigan
Lake of the Clouds Porcupine Mountains
Presque Isle River falls Upper Peninsula

places.name: Port Alsworth

visits.text: Port Alsworth is a hamlet of about fifty residents perched on the shore of Lake Clark—it's the only inhabited center in the national park and the logical starting point for exploring the surrounding 4 million acres. The NPS Visitor Center is here, with rangers available to orient your hikes. Stop in as soon as you arrive: terrain conditions change quickly and rangers know which areas have active bears.

Tanalian Falls—a 30-foot cascade tumbling over ancient lava flows through birch forest—is accessible via a 4-mile round-trip trail from Port Alsworth. The path cuts through birch groves that turn golden in autumn and dense conifer forests in summer. Lake Kontrashibuna on the return loop offers perfect reflections of the Chigmit Range on calm days.

Lake Clark stretches 42 miles against a backdrop of snow-capped peaks and active volcanoes—Mount Redoubt (10,197 ft) and Mount Iliamna (10,016 ft) dominate the southern horizon. The park was designated in 1980 in part to protect the salmon spawning grounds that constitute one of the most important sources in the Bristol Bay basin. From the shore at Port Alsworth, the reflections of the Chigmit Range in the turquoise water are worth the flight alone.

activities.text: The coastal areas of Silver Salmon Creek and Chinitna Bay, accessible by floatplane from Port Alsworth, are among Alaska's best spots for observing coastal brown bears. In spring and summer, bears dig in the mud for clams or graze on salt marsh grasses. Local operators from Port Alsworth run day trips—expect 3 to 5 hours on-site.

Kayaking on Lake Clark from Port Alsworth—rent on-site or through lodges. Waters are usually calm in the morning, allowing you to approach wild shorelines where moose and bald eagles are common. For anglers, arctic char, rainbow trout, and arctic grayling abound in the Tanalian River, 200 yards from the lodge.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Adventure - 14 Days Through the World's Most Remote National Parks

map_keywords: Lake Clark National Park Port Alsworth
Lake Clark Visitor Center Port Alsworth

visits.text: Olympic National Park — Hurricane Ridge — Drive the scenic mountain road climbing to 1,631 meters with panoramic views across the Olympic Mountains and Strait of Juan de Fuca. Wildflower meadows bloom July through August while black-tailed deer graze alpine slopes.

Hoh Rainforest — Walk among towering Sitka spruce and western hemlock draped in club moss in one of the contiguous United States' only temperate rainforests. The Hall of Mosses Trail reveals an otherworldly landscape receiving 3.6 meters of annual rainfall.

Ruby Beach — Descend to this dramatic Pacific coastline where sea stacks rise from the surf and driftwood logs litter the wide sandy beach. The Olympic coastal strip preserves 73 miles of wilderness shoreline accessible only by foot.

activities.text: Sol Duc Hot Springs — Soak in natural mineral pools ranging from 99-104°F surrounded by old-growth forest. The three developed pools fed by underground springs offer relaxation after hiking Olympic trails.

Lake Crescent scenic drive — Navigate the winding highway along this glacially-carved fjord-like lake reaching 190 meters deep. The crystalline blue waters reflect surrounding peaks and forests in this pristine alpine setting.

Port Angeles waterfront dinner — Sample fresh halibut and clam chowder with views across the Strait of Juan de Fuca to Vancouver Island's mountains. The working port town serves as gateway to Olympic National Park.

days_plan.itin_title: Washington State's I-90 Corridor: Seattle, Snoqualmie Pass & Alpine Lakes

places.name: Port St Joe

visits.text: Dr. Beach ranked it number one in the United States in 2002. Under the rules of that ranking, the distinction cannot be awarded twice - so St. Joseph Peninsula quietly dropped off the list and stayed crowd-free. Ten miles of quartz-white sand, dunes among the tallest east of the Mississippi, no high-rises. The 2,716-acre park at Cape San Blas also protects 240+ bird species, three nesting sea turtle species, and the endangered St. Andrews beach mouse. Hurricane Michael cut the peninsula in two in 2018; a bridge reconnects the halves now. Book cabins months ahead - spring weekends sell out in January.

The bay side of St. Joseph Peninsula is a different beach from the Gulf side - calm, shallow, protected, the water over seagrass beds so clear it looks unreal. The 45-minute Maritime Hammock Trail connects the campground to a sheltered cove where bay scallops feed in the grass at your feet. In late summer and early fall, local boaters anchor here specifically for scalloping season. Eagle Harbor concession rents kayaks and canoes seasonally. Low tide gives the best underwater visibility.

Port St. Joe's Constitution Convention Museum State Park stands on the site where Florida's first constitution was drafted in 1838, seven years before statehood. It's one of only two original museum buildings erected by the Florida Park Service - the other is the Gorrie Museum you saw yesterday in Apalachicola. A small stop that threads a coherent line through the political history you started at the Capitol in Tallahassee. Small admission fee; thirty minutes is enough.

activities.text: Nine miles of paved path run the length of Cape San Blas, connecting the state park at one end to the Cape San Blas village at the other. At certain points the peninsula is barely 200 yards wide - you look left at the Gulf of America, right at St. Joseph Bay, and see two different blues simultaneously. Bikes rent at Scallop Cove near Eagle Harbor. Go in the morning before the heat.

Between May and October, loggerhead and green sea turtles haul up onto this Gulf-side beach to nest. Rangers mark every nest and monitor every emergence. The rule is absolute: no artificial lights on the beach at night - flashlights, phone screens - because the glow disorients nesting females and hatchlings making their run to the sea. Check the park website for ranger-led turtle watches on select summer evenings. They fill quickly.

days_plan.itin_title: Florida Panhandle: Tallahassee to Pensacola on the Emerald Coast

map_keywords: St Joseph Peninsula State Park Cape San Blas
Constitution Convention Museum Port St Joe

visits.text: The Old Port's cobblestone streets run down to the water, and the smell of low tide and lobster rolls hits you before you reach Commercial Street. This district - a working waterfront flanked by 19th-century brick buildings on the National Register of Historic Places - burned to the ground in 1866 and rebuilt almost overnight. Today Fore, Middle, and Exchange Streets pack in fishmongers, breweries, and galleries with barely a chain in sight. If you're staying the night, Tommy's Park at Middle and Exchange has the city's visitor information booth.

Founded in 1882, the Portland Museum of Art holds the largest collection of Winslow Homer's work in New England - Homer spent the last 27 years of his life at Prouts Neck, just up the coast, and the museum owns 17 of his paintings. The main building was designed in 1983 by I.M. Pei, a striking granite cube that bridges the 1801 McLellan House. The Payson Collection on the upper floor pulls in Renoir, Degas, and Monet, making this Maine's most substantial European collection. Closed Mondays and Tuesdays; plan 1.5 to 2 hours.

Drive 15 minutes south to Fort Williams Park in Cape Elizabeth and you're standing at the base of Portland Head Light, commissioned by George Washington in 1787 and lit for the first time in January 1791. It's the oldest lighthouse in Maine, and one of only four colonial lighthouses still standing with their original towers. Edward Hopper painted it in 1927; that watercolor is now at the Boston Museum of Fine Arts. The tower itself opens to the public only once a year (Maine Open Lighthouse Day in September), but the grounds and the museum in the former keeper's quarters are open spring through fall.

Victoria Mansion on Danforth Street is the finest surviving example of Italian villa-style architecture in the United States - built between 1858 and 1860 for hotelier Ruggles Sylvester Morse, and untouched since. Original frescoed ceilings, stenciled walls, carved woodwork, and Gustave Herter-designed furniture give you a complete snapshot of high Victorian taste. Even the draperies are original. Tours run about 45 minutes and cover details no placard can convey.

Portland Observatory on Munjoy Hill is the only surviving maritime signal tower in the United States, built in 1807 by Lemuel Moody to alert merchants to incoming ships. From the octagonal cupola - 86 steps up - the view sweeps across Casco Bay, the harbor islands, the White Mountains on a clear day, and the full Portland peninsula below. The tower is engineered from a single continuous piece of interlocking timber.

The Eastern Promenade on Munjoy Hill is Portland's great waterfront park - 78 acres of lawn, trails, and shoreline overlooking Casco Bay. The paved trail runs 2.5 miles from the Old Port along the waterfront to Back Cove, with benches facing east toward the ocean. This is where Portland comes to watch the sun rise over the harbor islands and the lobster boats go out. In summer the park hosts free Concerts at the Promenade.

activities.text: Portland runs on lobster and craft beer, and the Old Port puts both in reach of each other. Eventide Oyster Co. on Middle Street helped put Portland on the national food map - their brown butter lobster roll, served on a Chinese bao bun, has been called the best in New England. Around the corner, Fore Street restaurant has been sourcing from Maine farms since 1996 and wood-fires almost everything. Book well in advance for both, especially July and August.

Casco Bay Lines runs ferry service from the Old Port to the cluster of islands in the bay - Peaks Island is the closest, 20 minutes out, with rentable bikes and a small civil war history museum. The Great Chebeague Ferry takes about 45 minutes and deposits you on an island with no cars and a tiny general store. Check the schedule before you go: sailings reduce significantly in shoulder season.

Portland's food scene runs well past lobster rolls, though those are still mandatory. The Public Market House on Monument Square has local vendors selling Maine cheeses, charcuterie, and bread. A few blocks south, the Congress Street Arts District has the best concentration of independent restaurants: from Drifters Wife (natural wine bar and small plates) to Eventide for brown-butter lobster. For a final-night dinner, Fore Street's wood-fired menu has been setting the standard for Maine farm-to-table since chef Sam Hayward opened it in 1996.

Portland's craft brewery scene is worth an afternoon of its own. Allagash Brewing on Industrial Way, one of the breweries that put American craft beer on the map with its Belgian-style White Ale, offers free tours Friday through Sunday. Bissell Brothers on Thompson's Point fills a converted industrial space and keeps roughly 15 beers on tap. Both are a short drive from downtown.

days_plan.itin_title: Oregon Natural Wonders: Crater Lake to Pacific Coast Road Trip
Oregon Natural Wonders: Crater Lake to Pacific Coast Road Trip
Inland Maine: Moosehead Lake, Mount Katahdin & the North Woods
Inland Maine: Moosehead Lake, Mount Katahdin & the North Woods
Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England
Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England

visits.text: Strawbery Banke is unlike anything else: it's the only outdoor museum in the United States preserving the evolution of a single neighborhood over more than 350 years—from colonial New Hampshire through the 1950s. Across 10 acres, more than 37 restored buildings transport you through Colonial, Georgian, and Federal styles. The Pitt Tavern, built in 1766, hosted George Washington and the Marquis de Lafayette. Open May through October.

activities.text: Market Square and the lanes of downtown Portsmouth deserve an hour of wandering before you get back on the road. Settle onto a terrace: it's a lively college town with a remarkable concentration of bars and restaurants for its size. Seafood dinner here, then departure toward Portland tomorrow morning.

days_plan.itin_title: Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England

visits.text: Marvel at Providence Canyon, Georgia's 'Little Grand Canyon' where vivid walls of red, orange, pink, and white sandstone rise dramatically above wildflower-lined trails and pine forests. Poor farming practices in the 1800s triggered erosion that carved these spectacular formations, transforming agricultural failure into geological wonder. The Canyon Loop Trail winds through the colorful gorges offering photographers extraordinary compositions.

Discover rusted vintage automobiles from the 1950s along the quirky White Blaze Trail, abandoned relics slowly being reclaimed by the surrounding forest. Backcountry camping sites allow overnight visitors to experience canyon walls glowing golden at sunrise and sunset. The unexpected southwestern landscape feels transported from Arizona to rural Georgia.

activities.text: Hike through pine-scented woods to remote backcountry campsites where crackling campfires beneath starry skies create unforgettable wilderness experiences. The park's dramatic terrain changes character throughout the day as shifting sunlight paints the multicolored canyon walls in ever-changing hues.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

places.name: Providence Ri

visits.text: Benefit Street, nicknamed the "Mile of History," displays one of the greatest concentrations of Colonial and Victorian houses in the United States on a single stretch. Walk it slowly: the First Baptist Church in America (1775) is the third church built by the congregation founded in 1636 by Roger Williams, Providence's founder. Brown University, founded in 1764, was the first American college to admit students regardless of religious affiliation.

The RISD Museum deserves more than a quick pass—devote at least an hour to it. Its 100,000 works span antiquity to the present in a building adjoining the campus of the nation's most prestigious design school. Asian ceramics, French Impressionism, American industrial design: the quality far exceeds what you'd expect from a city this size.

Before departing for the Cape, climb to Prospect Terrace Park for the best view of Providence: a statue of Roger Williams contemplates the city from College Hill. Five minutes, no more—then hit the road.

activities.text: Don't miss WaterFire if the dates align: at sunset, more than 80 braziers ignite in the middle of the Woonasquatucket, Moshassuck, and Providence rivers, carried by silent torchbearers on boats. Created by Barnaby Evans (Brown, class of 1975) in the 1990s, the event attracts nearly a million visitors per year. Calendar of "full lightings" on waterfire.org—May through November.

End the evening on Federal Hill, a 10-minute walk from downtown: pasta restaurants, delis, and cafés under string lights await you in Providence's Italian neighborhood. The arch at the entrance to DePasquale Square bears the pigna, a pinecone symbol of welcome from Naples.

days_plan.itin_title: Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England
Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England

map_keywords: First Baptist Church in America Providence
Prospect Terrace Park Providence Rhode Island

visits.text: The North Carolina State Capitol in downtown Raleigh deserves a quick stop on your route to Charlotte. This gray granite building in neoclassical style completed in 1840 houses, in its rotunda, a copy of the Washington statue by Houdon. Raleigh was founded in 1792 specifically as the state capital, on neutral ground chosen for its central location in North Carolina.

Fayetteville Street, a one-kilometer pedestrian axis between the Capitol and the Convention Center, is the backbone of Uptown Raleigh. Have lunch here before hitting the road: The Pit serves the best vinegar-based North Carolina barbecue, Beasley's Chicken + Honey is the best fried chicken table in the Southeast.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltimore to the Outer Banks - Historic America from Chesapeake Bay to Charlotte

places.name: Rangeley

visits.text: Pull over at Height of Land on Route 17 and the view opens without warning: Mooselookmeguntic Lake and Upper Richardson Lake spread far below, hemmed in by forested ridgelines stretching west to the White Mountains on the New Hampshire border. This is the highest point on any numbered state highway in Maine. The Appalachian Trail crosses right here - on summer days you'll find thru-hikers at the parking area, packs enormous, heading north. Stone seating and interpretive panels make it a proper stop, not just a windshield view.

Off Dodge Pond Road, the Wilhelm Reich Museum sits on the hilltop property called Orgonon, where Austrian psychiatrist Wilhelm Reich studied what he called 'orgone energy' - a universal life force he believed permeated all living matter and the atmosphere. The FDA seized and destroyed his books and equipment in 1956; he died in federal prison in 1957. The 1949 stone observatory and his second-floor study, library, and laboratory look exactly as he left them - original mid-century furniture and scientific apparatus. Trails lace the largely forested 175-acre property, with views over Rangeley Lake.

The Outdoor Heritage Museum near Oquossoc preserves the Rangeley Lakes region's sporting history: a recreated 1900 log sporting camp, displays on world-record brook trout (10-12 lbs.) landed here in the 1880s, the famed Rangeley boat (a locally designed rowing craft), and a gallery dedicated to fly-tier Carrie Stevens, whose streamer flies in the 1920s spread Rangeley's fishing fame across New England. Free exhibits on the front lawn invite a stop even when the museum is closed.

activities.text: Saddleback Mountain's summit sits at 3,985 feet, reached via the Appalachian Trail in about 4 miles from the trailhead on Saddleback Mountain Road - not the ski area, a different GPS entry. Above treeline for the last mile, the open ridge walk between the main summit and The Horn is one of the finest in western Maine. Budget 5-7 hours round trip. The ski area at the base reopened in 2020 after a long closure.

Rangeley Lake at 1,516 feet of elevation, surrounded entirely by forest, rewards a morning on the water. Canoe and kayak rentals from Ecopelagicon Nature Store on Pond Street put you on Haley Pond with direct paddling access to the main lake. Loons are common; bald eagles occasionally circle the western shore. The town beach on Main Street has a free boat launch.

days_plan.itin_title: Inland Maine: Moosehead Lake, Mount Katahdin & the North Woods

map_keywords: Height of Land Route 17 Rangeley Maine

places.name: Rapid

visits.text: Drop your bags, then head straight to the Museum of Geology on the South Dakota School of Mines campus—and don't let the university address put you off. The Cretaceous and Oligocene fossil collections here rank among the most comprehensive in the country, and admission is completely free. Plan for 90 minutes, and you'll leave with a genuine understanding of what you're about to see in the Badlands tomorrow.

Seven painted concrete dinosaurs watch over you from Skyline Drive—built in 1936 as part of a WPA public works program, proudly kitsch and beloved by locals. Walk up from the parking area: the view across the entire city with the Black Hills as backdrop beats most paid overlooks in the region. 30 minutes total.

Head downtown for the "City of Presidents": since 2000, a life-sized bronze statue of every American president occupies a street corner along Main Street. Some stand, others sit, a few look like they're hailing a cab. Download the map from the city website and turn it into a game—which one will you find first?

Before leaving, step inside Prairie Edge Trading Company at the corner of Main Street and 6th. Pendleton blankets, handcrafted turquoise jewelry, moccasins, Lakota drums—this is one of the finest collections of contemporary Native American art in the United States. Come to browse and understand, not necessarily to buy, though you'll rarely leave empty-handed.

Before returning the car, detour to the Chapel in the Hills, in a wooded neighborhood on the outskirts. It's an exact replica of Norway's 12th-century Borgund Stave Church, built in 1969 by a Lutheran pastor struck by the connections between Dakota's Scandinavian immigrants and their homeland. Free entry. The adjacent museum on Dakota's Scandinavian heritage is worth 30 minutes.

If you have time before your flight, the Journey Museum & Learning Center is a good synthesis of everything you've just traveled through: Black Hills geology, 10,000 years of Lakota culture, gold rush, pioneers. Nothing you haven't already seen in the field, but contextualized clearly. Plan for 90 minutes.

activities.text: Rapid City has a solid craft beer scene for a city of 80,000. Hay Camp Brewing is known for seriously funky sours; Firehouse Brewing, the oldest in South Dakota, operates from a converted fire station—go for the architecture as much as the pint. Expect under $8 per glass, and take time to chat with locals.

Finish the evening at Main Street Square, the pedestrian heart of downtown. In summer, free outdoor concerts happen several nights a week—check the schedule on the city website. Choose a restaurant on the surrounding streets and eat outside if weather permits: you've got a big day ahead tomorrow.

Take time for a real breakfast downtown before the airport—several coffee shops and brunch spots open early around Main Street Square. Do one last walk among the presidential statues. It's the kind of morning you'll remember for a long time.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Hills & Badlands: Road Trip Through the Heart of the American West
Black Hills & Badlands: Road Trip Through the Heart of the American West

map_keywords: South Dakota School of Mines Rapid City
Prairie Edge Trading Company Rapid City
Chapel in the Hills Rapid City

visits.text: Pull off I-80 in Rawlins for the Wyoming Frontier Prison - Wyoming's first state penitentiary, 80 years in operation from 1901 to 1981, roughly 13,500 inmates including Big Nose George. Guided tours run through the dungeon, solitary confinement cells, and the gas chamber where 14 executions took place. One-hour tours run year-round. The building is on the National Register of Historic Places.

Step into the Carbon County Museum a few blocks away - free admission, 30,000 artifacts, and the only place in the world where you can see shoes custom-made from the skin of outlaw Big Nose George Parrot after his 1881 hanging. Exhibits also cover Thomas Edison's visit to the Rawlins area, where he reportedly found inspiration for his lightbulb filament while fishing, and Wyoming women's history. Allow 30-45 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Wyoming Beyond Yellowstone: Cowboys, Hot Springs & Bighorn Medicine Wheel

visits.text: Redding - California's Sunniest Secret with 300+ Days of Brilliance: This underrated gateway to Northern California's wilderness basks in more sunny days than almost anywhere in the state. The striking Sundial Bridge, a gleaming white cable-stayed span designed by Santiago Calatrava, serves as the world's largest working sundial.

Shasta Dam - The Second-Largest Concrete Dam in America: Rising 602 feet above the Sacramento River, this engineering marvel created the spectacular Shasta Lake with its 365 miles of shoreline. Free tours descend into the dam's interior.

activities.text: McArthur-Burney Falls Memorial State Park - 'The Eighth Wonder of the World': President Theodore Roosevelt's famous description still holds true. Watch 100 million gallons of crystal-clear water cascade 129 feet daily, emerging from underground springs that keep the falls flowing year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Redwood National Park - Where Earth's Tallest Living Giants Touch the Sky: Stand humbled beneath trees that were ancient when Rome was young. These coastal redwoods soar over 350 feet, creating cathedral-like groves where filtered sunlight streams through canopies that have witnessed millennia. A UNESCO World Heritage Site protecting some of the most magnificent forests remaining on Earth.

Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park - Drive Through a Living Tunnel of Green: Navigate the legendary Howland Hill Road as it winds through primeval old-growth forest so dense the canopy forms a continuous emerald ceiling. Some trees here sprouted before the pyramids were built.

Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park - Where Roosevelt Elk Roam Free: Encounter herds of majestic elk grazing in meadows surrounded by towering redwoods. Fern Canyon, with its 50-foot walls draped in seven species of ferns, served as a filming location for Jurassic Park.

activities.text: Avenue of the Giants - 31 Miles of Jaw-Dropping Wonder: This legendary scenic drive threads through the largest remaining contiguous old-growth redwood forest in the world. Multiple pullouts invite you to step out and crane your neck skyward in disbelief.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Hoover Dam — Tour this engineering marvel completed in 1936 creating Lake Mead, America's largest reservoir by volume. The 221-meter concrete arch-gravity dam generates 4 billion kilowatt-hours annually while taming the Colorado River.

Lake Mead National Recreation Area — Boat or drive along this 177-kilometer reservoir backed by dramatic desert mountains. The lake created by Hoover Dam offers recreation amid landscapes where ancient civilizations left petroglyphs.

Extraterrestrial Highway — Navigate State Route 375 through remote desert passing the classified Area 51 military base. The 158-kilometer stretch features alien-themed attractions and conspiracy theories about UFO sightings.

National Automobile Museum — Discover 200 vintage cars including Elvis's 1973 Cadillac and John Wayne's Corvette in this world-class collection. The museum showcases automotive history from horseless carriages to modern supercars.

Nevada Museum of Art — Explore contemporary art with focus on landscapes and environment in this architectural landmark. The museum houses the Center for Art + Environment documenting land art and environmental themes.
Truckee Riverwalk — Stroll along this urban trail following the river through downtown past shops, galleries, and public art. The revitalized corridor connects parks and cultural attractions.
Fleischmann Planetarium — Learn about astronomy and space exploration at University of Nevada's planetarium. The facility offers star shows and exhibits explaining the cosmos.

activities.text: Desert scenic drive — Cross Nevada's vast basin and range topography under endless skies. The journey reveals the state's emptiness with occasional ghost towns and roadside diners.
Reno Arch photo — Snap pictures beneath the famous arch proclaiming The Biggest Little City in the World. The neon gateway has welcomed visitors to downtown Reno since 1926.

Casino dinner and gaming — Try your luck at Reno's casinos offering more relaxed atmosphere than Las Vegas. The original Nevada gaming destination maintains old-school charm with affordable table limits.
Downtown Reno revival — Explore the revitalized downtown featuring murals, breweries, and the RiverWalk District. The city reinvented itself beyond gaming with arts and outdoor recreation focus.

Basque cuisine dinner — Savor family-style Basque meals at restaurants preserving sheepherder traditions. Reno's Basque community maintains culinary heritage with multi-course dinners served communally.

days_plan.itin_title: Nevada's Great Contrasts: Las Vegas Neon to Red Rock Desert Silence
Nevada's Great Contrasts: Las Vegas Neon to Red Rock Desert Silence

places.name: Richmond Va

visits.text: Few American buildings have played as many roles—and you can walk through them all in a single visit to the Virginia State Capitol. Designed by Thomas Jefferson after the Roman temple of the Maison Carrée in Nîmes, it housed the Confederate Congress from 1861 to 1865. Don't miss the rotunda: the marble statue of Washington by Houdon—commissioned in 1784 and considered the most faithful portrait of the general—deserves a few minutes on its own. Free admission.

Spend at least two hours at the American Civil War Museum at Tredegar Iron Works. The Tredegar foundry produced approximately 1,100 cannons for the Confederacy—nearly half of its domestic artillery production. The museum tells the war from three perspectives: Confederate, Union, and freed slaves. The 19th-century brick warehouses are beautifully restored.

The White House of the Confederacy on East Clay Street survived the war intact when the rest of Richmond burned—visit it to understand just how comfortably the Confederate government lived while its soldiers died. Jefferson Davis resided here as president from 1861 to 1865. Lincoln himself toured it on April 4 and 5, 1865, two days after the city fell. Guided tour of 45 minutes.

Take time to wander Hollywood Cemetery, on the heights overlooking the James River. Jefferson Davis, James Monroe, John Tyler, and 18,000 Confederate soldiers rest here—the latter grouped around a granite pyramid erected in 1869. The views over the river rapids are among Richmond's finest. Open from sunrise to sunset.

Shockoe Bottom, a stone's throw from downtown, is the place many visitors pass without stopping—wrongly. It was the second-largest slave market in the United States between 1830 and 1865: more than 350,000 people were bought and sold here. The Richmond Slave Trail connects the major sites over 3 miles along the canal.

activities.text: Don't leave Richmond without walking the Canal Walk: 1.25 miles along the James River and Kanawha Canal, with 19th-century locks and panels telling the city's industrial and slavery history. In the evening, Shockoe Slip is packed with restaurants in former warehouses.

If you're here on a Saturday between May and November, the South of the James market gathers the best local producers. Otherwise, hit a seafood bar in Shockoe Bottom for Chesapeake Bay oysters—mignonette sauce or straight up—before hitting the road.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltimore to the Outer Banks - Historic America from Chesapeake Bay to Charlotte

map_keywords: White House of the Confederacy Richmond

visits.text: Seneca Falls Women's Rights National Park — Visit the birthplace of the American women's rights movement where the first Women's Rights Convention convened in 1848. The Wesleyan Chapel and Declaration Park commemorate Elizabeth Cady Stanton, Susan B. Anthony, and 100 signers.

George Eastman Museum — Explore the world's oldest museum dedicated to photography and film in Kodak founder George Eastman's 1905 Colonial Revival mansion. The collection spans 400,000 photographs and 28,000 films documenting the evolution of visual media.

High Falls — Stand at the edge of this 29-meter waterfall on the Genesee River that powered Rochester's 19th-century flour mills and breweries. The gorge's Silurian dolostone layers reveal 400 million years of geological history.

Strong National Museum of Play — Discover the world's only collections-based museum devoted solely to play, housing the National Toy Hall of Fame. Interactive exhibits celebrate video games, dolls, and classic toys across 14,000 square meters.

activities.text: Public Market browsing — Sample local produce, artisan breads, and ethnic specialties at this indoor-outdoor market operating since 1905. Over 300 vendors gather on Saturdays offering everything from hydroponic greens to Nicaraguan pupusas.

Genesee Riverway Trail cycling — Pedal along this 39-kilometer paved trail following the Genesee River through parks, wetlands, and historic districts. The route passes three waterfalls and connects downtown Rochester to Lake Ontario.

Charlotte Beach sunset — Relax on this sandy Lake Ontario beach where the Genesee River meets the Great Lake. The historic 1905 Dentzel Carousel and pier offer family entertainment as the sun sets over Canadian waters.

days_plan.itin_title: Empire State Road Trip: From Manhattan Skyscrapers to Niagara Falls

places.name: Rock Harbor Isle Royale

visits.text: Isle Royale receives around 25,000 visitors a year—compared to 3 million for the Grand Canyon. Accessible only by boat or seaplane, the 45-mile-long island is home to a wolf and moose population that's been continuously studied since 1958: the longest predator-prey study in the world. Start at the Rock Harbor Visitor Center to orient your days on the island.

The Rock Harbor Lighthouse, built in 1855 from red sandstone, is the oldest on the island. It now houses exhibits on the maritime history of Lake Superior—nine wrecks lie in the waters around the park. The NPS guided tour includes the adjacent Edisen Fishery, a family operation restored to its 1940s state.

Scoville Point is 2 miles from Rock Harbor via the Stoll Memorial Trail, under a canopy of spruce. The rocky point overlooks Lake Superior—on clear days, you'll spot freighters on the horizon and sometimes the Canadian shoreline. Leave at 5:30 a.m. for sunrise over the lake; you'll have it to yourself.

The Greenstone Ridge Trail is the island's backbone: 40 miles from east to west, from Rock Harbor to Windigo, through boreal forests and exposed ridges. At minimum, hike the segment to Mount Franklin—4 miles from Rock Harbor, 1,000 feet of elevation gain—for views of Canada and Lake Superior's north shore. Waterproof boots essential.

Suzy's Cave is a sea arch carved into rock by waves from a prehistoric Lake Superior that was higher than today's level. It's a 1.5-mile walk from Rock Harbor. Water now disappears into a crevice plunging into the island's interior—the geology is as striking as the visual spectacle.

activities.text: Rent a canoe or kayak at the Rock Harbor Lodge dock to paddle in Tobin Harbor, the sheltered arm of the lake just north of Rock Harbor. Motorboats aren't allowed here—loons, herons, mergansers, and boreal vegetation spilling into the water. The silence is almost unsettling compared to the mainland.

Attend the evening ranger program at Rock Harbor: wolves, moose, Ojibwe history, or Canadian Shield geology depending on the schedule. The rangers live on the island for the entire season and know every corner of the park. Free, no reservation needed.

Take the MV Sandy from Rock Harbor Lodge to Raspberry Island for a ranger-guided walk through the spruce bog. The island is only accessible by private boat or this NPS tour. A boardwalk crosses an ecosystem of sphagnum moss and stunted spruce—fragile, silent, mesmerizing. The tour lasts 2.5 hours total.

Fishing for lake trout and salmon is excellent in the cold waters around Isle Royale. Rock Harbor Lodge organizes half-day charter fishing trips with a captain—all gear provided, license included. The Lighthouse Restaurant at the lodge will cook your evening catch if you ask. Book in advance.

days_plan.itin_title: Minneapolis, Voyageurs, Isle Royale & the Upper Peninsula
Minneapolis, Voyageurs, Isle Royale & the Upper Peninsula

map_keywords: Rock Harbor Visitor Center Isle Royale

visits.text: You wander through 12 acres of koi-filled ponds, cascading waterfalls, winding gravel paths, and meticulously trained Japanese maples along Spring Creek. Every rock placement, tree position, and water feature was designed by master craftsman Hoichi Kurisu, who has been involved with the gardens since 1978. The layout is dense enough to fill 90 minutes without retracing your steps. The on-site Fresco at the Gardens cafe serves locally sourced Asian-inspired lunch. Admission charged.

activities.text: Downtown Rockford's Burpee Museum houses Jane, one of the most complete juvenile Tyrannosaurus rex fossils ever discovered, unearthed in South Dakota in 2001. The permanent dinosaur gallery built around Jane has won national recognition for its interpretive approach to fossil science. The museum sits a 10-minute drive from Anderson Gardens. Admission charged.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa Backroads: From the Missouri Bluffs to the Mississippi Cliffs

places.name: Rockport Tx

visits.text: Aransas National Wildlife Refuge, established in 1937 on 115,000 acres of coast and marshland, is the only wintering site for the last remaining wild population of whooping cranes in the world. The species had fallen to just 15 individuals in the 1940s - the 2024-2025 season count reached a historic 557 cranes. These birds stand five feet tall, have a 7.5-foot wingspan, and migrate each fall from Wood Buffalo National Park in Canada, over 2,500 miles. The best way to see them is from the refuge's auto tour route or by boat tour from Rockport, between November and March. Outside this period, you can still observe over 400 bird species in the refuge.

Fulton Mansion, built in 1877 by Colonel George Fulton, was ahead of its time: running water, hot-air central heating and ventilation in every room - all rarities in Texas at the time. The Second Empire-style house, with its mansard roofs and corner tower, has survived several major hurricanes. If you're passionate about 19th-century architecture, the guided tour is truly worthwhile - it tells how the Fulton family built their fortune in the Texas cattle industry.

The Big Tree at Goose Island State Park is a live oak (Quercus virginiana) estimated to be over 1,000 years old with a trunk circumference of roughly 35 feet. It's one of the most massive trees in Texas. Push on to the park - it's a quick 20-minute stop that leaves a lasting impression.

activities.text: Operators offer kayak outings in Aransas Bay, among the seagrass beds and sandbars. The waters are calm and shallow, ideal for observing dolphins and brown pelicans at very close range. From November through March, boat tours allow you to skirt the refuge's east shore to watch whooping cranes feeding in the salt marshes less than 150 feet away.

days_plan.itin_title: Gulf Coast: Lafayette to South Padre Island - 9 days

map_keywords: Aransas National Wildlife Refuge whooping cranes
Goose Island State Park Big Tree

places.name: Roswell

visits.text: Embrace the extraterrestrial atmosphere of Roswell, world-famous for the alleged 1947 UFO incident that transformed this agricultural town into a mecca for alien enthusiasts and curious travelers. The International UFO Museum and Research Center presents exhibits examining the incident, government investigations, and broader questions about extraterrestrial life. The entire downtown district celebrates its otherworldly reputation with themed attractions and memorable photo opportunities.

Discover alien-themed street lights, murals, and memorabilia throughout downtown Roswell, where the community has embraced its unique place in American pop culture. The town offers a lighthearted exploration of one of the most persistent mysteries in modern history, regardless of personal beliefs about what actually occurred in the New Mexico desert.

activities.text: Explore beyond the alien attractions to discover Roswell's genuine history as a ranching and agricultural center in southeastern New Mexico. Museums and historic sites document the region's development from frontier territory to modern community, offering context for understanding how an isolated desert town became internationally famous.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

map_keywords: International UFO Museum and Research Center

visits.text: Explore the Person County Museum of History, where comprehensive artifact exhibitions provide a detailed and engaging account of Roxboro's fascinating past. This welcoming museum offers visitors the opportunity to understand the heritage and development of this delightful Person County community through carefully curated displays and historical documents.

activities.text: Relax in the open spaces of Longhurst Park, the ideal environment for outdoor recreation including walking trails and sports facilities. The park provides a refreshing escape where visitors can enjoy the small-town atmosphere that makes Roxboro so charming.

Experience downtown Roxboro's socially charged atmosphere, where the family-friendly Kirby Cultural Arts Complex hosts entertaining stage performances. Browse for unique treasures at local shops while engaging with friendly locals proud to share their community's heritage and hospitality.

days_plan.itin_title: North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains

places.name: Sabbathday Lake Me

visits.text: Twenty miles north of Portland, down a quiet country road in New Gloucester, Sabbathday Lake Shaker Village has been here since 1783. It is the last active Shaker community in the world - a 1,750-acre working farm with 17 historic buildings, a museum, and as of late 2025, three members: Brother Arnold Hadd, Sister June Carpenter, and newly-received novice Sister April Baxter. At its peak in 1850, the worldwide Shaker movement had 5,000 believers. The celibate faith limited membership by birth, and the Canterbury, New Hampshire community - the last other active one - closed in 1992. Guided tours of the buildings run May through October; the Shaker Store sells herbs grown in gardens cultivated here since 1799.

days_plan.itin_title: Inland Maine: Moosehead Lake, Mount Katahdin & the North Woods

map_keywords: Sabbathday Lake Shaker Village New Gloucester Maine

visits.text: Sacramento - America's Farm-to-Fork Capital: California's capital city sits at the confluence of the Sacramento and American Rivers, surrounded by the most productive agricultural region on Earth. The historic Old Sacramento Waterfront district preserves Gold Rush-era architecture along wooden boardwalks.

California State Capitol - Where the Golden State Governs: This stunning neoclassical building, completed in 1874, anchors a 40-acre park filled with memorials, gardens, and trees from around the world. Free tours reveal legislative chambers adorned with California history.

Crocker Art Museum - The West's Oldest Public Art Museum: Founded in 1885, this institution houses one of the finest collections of California art, European master drawings, and international ceramics, all within a striking contemporary expansion adjoining the original Victorian Italianate gallery.

activities.text: Old Sacramento Historic District - Step Back to the Gold Rush: Wooden sidewalks, horse-drawn carriages, and restored 1850s buildings create an immersive journey to California's most transformative era. The California State Railroad Museum houses the largest collection of railroad locomotives and cars in North America.

Sacramento Mural Trail - Urban Art Gallery Without Walls: Over 600 murals transform the city into an open-air museum, with new works constantly appearing on buildings throughout downtown and surrounding neighborhoods.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

places.name: Saint Paul

visits.text: Minnesota State Capitol—Don't miss this Cass Gilbert building (the same architect behind New York's Woolworth Building), completed in 1905. Look up: the marble dome soars 223 feet—it's the second-largest self-supported dome in the world after St. Peter's Basilica in Rome. At the apex of the south entrance, spot Daniel Chester French's gilded quadriga. Guided tours are free.

Cathedral of Saint Paul—You'll see it from a distance: this granite cathedral, completed in 1915, dominates the city from St. Anthony Hill. The copper dome rises 306 feet, and the interior seats 3,000. Architect Emmanuel Masqueray drew inspiration from St. Peter's in Rome. Step inside—it's free and the acoustics are remarkable.

Summit Avenue—From the cathedral, walk down this 4.5-mile avenue lined with 373 Victorian mansions. It's the longest stretch of intact Victorian homes in the United States. At 240 Summit, the James J. Hill House (1891) is a 36,000-square-foot red sandstone mansion—the railroad magnate who built the Stone Arch Bridge lived here.

Science Museum of Minnesota—On the banks of the Mississippi, this interactive museum appeals to families. The Omnitheater (a domed IMAX screen) is worth the ticket. The exhibits on dinosaurs and Minnesota geology are well executed.

Landmark Center—Enter this former federal courthouse from 1902, built in pink sandstone. The five-story atrium is worth the visit alone. Know that gangster John Dillinger stood trial here in 1934. Admission is free.

Historic Fort Snelling—At the confluence of the Mississippi and Minnesota Rivers, this 1825 fort is one of the oldest military posts in the American West. Period costume re-enactments immerse you in the lives of 19th-century soldiers and civilians. The fort's history also includes the 1862-63 Dakota internment camp—a painful chapter told with honesty.

activities.text: End the day on Grand Avenue, which runs parallel to Summit. You'll find shops, cafés, and restaurants in brick buildings—the atmosphere is quieter than Minneapolis, more residential.

Como Park Zoo & Conservatory—If you're traveling with family, this free zoo and botanical garden (suggested donation) is perfect. Smaller than the Minneapolis Zoo, but the tropical conservatory is worth the stop—especially if it's chilly outside.

days_plan.itin_title: Minnesota in 10 Days: Twin Cities, Lakes, and Lake Superior's North Shore
Minnesota in 10 Days: Twin Cities, Lakes, and Lake Superior's North Shore

map_keywords: James J Hill House Saint Paul
Science Museum of Minnesota Saint Paul

visits.text: Salem is hard to approach lightly—prepare yourself for that from the moment you enter town. In 1692, 25 people perished here, executed or dead in custody, after witchcraft accusations ignited the Puritan community within months. The Salem Witch Museum traces these events through 13 life-size scenes in a former neo-Gothic church (1844-1846). The Witch House, home of Judge Jonathan Corwin, is the only structure directly connected to the trials. In October, the town is overrun—prefer June or September.

activities.text: Don't walk past the Peabody Essex Museum without entering: beyond a world-class maritime and Asian art collection, it preserves the original documents from the 1692 trials—handwritten petitions, execution orders, witness depositions. 45 minutes is enough for the trial-related galleries.

days_plan.itin_title: Boston, Maine & the Islands: 10 Days Through New England

visits.text: Salina - Kansas's Emerging Arts Capital: Even longtime locals discover something new with each visit to this booming downtown, where fresh attractions, murals, and revitalized streetscapes continue transforming the city's core.

Mural at the Mill - 100 Feet of Monumental Art: Australian artist Guido van Helten's stunning work wraps three sides of a flour mill, the first of many murals coordinated by the Salina Kanvas Project that have transformed the city into an open-air gallery.

Salina SculptureTour - 20 Works Dressing Downtown Sidewalks: This rotating outdoor exhibition changes every spring, with pedestrians voting for their favorite sculptures among the works displayed throughout the city center.

The Stiefel Theatre - Art Deco Splendor Under a Neon Spire: This beautifully restored 1931 showpiece hosts live performances beneath its distinctive illuminated tower, a beacon of culture on the Kansas plains.

activities.text: Downtown Salina Walking Tour - Art at Every Turn: Wander the revitalized streetscape discovering murals, sculptures, and the creative energy that has made Salina a destination for art lovers.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

visits.text: Guadalupe Mountains National Park - This park protects an ancient Permian reef 265 million years old, now thrust up to 8,751 feet. El Capitan, the limestone cliff marking the park's southern entrance, served as a landmark for travelers for centuries. Stop at the Pine Springs Visitor Center for maps and trail conditions.

Frijole Ranch - This 19th-century ranch house, 2 miles from the visitor center, holds a small museum about pioneer and Native American history in the region. The spring that feeds the ranch attracts varied wildlife - you might spot elk or wild turkeys.

activities.text: Guadalupe Peak hike - If you're fit, this is THE challenge: climbing to the highest point in Texas (8,751 feet). The trail is 8.5 miles round-trip with 3,000 feet of elevation gain. Expect 6 to 8 hours. At the summit, a stainless steel pyramid marks the 'rooftop of Texas.' Start at dawn and bring at least 3 liters of water. If that's too ambitious, the Devil's Hall Trail (4.2 miles, 2.5 hours) is a shorter alternative that descends into a spectacular canyon.

days_plan.itin_title: Desert Parks of Texas and New Mexico: Big Bend, Carlsbad, White Sands, and Guadalupe

places.name: Salt Lake

visits.text: Temple Square - This 35-acre complex in downtown Salt Lake City serves as worldwide headquarters for The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. The Salt Lake Temple, built between 1853 and 1893 from quartz monzonite blocks quarried in Little Cottonwood Canyon, has been undergoing major seismic renovation since 2019. The work concludes at the end of 2026, with a grand public open house scheduled from April through October 2027 - the first time the interior will be accessible to the public since 1893. In the meantime, you can visit the Conference Center, the Tabernacle - an 1867 acoustic marvel where a 360-voice choir performs - and the beautifully landscaped grounds. Admission is free, with guided tours available in over 40 languages.

Head up to the Utah State Capitol for panoramic views over the city and the Wasatch Mountains. The 1916 granite building is open for self-guided tours - the marble corridors and rotunda are worth a look. Allow 30 minutes.

San Rafael Swell - On the return drive to Salt Lake City via I-70, you'll cross the San Rafael Swell, a 60-mile geological dome eroded into canyons, mesas, and colorful cliffs. Several viewpoints are accessible directly from the interstate. If you have time, make the short detour to the Wedge Overlook - nicknamed Utah's "Little Grand Canyon" for its plunging view into a 1,000-foot-deep canyon.

Stop in Green River for coffee and a visit to the John Wesley Powell River History Museum. Powell explored this region in 1869 during his historic descent of the Colorado - this is where he launched his boats. The small town is also known for its watermelons, grown with irrigation along the Green River.

activities.text: Stock up on road trip supplies: water, snacks, SPF50 sunscreen, and a hat. You won't see another major supermarket until Moab, five days from now. Also check the weather forecast for the parks - summer afternoon thunderstorms can trigger deadly flash floods in the canyons. Important note: the drive to Springdale is 307 miles, roughly 4.5 hours of driving. Leave before noon to arrive in late afternoon and catch the golden light on Zion's cliffs.

The Moab to Salt Lake City drive via I-70 then US-6 crosses varied landscapes: red desert, canyons, then forested Wasatch mountains as you approach the capital. Allow about 3 hours 45 minutes of driving without stops. Fill up in Green River - it's the last gas station before a long stretch.

Utah's Mighty Five: Zion, Bryce Canyon, Capitol Reef, Arches, and Canyonlands
Salt Lake City to Great Basin Road Trip: Desert, Salt & Stars
