City Creek Center Salt Lake City
Natural History Museum of Utah Salt Lake City

visits.text: In Salton City, Chris meets Ronald Franz, an old man who becomes deeply attached to him and offers to adopt him, a proposal he refuses to preserve his freedom.

activities.text: Vol prochaine étape

days_plan.itin_title: Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey

places.name: San Antonio

visits.text: The Alamo is the chapel of Mission San Antonio de Valero, built around 1718 by Franciscans. The building entered history on March 6, 1836, at the end of a 13-day siege: between 183 and 257 Texan defenders (including Davy Crockett, former Tennessee congressman, and Colonels Bowie and Travis) held out against General Santa Anna's army before being killed to the last man. This defeat became the symbol of Texan resistance - six weeks later, Sam Houston crushed Santa Anna at San Jacinto to the cry of "Remember the Alamo!" Free entry, but crowds are massive during the day - arrive at opening.

San Antonio Missions National Historical Park protects four 18th-century Franciscan missions: Concepción (1755, the best preserved), San José (dubbed the "Queen of the Missions"), San Juan, and Espada. Together with the Alamo, San Antonio's five missions were inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site on July 5, 2015 - the first Texas site to achieve this status. The park's four missions remain active Catholic parishes. Biking is the best way to connect them via the Mission Reach Trail along the river.

Market Square (El Mercado) is the largest open-air Mexican market in the United States. The two main halls offer crafts, ceramics, textiles, and food products imported from Mexico. Push to the back of the halls - the most authentic and least touristy stalls are in the second row. The surrounding neighborhood comes alive in the evening with restaurants and outdoor music.

The King William Historic District is a neighborhood of Victorian and Neoclassical mansions from the 19th century, built by German merchants enriched through cattle and cotton trade after the Civil War. The name comes from King Wilhelm I of Prussia - the German founders named their main street after him. Some thirty homes are preserved; several are open to visitors. It's San Antonio's most elegant and peaceful residential quarter - stroll in the morning, when the streets are nearly deserted.

The Japanese Tea Garden was landscaped in 1917 in an abandoned limestone quarry north of downtown. Pools fed by natural springs, terraced gardens, and artificial waterfalls make it an unexpected refuge in the heart of San Antonio. Free admission - ideal early morning before the heat rises.

The Briscoe Western Art Museum occupies a 1929 Neoclassical building facing the River Walk. Its collection focuses on the art and culture of the American West: paintings, sculptures, embroidered saddles, posters, and vintage photographs - a more nuanced reading of the Western myth than you might expect. The view from the terrace over the River Walk is a bonus.

activities.text: The San Antonio River Walk (Paseo del Río) is a 15-mile network of pedestrian paths one level below street grade, along the San Antonio River. The Museum Reach section, toward the Pearl District, is quieter and greener than the commercial downtown stretch. Water taxis shuttle between neighborhoods. Even if you've seen photos, the descent a few yards below the city streets always surprises.

The Pearl District occupies the buildings of the former Pearl Brewery (1883), converted into a culinary complex, hotel, and farmers market. The Saturday morning farmers market is one of the city's most active, with Hill Country cheeses, smoked meats, vegetables, and preserves.

Natural Bridge Caverns are the largest commercial caves in Texas, 19 miles north of San Antonio in Comal County. Discovered by students in 1960 and opened to the public since 1964, they're named for an 89-foot natural bridge spanning the entrance. Interior temperature holds constant at 70°F - welcome in Texas summer. The standard guided tour (1h 15min) passes beneath formations tens of millions of years old in chambers reaching 100 feet high.

The Mission Reach Trail is a completely flat 8-mile bike path connecting the national park's four missions along the San Antonio River, from downtown to Mission Espada. It's the most pleasant way to see all the missions without a car. You'll find bike rentals at the Pearl District or at Mission Concepción.

visits.text: San Diego - America's Finest City: Year-round perfect weather, 70 miles of beaches, and a relaxed vibe distinguish California's second-largest city. From world-class attractions to legendary surf breaks, San Diego delivers coastal California at its best.

San Diego Zoo - One of the World's Great Zoos: This 100-acre facility in Balboa Park houses over 12,000 animals representing 650 species, many in innovative open-air habitats that have set the standard for modern zoos.

Balboa Park - The Smithsonian of the West: This 1,200-acre urban cultural park houses 17 museums, performing arts venues, and the zoo within stunning Spanish Colonial Revival architecture built for the 1915 Panama-California Exposition.

USS Midway Museum - Walk the Deck of a Floating City: This decommissioned aircraft carrier served from 1945 to 1992, and now hosts docent-led tours through living quarters, engine rooms, and a flight deck lined with historic aircraft.

Gaslamp Quarter - Victorian Splendor Meets Urban Energy: Sixteen blocks of restored Victorian architecture house San Diego's premier dining, entertainment, and nightlife district, all walkable from the convention center and waterfront.

activities.text: La Jolla Cove - Sea Lions, Seals, and Snorkeling: This small beach flanked by sandstone cliffs offers some of Southern California's clearest water, with sea lions lounging on nearby rocks and garibaldi (California's state fish) flashing orange in the shallows.

Coronado Beach - Where the Beach Meets the Grand Dame: The iconic Hotel del Coronado has anchored this beach since 1888, its distinctive red turrets rising above some of the finest sand in California.

Cabrillo National Monument - Where European Exploration Began: This point at the tip of Point Loma marks where Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo became the first European to set foot on the West Coast in 1542, with tide pools and panoramic views as bonuses.

Craft Beer Capital of America - 150+ Breweries and Counting: San Diego has emerged as America's premier craft beer destination, with pioneering breweries producing styles from hop-forward IPAs to innovative barrel-aged creations.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

places.name: San Francisco

visits.text: Start with Haight-Ashbury, the neighborhood that embodied the Summer of Love in 1967—Janis Joplin, the Grateful Dead, and Jefferson Airplane all had homes here. Take a detour to 710-720 Steiner Street, facing Alamo Square: this is where the Painted Ladies stand, seven Victorian houses built in the 1890s and painted in three or more colors. San Francisco once had 48,000 houses in this style between 1849 and 1915—only about 10,000 remain today. Arrive before 9 AM to catch them without the tour buses.

Fisherman's Wharf and Pier 39 form the liveliest stretch of waterfront in the city. Don't miss the sea lions that have colonized the K-Dock pontoons since 1989—there can be several hundred depending on the season. If you're hungry, this is where you'll find San Francisco's best clam chowder served in a sourdough bread bowl.

The cable car is one of the only forms of public transport designated a National Historic Monument in the United States. The Powell-Hyde line climbs up Russian Hill before descending toward Fisherman's Wharf; the Powell-Mason line connects the Wharf to Union Square. Expect 15 to 20 minutes for the ride, and be patient during peak hours—lines can get long.

Cross the Golden Gate Bridge on foot or by bike to grasp its true scale: 1.7 miles long, towers rising 746 feet above the water, and a central span of 4,200 feet. Built between 1933 and 1937 for $35 million—well below the initial $100 million estimate. Go early in the morning for optimal light and to avoid the sometimes fierce midday wind.

San Francisco's Chinatown is the oldest in the United States, founded in 1848. Its 24 blocks contain the city's highest population density. Wander along Grant Avenue, the main artery, and duck into the perpendicular alleyways to find the grocery stores, herbalists, and temples that tourists usually miss.

City Lights Books, in the heart of North Beach, is the bookstore founded in 1953 by poet Lawrence Ferlinghetti—the first American publisher of Allen Ginsberg. It stays open until midnight every day. Next door, Vesuvio Café was Jack Kerouac's favorite bar. The entire North Beach neighborhood was the birthplace of the Beat Generation—an hour's stroll covers the essential sites.

Highway 1 from Monterey to San Francisco passes through Carmel-by-the-Sea, a small village whose municipality prohibits neon signs, traffic lights, and regular sidewalks to preserve its quaint character—Ansel Adams lived and worked here. Take the detour via 17-Mile Drive on the Pebble Beach peninsula (private road, toll): it skirts the most prestigious golf courses in the United States and offers views of the rocky coast, including the Lone Cypress, a Monterey cypress standing alone on its rock for over 250 years. Further north, Santa Cruz features its Boardwalk—the only major seaside amusement park still operating in California—with a Ferris wheel overlooking the Pacific Ocean.

activities.text: Alcatraz deserves half a day. The 22-acre island, 1.5 miles offshore, houses the former federal prison that operated from 1934 to 1963—Al Capone was incarcerated here as prisoner number 85. Book your ferry from Pier 33 well in advance: tickets sell out fast, especially in summer. Plan for 2.5 to 3 hours on the island, including the audio tour.

End the day at the Palace of Fine Arts, a neoclassical rotunda built for the 1915 Panama-Pacific Exposition. The original structure was made of plaster and cardboard—it was rebuilt in concrete between 1964 and 1975 to preserve it. In late afternoon, the golden light on the dome and the reflecting lagoon is particularly stunning.

In the evening, head to the Castro for dinner. This has been the heart of San Francisco's LGBT+ community since the 1970s—Harvey Milk opened his camera shop here in 1973 before being elected to the city council. The restaurants are numerous and varied, the atmosphere relaxed and welcoming.

Arrive in San Francisco by late afternoon and climb Twin Peaks before sunset—two hills at 925 feet elevation in the heart of the city, offering the best panoramic view of all of San Francisco, the bay, the Golden Gate, and the peninsula. It's the only place where you can simultaneously see all the neighborhoods traversed during this road trip. A beautiful way to come full circle.

places.name: San Luis Obispo

visits.text: Hearst Castle — Explore the extravagant palace with 56 bedrooms and 61 bathrooms built between 1919 and 1947 for press magnate William Randolph Hearst. Architect Julia Morgan created this Hispanic-Moorish fantasy perched on La Cuesta Encantada, the enchanted hill.

Elephant Seal Vista Point — Observe the northern elephant seal colony sprawling lazily on the beaches of Piedras Blancas. These marine mammals that can weigh up to 2 tons have gathered here for molting and breeding since 1990.

Morro Rock — Contemplate this 176-meter volcanic dome emerging majestically from the ocean. Formed 23 million years ago, this volcanic neck sacred to the Chumash shelters nesting peregrine falcons and marks the entrance to the bay.

Mission San Luis Obispo de Tolosa — Visit this mission founded in 1772 by Father Junípero Serra, fifth in the chain of California missions. Its museum traces the tumultuous history of interactions between Franciscans and the native Chumash population.

activities.text: Stroll through the Farmers Market — Wander through the Thursday evening farmers market that has transformed Higuera Street into a lively street festival since 1983. Local producers, street musicians and barbecue vendors create a festive atmosphere typically Californian.

Wine tasting in Edna Valley — Wind through the rolling vineyards planted with Pinot Noir and Chardonnay in this renowned wine appellation. Family tasting rooms offer food and wine pairings in a bucolic setting 8 kilometers from downtown.

Hiking to Bishop Peak — Climb to the summit of this 474-meter volcanic peak, highest point of the Nine Sisters. The steep 6.5-kilometer trail rewards the effort with panoramic views of the valley and sparkling ocean.

days_plan.itin_title: Driving the Pacific Coast Highway: A Golden Gate Bridge to Hollywood Road Trip
The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

map_keywords: Mission San Luis Obispo de Tolosa

visits.text: You enter the Cedar Point peninsula, a 7-kilometer sand spit battered by Lake Erie waters where the first amusement park opened in 1870 - the second oldest operating park in the United States after Lake Compounce. What began as a beach with refreshment stand and dance floor has become the largest roller coaster complex in North America, with 18 coasters including the Millennium Force (93 meters high) and the recent Siren's Curse, inaugurated in 2025 as North America's tallest and fastest tilt coaster. At the peninsula's tip stands the Cedar Point lighthouse, built in 1862, listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1984 and the park's oldest permanent structure. The main season runs from May to September, with Halloween-themed days in October.

Aboard the Jet Express from Sandusky harbor, you reach the largest American island in Lake Erie in 20 minutes - 11 square kilometers of vineyards, forests, and polished limestone beaches. The island is famous for its Inscription Rock, sandstone slabs on which the Erie Native Americans carved petroglyphs centuries ago, and for its Glacial Grooves - grooves carved into limestone rock by passing glaciers during the last ice age about 18,000 years ago, among the most accessible in the United States. The island's center is easily explored by rental bike from the dock. Avoid July-August weekends for a quieter experience.

Sandusky is one of Ohio's most military-history-laden cities: during the War of 1812, Sandusky Bay served as a supply base for Commodore Oliver Hazard Perry's fleet, which defeated the British navy on September 10, 1813, at the Battle of Lake Erie, 40 kilometers northwest toward Put-in-Bay. Earlier, in the 1850s, Sandusky was one of the last stops on the Underground Railroad that helped slaves cross Lake Erie to Canada. The Sandusky Maritime Museum, on the waterfront, traces these stories with documents and ship models. In the evening, the Jackson Street Pier promenade offers remarkable sunsets over the bay.

activities.text: Put-in-Bay is the main town on South Bass Island, 6 kilometers from the American coast, known as the site of the American naval victory of 1813. Perry's Victory and International Peace Memorial - a 107-meter Doric column, inaugurated in 1915 - dominates the island and can be seen from shore. You can climb to the observation deck for the region's highest view. The island also has the third largest limestone cave in the United States (Crystal Cave), carved by underground waters with walls lined with celestite crystals.

Cedar Point Beach extends over a kilometer along the peninsula's north shore, facing Lake Erie - the same white sand beach that attracted the first visitors arriving by steamboat from Sandusky in the late 19th century. Lake Erie, though the shallowest of the Great Lakes with an average depth of 19 meters, warms faster than its neighbors and offers pleasant swimming temperatures from July to September. In the evening, after the park closes, the beach remains accessible and the sunset over the water is one of the region's finest.

days_plan.itin_title: Pittsburgh to Detroit: Steel, Rock & Motown - 9 Days Across the Great Lakes

visits.text: Old Mission Santa Barbara — Discover the queen of California missions founded in 1786, the only one to have remained under continuous Franciscan administration. The neoclassical church with its double-tower facade houses a golden altarpiece and Chumash wall frescoes.

Santa Barbara County Courthouse — Admire this Spanish-Moorish architectural masterpiece completed in 1929 after the destructive 1925 earthquake. The El Mirador clock tower offers a 360° view of the red tile roofs and sparkling Pacific.

State Street — Stroll the main palm-lined shopping artery where designer boutiques, art galleries and fusion restaurants sit alongside Spanish colonial style buildings. The uniform architecture creates a Mediterranean atmosphere unique in California.
Stearns Wharf — Walk along California's oldest wooden pier built in 1872. Artisan shops and the Sea Center aquarium occupy the historic buildings while fishermen and pelicans share the docks.

activities.text: Wine tasting on the Urban Wine Trail — Explore the thirty or so tasting rooms concentrated in the Funk Zone, a former industrial district converted into an urban wine paradise. Winemakers from Santa Ynez Valley present their award-winning vintages in renovated warehouses.

Beach bike ride — Pedal along the bike path running 5 kilometers of golden sand beaches lined with palm trees. Stop at East Beach for an impromptu beach volleyball match or a surfing session in the gentle waves.

Sunset at Butterfly Beach — Settle in on this exclusive Montecito beach where celebrities blend incognito with locals. The sun plunging into the Pacific behind the Channel Islands creates memorable flaming skies.

days_plan.itin_title: Driving the Pacific Coast Highway: A Golden Gate Bridge to Hollywood Road Trip
The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Santa Cruz - California's Original Surf City: This laid-back university town at the northern tip of Monterey Bay has shaped surf culture since the sport arrived on the mainland in 1885. The world-famous break at Steamer Lane draws elite surfers while beginners learn on gentler waves at Cowell's Beach.

Santa Cruz Beach Boardwalk - California's Last Remaining Seaside Amusement Park: This 1907 landmark operates two National Historic Landmarks: the 1924 Giant Dipper wooden roller coaster and the 1911 Looff Carousel. Free admission lets visitors soak up the vintage atmosphere without riding.

Natural Bridges State Beach - Where Monarchs Meet the Sea: This coastal park features a dramatic natural stone arch emerging from the surf, and from October through February, eucalyptus groves shelter thousands of migrating monarch butterflies.

activities.text: Mystery Spot - Where Gravity Gets Confused: This roadside attraction has baffled visitors since 1939 with optical illusions and seemingly impossible physical phenomena in a small patch of redwood forest. Kitsch or science experiment? You decide.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: Marvel at Saint Francis Cathedral Basilica, a magnificent Romanesque Revival structure whose beautiful interior and stunning stained glass windows create an atmosphere of profound peace and artistic grandeur. The cathedral stands as the spiritual heart of Santa Fe and represents one of the finest examples of ecclesiastical architecture in the American Southwest.

Visit the Loretto Chapel, renowned for its mysterious spiral staircase that appears to defy architectural principles, constructed without any central support and using only wooden pegs. Legend attributes its creation to a mysterious carpenter who appeared to answer the sisters' prayers and vanished without payment upon completion. The tiny chapel and its miraculous staircase draw visitors from around the world.

activities.text: Stroll through Santa Fe's central Plaza, the historic heart of America's oldest capital city, surrounded by stunning adobe architecture, vibrant street performers, and exceptional artisan galleries. The plaza has served as the city's commercial and cultural center since its founding in 1610, making it one of the most historically significant public spaces in the United States.

Explore the Railyard Park district, where preserved railway heritage meets contemporary arts and culture in a revitalized urban space. Old railway details and industrial architecture provide atmospheric backdrops for galleries, markets, and community gatherings that celebrate Santa Fe's creative spirit.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

places.name: Santa Monica

visits.text: Wigwam Motel (Rialto/San Bernardino) - 19 concrete teepee-shaped rooms, built in 1949, surrounded by vintage cars. One of Route 66's most photographed motels. If you're not staying overnight, a photo from the parking lot is enough.

Santa Monica Pier - The 'End of the Trail' sign (installed in 2009) marks the official end of Route 66. You've driven 2,448 miles from Chicago. The Pacific is before you. Get your photo near the sign, then turn around: the Pacific Park Ferris wheel, the historic 1922 carousel, and arcade are there. It's the end of the journey and the beginning of California.

Venice Beach - Walk 2 miles south of the pier to Venice Beach, California's most eccentric boardwalk: bodybuilders working out outdoors, street performers, skaters, vendors selling everything and anything. The original Muscle Beach has been here since 1934.

activities.text: Pacific sunset - You've crossed the Illinois plains, Texas, the New Mexico and Arizona deserts, the Mojave. Sit on the Santa Monica sand and watch the sun descend into the Pacific. It's the perfect ending.

days_plan.itin_title: Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica

map_keywords: Santa Monica Pier End of Route 66

visits.text: Sarasota - Where Beach Vibes Meet Big City Culture: This Gulf Coast gem combines laid-back coastal living with world-class cultural institutions, creating an unusually sophisticated beach destination.

The Ringling - A Compound of Museums Celebrating Art and Circus: John Ringling's legacy includes his massive art collection, the Museum of the Circus, and Ca' d'Zan-his lavish Venetian Gothic mansion overlooking Sarasota Bay.

Siesta Key - America's Best Beach Sand: The quartz crystal sand of Siesta Key Beach stays remarkably cool even in blazing heat, a unique quality that has earned it 'best beach' titles from numerous publications.
Lido Beach - Siesta's Quieter Neighbor: Less crowded but equally beautiful, Lido Key offers a more laid-back alternative with the same stunning Gulf water.

St. Armands Circle - Upscale Shopping in a Garden Setting: This circular shopping district features upscale boutiques, galleries, and restaurants arranged around a beautifully landscaped central park.

activities.text: Siesta Key Village - Beachside Bohemia: Browse surf shops, sample local brews at hole-in-the-wall bars, and soak up the relaxed atmosphere of this beloved beach community.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

places.name: Saratoga

visits.text: In Saratoga's City Park on Walnut Street, a public mineral hot spring sits open 24 hours a day, 365 days a year, free of charge - the Hobo Hot Pool. It ranges from 106°F at its coolest end to 119°F at the 'Lobster Pot.' The current pool was discovered in 1917 after a flood eroded the banks of Spring Creek. Native Americans used the original springs here as neutral territory for centuries; the town was named Saratoga in 1884 after an Iroquois word meaning 'place of miraculous water in the rock.' Restrooms and changing facilities are on site.

The Hotel Wolf on Bridge Street has been operating since the late 19th century and remains the social hub of the North Platte Valley. The bar and restaurant serve some of the better steaks in the region. Sit down at lunch and listen to the tables around you - ranchers, outfitters, and visiting anglers talking about water levels and hatch conditions. This is what Saratoga actually is.

Five miles north of town on WY-130/230, the Saratoga National Fish Hatchery raises multiple trout species for federal and state hatcheries nationwide and runs a captive breeding program for the endangered Wyoming Toad. Free to visit; a short self-guided tour explains the operation. Context worth having: the North Platte at Saratoga holds over 4,000 catchable wild trout per mile and has not been stocked since 1979.

activities.text: A guided float trip on the Upper North Platte is the full-day option - 70 miles of blue-ribbon water holding over 4,000 wild trout per mile (browns and rainbows averaging 13-17 inches), unstocked since 1979. The section from Bennett Peak to Treasure Island to Saratoga runs through canyon scenery and cottonwood-lined banks. Mid-June to early August is peak season when spring runoff clears. Reserve at least a week in advance in summer.

Twenty minutes south of Saratoga lies Encampment, and from there Battle Highway (WY-70) heads west to 'Aspen Alley' - a half-mile stretch of dirt road where tall aspens arch completely overhead, glowing gold in late September. The Grand Encampment Museum covers the 1890s-1900s copper mining boom with over a dozen original buildings; free admission. A 2-3 hour loop that shows a completely different face of Carbon County.

days_plan.itin_title: Wyoming Beyond Yellowstone: Cowboys, Hot Springs & Bighorn Medicine Wheel
Colorado-Wyoming Road Trip: National Parks, Rockies & Wide Open Spaces

map_keywords: North Platte River fly fishing Saratoga

visits.text: Fall under the spell of Savannah, one of America's most enchanting cities, where cobblestone streets wind past 22 oak-shaded squares draped in Spanish moss beneath grand antebellum mansions. Founded in 1733, the city's original grid plan created the intimate green spaces that define its romantic character today. The blend of architectural styles spanning three centuries creates streetscapes of extraordinary beauty and historical significance.

Explore the hauntingly beautiful Bonaventure Cemetery, where ornate Victorian monuments and moss-draped oaks create an atmosphere of melancholic grandeur overlooking the Wilmington River. The oak-canopied avenue through Wormsloe Historic Site, lined with 400 live oaks planted in the 1890s, ranks among the most photographed roads in the American South. The elegant Mercer-Williams House offers tours through Savannah's storied past.

activities.text: Stroll along River Street where historic cotton warehouses now house eclectic shops, restaurants, and lively entertainment venues overlooking the Savannah River. Sunset paddlewheel cruises offer views of the brick waterfront accompanied by live jazz and Southern cuisine. Ghost tours after dark reveal the city's supernatural reputation as one of America's most haunted destinations.

Wander through the lush landscapes of Forsyth Park, anchored by an iconic 1858 fountain modeled after fountains in Paris and Cusco. The 30-acre park provides a green oasis for picnics, concerts, and people-watching beneath centuries-old trees. The surrounding Victorian district features some of Savannah's finest residential architecture.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

places.name: Scottsbluff

visits.text: Coming from Alliance, you'll spot Chimney Rock from 30 miles away - a 325-foot spire of Brule clay and volcanic ash rising from the North Platte River valley like a stone needle. Stop here first, 22 miles before reaching Scottsbluff. Between 1843 and 1869, historians found that 95 to 97 percent of westward emigrants who kept journals mentioned Chimney Rock by name, making it the single most-referenced landmark on the entire Oregon, California and Mormon trail system. Erosion and lightning strikes have reduced it by an estimated 100 feet since pioneer days. Congress designated it a National Historic Site in 1956. The Abbott Visitor Center in Bayard has exhibits and a trail film; since August 2023, the Walk to the Rock trail lets you approach across 360 acres of protected prairie on a 0.8-mile accessible path.

Scotts Bluff National Monument, two miles west of Gering, towers 800 feet above the North Platte River - the second-most-mentioned landmark in pioneer diaries after Chimney Rock. Over 250,000 emigrants passed between 1843 and 1869; after 1850, most funneled through Mitchell Pass, so narrow that 300,000 people and their wagons all crossed the same strip of ground, carving deep parallel ruts still visible and walkable today. The Oregon Trail Museum at the base holds the world's largest collection of William Henry Jackson's artworks - paintings and photographs of the westward migration made in the 1860s and 70s. Drive the 1.6-mile Summit Road to the top: on a clear day you can see Chimney Rock 20 miles east and Laramie Peak 90 miles west.

The Legacy of the Plains Museum in Gering tells the agricultural history of the North Platte Valley - from Oregon Trail emigrants who first broke ground here to the irrigation systems that made western Nebraska productive farmland. The collection spans pioneer wagons, farm equipment and oral history archives of the families who settled the valley from the 1880s onward. It is the quieter daily-life counterpart to Scotts Bluff's epic landscape: the story of what happened after the covered wagons stopped. Paid entry; plan 45 to 60 minutes.

activities.text: Hike the Saddle Rock Trail at Scotts Bluff - 1.6 miles one way, climbing 435 feet from the visitor center to the summit. The trail passes through two hand-dug tunnels blasted by Civilian Conservation Corps crews in the 1930s; the top opens onto a 360-degree view of the North Platte valley, the Wyoming border, and the flat Nebraska plains reaching east to the horizon. Then walk the 0.5-mile Oregon Trail Pathway from the wagon display at the base - the actual ruts carved by 300,000 emigrants are visible as parallel troughs in the dry grass. You can stand in them.

After the bluff, explore downtown Scottsbluff and the adjacent city of Gering along the North Platte River - the two cities face each other across the river and share a relaxed western character. A walk along the Scottsbluff Riverwalk connects the two downtowns along the river bank. For dinner, the historic district along Broadway in Scottsbluff has several locally owned steakhouses and Mexican restaurants that serve the ranching community of western Nebraska.

days_plan.itin_title: Nebraska Sandhills & Pioneer Trails: From Omaha to the Rockies

map_keywords: Scotts Bluff National Monument Gering Nebraska
Chimney Rock National Historic Site Bayard Nebraska
Legacy of the Plains Museum Gering Nebraska

visits.text: Peter Weir didn't build a set for The Truman Show. He found one. Seaside - planned in 1981 by Robert Davis following New Urbanism principles - was already so perfectly ordered that the film crew barely needed to intervene when they arrived in 1997. Pastel cottages, brick streets, white picket fences, a town square with a working grocery store (Modica Market, family-run since 1988). The Truman House is at 31 Natchez Street, still a private home. Walk to the Tupelo Street gazebo where Jim Carrey's awakening scenes were shot - it still stands, still circles the same roundabout.

Grayton Beach State Park interrupts 30A's planned villages with 2,220 acres of dune-and-scrub habitat and a beach that consistently ranks among the nation's best. The real draw is Western Lake behind the dunes: a rare coastal dune lake that periodically breaches its sand barrier and connects directly to the Gulf. Fewer than 100 such lakes exist in the world; almost all are concentrated on this 24-mile Panhandle stretch. Take the half-mile nature trail to the overlook above the lake before you hit the beach. Fee applies; arrive before 10 a.m. in summer.

Scenic Highway 30A runs 24 miles between Inlet Beach and Dune Allen, threading a sequence of planned coastal villages each with its own architectural identity. Rosemary Beach leans Caribbean; Alys Beach is all white stucco and shadow; WaterColor breaks up the rooflines with Florida vernacular. Between the villages the road passes through scrub pine and past the coastal dune lake corridor that makes this stretch unlike anywhere else in Florida. No admission - drive it, stop where something catches you, eat when you're hungry.

activities.text: The 18-mile paved bike path along 30A is one of the few Florida cycling routes that requires no athletic ambition - flat, mostly shaded, connecting Seaside to Rosemary Beach to Alys Beach in one continuous thread through scrub pine and past the dune lakes. Rentals in Seaside's central square cost less than an hour of resort parking. Go early on a weekday before traffic builds on the road running alongside.

Seaside's Central Square fills at sunset around the food truck vendors that have anchored the community's social life since its early years. Tacos, flatbreads, fresh-churned ice cream, cold beer - the square runs on its own logic, and the best table is no table at all. After dark, take the Coleman Pavilion walkway down to the beach: the same shore where Truman Burbank stood at the edge of his manufactured world and realized something was wrong.

days_plan.itin_title: Florida Panhandle: Tallahassee to Pensacola on the Emerald Coast

visits.text: Pike Place Market — Explore America's oldest continuously operating public market founded in 1907, where fishmongers toss salmon and vendors sell fresh flowers, artisan cheeses, and local crafts. The nine-acre complex contains over 225 businesses across multiple levels descending toward Elliott Bay.

Space Needle — Ascend 184 meters to the observation deck of Seattle's iconic 1962 World's Fair landmark designed by John Graham & Company. The rotating glass floor and 360-degree views encompass Puget Sound, Mount Rainier, the Cascades, and the Olympic Mountains.

Chihuly Garden and Glass — Marvel at Dale Chihuly's spectacular blown glass sculptures displayed in indoor galleries and outdoor garden beneath the Space Needle. The Glasshouse centerpiece features a 30-meter suspended sculpture of red, orange, and yellow forms.

Seattle Art Museum — Discover collections spanning ancient to contemporary art in this downtown museum founded in 1933. The building by Robert Venturi houses Northwest Coast Native art, European paintings, and contemporary installations across 312,000 square feet.

Museum of Flight — Explore the largest independent air and space museum on the West Coast with over 175 aircraft and spacecraft. Highlights include the original Air Force One, a Concorde, and the Space Shuttle Trainer housed in six connected buildings.

Ballard Locks — Watch boats navigate between Puget Sound and Lake Union through this engineering marvel completed in 1917. The fish ladder allows visitors to observe salmon migrating upstream while ship traffic passes through massive lock chambers.

Fremont neighborhood — Discover Seattle's self-proclaimed Center of the Universe featuring quirky public art including the Fremont Troll sculpture under the Aurora Bridge. Vintage shops, brewpubs, and Sunday markets create bohemian atmosphere.

activities.text: Original Starbucks visit — Sip coffee at the first Starbucks opened in 1971 at Pike Place Market, maintaining its original brown mermaid logo. The flagship store preserves the company's coffee roasting heritage before global expansion.

Underground Tour — Descend beneath Pioneer Square streets to explore the buried 1890s city preserved after the Great Seattle Fire of 1889. Guided tours reveal original storefronts and sidewalks now one story underground after street levels were raised.

Waterfront evening stroll — Walk along Elliott Bay past the Seattle Great Wheel and ferry terminals as the sun sets behind the Olympic Mountains. The revitalized waterfront features restaurants, shops, and public art with Puget Sound views.

Coffee culture tour — Visit specialty roasters and cafes showcasing Seattle's coffee obsession beyond Starbucks. Third-wave coffee shops emphasize single-origin beans, pour-over methods, and latte art.

Sunset cruise on Puget Sound — Glide past islands, ferries, and container ships as the Olympics turn pink in alpenglow. Evening cruises offer views of the Seattle skyline illuminated against mountain silhouettes.

days_plan.itin_title: Washington State's I-90 Corridor: Seattle, Snoqualmie Pass & Alpine Lakes
Washington State's I-90 Corridor: Seattle, Snoqualmie Pass & Alpine Lakes

places.name: Sedona

visits.text: Ascent from Havasupai (10 miles, pre-dawn start), then 4.5-hour drive to Sedona. You'll arrive tired—that's normal. Go straight to Airport Mesa for sunset: this 360° viewpoint overlooks Sedona's red sandstone formations with the green mountains of Coconino National Forest in the background. The parking lot is small—arrive 30 minutes before sunset.
If you still have energy, visit Tlaquepaque Arts & Shopping Village—an artists' village built in Mexican colonial style, with galleries, pottery studios, and restaurants under sycamore trees.

Cathedral Rock is THE Sedona hike. The trail is 1.2 miles one-way (short but steep—750 feet of elevation gain), with sections where you scramble on all fours up sandstone. From the saddles at the top, the view of Sedona's red formations and Oak Creek valley is spectacular. Start early—the parking lot fills before 8am.

In the afternoon, do the Bell Rock & Courthouse Butte loop (3.7 miles, easy). Bell Rock is a perfectly bell-shaped formation—you can climb its lower slopes. Courthouse Butte is more massive and the trail circles it. It's one of Sedona's vortexes.

Take AZ-89A north to drive through Oak Creek Canyon—nicknamed the "Grand Canyon's little cousin." The road winds for 12 miles between red cliffs covered in pines. Stop at Slide Rock State Park—a natural 80-foot waterslide in sandstone polished smooth by Oak Creek water. Kids (and adults) love sliding in the icy water.

Heading back to Sedona, stop by the Chapel of the Holy Cross—a church embedded in a red sandstone cliff, built in 1956. The architecture is striking: a 90-foot cross integrated into the glass and concrete façade, with red rock as the back wall. The view from the parking lot is already beautiful, but go inside—the light-flooded interior is worth the visit.

activities.text: Sedona is known for its "vortexes"—points where earth energy is supposedly concentrated. The four main vortexes are at Airport Mesa, Cathedral Rock, Bell Rock, and Boynton Canyon. Whether you believe in them or not, they're also Sedona's best hiking spots.

If you want another hike, Devil's Bridge Trail (4 miles round-trip) leads to Sedona's largest natural sandstone bridge—50 feet wide suspended above the void. The photo on the bridge went viral on social media. The trail isn't difficult but the Dry Creek Road parking lot fills fast.

The Red Rock Scenic Byway (AZ-179) between Sedona and Village of Oak Creek is one of Arizona's most beautiful scenic roads—7.5 miles with Bell Rock, Courthouse Butte, and Cathedral Rock as backdrop. Drive it in late afternoon when the sun grazes the red formations.

days_plan.itin_title: Arizona Grand Loop: Grand Canyon and Desert Highways
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons

map_keywords: Chapel of the Holy Cross Sedona

visits.text: Stand at the west end and look across. The Edmund Pettus Bridge arches high enough that from the starting point you can't see what's on the other side - the 600 marchers on March 7, 1965 had no idea what waited for them until they reached the crown. Then they saw it: 150 state troopers and possemen, some on horseback, stretched across Route 80. They were given one minute's warning, then charged. John Lewis suffered a skull fracture. Amelia Boynton was beaten unconscious; the photograph of her lying on the roadway ran on front pages worldwide. The bridge is 1,248 feet long. The name across the crossbeam belongs to Edmund Pettus, Confederate general and Grand Dragon of the Alabama KKK. National Historic Landmark since 2013. Always open, always free.

Six blocks from the bridge, the twin Romanesque towers of Brown Chapel AME Church rise above the surrounding houses. This is where the congregation organized in 1866, one year after Emancipation - the 1908 building by Black builder A.J. Farley is the only surviving work he's known to have built. Martin Luther King Jr. spoke from this pulpit on January 2, 1965, urging registration. On March 7, 600 marchers gathered on these steps and walked two-by-two through downtown. Brown Chapel provided food, shelter, and organizing space throughout the entire 1965 campaign. National Historic Landmark 1997. Tours by appointment: (334) 874-7897.

The National Park Service's free Selma Interpretive Center, blocks from the bridge, puts the full year of 1965 in context - because Bloody Sunday was the visible moment of a campaign that had been running since 1963. In Dallas County that year, Black citizens made up more than half the population but just 2 percent of registered voters. Rangers explain the voter registration drives, the violence, and the three marches. The center marks the official start of the Selma to Montgomery National Historic Trail, a 54-mile federally designated route following the 1965 march. Free admission; the 25-minute ranger film is worth watching.

activities.text: The Selma Civil Rights Heritage Trail links the bridge, Brown Chapel, the Old Depot Museum, and the National Voting Rights Museum on Water Avenue - all on foot, about a mile, flat ground. At the foot of the bridge, the Voting Rights Museum holds oral histories from marchers who were present on Bloody Sunday. The signage on the trail is clear; no guide is needed. Small admission fee at the Voting Rights Museum.

Drive east on US-80 to the Lowndes County Interpretive Center, a free NPS site about 20 miles out on the historic march route. Lowndes County is where marchers camped in open fields on Day 3 of the third march under National Guard protection, and where Viola Liuzzo - a white civil rights worker from Detroit - was shot and killed by KKK members on March 25, 1965, as she drove marchers back to Selma after the march reached Montgomery. The center tells that story without softening it. Free admission.

days_plan.itin_title: Alabama Deep South Itinerary: Civil Rights, Music and Gulf Coast
Alabama Civil Rights Trail: Birmingham to Mobile
The Black Belt: Atlanta to New Orleans Through the Deep South

places.name: Seneca Rocks Wv

visits.text: You're spotting from afar a white quartzite spire bursting from the forest like a saw tooth - Seneca Rocks rises nearly 280 meters above the confluence of Seneca Creek and the North Fork of the South Branch Potomac. It's the only wall in the Eastern United States whose summit is inaccessible without rope climbing techniques. During World War II, in 1943 and 1944, the Army's 10th Mountain Division trained here on alpine climbing before fighting in Italy's Apennines; the soft iron pitons they left in the rock are still visible on certain routes. The trail from the Discovery Center climbs 1.3 km to an observation platform overlooking the valley at 700 meters elevation gain - accessible to all fitness levels.

You're driving 70 km north of Seneca Rocks and setting foot on the highest plateau east of the Mississippi - Dolly Sods extends across 17,371 acres between 800 and 1,257 meters elevation in the Monongahela National Forest. The name comes from an 18th-century German family, the Dahle, who used these mountain meadows to graze their sheep. The flora resembles Canadian tundra: dwarf birches, sphagnum moss, wild blueberries, and spruces twisted into flags by relentless winds. The trails around Bear Rocks offer spectacular views of the wooded Allegheny gorges - bring water, there's no potable source on the plateau.

You're visiting one of the region's oldest intact farms: the Sites House, built in 1839 by a German immigrant family named Seitz (Anglicized to Sites), still stands a few hundred meters from the Discovery Center. It was built on land that German families began claiming in 1761, attracted by fertile soil and cheap land. Germany Valley, which extends around Seneca Rocks, actually takes its name from these settlers. The NPS has restored the wood and stone house; the interior is open during summer open-house days.

activities.text: You're harnessed at the base of a wall of hard, rough quartzite - ideal for beginners because it offers good holds even on the first routes. Seneca Rocks has over 400 routes rated from 5.0 to 5.14b, but beginner routes already let you reach intermediate ledges with striking valley views. Seneca Rocks Climbing School, founded in 1971, is one of the oldest climbing schools in the United States and has trained several generations of East Coast climbers.

You're exploring Dolly Sods on 47 miles of trails, many of which follow old logging railroad beds from the early 20th century, when the region was entirely clear-cut by lumbermen. The fires that followed burned down to the humus, leaving only bare rock - hence a slow-recovery vegetation that still resembles Canadian heath a hundred years later. The Red Creek Trail follows a crystal-clear stream and offers some of the wildest camping sites on the entire East Coast. On weekdays and off-season, you'll have the plateau to yourself.

days_plan.itin_title: West Virginia Loop - From the Appalachians to New River Gorge

map_keywords: Seneca Rocks Discovery Center West Virginia
Dolly Sods Wilderness Bear Rocks WV

visits.text: Jour 1: Alaska SeaLife Center - This marine research facility and public aquarium in Seward provides close encounters with Alaska's marine wildlife, including Steller sea lions, harbor seals, sea otters, and seabirds like puffins and murres. The center conducts important research on marine ecosystems and wildlife rehabilitation.

Jour 1: Exit Glacier - The only road-accessible portion of Kenai Fjords National Park features trails leading directly to the face of this actively retreating glacier. The 0.8-mile trail to the glacier face passes markers showing the ice's position in previous years, dramatically illustrating glacial recession. The strenuous Harding Icefield Trail climbs 3,500 feet over 8.2 miles to views of the vast ice field feeding Exit Glacier and dozens of others.

Jour 1: Seward Historic Waterfront - This picturesque port town at the head of Resurrection Bay was established in 1903 as the ocean terminus of the Alaska Railroad. The small boat harbor, galleries, and historic buildings reflect the community's fishing heritage and role as gateway to Kenai Fjords.

Jour 2: Kenai Fjords National Park - This 670,000-acre park protects the Harding Icefield, one of the four largest ice caps in the United States, and the spectacular coastal fjords carved by its glaciers. Tidewater glaciers calving into the sea create a landscape of extraordinary beauty and ecological richness.

Jour 2: Holgate Glacier and Aialik Bay - Full-day cruises venture deep into the park's fjords to witness massive tidewater glaciers actively calving icebergs into the sea. The thunderous crack and splash of ice breaking from glacier faces creates one of nature's most dramatic spectacles.

Jour 2: Northwestern Fjord - Extended cruises reach this remote arm of the park, where multiple glaciers flow from the Harding Icefield and wildlife viewing opportunities multiply with distance from Seward.

activities.text: Jour 1: Resurrection Bay Kayaking - The protected waters of the bay offer excellent paddling with mountain and glacier views, wildlife observation opportunities, and access to remote beaches and coves.

Jour 2: Kenai Fjords Wildlife Cruise - Half and full-day boat tours explore the park's coastal ecosystems, with regular sightings of humpback whales, orcas, sea otters, Steller sea lions, and harbor seals. Seabird colonies host thousands of puffins, cormorants, and kittiwakes on rocky islands and cliffs.

Jour 2: Fishing Charters - The nutrient-rich waters of Resurrection Bay and adjacent Gulf of Alaska support excellent fishing for halibut, salmon (kings, silvers, and sockeye), lingcod, and rockfish throughout the summer season.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords

places.name: Sheridan

visits.text: Forest Road 12 off US-14A leads to a lower parking lot, from which a 1.5-mile gravel road climbs to the Medicine Wheel: white limestone boulders arranged in a circle 82 feet across, 28 radial spokes extending from a central cairn, six outer cairns at the rim. All of this sits at 9,642 feet and has been visited by Native Americans for at least 7,000 years. Designated a National Historic Landmark in 1970, it remains an active ceremonial site used by tribes from across the Northern Plains - negotiations over the 1996 Historic Preservation Plan involved representatives of 16 tribes. If a ceremony is underway when you arrive, stand back and observe quietly. Access road opens July 1.

King's Saddlery at 184 N. Main Street is a working shop, not a museum - leather smell, power tools, artisans building saddles and ropes. Don King opened it in 1963 after learning his trade riding cattle trails through Texas, Arizona and California as a child; his custom saddles are in the Cowboy Hall of Fame and the ProRodeo Hall of Champions. Behind the shop is the Don King Museum - free to enter - with historic saddles, wagons, photographs, and leather-working tools spanning a century of Western craft. Ask the staff to watch them work.

The Historic Sheridan Inn at 856 Broadway Street opened in 1893 - built by the Chicago, Burlington and Quincy Railroad and designed by Thomas Kimball after a Scottish hunting lodge. It was the first building in the area with electric lights and bathtubs, and was said at opening to be the finest hotel between Chicago and San Francisco. Buffalo Bill Cody was a part-owner who auditioned Wild West show performers from the wraparound front porch. Reopened in 2015 after a 50-year closure; walk in for a meal or a drink without staying.

activities.text: The descent into Sheridan via the Bighorn Scenic Byway (US-14) from Burgess Junction to Dayton offers two stops worth pulling over for: Shell Falls, where water plunges 120 feet over granite cliffs with an interpretive walkway, and Steamboat Point, where a steep single-track trail reaches a 360-degree viewpoint over the valley. The full run is 58 miles; allow 90 minutes. US-14 handles all vehicle sizes - US-14A does not.

Sheridan's Main Street in the evening is worth lingering in - the 1923 WYO Theater at 42 N. Main still hosts live performances in a building that newspapers called 'Wyoming's Wonder Picture Palace' when it opened. A few blocks away, the Mint Bar has been a gathering spot since 1907: ranchers, rodeo cowboys, and passing travelers on the same barstools. No performance for tourists; just a working Western town doing what it does.

days_plan.itin_title: Wyoming Beyond Yellowstone: Cowboys, Hot Springs & Bighorn Medicine Wheel

map_keywords: Trail End State Historic Site Sheridan

visits.text: Behold Tsé Bitʼaʼí (Shiprock), a dramatic volcanic rock formation rising over 1,500 feet above the surrounding desert plain on Navajo Nation land. This sacred site holds profound spiritual significance to the Navajo people, who consider it a place of great power and cultural importance. The jagged peak and radiating dikes create one of the most distinctive natural landmarks in the American Southwest.

Learn about the geological and cultural significance of this ancient volcanic neck, the eroded remnant of a volcano that erupted approximately 27 million years ago. The formation's English name derives from its resemblance to a 19th-century clipper ship, while Navajo traditions associate it with a great bird that carried their ancestors to safety. Photography from surrounding viewpoints captures the monument's dramatic presence against endless desert skies.

activities.text: Experience the vast landscapes of the Navajo Nation, the largest Native American reservation in the United States, where traditional culture and natural beauty interweave across millions of acres of high desert terrain. Respect for sacred sites and Navajo customs enriches any visit to this remarkable region.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

visits.text: The Shreveport Municipal Auditorium is a National Historic Landmark - and the history contained within its walls is worth the visit alone. This is the stage where 19-year-old Elvis Presley made his major debut on October 16, 1954, after receiving a lukewarm reception at Nashville's Grand Ole Opry two weeks earlier. The Louisiana Hayride, a radio show broadcast on KWKH (50,000 watts, reaching 28 states), offered him a contract at $18 per show. He returned every Saturday for over a year before Colonel Parker bought out his contract for $10,000. Hank Williams, Johnny Cash, George Jones - they all passed through here before hitting the big time. A bronze statue of Elvis now stands on the front steps. If you love country and early rock and roll, take a close look at the stage floor - this is where it all began.

The Highland Historic District features Shreveport's finest homes: Victorian and Craftsman houses from the 1900s-1920s lined with centuries-old oak trees. It's the best-preserved residential neighborhood in northern Louisiana. Plan a leisurely walk - the streets are quiet and the architecture is worth seeing.

The Red River District is Shreveport's converted riverfront, with old warehouses transformed into restaurants, bars, and galleries. The evening view of the Red River and illuminated bridges is particularly striking. This is where your Deep South road trip begins.

activities.text: Stroll the Shreveport Riverwalk along the Red River: the floating casinos moored across the way on the Bossier City side, the steel bridges, and the evening light on the river create a distinctly Louisianan scene. Plan an hour to explore the riverfront leisurely before dinner in the district.

days_plan.itin_title: The Deep South via Mississippi: Shreveport to New Orleans - 10 Days

places.name: Sioux

visits.text: The Sergeant Floyd Monument stands on a bluff overlooking the Missouri — a 100-foot Egyptian-style obelisk of Kettle River sandstone completed in 1901 and among the first 92 sites designated as National Historic Landmarks in the United States in 1960. Floyd's remains are sealed at its base. He died on August 20, 1804, age 22, likely from a ruptured appendix — the only member of the Corps of Discovery to perish on the entire expedition. His last words to Clark were: 'I'm going away and I want you to write me a letter.' The grave was twice washed away by Missouri River erosion before the Floyd Monument Association secured permanent burial here. Free site; the overlook of the river valley is the reason to linger.

The Lewis and Clark Interpretive Center on the riverfront levee goes deeper into the Great Plains section of the 1804 journey than almost any other stop on this trail. Interactive exhibits cover the Lakota tensions near present-day Pierre, Floyd's death, the natural history the Corps documented, and the Missouri as the highway of the interior. The center sits in a converted pavilion with direct views of the river bend the expedition navigated. Paid entry; open daily.

Built in 1918, the Woodbury County Courthouse at 7th and Douglas is a National Historic Landmark — designed by William Gray Purcell and George Elmslie, two protégés of Louis Sullivan, in the organic Prairie style. The exterior terra cotta ornament and the central rotunda are among the most elaborate public interior spaces in the upper Midwest. Free to enter during business hours. The Sioux City Art Center, three blocks away, holds regional and contemporary works with free admission and is worth a 30-minute stop.

activities.text: Stone State Park in the northwest corner of Sioux City has bluff trails reaching 1,000 feet of elevation with overlooks of the Missouri Valley and three states simultaneously — Iowa, Nebraska, and South Dakota. The Dorothy Pecaut Nature Center inside the park runs natural history programming and exhibits on the Missouri River ecosystem the Corps navigated. The Sergeant Floyd River Museum on the south end of town doubles as the city visitor center and has a 19th-century sternwheeler on display.

The Historic Fourth Street District two blocks from the river is Sioux City's restaurant and entertainment corridor. The city has a strong Czech and German immigrant food culture that persists in family-owned bakeries and supper clubs. If you're arriving in the evening, Fourth Street is where you eat — several restaurants specialize in grilled Midwest beef and the craft beer scene on this block has grown considerably in recent years.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: Sergeant Floyd Monument Sioux City Iowa
Lewis Clark Interpretive Center Sioux City

visits.text: Head to Sioux Falls, South Dakota's largest city. Falls Park is the heart of town: the Big Sioux River cascades over pink quartzite formations 1.7 billion years old. In season, 14,000 liters of water per second rush over the rocks. Climb the observation tower (5 stories) for an overview, then descend onto the walkways at water level. The ruins of an old 1881 stone mill add a photogenic touch to the scene.

Downtown Sioux Falls has completely reinvented itself in recent years. SculptureWalk is an outdoor trail of over 60 contemporary sculptures installed along the sidewalks—they change annually. Phillips Avenue is the main street, with pink quartzite buildings (the same stone as the falls) converted into restaurants, galleries, and bars.

activities.text: In the evening, sample Sioux Falls' food scene—the city has exploded gastronomically with chefs highlighting Great Plains products: bison, sweet corn, wild berries. For local beer, downtown craft breweries abound.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa and Midwest Road Trip: Great Plains Loop

visits.text: In Slab City, Chris lives in an alternative desert community, reunites with Jan Burres and shares a simple and marginal daily life.

days_plan.itin_title: Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey

places.name: Sleeping Bear Dunes Empire

visits.text: You climb by hand and feet through fine white sand that gives way beneath each step - the Dune Climb is an 87-meter ascent on a 30° slope, with no marked path, just sand and sky. Everyone climbs. Many run down, their knees giving way beneath them in an involuntary cascade. At the top, you're not yet at the summit of the main bluff - you must still walk 3 kilometers across a sand plateau to reach the edge of the cliffs that drop 140 meters above Lake Michigan. The water below is turquoise. The forest of birches and maples encircles the promontory. The Ojibwe say that the two Manitou islands offshore are the cubs of the mother bear who fell asleep waiting for them on this shore after crossing the lake from Wisconsin.

12 stops over 12 kilometers of winding road through the national lakeshore - the Pierce Stocking Scenic Drive owes its name to the local logger who traced it in the 1960s and who donated his property to the park before dying in 1976. Stop 9 induces vertigo: a wooden promontory perched at the bluff's edge at 140 meters altitude, Lake Michigan before you, wind in your face, and below the white sand beaches that seem to belong to another planet. Stop 10, the Sleeping Bear Dune Overlook, shows the named dune - a wooded hill shrinking decade by decade under erosion, which will have disappeared in a few centuries.

Glen Lake stretches just east of the dunes - a 7-kilometer inland lake with extraordinarily transparent waters. You'll easily see the bottom at 10 meters depth in calm weather. The lake formed about 10,000 years ago when glaciers carved a depression in the rock then retreated. The two arms - Big Glen Lake and Little Glen Lake - are separated by an isthmus that Route 22 crosses in five seconds. Stop at the public beach to swim or simply put your feet in the water before tackling the dunes.

activities.text: The Empire Bluff Trail is Sleeping Bear's secret trail: less known than the Dune Climb, it climbs 1.2 kilometers through mixed forest to a promontory overlooking Lake Michigan and the northern dunes. The vegetation is dense until the last 200 meters, then suddenly opens onto 180 degrees of lake. In October, red maples and golden beeches frame the view.

South Manitou Island is one of the two Manitou islands - the cubs of the Ojibwe legend - accessible only by ferry from Leland harbor. The island is entirely within the national park, without cars or amenities. It's known for its 500-year-old giant cedars in the Valley of the Giants, its 1871 lighthouse still standing, and its abandoned 1900s farms when the white pine forest was entirely razed after the Chicago fire of 1871. A day on the island suffices for the walking tour with the ranger.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Lakes Grand Loop: Chicago, Milwaukee, Door County, Sleeping Bear, Mackinac, Pictured Rocks - 12 Days

map_keywords: Sleeping Bear Dunes Dune Climb Empire Michigan
Pierce Stocking Scenic Drive Sleeping Bear

places.name: South Bend

visits.text: You enter Notre Dame campus via the main drive and the Golden Dome catches your eye before you've even parked the car - a 68-foot gilded dome crowned with a statue of the Virgin Mary, topping the Main Building reconstructed in 1879 after a fire. The Basilica of the Sacred Heart, consecrated in 1888 and elevated to minor basilica status by Pope John Paul II in 1992, houses the world's largest collection of 19th-century French-style stained glass - imported directly from France in the late 19th century. The organ added in 2016 cost $4 million. The Grotto of Our Lady - a 1/7-scale replica of the Massabielle grotto in Lourdes - glows with candles at all hours of day and night. Free campus access; free guided tours from Eck Visitor Center on weekdays.

You climb the steps of the Studebaker National Museum and find yourself facing 150 years of American industrial history, from the Conestoga wagon to the last car model off the lines in 1963. The most historically charged piece sits on the ground floor: an unassuming carriage - the four-horse landau that carried Abraham Lincoln from his hotel to Ford's Theatre in Washington on the evening of April 14, 1865, hours before his assassination. The Studebaker family began building Conestoga wagons for western pioneers in 1852, converted to automobiles in 1902, and delivered 275,000 jeeps to the US Army during World War II. Open Monday-Saturday 10am-5pm, Sunday noon-5pm; admission: $15 combined with The History Museum.

You walk along the St. Joseph River until you see white foam from rapids from the pedestrian bridge - you're facing the East Race Waterway, America's first artificial whitewater course built using this hydraulic technique, created in 1977 in a river arm. At 450 meters long, it served as an official kayak World Cup course in 1979. In summer, inflatable tubes are available for rent to ride the rapids with family. In the evening, the River Lights inaugurated in 2015 illuminate the St. Joseph River until midnight - the riverside walk between the East Race and downtown South Bend takes 20 minutes on foot and is worth the detour after dinner.

activities.text: You approach Notre Dame Stadium and look up at the north facade: a 92-foot mosaic depicts Christ with arms raised - fans have dubbed it 'Touchdown Jesus' because it resembles a referee's signal. Opened in 1930 with 59,000 seats, renovated in 1997 and 2017 to reach 80,795 spectators, the stadium has hosted some of NCAA history's most decorated teams. Outside of game days, the plaza is freely accessible to photograph yourself in front of the mosaic. On fall game days, the campus atmosphere from morning onward transforms South Bend into the capital of American college sports - book well in advance.

You push open the door at South Bend Chocolate Company - an artisan chocolatier founded in 1991 that makes its bars and confections in its own workshops. A small free museum traces cacao history from the Maya to 20th-century industrial chocolatiers, with a collection of antique molds and vintage advertisements. The main store adjoins downtown and serves as a Notre Dame fan rallying point before home games. Hot chocolate prepared to order is the winter specialty; salted butter caramel sells year-round and represents the house's most renowned in-house production.

days_plan.itin_title: Indiana Circuit: Dunes, Caves, and Cities
Detroit to Chicago: Great Lakes Road Trip - 8 Days

map_keywords: University of Notre Dame Golden Dome Main Building Indiana
Basilica Sacred Heart Notre Dame campus Indiana
Studebaker National Museum 201 Chapin Street South Bend
Grotto Our Lady Lourdes Notre Dame replica campus
East Race Waterway St Joseph River South Bend

places.name: South Padre Island

visits.text: Sea Turtle Inc. is a nonprofit organization founded in 1977 by Ila Fox Loetscher, known locally as 'The Turtle Lady of South Padre Island'. Initially created in her backyard garden to protect Kemp's ridley turtles - a species on the brink of extinction - the organization moved in 1999 to its current facility and now treats over 100 turtles per year. During the great Texas freeze of February 2021, Sea Turtle Inc. rescued several thousand hypothermic turtles in a matter of days. You can visit Tuesday through Sunday; the daily 'Turtle Talks' at 11:30 AM are highly informative. If you're there in summer, inquire about baby turtle releases on the beach.

Isla Blanca Park occupies the southern tip of South Padre Island, with a fishing pier, a well-equipped beach and a direct view of the Brazos Santiago Pass - the channel through which large ships enter Laguna Madre. It's also one of the best kitesurfing and windsurfing spots on the Texas coast thanks to steady Gulf winds.

Palo Alto Battlefield National Historical Park, about 12 miles north of Brownsville, preserves the field of the first battle of the Mexican-American War, fought on May 8, 1846. General Zachary Taylor's 2,300-strong U.S. Army faced a 3,700-soldier Mexican force. The battle marks the beginning of a conflict that would result in Mexico ceding one-third of its territory to the United States - a war that deeply divided American opinion. The NPS visitor center offers an effective introductory film; don't miss the battlefield map to locate troop movements.

Brownsville, on the Rio Grande, faces Matamoros on the Mexican side. The city retains a 19th-century historic downtown with Spanish colonial houses and shaded plazas recalling its past as a prosperous border city. You can walk along the river's edge - the Rio Grande here is surprisingly narrow, barely wider than a canal.

activities.text: South Padre Island's beach offers 34 miles of fine white sand and turquoise waters at 82-86°F from June through September. The island's north section, less crowded than the city's waterfront, gives a sense of what Padre Island looked like before development. Arrive early in the morning to have the beach to yourself before 10 AM.

Laguna Madre, a 130-mile-long hypersaline lagoon separating South Padre Island from the mainland, is one of the few hypersaline lagoon systems in the world. Operators offer kayak or stand-up paddle outings in this calm, shallow water frequented by dolphins, stingrays and brown pelicans. Leave early - dolphins are most active between 7 and 10 AM.

Operators from South Padre Island run boat tours to observe bottlenose dolphins in the Brazos Santiago channel, the passage between the island and the coast. These dolphins are permanent residents of the lagoon and channel - observation odds exceed 90% according to local operators. Late afternoon, the raking light on the water makes for easier photography.

The bay-side sunset on South Padre Island is one of the most beautiful day's endings on the Texas coast. Laguna Madre takes on orange and pink hues for 20 to 30 minutes before turning violet. Head to the island's west side (Bay Side) an hour before official sunset - in summer, count on around 8 PM. It's the perfect place to wrap up this 9-day road trip before heading to Harlingen airport (40 min) or McAllen (1h) the next morning.

days_plan.itin_title: Gulf Coast: Lafayette to South Padre Island - 9 days
Gulf Coast: Lafayette to South Padre Island - 9 days

South Padre Island sunset bay side
Brownsville historic downtown Texas Mexico border
Palo Alto Battlefield National Historical Park

visits.text: Discover downtown Spartanburg's revitalized urban core, where recent development has created a walkable district suited to relaxed exploration. The compact downtown divides into distinct neighborhoods, each offering its own character and dining options. Historic architecture mixes with contemporary additions in this evolving Upstate community.

activities.text: Experience the Hub City's emerging cultural scene, where local establishments reflect regional tastes in comfortable settings. The downtown area has transformed into a destination for travelers seeking authentic small-city Southern atmosphere. Spartanburg's position along major routes makes it an ideal stopping point between coastal and mountain destinations.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

visits.text: Long drive west across the Iowa prairies—3.5 hours of corn and soybean fields stretching to the horizon. You arrive at the Iowa Great Lakes, the state's resort area. West Okoboji Lake is renowned for its clarity—it's one of only three blue water lakes in the world formed by glaciers, along with Lake Geneva and a lake in Manitoba. Settle onto the beach at Arnolds Park in early afternoon.

Arnolds Park Amusement Park, open since 1889, is Iowa's oldest operating amusement park. This isn't Disneyland—it's a retro lakeside carnival with wooden roller coasters, vintage rides, and a "1950s American summer" vibe. Next door, the Iowa Great Lakes Maritime Museum traces the region's nautical history.

activities.text: Late in the day, drive around the lake or rent a pontoon to cruise the water. The sunset over West Okoboji is one of Iowa's most beautiful—locals compare it (with considerable enthusiasm) to those of Lake Como.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa and Midwest Road Trip: Great Plains Loop

places.name: Spring Green

visits.text: Taliesin - Frank Lloyd Wright's residence, studio, and 800-acre estate in the Driftless Area hills. The name comes from Welsh meaning 'shining brow' - Wright built his house on the hillside, not the summit, because 'if you build on top of the hill, you lose the hill.' Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2019, it's Wisconsin's only publicly accessible UNESCO site. Budget 2-4 hours depending on the tour chosen.
Hillside Home School (1903) - The school Wright designed for his aunts, with its enormous assembly hall and diamond-paned windows. Later became the Taliesin apprentice architects' workshop.
Romeo and Juliet Windmill (1896) - One of Wright's early commissions. The structure of interlocking diamond and octagon was so bold his uncle bet it wouldn't stand. It's still there, 130 years later.
Riverview Terrace Café - Wright's last restaurant design in the world. Panoramic windows overlooking the Wisconsin River. Local cuisine, relaxed atmosphere. Make it a lunch stop.

activities.text: Taliesin Guided Tour - Four tour options, from 1h to 4h. The 'Highlights' tour (2h) offers the best time-to-content ratio: you visit the school, Wright's residence, and studio. The 'Estate' tour (4h) covers the entire grounds including Midway Barn and gardens. Booking essential.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

map_keywords: Taliesin Frank Lloyd Wright Spring Green

visits.text: Zion Canyon Scenic Drive - The 6-mile road climbs into the canyon via free shuttle bus (mandatory April through October). Navajo Sandstone cliffs soar 2,000 feet on either side. First stop: Court of the Patriarchs, three monoliths named Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob by a Methodist minister in 1916. The view from the platform is one of the park's most photographed scenes.

Emerald Pools - Three tiered pools fed by springs seeping through the sandstone. The Lower Pool is an easy 20-minute walk. If you have the energy, continue to the Upper Pool - the waterfall drops 100 feet from a ledge into a red rock amphitheater. Allow 1.5 hours for the full loop.

Continue by shuttle to the Temple of Sinawava at the end of the road. This natural temple takes its name from the Paiute coyote-god. Here the canyon narrows to just 20 feet wide and 2,000 feet deep - the gateway to The Narrows. The Riverside Walk, a paved 1-mile trail along the Virgin River, leads you to the canyon entrance. Even without wading in, the stroll is magnificent.

Angels Landing OR The Narrows - Today is built around a choice. Both hikes are long and demanding; doing both in one day is only realistic for highly experienced hikers. Choose the one that suits you. Option 1: Angels Landing. The trail climbs 1,500 feet over 2.5 miles one-way. Walter's Wiggles - 21 steep switchbacks carved into the rock in 1926 - haul you up to Scout Lookout. From there, the final section follows a narrow ridge with chains as the only safety measure and sheer drops on both sides - a 1,500-foot fall. A permit is mandatory (online lottery system). If you don't have a permit or suffer from vertigo, Scout Lookout already offers spectacular canyon views. Start early in the morning.

Weeping Rock - After the effort of Angels Landing, this short 0.5-mile trail leads to a rock overhang where water seeps constantly. The water you see dripping is actually rainfall from 1,200 years ago, filtered slowly through the sandstone. It's cool, refreshing, and takes 20 minutes.

activities.text: In late afternoon, wander through the village of Springdale. Art galleries and outdoor patios line the road leading to the park entrance. If you're planning to do The Narrows tomorrow, stop by an outfitter to rent neoprene socks, a walking stick, and waterproof pants - essential gear if the water is cold or the current strong.

Option 2: The Narrows from the bottom - If you prefer canyons to heights, or if you don't have an Angels Landing permit, enter The Narrows from the Temple of Sinawava. You'll walk directly in the Virgin River between 2,000-foot walls just a few yards apart. No permit needed for the bottom-up out-and-back route - go as far as you like and turn around. Allow 3 to 5 hours depending on your ambition. Warning: flash floods are deadly. Don't attempt this hike if storms are forecast. If you're a strong hiker and leave at dawn, you can attempt both - but plan for a 10-hour day and bring plenty of water.

If you have time in late afternoon, take the Zion-Mount Carmel Highway eastward. The 1.1-mile tunnel carved through the sandstone between 1927 and 1930 is an engineering feat - windows cut into the rock offer dizzying views into the canyon. On the other side, don't miss Checkerboard Mesa, a sandstone dome striped with horizontal and vertical lines that resembles a giant chessboard.
