visits.text: Begin your Appalachian Trail experience at Springer Mountain, the legendary southern terminus of the 2,190-mile footpath stretching to Maine. Georgia's 79-mile section winds through misty forests, wildflower meadows, rhododendron tunnels, and offers spectacular panoramic overlooks. Standing at the bronze plaque marking Mile Zero creates a profound connection to one of America's greatest hiking traditions.

Trek to Blood Mountain, the highest point on Georgia's Appalachian Trail at 4,458 feet, where ancient stone shelter ruins and sweeping ridge views reward determined hikers. The challenging terrain passes through diverse ecosystems supporting black bears, deer, salamanders, and countless songbirds. Backcountry camping sites offer stargazing far from light pollution.

activities.text: Visit the legendary Mountain Crossings outfitter at Neel Gap, the only building the Appalachian Trail passes directly through. The famous 'shoe tree' outside displays worn-out hiking boots left by through-hikers, many containing handwritten notes and memories. Detour to misty Long Creek Falls for a serene waterfall tucked into the forest.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

places.name: Springfield

visits.text: Gemini Giant (Wilmington, IL) - This 28-foot fiberglass giant holding a Gemini rocket is the first of the 'Muffler Men' you'll encounter on the Route. Built in the 1960s to attract motorists, it stands in front of a former restaurant. Quick photo stop.

Pontiac Murals - Don't pass through without stopping - this small Illinois town has transformed its walls into an open-air gallery. Over 20 murals tell the story of Route 66. The Route 66 Hall of Fame Museum is right next door - free admission.

Abraham Lincoln Home (Springfield) - The only house Lincoln ever owned, preserved by the National Park Service. Pick up a free ticket at the visitor center for the guided tour - first come, first served. The entire neighborhood is preserved as it was in the 1860s.

Gateway Arch (St. Louis) - This 630-foot stainless steel arch, the tallest structure in the United States when it opened in 1965, marks the Gateway to the West. You can ride to the top in tiny capsule-like trams (book online). Below, the museum traces westward expansion. Even if you don't go up, the view from the park along the Mississippi is impressive.

Chain of Rocks Bridge - Stop on this abandoned 1929 bridge over the Mississippi - it has a quirk: it makes a 22-degree bend right in the middle. Now reserved for pedestrians and cyclists, it's one of the most photogenic spots on the Route. Free entry.

Meramec Caverns (Stanton, MO) - These caves, 60 miles southwest of St. Louis, were supposedly used as a hideout by outlaw Jesse James according to local legend. The guided tour lasts 1h20 and descends 60 feet underground. Temperature stays constant at 60°F - bring a jacket.

activities.text: Lunch at the Cozy Dog Drive In - This is where the corn dog (deep-fried cornmeal-battered sausage on a stick) was invented in 1946. The restaurant is still run by the same family and the walls are covered with Route 66 memorabilia.
Frozen treat at Ted Drewes - This St. Louis institution since 1929 serves frozen custard so thick the server flips the cup upside down to prove it won't fall out. The line is part of the experience.

days_plan.itin_title: Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica
Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica
Ozarks & Branson: From Missouri Hills to Arkansas Art Galleries

map_keywords: Chain of Rocks Bridge St Louis
Ted Drewes Frozen Custard St Louis

visits.text: Castillo de San Marcos — Explore America's oldest masonry fort constructed 1672-1695 from coquina limestone by Spanish colonial forces. The star-shaped fortress withstood multiple sieges with its unique shell-rock walls that absorbed cannonballs rather than shattering.

St. Augustine Lighthouse — Climb 219 steps up this 1874 lighthouse for panoramic views of America's oldest continuously occupied European settlement. The 50-meter tower's first-order Fresnel lens still guides ships along Florida's northeast coast.

Colonial Quarter — Walk through four centuries of St. Augustine history in this living history museum. Costumed interpreters demonstrate blacksmithing, leather working, and colonial crafts in reconstructed 16th-18th century buildings.

St. George Street — Stroll this pedestrian-only colonial thoroughfare lined with Spanish architecture housing galleries, restaurants, and shops. The brick-paved street retains its historic character with balconies, courtyards, and gas lanterns.

activities.text: Fountain of Youth Archaeological Park — Visit the legendary spring where Ponce de León supposedly searched for eternal youth in 1513. The 15-acre park preserves the Timucua Native American village site and Spanish colonial artifacts.

Ghost tour of historic district — Join an evening walking tour through shadowy streets hearing tales of pirates, soldiers, and spirits haunting America's oldest city. Guides recount 450 years of history in the candlelit colonial quarter.

Sunset at the Old City Gates — Watch the sun set through the reconstructed 1739 entrance gates to the colonial city. The coquina pillars frame views down St. George Street as the historic district glows in golden light.

days_plan.itin_title: Florida's Diverse Coast: Everglades Adventure to Miami Art Deco
The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

places.name: St Charles Mo

visits.text: Down at Bishop's Landing on the Missouri River, the Lewis & Clark Boat House and Museum keeps the full-scale operational replicas of the keelboat and both pirogues that carried the Corps out of this town on May 21, 1804. The keelboat was built by volunteers from the Discovery Expedition of St. Charles using period-accurate tools and methods reconstructed from the expedition's journal descriptions — the original construction plans were never preserved. Clark's journal entry that day is brief: the crew had partied until 5 a.m. the night before and could only manage a few miles upriver. Open Monday to Saturday 10 to 5, Sunday noon to 5; paid entry. The 15-foot bronze of Lewis, Clark, and Seaman just outside is the tallest Lewis and Clark monument on the entire trail.

Federal-style brick buildings line Historic Main Street, and the one at 200 South Main deserves particular attention: the First Missouri State Capitol, where the new state's legislature met from 1821 to 1826 before Jefferson City was ready. A guided tour of the 11-room building covers the debates over slavery, states' rights, and taxation that shaped early Missouri — all while commerce hummed in the Peck Brothers dry goods store downstairs. Free admission; tours run throughout the day.

South Main Street runs directly alongside the Missouri River, and the Katy Trail — 240 miles of converted rail corridor — begins right here. Even if you only walk half a mile north from Frontier Park, you're on the same riverbank the Corps navigated in 1804. The trail is packed dirt and crushed limestone, flat and easy, with cottonwoods overhead and the river in earshot. Bike rentals are available from local outfitters near the trailhead; a one-hour pedal in either direction rewards you with Missouri River bottomland scenery.

activities.text: Historic Main Street has 25-plus restaurants, and the strip from South Main to Second Street covers everything from old-school Missouri bar food to riverside patios. Eat dinner outside if weather allows — this is one of the few spots on the trail where you can watch the Missouri River while you eat. Lewis and Clark were guests of honor at dinners here the night before departure; several restaurants stay open past 9 p.m. on weekends.

Route 94 west of St. Charles follows the Missouri River into Defiance and Augusta through Missouri wine country, with hillsides planted in Norton, Vignoles, and Traminette. Lewis and Clark passed this exact landscape in 1804. Chandler Hill Winery in Defiance and Sugar Creek Winery in Augusta are the two closest tasting rooms; the round-trip scenic drive takes about 90 minutes with one stop. The road itself is as much the point as the wine.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: Lewis Clark Boat House Museum St. Charles Missouri
First Missouri State Capitol St. Charles
Historic Main Street St. Charles Missouri

places.name: St Francisville

visits.text: The Myrtles Plantation was built in 1796 by General David Bradford, a Whiskey Rebellion insurgent who fled Pennsylvania after George Washington ordered his arrest. The Creole cottage-style house, its 22 rooms and long veranda draped with live oaks covered in Spanish moss make it one of the most cited addresses in American paranormal tourism guides. The legend of the slave Chloe - whose ear was allegedly cut off before she poisoned the master's family - isn't historically documented, but the candlelit nocturnal guides don't hold back. The only verified murder on the property is that of William Winter in 1871, shot on the veranda and said to have died climbing the 17 stairs.

Rosedown Plantation, built in 1835, is among the South's best-preserved with its 28 acres of French-designed formal gardens - oak alleys, camellia beds, and cast-iron statues imported from Paris. The main house and its period outbuildings remained in the same family for five generations before being purchased by the State of Louisiana in 2003. If you visit in spring, the blooming azaleas and camellias make these gardens among the Deep South's most beautiful.

The Audubon State Historic Site houses Oakley House, the plantation where naturalist John James Audubon lived and worked from June to October 1821, during which he drew 32 of the birds in his monumental Birds of America. The surrounding woods are still frequented by the species he observed there - bring binoculars and allow 30 minutes walking the trails behind the house, particularly rich at daybreak.

activities.text: Hiking in Tunica Hills on the Clark Creek trail: 6 km of trails winding through forested gorges with waterfalls and steep ravines, terrain exceedingly rare in nearly flat Louisiana. The best of this nature is concentrated in the first 3 kilometers from the Pond Store Road parking area.

days_plan.itin_title: The Black Belt: Atlanta to New Orleans Through the Deep South
Deep Louisiana: Jazz, Bayous & Plantations - 9-Day Loop from New Orleans
The Deep South via Mississippi: Shreveport to New Orleans - 10 Days

map_keywords: Audubon State Historic Site St Francisville

visits.text: Charlevoix - A driving day with three stops, covering about 125 miles total between Traverse City and St. Ignace. First stop: Charlevoix, a harbor town wedged between Lake Michigan and Lake Charlevoix, 45 minutes from Traverse City. It's worth the detour for its "Mushroom Houses" - 28 stone cottages with undulating roofs and whimsical mushroom shapes, built between 1919 and 1973 by Earl Young, a self-taught builder who never obtained an architecture degree. He worked without blueprints, using limestone blocks and boulders hauled from Lake Michigan. Pick up a walking tour map at the visitor center on Mason Street - the finest examples cluster along Park Avenue and Clinton Street.

Petoskey - Gaslight District - Continue on US-31 to Petoskey, a resort town perched on Little Traverse Bay. The historic Gaslight District, listed on the National Register of Historic Places, contains 278 period buildings and about 170 shops and restaurants. Look for the Ernest Hemingway statue - he spent summers here in his youth and frequented the City Park Grill, which still operates today. Don't leave without collecting a "Petoskey stone" from the beach - this 350-million-year-old coral fossil is Michigan's official state stone.

Mackinac Bridge - After Petoskey, reach Mackinaw City to cross the Mackinac Bridge, nicknamed the "Mighty Mac." Opened in 1957, this suspension bridge measures 5 miles total (26,372 feet). Its suspension span between anchorages (8,614 feet) makes it the longest suspension bridge in the Western Hemisphere by this measure - though its main span between towers (3,800 feet) ranks third in North America, behind the Golden Gate and Verrazzano-Narrows. Engineer David Steinman designed it to withstand violent strait winds: the deck can shift laterally 35 feet under extreme gusts, and the roadway surface is open-grated to let wind pass through. Five workers died during the 42 months of construction. The crossing takes about 10 minutes by car - enjoy views of Lake Michigan to the left and Lake Huron to the right.

Mackinac Island - Take the ferry from St. Ignace (15-minute crossing) to reach this island where cars have been banned since the 1920s. Everything happens on foot, by bicycle, or in horse-drawn carriages. The island welcomes nearly a million visitors per year but counts only 600 permanent residents. Upon docking, you'll arrive in a Victorian-fronted village lined with fudge shops - it's been the local specialty for over a century.

Grand Hotel - Perched on a bluff overlooking the straits, this 1887 hotel boasts the world's longest porch: 660 feet long, lined with rocking chairs. Five U.S. presidents have stayed here. Thomas Edison gave the first public demonstration of his phonograph here. The film "Somewhere in Time" starring Christopher Reeve was shot here in 1980. At minimum, climb up to the porch for the view - a small admission fee is charged if you're not a hotel guest.

Fort Mackinac - This fort built by the British in 1780 towers over the harbor from a limestone bluff. It played a role in the War of 1812 between Americans and British. You'll find 14 restored period buildings, cannon-firing demonstrations in season, and panoramic views of the straits and Mackinac Bridge in the distance.

Arch Rock - Continuing around the island on foot or by bike, don't miss this natural limestone arch perched 150 feet above Lake Huron. Formed by erosion, it measures roughly 50 feet across. A 207-step staircase leads up from the coastal road.

activities.text: Petoskey Stone Hunting at Petoskey State Park - Before leaving the area, stop at Petoskey State Park. The beach pebbles hide Petoskey stones - hexagonal Devonian coral fossils. Wet the stones to reveal the pattern - it shows up better on a damp surface. Allow 30 to 45 minutes for a beach stroll.

Bike Loop Around the Island - The coastal road runs 8.2 miles and traces Lake Huron around the island's entire perimeter. It's flat, easy, and the scenery alternates between limestone cliffs, forest, and beaches. Bike rentals are available near the dock. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours without stops.

Sunset from Straits State Park - Back in St. Ignace, head to this small state park to watch the sun set behind the towers of Mackinac Bridge. The silhouette of the illuminated bridge at twilight is one of Michigan's most photogenic images.

days_plan.itin_title: Michigan Great Lakes Road Trip: Dunes and Shorelines
Michigan Great Lakes Road Trip: Dunes and Shorelines

places.name: St Louis

visits.text: Six hundred and thirty feet of stainless steel curves over the Mississippi, and you feel it before you see it — the Gateway Arch pulls the eye straight up from the moment you step into Gateway Arch National Park. Designed by Eero Saarinen in 1948 and built between 1963 and 1965, it stands as the tallest arch in the world, equal in height and width. Below it, the Museum of Westward Expansion connects the Arch directly to the Lewis and Clark Expedition, which launched from this region in 1804. Book tram tickets to the observation deck well in advance — the tiny capsule ride is part of the experience, and views stretch 30 miles on a clear day.

The green dome of the Old Courthouse is impossible to miss from the Arch, and the building earns a stop on its own terms. On April 6, 1846, Dred and Harriet Scott filed for their freedom in this building — one of more than 300 freedom suits filed here before 1860. Their case reached the Supreme Court in 1857, where Chief Justice Roger Taney ruled that enslaved people were not citizens; a decision many scholars believe accelerated the Civil War. The courthouse, completed in its current form in 1864, is open daily 9 to 5 with free admission. The mock trial program run by rangers brings the Scotts' case back to life.

Cross the Mississippi to Hartford, Illinois, and you're standing at the spot where the Corps of Discovery spent the winter of December 1803 to May 14, 1804. The Lewis & Clark State Historic Site holds a full-scale replica of the 55-foot keelboat, cut away on one side so you can see exactly how 200-plus bundles, barrels, and boxes were packed for the journey. Six galleries in the 14,000-square-foot interpretive center cover the preparation phase in detail that the Arch museum doesn't reach. Plan 45 minutes minimum — the confluence viewing area outside, where the Missouri meets the Mississippi, is worth lingering over.

activities.text: Walk the levee along the Mississippi south of the Arch after dark and you get the full scale of Saarinen's curve lit against the sky. During the day, Gateway Arch Riverboats run one-hour narrated cruises on replica paddlewheelers — a gentle way to see St. Louis from the water and understand why this bend in the river made the city. Boats depart from the levee just north of the Arch; check the schedule as departures vary by season.

The City Museum is not a museum — it's a multi-story playground built from salvaged architectural fragments, with caves, tunnels, rooftop Ferris wheel, and a ten-story slide. Built by artist Bob Cassilly in a former shoe factory, it opened in 1997 and has no real equivalent anywhere in the country. Two miles from the Arch; open until midnight on weekends. If you're travelling with kids or simply want to end Day 1 somewhere genuinely strange, allow two hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Chicago to New Orleans Road Trip: Route 66 & the Mississippi
Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: Camp Dubois Lewis Clark Hartford Illinois

visits.text: St. Petersburg - The Sunshine City: This bayside community consistently breaks records for consecutive sunny days, combining beach-town relaxation with an increasingly sophisticated arts scene.

The Dalí Museum - The World's Most Comprehensive Salvador Dalí Collection: This striking waterfront museum houses over 2,400 works by the surrealist master, including seven of his 18 monumental 'masterwork' paintings.
St. Pete Murals - An Open-Air Gallery: Over 700 murals transform the city into one of America's largest outdoor art exhibitions, with new works constantly appearing on buildings throughout downtown.
The Don CeSar - The Pink Palace of St. Pete Beach: This iconic 1928 resort, painted its distinctive Pepto-Bismol pink, has hosted everyone from F. Scott Fitzgerald to Franklin D. Roosevelt.
Fort De Soto Park - Five Interconnected Islands of Paradise: This 1,136-acre park on Tampa Bay's southern tip offers camping, fishing, hiking, and some of the most beautiful beaches in Florida.

activities.text: St. Pete Beach Water Sports - Gulf Coast Adventures: The calm, warm waters of the Gulf of Mexico provide ideal conditions for paddleboarding, parasailing, and kayaking.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

visits.text: Explore St. Simons Island, the largest of Georgia's Golden Isles, where wide golden beaches, moss-draped oaks, shimmering salt marshes, and charming village atmosphere create quintessential coastal Georgia scenery. The historic lighthouse dating to 1872 offers panoramic views from its tower after climbing 129 steps. Over 30 miles of bike paths wind past tree spirits carved into ancient oaks by local artists.

Visit Fort Frederica National Monument, the substantial ruins of a colonial-era British fortification established in 1736 to defend against Spanish incursion from Florida. The moss-covered walls and archaeological excavations reveal 18th-century military life on the frontier. Cannon's Point Preserve offers hiking through wild maritime forest where salty marsh aromas drift on ocean breezes.

activities.text: Relax on sunlit East Beach, browse boutiques and galleries in the charming Pier Village, and watch spectacular sunsets paint the marshlands in brilliant colors. The island's laid-back atmosphere invites visitors to slow down and embrace the gentle rhythms of Georgia's coast.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

visits.text: Jim Gallien drops him off at the beginning of the Stampede Trail, tries to dissuade him but Chris ventures alone into the taiga toward the abandoned bus.

days_plan.itin_title: Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey

places.name: Stanley

visits.text: Stanley has a permanent population of roughly 70 people but three designated national scenic byways converge here, and on summer weekends the population multiplies tenfold. The town sits at 6,260 feet in a bowl-shaped basin below the Sawtooths - 57 peaks over 10,000 feet filling the western sky. This bowl shape is also why Stanley records overnight lows below freezing on average 294 days a year and was measured as the coldest place in the contiguous U.S. more often than any other weather station between 1995 and 2015. The Stanley Historical Museum, housed in the 1933 Valley Creek Ranger Station, gives the historical context in 20 minutes.

Five miles south on Highway 75, Redfish Lake spreads out at 6,500 feet below the serrated granite of the Sawtooth range - one of the clearest alpine lakes in the American West, fed almost entirely by snowmelt. The Redfish Visitor Center and Gallery at the lake's edge is the oldest National Forest visitor center in the contiguous United States, built in the early 1960s. In late summer and fall, sockeye salmon complete a 900-mile migration from the Pacific to spawn here. The Redfish Lake Lodge runs a sunset wine-and-appetizer lake cruise in summer - reserve a seat in advance, it sells out.

In December 2017, the Central Idaho Dark Sky Reserve became the first of its kind in the United States - 1,416 square miles encompassing the entire Sawtooth NRA, from Ketchum to northwest of Stanley. The designation requires that area businesses and municipalities limit light pollution, which means that on clear moonless nights over Stanley, the Milky Way appears as a textured band with visible lanes of intergalactic dust. No equipment needed. Just get away from the streetlights.

Eleven miles west on Highway 21, the Iron Creek Trailhead is the launch point for the Sawtooth Lake Trail - arguably the best single day hike in the range. The route climbs 4.3 miles through open forest and then granite scree to Sawtooth Lake, the largest alpine lake in the Sawtooth Wilderness, sitting under the sheer face of Mount Regan. The Sawtooth Wilderness was established in 1972 and covers 217,000 acres; Thompson Peak at 10,751 feet is the highest point. Expect snow on the upper trail before mid-June. The parking area fills before 9 AM on summer weekends.

The 3-mile side road to Stanley Lake branches off Highway 21 and dead-ends at one of the most photographed lakes in the Sawtooths - a glacially carved basin directly below 9,860-foot McGown Peak, whose reflection dominates the surface on calm mornings. Motorized boats are limited here, so the water is quiet. This is also the spot, at mile 117 on Highway 21, where the Sawtooth peaks first come into view for drivers approaching from Boise - a good indicator of what's been visible behind you all day.

Thirty miles south of Stanley on Highway 75, the Galena Summit pullout at 8,701 feet gives the best single view on this entire route: the Sawtooth Valley unrolling northward below, the Salmon River as a thread of silver at the valley floor, and on clear days, a wall of peaks extending to the horizon in every direction. The overlook is named for Senator Frank Church and his wife Bethine - Church championed the 1980 Central Idaho Wilderness Act that protected much of what you're looking at.

activities.text: Join one of the Stanley-based outfitters for a half-day on the Salmon River. The Upper Salmon runs Class III and IV rapids through a canyon flanked by the Sawtooths; June brings the highest water (snowmelt-fueled Class IV), while July and August mellow to Class III. Sawtooth Adventure Company runs full-day guided paddle-raft and inflatable kayak trips for all ability levels. At 425 miles, the Salmon is one of the longest free-flowing rivers in the lower 48 - this stretch is its headwaters.

Snake Pit Hot Spring sits at the edge of town and costs nothing - rock pools fed by a thermal spring, with a view of the valley and the Sawtooth peaks. A small herd of deer routinely wanders through at dusk. Several other springs are within a 30-minute drive on Highway 75 toward Ketchum; the Mountain Village Lodge in Stanley also has access to commercial hot spring pools if you want facilities and privacy.

Rent a kayak or paddleboard at Redfish Lake and spend the morning on the water. The Bench Lakes Trail begins on the west shore and leads to four smaller alpine lakes in about 4 miles round trip. For something different, the Redfish Lake Corrals - operated by Mystic Saddle Ranch since 1969 - runs guided horseback rides through the Ponderosa pines along the lakeshore and into the wilderness above. Either way, plan to be back at the lake by late afternoon when the alpenglow hits.

Stanley's handful of restaurants punch well above their weight. The Stanley Baking Co. handles breakfast and lunch, with cinnamon rolls that are discussed with unusual seriousness by people who have driven three hours for them. The Stanley Supper Club handles dinner. After eating, the Stanley to Redfish Lake Trail - a 5-mile paved and gravel path built with Sawtooth Society grant funds - makes a good evening walk.

days_plan.itin_title: Idaho: Sawtooth Mountains, Sun Valley & Craters of the Moon
Idaho: Sawtooth Mountains, Sun Valley & Craters of the Moon

map_keywords: Sawtooth Lake Trail Iron Creek Stanley

places.name: Steamboat Springs Co

visits.text: Steamboat Springs owes its name to early French explorers who, hearing a spring bubbling along the Yampa River, thought they glimpsed the paddle wheels of a steamboat. The spring disappeared during railroad construction in 1908, but the town retains its character as a real Colorado mountain town—not a ski-resort boutique village, but a real town with locals' bars on Lincoln Avenue.

Strawberry Park Hot Springs, 6 miles north of town, are carved into rock at 160°F at the source and naturally cooled to 100–108°F in the pools. The setting is wild—forest, boulders, steam in the cold air. Reservations required on weekdays, first-come on weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: Colorado-Wyoming Road Trip: National Parks, Rockies & Wide Open Spaces

map_keywords: Strawberry Park Hot Springs Steamboat Springs
Old Town Hot Springs Steamboat Springs

visits.text: Stillwater—Founded in 1843, this is Minnesota's oldest town. Walk down Main Street toward the St. Croix River: 19th-century brick buildings now house bookstores, antique shops, and restaurants. Don't miss the Stillwater Lift Bridge (1931), a working vertical lift bridge that spans the river into Wisconsin.

Walk down to the banks of the St. Croix—this clear-running river, a tributary of the Mississippi, is designated a Wild and Scenic River. The forested bluffs on both sides give the valley the feel of a miniature fjord.

activities.text: Canoe or kayak the St. Croix River—outfitters offer rentals right on the water. The current is gentle, the banks wild. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a relaxed float.
Stillwater is known for its bookstores. Step inside one—some occupy vaulted brick cellars. If you're a wine enthusiast, several wineries have set up in the valley.

days_plan.itin_title: Minnesota in 10 Days: Twin Cities, Lakes, and Lake Superior's North Shore

visits.text: You pull up to a sandstone building that is literally made from Route 66 itself. Roy Rives bought the entire supply of sandstone scraped up during the road's own paving for five dollars and used it to build this cafe, which opened August 4, 1939. It served as a 24-hour Greyhound bus stop through World War II and kept going through decommissioning, a 1999 tornado, and a 2008 fire that gutted everything but the stone walls. Current owner Dawn Welch, who took over in 1993, inspired the character of Sally Carrera - the blue Porsche attorney - in Pixar's 2006 film Cars, after the research team stopped here in 2001. The original neon sign, restored in 2025, still glows over the entrance.

You continue west to Arcadia, where farmer William Harrison Odor built a perfectly round barn in 1898 using native bur oak boards soaked while still green and bent into the curves needed for the walls and rafters. His son later explained the design: 'My father figured if they had something round, it would hit and go around it instead of through it.' When Route 66 came through Arcadia in 1926, the barn became a landmark. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1977 and restored in 1992, it's free to enter and open daily. Half a mile west, POPS opened in 2007 with a 66-foot LED soda bottle sign weighing 4 tons - over 700 varieties of bottled soda on glass shelves arranged by color, plus burgers and milkshakes.

days_plan.itin_title: Oklahoma Route 66 & Native Heritage: Tulsa to OKC

visits.text: Sturgeon Bay - Door County's 'gateway' is also a shipbuilding town. The canal cutting the peninsula in two connects Green Bay to Lake Michigan. Downtown is pleasant with art galleries, restaurants, and shops.

Door County Maritime Museum - The history of shipbuilding and Great Lakes navigation. Models, tools, and shipwreck stories (Door County owes its name to the dangerous currents of 'Death's Door,' the passage between the peninsula and Washington Island).

Cana Island Lighthouse - 30 minutes north of Sturgeon Bay. Cross the gravel causeway (sometimes flooded) to this 9-acre island. The 89-foot lighthouse dates from 1869. Climb the 97 steps for the view. Door County has 11 lighthouses total - more than any other Wisconsin county.

activities.text: Wine and Cider Tasting - Door County has several wineries and cideries. The peninsula's cherries produce fruity wines and easy-drinking ciders. Ask to try cherry wine, the local specialty.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

visits.text: Prepare for the hike to Havasupai—one of the most spectacular and difficult-to-access places in the United States. From Hualapai Hilltop, the trail first descends 2,600 feet in 1.5 miles of switchbacks, then follows a sandy wash for 7 miles to the village of Supai—the country's most isolated town, 700 residents, accessible only on foot, horseback, or helicopter. Mail still arrives by pack mule.

Two miles past the village, Navajo Falls appears on your left—a series of turquoise waterfalls amid red sandstone. Then Havasu Falls, the star: a 100-foot turquoise waterfall tumbling into a pool surrounded by travertine. The color comes from calcium carbonate dissolved in the water. This is the image you've seen all over Instagram—and in person, it's even better.
The campground is just past Havasu Falls, along Havasu Creek. Set up your tent anywhere in the designated area—first come, best spot. The creek water is 70°F year-round.

Hike down to Mooney Falls, half a mile from the campground. It's Havasupai's tallest waterfall—200 feet of freefall. To access it, you descend through a passage in the rock with chains and metal ladders bolted to the wall. It's steep, slippery, and vertiginous—but the turquoise pool at the bottom is worth the effort.

Third day at Havasupai—savor it. Explore Fifty Foot Falls, a more discreet waterfall between the village and Havasu Falls, often overlooked by hurried hikers. The pool is smaller and more intimate. The rest of the day, swim in Havasu Creek, read by the water, photograph the changing light on the cliffs. This is why you came.

activities.text: A permit is mandatory—3-night minimum, about $455 per person. Reservations open February 1st and sell out in minutes. No day hiking allowed. Leave before dawn to avoid heat—carry at least 3 liters of water. No services on the trail. This is true backcountry.

If you're a strong hiker, continue 3.5 miles beyond Mooney Falls to Beaver Falls—a series of terraced waterfalls with natural swimming pools. The trail follows Havasu Creek with several stream crossings. Allow 4 hours round-trip from the campground. The rest of the time, swim in the pools—the 70°F water is perfect after hiking.

Mentally prepare for tomorrow: the climb back to Hualapai Hilltop is 10 miles with 2,600 feet of elevation gain. Leave before dawn—heat in the wash is brutal after 9am. Some hikers book a helicopter for the ascent ($80 per person), but seats are limited and flights don't start until 10am.

days_plan.itin_title: Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons

places.name: Superior

visits.text: Big Manitou Falls - Wisconsin's highest waterfall awaits you 20 minutes south of Superior. The Black River plunges 165 feet (50 meters) into a basalt gorge carved 1.2 billion years ago by lava flows. Native Americans called it 'Gitchee Manitou' - the voice of the Great Spirit. Multiple viewing platforms offer unobstructed views from both sides of the gorge. Arrive early morning to beat the crowds and catch the best light.

Little Manitou Falls - Two miles upstream, the river splits into two channels and drops 31 feet (9 meters) across a wide basalt shelf. Less dramatic than its big sister, but more accessible: you can scramble down to the pool to feel the spray. Watch for slippery rocks.

Interfalls Lake and Beach - Between the two falls, the Black River slows and forms this man-made lake bordered by pines and spruces. The sandy beach (trucked in from Lake Superior shores in the 1930s by the Civilian Conservation Corps) makes for a pleasant swimming break.

CCC Buildings - The Civilian Conservation Corps transformed this park between 1935 and 1941. The cut-stone shelter, nature center, and former bathhouse still stand. Interpretive signs tell the story of these young workers who came primarily from Illinois.

activities.text: Big Manitou - Little Manitou Loop Trail - This 4-mile trail follows the Black River and passes both waterfalls. Moderate elevation gain (215 feet). In fall, maples and birches frame the gorge in red and gold.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

map_keywords: Big Manitou Falls Pattison State Park

visits.text: Talkeetna Historic District - This charming frontier village at the confluence of three rivers has served as the traditional base camp for Denali climbers since the early 1900s. The compact Main Street preserves authentic gold rush-era architecture, with original wooden storefronts now housing local artisans and outfitters. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places, the town offers an authentic glimpse into Alaska's pioneering heritage.

activities.text: Denali Flightseeing - Experience breathtaking aerial views of North America's highest peak and the surrounding Alaska Range. Small aircraft circle massive glaciers, navigate between towering granite spires, and offer perspectives of Denali's 20,310-foot summit that few ever witness. Weather permitting, glacier landings provide the opportunity to step onto ancient ice fields.

Susitna River Rafting - Float through pristine wilderness where the Susitna, Talkeetna, and Chulitna rivers converge. These glacially-fed waterways wind through boreal forest with views of the Alaska Range, offering opportunities to spot moose, eagles, and salmon depending on the season.

Talkeetna Zipline Tours - Soar above the Talkeetna River valley on aerial cables stretching through old-growth spruce forest, with panoramic mountain views serving as the backdrop to this elevated wilderness experience.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords

visits.text: Two buildings share the same address in downtown Tallahassee: the 22-story New Capitol completed in 1977 and, directly in front of it, the original 1845 structure it almost replaced. The Florida Historic Capitol Museum is that survivor - restored to its 1902 appearance after a citizens' campaign saved it from demolition. Under the stained-glass dome, you move through the restored Governor's Suite and Supreme Court chambers where Florida's political battles were fought. Tallahassee was chosen as capital in 1824 because it sat precisely halfway between St. Augustine and Pensacola - two commissioners walked toward each other from opposite coasts and met in the middle. Free admission; allow 75 minutes.

FSU English professor Dr. Laura Jepsen bought a three-acre pasture on High Road in 1956 because she fell in love with a tree. The live oak at Lichgate is estimated at 300 years old, its branches sweeping the ground like a billowing skirt, and everything she built - Tudor-style cottage, labyrinth, gardens - was designed to protect it. The granite came from the same Georgia quarry as the Old State Capitol steps; the cypress paneling from Tallahassee swamps. On the National Register of Historic Places since 2006, maintained by volunteers, it's free and open dawn to dusk. Find the driveway by the two white posts on High Road - most locals have never been.

Fifty-two acres of live oak hammock just outside the city house one of the stranger museums in Florida. The Tallahassee Museum combines restored 19th-century cracker buildings with living wildlife habitats - Florida panthers, red wolves (one of only two surviving core populations in the country), black bears, and otters along boardwalk trails you follow through the hammock. Founded in 1957 as a junior science museum, it holds national accreditation. The Tree-to-Tree Adventures zipline courses run above the canopy for anyone who wants elevation with their ecology. Buy timed tickets online for weekends.

activities.text: Fifteen miles south of Tallahassee on Highway 267, one of the world's deepest and largest freshwater springs pumps 400 million gallons a day from the Upper Floridan Aquifer. The water stays 70 degrees year-round; the vent drops 190 feet, perfectly clear to the bottom. Board the flat-bottom riverboat at Edward Ball Wakulla Springs State Park and keep your eyes on the banks - manatees have been regular here since 1998. Hollywood discovered the place: Tarzan's Secret Treasure filmed in 1941, Creature from the Black Lagoon in 1954. The 1937 Spanish lodge restaurant serves breakfast. Book the riverboat tour the evening before.

Railroad Square Art District started as a WWII industrial warehouse park and became the city's creative center without ever trying to look like one. Seventy-plus galleries, studios, and restaurants occupy the converted hangars. The Friday art walks pull locals who drift between wood-fired pizza and craft beer and hand-thrown pottery. First evening, no plan: this is where you go.

days_plan.itin_title: Florida Panhandle: Tallahassee to Pensacola on the Emerald Coast

visits.text: Marvel at Tallulah Gorge, a dramatic 2-mile-long chasm plunging nearly 1,000 feet deep where five magnificent waterfalls cascade over ancient rock formations. A suspension bridge spans the gorge 80 feet above the rushing Tallulah River, providing heart-stopping views of the canyon walls and roaring cascades. Free permits allow adventurous hikers to descend to the gorge floor for an intimate encounter with this geological wonder.

Explore over 20 miles of hiking and biking trails radiating from the gorge rim, each offering different perspectives of the dramatic landscape carved over millennia by rushing waters. The park's historic dam and interpretive center document the area's role in early hydroelectric development. Overlooks along the rim trail provide photographers with stunning compositions of waterfalls and canyon walls.

activities.text: Experience the exhilarating natural waterslide at Sliding Rock, where smooth granite channels visitors into refreshing plunge pools surrounded by moss-covered boulders and echoing canyon walls. The nearby lake beach offers calmer swimming for those seeking relaxation after gorge adventures.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

visits.text: Tampa Bay - Where Theme Parks Meet Championship Sports: This Gulf Coast metropolis combines world-class attractions with a thriving food and drink scene that rivals any city in Florida.

Busch Gardens Tampa Bay - Theme Park Meets World-Class Zoo: This unique combination of African wildlife preserve and thrill ride destination features one of the country's best roller coaster collections alongside remarkable animal habitats.

Historic Ybor City - Tampa's Latin Quarter Since 1885: Once the cigar capital of the world, this National Historic Landmark District preserves Cuban, Spanish, and Italian heritage through architecture, restaurants, and the ongoing cigar-rolling tradition.
Tampa Riverwalk - 2.6 Miles of Waterfront Wonder: This continuous pedestrian path connects downtown museums, parks, restaurants, and attractions along the Hillsborough River and Tampa Bay.

activities.text: Tampa Craft Beer Trail - Florida's Brewing Capital: Pioneering breweries like Cigar City and Coppertail have established Tampa Bay as a craft beer destination, with dozens of taprooms offering local brews.
Tampa Bay Sports - Championship Caliber: Home to the NFL's Buccaneers and NHL's Lightning-both recent champions-the region offers professional sports events year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

places.name: Taos

visits.text: Experience Taos Pueblo, a UNESCO World Heritage Site with over 1,000 years of continuous habitation, representing one of the oldest continuously inhabited communities in North America. The remarkable multi-story adobe structures, some reaching five stories, have been home to the Tiwa-speaking Puebloan people since before European contact. Visitors can explore the living village and learn about traditions that have survived for a millennium.

Discover the quaint mountain town of Taos, nestled beneath stunning peaks and surrounded by artistic heritage that has attracted creative spirits for over a century. The surrounding Wheeler Peak Wilderness Area offers challenging hiking and skiing opportunities with breathtaking views from New Mexico's highest summit at 13,167 feet.

activities.text: Experience the thrill of rafting down the Rio Grande through rugged, wild terrain where the river has carved dramatic gorges through the volcanic landscape. Outfitters offer trips ranging from gentle floats to challenging whitewater adventures through some of the most spectacular canyon scenery in the Southwest. The Rio Grande del Norte National Monument protects this remarkable landscape for future generations.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

map_keywords: Rio Grande del Norte National Monument

visits.text: Temecula Valley Wine Country - Southern California's Napa: Nearly 50 wineries grow over 30 varietals in this warm-climate region, producing distinctive wines that have earned national recognition. Rolling hills dotted with oaks create a surprisingly Tuscan atmosphere.

Old Town Temecula - The Real Old West: This six-block historic district preserves 1880s wooden storefronts where cowboys once hitched their horses. Today, antique shops, restaurants, and wine tasting rooms fill the authentic buildings.

Temecula Valley Balloon & Wine Festival - Rise Above the Vines: This annual June event sends dozens of colorful hot air balloons floating over wine country, combining morning flights with wine tastings and live entertainment.

activities.text: Hot Air Balloon Sunrise Flights - Float Over Wine Country at Dawn: Year-round morning launches drift over patchwork vineyards as mist rises from the valleys, followed by traditional champagne toasts upon landing.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

places.name: Terlingua

visits.text: Santa Elena Canyon is Big Bend's most spectacular gorge: limestone walls rise 1,500 feet above the Rio Grande for 7 miles - on the Texas side Mesa de Anguila, on the Mexican side Sierra Ponce. These limestone layers were deposited 60 to 130 million years ago in a shallow inland sea. The 1.6-mile round-trip trail from the parking lot follows the river and penetrates the canyon - arrive early morning, when the raking light is most beautiful and there's no one around. In rainy season, check Terlingua Creek water level before you go: the ford on foot may be impassable.

The Ross Maxwell Scenic Drive is a 30-mile scenic road traversing the Chihuahuan Desert from Panther Junction junction to Santa Elena Canyon. Sotol Vista and Mule Ears Overlook stops offer views of black volcanic formations and desert plains stretching to the horizon. Don't rush through - allow 2 hours with stops at the viewpoints.

Terlingua was a thriving mercury mining town until the market collapsed in 1942. The hillside cemetery, with its rusted wrought-iron crosses and adobe headstones, is the most photogenic remnant of that past. The site has hosted the world chili cook-off championship since 1967 - two separate competitions take place in November on the same dusty plain.

The Chisos Basin is a verdant oasis suspended at 5,400 feet elevation in the heart of the volcanic Chisos massif - the only mountain range entirely contained within a U.S. national park. The air here runs 27°F cooler than at the Rio Grande, with oaks and pinyon pines replacing cacti. If you're staying at the basin lodge, book several months ahead - it's the park's only interior lodging.

The Rio Grande Hot Springs are 105°F thermal springs flowing directly at the river's edge, on the site of a former turn-of-the-century spa hotel whose adobe walls still stand. Access via a 1.9-mile track from Rio Grande Village Road. Soak while looking across at Mexico - the spot sees less traffic than the park's major sites.

activities.text: The crossing to Boquillas del Carmen, Mexico is authorized through the park's official port of entry, open Wednesday through Sunday. A rowboat ferries you across the 100 feet of the Rio Grande, then you enter a village of 150 residents with no road access except this ferry. Passport required - present yourself at the border control kiosk upon arrival. The atmosphere is peaceful, very different from other border crossings.

Sunset over the Rio Grande from the heights of Terlingua: the orange light setting fire to the Mexican cliffs across the river is worth the trip alone. Settle on the Ghost Town bar terrace - the atmosphere is unique, among backpackers, geologists, and expats who've found their edge of the world here.

The Window Trail (5.6 miles RT, 1,000 ft descent) drops through a canyon from the basin to a natural pour-off where the view plunges onto the desert 2,000 feet below - it's "the window." The sunset seen through this opening is one of Big Bend's iconic images: leave 2.5 hours before dusk to be there at the right moment. The Lost Mine Trail (4.8 miles RT, 1,150 ft gain) offers a 360° panorama over the Chisos, Mexico's Sierra del Carmen, and the northern desert plains. Choose one according to your energy: attempting both in one day is not recommended.

places.name: The Forks Me

visits.text: The drive from Millinocket to Rangeley runs over 3h30 without stops - so The Forks is the natural break. This is where the Dead River meets the Kennebec, and where Northern Outdoors launched commercial whitewater rafting in Maine in 1976, turning these Class III-IV rapids into a 60,000-rafter-a-year industry. Even if you're not rafting today, stop for lunch at the Kennebec River Pub & Brewery and walk to the river confluence - two powerful rivers coming together in granite walls is genuinely dramatic. The full-day Kennebec trip is one of the best rafting experiences in New England for mixed groups and families.

Moxie Falls drops 90 feet over a granite ledge in a narrow gorge, a 15-minute hike from the trailhead off Lake Moxie Road. It's one of the highest free-falling waterfalls in New England and a well-kept secret compared to better-marketed waterfalls elsewhere in Maine. The trail is largely flat with a short steep descent near the base.

days_plan.itin_title: Inland Maine: Moosehead Lake, Mount Katahdin & the North Woods

map_keywords: The Forks Maine Kennebec River whitewater rafting

places.name: Thermopolis

visits.text: Hot Springs State Park smells of minerals from the parking lot. The Big Spring - Wyoming's first state park since 1897 - discharges more than 1.8 million gallons of water per day at a constant 128°F, flowing over rainbow-colored travertine terraces in layers of orange, white, and rust. The free public Bath House at the park's core is a condition of a treaty signed that year with the Eastern Shoshone and Northern Arapaho, who sold the land on the condition the waters remain free. The Bath House, cooled to 104°F, limits soaks to 20 minutes. Cross the suspension footbridge over the Bighorn River for the best view of the terraces.

In 1993, fossil hunters found dinosaur bones weathering out of the hills on Warm Springs Ranch ten minutes from downtown Thermopolis. The Wyoming Dinosaur Center grew from that discovery and now houses 58 mounted skeletons and over 20,000 bones from the Thermopolis bonebeds - including the Thermopolis Specimen of Archaeopteryx, one of the most complete ever found. Watch scientists at work in the preparation lab year-round; the Dig for the Day program (late May through mid-September) puts you on a real excavation site with actual paleontology tools. Book in advance.

Four miles south on US-20, Wind River Canyon closes around the road - sheer walls, the Wind River running alongside, rock layers exposing more than 2.5 billion years of geological history in one of the most complete sequences visible anywhere in Wyoming. Keep your eyes on the upper ledges for bighorn sheep. The canyon runs about 14 miles; allow 20-30 minutes with stops.

activities.text: For a longer soak than the 20-minute Bath House limit allows, Star Plunge or Hellie's Tepee Spa charge paid entry but offer waterslides, multiple pools, and no time restrictions - both fed by the same mineral waters. Star Plunge has been operating since 1900. The State Bath House allows a second 20-minute session if you wait two hours between them.

Twenty-one miles northwest of Thermopolis, the Legend Rock Petroglyph Site carries the work of three prehistoric cultural groups on sandstone cliffs spanning roughly 500 AD through the 19th century - hundreds of yards of images. Pick up the gate key at Hot Springs State Park headquarters before leaving town; free to visit. The road turns to gravel for the last few miles. Bring water: it's exposed high desert.

days_plan.itin_title: Wyoming Beyond Yellowstone: Cowboys, Hot Springs & Bighorn Medicine Wheel

map_keywords: Hot Springs State Park Thermopolis Wyoming
Wind River Canyon Scenic Byway Wyoming

places.name: Three Rivers

visits.text: The General Sherman Tree is the largest living tree on the planet - by trunk volume. It stands 83.8 meters tall, 11 meters in diameter at the base, and contains 1,487 cubic meters of wood. It's between 2,200 and 2,700 years old, meaning it was already a young tree when Rome was founded. It's neither the oldest (that title goes to a bristlecone pine in the Sierra Nevada) nor the tallest, but no other tree disputes its mass. Notable fact: it continues growing each year, adding the equivalent of an 18-meter tree's worth of new wood. The paved trail from the parking lot descends 500 meters to the tree - the return is uphill, so take your time as the 2,100-meter elevation makes itself felt.

Moro Rock is a granite dome that rises from the sequoia forest like a ship's prow. The stone staircase carved into the rock climbs in 300 steps to the summit at 2,050 meters elevation - allow 20 minutes. The 360° view of the Great Western Divide and Kings Canyon gorge is worth every step. Don't miss the Tunnel Log on the road to Crescent Meadow either: a sequoia that fell naturally in December 1937, through which park staff carved a tunnel in 1938 to let cars pass - it measures 2.4 meters wide and 8 meters high.

activities.text: Stop by the Giant Forest Museum before leaving the forest. The building dates from 1928 and houses exhibits on giant sequoia ecology - why they resist fires (their bark can reach 60 centimeters thick and doesn't burn), how they reproduce only through fire, and what we know about their shallow roots that intertwine with neighboring trees to share stability. In 2021, a fierce wildfire threatened the General Sherman - rangers wrapped its base in reflective fire-retardant sheeting normally reserved for structures. The tree survived undamaged.

Take the Congress Trail starting from the General Sherman: a 5-kilometer loop that passes through dozens of giant sequoias, several with diameters larger than your living room. The forest is silent, the ground soft beneath the needles, and the light filtered through the canopy is photographically rare. Allow 2 hours for the complete loop.

days_plan.itin_title: California National Parks Road Trip: Yosemite, Sequoia, and Death Valley
Sierra California Road Trip: Sequoia and Kings Canyon

map_keywords: General Sherman Tree Sequoia National Park
Giant Forest Museum Sequoia National Park

places.name: Toledo

visits.text: You enter one of the few major U.S. museums offering completely free admission - a tradition rooted in the institution's very founding in 1901 by Edward Drummond Libbey, Toledo's glass magnate. The 1912 Greek Revival main building, with its Ionic columns, sits alongside the Glass Pavilion, inaugurated in 2006 - a building entirely of curved glass designed by Japanese firm SANAA, the first American project by this architecture office that would receive the Pritzker Prize in 2010. Toledo being nicknamed the "Glass City" due to its glass industry since the 19th century, this pavilion is both a showcase for the historic glass collection and an architectural demonstration of what the material can achieve. The museum holds works by Van Gogh, Rembrandt, Picasso, and Cézanne among its 30,000 pieces.

30 kilometers south of Toledo on the Maumee River, Fort Meigs is the largest reconstructed wooden palisade fort in the United States. This is where in May 1813, General William Henry Harrison (future president in 1841) withstood a siege of more than a week by British forces and their Native American allies commanded by Tecumseh. The American victory, combined with the Battle of Lake Erie in September 1813, definitively established American sovereignty over northern Ohio. Period-costumed guides reenact life in the fort.

Located in Toledo on the banks of the Maumee River, this museum tells the story of the five Great Lakes - a 244,000 square kilometer basin that holds 20% of the world's surface freshwater. The centerpiece is the Col. James M. Schoonmaker, a real 182-meter lake freighter launched in 1911 that you can board to visit the pilothouse, engine room, and crew quarters. The permanent exhibition traces the history of commercial navigation on the Great Lakes from the first Native American canoers to the Edmund Fitzgerald disaster in November 1975.

activities.text: Toledo Botanical Gardens extends over 18 hectares with collections of roses, herbs, hydrangeas, and a deciduous forest whose autumn colors are spectacular. Founded in 1964, they're managed as a community space with public art programs - sculptures and installations line the walkways between flower beds. The medicinal herb garden, inspired by medieval monastic gardens, is particularly well documented with panels explaining each plant's use by settlers and Native Americans.

The Toledo Museum's Glass Pavilion regularly offers live glassblowing demonstrations in its studio open to the public - you watch artists shape works from molten glass at 1,000°C. In 1962, it was in a garage on the museum's very site that Harvey Littleton organized the first studio glassblowing workshop that launched the global Studio Glass Art movement. Toledo claims to have triggered a worldwide artistic revolution from a garage, and this studio continues that tradition.

days_plan.itin_title: Pittsburgh to Detroit: Steel, Rock & Motown - 9 Days Across the Great Lakes

map_keywords: Toledo Museum of Art Glass Pavilion
Toledo Museum of Art Monroe Street
National Museum of the Great Lakes Toledo

visits.text: The O.K. Corral is the site of the most famous gunfight in American history. On October 26, 1881, around 3 p.m., Wyatt Earp, his brothers Virgil and Morgan, and Doc Holliday confronted the Clantons and McLaurys. Thirty seconds of gunfire: three dead on the outlaw side—Billy Clanton, Tom McLaury, and Frank McLaury—and two Earps wounded. The complex today includes reconstructed period stables, C.S. Fly's photo gallery, and the Historama—a multimedia presentation narrated by Vincent Price—included with admission. Reenactments are performed several times daily.

Boothill Graveyard sits on a small hill at the north entrance to town. About 300 people were buried here between 1878 and 1884, including Billy Clanton, Tom McLaury, and Frank McLaury, who fell during the gunfight. The epitaphs are often terse, sometimes sardonic—"Hanged by mistake," "Murdered in the streets." Entry is free. Allow 30 minutes.

The Tombstone Courthouse State Historic Park, built in 1882, is the town's best museum for understanding the mining and legal history of Cochise County. Wyatt Earp's razor is on display, along with the rifle of Ed Schieffelin, the prospector who discovered silver in the area in 1877 and founded the town in 1879. A courtroom mock-up and a reconstructed jail cell give a concrete sense of frontier justice.

The Tombstone Epitaph is Arizona's oldest newspaper, still published today. Its museum, free and located behind the Crystal Palace Saloon on Fifth Street, displays the original front page from October 27, 1881, covering the gunfight, printed on an old Washington hand press. The paper was founded by John Clum—Tombstone's reformist mayor, who had recruited the Earps to maintain order in town.

activities.text: Allen Street is Tombstone's main drag, entirely preserved in its 1880s state and closed to traffic. Actors in period costume roam all day. Order a sarsaparilla (a typical Wild West non-alcoholic beer) at the Crystal Palace Saloon, founded in 1879, and watch souvenir vendors offer photos in western costume. Kitsch, yes—but remarkably effective for time travel.

Several underground mine tours are offered around Tombstone—notably the Good Enough Mine, which produced silver during the 1880s boom. The circuit lasts about an hour and descends into the original shafts. It's a solid introduction before the more in-depth visit to the Queen Mine in Bisbee the next day.

days_plan.itin_title: Wild Arizona: Tucson, Tombstone & Bisbee

visits.text: You walk into Monroe Elementary, a two-story Italian Renaissance Revival brick building from 1926 that was one of four segregated schools serving Topeka's African American children. Oliver Brown and 12 other parents filed suit in 1951, the Supreme Court consolidated their case with four others, and on May 17, 1954 it ruled that 'separate educational facilities are inherently unequal.' The school closed in 1975, nearly went to auction, and was saved by the Trust for Public Land before opening as a National Historic Site in 2004 on the decision's 50th anniversary. Ranger-led programs and an orientation film deepen the visit.

Look up from the rotunda and you are 304 feet below the tip of a copper dome that stands taller than the one in Washington, D.C. (288 feet), though with roughly half the diameter. Construction ran from 1866 to 1903 - 37 years for $3.2 million. John Steuart Curry's 1930s second-floor murals include Tragic Prelude, depicting an oversize, raging John Brown flanked by flames and a tornado - the only portrait of a convicted traitor in any U.S. state capitol. The dome tour climbs 296 steps with no elevator; reserve your spot in advance.

Stroll into the Reinisch Rose Garden and you are surrounded by over 4,500 rosebushes in 180 varieties, laid out in 1930 by Chicago landscape architect Emmett Hill on land donated by the heirs of Civil War veteran Guilford Gage in 1899. The garden is one of 23 All-America Rose Selections test gardens in the United States, which means serious horticultural work happens here alongside the wedding photos. The Topeka Zoo within the same 160-acre park houses a walk-through tropical rainforest, one of the first in the U.S. The 1908 carousel still runs to its original Wurlitzer band organ.

activities.text: Walk through 30,000 square feet of Kansas history and you will pass a full-sized Atchison, Topeka & Santa Fe locomotive, a Southern Cheyenne-style tipi, a reconstructed 1950s diner, and a strong section on the Bleeding Kansas era that many historians consider the true opening of the Civil War. The Oregon and Santa Fe Trail galleries are particularly well done, and the overall arc - from prehistoric times through the 20th century - gives good context for everything you have seen and will see on this loop.

Pick up the free map at the Brown v. Board visitor center and follow Topeka's civil rights geography beyond Monroe School. Constitution Hall at 429 S. Kansas Avenue - called the 'Free State Capitol' - is where Free State supporters drafted the Topeka Constitution in 1855 rejecting slavery; it also served as a stop on the Underground Railroad and reopened as a museum in late 2025 after extensive restoration. A community mural painted by volunteers faces the park from across the street. Topeka's Brown v. Board site was added to the U.S. Civil Rights Trail in 2021.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends
Kansas Flint Hills & Frontier: Wichita to Dodge City

visits.text: Fruita Historic District - As you enter Capitol Reef, you suddenly pass through a ribbon of green in the middle of the red desert. It's not a mirage: it's Fruita, a settlement founded in the 1880s by Mormon pioneers who planted orchards along the Fremont River. Never more than 10 families lived here - the last resident left in 1969. The National Park Service now maintains over 2,700 original fruit trees: cherries, apricots, peaches, apples. From June through October, you can pick the fruit yourself (honor-system scale and payment box at orchard entrances).

Gifford Homestead - This restored farmhouse amid the orchards sells homemade fruit pies, ice cream, and artisanal jams. Don't leave without trying the cherry or peach pie - it's one of Utah's national parks' best-kept secrets. Open mid-March through October.

Fremont Petroglyphs - Along Highway 24, a wooden boardwalk leads to a panel of petroglyphs carved by the Fremont people 1,000 to 2,000 years ago. You'll see bighorn sheep, human figures, and geometric shapes. Access is easy and free - it's 1.2 miles east of the visitor center.

Capitol Reef Scenic Drive - This 8-mile road runs along the western face of the Waterpocket Fold, a 100-mile geological wrinkle that cuts through the entire park from north to south. The name "Capitol Reef" comes from the white Navajo Sandstone domes that reminded early explorers of the U.S. Capitol, and the impassable cliffs they compared to reefs blocking ships. The road passes beneath 600-foot walls of red Wingate Sandstone - pull over often, each turn reveals a new vista.

Grand Wash Trail - At the end of a driveable dirt road, this flat 2.25-mile trail threads through a canyon with vertical walls that narrow in places to just a few yards. It's one of only four passages through the Waterpocket Fold. Easy and spectacular.

Hickman Bridge - This 133-foot natural sandstone bridge is accessible via a 1-mile trail from Highway 24. The climb is short but steep. Along the way, you'll pass the ruins of an ancient Fremont granary, tucked into an alcove.

activities.text: Cohab Canyon Trail - This 1.75-mile out-and-back trail climbs steeply above Fruita and offers a bird's-eye view of the orchards and the Waterpocket Fold cliffs. The name comes from "cohabitation": according to local lore, polygamous Mormons hid here to evade federal marshals in the 1880s. The ascent is steep at first, but the canyon at the top is beautiful.

Capitol Gorge Trail - Before the modern road was built in 1962, this narrow canyon was the only vehicle route through the Waterpocket Fold. Wagon and car travelers passed through here, and many carved their names in the rock - you'll see the "Pioneer Register" with inscriptions dating to the 1870s. The trail is 2 miles out-and-back, flat and easy. If you continue a bit farther, you'll reach the "water pockets" - natural potholes carved in the sandstone that hold rainwater for months.

In late afternoon, stop at the base of Chimney Rock, an isolated sandstone pillar visible from Highway 24. Capitol Reef is a certified Dark Sky Park - if you stay after sunset, the minimal light pollution makes it one of Utah's best stargazing spots.
