Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Southwest Desert Road Trip: Zion, Bryce & Grand Canyon
Utah Canyon Loop: Capitol Reef & Goblin Valley
Utah's Mighty Five: Zion, Bryce Canyon, Capitol Reef, Arches, and Canyonlands
Utah's Mighty Five: Zion, Bryce Canyon, Capitol Reef, Arches, and Canyonlands

visits.text: Front Street and Historic Downtown Traverse City - Start with a stroll down Front Street, the main thoroughfare lined with Victorian buildings converted into shops and tasting rooms. Along the way, spot the City Opera House at No. 106: built in 1891, it was the city's first building to have electricity and still hosts performances today. Traverse City is dubbed the "Cherry Capital of the World" - the surrounding five counties produce 40 to 50 percent of the nation's tart cherry harvest. If you're here in July, the National Cherry Festival has been running since 1925 with eight days of festivities, including a parade and pie-eating contests.

Old Mission Peninsula - Head north along this 18-mile finger of land that separates the two arms of Grand Traverse Bay. The lighthouse at the peninsula's tip sits almost exactly on the 45th parallel - halfway between the equator and the North Pole. All along the drive, you'll pass cherry orchards and vineyards - the sandy soil and Lake Michigan's microclimate are perfect for riesling and pinot gris. Stop at one of the wineries for a tasting.

Sleeping Bear Dunes National Lakeshore - Thirty minutes west of Traverse City, this national park protects 35 miles of Lake Michigan shoreline, established in 1970. The name comes from an Ojibwe legend: a mother bear fleeing a fire in Wisconsin swam across the lake with her two cubs. The cubs drowned from exhaustion and became North and South Manitou Islands. The mother, asleep on the bluff waiting for them, was covered in sand by the Great Spirit. The "perched" dunes rise 450 feet above the lake - formed 12,000 years ago as glaciers retreated.

Pierce Stocking Scenic Drive - This 7.4-mile loop winds through forest before emerging at spectacular overlooks. At Overlook #9, you'll discover the dunes' dizzying plunge toward Lake Michigan's blue-green waters, with the Manitou Islands on the horizon. Pierce Stocking, a land speculator who fell in love with the area, created this drive in the 1960s before the park was officially designated.

Dune Climb - This is the park's most popular attraction: a vast 130-foot sand ramp you can scramble up on foot. If you have the energy, continue beyond the crest - a 3.5-mile trail leads all the way to Lake Michigan through forest and dunes. Stay on marked paths: vegetation is fragile and protected.

Glen Haven Historic Village - This restored lakeside hamlet once housed a maritime rescue station in the 19th century. You'll find the Coast Guard building, a blacksmith shop, and D.H. Day's original general store, all telling the story of Great Lakes maritime and timber commerce. Admission is free.

activities.text: Sunset at Clinch Park Beach - Settle in facing Grand Traverse Bay to watch the sun sink over Lake Michigan. The water turns shades of turquoise striking enough to rival tropical seas. Pack a picnic with local cherry products - jam, sausage, or pie.

Walk along the Boardman River - Follow the pedestrian trail that traces the river through downtown. In late afternoon, light filters through the trees and the atmosphere turns peaceful. The main section takes about 30 minutes.

Empire Bluff Trail Hike - This short loop (1.5 miles round-trip) climbs through hardwood forest to a stunning panorama over South Bar Lake, Lake Michigan, and the dunes. Best in late afternoon for golden light. Moderate difficulty, allow 45 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Michigan Great Lakes Road Trip: Dunes and Shorelines
Michigan Great Lakes Road Trip: Dunes and Shorelines
The Great Lakes Grand Loop: Chicago, Milwaukee, Door County, Sleeping Bear, Mackinac, Pictured Rocks - 12 Days

visits.text: Perrot State Park - Limestone bluffs rise 500 feet above the Mississippi. The name 'Trempealeau' comes from French 'la montagne qui trempe à l'eau' - the mountain that soaks in the water - an isolated peak rising from the floodplain like an island. From the bluff tops, the view across the Mississippi Valley is vast: meanders, wooded islands, and transport barges.
Trempealeau Mountain - Climb to the summit of this 400-foot remnant butte for 360° views of the Trempealeau and Mississippi rivers' confluence. The trail is 2 miles round-trip.
Trempealeau River Confluence - At the mountain's base, the river joins the Mississippi in a calm meander. Marshy area good for birding.

activities.text: Kayaking Trempealeau Bay - Paddle through backwaters and channels of the Mississippi. The water is calm here, lined with willows and populated by herons and bald eagles. Outfitters rent kayaks at the water's edge.
Bluff Hiking - Brady's Bluff Trail (1.5 miles round-trip, 500 feet elevation gain) is the most popular trail. The climb is steep but short, and the summit reward is worth it.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

places.name: Tucson

visits.text: Mission San Xavier del Bac rises in brilliant white about nine miles south of downtown, on Tohono O'odham Nation land. The current building was constructed by Franciscans between 1783 and 1797—the Jesuits, who had founded an earlier mission on this site in 1692, had been expelled from Spain in 1767. It's the oldest intact European structure in Arizona. The interior is covered with baroque murals and sculptures that have earned it the nickname "the Sistine Chapel of North America." Arrive early in the morning to avoid school groups.

The Arizona-Sonora Desert Museum, west of Tucson bordering Saguaro National Park, is neither quite zoo nor quite botanical garden—it's both at once, entirely outdoors. Rattlesnakes, river otters, Mexican wolves, and 300 plant species from the Sonoran Desert coexist across 52 acres. Plan at least two hours. In extreme heat, come at opening time (7:30 a.m.)—the animals are active in the morning.

Perched on Tucson's eastern edge, the Pima Air & Space Museum houses more than 300 aircraft across 200 acres—one of the largest private aviation collections in the world. You'll encounter an SR-71 Blackbird, B-29s, and Cold War prototypes. The site adjoins Davis-Monthan Air Force Base, where 4,000 decommissioned military aircraft rust in the open desert—the infamous "Boneyard."

The Barrio Histórico, an adobe neighborhood downtown, contains some of Tucson's oldest houses, built from the 1850s onward. The earthen walls have retained their vivid colors—ochre, turquoise, salmon. This is where el Presidio San Agustín del Tucson once stood, the Spanish fort founded in 1775. The walk is brief (30 minutes on foot) but gives an honest sense of what the city was like before the railroad arrived.

activities.text: Saguaro National Park West runs along the Tucson Mountains just behind the Desert Museum—it's the densest area for saguaros, those cacti that can exceed 40 feet in height and live 150 years. The Bajada Loop Drive (7.5 miles of gravel) lets you see everything without walking in extreme heat. If you prefer hiking, the Valley View Overlook Trail (1.5 miles round-trip) offers a panorama across the plain at sunset.

Tucson is the first American city designated a UNESCO Creative City of Gastronomy—and that shows on the plate. Devote your evening to dinner in the Fourth Avenue district or around Mercado San Agustín to explore border cuisine: tacos al pastor, chiles en nogada, Sonoran hot dogs (in a grilled bolillo roll, with beans, onions, and crema). The Mexican restaurants here are often run by families from Sonora, not chains.

days_plan.itin_title: Arizona Grand Loop: Grand Canyon and Desert Highways
Wild Arizona: Tucson, Tombstone & Bisbee

map_keywords: Mission San Xavier del Bac Tucson

places.name: Tucumcari

visits.text: Cadillac Ranch (Amarillo) - Cross the field (400 meters from the parking lot) to see ten Cadillacs planted nose-first in the ground, all covered in multicolored graffiti. This 1974 installation, financed by eccentric millionaire Stanley Marsh, pays tribute to the evolution of the Cadillac tail fin. Bring spray paint - this is one of the few places in the world where graffiti is encouraged. You'll need to walk 400 meters across the field from the parking area.
MidPoint Cafe (Adrian, TX) - You're exactly halfway between Chicago and Los Angeles: 1,139 miles on each side. The sign in front of the cafe confirms it. Try the Ugly Crust Pie - the house specialty.

Blue Swallow Motel (Tucumcari) - Arrive at dusk: the blue neon swallow promising '100% Refrigerated Air' is the most beautiful lighted sign on all of Route 66. This 1939 motor court, listed on the National Register, has garages attached to each room - standard back when every traveler wanted to keep an eye on their car. If you can book a room (reserve 2 months ahead in summer), it's the ultimate Route 66 experience.

activities.text: Walk through Tucumcari's neon lights - Over two dozen vintage neon signs illuminate the main street after dark. The Blue Swallow is the star, but also look for the Palomino Motel, Safari Motel, and Teepee Curios. Walk slowly - it's an open-air museum.

days_plan.itin_title: Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica
The Rain Man Route: An American Road Trip

map_keywords: Blue Swallow Motel Tucumcari New Mexico

visits.text: You walk into one of America's great concentrations of Zigzag Art Deco architecture, every facade paid for by oil money between 1927 and 1939. The Philcade Building at 511 S. Boston, built by oil tycoon Waite Phillips in 1930, has a lobby shaped like a 'T' for Tulsa - a mix of Art Deco and Art Nouveau that drew national architectural attention at the time. Two blocks south, the Boston Avenue Methodist Church from 1929 rises like a limestone rocket; designated a National Historic Landmark in 1999, it was one of the few major Art Deco buildings in America designed by a woman, art teacher Adah Robinson. Tulsa's deco spans three distinct phases: Zigzag, PWA Moderne, and Streamline. Book the Saturday morning walking tour by Tulsa Tours for inside access to locked lobbies.

You look up at a 1929 limestone tower that National Historic Landmark status since 1999 has still not fully rendered ordinary. Adah Robinson, an art teacher rather than a licensed architect, drove the design - making this one of very few major Art Deco structures in the world with a woman as primary creative force. Step close to the entrance terracotta reliefs: stylized wheat, the cross, and elongated human figures all push skyward in the Zigzag manner. Guided tours of the interior run on Sundays after the 11am service and on most weekday mornings - call ahead to confirm timing before you drive over.

You enter a museum that one American art historian called 'a kind of Smithsonian Institution of the American West' - founded by Thomas Gilcrease, a Muscogee Creek Nation citizen who turned oil wealth into one of the most comprehensive collections of Western American art and history in existence. He opened his first public gallery on his Tulsa estate in 1949 and eventually deeded 350,000 items - and 13 acres of land - to the city. Among the holdings: 18 of the 22 bronze sculptures ever cast by Frederic Remington, and over 500 paintings by 20th-century Native American artists. A new building opened in 2024, finally giving the collection the display environment it deserves. Arrive early on weekdays to avoid school groups.

You walk into a neighborhood that, by 1921, had two newspapers, multiple theaters, a hospital, hotels, and scores of Black-owned businesses - all built within a segregated system that left residents no other choice. In eighteen hours on May 31-June 1, 1921, white mobs burned 1,256 homes and killed a death toll historians place between 100 and 300; 10,000 people were left homeless and 6,000 spent days under armed guard at the fairgrounds. The massacre was erased from local history for decades, recognized by an Oklahoma state commission only in 1997. The Greenwood Cultural Center and the Black Wall Street Memorial anchor this walk through a rebuilt neighborhood that carries the weight of what happened here.

You step into a 72-room Italian Renaissance villa built in 1927 for oil magnate Waite Phillips, who surprised Tulsa in 1938 by donating the entire estate - and 23 acres of formal gardens - to the city as an art museum. It opened October 25, 1939. The permanent collection runs to 16,000 objects, with particular depth in Native American art: Phillips had asked the museum to 'perpetuate the culture of people to whom Oklahomans are especially indebted,' and more than half the holdings reflect that charge. The 2004 garden renovation cemented a reputation as the most beautiful place in Oklahoma. Plan at least two and a half hours - the grounds alone earn the trip.

You walk into a 100-acre riverside park that, when it opened September 8, 2018, set a U.S. record as the largest private gift ever made to a community park - $465 million from the George Kaiser Family Foundation. The land sits on what was Muscogee Nation territory; the main trail was renamed the Tuckabache Trail to honor the Muscogee medicine man to whom it was originally allotted. Over 3 million people came in year one alone, tripling every projection. The Chapman Adventure Playground, ONEOK Boathouse, skate park, and wetlands trail give the park enough range that two hours here still feels like you've only scratched it. Entry is free.

activities.text: You'll spend two hours inside four Art Deco lobbies - one of them normally locked to the public - hearing stories about the oil barons who commissioned these buildings and learning to read three distinct phases of the style. Your guide explains why Zigzag Art Deco arrived in Tulsa just two years after its 1925 Paris debut, and why typically conservative utility companies embraced the most flamboyant style of the Roaring Twenties. AARP named it a top-10 walking tour in America. Book online at tulsa.tours at least a few days ahead - Saturday morning slots fill quickly.

Tonight you eat and drink in the Tulsa Arts District - formerly the Brady Arts District - a stretch of red-brick warehouses just north of the railroad tracks that has been Tulsa's cultural engine since the 1990s revitalization. You'll find the Woody Guthrie Center here (Guthrie was born in Okemah, Oklahoma, in 1912), the Bob Dylan Center, and Cain's Ballroom, which has hosted touring acts since 1930 and is still one of the best small venues in the country. The First Friday Art Crawl fills the streets on the first Friday of each month. For dinner, Restaurant Basque and Amelia's Wood Fired Cuisine both earn consistent local praise.

You push off from the ONEOK Boathouse onto Peggy's Pond, a sheltered lagoon connected to the Arkansas River where kayaks, canoes, and paddleboats are available to rent. Paddle under the Williams Crossing pedestrian bridge - completed Labor Day 2024 - for a view back at the park's skyline. The boathouse runs seasonally; weekday mornings are quietest on the water. It's a low-effort, high-reward extension of your time at the park before you head into the evening.

If your second night in Tulsa falls on a first Friday, you're in luck - galleries, studios, and museums across the Tulsa Arts District stay open into the evening with free or reduced admission, and the streets fill with locals and visitors moving between venues. On any other evening, the district's restaurants and bars draw a crowd until late. The Woody Guthrie Center displays rotating exhibits on Guthrie's life and the folk tradition he shaped; the Bob Dylan Center opened in 2022 and documents six decades of Dylan's archive. Cain's Ballroom at 423 N. Main has hosted touring acts continuously since 1930 and almost always has something on.

days_plan.itin_title: Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica
Oklahoma Route 66 & Native Heritage: Tulsa to OKC
Oklahoma Route 66 & Native Heritage: Tulsa to OKC

places.name: Tusayan

visits.text: On the route to the Grand Canyon via US-89, stop at Cameron Trading Post, a trading post founded in 1916 on Navajo lands, beside the Little Colorado River. Its Navajo craft gallery—rugs, silver and turquoise jewelry, pottery—is one of the region's most comprehensive. The suspension footbridge behind the trading post offers a plunging view of the river's orange gorges.

Enter the park through the east entrance and first get a sense of the canyon from Desert View—the stone tower built in 1932 by architect Mary Colter on the South Rim stands at 7,480 feet. From there, the view extends some 20 miles of canyon, with the Colorado visible below. Then continue west on the Desert View Drive, stopping at Lipan Point: the river describes a curve rarely seen from such heights.

Mather Point is the South Rim's most frequented viewpoint—and for good reason: at 7,120 feet elevation, it plunges more than 4,500 feet above the Colorado, and on clear days you can see up to 60 miles west. It's named after Stephen Mather, the first director of the National Park Service. Arrive at dawn to watch the walls ignite—and to avoid the crowds that arrive by 9 a.m.

Walk along the Rim Trail to Yavapai Geology Museum (0.7 miles from Mather Point), built in 1928 from local limestone and ponderosa pine to blend into the landscape. Its panels trace 1.8 billion years of geological history—each yard of the canyon wall represents roughly one million years. The view through the museum's picture windows is among the South Rim's widest.

Continue west on the Rim Trail to Grand Canyon Village Historic District: the stone buildings constructed by Mary Colter in the 1930s (El Tovar Hotel, Lookout Studio, Bright Angel Lodge) are all designated historic landmarks. Step inside Lookout Studio, perched at the cliff's edge since 1914—the architecture plays with the rocks to the point where it's hard to tell where the building ends and the rock face begins.

activities.text: Position yourself at Mather Point or Yavapai Point for the golden hour and sunset—the canyon walls successively turn from yellow to blood red to orange depending on the light. If you arrive in late afternoon, it's not uncommon to see the colors shift within twenty minutes. Plan to be there 45 minutes before sunset.

Descend a few hundred yards on the Bright Angel Trail to feel the canyon from the inside—the first switchbacks reveal rock layers you couldn't see from above. Don't go below Indian Garden (9-mile round trip, too ambitious for a single day in high heat). The park recommends not descending below Two-Mile Resthouse in summer.

Take the free Hermit Road shuttle (Red Route) to Hermits Rest west of the village—7 miles of viewpoints with no private vehicles allowed, with stops like Pima Point and Monument Creek Vista. The shuttle runs year-round in the outbound direction, which allows you to alternate walking and riding the bus.

Arizona Grand Loop: Grand Canyon and Desert Highways
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons
Utah-Arizona Road Trip: National Parks and Canyons

places.name: Tuskegee

visits.text: Moton Field sits just off I-85 and the airport runway is still active - you may hear a small plane overhead as you walk into Hangar 1. Inside, a canary-yellow PT-17 Stearman biplane is the first thing you see: that is where it began, primary flight training for men the Army had decided would probably fail. Between 1941 and 1946, 992 Black pilots were trained here and at nearby Tuskegee Army Air Field. The park is free and open Monday through Saturday. Walk through both hangars: Hangar 1 covers the training, the Double V campaign - victory abroad against fascism, victory at home against racism - and includes a WWII-era flight simulator. Hangar 2 centers a restored P-51 Mustang. Climb the three flights to the original control tower for a 360-degree view of the airfield.

A ten-minute drive brings you to the Tuskegee University campus and the George Washington Carver Museum. The building was the school's laundry until 1938; Carver himself funded much of its conversion into a museum, dedicated in 1941 by Henry Ford. Inside: his laboratory equipment - he started with a single microscope - alongside the more than 300 industrial products he developed from peanuts, sweet potatoes, and native clays. Also on display, the paintings he cared about as much as his chemistry. The museum is open Thursday and Friday, 9 a.m. to 4:30 p.m. Call ahead to confirm hours.

A few hundred meters from the Carver Museum on the same campus, The Oaks - Booker T. Washington's home - is open for NPS ranger-led tours. Washington founded the school in 1881 with nothing, building it literally brick by brick using student labor, then recruited Carver from Iowa State in 1896. The small campus cemetery nearby holds the graves of both Washington and Carver. Walk it: it takes ten minutes and it is quietly affecting. The campus itself - the brick buildings, the live oaks - was built largely by students learning trades while they earned their education.

activities.text: The Tuskegee Human and Civil Rights Multicultural Center, on Franklin Road in downtown Tuskegee, documents the 40-year Tuskegee Syphilis Study - in which the U.S. Public Health Service monitored the progression of untreated syphilis in 399 Black men who were told they had 'bad blood.' The study ran from 1932 to 1972, long after penicillin was available and known to be effective. President Clinton formally apologized in 1997. Check hours before visiting.

If you have energy in the evening, the Tuskegee University campus invites a quiet walk after hours. The 1922 Booker T. Washington Monument - sculptor Charles Keck's bronze depicting Washington lifting the veil of ignorance - stands near the main entrance. The campus has been a National Historic Landmark since 1966, the first HBCU to receive that designation.

days_plan.itin_title: The Black Belt: Atlanta to New Orleans Through the Deep South

map_keywords: Tuskegee Airmen National Historic Site Moton Field
The Oaks Booker T Washington Tuskegee

places.name: Twentynine Palms

visits.text: Joshua Tree National Park sits at the boundary between two deserts - the Mojave to the north, cooler, where Joshua trees grow (arborescent yuccas that can live 500 years), and the Colorado Desert to the south, hotter, populated with cholla and ocotillo. Hidden Valley is its geological heart: a closed cirque of quartz monzonite boulders rising from the ground over millions of years. Local legend says it was a cattle rustlers' hideout in the 19th century. Today it's mainly a climbing paradise - over 8,000 documented routes in the park, many starting from here. The 1.6-kilometer nature loop is accessible to all and passes beneath spectacular formations.

Skull Rock, accessible directly from the parking lot on Park Boulevard, is one of the park's most photographed rocks - depressions carved by water erosion give it the appearance of a skull. A 2.7-kilometer nature trail starts there and runs along several remarkable formations with interpretive panels on desert geology. It's a good introduction if you haven't done Hidden Valley.

activities.text: Late afternoon, descend toward the Cholla Cactus Garden on Pinto Basin Road - a transition zone between the two deserts where thousands of "teddy bear" chollas cover 4 hectares. Don't be fooled by their fluffy appearance: their hollow spines barb into skin at the slightest brush and curve once in contact with moisture, making them extremely difficult to remove. Stay on the trail and wear closed-toe shoes. Late afternoon light creates a golden halo around each cactus - it's the ideal time for photos.

Joshua Tree is one of the American West's finest parks for stargazing - a certified Dark Sky Park with no significant light pollution for dozens of kilometers in any direction. Park in one of the park's isolated parking areas after nightfall and look skyward. Tour operators offer guided astronomy outings from Twentynine Palms.

days_plan.itin_title: California National Parks Road Trip: Yosemite, Sequoia, and Death Valley

map_keywords: Hidden Valley Nature Trail Joshua Tree National Park
Skull Rock Joshua Tree National Park
Cholla Cactus Garden Joshua Tree National Park

places.name: Twin Falls

visits.text: At the rim of the Snake River Canyon, Shoshone Falls drops 212 feet - taller than Niagara - across a horseshoe-shaped cliff nearly 1,000 feet wide. The canyon itself was carved in a matter of weeks during the Bonneville Flood roughly 14,000 years ago, when Lake Bonneville released 1,100 cubic miles of water in a catastrophic overflow. Thomas Moran, who had already painted Yellowstone, came here in 1900 for the Pan-American Exposition. See it in spring: by summer, irrigation diversions on the Snake can reduce the flow to a trickle. Admission is $5 per car from March 30 through September.

The I.B. Perrine Bridge is 1,500 feet long and its deck hangs 486 feet above the Snake River - the only man-made structure in the United States where BASE jumping is legal year-round without a permit. On most weekends, jumpers step off the east side and deploy their parachutes two seconds into a five-second freefall, drifting to the canyon floor. Evel Knievel's 1974 steam-rocket launch ramp is still visible on the south rim, 1.6 miles east of the bridge. The pedestrian walkway is free.

The Canyon Trail descends from the south rim to the Snake River floor and continues east along the water, passing under the bridge's arches. From the canyon bottom, you can rent a kayak and paddle upstream to Blue Heart Springs - a turquoise spring that emerges directly from the basalt wall and is only reachable by water. The canyon rim trail loop above is 3 miles and free.

activities.text: TandemBASE, based in Twin Falls, runs commercial tandem jumps off the Perrine Bridge - you're strapped to a qualified instructor and fall 486 feet in about five seconds before the parachute opens. Minimum age 18; fee applies. If that's a firm no, the canyon walls below the bridge have bolted sport climbing routes, and the visitor center at the south end of the bridge points you to current access.

Magic Valley - the agricultural region around Twin Falls - produces more than 70% of all commercial trout eaten in the United States, raised in spring-fed farms whose water comes out of the canyon walls at a constant 59°F year-round. Rock Creek Restaurant and Snake River Brewing both serve it well, alongside local dairy cheese and Magic Valley potatoes. The Thursday summer farmers' market on Shoshone Street (June through October) is one of the better ones in southern Idaho.

days_plan.itin_title: Idaho: Sawtooth Mountains, Sun Valley & Craters of the Moon

map_keywords: Perrine Bridge BASE jumping Twin Falls
Snake River Canyon Trail Twin Falls

visits.text: Escape to Tybee Island, Savannah's laid-back beach retreat where wide sandy shores, rolling dunes, and tranquil marshlands create a carefree coastal atmosphere. The historic lighthouse, Georgia's oldest and tallest, has guided mariners since 1736 and offers panoramic views from its observation deck after climbing 178 steps. The island maintains nostalgic beach-town charm with colorful cottages and relaxed local culture.

Explore the imposing Fort Pulaski National Monument, a masterpiece of 19th-century military engineering whose massive brick walls were breached by rifled cannon during the Civil War, forever changing the course of warfare. The moated fortification and surrounding marshlands offer walking trails rich with birdlife and historical significance. Interpretive programs bring the fort's dramatic history to life.

activities.text: Swim and sunbathe at lively South Beach, spot dolphins offshore, and watch pelicans diving for fish as the sun sets over the Atlantic. The Tybee Pier and Pavilion area buzzes with activity while quieter stretches of shore provide peaceful escapes. Over 200 bird species make this island a rewarding destination for wildlife observation throughout the year.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia's Grand Circle: Caucasus Mountains, Tbilisi & Black Sea Coast

visits.text: Oak Alley Plantation - The alley of 28 three-hundred-year-old live oaks leading to the mansion is the most iconic image of Louisiana. Planted between 1725 and 1750, these trees form a 250-meter tunnel of green. The house, built in 1837 by Jacques Télesphore Roman (brother of the governor), is a 21-meter square surrounded by 28 columns—as many as there are oak trees. Allow at least 2 hours. Don't miss the slavery exhibit in the reconstructed slave quarters: it's there you learn that an enslaved man named Antoine grafted the first commercial pecan trees in 1846.

Whitney Plantation - Twenty minutes from Oak Alley, this is the only Louisiana plantation entirely dedicated to the history of slavery. No romantic narrative here—the guided tour (mandatory) focuses on the lives of enslaved people, with testimonies carved in stone. Count on 1.5 hours.

activities.text: Drive the River Road - The road hugging the Mississippi between New Orleans and Baton Rouge passes a dozen plantations. The stretch between Vacherie and Donaldsonville is the most spectacular—historic mansions, sugar cane fields, and centuries-old oaks.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Louisiana Road Trip: Jazz and Bayous

visits.text: Jour 1: Worthington Glacier State Recreation Site - Located along Thompson Pass at 2,800 feet elevation, this glacier flows down from the Chugach Mountains to within easy viewing distance of the Richardson Highway. A paved trail leads to observation platforms, while more adventurous visitors can hike the two-mile Worthington Glacier Trail for closer access to the ice.

Jour 1: Bridal Veil Falls - This spectacular 900-foot cascade plunges down sheer cliffs along Keystone Canyon, visible from roadside pullouts and accessible via a mile-long trail to an overlook. The waterfall is most impressive during spring snowmelt and after summer rains.

Jour 1: Keystone Canyon - The Richardson Highway threads through this dramatic gorge carved by the Lowe River, with towering rock walls and multiple waterfalls creating one of Alaska's most scenic drives. Historic remnants of the failed Trans-Alaska Railway attempt are visible along the canyon walls.

Jour 2: Valdez Harbor and Waterfront - This ice-free port at the head of a fjord serves as the terminus of the Trans-Alaska Pipeline and a major fishing harbor. The downtown area rebuilt after the 1964 earthquake offers views of surrounding peaks rising directly from the water.

Jour 2: Prince William Sound - This island-studded waterway encompasses 15,000 square miles of protected waters, tidewater glaciers, and abundant marine wildlife. The sound's deep fjords and sheltered bays create ideal conditions for viewing sea otters, seals, sea lions, and whales.

activities.text: Jour 1: Thompson Pass Scenic Drive - The approach to Valdez crosses this 2,678-foot pass through the Chugach Mountains, an area receiving some of North America's highest snowfall totals. In summer, alpine meadows burst with wildflowers while glaciers cling to surrounding peaks.

Jour 2: Glacier Cruise - Day cruises venture into Columbia Bay to witness Columbia Glacier, one of Alaska's largest tidewater glaciers. These journeys navigate through floating icebergs while naturalists explain glacial geology and point out wildlife including puffins, kittiwakes, and marine mammals.

Jour 2: Sea Kayaking - Guided paddling excursions explore the fjords and bays surrounding Valdez, offering intimate access to marine ecosystems inaccessible by larger vessels. Multi-day expeditions reach remote glaciers and camping beaches.

Jour 2: Wildlife Viewing - Prince William Sound's nutrient-rich waters support abundant marine life, with sea otters, harbor seals, Steller sea lions, humpback whales, and orcas regularly observed. Seabird colonies host thousands of puffins, cormorants, and eagles.

days_plan.itin_title: Alaska Grand Circuit Itinerary: Denali, Wrangell and Kenai Fjords

places.name: Valentine

visits.text: The Niobrara National Scenic River Visitor Center on Highway 20 in Valentine is where you orient yourself to one of the most biologically surprising places in the Great Plains. Congress set aside 76 miles of the Niobrara for preservation in 1991; six distinct ecosystems converge in this single river valley - northern boreal forest, western coniferous forest, eastern deciduous forest, tallgrass prairie, mixed-grass prairie, and Sandhills prairie - a botanical crossroads found nowhere else on the continent. In October 2023, the Niobrara became the first river in the United States designated a Quiet Trail by nonprofit Quiet Parks International, recognized for the near-total absence of human-made noise. Rangers here are knowledgeable; ask them to sketch the best float route before you head to the river.

Fort Niobrara National Wildlife Refuge, four miles east of Valentine on Highway 12, protects 19,131 acres of prairie and canyon on the site of an 1880 military fort built to shield settlers from Sioux raids. A free driving tour winds through bison and elk pastures - herds of roughly 300 bison and several dozen elk are reliably visible from the road, often within 50 feet. The refuge launch site is the standard put-in for the most scenic Niobrara float: 26 miles of Class I-II water to Smith Falls, passing more than 90 side-canyon waterfalls and sandstone cliffs lined with paper birch and ponderosa pine.

Smith Falls State Park, 14 miles east of Valentine off Highway 12, protects Nebraska's highest waterfall. Smith Falls drops approximately 63 feet into a narrow side canyon before joining the Niobrara below - named for Frederic Smith, who homesteaded this land in the 1880s, and designated a state park in 1992. A footbridge across the Niobrara and an accessible boardwalk through a canyon of birch and pine lead to the base. The sound of the water carries long before you see it. This is also the primary take-out for float trips from Fort Niobrara.

activities.text: Rent a canoe or kayak in Valentine and float the Niobrara - the stretch from Fort Niobrara to Smith Falls runs about 26 miles and takes four to six hours. The river averages two to three feet deep, runs cold and clear year-round fed by the Ogallala Aquifer, and passes waterfalls, fossil-bearing sandstone cliffs, and canyon sections where the trees close overhead. Several outfitters on Highway 20 in Valentine provide gear and shuttle service. This is the defining experience of the Sandhills region.

In the evening, drive south on US-83 for 10 miles to take in the full scale of the Sandhills at dusk - 19,300 square miles of grass-stabilized sand dunes, the largest such formation in the Western Hemisphere, rolling to every horizon. The wind almost never stops. Merritt Reservoir, 26 miles southwest of Valentine, is a designated International Dark Sky Park; the Milky Way here on a clear night is as vivid as anywhere in the contiguous United States.

days_plan.itin_title: Nebraska Sandhills & Pioneer Trails: From Omaha to the Rockies

map_keywords: Niobrara National Scenic River Visitor Center Valentine
Fort Niobrara National Wildlife Refuge Valentine
Smith Falls State Park Valentine Nebraska

places.name: Vicksburg

visits.text: Established February 21, 1899, the Vicksburg National Military Park protects 1,800 acres of battlefield where Grant besieged Confederate defenders for 47 days - May 18 to July 4, 1863. When Vicksburg surrendered, the Confederacy was split in two and the Union held the entire Mississippi River. The park's 1,325 monuments and markers are second in number only to Gettysburg's, with 20 miles of reconstructed trenches and 144 emplaced cannon. Drive the 16-mile tour road first, then walk the key positions - the sight lines explain the siege more clearly than any text panel ever could.

The USS Cairo is the only surviving Civil War ironclad gunboat in the country, and it sits under a shelter inside the military park looking exactly as battered as you'd expect after 102 years submerged in the Yazoo River. Confederate forces sank it on December 12, 1862 with an electrically detonated mine - the first U.S. warship ever lost to a floating mine - going down in 12 minutes. It was raised in 1964 and transferred to the National Park Service in 1972. The attached museum holds thousands of crew artifacts recovered from the wreck: boots, combs, pocket knives, remarkably intact after a century in the mud. Budget 60 minutes.

General Pemberton reportedly watched Union naval attacks on the Mississippi from the bell tower of the Old Court House during the siege - and when Vicksburg fell on July 4, 1863, Grant's troops marched in and raised the Union flag from the same roof. The Greek Revival building at 1008 Cherry Street was constructed between 1858 and 1860 by enslaved craftsmen; it nearly came down in 1939 before historian Eva Whitaker Davis rallied the community and saved it, reopening it as a museum in 1948. Inside: Civil War newspapers printed on wallpaper when paper ran out, the tie Jefferson Davis wore at his Confederate presidential inauguration, and Native American Mississippian-culture ceramics. National Historic Landmark since 1968.

activities.text: Along Vicksburg's floodwall, artist Robert Dafford's 32 life-sized murals run for several hundred feet, first unveiled April 12, 2002. They depict the city's history from Native American settlement through the Civil War, the steamboat era, and the 20th century. Walk the entire length - each panel has a text plaque, and the Mississippi River overlooks at Louisiana Circle or Navy Circle off South Washington Street are among the most dramatic on the entire river. Lincoln called Vicksburg 'the key' to winning the war; standing above the water on the bluffs, you see why.

Solly's Hot Tamales on Washington Street has been open since 1939 - Vicksburg's version of the Delta tamale, made to a three-generation recipe that's subtly different from Greenville's. Dinner here or at one of the other restaurants along Washington Street closes a dense Civil War day with food that's rooted in the living culture of the region. The walkable stretch of Washington Street near the park has several good local options.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep South Road Trip: New Orleans and the Natchez Trace
Mississippi Delta Blues Trail: Memphis to Jackson
The Black Belt: Atlanta to New Orleans Through the Deep South
The Deep South via Mississippi: Shreveport to New Orleans - 10 Days

map_keywords: Old Court House Museum Vicksburg Cherry Street

places.name: Virginia

visits.text: Virginia City — Step into this authentically preserved 1860s mining boomtown perched on Mount Davidson's slopes. The National Historic Landmark preserves boardwalks, Victorian buildings, and saloons from when Comstock Lode silver discovery created Nevada Territory.

The Way It Was Museum — Explore mining equipment, minerals, and artifacts explaining how the Comstock Lode produced 500 million dollars in silver and gold. The museum occupies the fourth floor of the 1876 Knights of Pythias building.

Chollar Mine — Descend into a real silver mine with original 1860s square-set timbering still supporting tunnels. Guided tours explain mining techniques that extracted precious metals from 300 meters underground.

St. Mary in the Mountains Catholic Church — Visit this 1876 Gothic Revival church built with mining wealth featuring Italian marble and stained glass. The cathedral replaced an earlier church destroyed in the Great Fire of 1875.

activities.text: C Street walking tour — Stroll the main street lined with saloons, shops, and museums occupying original 1860s buildings. Wooden boardwalks and Victorian storefronts recreate Wild West atmosphere.
Virginia & Truckee Railroad ride — Board vintage steam trains running the historic route built in 1869 to haul Comstock ore. The scenic journey passes mining ruins with mountain views.
Saloon experience — Sample local beers at authentic Old West saloons with swinging doors and antique bars. Some establishments maintain 1860s character serving spirits to modern visitors.

days_plan.itin_title: Nevada's Great Contrasts: Las Vegas Neon to Red Rock Desert Silence

map_keywords: St. Mary in the Mountains Church

places.name: Virginia Beach Va

visits.text: To see the Cape Henry Lighthouse, the first lighthouse authorized by the U.S. Congress (Lighthouse Act of 1789) and the first public work of the new federal government, operational in 1792—you'll need to pass through military security. The lighthouse sits on Fort Story base: Gate 8, photo ID required, then free shuttle. Right next to it, a cross marks the First Landing site, where English colonists set foot on April 26, 1607, before sailing upriver to Jamestown.

First Landing State Park, north of Cape Henry, covers 2,888 acres of coastal forest with 19 miles of trails. Walk them early in the morning before the heat—the subtropical forest at this latitude is a rarity on the East Coast, with its live oaks and canopies that exist nowhere else in northern Virginia.

activities.text: Walk or bike the Virginia Beach boardwalk in the evening—3 miles along the Atlantic, seafood restaurants on both sides, waves as your soundtrack. The beach stretches 35 miles long: one of the world's longest urban beaches.

The Virginia Aquarium & Marine Science Center on General Booth Boulevard is worth 2 to 3 hours of your time: over 10,000 marine animals and an outdoor reconstruction of the Chesapeake marshes that you won't see anywhere else. Less crowded and cheaper than Baltimore's National Aquarium.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltimore to the Outer Banks - Historic America from Chesapeake Bay to Charlotte

map_keywords: First Landing State Park Virginia Beach

places.name: Waco

visits.text: Dr Pepper Museum: in 1885 - one year before Coca-Cola - a pharmacist named Charles Alderton was mixing fruit extracts in the back room of the Old Corner Drug Store in Waco. The drink was initially called 'Waco' before his boss renamed it Dr Pepper. The 1906 bottling plant that now houses the museum is listed on the National Register of Historic Places. Three floors of archives on the soda industry, a vintage soda fountain, and the chance to concoct your own soda in a workshop.

Waco Mammoth National Monument: in 1978, two men out collecting Indian arrowheads discovered a mammoth femur protruding from a ravine. What they found after 19 years of excavation is the only nursery herd of Columbian mammoths ever uncovered in the United States: 23 individuals, nearly all females and juveniles, likely victims of a Bosque River flood 70,000 years ago. The bones remain in place in their original positions - you tour them with a ranger under a large covered shelter.

Magnolia Market at the Silos: the former grain silos transformed into an urban market by Chip and Joanna Gaines popularized the concept on the TV series Fixer Upper. Whether you're a fan of the show or not, the site is worth a stop - manicured gardens, terrace food trucks, shops and a bakery. Plan an hour: it's larger and livelier than you'd expect.

activities.text: Tasting at Balcones Distilling: founded in 2008, Balcones is the distillery that put Texas craft whiskey back on the world map. Their True Blue, made from Hopi blue corn grown in Texas, has won multiple international awards. Guided tours let you see the copper stills and understand the cold-distillation process unique to their method.

days_plan.itin_title: Texas Triangle: Dallas, Austin, San Antonio & the Hill Country - 8 Days

map_keywords: Magnolia Market at the Silos Waco

places.name: Wall Sd

visits.text: The billboards start appearing 300 miles before Wall. Dorothy Hustead came up with the idea in 1936: her pharmacy was dying on the edge of US-16, so she put up signs promising free ice water, and cars started stopping. Today, 2 million visitors a year pour into these 75,000 square feet of madness—25-meter dinosaur, giant jackalope, mini chapel, Texan cafeteria. Coffee is still a nickel. Water is still free. It's kitsch, unapologetic, and thoroughly American.

Six miles down I-90, don't pass without stopping at Minuteman Missile National Historic Site. Beneath your feet lies a real 1960s nuclear silo, operational until 1991—rangers take you down into the underground Delta-01 command post and explain how it actually worked. Book on Recreation.gov before you leave: slots fill up the day before.

Enter via I-90 exit 131 and head straight to the Ben Reifel Visitor Center before doing anything else. Not out of obligation—but because understanding what you're about to see changes everything. The strata in the Badlands Wall record 75 million years of geological history: Cretaceous inland sea, Oligocene savanna, volcanic ash. The buttes erode at one inch per year. In 500,000 years, none of this will remain.

Big Badlands Overlook awaits just past the east entrance—and here you understand why the Lakota called this place Mako Sica, "bad land." The wall of buttes and pinnacles stretches as far as the eye can see, separating two prairies at different elevations. The town of Wall took its name from this natural cliff. Stop for 20 minutes; late afternoon light turns everything into amber blocks.

The Fossil Exhibit Trail runs half a mile and passes replicas of fossils in situ—prehistoric rhinos, camels, giant tortoises. This isn't a theme park: real fossils are still in the rock around you, except the NPS won't let you take them. The Badlands contain the world's richest collection of Oligocene mammal fossils. Wheelchair accessible.

Windows Overlook then Door Trail—skip neither. From the Windows, natural arches eroded at the top of the cliffs frame the lower prairie 500 feet below. The Door Trail (half-mile round trip) literally takes you through a breach in the Wall to emerge in the interior labyrinth of the formation. Bring water, hat, sunscreen—the sun beats down mercilessly.

Roberts Prairie Dog Town borders the road, and it's better than its name suggests. These social rodents live in organized underground cities with sentries, tunnels, and nurseries—John James Audubon described them as "the noisiest creature on the Great Plains." Stay in your car, approach slowly: they fear nothing and let you photograph them from 10 feet away.

Finish at Pinnacles Overlook, at the western end of the park, where the pinnacles—slender columns isolated by erosion—reach their most spectacular form. It's also in the nearby South Unit that one of the last Ghost Dances of 1890 was performed, a few weeks before the Wounded Knee massacre. We're not saying it's a joyful place—but we're telling you it's a powerful one.

activities.text: The Notch Trail (1.5 miles round trip) is the most spectacular in the park—and few people do it because it starts with a rope ladder fixed into a crack in the Wall. Push past that: on the other side, a narrow canyon opens onto a panoramic terrace overlooking the White River Badlands. Closed-toe shoes, hat, water. Leave before 8am in summer.

An hour before sunset, get back on the Badlands Loop Road (SD-240) and drive slowly, windows down. The buttes turn orange then brick red, bison and pronghorn come out to graze. Park randomly at overlooks and listen: wind through the rock formations, sometimes a coyote in the distance. It's the best time of day.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Hills & Badlands: Road Trip Through the Heart of the American West
Black Hills & Badlands: Road Trip Through the Heart of the American West

visits.text: Wamego OZ Museum - Where a Kansas Farm Girl Became Hollywood Legend: This whimsical museum houses over 2,000 artifacts telling the story of how L. Frank Baum's children's novel about a tornado-swept Kansas girl became one of the most-watched films in cinema history.

OZ Museum Collection - Original 1939 Movie Artifacts: Browse signed photographs by the film's stars, original L. Frank Baum books, and production memorabilia from the beloved 1939 movie that made 'There's no place like home' an American catchphrase.

activities.text: Downtown Wamego - Small Town Charm on the Prairie: Explore the charming main street of this Kansas community that has embraced its connection to one of America's most beloved stories.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

places.name: Washington Dc

visits.text: Smithsonian Institution Building (The Castle) - Your museum marathon begins at the founding building of the Smithsonian, but be warned: the Castle has been under renovation since February 2023 (its first major overhaul in 50 years). A temporary reopening is planned from May 22 to September 7, 2026 for the United States' 250th anniversary celebrations. If you're there during this window, you'll be able to visit the visitor center and the American Aspirations exhibition. This neo-Gothic red sandstone castle, designed by James Renwick Jr. (the same architect behind New York's St. Patrick's Cathedral), was completed in 1855. Don't miss James Smithson's crypt - this British scientist who never set foot in the United States bequeathed his entire fortune to establish what became the world's largest museum complex. His remains were brought back from Genoa in 1905. If the Castle is closed during your visit, admire the exterior facade and use the virtual visitor center on si.edu to plan your visits.

Enid A. Haupt Garden - Right behind the Castle, this 4-acre garden is a little gem many visitors miss. Since 1987, it has featured brick walkways, fountains, and meticulously maintained flower beds. The underground entrances to the African Art Museum and the Asian Art Museum are located here. It's the perfect spot to map out your program before tackling the museums.

National Museum of American History - This is where you'll see the Star-Spangled Banner, the original 9-by-10-meter flag that flew over Fort McHenry in 1814 and inspired the national anthem. This wool and cotton flag, sewn by Mary Pickersgill in Baltimore, is preserved in a special chamber with floor-to-ceiling glass walls. Note that photography is prohibited in this room. The museum houses 1.7 million objects - you won't be able to see everything in one day.

National Air and Space Museum - One of the world's most visited museums holds the planet's largest collection of aircraft and spacecraft: approximately 60,000 objects. Right from the entrance, the Boeing Milestones of Flight Hall puts you face-to-face with the showstoppers. Plan for at least 3 to 4 hours, and book your free timed-entry pass online - it's required.

National Museum of Natural History - The world's most visited natural history museum welcomes you with Henry, a 4-meter-tall, 11-ton mounted African elephant that has stood in the rotunda since 1959. The museum is larger than 18 football fields and houses the world's largest natural history collection. Free admission, no reservation required.

National Museum of African American History and Culture - Opened in 2016, this is the only national museum entirely devoted to African American history. The 10-story building (5 above ground, 5 below) is clad in a pierced bronze crown inspired by West African decorative metalwork. With more than 45,000 objects in its collection (3,500 on display), plan for a full day. Book your free timed-entry pass online - it's essential, as the museum often sells out.

National Museum of the American Indian - This building with golden limestone curves, designed to evoke natural rock formations, houses collections devoted to the indigenous peoples of the three Americas. The architecture itself deserves attention: prismatic windows cast light onto the interior walls, and a 36-meter-high central dome crowns the entrance hall. The outdoor space recreates a wetland landscape with plants used by indigenous nations.

National Museum of African Art - This underground museum, accessible from the Haupt Garden, is the only American national museum devoted exclusively to African art. You'll discover wood and bronze sculptures, textiles, ceremonial masks, and ceramics spanning millennia of artistic creation. The contemporary African collections are particularly strong - don't expect only ancient art.

National Museum of Asian Art (Freer Gallery and Arthur M. Sackler Gallery) - Connected by an underground passage, these two galleries form the Smithsonian's Asian art museum. The Freer Gallery holds the world's most important collection of Whistler's works, including the Peacock Room: a London reception room entirely decorated by James McNeill Whistler in 1877 - blue-green and gold walls, life-size painted peacocks, transported from London piece by piece. The Sackler Gallery displays Chinese ritual bronzes dating from the Shang Dynasty (around 1500 BC), Japanese ceramics, Mughal Indian miniatures, and calligraphy. Don't miss the Moongate Garden between the two buildings - a circular Chinese-inspired garden perfect for a break.

Smithsonian American Art Museum - Housed in the former Patent Office Building (an 1836 Greek Revival structure), this museum traces three centuries of American art. You'll see canvases by Georgia O'Keeffe, Edward Hopper, Winslow Homer, and Hudson River School artists. The folk art collection is one of the most important in the country. On the 3rd floor, the Luce Foundation Center displays more than 3,300 objects in glass cases - a visible storage facility open to the public.

National Portrait Gallery - In the same building (both museums share the Patent Office Building), the National Portrait Gallery displays portraits of significant figures in American history. Each president has their official portrait here. The interior courtyard covered by a Norman Foster-designed undulating glass roof is a perfect place to catch your breath between galleries.

Renwick Gallery - Steps from the White House, this Smithsonian American Art Museum gallery occupies an elegant 1859 Second Empire building, also designed by James Renwick Jr. It's devoted to contemporary American craft: blown glass, carved wood, textiles, ceramics, metalwork. Monumental installations often occupy entire rooms - the Grand Salon on the 2nd floor, with its salon-style floor-to-ceiling paintings, is worth seeing on its own.

National Postal Museum - Right across from Union Station, this often-overlooked museum is a gem. Housed in the former Washington main post office (a 1914 Beaux-Arts building), it traces the history of the American postal service from Pony Express riders to mail planes. You'll see stagecoaches, historic mailboxes, mail planes suspended from the ceiling, and one of the world's largest stamp collections. A playful museum, perfect if you're visiting with children.

Union Station - Even though it's not a museum, Washington's main train station deserves a stop. Inaugurated in 1907, it's a Beaux-Arts masterpiece with a 29-meter-high gilded coffered vault inspired by Rome's Baths of Diocletian.

Smithsonian's National Zoo - The National Zoo spans 66 hectares in the Woodley Park neighborhood, right in the heart of the city. Founded in 1889, it's one of the oldest zoos in the United States. It houses approximately 2,200 animals from 400 species, one-fifth of which are threatened. Like all Smithsonian sites, admission is free - but you must book a timed-entry pass online. Take the Metro (Woodley Park or Cleveland Park stations, red line).

Steven F. Udvar-Hazy Center (Chantilly, Virginia) - The only excursion outside Washington on this itinerary, and it's well worth the trip. This giant 70,000-square-meter hangar near Dulles Airport is the Air and Space Museum's annex - it houses aircraft too large for the Mall museum. You'll see the Space Shuttle Discovery (39 space missions over 27 years of service), the Lockheed SR-71 Blackbird (the fastest plane ever built, capable of flying at over Mach 3.3), the B-29 Enola Gay that dropped the atomic bomb on Hiroshima, and an Air France Concorde. Admission is free, parking costs $15. To get there by public transit, take the Metro's Silver Line to Innovation Center, then Fairfax Connector bus 983 which drops you at the entrance.

Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden - This 70-meter-diameter concrete cylinder, perched on four pillars 4 meters above ground, is Washington's first contemporary art museum. Designed by Gordon Bunshaft and opened in 1974, it houses more than 12,000 works. The collection was built from collector Joseph H. Hirshhorn's 1966 donation of 6,000 pieces. You'll see Picasso, Warhol, Rothko, de Kooning, Hopper. The visit flows naturally - you follow the circle on each floor.

Arts and Industries Building - Right next to the Castle, this Victorian-style building completed in 1881 is the oldest museum on the Mall. Designed by Adolf Cluss (who had already renovated the Castle after the 1865 fire), it hosted President Garfield's inaugural ball before it was even finished. After years of closure, it reopens intermittently for temporary exhibitions. The interior architecture is worth the trip: wrought-iron structure, vast halls of natural light, and decorative columns.

The National Mall memorials - For your last day, step out of the museums and take time to visit the monuments. The Lincoln Memorial (1922) with its 6-meter-high marble statue is at the west end of the Mall - this is where Martin Luther King delivered his "I Have a Dream" speech in 1963. The Washington Monument, a 169-meter obelisk completed in 1884, offers a panoramic view of the entire city (book free tickets online). The Vietnam Veterans Memorial (a black granite wall bearing the 58,318 names of soldiers killed) and the Korean War Veterans Memorial complete the circuit.

activities.text: Get your bearings on the National Mall - Use this first day to walk along the Mall and scout out the museums you'll visit in the coming days. From the Lincoln Memorial to the Capitol, you have about 3 km of walkway lined with free museums. Pass by the Washington Monument and the memorials to get an overview before diving into the collections.

Explore the pop culture collections - On the 3rd floor, you'll find the famous ruby slippers Dorothy wore in The Wizard of Oz, Abraham Lincoln's top hat, Muhammad Ali's boxing gloves, and Duke Ellington's sheet music. The Entertainment Nation gallery brings together 60 objects from American pop culture in 670 square meters. Don't miss Julia Child's kitchen either, reconstructed piece by piece.

Browse the presidential and First Ladies galleries - The American Presidency exhibition traces the history of presidents through personal and official objects. Right next door, the First Ladies gallery showcases the inaugural gowns worn at inaugural balls. You'll also see the desk on which Jefferson drafted the Declaration of Independence.

See the icons of aviation - The 1903 Wright Flyer stands at the center of its gallery: it's the very first powered aircraft to fly. During its 4th test at Kitty Hawk on December 17, 1903, piloted by Wilbur, it covered 852 feet (255.6 meters) in 59 seconds. Just above, Lindbergh's Spirit of St. Louis hangs suspended - the cockpit door is slightly ajar, at Lindbergh's own request. You'll also see the Blériot XI that crossed the English Channel in 1909.

Explore the conquest of space - You'll be able to touch a real piece of lunar rock brought back by the Apollo 17 mission. The suit Neil Armstrong wore during his first steps on the Moon is displayed here. Also look for John Glenn's Mercury Friendship 7 capsule (first American in orbit, 1962), the Apollo lunar module, and the F-1 engine from the Saturn V rocket. If you have time, the planetarium and IMAX theater are worth a visit.

Marvel at the Hope Diamond and the gem collection - In the Janet Annenberg Hooker Hall of Geology, Gems and Minerals, you'll see the Hope Diamond: a 45.52-carat deep blue diamond. This diamond was part of the French Crown Jewels under the name "Blue Diamond" since its purchase by Louis XIV in 1668. It was stolen during the French Revolution, recut, and changed hands several times before being donated to the Smithsonian in 1958. The hall also displays the Star of Asia sapphire, the Gachala emerald, and more than 7,500 individual gems. A sample from the asteroid Bennu is also on display.

Explore the Deep Time Hall of Fossils - The David H. Koch Hall of Fossils spans nearly 2,900 square meters and traces 4.6 billion years of Earth's history. You'll see 46 complete dinosaur specimens, including a T. rex and a Triceratops. The paleontology lab is visible from the gallery - you can watch scientists extract fossils in real time. Head up to the Sant Ocean Hall to see a life-size replica of a North Atlantic right whale.

Descend into the underground history galleries - The museum is visited from bottom to top, moving forward chronologically. On the underground levels, you'll traverse the history of slavery with a cabin from an Edisto Island plantation (South Carolina), Nat Turner's Bible (who led a slave rebellion in 1831), a segregated railroad car, and a guard tower from Angola Prison in Louisiana. You'll also see remnants of the São José, a slave ship discovered off the coast of South Africa.

Explore the culture and sports galleries - On the upper floors, the tone shifts. You'll find the shawl Queen Victoria gave to Harriet Tubman, Chuck Berry's red Cadillac, Gabby Douglas's leotard, Jesse Owens's spikes (1936 Olympics), and nine Olympic medals from Carl Lewis. The Musical Crossroads gallery traces African American influence from blues to hip-hop. The piano from August Wilson's play The Piano Lesson is also on display - a piano on which an enslaved man had carved his family's history.

Browse the exhibitions on indigenous nations - The Americans exhibition traces the presence of Native Americans in popular culture: sports team names, films, brands, place names. If you're going with children, the imagiNATIONS space offers interactive activities. The permanent collections feature more than 800,000 objects ranging from the Arctic to Tierra del Fuego.

Lunch at the Mitsitam Café - This is probably the best cafeteria of all the Smithsonian museums. The menu offers dishes inspired by indigenous culinary traditions, with five themed stations (North, South, Great Plains, Forests, Meso-America). A real meal, not just a sandwich break.

Compare artistic traditions from two continents - Take advantage of this day to observe parallels between African and Asian art: Benin bronzework versus Chinese ritual bronzes, Congolese Kuba textiles versus Japanese silks. Both museums are less crowded compared to the Mall's giants - you'll have peace and quiet.

Wander through the Penn Quarter neighborhood - Leaving the museum, you're in one of Washington's liveliest neighborhoods. Take the opportunity to explore the area's art galleries and Chinatown, whose grand entrance arch is just steps away. It's also a good neighborhood for dinner.

View the White House from Lafayette Square - Leaving the Renwick, cross the street: you're facing the White House. Lafayette Square offers the clearest view of the north facade. Take time to walk around via the Ellipse on the south side for another perspective. You can't go inside, but the proximity is striking.

Stroll along Pennsylvania Avenue - From the White House to the Capitol, Pennsylvania Avenue is Washington's ceremonial artery. This is where presidential motorcades pass on inauguration day. Along the way, you'll pass the Old Post Office, the FBI Building, and the National Archives (where the Declaration of Independence and Constitution are displayed - free admission).

Explore the Capitol Hill neighborhood - From Union Station, head toward the Capitol through residential streets lined with Victorian brick houses. This is locals' Washington, far from the touristy Mall. The Eastern Market (open Saturdays and Sundays) is a good spot for fresh produce, crafts, and street food.

See giant pandas Bao Li and Qing Bao - The two pandas, who arrived from China in October 2024, have been the zoo's stars since their public debut in January 2025. Bao Li (born August 4, 2021) is the grandson of Mei Xiang and Tian Tian, who lived here for 23 years. Qing Bao (born September 12, 2021) is more independent - you'll often find her perched in trees. The Smithsonian's panda program has lasted more than 50 years and helped move the species from "endangered" to "vulnerable" status.

Walk the Asia Trail and other exhibits - The Asia Trail brings together seven Asian species: sloth bears, red pandas, clouded leopards, otters, and more. Continue to the great apes, Asian elephants (in the Elephant Trails habitat opened in 2013), big cats, and the aviary. Plan for the morning - animals are more active before noon.

Climb the Donald D. Engen observation tower - From the top, you have a panoramic view of Dulles Airport runways and surroundings. It's also a chance to watch planes take off and land - a spectacle that fascinates children and adults alike.

Watch historic aircraft restoration - The Mary Baker Engen Restoration Hangar is visible from a glass-walled balcony. You can watch Smithsonian specialists restore historic aircraft - precision work that sometimes takes years per plane.

Visit the sculpture garden - The 5,300-square-meter Sculpture Garden, sunken 2 to 4 meters below street level, is a space unto itself. You'll find Rodin's Burghers of Calais - one of twelve original castings of this 1889 bronze sculpture depicting six notables offering their lives to save their city during the Hundred Years' War. Also look for Alexander Calder's mobiles and stabiles, Henry Moore's organic forms, and sculptures by Matisse, Giacometti, and Jeff Koons.

Discover Barbara Kruger's permanent installation - On the lower level, Belief+Doubt covers walls, floors, and staircases with black, white, and red texts that question power, consumption, and beliefs. It's one of Washington's most photographed installations.

Taste Washington's culinary specialties - Use this penultimate day to explore DC's food scene. The half-smoke is the iconic local sausage, half-pork half-beef, served grilled with mustard and onions. Mumbo sauce (a sweet-spicy condiment typical of DC) accompanies fried chicken wings. And if you haven't tried Ethiopian food yet, the area around U Street is home to the largest Ethiopian community outside Africa - the injera and spicy stews are remarkable.

Stroll through the Mall gardens - The Mary Livingston Ripley Garden (between the Hirshhorn and the Arts and Industries Building) and the Katherine Dulin Folger Rose Garden (behind the Castle) are two small gardens often overlooked. In late afternoon, the Mall empties out and the golden light on museum facades is superb.

Walk around the Tidal Basin - If you're visiting between late March and mid-April, the cherry blossoms around the Tidal Basin are an extraordinary sight - approximately 3,000 trees gifted by Japan in 1912. Even out of season, the loop around the basin (about 3 km) passes the Jefferson Memorial, the Martin Luther King Jr. Memorial, and the Franklin Delano Roosevelt Memorial. Allow 1.5 hours to see everything at a relaxed pace.

Final Mall pass at sunset - The Mall is even more beautiful in late afternoon. The low light on the Capitol at one end and the Lincoln Memorial at the other creates a panorama you won't forget. It's the best way to conclude 15 days at the heart of American history and culture.
