visits.text: Explore picturesque Waynesville, sandwiched between the Great Smoky Mountains and the Blue Ridge Mountains. This charming little town in Haywood County captivates visitors with sublime mountain views and a welcoming atmosphere that embodies the spirit of Southern Appalachian hospitality.

Catch an entertaining live performance at Haywood Arts Regional Theater, where talented local and regional artists showcase their skills. This celebrated venue brings cultural enrichment to the mountain community and offers visitors an authentic taste of Appalachian arts and entertainment.

activities.text: Experience downtown Waynesville's bustling atmosphere featuring an eclectic mix of galleries, antique shops, and local boutiques. Visit Haywood's Historic Farmers Market to meet hospitable locals, sample fresh farm produce, and discover unique heritage crafts that make perfect mementos of your mountain visit.

days_plan.itin_title: North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains

visits.text: Take exit 4 off I-80 and drive north. Suddenly, nothing—just white as far as you can see. The Bonneville Salt Flats cover 120 square kilometers, a salt crust left by the evaporation of ancient Lake Bonneville 15,000 years ago. Since 1914 people have been setting speed records here; in 1970 someone hit 1,001 km/h. In dry weather you can drive right onto it. In wet weather, it becomes a perfect mirror reflecting the sky to infinity. No services on-site—grab water in Wendover first.

A few kilometers away, the Historic Wendover Airfield Museum preserves the memory of one of World War II's most secret bases. This is where the 509th Composite Group trained before dropping the atomic bombs in 1945. At its peak: 20,000 people on this base in the middle of the salt desert. You can climb the control tower and see a replica of the Little Boy bomb in the hangar.

days_plan.itin_title: Salt Lake City to Great Basin Road Trip: Desert, Salt & Stars

places.name: West Branch

visits.text: Herbert Hoover National Historic Site—The 31st U.S. President was born in this tiny two-room house in 1874. His father was a blacksmith. The original cottage, remarkably small, gives a tangible sense of the modest beginnings. Around it, a tallgrass prairie has been recreated to show the landscape Hoover saw as a child.

Herbert Hoover Presidential Library & Museum—The museum traces the life of a man who lived many lives: mining engineer in Australia and China, humanitarian relief coordinator during World War I, then president during the Great Depression. The Depression exhibit is especially well done.

Walk the site's loop trail—the blacksmith shop, Quaker meetinghouse, and the graves of Herbert and Lou Hoover, on a wooded hilltop overlooking the site, can be seen in 30 minutes. The grounds are peaceful and well-maintained.

activities.text: Stroll through the recreated tallgrass prairie—In summer, the grasses reach shoulder height and wildflowers attract butterflies and birds. A marked trail loops around in 30 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa Reimagined: From Glacial Lakes to Mississippi Bluffs

map_keywords: Herbert Hoover National Historic Site West Branch

visits.text: Glacier National Park — Enter this 4,100-square-kilometer wilderness preserving pristine ecosystems from prairie to alpine peaks. The Crown of the Continent features 700 lakes, 25 active glaciers, and abundant wildlife including grizzlies, wolves, and mountain goats.

Going-to-the-Sun Road — Drive this engineering marvel crossing the Continental Divide at 2,026-meter Logan Pass. The 80-kilometer scenic road completed in 1932 hugs cliff faces with stunning glacier and valley views.

Lake McDonald — Photograph the park's largest lake stretching 16 kilometers with mountains reflected in pristine waters. The glacially-carved lake reaches 141 meters deep surrounded by old-growth cedar and hemlock forests.

Trail of the Cedars — Walk this wheelchair-accessible boardwalk through ancient rainforest featuring 500-year-old western red cedars. The 1.1-kilometer loop crosses Avalanche Creek on a suspension bridge.

Logan Pass — Hike from the Continental Divide summit where snow persists into July. The alpine visitor center offers exhibits and access to Hidden Lake Trail through wildflower meadows frequented by mountain goats.
Hidden Lake Overlook — Trek 4.8 kilometers roundtrip to dramatic views of turquoise Hidden Lake surrounded by peaks. The trail crosses alpine tundra revealing bighorn sheep and hoary marmots.
Jackson Glacier Overlook — View one of Glacier's most accessible glaciers from this roadside pullout. The ice field has receded significantly but remains impressive against the Continental Divide.
St. Mary Falls — Hike 2.6 kilometers through forest to this 10-meter waterfall cascading over bedrock. The trail follows Rose Creek through wildflower meadows with mountain views.

Lewis and Clark Caverns State Park — Tour Montana's largest known limestone cavern featuring stalactites, stalagmites, and helictites. The 90-minute guided walk descends into chambers carved by water over millions of years.
Gates of the Mountains — Boat through this section of the Missouri River where limestone cliffs rise 370 meters. Lewis and Clark named the formation in 1805 during their expedition upriver.
Helena historic downtown — Explore Montana's capital featuring Victorian architecture from gold rush prosperity. Last Chance Gulch preserves 1880s buildings housing shops and restaurants.

activities.text: Wildlife watching — Scan meadows and roadside for grizzly bears, black bears, bighorn sheep, and mountain goats. Early morning and evening offer best viewing opportunities along Going-to-the-Sun Road.
Apgar Village exploration — Browse visitor center exhibits, rent boats, and dine at this lakeside village. The park's west entrance hub offers services and lake access.
Scenic boat tour — Cruise historic wooden boats across pristine lakes hearing park history and geology from naturalist guides. Tours operate on Lake McDonald and St. Mary Lake.
Evening ranger program — Attend talks about grizzly bears, glaciers, or night skies at park amphitheaters. Rangers share ecological knowledge and conservation challenges.
Huckleberry treats — Sample Montana's signature berry in ice cream, pie, or jam at park lodges. Wild huckleberries ripen in late summer throughout Glacier's forests.
Scenic drive return — Navigate mountain passes and valleys back to Glacier through heart of the Rockies. The journey showcases Montana's vast wilderness and big sky.
Farewell Montana dinner — Celebrate the journey with bison ribeye or grilled trout at a mountain lodge. Montana cuisine emphasizes wild game and locally raised meats.

days_plan.itin_title: Montana's Wild Landscapes: Glacier National Park to Yellowstone Geysers
Montana's Wild Landscapes: Glacier National Park to Yellowstone Geysers
Montana's Wild Landscapes: Glacier National Park to Yellowstone Geysers

places.name: West Palm Beach

visits.text: West Palm Beach - Florida's Emerging Cultural Capital: This rapidly developing city has cultivated world-class dining, entertainment venues, and high-end shopping while maintaining easy access to pristine Atlantic beaches.

Palm Beach - Billionaire's Row: Across the Intracoastal Waterway, Palm Beach proper showcases some of America's most valuable real estate, with beachfront mansions owned by the nation's wealthiest families.

John D. MacArthur Beach State Park - Preserved Paradise: This barrier island park protects one of the last remaining undeveloped stretches of the Palm Beach coast, with hiking trails, kayaking, and swimming on a pristine Atlantic beach.

Norton Museum of Art - World-Class Collection in the Tropics: This recently expanded museum houses significant European, American, and Chinese art collections in stunning galleries designed for natural light.

activities.text: Jupiter - Palm Beach's Best-Kept Secret: This small northern town offers outstanding beaches, historic lighthouse tours, and a laid-back atmosphere that contrasts with Palm Beach's formality.
Delray Beach and Boca Raton - Atlantic Avenue Strolling: These neighboring beach communities offer vibrant downtown districts filled with restaurants, boutiques, and galleries steps from the ocean.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate Florida Keys Road Trip: From Miami to Key West

map_keywords: John D. MacArthur Beach State Park

visits.text: Experience White Sands National Park, where the world's largest gypsum dune field creates a surreal landscape of brilliant white waves stretching across 275 square miles of the Tularosa Basin. The pristine white dunes, constantly shifting with the wind, provide an otherworldly environment unlike anywhere else on Earth. This rare ecosystem supports uniquely adapted plants and animals found nowhere else.

Walk the Interdune Boardwalk and Dune Life Nature Trail to discover how life persists in this seemingly barren landscape of pure white gypsum crystals. Interpretive signs explain the geological processes that created this remarkable feature and the adaptations that allow plants and animals to survive in such an extreme environment.

activities.text: Sled down the pristine white dunes on specially designed sand discs available at the visitor center, creating unforgettable memories in one of the most photogenic landscapes in America. Sunset transforms the white sands into a canvas of pink, orange, and purple hues, offering photographers extraordinary opportunities for capturing the desert's ethereal beauty.

days_plan.itin_title: New Mexico Odyssey: Pueblo Culture, Volcanic Fields and White Sands

visits.text: Start with downtown Whitefish—Architectural Digest named it one of America's 25 most beautiful small towns, and you'll quickly see why. Central Avenue packs art galleries, boutiques, and restaurants into an authentic mountain-town setting. Don't miss the Whitefish Depot, the historic rail station where Amtrak's Empire Builder (Chicago-Seattle line) still stops. The small museum inside chronicles the town's history as a railroad settlement founded in the early twentieth century.

Head down to City Beach, the public beach at the end of Whitefish Lake. The lake stretches 11 km long by 4 km wide, with a maximum depth of 68 meters. The water runs clear and the beach is lifeguarded in summer—you'll see families, paddleboarders, and kayakers. If you prefer a wilder setting, push on to Whitefish Lake State Park on the west shore.

Last big hiking day: rise early to tackle the Iceberg Lake trail, one of the finest hikes in all of Glacier National Park. The trailhead sits behind Swiftcurrent Motor Inn in the Many Glacier valley. Figure 16 km round-trip with 390 m elevation gain—it's rated moderate and doable in 5 to 6 hours. The trail crosses fir forests, wildflower meadows, and follows the Ptarmigan Wall, a 450-meter-high ridge carved by glaciers. At the end, Iceberg Lake awaits at 1,858 m elevation, wedged between 900-meter cliffs—ice blocks float on its surface even in midsummer because sunlight almost never reaches the water. If you're brave, dip your feet—the water is frigid but invigorating.

The Many Glacier area is the park's best spot for observing grizzly bears. The Iceberg Lake trail corridor is particularly active in summer when huckleberry bushes are loaded with berries. Make noise while walking, keep your bear spray accessible (not in your backpack), and hike in a group if possible. If the trail is closed for bear activity (this happens regularly in July-August), fall back on the Cracker Lake Trail (19 km R/T) whose intensely turquoise lake is worth the effort.

Last morning in Whitefish before departure. If your flight is late in the day, ride the gondola to the summit of Whitefish Mountain Resort (15 minutes' drive from downtown). In summer the resort offers scenic lift rides, a zip-line course (Montana's longest), alpine slides, and a network of mountain biking trails. From the Summit House at the top, the view sweeps across the entire Flathead Valley with the mountains of Glacier Park in the distance.

If you'd rather stay in town, the Whitefish Trail offers a network of hiking and biking trails circling the town. The Living Wetland Interpretive Nature Trail loop is easy and short—perfect for a last peaceful walk through the wetlands bordering the Whitefish River.

activities.text: Spend the evening touring downtown's craft breweries. Whitefish has several microbreweries that close at 8pm (Montana law for craft breweries). Sample the huckleberry specialties—this wild local berry shows up in beers, ice creams, and even vodkas.

Pack your gear for the coming days: buy bear spray at one of the downtown outdoor shops (figure around forty dollars), verify your camping reservations if you're camping, and note that gas isn't available inside Glacier National Park.

After the hike, treat yourself to ice cream at the Swiftcurrent Motor Inn—you'll have earned it. Then take the road back to Whitefish via US-89 south and US-2 west (about 2.5 hours driving). Enjoy the evening dining at one of Whitefish's downtown restaurants. The town is known for its culinary scene—several local chefs have earned James Beard Foundation Award nominations.

Make a final tour of Central Avenue's shops to pick up souvenirs—Montana t-shirts and huckleberry products are the classics. Glacier Park International Airport (FCA) is only 15 minutes' drive from Whitefish, giving you plenty of margin to enjoy the morning.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Rockies Loop: Glacier & Waterton
Northern Rockies Loop: Glacier & Waterton
Northern Rockies Loop: Glacier & Waterton

visits.text: You cross one of two cable-stay pedestrian bridges - each shaped like a bow and arrow - to reach the 5-ton steel figure that Kiowa-Comanche artist Blackbear Bosin donated to the city in 1974. The plaza around the base describes Plains Indian ways of life through interpretive panels. Bosin designed the piece in 1968 and lived only six years after its dedication before dying in 1980. Access is free around the clock until midnight, and the nightly Ring of Fire makes the after-dark visit worth planning for.

Step onto the dirt roads of this 23-acre open-air museum and you are in Wichita between 1865 and 1880, when the Chisholm Trail ran right through here. Over 60 buildings line the grounds - more than 40 of them fully furnished - and costumed interpreters demonstrate blacksmithing, printing, and carpentry in real time. Established in 1952, it holds accreditation from the American Alliance of Museums, a distinction fewer than 3 percent of U.S. museums earn. Go on a weekday morning to avoid school groups.

You ride a glass elevator past Ivan the T. rex skeleton to reach three floors of a restored 1914 brick warehouse in Old Town, where an Egyptian mummy, a 34-foot Tylosaurus fossil from the Kansas Cretaceous sea, Roman emperor coins, and a section of the Berlin Wall share the same building. The museum opened in 2001 as the Museum of Ancient Treasures, moved to this Old Town address in 2003, and now draws around 45,000 visitors a year. It specializes in nothing - and that eclecticism is the whole point.

The largest art museum in Kansas opened in 1935 on an endowment left by Louise Caldwell Murdock, who in 1915 instructed that her estate fund an American art collection in memory of her husband. Today the permanent collection exceeds 10,000 works - Edward Hopper, Georgia O'Keeffe, Winslow Homer, Mary Cassatt, and Charles Russell among them. General admission to the permanent collection is free, and the Art Garden with outdoor sculpture by Tom Otterness and Henry Moore is open 24 hours.

You walk into a $62 million building that Canadian architect Moshe Safdie designed specifically to sit on the banks of the Arkansas River - the geometric toroid shape is a deliberate response to the waterfront site. The museum opened April 1, 2000, and Safdie later applied lessons from it to the Crystal Bridges Museum in Bentonville and the Kauffman Center in Kansas City. Inside, the 'Design, Build, Fly' exhibit lets you rivet an airplane wing, test landing gear, and design an aircraft seat - Wichita-specific content for a city where Cessna, Beechcraft, and Learjet all built their planes. The Boeing Dome Theater shows films on a 60-foot, 360-degree screen; dome tickets can be purchased separately.

Step into the museum adjacent to the Keeper of the Plains and you are looking at 39 original works by Blackbear Bosin - the Kiowa-Comanche artist who created the sculpture outside. The permanent collection also includes traditional Plains Indian beadwork, ceremonial objects, and historical photography. The museum itself was the direct result of community organizing by Native residents of Wichita through the 1960s, who lobbied the city for a gathering space where all Plains tribes could share their culture.

Cross the Arkansas River to Delano, which was founded in the 1870s just outside Wichita's city limits precisely so that gambling, brothels, and saloons could operate without interference from Wichita lawmen. Train-masters who visited the establishments are said to have carried their red caboose lanterns, which is where the expression 'red-light district' comes from. Wichita annexed Delano in 1880. Today it is an eclectic stretch of independently owned shops, galleries, and restaurants along West Douglas Avenue, with public art celebrating the Chisholm Trail throughout.

activities.text: Walk the brick-lined grid of Old Town after dinner and you are in one of the American Planning Association's 'Great Places in America' - a designation it received in 2008 for its adaptive reuse of late-19th-century warehouses. The Hotel at Old Town was the Keen Kutter brand warehouse in 1906, billed at the time as the largest warehouse in the world, and its lobby still displays the largest authentic Keen Kutter collection in existence. Start at River City Brewing Co. or Nortons Brewing Co. before working your way through more than 100 businesses in the district.

Follow the trail markers embedded in the sidewalks and you are walking the original Chisholm Trail, which ran along the east side of Wichita from 1867 to 1871. Jesse Chisholm, for whom the trail is named, established a trading post here in the 1860s; Wichita was incorporated in 1870 and became a Texas cattle railhead in 1872. Pick up the self-guided map at the Wichita-Sedgwick County Historical Museum, housed in the original 1890 City Hall with its 170-foot clock tower.

Rent a kayak or bike from Boats and Bikes on the river and you can reach all five Museums on the River institutions - Exploration Place, the Wichita Art Museum, Old Cowtown Museum, Botanica Wichita, and the Mid-America All-Indian Center - on a 2.5-mile paved loop. The cable-stay pedestrian bridges crossing both rivers to the Keeper plaza were specifically designed in the shape of bows and arrows, part of the $20 million restoration completed in 2007. The loop is free to walk year-round; Boats and Bikes operates April through October.

Choose your farewell meal from three Wichita institutions. Old Mill Tasty Shop at 604 E. Douglas has run its marble-countered soda fountain since 1932, with blue-plate specials changing daily and malts mixed by hand. NuWay Burgers on West Douglas Avenue has served its signature 'crumbled' ground beef patty - unique to the chain - since 1930. In Delano, Hopping Gnome Brewing brews small-batch beers in a 1920s commercial building on West Douglas. All three are within easy reach of ICT airport for an early departure.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends
Kansas Flint Hills & Frontier: Wichita to Dodge City
Kansas Flint Hills & Frontier: Wichita to Dodge City

places.name: Williamsburg Va

visits.text: Colonial Williamsburg isn't a theme park—it's a real colonial town reconstructed on 301 acres that you'll explore on foot all day. It contains 89 original 18th-century buildings and hundreds of faithful reconstructions, thanks to funding from John D. Rockefeller Jr. since 1927. Buy your ticket in advance: it grants access to buildings and demonstrations.

At the Colonial Williamsburg Capitol, a costumed guide explains why Virginia outpaced the entire nation. On May 15, 1776—50 days before the national Declaration—the Virginia delegation here received orders to vote for independence. The burgesses' chamber, with its wooden benches, is faithfully reproduced.

The Governor's Palace deserves as much time for its 10-acre formal gardens as for the interior—plan at least 90 minutes for both. Seven royal governors resided here, then Patrick Henry and Thomas Jefferson as the first elected governors. Jefferson's library, recreated identically, is a detail that stands out.

Bruton Parish Church on Duke of Gloucester Street may be an original 1715 building, but it still welcomes worshippers today—enter to see the pews where Washington and Jefferson prayed. The parish register goes back to 1662. Free admission, volunteer guides on site.

Step through the door of the Raleigh Tavern—rebuilt on its original foundations—to understand where revolutionary decisions were really made. Members of the House of Burgesses gathered here after the royal governor shut down Parliament to silence them. Next door, the Raleigh Tavern Bakery still serves period gingerbread cakes.

activities.text: Spend time with Colonial Williamsburg's artisans: blacksmith, printer, cooper, wigmaker. These aren't extras—they're true masters working with 18th-century tools. Try some tasks if the opportunity arises. The musket-firing demonstration on Market Square is particularly striking.

In the evening, book a table at one of the four historic taverns—Chowning's, King's Arms, Shields, or Christiana Campbell's. Colonial menu: crab stew, cider chicken, sweet potato pie, beeswax candles. Reservations required, especially in summer. Budget $35 to $50 per person.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltimore to the Outer Banks - Historic America from Chesapeake Bay to Charlotte

map_keywords: College of William and Mary Williamsburg

places.name: Williston

visits.text: Twenty-five miles southwest of Williston, right on the North Dakota-Montana border, Fort Union Trading Post National Historic Site rises from the floodplain with its white palisade walls and red-roofed towers exactly as the original looked in 1851. John Jacob Astor's American Fur Company built Fort Union in 1828, and for 39 years it controlled the buffalo robe and fur trade across a vast Northern Plains territory — over 25,000 robes and $100,000 in merchandise changing hands annually. Assiniboine, Crow, Blackfeet, Mandan, and six other nations traded here. The reconstructed Bourgeois House holds museum exhibits; rangers in period dress staff the trade room. Free admission.

Two miles downstream, Fort Buford State Historic Site marks where the U.S. Army dismantled Fort Union in 1867 — using its own timber and stone to build Buford — and where Sitting Bull surrendered on July 19, 1881, famously handing his rifle to his young son first before giving it to the commander. A small museum holds the original surrender document. The Missouri-Yellowstone Confluence Interpretive Center, a half-mile further, stands at the exact meeting point of the two rivers where Lewis arrived on April 26, 1805, calling it the most remarkable river junction in North America. Free state park entry.

The drive back to Williston from Fort Union follows Route 1804 through Missouri River bottomland that looks much as Karl Bodmer painted it during his 1833 journey up the river with Prince Maximilian of Wied. The cottonwood galleries, oxbow ponds, and sandbar configurations remain relatively intact through this corridor. Williston itself was the epicenter of the 2010s Bakken oil boom; the Frontier Museum on 2nd Avenue West — an open-air village of restored homestead buildings from the 1800s to 1950s — and the James Memorial Art Center document the territory's earlier history.

activities.text: The Missouri River bottomlands around the Fort Union-Fort Buford corridor are some of the least-disturbed riparian habitat remaining on the Upper Missouri — cottonwood galleries, oxbow ponds, and sandbars that look much as they did in 1805. Walk the park trails from Fort Union toward the river in the early morning and you'll likely see white-tailed deer, wild turkey, and migrating waterfowl. Bald eagles are common year-round in the cottonwood groves.

The annual Fort Union Rendezvous in June draws hundreds of buckskinners and mountain man reenactors to the site for two days of 19th-century fur trade demonstrations, flintlock shooting, and period camp life. If your dates align, it's one of the most historically vivid events on the entire Lewis and Clark Trail. Outside of that, the Williston Saturday farmers' market at city park runs through summer and serves both the oil-field and ranching communities — unusually good produce for this latitude.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

Fort Buford State Historic Site Williston

visits.text: Stroll along the historic Riverwalk in downtown Wilmington, a picturesque waterfront promenade stretching along the Cape Fear River. This charming walkway winds through the city's beautifully preserved historic district, offering stunning river views, unique shops, and glimpses of antebellum architecture. The Riverwalk perfectly captures the essence of Southern coastal charm.

activities.text: Explore Wilmington's historic downtown district, where centuries-old buildings house boutiques, galleries, and eateries along tree-lined streets. The city's rich maritime heritage and well-preserved architecture make it one of North Carolina's most photogenic destinations. Take time to admire the blend of antebellum mansions and waterfront charm that defines this coastal gem.

days_plan.itin_title: North Carolina Road Trip: From Outer Banks Beaches to Blue Ridge Mountains

visits.text: Wilson Lake - The Clearest Lake in Kansas: Red sandstone cliffs and pillars interrupt the shoreline of this remarkably clear reservoir, creating scenery more reminiscent of the Southwest than the Great Plains.

Wilson State Park - Where Smoky Hills Meet Crystal Waters: Lake lovers fish, paddle, boat, and swim while mountain bikers conquer the 25-mile Switchgrass Trail that snakes through canyons carved into the surrounding Smoky Hills.

activities.text: Switchgrass Trail - 25 Miles of Mountain Biking Adventure: This challenging trail system winds through Smoky Hills canyons, offering technical terrain and scenic views rarely associated with Kansas.

Cheney Lake Sailing - Kansas's Premier Wind Sports Destination: Steady prairie breezes have made this lake one of the nation's top sailing and windsurfing destinations, drawing a dedicated seafaring following.

days_plan.itin_title: Kansas Prairie Odyssey: Tallgrass Seas, Cowtowns & Western Legends

visits.text: New England Air Museum - The Largest Aviation Collection in the Northeast: Explore an extraordinary assemblage of aircraft, helicopters, engines, and memorabilia spanning the entire history of flight. Highlights include the oldest surviving aircraft in America (a hot air balloon basket) and the oldest Connecticut-constructed airplane.

Historic Aircraft Collection - From Pioneers to Jets: Walk among vintage fighters, bombers, and experimental aircraft in massive hangars, with knowledgeable volunteer docents-many former pilots and aviation professionals-ready to share stories behind each machine.

activities.text: Redbird Flight Simulator - Take the Controls: Experience the thrill of piloting through this realistic simulator, while younger visitors can build paper airplanes and participate in family-friendly aviation scavenger hunts throughout the facility.

days_plan.itin_title: Connecticut Coastal Drive: From Maritime Mystic to Ivy League New Haven

places.name: Winslow

visits.text: El Rancho Hotel (Gallup, NM) - This 1937 motel, nicknamed the 'Playground of the Stars,' hosted Hollywood stars during western film shoots in the 1940s-50s. Each room is named after a star - John Wayne, Katharine Hepburn, Ronald Reagan. Even if you don't stay overnight, the lobby and its hunting trophies are worth a look.

Petrified Forest National Park - Don't miss this park protecting the world's largest concentration of petrified wood: 225-million-year-old tree trunks transformed into colorful quartz crystals. The 28-mile scenic drive also crosses the Painted Desert - hills striped with red, purple, and white. Don't take anything: it's illegal and the park estimates it loses 12 tons of petrified wood annually to visitors.

Standing on the Corner Park (Winslow, AZ) - 'Take it easy, take it easy / Standing on the corner in Winslow, Arizona...' - the 1972 Eagles song saved this town from decline. A bronze statue and mural mark the street corner. Five-minute photo stop, but the song will stick in your head all day.

activities.text: Stay at La Posada (Winslow) - If you can book a room at this 1929 former Harvey House hotel, designed by architect Mary Colter, it's the most beautiful lodging on the entire Route 66. The Turquoise Room restaurant serves refined Southwest cuisine. Even without staying overnight, visit the gardens and lobby.

days_plan.itin_title: Route 66: Chicago to Santa Monica

map_keywords: El Rancho Hotel Gallup New Mexico
Standing on the Corner Park Winslow Arizona

visits.text: You arrive at a 6,100-square-foot museum that opened in May 2015, standing within 100 feet of the modest four-room house where Marion Morrison was born on May 26, 1907. Inside, you find the world's largest public collection of John Wayne memorabilia - film costumes, original scripts, his personal station wagon, and 24 actual seats salvaged from Grauman's Chinese Theatre at a 2013 auction. Budget 75 minutes to take in both the museum galleries and the adjacent birthplace home, where a docent shares stories of the Duke's early years. Admission charged; open daily 10am-5pm.

You drive a few miles west of Winterset to the Roseman Bridge, built in 1883 - the most photographed of Madison County's six surviving covered bridges and the one featured prominently in the 1995 film. Walk through it slowly. Take your time. This is what Iowa's backroads look like at their finest. Free access, open year-round.

activities.text: You wander the Winterset Historic Square, designated a National Historic District in 2015. At its center stands an 1870s limestone courthouse crowned with a distinctive dome, ringed by locally-owned shops and cafes. Pick up lunch here before heading east to Des Moines. The Iowa Quilt Museum and the old county jail - now home to the 1st Avenue Collective gallery - both sit within a block of the courthouse square.

days_plan.itin_title: Iowa Backroads: From the Missouri Bluffs to the Mississippi Cliffs

visits.text: Upper Dells Sandstone Gorges - The Wisconsin River carved these canyons through 500-million-year-old Cambrian sandstone. The walls rise 100-130 feet and take spectacular shapes: columns, arches, mushroom rocks. The only way to see them is by boat - the shores are private and protected.

Witches Gulch - Accessible only from the cruise, this narrow gorge is carpeted with moss and ferns. A wooden walkway winds between sandstone walls so close together there's almost never direct sunlight. Photographer H.H. Bennett discovered and named it in the 1860s - his photos launched tourism at Wisconsin Dells.

Stand Rock - An isolated sandstone pillar facing a cliff. Since 1886, a trained dog has jumped the gap between cliff and rock to recreate Bennett's legendary photograph. It's Wisconsin's most iconic image.

activities.text: Upper Dells Cruise - The 2-hour tour goes upriver between sandstone cliffs, with two land stops: Witches Gulch and Stand Rock. The guide tells Native American legends, raftsmen (log floaters) history, and canyon geology. Departs from downtown.
Lower Dells Cruise (optional) - The 1-hour tour goes downriver passing Hawk's Bill, the Rocky Islands, and other formations. Less spectacular than the Upper Dells but complementary.

days_plan.itin_title: Wisconsin's Essential Wonders

places.name: Wolf Point Mt

visits.text: Wolf Point is the largest town on the Fort Peck Indian Reservation, home to Assiniboine and Sioux communities who have lived along the Missouri since before Lewis and Clark passed through in May 1805. The Wild Horse Stampede, held each July, is the oldest rodeo in Montana — running since 1915. Outside of rodeo season the town has a small regional museum and the Missouri River access points used by the expedition. Fuel up, stretch, and take 20 minutes on the river before continuing west.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: Wolf Point Montana Fort Peck Indian Reservation
Wolf Point Wild Horse Stampede Montana

visits.text: Columbia River Gorge — Cross this dramatic 1,200-meter-deep canyon where the Columbia River cuts through the Cascade Range. The 130-kilometer gorge creates the only sea-level route through the mountains, featuring over 77 waterfalls.

Multnomah Falls — Admire Oregon's tallest waterfall plunging 189 meters in two major tiers connected by the historic Benson Bridge built in 1914. The iconic cascade attracts over 2 million visitors annually to its easily accessible viewing platform.

Maryhill Museum of Art — Explore this eclectic collection housed in a 1914 mansion overlooking the Columbia River. Holdings include European paintings, Auguste Rodin sculptures, Native American artifacts, and Queen Marie of Romania's furnishings.

Yakima Valley wine country — Drive through Washington's premier wine region producing 40% of the state's grapes. The rain shadow of the Cascades creates ideal conditions for Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Chardonnay.

activities.text: Wine tasting tour — Visit family-owned wineries and tasting rooms sampling award-winning Washington wines. Many vineyards offer tours explaining viticulture practices and the valley's unique terroir.

Historic Highway 14 drive — Navigate this scenic route along the Columbia's north bank past basalt cliffs, orchards, and Native American fishing sites. The highway offers dramatic gorge views and access to small riverside communities.

Farm-to-table dinner — Savor cuisine featuring Yakima Valley produce including hops, apples, cherries, and wine. The agricultural region supplies ingredients to restaurants throughout the Pacific Northwest.

days_plan.itin_title: Washington State's I-90 Corridor: Seattle, Snoqualmie Pass & Alpine Lakes

places.name: Yankton Sd

visits.text: Yankton sits at the base of Gavins Point Dam, the smallest of the six Missouri River mainstem dams but the one that creates Lewis and Clark Lake — a 25-mile reservoir over what was open river in September 1804. The dam visitor center connects the engineering to the Lewis and Clark passage. Yankton's 1924 Meridian Bridge, a swing-span structure over the Missouri with a pedestrian walkway, gives you the same crossing-angle river view that expedition members would have known. It's 80 miles north of Sioux City — a worthwhile break before the long Plains stretch to Pierre.

days_plan.itin_title: Lewis & Clark Trail: St. Louis to Great Falls, Retracing the 1804 Expedition

map_keywords: Gavins Point Dam Yankton South Dakota

visits.text: Explore York's traditional Southern town center, where the historic York County Courthouse and prominent commercial buildings line streets that retain their 19th-century character. As part of South Carolina's Olde English District, the town connects to important Revolutionary War history. The McCelvey Center, housed in a handsome brick building, serves as a Southern Revolutionary War Institute with exhibits and programs.

activities.text: Enjoy the outdoors at Anne Springs Close Greenway, an expansive natural area well-suited for quiet afternoon hikes, horseback riding, and nature observation. The greenway's trails wind through diverse habitats providing peaceful escapes from busier destinations. York's combination of history and natural beauty offers a fitting conclusion to a South Carolina journey.

days_plan.itin_title: South Carolina Grand Tour: Charleston to Blue Ridge Mountains

visits.text: Yosemite National Park - America's Cathedral of Stone and Water: Sheer granite walls rise 3,000 feet from the valley floor as waterfalls plunge from heights that dwarf skyscrapers. This UNESCO World Heritage Site inspired the conservation movement and continues to humble millions of annual visitors.

El Capitan - The World's Most Famous Big Wall: This 3,000-foot vertical granite monolith draws elite climbers from around the globe attempting multi-day ascents of routes like The Nose. Even from the valley floor, watching climbers as tiny specks on the face is mesmerizing.

Half Dome - Yosemite's Iconic Sentinel: This distinctive granite dome, cleaved as if by a giant's axe, rises 4,737 feet above the valley floor. The cables route to the summit remains one of America's most sought-after hiking achievements.

Yosemite Falls - North America's Tallest Waterfall: Water plunges 2,425 feet in three tiers, creating a spectacle visible from throughout Yosemite Valley. Peak flow occurs in May and June when snowmelt transforms the falls into a thundering curtain of white.

activities.text: Tunnel View - The Most Photographed Vista in America: This overlook presents El Capitan, Half Dome, and Bridalveil Fall in a single sweeping panorama that has graced countless postcards, calendars, and desktop backgrounds.

Glacier Point - Stand 3,214 Feet Above the Valley: This dramatic overlook provides bird's-eye views of Yosemite's greatest hits, with Half Dome at eye level and the High Sierra stretching to the horizon.
Mariposa Grove - Walk Among Giant Sequoias: Over 500 mature giant sequoias populate this ancient forest, including the 1,800-year-old Grizzly Giant with its 96-foot circumference.

days_plan.itin_title: The Ultimate California Road Trip: From Redwood Forests to Desert Valley Depths

visits.text: With a small kayak, Chris goes down the Colorado River to the canals of the Sonora Desert, crosses the border into Mexico then returns exhausted to the United States on foot.

days_plan.itin_title: Into the Wild Trail: Retracing Chris McCandless's Legendary American Odyssey

visits.text: You cross the world's only Y-shaped bridge - and you could end up on the same side of the river as you started, depending on which of its two branches you took. Zanesville's Y-Bridge has spanned the confluence of the Licking and Muskingum rivers since 1814, when the first wooden bridge was inaugurated by architect Rufus Scott. The current bridge - the fifth of its kind - was built in 1984 in steel and concrete to replace the fourth (1902), itself a pioneer of reinforced concrete in its time. The structure is so unique that aviator Amelia Earhart declared that Zanesville was 'the most recognizable city in the country' seen from the air. Ripley's Believe It or Not immortalized the bridge with the formula: 'the only bridge in the world you can cross and stay on the same side of the river'. Park at Putnam Hill Park for the overhead view.

You climb to the Putnam Hill overlook and suddenly have beneath your eyes the only panoramic view of the entire Y-Bridge and the confluence of the two rivers. From this hill, you understand at once why the engineers of 1814 built a Y-shaped bridge rather than two separate bridges: the two waterways meet at almost a right angle, and a single three-branched structure solved the problem in one go. What you don't see from below, you see here: the strange intersection that emerges in the middle of the waters. Local anecdote: in 1810, Zanesville was briefly Ohio's capital - it replaced Chillicothe for two years before Columbus became the permanent capital in 1816. This park is perfect for a picnic with a view.

You push open the doors of the Zanesville Museum of Art and discover a town that was briefly the world capital of art pottery: local clays and coal energy from Muskingum County gave birth in the early 20th century to manufacturers like Weller Pottery, Roseville Pottery, and J.B. Owens Pottery - Weller alone produced three train cars of pottery per day, making it the world's largest manufacturer. You walk through the galleries admiring the matte and satin glazes in shades of moss green, earth brown, and olive that the Art Nouveau of the 1900s made famous. These pieces today fetch thousands of dollars at auction in major houses. Arrive on Tuesday: admission is free.

activities.text: You glide on the Muskingum River and pass your first lock manually operated by a state lockmaster - the 11 locks of the Muskingum are the only ones in the United States still manually operated, a system dating from the 19th century. You paddle between red-orange sandstone cliffs whose reflections dance on the calm water, you pass great blue herons motionless on gravel banks, and corn belt farms descend to the water's edge with their rusty silos. The river is still unknown to travelers outside Ohio - you'll mostly encounter local brown trout fishermen. Ask your outfitter for the downstream section toward McConnelsville: the wildest and most peaceful of the entire route.

You drive on the National Road - the first federally funded road in America, built between 1811 and 1837 from Cumberland, Maryland, to Vandalia, Illinois - and realize that today's US Route 40 follows exactly the original route. It reached Zanesville in 1826 and the Y-Bridge immediately became the most strategic passage on its Ohio route. You look for period buildings along the road: the entrance to the National Road/Zane Grey Museum in Norwich, the stone arches of the old stagecoach stations in Gratiot. For enthusiasts, the 1812 Norfolk Inn in Norwich is one of the rare intact National Road buildings still standing in this section of Ohio.

days_plan.itin_title: Interior Ohio Loop - From Columbus to Mohican Gorges
